initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
Forbrugerrådet Tænk welcomes the EC’s inception impact assessment, which contains many points we agree with. We welcome a revision of the TSD and we agree that the main aspects to be tackled are chemical risks, internet-connected toys, and compliance & enforcement. 

We suggest mandatory labelling of the chemical content in toys. Labelling like we know from food and cosmetics. This will enable consumers to make an informed choice. This way consumers can also avoid chemicals not yet regulated, because the regulatory process is lengthy.

Please see further comments in the document attached (answers from BEUC and ANEC), which we support.

Sincerely,

Claus Jørgensen and Helen Amundsen,
Forbrugerådet Tænk

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+, KA131, KA171 – 2021-27 

The new Erasmus-program generation 2021-27 offers more opportunities for students and staff which certainly increase the administrative complexity of the program.

The ambitious aim, to reduce this complexity by introducing a digital tool (EWP) for its implementation, unfortunately failed: EWP was badly prepared and is far from its original objective, to assist universities in their daily business. The testing phase of EWP, which should have been finalised before the programme start, has been shifted into the running programme itself and universities are struggling to find improvised solutions (e.g. for OLA, IIA, BM). This is time consuming – for university staff as well as for students.

Moreover, the doubled budget for Erasmus+ is not yet visible for the institutions. We have less money for scholarships!

It is to be hoped that problems can be solved during the next years and that Erasmus+ will get back its good reputation.
For the next program-generation we wish us more continuity in proven administrative processes and a better effort/effectiveness relationship.

It is to be hoped that problems can be solved during the next years and that Erasmus+ will get back its good reputation. For the next program-generation we wish us more continuity in proven administrative processes and a better effort/effectiveness relationship.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
As outlined in the Inception Impact Assessment (IIA) EU toy safety rules fail to adequately safeguard children’s health and well-being. Thus BEUC welcomes the Commission’s plan to revise the Toy Safety Directive (TSD) by building on its evaluation and Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability (CSS). In particular, the revision must:

- ENABLE CHEMICAL SAFEGUARDS TO BE SET FOR ALL TOYS as proposed in the IIA. The Commission should also be empowered to establish protective requirements for other essential safety aspects, such as mechanical or physical risks: for instance while impulse noise limits should be revised, visibility and legibility requirements should be set for warnings. Introducing a fast-track mechanism to address sudden emerging risks is also a priority. 

- REDUCE CHILDREN'S EXPOSURE TO HARMFUL CHEMICALS, including by extending the generic risk management approach in line with the CSS and by substantially reducing the generic limits for derogated CMR substances, as proposed in the IIA. This should include known, presumed or suspected EDCs as children are more vulnerable to their negative effects. The outlined option to consolidate and further strengthen existing chemical limits should be explored, along with the possible creation of positive lists drawing, e.g. on the Cosmetics or Plastic Food Contact Material Regulations. Options to protect children against possible combination effects are missing in the IIA but must be identified. See further recommendations bit.ly/3ncHIw6 

- MAKE TOY LABELS WORK FOR CONSUMERS, e.g. by exploring how an obligation to declare the chemical content of toys should be introduced as proposed in the IIA. This could help consumer to make informed purchasing choices while also facilitating enforcement efforts. Digital labelling can complement but must however never replace on-product labels.

- ADDRESS NEW RISKS FOR CHILDREN'S WELL-BEING. Updating the concept of safety in line with what has been proposed in the draft General Product Safety Regulation (GPSR) would allow to cover threats from connected toys or toys that include artificial intelligence (e.g cybersecurity or psychological risks) and this during their entire lifespan. Introducing EC-type examination for such products and other categories of toys should also be explored, in combination with additional measures to improve the traceability and accountability in the supply chain.

- PROTECT CHILDREN FROM DANGEROUS TOYS SOLD ONLINE: in line with our recommendations for the GPSR, the definition for economic operators must be revised to acknowledge the role of online marketplaces in the supply chain: as the concept of authorised representative won’t solve all the issues posed by the online sales of non-compliant and dangerous toys, clear obligations must be created for online marketplaces to detect, remove and prevent the reappearance of such products. Consistency must be ensured in the different legislative frameworks so marketplaces can be addressed with all enforcement measures and be held liable when no other responsible economic operator can be identified/takes action.

- IMPROVE MARKET SURVEILLANCE AS WELL AS COMPLIANCE AND ENFORCEMENT, e.g. by converting the TSD into a Regulation and ensuring that it will work well with the Market Surveillance Regulation and other horizontal or sector specific legislation that are either under development (AI Act) or revision (Radio equipment Directive, GPSD, etc). In this context, it is also key that Member States significantly increase their capacities to enforce the TSD and that cooperation at different level is fostered, i.e. between national authorities and with the EU, across sectors, with consumer organisations and at international level. Ensuring more consistency in the reporting obligations of Member States and allowing for a systematic collection of EU accidents and injury data are also important to consider. Provisions on penalties should be aligned with those in the proposed GPSR.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
The International Fragrance Association (IFRA) welcomes the opportunity to comment on the Inception Impact Assessment on the revision of the Toy Safety Directive. IFRA would like to raise the following comments:

1) The extension of the generic approach to risk management should be based on risk, not hazard.
For more than 40 years, IFRA has carefully examined the safe use of fragrance ingredients and established the IFRA Standards based on a continuously refined risk assessment approach for fragrance ingredients. Chemical risk management should continue to be based on a good understanding of their uses and of potential exposure. The fragrance industry is concerned about the potential ban of substances irrespective of the risk and exposure. This is particularly important for fragrances, which typically only represent a small fraction of the toy but can play a key role for consumers’ satisfaction.

2) The simultaneous exposure to multiple chemicals should be based on current scientific knowledge and subject to impact assessment.
Introduction of further requirements has to be carefully evaluated and would require a detailed impact assessment to fully understand the effects. It also needs to be built on solid scientific foundations. A fragrance mixture is typically composed of a combination of dozens to hundreds of fragrance ingredients and are present at (very) low levels in the final product.

3) The revision of the Toy Safety Directive is an opportunity to ensure alignment with latest EU provisions on fragrances
IFRA welcomes the revision of the Toy Safety Directive as an opportunity to include appropriate legal mechanisms in the future Toy Safety Directive/ Regulation to update the applicable rules with the latest EU provisions on fragrances (Annex II, including the lists of prohibited fragrances and fragrances to be labelled on the toys) through comitology. This would ensure that the tailor-made labelling requirements for specific allergenic fragrances in certain experimental toy sets can be easily updated when the lists of allergenic fragrances are amended.
When proceeding with this revision, IFRA would also welcome consistency with the Cosmetic products Regulation, and notably with regard to the date of application of the extended list of fragrance allergens.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Europeans for Safe Connection welcome the evaluation of the WEEE. It is crucial that the environmental footprint from the waste from electrical equipment will be evaluated thoroughly and an updated WEEE being written accordingly to protect the environment and health of humans. As we ask in proposal nr. 13 of the Stop 5G Stay Connected but Protected ECI: Include "Waste from Electrical and Electronical Equipment" (WEEE), other waste products, and the environmental impacts of mining for rare earth minerals and metals used in electronical equipment in the Zero pollution action plan. It is devastating to see that only on average 42,5% of the WEEE is recycled in the EU when countries like Estonia recycle 76%, Norway 72%, Iceland 71% and Sweden 70%. What kind of recycling system these countries have implemented? When measured per capita, Europeans (16.2 Kg) produce the most e-waste per person, followed by Oceania (16.1 Kg) and the Americas (13.3 Kg). Europe recycle the highest % WEEE worldwide but it is still only 42,5%. We must do better than that. Approximately 21% of the WEEE comes from computers, laptops, mobilephones, wifi routers, ect. There are several high-value raw materials in technology devices including gold, silver, copper, and iron. E.g. recycling one million used cell phones can recover as much as 350 kg of silver, 15876 kg of copper, 34 kg of gold, and 15 kg of palladium according to https://theroundup.org/global-e-waste-statistics/ It should be a top priority that we in the EU have effective recycling systems, where all electronic waste is considered a valuable resource and where it is properly recycled within the EU borders. Just like some EU countries have for recycling bottles. For example, in Denmark there is a deposit on bottles and the deposit is paid to the person that returns the bottles to the recycling system. It is crucial that the individual valuable metals, minerals and chemicals must be recoverable from the used electronics and then reused in new electronics. Due to the speed and scope of technological development, the search for resources to produce new electronics in EU and worldwide has increased significantly. Mining for metals and minerals is destructive to the local environment and too often harmful to the health of local people. As we ask in proposal 14 Update Ecodesign Directive from 2009 setting tight limits to resource and energy waste of all connected devices including antennas and satellites. It is important that all electronics are designed and produced so that metals, minerals and chemicals can be extracted from the electronics when they end up as waste and it is also very important that the resources and energy consumption is minimized. All electronics must be Easy to repair, Easy to recycle, Easy to extract all metals, mineral and chemical substances and of course More lifelong lasting. It is also very important that the EU demands that all EE produced within the EU and imported EE complies to the RoHS Directive which restricts hazardous substances in Electrical and Electronic Equipment (RoHS). The RoHS Directive currently restricts the use of ten substances: lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, polybrominated biphenyls (PBB) and polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDE), bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and diisobutyl phthalate (DIBP). https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/waste-and-recycling/rohs-directive_en Best regards Pernille Schriver on behalf of Europeans for Safe Connections This feedback was sent from a wired internet connection - No use of harmful radiation - Less electricity consumption - Increased data security

initiative: State aid rules for banks in difficulty - evaluation
feedback_text:
During the major banking crisis, the federal government, with a left-bottom-right touch, has prevented the collapse of banks, the economy and supply.
Further rescue at the expense of the people would hardly play a role in the expected monetary burden on citizens.
But I am strongly opposed to other security packages. These would further support and even partially legitimise the irresponsible behaviour/economy of financial institutions.

Further rescue at the expense of the people would hardly play a role in the expected monetary burden on citizens. But I am strongly opposed to other security packages. These would further support and even partially legitimise the irresponsible behaviour/economy of financial institutions.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
We appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback on relevant issues for the impact assessment and  ask to consider the following issues in detail during impact assessment: 

PEF for 100% H2: for Lot 2
We welcome the possibility to indicate future H2-ready appliance on the upcoming energy label. With regards to energy transition, the PEF should be based on expected sources in the near future rather than steam reforming with current energy mix. This could lead to significantly lower values and could support the technology. 
The H2 targets, especially for green H2, within the EU should be considered and analysed in further detail. European and national H2 strategies are pushing towards renewable H2, the mentioned target values for H2 production could be used as a basis for further developments. Studies on national level (DE: DVGW; IINAS; bdew…) should also be taken into account. 


T peak: Lot 2
Test conditions for DHW performance of heat pumps are still under discussion. Various proposals can be found from fulfilment of test requirements at 55°C peak temperature to a test temperature of 50°C with or without mathematical corrections by the help of malus percentage points.  The attached document offers an alternative calculation method, applicable to test temperatures between 52°C and 55°C. It provides correction factors for the COP, which better reflect the real behavior of heat pumps compared to a malus system.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
With Erasmus, our school develops, internationalises, meets its needs and mainstreams its educational work.
Really “opens the eye” of participants.It is an important project that should be continued because it helps to professionally improve graduates in a more digital working environment, foster a common European thinking on the environment, include and integrate weak social groups. 

There is no better feedback from the spontaneous impressions of students who participated in three programmes we have implemented:

items that we had no other than in the books.’

we visited was one of the best we have seen many species of fish. With training
in clinics and centres we learn important things for the health sector, especially in
physiotherapy I am interested to follow.”

“I explored my horizons and I knew new people”

“The 14 days of my stay abroad gave me the opportunity to learn about culture,
culture and history of another people, in particular I have been impressed by the organisation and the
infrastructure where specific groups of people (persons with special needs) are not excluded.
I found that there was no social exclusion, for example women were working in
car manufacturer BMW, people with an appearance different from what I had used in the
Greece (clothing, squid, tattoo) worked in municipal bodies.The education system
it differs from our own in terms of infrastructure and subsequent professional rehabilitation
learners have expanded my spiritual horizons, changing my attitudes in my life. ”

“The experience of my presence in a country where they speak a language other than my own, have
different cultures and lifestyles. I acquainted with the history of the site through
visits to museums and guided tours. I contacted the country’s education system
visiting a vocational school and I found differences and similarities with our own”

“The most important experience may have been that we realised the wide range of job opportunities
employment of vocational education graduates in the European labour market, in
areas that a Greek provincial city like ours unfortunately does not offer.
It was also the socio-cultural experience that we gained in the places where we had a great deal of importance.
we visited, where it became apparent that anyone trying to do so could co-exist and co-exist and
be integrated into learning and work environments other than their own”

“I first travelled abroad and the combination of education and cultural activities created a
experience that will leave me unfortunate.”

“The experiences we have gained through ERASMUS+ will certainly be needed in
future as I attended courses that I learned to work, I am kindly featuring with them
clients, as well as my colleagues, take initiatives when I must and at the same time
I travelled to a country I didn’t know if I could visit. My Barcelona museums
they spurred the interest to travel to other countries to be able to train.”

“Through my chance to participate in the Erasmus programme, I have been impressed with
museums we visited, the aesthetic centres. I would certainly propose it to other people. The
Erasmus really opens the horizons of a person”

“The positive aspect of Erasis was with a proposal, the only experience I was given to fill with

construction). 
I do not think that there is a negative aspect.”

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Thank you for a very meaningful programme!

We are very grateful that our students have been able to take part in traineeships, study visits and teaching visits to different countries of the European Union. Our resources would certainly not have opened up such a wide range of opportunities. 

Students’ feedback has been largely positive and they have a good understanding of the added value of studying a traineeship abroad. Teachers and staff have also been very satisfied with the mobility they have travelled. The activities carried out under the project are essential to promote the internationalisation of the higher education institution and increase our competence in the international educational landscape.
text_raw: Thank you for a very meaningful programme! We are very grateful that our students have been able to take part in traineeships, study visits and teaching visits to different countries of the European Union. Our resources would certainly not have opened up such a wide range of opportunities. Students’ feedback has been largely positive and they have a good understanding of the added value of studying a traineeship abroad. Teachers and staff have also been very satisfied with the mobility they have travelled. The activities carried out under the project are essential to promote the internationalisation of the higher education institution and increase our competence in the international educational landscape.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Water heaters are among the most energy-guzzling technologies in our homes and one with the largest potential for energy savings and climate emissions. While they are technically among the appliances that can best profit from local production of renewable energy (i.e. in combination with solar thermal solutions), as of now water heaters are a major driver for gas and electricity domestic consumption. 

Remarkably, the relevance of this consumption in UE customers’ bills varies a lot across the EU being a key spending factor in countries where space heating demand is the lowest. 

They should be regulated as much as possible to align this policy not only with the ambition of the EU’s 2030 climate targets and the different targets set in the Fit-for-55 Package but also with the urgent call for energy independence underlined by the recent REpowerEU communication.
Effective ecodesign and energy labelling regulations have a key role as they contributed to annual CO2 savings of 80Mt so far, together with space heaters. However, we are deeply concerned about some of the provisions contained in the Commission’s proposal discussed in the last Consultation Forum (September 2021), and remarkably about the weakened ecodesign requirement for water heaters and tanks.
The revision of the Ecodesign regulation (2013/814) and Energy labelling regulation (2013/812) must focus on:
- Setting the efficiency requirement at 110% for XXL water heater sizes and above. This would allow getting rid of most of the fossil appliances, as all XXL and above water heaters should have higher efficiency limits, as well as the phase-out of fuel-fired and electric water heaters for the XXL, 3XL and 4XL sizes. 
- The extension of the regulation to other domestic water heating products that are quickly becoming a common feature in European kitchens. If water heaters delivering boiling water are not included in the scope of this regulation, they should be included in this review clause.
- The establishment of third-party assessment, which should determine the energy efficiency and the load profile with the largest reference energy (Qref) that can be supplied by the water heater. For solar water heaters, maintaining and supporting the existing de-facto third-party assessment, Solar Keymark. 
- Availability of spare parts during the whole lifetime of the product. These should be delivered in 15 days maximum, given the critical function these appliances perform in households, notably in winter.
- Testing in normal and comfort mode, not in eco-mode or other modes that limit their supply of hot water or increase time to start providing hot water.
 
Furthermore, we do not support the use of H2 for heating, not only for the unlikelihood of this commodity becoming a large share of the domestic supply anytime soon but also for the reason that, in the current geopolitical situation, the use of H2 in hard-to-abate sectors others than heating would displace much greater quantities of imported gas. Nevertheless, should a provision for hydrogen readiness be agreed upon by the majority of Member States, we would support the double scale proposal of the EC (pag. 19) benchmarking the appliance’s efficiency with 100% CH4 and 100% H2 fuel supplies.

We strongly oppose the requirement for all gas-fired water heaters to be able to operate with a blend of fossil gas and up to 20% hydrogen. 
There will not be enough renewable hydrogen produced to replace the amount of gas currently used for water heating. Burning hydrogen in gas-fired water heaters is extremely inefficient and costly, and direct electrification should be the preferred option. 

The Climate crisis is the most important topic under discussion in the EU, we firmly need to slash energy consumption by a powerful and bold application of ecodesign and energy labelling measures.

The feedback is a joint action from ECOS, EEB (European Environmental Bureau) and Coolproducts coalition.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I cannot talk about the 2014-2020 programme because I have not been able to know it, but with regard to the new programme 2021-2027 I can comment on the following strengths:

— Building on the needs and challenges of the Centre helps to improve it by analysing its weaknesses and by directing it towards practices that improve it.

— The importance of sustainability in the programme helps students of all ages to understand that if we do not care for our planet, our future is difficult

It encourages families to whom it would be impossible to pay for their children for socio-economic reasons, and in the context of an educational project that gives them values such as: solidarity, autonomy, development of language skills or teamwork among others

— Allows students to learn about ICT and therefore to be prepared for their future work

As a weakness I would only point out: do something more intuitive to raise projects as they are initially difficult and some schools may drop out in the effort to apply for an Erasmus + project 

Thank you very much for everything.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
We welcome the EU Commission initiative to revise the Toy Safety Directive in relation to widening the scope of toxic chemicals to be banned for children toys and products intended for children use. We also very much encourage the EU community to widen the scope for toys and children products above the age of 36 months. 
Arnika and IPEN agree wholeheartedly to broaden the scope of the Directive toward all endocrine disruptors or substances that are persistent and bioaccumulative. Arnika and other NGOs across Europe have repeatedly demonstrated the presence and/or contamination of toys with persistent organic pollutants (POPs) as well as endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs). 

Our recommendations:

● Extension of the EU Toy Safety Directive should allow for automatic banning of all persistent, bioaccumulative (PBT or vPvB) and endocrine disrupting chemicals from all products covered by the Directive. Class-based approach allowing for banning brominated flame retardants, per- and polyfloruoalkyl substances, chlorinated paraffins and phthalates as groups should be applied.
● Current and future exemptions to total banns over PBT, vPvB and EDC should be eliminated as these often cover the mostly used applications (such the derogation of PBDEs limit for electronics in ROHS directive).
● The EU Toy Safety Directive should carefully work to include all toy-like items (sport and art accessories or hair supplies designed and used by children similarly as toys) under its scope. 
● Problematic materials (i.e., PVC) demanding applying various additives with persistent, bioaccumulative (PBT or vPvB) and endocrine disrupting properties should be also covered by the Directive.
● All legal thresholds should be based on total, not migratory, levels to cover all potential uses of the items covered by the Directive.
● Equal standards should be applied to articles made of virgin and recycled materials. 
● The Commission shall prioritize implementing the Digital Product Passport for toys and children articles.

More detailed submission to be found in the attached document.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The Detergent Regulation provides a platform for advanced protection of consumers and the environment, by enshrining industry’s voluntary approaches (e.g. phosphate restrictions) into a consistent, coherent and progressive sector-specific legislation. Overall, we believe consumer-centric legislation supports consumers to use products safely, effectively, and appropriately, whereas horizontal legislation such as REACH or CLP focus on restricting the use, rather than empowering consumers to make sustainable choices and to use the product safely, effectively, and sustainably. CLP is limited to providing hazard-based information on the product and some of its constituents, linked to precautionary information that consumers can often not act upon nor understand. 
Therefore, we generally believe that EU consumers’ interests are best served by maintaining sector-specific legislation, in particular the Detergent Regulation, rather than it being absorbed into broader pieces of legislation such as REACH. For this reason, we believe that the current regulation should be maintained and have identified some areas for further optimisation.

Biodegradation
The Detergent Regulation has been instrumental in environmental protection through its requirement on biodegradation for surfactants, well before REACH came into existence. At Unilever, we aim to use 100% biodegradable ingredients by 2030, globally. We therefore believe that the time is right to expand biodegradation requirement to all organic chemicals used in detergents, of course allowing industry sufficient time to convert. Such biodegradability requirements should typically demand ultimate biodegradability of the molecules within a given time-frame relevant to the different substances used. For example, dedicated methods for polymers might be required. By demanding biodegradability of all organic chemicals used, the Detergent Regulation could again be at the forefront of protection of the environment. 

Simplification of labelling
Different pieces of research have shown that consumers fail to understand some of the labelling information e.g. CLP pictograms, and struggle with the amount of information associated with these. 
Besides, there are overlaps between labelling requirements under the Detergent and CLP regulations that may further increase confusion and open the door to possible inconsistencies. Therefore, we would propose a simplification of labelling requirements under the Detergent Regulation to focus on consumer relevant safety information that is specific to detergent products, in a way that is easy to understand, and that can actually be used by consumers.

Digitalisation of labelling information 
We believe that the development of digital technologies is an opportunity to provide tailored, actionable, and detailed information to consumers. Not only can it help in preventing overload of information on the packs, it can also be instrumental to meet the demands of specific consumers that are unable to read what’s on pack or need dedicated information quickly (e.g. on allergens). Moreover, future sustainable consumption modes designed to reduce packaging like in-store re-filling and at home refills may even more rely on digital information to correctly inform consumers. As ways of doing shopping continue to evolve, the digitalisation of information is becoming even more important. The Detergent Regulation has been a pioneer in this area, requiring both on-pack and digital information on products since 2005. Further expanding the provision of digitalisation of information to consumers would be a logical step to keep the Detergent Regulation future proof.  

Concluding, the Detergent Regulation is a consumer-oriented legislation that is fit for purpose and effective. It has positively served the needs of consumers and all stakeholders and can be further optimised in full alignment with the objectives of the EU Green Deal and other upcoming initiatives.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In the current situation, I can only give feedback on the Erasmus+ 2014/20 program. Thanks to the initiative of the European Union with the implementation of the Erasmus program, the EWE Consortium that we coordinate has gone from being made up of 3 educational centers to a total of 22 vocational training centers, with more centers joining each year with a forecast of integrating educational centers of different autonomous communities in the period 2021/27. In 2014 we participated with 60 professional training students from the Professional Family of physical activities, currently for the 2022/23 academic year we will send more than 160 professional training students from different professional families. Throughout the Erasmus+ 2014/20 program, around 600 vocational training students from the entire Valencian Autonomous Community have been able to benefit from this program. At the end of the Erasmus+ 2014/20 program, we began with the training through Job shadowing of the teachers of the Consortium centers, currently for the 2022/23 academic year more than 100 teachers will carry out Job shadowing or Training Course in Europe and we have also open this training opportunity to non-teaching staff of our centers.
Thanks to the Erasmus+ 2014/20 program, the professional training of our autonomous community has improved its quality, strengthened its European participation and become aware of citizenship of the European Union.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Like most Member States, Romania has not reached its collection targets; Eurostat data from 2019 ranks Romania last in the EU, with a 27.5% collection rate. At the same time, the report of the National Agency for Environmental Protection - ANPM, indicates for 2019 a collection rate of 37.6%, 10% above Eurostat. The transposition of the WEEE Directive through the adoption of the Emergency Ordinance 5/2015 had some shortcomings. 1. Firstly, Romania still does not have a WEEE collection infrastructure. Thus, Art. 5, para. (2) of the WEEE Directive has been transposed in GEO 5/2015 by Art. 10 para. (2), which does not require local authorities to set up collection centres. As a result, local authorities have not set up the necessary infrastructure, and there are currently only a few such centres in partnership with PROs. The quantities collected through such centres are less than 2% of the total quantities collected in Romania. 2. The ban on landfilling of WEEE has been transposed only formally, in reality it is not enforced. Awareness campaigns are not continuous and so far have only been carried out by PROs in partnership with local authorities, with no consistent and continuous message at central level. 3. Preparation for re-use has also been introduced only formally, without implementing rules, although the principle has been transposed by Article 16 of GEO 5/2015, a ministerial order setting out the methodological rules for the implementation of the principle is needed in order to implement this provision on a large scale. In Romania, there is no data on preparing for re-use, and the figures are, we believe, extremely low. 4. Romania ignores the existence of the alternative on setting collection targets in the WEEE Directive, the authorities have not carried out any national study showing the amount of WEEE available on the market. Coalitia PRO DEEE raises an alarm about the newly published data in the e-tool for WEEE generated, we do not belive the data to be real, they are rough estimates. 5. There are problems with the treatment of WEEE, the transposition of Art. 8(1) and (5) has been done, but there are still major problems with implementation. Order No 417/2021 has been approved but unfortunately it is not mandatory to apply the standards and some operators do not apply them. Romania is still facing a number of problems. 1. Informal collection and improper treatment. Informal collection for the sale of scrap metal is still common practice in Romania. It is extremely useful to start a nationwide study to make substantiated estimates of the quantities of WEEE that end up at the scrap yard. 2. The existence of a loose legislative framework for the authorisation of PROs, without financial guarantees to ensure market soundness and without targets in the first years, which leads to chaos in the WEEE market. Currently there are 20 PROs on the market in Romania, more than half of which have no collection targets and may charge low and unrealistic fees for WEEE management. There is no coordination system at national level in Romania. 3. Another very important issue is the penalty of 4 lei/kg or 8 lei/kg for not meeting the WEEE targets. So far no market study has been carried out by the Ministry of Environment to determine the appropriate amount of such a contribution in relation to the value of the services that would have been necessary to meet the unmet targets. The current high contribution favours excessive price speculation in accessing WEEE. Proposals: - We propose, as an alternative to the Directive, the development and adoption of a EU WEEE Regulation which would close the transposition gaps in Member States; - Mandatory application of CENELEC standards by MS; - Adapt targets for re-use/preparedness for re-use and not for recycling/recovery; - Start studies at European level on quantities available on Member States' markets, substantiated estimates etc - Introduce monitoring of the application of EU law

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
I would like to comment on the following feedback on the amendment of the Toy Safety Directive:

Improving the overall risk assessment is always good, but it should not be burdensome compared to current practice. Smaller manufacturers also need to be able to meet the requirements at a moderate cost in order to remain competitive with larger companies with better financial means to enable a heterogeneous industry.

The Digital Pass Programme should be seen as an opportunity, but with limitations on the information to be shared. It is important that we, as a business, understand what information is shared. A consumer should be able to benefit from the information disclosed. The disclosure of technical documents and formulae should be prohibited as it can lead to abuse or cyber-attacks. In this case, the information should only be used to ensure the safety of the product — this should be the main concern for digital passports.

As long as the risk is obvious and measurable, we support a rapid reporting mechanism and the move from a directive to a regulation to ensure EU-wide harmonisation with improved efficiency in updates and changes.

There are reporting obligations, but we have found that they are not implemented by all Member States. It would be impossible to achieve improvements if the existing procedures were not applied — the problem is that practices and procedures must be followed uniformly by each Member State. Internal market members should communicate internally, but often a Member State cannot see what other Member States do, which makes it difficult to react.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Note: Any views expressed are the views of the German Environment Agency and may not in any circumstances be regarded as stating an official position of the German Federal Ministry for the Environment, Nature Conservation, Nuclear Safety and Consumer Protection or the Federal Republic of Germany. 1 General Comments 1.1 Need for adaptation of the WEEE Directive We welcome the initiative to revise the WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU. With regard to the circular economy of electrical and electronic equipment, we see a noticeable need for adaptation of the currently applicable European legal provisions, mainly for the following reasons: In context of the European Green Deal and the EU Circular Economy Action Plan, only a comprehensive life cycle approach for electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) will be able to effectively and sustainably address the current challenges with regard to climate change and resource efficiency. Therefore, the (future) requirements for EEE design (e.g., ESPR, RoHS) and WEEE management (WEEE Directive) should be intertwined and should complement one another in a coherent way. There is a need for a compulsory verification of EEE producers also by online marketplaces/fulfilment service providers to prevent free riders from third countries in the new EU-law on (W)EEE. In line with the Circular Economy Action Plan, the EPR principle should be strengthened to promote a better WEEE collection. Ensuring environmentally sound and proper WEEE collection and achieving collection targets also includes a dense network of collection and take-back points as well as (harmonized and) specific minimum consumer information requirements regarding disposal. Treatment requirements should be updated according to current challenges due to recent developments of WEEE treatment technologies as well as of EEE design and composition. In addition, preparation for reuse should be strengthened and high quality recycling enhanced. Monitoring requirements should be reconsidered in terms of setting ambitious but reasonable targets accompanied by appropriate calculation methods for which the necessary data basis is realistically determinable. 1.2 Legal framework of the future WEEE Directive We suggest that the new EU legislation in the area of (waste) electrical and electronic equipment short: (W)EEE should continue to be based at least also on the environmental legal basis of Article 192 paragraph 1 TFEU (especially if it changes into a regulation), because only in this way mandatory national specifics that reinforce protection can be maintained in the future. With the inclusion of new aspects and life-cycle phases, the complexity of the legal provisions increases: A number of issues concerning the life cycle of EEE have already been covered by separate pieces of legislation, especially the Restriction of Hazardous Substances (2011/65/EU) and the Ecodesign Directive (2009/125/EG). The different aspects have different motivations and legal bases in European Primary Law (product-related legislation is based on Article 114 TFEU while waste-related legislation is based on Article 192 paragraph 1 TFEU). To avoid inconsistencies, overlapping (double) regulations, unwanted effects and inefficient implementation or enforcement, an in-depth analysis is necessary to identify the most appropriate legal instrument and legal area for each new aspect. Other current initiatives of the European Commission affect products in the scope of the current WEEE directive: E.g. the proposal for an Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (COM(2022) 142 final), the proposal for a Batteries Regulation (COM(2020) 798 final) and the review of the European ELV Directive (2000/53/EC). When revising WEEE legislation, special care should be given to the interface between relevant legislation concerning both scope and depth. Initially all generalizable life-cycle aspects of EEE not covered by other legislation should be identified.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The programme has evolved successfully in the previous programme period. 

Initiatives such as the Accreditation of beneficiaries of KA1 are welcome as they definitely aim at minimising the burden of NAs as well as beneficiaries (since we had complaints of them repeating themselves in annual applications for funding). 

Lumpsum approach is very much welcome in KA2. However, ranking lists are too many. They should be limited to sectoral ranking lists or at least small and large scale ranking lists per sector. More ranking lists are not so necessary as evaluators take into account the principle of proportionality. 

There is definitely room for improvement as far as IT tools are concerned at both ends, namely beneficiaries and NAs. With regards to PMM the logic and functionalities are not friendly enough. 

Newcomer institutions could be given an advantage at application phase as currently there are many beneficiaries that have application writing as their profession. Therefore, newcomer or smaller applicants cannot compete against them. 

The evolution from the previous programme on the aspect of checks is huge. More resources might be needed to implement all these changes. Also the Pandemic and the Economic crisis give us ground to simplify our procedures more, by developing checklists, targeted checks on risky areas etc. The EC should be more supportive in all these changes.

But in the end the Erasmus+ projects give so mush for the organisation so we will try to come up with a good idea and still try it.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
text_raw: My experience with the Erasmus+ office in Cyprus is excellent. It is obvious that people working in the service want to help carry out the project as well as possible in order to maximise its impact. The procedures are likely to be simplified in terms of individual elements (such as in cases where elements of a project need to be changed), while in relation to the individual elements that the evaluation will assess, I can note the following: • the enhanced inclusion and diversity measures of the programme; The programme enhances diversity by allowing non-discriminatory and bottom-up actions. • the Programme’s contribution to mainstreaming climate actions; The programme includes climate actions, both horizontal (such as green travelling) and vertical (such as climate-focused financing). • simplifications such as the introduction of the lump sum approach in cooperation projects; I believe that the introduction of the lump sum approach to cooperation projects should be extended, as this would allow more participants to participate in actions such as co-participative or educational activities, and to spread the impact of the programme further. • the participation of the outermost regions and overseas countries or territories in the Programme; The participation of outermost regions seems to be supported by the programme, so that the participation of overseas regions would be better facilitated, as often the participation of groups/scientists from these regions can give key elements to a project. • the progress of institutions pursuing an objective of European interest and funded under the Jean Monnet actions in order to achieve the objectives of the programme. DG/DK.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
IBERDROLA welcomes the “Fit for 55 Package”

The Green Deal though the “Fit for 55 Package” offers a unique opportunity to set a proper regulatory framework that boosts momentum on the most efficient renewable alternatives to enable decarbonisation and the achievement of EU climate objectives. 

IBERDROLA calls for and ambitious revision of the labelling and ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters

We believe that the following are the main points that should be considered as part of this revision:

• The electricity Primary Energy Factor (PEF) should be dynamic and forward looking and updated in line with the National Climate and Energy Plans (NECP)

The current ecodesign and labelling class limits are still using the outdated PEF of 2.5 instead of the current one of 2.1 in accordance with the Energy Efficiency Directive 2018/2002/EC (EED). However, updating this value to existing regulation is not enough to reflect the real savings of electrification and implement what the Energy System Integration Strategy stated on this regard: “PEF is an important tool to facilitate comparison of savings across energy carriers. Most renewables are 100% efficient and have a low PEF. The PEF should reflect the real savings brought about by renewable electricity and heat”.

Current default PEF for electricity set by the EED is one of the main barriers to promote the electrification of end uses and, hence, to calculate the corresponding energy efficiency gains. 

A default PEF of 2.1 for electricity seems to be clearly outdated as it does not consider how the increase of renewables in the electricity mix has reduced primary energy consumption, energy dependency and emissions in most MSs. 

The default PEF value for electricity should adequately reflect the future increase of renewables in the electricity mix, it should be dynamic and forward looking, taking into consideration the future energy mix of the MS that is set on its National Climate and Energy Plans (NECP).

• Promotion of efficient and emission free alternatives phasing out fossil systems

Only efficient and emission free alternatives that are in line with EU’s decarbonisation objectives, with no lock in effect or stranded costs shall be considered and promoted. 

In this context, the following measures should be set:

o From 2025 all new heating systems sold shall be zero pollutant and CO2 free.

o The new Ecodesign rules should not require gas boilers on the market to be hydrogen-ready or to replace existing gas boilers with hydrogen ones. Hydrogen may never become a reality for consumers. Residential heating is and easy to abate sector that could be easily decarbonised through direct electrification.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
CESIO believes that: 
- The Detergents Regulation is generally fit for purpose, as was concluded following the last (ex-post) review in 2019. We therefore think that it is not necessary to reopen the Regulation. 
- Specially for surfactants, current biodegradation requirements are fulfilled by companies, and it is not necessary to change these. 
- The labelling requirement for surfactants requiring the “identification of the surfactant group and percentage on the packaging“ can be removed, because it does not give more useful information to consumers. There should no longer be a need to distinguish in Detergents labelling between groups of surfactants (i.e. anionic versus other), because similar requirements now apply to all groups of surfactants. Furthermore, CLP labelling requirements (which complement Detergents labelling requirements) already provide detailed information on the surfactants.  
- CESIO is now also considering whether any other points could be improved or clarified to better support the objectives of the Regulation. If any other point is not clear enough, then it could likely be further clarified via an existing FAQ document.

feedback_text:
The Erasmus + period for study I carried out in Aarhus (Denmark) from January 2020 to June 2020 was for me, despite the pandemic, an incredibly beautiful experience.
The unparalleled organisation and reception by the University of Aarhus (I was in the Faculty of Beavioural and Social Shiences, BSS) for all of us Erasmus students was of great help. Even before the departure, we received a lot of practical information on topics ranging from clothes to paperwork once they arrived, where and when.
In addition to this, the University offers an extremely useful housing service, so paying a tax to the University of Aarhus was able to choose the type of residence I preferred and, having chosen a shared apartment, I was directly in contact with the home owner and I did not have to look for accommodation alone. This service is excellent because it makes it possible to find reliable accommodation without losing time and without the risk of being scattered or, in any event, without the enormous difficulties involved in searching for home in another country where a language that is not known is spoken.
As regards university courses, exams, relations with professors and everything concerning the university in the strict sense, we have also been clearly explained to us in the Introduction Days, which I found to be very useful.
In addition to this, numerous events, games and activities have been organised to promote mutual knowledge among international students and facilitate the construction of friendships. It has also been important in order not to live badly away from their country of origin, but rather to start this new experience with fun and curiosity. I would like to thank the ‘Student House’ (Studenterhus) for this and for all the efforts made not to make us feel alone, but always welcome and welcome.
I also found a very interesting event where all student associations could not present their initiatives, such as sports groups, research groups, language courses, psychological support and much more in which all students could participate. 

I think that when a student can count on this organisation, clarity and welcome, the Erasmus experience becomes truly pleasant and not strenuous and stressful as it can sometimes be.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
On March 8th, the European Commission published their key Communication to react to the urgency to move away from EU’s dependency on fossil fuel imports “REPowerEU”. Heat pumps are seen as a central and key technology for reshaping the EU’s heating energy needs, today and in the future.  Heat pumps have a huge potential to reduce the EU’s dependency on fossil fuels, support the resilience of the EU-wide energy system and decarbonize the EU’s building stock. The Communication set high ambitions: to double the deployment of heat pumps in the next five years, resulting in a cumulative 10 million units over the next 5 years and to reach a 30 million newly installed heat pumps target by 2030.
It is important that these high-level goals are reflected and taken into account in the development of future Eco-design and Energy Labelling requirements for central hydronic space heaters and combination heaters , as well as water heaters . 
Indeed, looking at these ambitions, heat pump manufacturers will be required to deploy at a fast pace highly energy efficient and renewable technologies to meet the demand and the decarbonization goals. Therefore, any requirements set on the testing and design of the products themselves should not hamper this fast paced and ambitious deployment. Increasing the testing time of such equipment, as well as potentially the cost, will unnecessarily prolong the time to market of these solutions. 
Therefore, JBCE believes that any new requirement proposed should be read against and matched to complement the EU’s high-level decarbonization goals, thus ensuring access to the market for efficient heat pumps and a level playing versus fossil-fuel based technologies, that still often benefit from less ambitious efficiency requirements and reduced testing points.
For these reasons, JBCE would like to reiterate some of its previous positions: 
On Ecodesign
- Testing requirements should be maintained at a reasonable level, which ensures high repeatability, reproducibility and reliability. This is the reason, why JBCE deems that the compensation method is currently not ready for regulatory application. 
- JBCE support the inclusion of material efficiency requirements and suggests defining an explicit list of spare parts, covering the most critical parts. 
- JBCE has strong concerns on the proposal to include new monitoring requirements without detailed evaluation and impact assessment. As such proposal would lead to additional costs for end-users for a service that would often not be used. 
- JBCE recommends reconsidering the approach proposed on the peak temperature. Indeed, requiring heat pumps to reach 55°C, does not reflect today’s domestic hot water usage pattern. It will also increase the overall energy consumption of systems, thus, not responding to the goals set with the REPowerEU Communication for resilience, energy efficiency and energy dependency reduction. 
On the Energy Label: JBCE support the rescaling of the energy label from A-G, with a simple label understandable by consumers with a single scale allowing the comparison of all space and combination heating technologies. Such an approach will also support the objectives established by REPowerEU to identify and deploy the most efficient technologies. 
For further details, please refer to latest JBCE-JRAIA JBCE and JRAIA contributions to Consultation Forum on THE REVISION OF THE SPACE AND WATER HEATERS REGULATIONS. 

 1. Commission Regulations (EU) 811/2013 and (EU) 813/2013
 2.Commission (Delegated) Regulations (EU) 812/2013 and (EU) 814/2013

Indeed, looking at these ambitions, heat pump manufacturers will be required to deploy at a fast pace highly energy efficient and renewable technologies to meet the demand and the decarbonization goals. Therefore, any requirements set on the testing and design of the products themselves should not hamper this fast paced and ambitious deployment. Increasing the testing time of such equipment, as well as potentially the cost, will unnecessarily prolong the time to market of these solutions. Therefore, JBCE believes that any new requirement proposed should be read against and matched to complement the EU’s high-level decarbonization goals, thus ensuring access to the market for efficient heat pumps and a level playing versus fossil-fuel based technologies, that still often benefit from less ambitious efficiency requirements and reduced testing points. For these reasons, JBCE would like to reiterate some of its previous positions: On Ecodesign - Testing requirements should be maintained at a reasonable level, which ensures high repeatability, reproducibility and reliability. This is the reason, why JBCE deems that the compensation method is currently not ready for regulatory application. - JBCE support the inclusion of material efficiency requirements and suggests defining an explicit list of spare parts, covering the most critical parts. - JBCE has strong concerns on the proposal to include new monitoring requirements without detailed evaluation and impact assessment. As such proposal would lead to additional costs for end-users for a service that would often not be used. - JBCE recommends reconsidering the approach proposed on the peak temperature. Indeed, requiring heat pumps to reach 55°C, does not reflect today’s domestic hot water usage pattern. It will also increase the overall energy consumption of systems, thus, not responding to the goals set with the REPowerEU Communication for resilience, energy efficiency and energy dependency reduction. On the Energy Label: JBCE support the rescaling of the energy label from A-G, with a simple label understandable by consumers with a single scale allowing the comparison of all space and combination heating technologies. Such an approach will also support the objectives established by REPowerEU to identify and deploy the most efficient technologies. For further details, please refer to latest JBCE-JRAIA JBCE and JRAIA contributions to Consultation Forum on THE REVISION OF THE SPACE AND WATER HEATERS REGULATIONS. 1. Commission Regulations (EU) 811/2013 and (EU) 813/2013 2.Commission (Delegated) Regulations (EU) 812/2013 and (EU) 814/2013

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: CONEBI represents the European Bicycle, Pedal Assist E-Bike, Parts & Accessories Industries via its national industry members. Via the CONEBIs national industry members, more than 600 small, medium and large companies are represented in CONEBI. CONEBI welcomes the evaluation of the rules for Waste from electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE) and would like to provide its feedback on the overall functioning and scope of the legislation as it directly impacts the Bicycle Industry. The Bicycle Industry has made efforts in the last years to be compliant with the WEEE and believes that this is a suitable system for e-bikes in general as it implies take back at the municipal waste centres and leads to mandatory take back in some Member States legislation and therefore eases disposal for consumers. Should the Commission consider a revision of the scope, we believe that it could be extended to also cover type approved e-bikes. This would ensure that the consumers have similar rights for similar vehicles when handing them over to be recycled and that the bicycle industry, which is traditionally dominated by SMEs can easily apply the same set of rules and is protected from over-proportional burdens. We have also stated this position when providing our feedback to the revision of the ELV Directive in October 2021, where we asked the Commission to keep the end-of-life requirements of type-approved e-bikes as close as similar to those of bicycle and EPAC. In addition, we would like to point out that while the legislative frameworks for EPACs and Speed-EPACs differ due to their typical divergent use and corresponding technical layout (e.g. higher assisted speed and corresponding technical components for Speed-EPACs), when it comes to end-of-life handling, they both could be treated the same. Furthermore, it should be ensured that there are many and conveniently located collection points, making it as easy as possible for consumers to bring back their WEEE. Ideally, it should be possible to return all kinds of e-bikes as well as their components at the same place, as the EPACs and Speed-EPACs are often found in the same distribution channels and are used similarly by the end-users. In general, the importance of awareness campaigns and the role of education within the respective Member States should be stressed. Finally, it is necessary to ensure that all requirements are proportional and are not subject to frequent changes leading to increased burdens for companies. In some of the Member States, the existing collection systems are overtly complicated for producers, especially for the many small SMEs within the Bicycle Industry. The WEEE revision could therefore be an opportunity to streamline and simplify the rules throughout the EU; using the many already existing best practices within the national collection schemes for future harmonisation. CONEBI and the Bicycle Industry are looking forward to further discussions about the revision of the WEEE rules and stand ready to provide their expertise.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The positive benefits of international experience are well established and Eramsus + has demonstrated its added value over the years. While we welcome the opening of the programme to vocational and training institutions, we must deplore the following difficulties with regarding traineeships to work placements:
- The programme does not allow all jobseekers to benefit from a mobility experience as it was the case with the Leonardo program... A large number of jobseekers in the process of jobsearch are not registered to a vocational training organisation and are excluded from the program. Extending the program to work integration organisations would allow greater inclusion of vulnerable groups
- A lack of coordination and harmonisation of legislation on traineeships does not allow certain groups to have an international experience that guarantees their rights in terms of social security and labour law. A standard Erasmus + agreement should be recognised in all countries in order to secure experiences in companies.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
COGEN Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the review of the energy labelling of space and combination heaters. 
While fully supportive of an ambitious approach to energy efficiency of space heaters, COGEN Europe considers that the proposals made for micro-cogeneration systems are inconsistent with the “energy efficiency first” principle and the move towards smarter buildings that actively contribute towards increasingly resilient and integrated energy systems, as part of the broader EU Green Deal objectives. 
COGEN Europe has the following specific concerns:
1) The proposed micro-CHP energy savings methodology is not adequate to account for the benefits of this innovative solution, hindering its market introduction potential
2) The new micro-CHP methodology diverges from any established methods that are set in the Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) or the Energy Performance of Buildings Directive. When implemented, it will lead to different outcomes than other legislative frameworks, creating legal uncertainty (i.e. the EED and Taxonomy make a micro-CHP eligible for support, while Lot1 prevents it from being supported). 
3) The method provided for micro-CHP is not forward looking. It downplays the benefits of dispatchable electricity produced on site, often produced at the same time as winter power peak demand. Therefore, the method fails to anticipate the greater value of flexible electricity for the future more electrified energy system with higher penetration of intermittent renewable electricity, making mismatches between demand and supply more difficult and costly to manage. 
4) The European Commission's (EC) assessment concerning the combined effect of the rescaling and new micro-CHP method is not realistic or thorough. Preliminary assessment by industry places most micro-CHP systems (including packages) in class D, while the EC assumes that micro-CHP would qualify under classes B and C. The EC's analysis appears to take into account new prototypes that are not yet on the market potentially being classified as B, although such solutions are certainly outliers. 
5) Proposals on H2 and RES gases readiness are welcome, especially given the ongoing Ukraine crisis. It is now urgent and very clear that increasing RES gas and H2 use in buildings will help reduce our dependence on natural gas imports, along increased building efficiency, switching to more efficient heating solutions and electrifying where feasible. To achieve this objective, H2 and RES ready solutions must become more visible to consumers via energy labelling. COGEN Europe is however opposed to the introduction of a primary energy factor for H2 ready heating solutions. This would act as a penalty to more innovative H2-ready solutions, thus confusing consumers. A PEF applied to H2 may also more broadly disincentive its use across all sectors, slowing down Europe's ambition to create a robust H2-based economy.

Please find further background and argumentation in our detailed position attached.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I am Emilio Guzmán Gil, a teacher of therapeutic pedagogy and Erasmus + coordinator for some 8 years at the Huelin IES in Malaga. The best of the new Erasmus + programme, including being more inclusive, greener, democratic and digital, is that it is more flexible and autonomous. For my worst, although I believe that the European Commission has no responsibility, it is that the Erasmus + coordinators, at least in Andalusia, have no incentive or role recognised by the education administration, as is the coordinators of the bilingual or self-protection plan. As a result, we depend on the will of the school managers to reduce some time in our working hours, in my case one non-teaching hour per week. Erasmus + coordinators have a lot of work both at school and at home, both during our school hours, on holidays or in the afternoon.

Another type of problem that I have seen, both in my school and in others, is that some school teachers, even members of the management teams, do not sufficiently support these projects, do not support activities in general or as a host institution and do not take appropriate measures to ensure that mobile students are not adversely affected by the loss of classes while participating in Erasmus + activities.

On the other hand, attempts could be made to reduce the technical problems that sometimes occur on the different platforms. Some items have also been cut down with the increase in prices.

Finally, it would be interesting to have a list of the contact details of the accredited centres and the possibility of having an assessment of their trajectory. Some of them have been involved in some projects and some partner centres have not been up to the circumstances.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: ASSORAEE (https://assoambiente.org/settori/assoraee) is the Association - inside ASSOAMBIENTE (the Italian Association of waste management and recycling companies) - which represents companies treating WEEE and manages between 70-80% of the WEEE generated each year in Italy. According to the latest available data (source: Centro di Coordinamento RAEE the Italian coordination center of WEEE sector), more than 510.000 t of WEEE were managed by the plants operating in Italy in 2021. Of these, 392.347 t were represented by household WEEE and the remaining 118.020 t by professional WEEE. The quantities intercepted is around 34.56% of household WEEE, calculated with respect to the average of WEEE put on the market the three years before. This data marks a worrying delay of more than 30 percentage points compared to the 65% collection target set by the European Directive. Attached the ASSORAEE's position paper on the revision of WEEE directive where are highlighted the main criticalities and the possible solutions.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In terms of opportunity for grassroots organisations, Erasmus+ fundings represent a significant added-value to development and impact. The programme in 2021-27 brought positive changes such as the accessibility of fundings/projects to EU border regions (capacity-building in the field of sport for example) as well as for individuals (future mobility opportunities in the field of sport). 
Unfortunately, the barriers to access the fundings have rather increased with the 2021-27 programme, despite the announced simplification measures (with budget, lump-sums, etc.).

Application process:
The Erasmus+ guide is easy to navigate for experts in EU fundings only. It requires to be fluent in "EU" language (EU terminology and abbreviations) when the information could be simplified and more accessible. The length and amont of informative material might refrain applicants to invest time in hunting the information to build their application. The application platform and the several validation procedures are to many, non-transparent, lack direct communication channels, and clear explanation. It adds complexity into the process.

Implementation process:
When it comes to the tools, the Funding & Tender Portal, as well as the continuous reporting system are non-intuitive and encounter systematic technical problems (pages non available frequently). The fact that all project partners now have to sign documents and be part of the reporting on the platform has the direct opposite effect than desired: the project coordinator has to invest more time in training several individuals in using the digital tools (which encounters the limits developed above) rather than focusing on management, quality, evaluation, etc.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: I am giving feedback being the International Relations Coordinator at my university. I am working in this role since the new programme period (2021-27). There has been many changes in the administrative work as well as the new EU-portals. Esp. the portals, which are not only recommended but also partly obligatory, are not working the way they should. Furthermore, the interaction and connections between these portals is not self-explanatory and confusing in the day-to-day business as an IRO. The initiative of the paperless ERASMUS is a good initiative, but unfortunately, it seems the technical implementation is not optimal. Of course, the ERASMUS idea itself is great and I recommend all our students and staff to gain experience abroad. Just sometimes, I wonder, if the administrative effort is worth it, comparing to the financial support ERASMUS is offering. I am very thankful though to my national contact point, which is always very helpful with a problem-solving approach. For the future I expect the different ERASMUS tools to work better and able to be handled more intuitive.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The 2021-2027 programming provides a long-awaited positive simplification. It promotes access to the programme, in particular by making it possible for consortia to be carried out by means of a single accreditation for the entire duration of the programme. This makes it possible both to optimise the time spent on accreditation and to create collective dynamics in academic areas.
In this respect, the new programme should promote inclusion by allowing new institutions to participate.
The specific provisions for inclusion strengthen overall integration.
The new programming makes an important contribution to the expansion of the number and categories of beneficiaries in our school institutions. 
While accreditation is a simplification, the form is still dense for certain categories of schools and establishments.
Notwithstanding these positive elements, the main handicap of the 2021-2027 programming period lies in its fully disconnected calendar from the school ecosystem, resulting in a substantial number of schools and schools that are unable to organise themselves to respond in a timely manner to the deadlines. 
From an administrativo-financial point of view, we do not see any simplification, other than the drafting of a draft. For the application for funding, the agreement is more complicated to implement as there are no fixed rates anymore, and there are always reports to be made each year.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks
