initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Like most Member States, Romania has not achieved its collection targets; Eurostat data from 2019 ranked last, with 27.5 % collection rate, while the National Environmental Protection Agency (ANPM) shows a collection rate of 37.6 %. The transposition of the WEEE Directive through the adoption of Emergency Ordinance 5/2015 had some loopholes. 1. Firstly, even to date, Romania does not have a collection infrastructure for WEEE at the level of local public authorities. Local authorities have not put in place the necessary infrastructure, and there are only a few such centres in partnership with the TROs. The quantities collected through such centres are below 2 % of the total quantities collected in Romania. 2. The ban on landfilling of WEEE has been transposed only formally, in reality it is not applied. Awareness raising campaigns are not continuous and have so far only been carried out by TROs in partnership with local authorities and are not a coherent and continuous message at central level. 3. Preparation for re-use was also introduced only formally, without implementing rules. Thus, although the principle was transposed by Article 16 of Government Emergency Order No 5/2015, in order to implement this provision on a large scale, a ministerial order laying down the detailed rules for the application of the principle is needed. In Romania, there are no data on preparation for re-use, and we appreciate that the figures are extremely low. 4. Romania ignores the existence of the alternative regarding the setting of the collection target in the WEEE Directive Romania has not carried out any study at national level showing the quantity of WEEE available on the market. The WEEE PRO Coalition warns about the newly published data in the e-tool for WEEE generated, we argue that these real data, not based on rough estimates as published in the present case. 5. There are problems with the treatment of WEEE, the transposition of Article 8 (1) and (5) has been done, but there are still major implementation problems. Order No 417/2021 has been approved but unfortunately it is not compulsory to apply the standards and some operators do not apply them. Romania is still facing a number of problems, which we look at briefly below. 1. Informal collection and inadequate treatment. In Romania, informal collection for the sale of scrap is still a common practice. It is extremely useful to start a study of substantiated estimates at national level on the quantities of WEEE reaching scrap. 2. The existence of a relaxed legislative framework for authorising TROs, without guarantees to ensure creditworthiness on the market and without targets in the first years, leading to chaos in the WEEE market. There are currently 20 TROs on the Romanian market, of which more than half do not have collection targets and can charge low and unrealistic tariffs for WEEE management. In Romania, there is no coordination system at national level. 3. An important aspect is related to the penalty of RON 4/kg/8/kg for failure to meet the WEEE targets. To date, no market survey has been carried out by the authorities to determine the appropriate value of such a contribution, in relation to the value of the services that would have been necessary to achieve the unmet objectives. The current, high contribution fosters excessive speculation in access prices for WEEE. Proposals for the new EU WEEE Directive – Development and adoption of a European WEEE Regulation that would close transposition gaps by Member States; Mandatory application of CENELEC standards by member countries; Adapting targets for re-use/preparing for re-use and not for recycling/recovery; Start of studies at European level on the quantities available on the markets of the Member States – Monitoring the application of the legislation

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
The EU needs to rapidly reduce its dependence on fossil fuels, including Russian gas, to stop financing dictatorial regimes, meet its 2030 climate targets and preserve consumers’ purchasing power.
First, the deep thermal renovation of buildings needs to be accelerated much more vigorously and become an absolute priority for the EU and the Member States. Local biogas production is also a route to be developed.
The revision of the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Regulations for centralised heating is also an essential tool and an urgency. It needs to be finalised very quickly, and given the international context and the ongoing energy crisis, it is now clear that it needs to be much more ambitious than what has been proposed so far.
It should set a clear date before 2030 for a ban on the marketing of new fossil fuel oil and gas boilers. It should also ban inefficient electric boilers as soon as possible and strengthen requirements on heat pumps and hybrid systems to avoid poor quality installations that would lead to uncontrolled electricity consumption peaks. 
It would also be useful to introduce a third party performance certification system in order to ensure upstream verification of the actual quality of the equipment.
Finally, the requirements should apply to all boilers, including very large boilers (above 400 kW).
On the other hand, we have serious doubts about the relevance of a hydrogen sector for heating buildings. Green hydrogen, which will be available in limited quantities, should be used primarily for uses where there are no alternatives (e.g. industry).

CLER — Energy Transition Network 
https://cler.org/

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Test & Measurement Coalition was created in 2005 and represents an ad-hoc group of companies active in producing test & measurement industrial type products (Category 9). The Coalition members are leading companies in the sector, we estimate the coalition membership represents over 60% of the global production of industrial test and measurement products and other industrial equipment including chemical analysers. We have been actively participating in recent consultations on chemical and waste-related legislation, and have been closely involved in exchanges with key stakeholders. T&M have actively participated in the different studies and consultations on the harmonisation of the format for registration and reporting and on the frequency of reporting under the WEEE Directive. We are pleased to provide further information on our experience with WEEE compliance as well as recommendations on further improvements of the WEEE Directive. Please find our full recommendations in the attached paper. In summary, the T&M coalition recommends that the WEEE Directives is evaluated in light of the Green Deals objectives and the recent policy and regulatory developments. This requires the Commission to reflect on realistic and coordinated approach with various initiatives (Circular Economy Action Plan, Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability, revision of REACH and RoHS, SCIP, ESPR, Waste Framework Directive etc.), to avoid duplication, inconsistency and achieve a coherent result across these different measures. Industrial test & measurements instruments are extremely complex and intentionally designed for long useful lifespans with high reliability. Category 9 industrial products contribution to the stream of waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE) represents only 0.12% of total EEE. Our experience with WEEE Directive compliance shows that the current system of registration and reporting of WEEE is not sufficiently harmonised and does not address adequately the case of high mix, low volume producers of EEE with long product lifetime. This results in substantial administrative activities and costs both for industry, national registers, and Member States, who are obliged to register and report frequently on insignificant quantities of EEE which is not expected to become waste many decades after being placed on the market. Moreover, the current situation does not create any incentives for this industry segment nor bring any benefits to environment. T&M argues to improve effectiveness and efficiency via strategic rethinking of WEEE in the context of Circular Economy objectives. We strongly recommend transforming the WEEE Directive into Regulation to improve the coherence and consistency of the rules across the EU. We encourage the Commission to consider introducing a central EU register and harmonised content, format and frequency of reporting. We urge the Commission to address adequately the case of low volume producers by introducing a de minimis clause for small producers who contribute with a nominal fee to support the collection and recycling infrastructure management of the EU Member States. In preparation of the WEEE evaluation, we recommend that the Commission evaluates the effectiveness of the SCIP database and especially whether the information generated under SCIP was used and useful to waste operators to improve recycling and materials recovery.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
Generations Futures welcomed the opportunity to participate in the consultation on the roadmap for the revision of the Toy Safety Directive in order to better protect children from the risks associated with toys and in particular the chemicals they may contain.

Generations Futures recalls the urgent need to take the necessary measures to strengthen the protection of children against the risks of toys containing harmful chemicals. CMR substances, endocrine disruptors, persistent and bioaccumulative substances must be removed from toys intended for children without delay and without possible derogations. Toys worn in the mouth of very young children are a major source of exposure.

The shortcomings of the existing Directive need to be addressed and we expect action to be taken to meet the ambitions set out in the Sustainable Chemicals Strategy, in particular the ban on endocrine disruptors in consumer products and the adoption of recommendations and restrictions for childcare products. Biosurveillance studies, such as the Esteban Public Health Study in France (https://www.santepubliquefrance.fr/etudes-et-enquetes/esteban/les-resultats-de-l-etude-esteban), show that the French population, in particular women and children, is impregnated by a wide range of contaminants posing a threat to human health. These include bisphenols, phthalates, brominated flame retardants, etc. These substances can be found in toys consisting of plastics or foams. In addition, particular attention should be paid to toys made of recycled plastic materials, as a survey carried out by European NGOs shows that toxic substances (brominated flame retardants and brominated dioxins) have been found in children’s toys made from recycled electronic waste (https://www.env-health.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/11/Toxic_Soup_brochure_en_web04-1.pdf).

We support some of the actions included in this roadmap, in particular the general approach to risk assessment based on the hazardous properties of chemicals and extended to endocrine disrupting, persistent and bioaccumulative substances, as well as the introduction of labelling on the chemical composition of toys. On the other hand, the Roadmap foresees that the Commission sets limit values for all chemicals in toys intended for children under 36 months and in toys intended to be worn in the mouth. However, it is not acceptable that the same substances should no longer be subject to thresholds for children beyond that age, who still remain vulnerable. Although a general ban on CMR is announced, the Roadmap still provides for derogations for these substances in toys. We regret this and call for the removal of these substances without possible derogations. Finally, the cocktail effect of exposure to multiple substances must be taken into account in the revision of the Toy Safety Directive.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus + is a key tool for working towards social cohesion and building European citizenship. Beyond the multiple benefits it has for participants in mobility or cooperation projects in the particular education or training sector, Erasmus experiences have a major impact on the participating organisations and on the local communities in which they work. The projects enable European civil society to get in touch, know how to work and live in other countries, what education and training programmes are in the different sectors, what future challenges and projects are, and ultimately contribute to the creation of European communities of citizens working together for years. 
This programme should be an obligation for entities providing education and training to citizens at different stages of education and possible training settings, both formal and non-formal or informal. 
The programme allows organisations to develop internationalisation strategies, thus bridging existing gaps in national education and training systems’ programming in this field; it fosters language learning and intercultural competences that are key to building an integrated European society.
In this regard, the possibilities opened up by Erasmus Accreditation in the new 2021-2027 programme are essential, and it is worth mentioning in particular that municipal authorities are well considered as bodies that can boost the programme at local level and encourage the participation of different groups and local communities. The important role that local public administrations can play by providing access to the opportunities offered by the programme should be further disseminated. 
The open possibility for adult learners with fewer opportunities to participate in mobility projects should be extended to any adult who follows a lifelong learning programme.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ successfully progressed towards its objectives with adequate absorption rates under the circumstances. It contributed to the enhancement of national policies and the creation of synergies, not only between the program and the national policies, but also across all sectors of the programme. It could be stated that the added value of the program was considerable. 

Nevertheless, there is room for improvement and simplification of IT tools, as well as the application and implementation procedures. Higher involvement and interest would be encouraged by providing incentives and reducing administrative burden for interested parties. Finally, the program could become more inclusive by providing incentives for smaller and new beneficiaries, including informal youth groups.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: We fully support the European Electronics Recyclers Association(EERA) position: 1.- Mandatory standards across Europe will have a positive impact in the collection and treatment of WEEE. 2.- Its also important to harmonize the requirements and standards and they should be a binding in all countries. EERA calls for harmonised requirements across Member States in the form of an EU Regulation 3.- We support also that there should be a mandatory handover of WEEE to approved European WEEE collectors and compliant re-use and recycling operators. Kind regards Esteban Marijuán-Requeta

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: WEE in the EU is a directive which means when it is implemented at member state level this results in variations how the directive is implemented (for example the AGEC law, and the REP in France) and means that producers have to register and report in each member state which is a technical and cost burden for industry. Making WEEE an EU regulation with a single portal for producers would simplify the process and reduce the cost burden on manufacturers. The same problem currently exists with other extended producer responsibility directives such as batteries and packaging. The other issue is about a perceived lack of enforcement. There could be producers placing product on the market that are not registered. As mentioned above registration and data collection is a cost borne by the producer that is typically passed onto the customer through the initial purchase price. There is no visibility on each producer's compliance with the registration obligations and indeed, the absence of control can create a competitive disadvantage in relation to those who comply. Finally, the directive is not well suited to products like generator sets that can have a long lifespan (20 or more years), which could translate into a very low collection rate. It would be necessary to be able to analyze the categories more finely so as not to put general rules negatively impacting the products.

feedback_text: I have participated in the 2014-2021 program. The proposals that were funded after rigorous evaluation exercise were innovative and provided opportunities to the youth for skilling and learning across the international and inter-continental boundaries. The significance of ERSMUS + program is even more as it exposes the learners to multi-culture environment and it also promotes collaboration between HEIs for capacity building in different areas of higher education. The 2014-2021 program was successful in providing many learning opportunities to the youth in EU. Its successor is the 2021-2027 program, which has enhanced funding and scope. We expect that it will provide more befits to the youth and enhance their skills finally contributing to the growth of EU economy.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The European Copper Institute welcomes the European Commissions (EC) ambition to evaluate the effectiveness of the EU Directive 2012/12/EC (also known as WEEE Directive) about waste from electrical and electronic equipment. WEEE represents the fastest growing source of waste in the European Union (EU). Given that e-waste will reach significantly higher levels in the next decades due to the electrification efforts, the evaluation of the EU rules comes in a very timely manner. To deliver on the ambition to reduce e-waste generation, and to encourage collection, reuse and recycling of electrical and electronic products, as well as recovery of materials embedded therein, ECI wishes to contribute by pinpointing towards some problematic issues and areas for improvement. For this, there are two main considerations that should be made: 1. Significant losses occur at electrical and electronic equipment streams in the EU, when they become waste. They are either not collected or disappear from the EU statistics, leading to lack of sufficient data and unknown whereabouts. 2. Illegal exports and sub-standard treatment affect WEEE recycling, such as: Exports of WEEE to third countries with recycling facilities operating under low environmental performance standards Exports of WEEE as second hand, to circumvent controls (product export instead of waste, disappearing from EU statistics) Copper is required for the transition to climate neutrality due to its excellent electrical and thermal conductivity. It is important for ECI to highlight the challenges and give recommendations to make rules favourable for the minimization of losses. ECI asks the EC to take measures to address the collection challenges, being the main source of WEEE losses in Europe. These should include: Improved design for repair, re-use and recycling of electrical and electronic equipment in facilities with high environmental performance. Better dismantling and removability of certain components (e.g., batteries or printed circuit boards) could facilitate easier disassembly of products at end-of-life. Improved collection, separation, sorting and subsequent treatment of WEEE in the EU by underpinning investments in increasing recycling capacity, and with the use of state-of-the-art technologies operating upon high environmental standards to keep the value of materials within the loop for longer. Optimized arisings detection underpinned by economic incentives and increased enforcement in cooperation with targeted third countries to track illegal WEEE exports, prevent additional emissions from excessive transport, and exchange intelligence on imports of non-functional electronics, the residual effects of which outweigh any benefits from the import. Redesigning Extended Producer Responsibility schemes to incentivize producers to design their product to make it easier for the materials embedded therein, such as copper, to be reused, dismantled, and recycled at end-of-life. Harmonizing e-waste rules across EU legislation (e.g., Waste Shipment Regulation (WSR), Waste Framework Directive (WFD)) to: - Better control exports of WEEE so that they are collected and treated in a safe and sustainable manner, and to digitize the logistics system and facilitate better quality and wider data collection in line with the ongoing revision of WSR (electronic data sheet, electronic data interchange). - Harmonize waste classifications across Member States for more efficient recovery and recycling of metals fractions arising from WEEE, thereby helping in classifying shipments, reducing waiting times, and differentiating between waste shipments and illegal transports under the waste EU legal acquis. Raising consumer awareness and facilitating their increased access to dedicated and safe WEEE collection points at national level, or via additional informative measures (e.g., labelling clearly showing if a value material is embedded) or other incentives.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
A) Include in a clear way toner cartridges in the WEEE scope

B) Define new categories according to treatment processes' needs and specific critical materials:
1. Thermal exchange appliances
2. Large white appliances
3a. TV and monitors (CRT only) 
3b. Neon lamps and energy saving lamps
4a. Small domestic appliances, IT and telecommunications equipment (without lithium batteries)
4b. Small domestic appliances, IT and telecommunications equipment (with lithium batteries)
5. Photovoltaic panels from domestic installation
6. Toner cartridges

C) Define minimum standards for public and private collection-points to be included in the system paid by producers. Set additional high-level standard for public and private collection-points to enable price awards and contribution for collection paid by producers.

D) Stop "free riders" or producers/importers not contributing to the system with a specific tool: a unique code generated by individual and collective schemes for each transaction. Each code can be decrypted by citizens on the national clearing house website (or on a European level) revealing producer, name of the product, weight, paid fees, scheme receiving the fee. Not matching data can be highlighted by citizens.

E) Calculation of recovery rates. Set common easy one-page forms to be exchanged among recyclers in order to declare and share recycling-recovery-disposal rates.

F) Set dedicated annex, to be built in time, with EoW criteria and practical classification guidelines for material coming from WEEE treatment 

G) Set a dedicated official technical advisory body to give qualified classification opinion in order to reduce court trials just due to mis-matching of classification among recyclers and local authorities

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Acting on behalf of the ELEKTRO-Recovery Association of Employers of Electrical Waste and Packaging, an economic self-government organisation which brings together undertakings active in the collection, treatment and recovery of waste electrical and electronic equipment, packaging and packaging waste and the circular economy in Poland, I would like to present the Union’s position on the evaluation of Directive 2012/19/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 4 July 2012 on waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE): 1. Lack of standards Implementation of the 2012 Directive across Member States is not consistent and has led to an uneven playing field for the environment, a lack of consistency in treatment rules and an increased burden on documentation, reporting and enforcement. Rules between Member States for the same categories of products are different. It is important to harmonise the criteria, requirements and minimum standards for the treatment of WEEE, which should be binding on all EU Member States, e.g. in the form of an EU regulation. In addition, waste from WEEE needs to be better characterised (origin, composition, etc.) in order to simplify and optimise its recycling. In addition, the overall feasibility, transparency and accountability of the entire WEEE management process should be increased. The existence of different national policies for the management of WEEE reduces the effectiveness of, inter alia, recycling and reuse. 2. The high collection rates of WEEE and the problem of obtaining the corresponding amount of waste, Eurostat data from 2019 show that 24 out of 27 Member States have not collected enough WEEE and have not met the EU’s 65 % collection target. In practice, the methodological basis for setting collection targets does not seem realistic. In Article 7 of Directive 2012/19/EU, both collection rates methods are problematic and targets are not achievable for most Member States. In particular, the collection target of 65 % of the average weight of electrical and electronic equipment placed on the market in the last three years, which has been in place since 2019, appears to be very ambitious and difficult to achieve in this context if legal collection methods for waste electrical equipment are consistently and exclusively taken into account. On the other hand, the method for collecting 85 % of waste electrical and electronic equipment generated in a Member State is very strict and has a complex procedure. In the Member States, there are problems in obtaining the appropriate amount of waste, inter alia because commercial networks own a large part of the waste. In addition, the classification contained in the 2012 Directive established a link between the quantity of new EEE placed on the market and the volume of WEEE to be registered in the Member State concerned. Such a link between newly introduced EEE and the recording of old ones presupposes the presumption that, when new equipment is purchased, old vending machines are replaced in households and immediately disposed of as waste. In fact, however, this is not in line with practice, as in many cases appliances have a longer lifespan than the Directive – three years (PV panels, refrigerators, computers, washing machines, dishwashers and other equipment that are used for a long time). Moreover, older appliances often remain in households as alternative devices. With this in mind, there is an urgent need to reform the requirements and targets for WEEE collection at Union level. In the context of the evaluation of Directive 2012/19/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 4 July 2012 on waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE), we would like to ask the European Commission to consider the above issues and is available for cooperation.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The revision of the Directive 2012/19/EU on WEEE is more than necessary taking into account the fast-pace evolution in electric & electronic appliances market, as well as significant changes in WEEE market. The main aspects that should be amended in our view are as follows: 1. Revision of the collection target (65% of the average weight of the equipment put on the market in the 3 previous years) as this proved to be challenging for most member states, even the ones with advanced separate waste collection systems. In Romania, it is almost impossible to reach this target taking into account the poor infrastructure for separate collection of WEEE, the low involvement of the authorities and the lack of awareness of people in rural communities on the importance of separate collection. 2. The entire responsibility is on the producers, being transferred to EPR schemes that are competing in a profound speculative market to meet the targets. The black market (informal collectors) influence the pricing and no significant coercive measures are taken against them. This market status is not favorable for the increase of the quantity of WEEE collected, promoting price competition between EPRs and producers. There are plenty EPRs that recently entered on the market having dumping prices, taking into account that the target for the previous 3 years is small this means unfair competition, de-balancing the market and disadvantages for the organizations having important targets to achieve. Regulation is necessary on this market, otherwise the entire purpose of the regulation is misunderstood. 3. There is also huge pressure on WEEE recycling companies, that have big issues with the energy price increase and low prices obtained on secondary materials from WEEE treatment. If no incentives are enforced for recycled materials (which sometimes are cheaper than virgin material) it will be really difficult for recycling companies to remain competitive on the market. 4. Enforcement of the waste management hierarchy with actual incentives to promote reuse of WEEE instead of recycling. EU funding for repair & reuse centers could also bring significant benefits for the market, for the circular economy principles and for the environment itself. Eco-design regulations enforcement has a significant role in this matter, because they are still at declarative/legislative status in many countries. 5. Alignment of the WEEE Directive with other regulatory requirements subsequent to its release (circular economy package, waste directive, eco-design, etc).

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
We welcome this initiative to revise the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling requirements for space and combination heaters, to reflect technological development and the need to achieve higher energy efficiency.  

In previous occasions, we have consistently called for an accelerated switch towards more efficient and more climate friendly heating appliances, highlighting the need to align product policy to the EU climate neutrality goals by 2050 and the ongoing efforts to improve the energy performance of buildings.  

This debate took a whole new turn considering the latest geopolitical developments. In the recent Joint Communication on European Action for more affordable, secure and sustainable energy, the European Commission highlighted the importance that green energy technologies (including heat pumps) will have to lower the EU dependency on Russian gas. It is important that the right steps are taken in every relevant regulatory tool to accelerate the roll-out of heat pumps. We are convinced that Ecodesign and Energy Labelling measures for heating appliances can be one of the main drivers of this transition. 

Through the revision of these Regulations, the Commission should adopt strict energy efficiency requirements for gas boilers, electric heat pumps and hybrid systems to remove the worst performing products from the market. Considering the EU 2050 neutrality target and the proposed phase out of fossil fuels in residential heating by 2040 under the new Energy Performance of Buildings Directive, through this revision, the Commission should also consider restricting the placing on the market of gas boilers in the future. This should be done in a timeframe that allows consumers to make the necessary thermal insulation improvements, the heat pump industry to scale up production and grid operators to make the necessary investments in electricity grids, starting with the installation of smart meters. 

Heat pumps are an efficient and cost-effective way to heat homes, replacing fossil fuels boilers. Similarly, a new BEUC study shows that electric heat pumps will have a lower total cost of ownership than hydrogen boilers. We therefore strongly oppose the inclusion of hydrogen-readiness requirements in the revised Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Regulations. As hydrogen is unlikely to have any role in residential heating due to its high cost, we are concerned the proposed requirements will mislead consumers into thinking that hydrogen-ready boilers are a better option compared to non-hydrogen ready ones. 

In view of the expected increase in heat pump installations, flexible operation of heat pumps and hybrid systems will be important to reduce the stress on electricity grids and avoid eventual local outages. It is key that this revision also considers the introduction of demand side flexibility requirements for these appliances. 

We support the option to rescale the energy label, as it provides clearer information to consumers. There should be a single energy efficiency scale for all technologies, where the A class is left empty and fossil fuels boilers are placed in the G class. The energy label should be effective in redirecting the necessary investments and state support towards more efficient technologies, like heat pumps and hybrid systems, instead of gas boilers. 

Finally, we agree with the need to adopt material efficiency requirements, including requirements on repair information and spare parts availability. These should be set considering the long-expected lifetime of space heaters (around 15-20 years).

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I have over 15 years of experience in the faculty, in the national Erasmus agency and in the national research institute as a consultant and project advisor.
I have had the advantage of being employed by the faculty and have participated a few times in the Erasmus staff exchange week a few times, which has given me valuable insights into other HE institutions and allowed me to position my knowledge, identify knowledge gaps and management skills, etc. I would recommend the personal mobility/exchange experience to anyone working in academia, from accountants to librarians to project office managers and others. As a member of the national Erasmus agency team, I gained insight into the Erasmus programme and came into contact with end users. Many of them have a mobility handicap - they are afraid to fly by plane, go abroad, speak another language, etc. But where there is a will, there is a way. Now I work at a research institute and I see a different kind of handicap - I do not know what Erasmus can offer researchers and support staff through staff exchanges. Many researchers are looking for an opportunity for a short visit to another institute to learn a method, operate a machine, share knowledge and update their network. 
I believe that Erasmus could also be interesting for research institutions and would contribute to the achievement of EU goals, from missions to common values. 

I am at your disposal for further explanations and suggestions for inclusion.
text_raw: I have over 15 years of experience in the faculty, in the national Erasmus agency and in the national research institute as a consultant and project advisor. I have had the advantage of being employed by the faculty and have participated a few times in the Erasmus staff exchange week a few times, which has given me valuable insights into other HE institutions and allowed me to position my knowledge, identify knowledge gaps and management skills, etc. I would recommend the personal mobility/exchange experience to anyone working in academia, from accountants to librarians to project office managers and others. As a member of the national Erasmus agency team, I gained insight into the Erasmus programme and came into contact with end users. Many of them have a mobility handicap - they are afraid to fly by plane, go abroad, speak another language, etc. But where there is a will, there is a way. Now I work at a research institute and I see a different kind of handicap - I do not know what Erasmus can offer researchers and support staff through staff exchanges. Many researchers are looking for an opportunity for a short visit to another institute to learn a method, operate a machine, share knowledge and update their network. I believe that Erasmus could also be interesting for research institutions and would contribute to the achievement of EU goals, from missions to common values. I am at your disposal for further explanations and suggestions for inclusion.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: If the scenario for EU Member States is already complex to manage intramuros, the problem becomes faster when WEEE ends up in third countries with lax or non-existent environmental and health regulation, poor governance, very low educational level, high poverty rates, etc. If it is known that much of this litter is being exported illegally or legally, exploiting legal loopholes, with the devastating results known and that monitoring is proven; control and inspection are the most effective tools in the application of the mandatory standard, or the new Directive becomes more drastic and rigorous with quasi-criminal bad practices or completely forbid the export of this waste and treated in accordance with the Green Deal on its territory. If it authorises the export of WEEE, apart from rigorous monitoring, control and inspection, it should actively support third countries receiving WEEE to avoid transferring the problem outside the EU and creating or increasing it outside the EU, firstly because it contravenes the principles of the Green Deal and second because it can create a boomerang effect that would not only not solve the problem, but also add a number of costs of all kinds that nobody would like to bear. The Green Deal needs to be inclusive not only for those inside, but also for those from outside, and the same applies to the principle that no one is left behind.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Final evaluation 2014-2020
Has contained a complex set of rules with a wide range of administrative elements from a call
It is necessary to use the corresponding documents before funds from the next call can be taken, i.e. it is not a reading wound based and thus makes it difficult to explain this to students.
It is difficult to have good information gathered on a page where students have access to it at all times because it is to be adjusted between the Calls.
Thus, it would have been desirable that all documents linked to the programme should have been the same throughout the whole period, but that, as an HEI, we could have noticed on each semester which call the money has been taken.
It has been the case that some partner universities do not sign the Learning Agreement before the students are in place and this has put our students in a quiet sometimes. It is of course desirable that it should be clear before the replacement, but a certain degree of flexibility should be completely uncertain.
The part of “after the mobility” that has been found in the Learning Agreement is, in our view, an unnecessary part as students still receive this information from their host university. Moreover, students can now upload their grades themselves from various student portals. The need to use clear funds on a coal before starting in the next has made the management of the programme complicated. You always have several calls with whom you jonglate.
Moreover, the fact that exchange rates vary within a call does not facilitate the administration and should be one and the same rate for each call.
What has been strange in this programme is that the interpretation of the acquis seems to have been so incredibly different from one country to another. It seems to me that in Sweden we have actually worked hard to obtain recognition for a degree with a man in other countries, as it has been felt, in fact seems to ignore it and it is not always that students receive credit for their courses. This means that we get some students who are not at all motivated to study when they are on mobility, but perhaps more enjoyment of life.
Mid-term evaluation 2021-2027
A longer project period is positive. This means that we will hopefully be able to make even better use of the funds in each call. However, many new exciting elements of the new programme sometimes find it difficult how things are to be done in practice. 
This is a very good idea, but the problem is that legislation is so different in different countries that it is somewhat difficult to work with it.
For example, in Sweden, we are not allowed to collect information on parents’ income, for example, so this part is difficult for us to use.
Similarly, blended mobility is a new element that presents some challenges.
Taking the example of a BIP, it may be that in a country you have eligible courses within a professional programme, which is a system that is not so common in Sweden and this makes obtaining a BIP very complicated. For the foreign university with eligible courses, the student simply changes the course, here we need to find a part/a course corresponding to the apron.
it will be somewhat counterproductive when creating new opportunities to enable more people to benefit from Erasmus, but some elements are still to be met. 
We would like to see the removal of the requirement that mobility studies should be credited to a degree and the mere fact that the credits should be credited back to university would allow more students to take part in the various parts of the programme. It is then up to the students to decide whether you want to have your degree or not. 
Welcomes the possibility of additional funding for sustainable travel;

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
Slovak Competent Authority for the Detergents Regulation would like to thank the Commission for providing the roadmap - Detergents - streamlining and updating the EU rules and for the possibility to comment on it. 

In our opinion detergents labels are overloaded with many information arising from different pieces of legislation, sometimes with duplicities in information. These cause difficulties in reading of labels, orientation among many languages and in understanding of information aimed at consumers that lead to decreased effectiveness of the communication. One of the aims of the detergent legislation is to increase the awareness of product users about the safe use through labelling.

This issue is possibly connected also with the simplification of labelling requirements for detergents in connection with related CLP legislation.  We are of the opinion that the minimum letter size in labelling should be specified for printed labels - due to the insufficient font size, most labels are very poorly legible. 

SK CA would like to draw attention to the need to solve the issue of refill sales of detergents, since there has been lately a boom of such type of sales in Slovakia. As it is known, refill detergents are significantly more ecological and economical, comparing to commonly available detergents, therefore we believe this issue needs to be considered as priority.

Furthermore, it is also important to align the detergent and other related legislations concerning labelling of the chemical composition of consumer detergents.

We are in favour of using the new digital labelling of detergents, however we believe it is necessary to find a right balance between digital labelling and the printed form of label.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: In order to globally harmonize the WEEE Directive, ultimately it would be useful to standardize one Directive for all member states to look to with detailed implementation instructions, for producers and manufacturers located in the EU and distributing to the EU alike. To satisfy WEEE requirements there is usually a reporting component to each nation's government; for distributors who sell to multiple member states this proves a complicated process; a harmonized agency to report to which then passes along information would be useful, or at least a quick reference guide on where to report for each member state. As noted in the call for evidence evaluation, there needs to be synchronicity in calculating/meeting WEEE collection targets, but beyond this there should be harmonization in language used in member state legislation for collection targets, target goals, etc.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
The protection of children and the realization of the rights of the child and the right to live in a clean and healthy environmental should be one of the most important goals for the EU, as it has been laid down in the EU Strategy on the Rights of the Child . All products used by children should be subject to the precautionary principle. Toys are not justified of having any essential use, that would allow for the use of harmful chemicals or other threats to children`s safety or health. Therefore, we urgently request the EU Commission to include the following points in the revision of the TSD.

On chemicals:
• All uses of Endocrine Disrupting Chemicals (EDCs) in toys should be strictly prohibited, without any exemptions.  For EDCs no safe threshold limits can be set, as by default they should be considered as non-threshold chemicals.  Additionally, for toys there is no justification of any essential use that would allow the presence of EDCs.
• All CMRs should be banned in toys completely, without any derogations.
• All PFAS should be banned in toys completely, without any derogations.
• Limits for nitrosamines and heavy metals such as lead, cadmium are too high, these substances should be banned in all toys.
• Some allergenic fragrances are banned in toys.  However, to apply to precautionary principle all fragrances and sensitizers should be banned in toys. There is no justification for their essential use in toys. 
• PVC contains many toxic additives therefore it should be banned for the use in toys.
• To fully apply the precautionary principle, also manufactured nanomaterials should be prohibited in toys.
• Chemical limit values should only be set for chemicals if there is no general ban (a total ban should include EDCs, CMRs, fragrances, sensitizers, heavy metals, PBT, vPvB, SVHCs, PFAS and PVC). The limit values should then apply for all children and not only for those under 36 months. Simplification of limit values must not lead to lower protection levels, instead they should lead to better protection of all children.
• Where colorants and preservatives are needed, a positive list should be established, which should not contain any substances which are sensitizing or bioaccumulating. 
• Combined exposures leading to mixture effects must be considered. Safe levels of chemicals should not be set by one chemical alone.

On transparency and traceability:
• Full disclosure of ingredients along the supply chain should be introduced, to ensure the right to know for citizens. This will improve the traceability and accountability along the life cycle.
• A product passport should mandatorily contain all chemical ingredients and necessary sustainability information (e.g. correct disposal, origin)
• Data security and protection of personal data and privacy of children must be guaranteed through the revision of the TSD.

On market surveillance and enforcement:
• Market surveillance is too weak and has to be improved on all levels. The RAPEX notifications show that there are still too many toys on the market that do not comply with EU regulations.
• Mandatory third-party testing should be included, taking into account negative and positive experiences of the USA


Submission including footnotes attached and here: https://hej-support.org/toys-should-be-fun-and-not-toxic-revision-of-the-eu-toy-safety-directive/

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
When the EU felt 5 years ago that the new ERASMUS+ programme was intended to make it easier for students and administration, we were all hopeful and judged, including me. Unfortunately, the situation was different. Erasmus+ was already administratively challenging from 2014 onwards, both in terms of learning agreements and contracts. Short-term projects (Intensive Project) were no longer foreseen here, the OLS was introduced and often perceived as meaningless, except for refugees who had access to the programme in 2016. 

2021-27 --> estimates release = never! or perhaps in 2028 with the next programme? 

Neither the implementation of the programme, the allocation of the budget, nor the digitalisation so high has worked or is still working. The idea is indeed good to move away from paper versions, to digitise the project, but the EU has not taken into account that, in addition to its Dashboard system, other “place brain” programmes are on the market and do not allow all to communicate with each other, especially with the EWP. COVID has contributed to making this delay even longer. In particular, the administrators of the institutions, students and national agencies, who are trying to scale up, have been suffering. 

What was the idea of ERASMUS 35 years ago? Young people in the world to send them almost academically to hiking. As accessible as possible, as unbureaucratic as possible. That has in any case been successful. There is no doubt that ERASMUS is the biggest peace project, the largest partner agency (Tinder can hide!) with more than 1 000 000 ERASMUS Babies... and have experienced and learned thousands of young people, teachers and administrative staff. This has brought us closer. 

In my view, the management of the project and the execution of everything in the new programme SHALL SHALL be optimised, even mixed. For example, I question the usefulness of OLS. (Ratio of students who went to Finland: I made the assessment, simply clicked somehow and get A2 on the test). In my view, promoting language courses on the ground, as it has already been possible, would be more efficient. Not to mention the inlerable effort of an Inter-Institutional Agreement — the paper version or the extension e-mail is long-lived! — The deadline requested by the Commission to digitise all agreements by the end of the year is, in my view, illusory. 

So what has been improved now? — Clearly the humour between the co-ordinators of each partner institution. As many times as today we have never had to do each other, angry and together. This is also intertwined. Together, we create the hurdles with the desire for real red tape for ALL! — for all of us a leading philosophy of 35 years ago: Sending young people to the world, giving them experience to make the world a little more understandable and secretly calling us ‘Silent Peacemakers’.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Feedback on Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation 2021-2027
The Erasmus+ programme turned 35 this year and is one of the success stories of the European Union. The priorities and activities of the new programming period have moved in the right direction. The budget of the Erasmus+ programme has increased, and the fields of activities have expanded. Several positive initiatives have been launched, such as European University initiative, Centres of Vocational Excellence, Teacher Academies etc. The funding model of projects also became simpler and fairer because the lump sum system was introduced.
However, we would like to bring out some administrative bottlenecks. We see an urgent need to review and increase the individual grant support for the mobility and volunteering and the grouping of countries, which determine the amount of the grant. Estonia is currently in the 3. group, which includes countries with a lower living cost. Placing Estonia in the 3. group has for years been inappropriate. Furthermore, also considering the inflation, which in August 2022 was 25%, the current support grant amount is not sufficient anymore. It doesn’t cover the basic expenses regarding the housing but also food. Foreign students, also other participants like university teachers and staff, just cannot cope with these amounts in Estonia. We suggest analysing the feedback from participants on the adequacy of the amounts that has been asked from the participants. There is need to review the grouping of countries and amounts and bring them up to date for 2023. 
The complicated and slow progress of IT developments also needs to be noted, which significantly hindered the implementation of the programme and required better communication with NA's. Better access to participants’ data is expected for easier planning of activities. We would like to see rapid progress in solving IT problems.

Several positive initiatives have been launched, such as European University initiative, Centres of Vocational Excellence, Teacher Academies etc. The funding model of projects also became simpler and fairer because the lump sum system was introduced. However, we would like to bring out some administrative bottlenecks. We see an urgent need to review and increase the individual grant support for the mobility and volunteering and the grouping of countries, which determine the amount of the grant. Estonia is currently in the 3. group, which includes countries with a lower living cost. Placing Estonia in the 3. group has for years been inappropriate. Furthermore, also considering the inflation, which in August 2022 was 25%, the current support grant amount is not sufficient anymore. It doesn’t cover the basic expenses regarding the housing but also food. Foreign students, also other participants like university teachers and staff, just cannot cope with these amounts in Estonia. We suggest analysing the feedback from participants on the adequacy of the amounts that has been asked from the participants. There is need to review the grouping of countries and amounts and bring them up to date for 2023. The complicated and slow progress of IT developments also needs to be noted, which significantly hindered the implementation of the programme and required better communication with NA's. Better access to participants’ data is expected for easier planning of activities. We would like to see rapid progress in solving IT problems.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
Feedback in relation to the EU initiative "revision of regulation (EC) 648/2004 on Detergents”
We would very much welcome the improvement on technical developments in relation to refill concepts (in store or at home).
But there also might be near future challenges for products which will increase concentrations in an effort to be more sustainable. Higher concentrations might trigger more warnings while reducing the surface area of the packaging. This is partially a CLP issue, but still it is relevant to consider for this legislation as well.

The market would benefit from clear regulation in regard to e-commerce to general public and the required information, including a timeframe during which this information should remain accessible.
Furthermore e-commerce information should be provided in the same language of the website, not obligated to be available for every official language per country (specifically relevant e.g. Dutch product sold in the Dutch part of Belgium), nor in every official language of countries where the website can be visited. 

The EU market would benefit by harmonization/standardization of digital SDS for swift and flawless transfer of information between all parties in the supply chain as well as government of poison centers. (point is also raised for CLP, but it is worth mentioning here) This would help reduce the administrative burden. Just for clarification, the current SDS is already harmonized but is designed to be printed. This proposal suggests the use of a XML like data container file, which can be imported in specification systems and such.

Current legislation is lacking sustainability goals in relation to the goals of the EU green deal.
This could be done by phasing out certain chemicals which have a high environmental impact (even if this impacts happens outside of the EU).
Or set a dosage limit for the for consumer products (e.g. 55 ml).

In regard to hazardous substances we would prefer this would remain in the CLP or REACH as much as possible to avoid conflicting requirements and the applicability of multiple regulations on a single product.

Feedback in relation to the current legislation
- Improve clarity of Article 11 part 5. Clarification that other commercial text is not required in all languages, this is often discussed among suppliers/retailers. Currently there are different opinions between member state authorities in regard to this part which is not .
- Dutch translation Article 11 part 3 refers to annex VIII, instead of Annex VII
- Remove Article 11 part 6 altogether. There is already legislation dangerous food imitation products. Other national regulations should be avoided, as is indicated in article 1 of this legislation.
- Annex VII - Part B; Discrepancy between official translations should be resolved. NL translation relates 4.5kg to white laundry, as the ENG translation refers to Heavy Duty.
It would clarify if 4.5kg load is for regular color and white (dark/black?).
And for 2.5kg load that it is for delicates such as wool, silk.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The International Association for Soaps, Detergents and Maintenance Products, A.I.S.E., represents over 900 companies, supplying household and professional cleaning products and services across Europe. Our industry has a proven track record of initiatives that drive innovation, safe use and improve the sustainability of our sector. 

We agree with the Commission’s assessment (SWD Evaluation of Regulation (EC) No 648/2004) that the aims of the Detergents Regulation are relevant, the rules are consistent and complete, the Regulation has achieved its aims to a large extent. The Detergents Regulation will be central to continue addressing the specific needs for cleaning products, particularly in terms of environmental protection.

A.I.S.E strongly believes that the sector should continue to be regulated via the Detergents Regulation and not be incorporated into other chemical legislation or moved under the New Legislative Framework. In line with the Better Regulation Agenda the Detergents Regulation should be applied coherently and consistently with the overall legal framework. Overlaps and inconsistencies with the REACH, CLP and BPR should be addressed so there will be no discrepancies and duplications in future.

We summarise our key asks and provide more detail in the Annexed document:

1. The scope of the Detergent Regulation should remain for cleaning products including microbial cleaning products. 
2. We support improvement of the coherence of the regulatory framework for detergents by addressing the overlaps and inconsistencies with the REACH, CLP and Biocidal Products Regulations.
3. With regards to labelling we support the Commission study on simplification of the labelling requirements for chemicals and the use of e-labelling
4. The regulations to cover risk assessment and restriction of ingredients in detergents should continue to be: REACH, BPR and the General Product Safety Directive.
5. The phosphorous restrictions should not be extended to other product categories
6. For refill and bulk sale, guidance can provide clarity with regards how to implement already existing regulatory requirements. Digital labelling can also facilitate implementation.
7. The Detergent Regulation remains fit for purpose and we do not see the value of moving the Regulation under the NLF.
8. It is also vital that the future framework reflects the role of voluntary schemes in driving sustainable and innovation in our sector.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Without Erasmus +, my life would undoubtedly have been different. I feel now able to reach a lot more people and stand strong together to drive educational innovation. I have been participating in Erasmus + projects for about 10 years. First as a teacher at Stenden, now from my company Speel je Wijs. 
Erasmus + has not only met many new colleagues and students, but has also built up many international friendships. I have been able to publish books, including internationally, of which 1 in an Erasmus + project. Erasmus + has made my world literally bigger. 
It is of unprecedented value to think out of the box for educational improvements and working in international teams in different countries contributes to this. 
For me, an improvement point for Erasmus + is about travelling. Because of climate change, I am struggling to fly. I would prefer to reach out to colleagues in a central place for meetings and parts of the project, preferably reaching by train. As far as I understand, this can only happen if the central place in a country is 1 of the partners. I hope that there will be changes to these rules. 
Furthermore, I can only recommend Erasmus + and I am delighted. It inspires me and makes me (symbolic) a richer human. I live on part of this planet that we have called people the Netherlands, but Erasmus + has helped me to feel above all of the world.

GISAD welcomes the EU Commission's initiative to support lifelong learning in Europe and beyond. Refer-ence is made to this initiative only in the context of digitalisation. initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
This feedback relates to KA131 (formerly 103) only. At present, I look back on the 2014-20 programme as the halcyon days when everything worked, so my feedback on that programme is coloured by my experiences of the new 2021-27 programme. After a shaky start, Mobility Tool became easy to use and provided huge support to project management.
While 2021-27 got under way at the time of the Covid-19 pandemic, there were understandable delays and problems. However, the last two years have been very difficult as these problems persisted, and made daily management of the Erasmus+ programme harder than before.
1. Beneficiary Module - does not offer the functionality of Mobility Tool, in particular the option to import mobility details, which is crucial for HEIs with large numbers. Data entry of individual mobilities is a huge task that we did not have to do with MT. We are starting a second year of the BM not providing working fully, and any queries sent to the help desk are not answered.
2. OLA - this worked quite well two years ago, but has gone backwards in terms of student and staff experience. Messages about HEIs not being part of the platform, etc, do not make sense, and makes giving information and instructions to students and staff very difficult as we often have to revise previous messaging. Again, the helpdesk is unresponsive. Can we at least return to the level of operation that was there in 2020/21?
3. IIA - when this new process was being promoted, there was little - if any - mention of the Erasmus+ Dashboard being suitable only for HEIs with a small number of partnerships. By the time this was clarified, it was too late. What are HEIs to do if they do not use and do not want to use third-party providers? These services are expensive, and in our case, we have developed in-house systems that suit our mobility programmes. The process of agreeing IIA renewals for 2022/23 was time-consuming, especially when we knew we had to go through the online process eventually.
4. The delays and problems are making International Office staff look unprofessional. As mentioned above, we had promoted the new programmes and the new initiatives among our staff and then had to go back to explain that there were delays and some things are not working, without being able to give definite information on when systems would work as planned.
5. The information on managing BIPs is very minimal in the Programme Guide. In particular, it does not give information on how to fund 'independent' students who apply for a place on a BIP and then request funding. This is funding that we have not allocated, as we are not a partner in any BIPs so far, and we cannot foresee the level of interest among students. It is difficult to know where we stand on whether to fund these students or not.
6. Do not underestimate how weary and frustrated Erasmus staff in International Offices are. The 2.5 years since the start of Covid-19 have been stressful and difficult. We seem to have lost many of the tools we depended on and all the new processes are beset with problems. I have worked in this area for over 20 years, so I have a lot of previous experiences to compare the current ones to... I do not wish to be negative in my feedback but it feels like NAs, EUF, EC and other bodies do not fully appreciate the daily issues we are dealing with. We want to support our students and staff and believe in the Erasmus+, but our resilience is being tested. Please at least provide an adequate helpdesk support system - there are many questions!

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The new Erasmus + programme offers more opportunities, fewer opportunities, outside Europe. We feel positive and welcome the possibility for students and colleagues to gain experience abroad with financial support. However, we experience:
1. That we had a call2022 award but are not yet able to access the Benificiary module and have not yet received money (dated 9 spet 2022);
2. Colleagues already accredited had experienced the necessary problems with the new module;
3. Despite the fact that the OLS would become part of the Beneficiairy Module, a separate system has again been chosen. A system that is very cumbersome (create its own ECAS account, the language test can be done through many steps).
5. The Beneficiairy module and OLS have been introduced without instructions.
6. As the tools do not work well, we face a lot of additional administrative work.Resulting in unnecessary financial risks.
All in all, expectations (more accessible, less administration, more digitalisation of notifications and forms) have not been met at all.
7. It would be very pleased that the data to be entered into the BM could be used to draw up documents such as Learning Agreement/Grant Agreement. There is now a lot of double input of data.
8. More variation is possible in the amount that student receives with it. Now, the amount for a 2-week traineeship is fast on the tight side, while a 20-week grant is very high, especially if a student also receives a traineeship allowance and can apply for a grant for students living in the Netherlands.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: Erasmus + is undoubtedly the largest, most widespread and most easily accessible instrument for education and training through international mobility. This is particularly valuable for children, young people and students, especially those in more vulnerable situations (people with disabilities, people from rural areas, etc.) At the same time, however, we can see a progressive specialization of institutions - especially schools - when it comes to using Erasmus+ funds. The more experience in applying and implementing projects, the greater the chance of receiving funding for the next project. It is known that in schools or universities there is a natural turnover of students and pupils, and even teachers, so multiple participation in the Erasmus+ initiative by the same institution is most justified. At the same time, however, in the next perspective it would be worthwhile to put emphasis on reaching those schools and universities that, for various reasons, did not apply for funds from the program, or failed to obtain funding. In this context, training (precisely for those institutions that are joining the initiative for the first time) and a targeted information campaign would be useful.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The German Caritas Association warmly welcomes the EU’s willingness to invest in education and youth, as reflected in the significant increases in the budget of the Erasmus+ programme. The objectives of the programme are particularly relevant in times of uncertainty and represent a high European added value. The programme’s increased focus on inclusion and the fight against climate change since 2021 is also positive.

The consistent application of simplified cost options has significantly reduced the burden on promoters in project planning and management. At the same time, the complex programme guide applicable to all and the burdensome application forms undermine the simplifications in the programme. User-friendly and targeted information processing needs to be implemented as a matter of urgency. The guide and application forms need to be further simplified and redundancy avoided.

In mobility projects, it is challenging to find suitable and motivated partners. Since the partner institutions have a heavy burden in organising and supervising the participants in the project, it is necessary to provide for the possibility of paying the partner institution a compensation from the programme funds. It should also be possible to request a revisit within a project. 

The newly introduced accreditation for mobility projects is intended to alleviate the burden on long-term promoters who carry out many mobility. However, at least so far, the additional hurdle has not contributed to simplification. In order to allow mobility, especially for smaller organisations and new entrants, the implementation of short-term projects should be possible not only on a limited scale, but as an alternative of equal importance to accreditation.

In order to meet the high quality and policy objectives requirements of the programme, an increase in the organisational/management flat rate for mobility projects as well as an income-group cost option for personnel costs is needed. 

The distinction between cooperation partnerships and small-scale partnerships is in itself helpful and useful. However, a close evaluation needs to be carried out as to whether the set overall project flat rates at their various levels are sufficient and appropriate, or whether other possible overall project flat rates or the return to different standard scales of unit costs are more useful in practice. There is currently insufficient experience in this area.

It is particularly welcome that inclusion in Erasmus+ is taken more seriously from funding period to funding period and that inclusion strategies have been developed. All EU funding programmes must be designed in such a way that all people can participate in EU projects in their individual diversity by compensating for disadvantages. We therefore welcome, for example, the introduction of Discover EU Inclusion for young people with fewer opportunities. However, such offers need to be made more flexible in order to reach everyone. In order to participate in mobility projects, disadvantaged people need to be prepared and accompanied more broadly than the programme allows. Synergies with the new ALMA initiative should therefore be explored. 

Together with a number of different organisations at European, national and local level, and in cooperation with experts on their own behalf (e.g. people with disabilities, highly disadvantaged young people, etc.), the German Caritas Association has provided practical recommendations and concrete suggestions for an inclusive implementation of the EU’s Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps education programmes. The resulting position paper is attached to this contribution.

The programme’s increased focus on inclusion and the fight against climate change since 2021 is also positive. The consistent application of simplified cost options has significantly reduced the burden on promoters in project planning and management. At the same time, the complex programme guide applicable to all and the burdensome application forms undermine the simplifications in the programme. User-friendly and targeted information processing needs to be implemented as a matter of urgency. The guide and application forms need to be further simplified and redundancy avoided. In mobility projects, it is challenging to find suitable and motivated partners. Since the partner institutions have a heavy burden in organising and supervising the participants in the project, it is necessary to provide for the possibility of paying the partner institution a compensation from the programme funds. It should also be possible to request a revisit within a project. The newly introduced accreditation for mobility projects is intended to alleviate the burden on long-term promoters who carry out many mobility. However, at least so far, the additional hurdle has not contributed to simplification. In order to allow mobility, especially for smaller organisations and new entrants, the implementation of short-term projects should be possible not only on a limited scale, but as an alternative of equal importance to accreditation. In order to meet the high quality and policy objectives requirements of the programme, an increase in the organisational/management flat rate for mobility projects as well as an income-group cost option for personnel costs is needed. The distinction between cooperation partnerships and small-scale partnerships is in itself helpful and useful. However, a close evaluation needs to be carried out as to whether the set overall project flat rates at their various levels are sufficient and appropriate, or whether other possible overall project flat rates or the return to different standard scales of unit costs are more useful in practice. There is currently insufficient experience in this area. It is particularly welcome that inclusion in Erasmus+ is taken more seriously from funding period to funding period and that inclusion strategies have been developed. All EU funding programmes must be designed in such a way that all people can participate in EU projects in their individual diversity by compensating for disadvantages. We therefore welcome, for example, the introduction of Discover EU Inclusion for young people with fewer opportunities. However, such offers need to be made more flexible in order to reach everyone. In order to participate in mobility projects, disadvantaged people need to be prepared and accompanied more broadly than the programme allows. Synergies with the new ALMA initiative should therefore be explored. Together with a number of different organisations at European, national and local level, and in cooperation with experts on their own behalf (e.g. people with disabilities, highly disadvantaged young people, etc.), the German Caritas Association has provided practical recommendations and concrete suggestions for an inclusive implementation of the EU’s Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps education programmes. The resulting position paper is attached to this contribution.

initiative: External power supplies – ecodesign & information requirements (review)
feedback_text:
Following the consultation forum (CF) meeting held on 31st March 2022, in which the Commission presented a new approach to review the Ecodesign regulation on External Power Supplies (EPS), ECOS would like to provide the Commission with our comments.

Limits of “Back-to-back evaluation” procedure: We support a faster process but urge the Commission to improve on the proposed approach to ensure that there are sufficient opportunities for stakeholder comments to be considered. It is essential that the work is organised in such a way that allows for the necessary depth and breadth of technical analysis that is customary in a review study. 

Neglect of some technical aspects: Based on the CF proposals, there is a risk that important technical improvements to the EPS Ecodesign regulation are neglected:
• New efficiency requirements at different loads: Due to the Common Charger initiative, EPS may be used at a wide range of loading levels. Therefore, it is important to revise Ecodesign energy efficiency rules to include a 10% load efficiency requirement. Current US Department of Energy (DOE)’s work shows that further savings are also possible through tightening the efficiency requirements, or efficiency requirements could be specified at each of the loading points without averaging them. 
• Wireless charging efficiency: The Commission’s current proposal to leave wireless charging under the remit of the Radio Equipment Directive would fail to address wireless charging efficiency. It would be a huge oversight not to address this in the current review.
• Extending scope: Scope should be extended to maximise savings by including high power EPS, active power over Ethernet injectors, and external power supplies used with a wide range of radio equipment.

Need for additional consumer information: This is necessary beyond USB PD availability, so that consumers have clarity on the capabilities of their common cables and EPS units. 

For more detail on each of these points, please consult the short PDF attached.
