initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Dear Sir/Madam,

Thank you for giving us the opportunity to share our feedback.
We understand this evaluation covers also the procedures from the submission of applications to the execution of the projects.
Please, find some thoughts, comments and suggestions, below:

i) The application form may be considerably simplified without compromising the collection of the information required to evaluate the idea/project. Part of the difficulty we face as applicants is attributed to the electronic system in place, which does not seem to be very user-friendly when it comes to completing and submitting web forms.  
Also, parts of the application form that refer to horizontal activities like the management, the dissemination, etc, could be provided as default requirements by the Erasmus+ programme, based on EU-wide good practices, giving the option to applicants to describe how they would deviate from the defaults and why.
Also, filling-in efforts/budgets is very unfriendly, confusing and time-consuming without a real benefit. It does not really make sense to click on several hidden edit buttons to give few numbers and then continue with many many more. The simplification of the budget formulation may solve part of this issue; another idea would be to simply download a template to fill-in and upload. 

ii) The budget for a travel per person is never enough for people travelling from/to Cyprus. The calculations typically take into account the distances, however, there should be a different definition of the "distance" when it comes to travelling. Starting a mission from a small island usually takes at least 2 flights plus other means to arrive to a destination. Not to mention the higher prices of the tickets. We would suggest that the calculations take into account the "connectivity and price indexes" of each origin/destination.

iii) It is evident in the programme that the focus of the projects has moved towards quality and sustainable results. We feel this needs to continue in the same direction, with even more focus on the results and short-term and long-term impact, rather than on the details of the process. But this depends also on the KPIs you need to monitor.

We remain at your disposal if any clarifications are required.

With kind regards,
PHOEBE Research and Innovation Ltd

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
I would also like to give brief feedback on the planned amendments to the Toy Safety Directive.

As regards the lowering of the limit values for nitrosamines:
Planned change: ‘Current limits for (the Carcinogenic) nitrosamines and their precursors, the nitrosatable substances, are too high’ (European Comission, 2022, Page.1)

Comment: The Toy Safety Directive refers to DIN EN 71-12, which separately reduced the limit values for nitrosamines for some toys. Balloons have been listed separately here with the same limit value from the Toy Safety Directive. Balloons should continue to be considered separately. 

For the general estimation of exposure, the Scientific Committee on Consumer Products has published that intake levels from balloons are so low that they can be considered negligible. The Federal Institute for Risk Assessment has quoted this as follows (BFR, pp. 2, 2011): 

“On the basis of the release limit value of 0.05 mg N-nitrosamines/kg material, estimates of exposure via balloon nitrosamine intakes of 50 ng (0.00005 mg) per year and the resulting additional lifetime cancer risk is considered negligible by the Scientific Committee on Consumer Products (SCCP) (SCCP, 2007).”

Exposure for toys that are correctly placed in the mouth, with prolonged chewing and sucking, is of course much higher and not comparable to exposure from balloons (BFR, pp. 5, 2011).

In order to better estimate the maximum allowable intake and the associated lifetime risk of cancer, relevant exposure parameters such as contact time, contact surface area and the mass of the product should be considered. The SCCP published a good comparison, which was also quoted by the BFR (BFR, pp. 5-6, 2011).

‘If, in the case of a suckler, it is assumed that the infant has a whole sucrose weighing approximately 10 g in the mouth for 24 hours a day, then an acceptable daily intake of less than 100 ng of N-nitrosamines per day would be obtained. In the case of balloons, contact time is expected to be much shorter. The worst-case assumption is 1 hour of oral contact, which is also reflected in the measurement time for the release”.

‘Based on an area-based approach (1 kg × 400 dm², saliva contact with 10 cm², 0.25 g, release time for 1 hour), a maximum allowable intake of 10 ng/hour can be obtained when an air balloon is inflated. The SCCP expects one child to shoot up to 5 hours of balloons per year (SCCP, 2007). The annual intake of 50 ng of N-nitrosamines is correspondingly low if the legal limits are met, and the SCCP assesses the resulting additional lifetime cancer risk as negligent (SCCP, 2007).’

The use of balloons is also not typical of ‘mouthing’. I would therefore ask the European Commission to include the above points as well and to look at balloons separately.

Reduced the limit values also for children over 36 months of age:

Comment: The age limit of 36 months should be maintained as it already sets a precedent for previous limits and values. The CPSC also sets a benchmark of 36 months, which should also be maintained. The current age limit is well established in the market with well-understood symbols. The risk assessment is also based on age classification, so age changes do not affect the safety of the products. It is essential that any change in the age classification is scientifically proven and has a scientifically justified background.

Any effort or instrument that helps manufacturers to comply with chemical regulations and limit values is to be welcomed; However, it must benefit producers at all levels.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We carried out an Erasmus+ Youth Exchange. The preparation of the project was easy and the NA and its guide produced a good help. It would be even easier to apply if the application form could be uploaded in full in its own file. Perhaps there is one, but at least I didn’t find it. The same is true for the final reporting of the project, where it would be easier to see questions in advance in order to pre-write the answers with a word-processing tool. It is true that the excel table for final payments was out of time. If I had not been a ‘project professional’, I would have been in a shortage. I corrected the data several times when the title was not unambiguous and the long list also showed errors in keying. 
Thank you for the flexibilities of COVID-19 in terms of schedule and funding. 

In order to ensure that the management of the project is well managed, someone will be required to carry out daytime work. It is still too cumbersome to manage youth exchanges as a mere volunteering activity, although many easier improvements have been made throughout the journey. Is it possible to receive even a small management fee for the coordinator and partners?

Short-term mobility needs to be improved to ensure equality in participation. Specifically:
- working students in bachelor and master programs should also be allowed to participate without requiring them a virtual component
- requiring an Inter-Institutional Agreement to enable students to attend a summer school is a barrier to participation and should be removed

Universities also incur costs from providing support and services to regular incoming students (not just BIP participants), thus the costs should also be taken into consideration when calculating the overall grant amount provided to universities.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: European Recycling Platform (ERP) welcomes the current evaluation of EU rules on waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE) and would like to thank the European Commission for the opportunity to contribute to the ongoing call for evidence. As operator of producer responsibility schemes for WEEE in 12 European countries, we know the challenges related to the implementation of the WEEE Directive and are happy to share our experience and ideas on how to improve the framework for it to better deliver on its goals. In the attached paper, we focus on the following aspects: 1. Calculation of collection rates 2. Awareness raising campaigns 3. Modulation of fees 4. Central supervising entity 5. Enforcement

text_raw: For the period 2021-2027, it would be important to take into account a reorientation/reorganisation of national budgets in the middle of the implementation of the programme in order to increase implementation at the end of the period. National Agencies should therefore create budget lines for mobility covering: • Budget breakdown of mobility by education/training mode (e.g. Apprenticeships in Vocational Education — Schools, Vocational Education — others, Adult Education and Training; Higher education, etc.); • Allocation taking into account the pupils/trainees enrolled in the various modalities in the last 5 years; • Incorporate indicators of annual balance/reorientation according to the various budgetary arrangements and implementation of the previous year.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: The Erasmus+ programme of the European Union has practically transformed what our institution thought was possible with internationalization and deepening engagement with European universities. Prior to 2017, we had about 2 on-and-off partnerships with universities in Europe. An EACEA official visited Bhutan in 2017, and an organized workshop was held with representatives from all the tertiary education institutions to sensitize them on the opportunities available and encourage them to reach out to EU programme country institutions. Since then our College has established over 40 partnerships with European Universities and exchanged over a hundred staff and students through mobility programmes under KA1 as well as multi-year projects under KA2. We have even gotten the opportunity to meet other potential university partners during the course of our established KA1 and KA2 activities within and beyond Europe. The strides that we have made in just under 5 years, even despite the pandemic, were previously unimaginable. We achieved concrete outcomes in internationalization, a wider and deeper understanding of Europe and European values, and many once-in-a-lifetime exchanges of persons between institutions and countries that otherwise certainly would not have happened. People have literally experienced, and shared their experiences, of institutions and countries that some would not have even heard of before. Beyond the actual programme participants, there have similarly been wider impacts as well in our institutions and among the social/professional circles of all the participants. Academically, we have established credit exchanges, research partnerships (even publications), capacity-building projects, and other synergistic effects. The Erasmus+ programme certainly appears in our institution and indeed the whole education sector as the most prominent face of Europe, and it has undoubtedly represented Europe very well.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The assessment I wish to make relates essentially to mobility programmes: Intra-European Erasmus + and International Credit Mobilities.

With regard to intra-European mobility, I will go a bit against what we commonly hear about this programme because I find that it has ultimately not changed much or really since it was set up. It remains the most well-known exchange programme and has served as a basis for developing exchange partnerships with countries outside the European Area of Higher Education and Research. However, it has not really been used to consolidate very strong partnerships between European higher education institutions. This exchange programme is not always taken seriously. The image of festive is always attached to him... Institutional arrangements are very numerous and not always used. This remains a programme based on quantity and not on quality. As there is always a key action on its own, it is difficult to mix it with more structuring projects or just for funding. 
Learning mobility has a great deal of difficulty in being counted in the teaching volumes. As for training mobility, they are not properly integrated into colleagues’ careers. This is surprising when we know that the programme is 35 years old... He would have had to be slightly renovated since then. Now, with the initiative of the European Alliances, universities are seeking to structure their European partnerships. The geopolitical situation also encourages them to do so.
Universities also have difficulties in implementing the mobility of doctoral students because the teaching tools are not suitable, as are the validation of ECTS credits at this level of study. It is also really regrettable that the tools that are supposed to allow dematerialisation do not yet work optimally. The tools proposed for managing the Covid crisis and for Ukrainian students were cumbersome to put into practice. We feel that there is a gap between political discourse and a cumbersome and cumbersome technical-administrative response for teams managing mobility files on a daily basis. 

As regards international credit mobility, it is an excellent tool for developing partnerships and mobility with the European neighbourhood. However, management is cumbersome and cumbersome. The submission files are relatively large for a small number of mobilities obtained. 

Overall, mobility programmes have become more complex in their management and implementation as the tools intended to facilitate management do not always work. Management is extremely technical and time-consuming, resulting in an impression of distortion between the objectives sought when submitting projects and those actually achieved.
text_raw: The assessment I wish to make relates essentially to mobility programmes: Intra-European Erasmus + and International Credit Mobilities. With regard to intra-European mobility, I will go a bit against what we commonly hear about this programme because I find that it has ultimately not changed much or really since it was set up. It remains the most well-known exchange programme and has served as a basis for developing exchange partnerships with countries outside the European Area of Higher Education and Research. However, it has not really been used to consolidate very strong partnerships between European higher education institutions. This exchange programme is not always taken seriously. The image of festive is always attached to him... Institutional arrangements are very numerous and not always used. This remains a programme based on quantity and not on quality. As there is always a key action on its own, it is difficult to mix it with more structuring projects or just for funding. Learning mobility has a great deal of difficulty in being counted in the teaching volumes. As for training mobility, they are not properly integrated into colleagues’ careers. This is surprising when we know that the programme is 35 years old... He would have had to be slightly renovated since then. Now, with the initiative of the European Alliances, universities are seeking to structure their European partnerships. The geopolitical situation also encourages them to do so. Universities also have difficulties in implementing the mobility of doctoral students because the teaching tools are not suitable, as are the validation of ECTS credits at this level of study. It is also really regrettable that the tools that are supposed to allow dematerialisation do not yet work optimally. The tools proposed for managing the Covid crisis and for Ukrainian students were cumbersome to put into practice. We feel that there is a gap between political discourse and a cumbersome and cumbersome technical-administrative response for teams managing mobility files on a daily basis. As regards international credit mobility, it is an excellent tool for developing partnerships and mobility with the European neighbourhood. However, management is cumbersome and cumbersome. The submission files are relatively large for a small number of mobilities obtained. Overall, mobility programmes have become more complex in their management and implementation as the tools intended to facilitate management do not always work. Management is extremely technical and time-consuming, resulting in an impression of distortion between the objectives sought when submitting projects and those actually achieved.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Space and combination heaters are the main heating products for households, and buildings have the largest energy savings and emission reduction potential, compared to other energy consumption sectors. It is also the main market for EU gas consumption. In order to decarbonise this sector and break free from energy dependency, it is essential to regulate such products and phase-out fossil-fuel-based technologies, as recently remarked by JRC too.
Effective ecodesign and energy labelling regulations have a key role as they can potentially contribute to annual CO2 savings of 110Mt, equal to two-thirds of the total emission reductions needed in the built environment to reach climate neutrality.

However, the outcomes of the last Consultation Forum on these regulations showed a lack of ambition for such a transition: it does not set up a date to phase out inefficient electric and fossil-based appliances. Besides their mere effect of taking out of the market the least performing products, bans have proved efficient to send a clear message to both producers and consumers. 
Specific measures from the EU should aim at removing nearly all oil and coal heaters and drastically decrease natural gas for heating, steering the market towards cleaner and more efficient appliances, such as electric heat pumps and hybrid systems, facilitating the implementation of Fit-for-55 measures and making possible the provisions of REpowerEU.

Moreover, since the beginning of the revision process, a lot has happened in the heating market. Not only the general narrative around electrification of heating has changed, but several technologies have hit the market, from high flow temp heat pumps based on natural refrigerants to smaller hybrid units, inter alia, that allows for plug-in substitutions of existing gas boilers in all relevant situations.

We, therefore, urge the European Commission to put forward a revised proposal tightening the parameters of the Ecodesign regulation (EU 2013/813) by setting more stringent energy efficiency requirements. In particular, we recommend the following:
- The minimum requirement for seasonal heating energy efficiency should be set at 110% for all technologies, a benchmark allowing the end of fossil fuel and inefficient heaters sales, starting from 2025. 
- Alignment of the primary energy factors (PEF) with the current Energy Efficiency Directive.
- Introduction of third party conformity assessment, to increase the overall quality of the appliance.
- Inclusion of boilers, with a capacity between 400 kW and 1000 kW, into energy efficiency and extended resource-efficiency requirements.

As for the Energy Labelling Regulation (EU 2013/811), we strongly support the proposed rescaled A-to-G energy labels, leaving the A-class empty and removing the least energy and resource-efficient heating appliances from the market. But time is key: the technical lifetime of boilers can reach up to 25 years, leading to locking in EU households with fossil-fuel technologies. We, therefore, insist on the swift adoption of these proposals by 2022 and entry into force by 2023 at the latest.

We want to express our concerns on the use of hydrogen in the heating sector: because of the limited availability of renewable hydrogen, its end-use should be only for hard-to-abate sectors, such as industry, aviation and shipping. Alternatives such as direct electrification are both more efficient and economical, and less impactful than hydrogen-ready boilers.

The abovementioned actions will drastically reduce the dependency on fossil fuels while fostering EU energy security and reducing energy poverty, especially in the light of soaring prices and the Russian/Ukrainian crisis.

With the climate crisis ranking high on the EU agenda, we imperatively need to diminish energy consumption by the effective and ambitious implementation of ecodesign and energy labelling.

The feedback is a joint action from ECOS, EEB (European Environmental Bureau) and Coolproducts.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: [Part 3/3] Many work is being carried out in CENELEC bodies to develop standards in line with environmental and depollution challenges (e.g.: In 50625.1 (collection, logistics and treatment requirements for waste electrical and electronic equipment), only a handful of Member States such as France, Ireland, Belgium and the Netherlands chose to include and lay down these requirements in their legislation. Lastly, the Directive should make it possible to tighten up the provisions to combat the risk of fire or pollution at sites, such as the removability/removal of batteries, in particular lithium ion batteries (fire-fighting and recycling performance). Like the ban on single-use plastics, a common policy should be adopted to ban single-use EEE (other than medical EEE), particularly when it contains lithium batteries. 3. Implement the application of extended producer responsibility requirements (in particular for online sales): The compliance of online sellers faces numerous difficulties, in particular because sellers from third countries are required to contract with a European authorised representative, an obligation imposed on European producers and placing them on the European market when they market their products in an EU country. The volume of the sellers to be brought into conformity is very important, their identification is difficult and there is little monitoring of compliance with EC standards. Third party sellers are very often unaware of the weight of the equipment they sell, a large volume of misreporting could destabilise the calculation of collection targets. 4. Tackling illegal activities and sub-standard practices throughout the WEEE management process: Strengthening the traceability and control of used WEEE exports requires an adaptation of the customs classifications (i.e. the distinction between used and new EEE). Reporting on the quantities of used WEEE exported by stakeholders, if made mandatory, could make it possible to refine the quantities of used WEEE still available in each of the Member States. It would also be necessary to harmonise the inclusion of WEEE exports used for re-use in the performance of the Member States. (Summary of studies on the household and professional WEEE deposit extracted from the OCAD3E/Sofies deposit study, version of 26/11/2021.) The standards still do not take into account the new categories modified by the Directive. France’s ban on paying for ferrous and non-ferrous metals in cash is virtuous but not uniform, particularly in border countries. It seems necessary to prohibit cash payments within the EU in order to better combat environmental crime and illicit trafficking. Finally, cooperation between the customs services of the Member States needs to be strengthened. The fight against crime is therefore a major challenge in the management of electronic waste, all the more so since the penalties imposed by the failure to achieve rates are paralysing for eco-bodies, whereas they cannot combat illegal networks without the assistance of the competent authorities. Ecosystem is at the disposal of the European Commission for any question, information or exchange.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Evaluation: Digital tools: unfortunately, the beneficary module is still partially functioning. In general, IT tools (including dashboards and IIA) are so far very unreliable. The use of several platforms should also be criticised. The illegitimate OLS should be abolished as it is scarcely used and only involves additional work. In particular, the fact that this is again documented and recorded separately. 
The idea of making Erasmus more inclusive and thus reaching out to more students is very famous, but the administrative burden is sometimes out of proportion. Rather than becoming less, the work of universities has increased. The new forms and opportunities have also made the Erasmus Program even more complex. The fact that everything is now more or less possible has made it much more complicated. 
The Green Travel Top Up should be increased to create a real incentive to switch to sustainable modes of transport. I find the idea super, but this should be increased financially. 


Evaluation 2014-2020: the Learning Agreement was extended from 2 pages to 5 pages at the time. In other words, this made the work more cumbersome rather than simpler.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
My job provides new computers every four years for teachers. Here in Munich there are tax laws that prohibit my job from donating these devices. Since they are Apple Co. products any recycling that I am aware of requires external shipment (possibly back to California). Are old computers being sent to countries in Africa? Is WEEE watching videos of old technology from Europe being burned to extract metals that were once minded on that same continent? How can I support the EU efforts to think about the future of technology waste?

Kindly, 
Kathryn

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The fact that Erasmus+ is subsidised with real lump sums and therefore does not require the submission of supporting documents and lists of supporting documents is very positive. National funding bodies should take an example of this.

The complexity of the information, regulation and procedure as a whole could be reduced by hiding out any information, rules and processes that are not relevant to the applicant immediately after the selection of the eligible CAs. This should be a consistent principle from the initial information on E+, the user guide and the application form, the funding agreement to the proof of use. This could significantly improve user-friendliness. 

In principle, the share of flat-rate overheads or otrganisation costs should be increased, as many promoters are not sufficient to maintain the basic infrastructure needed for the projects.

National funding bodies should take an example of this. This should be a consistent principle from the initial information on E+, the user guide and the application form, the funding agreement to the proof of use. This could significantly improve user-friendliness. In principle, the share of flat-rate overheads or otrganisation costs should be increased, as many promoters are not sufficient to maintain the basic infrastructure needed for the projects.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
During the pandemic all our mobility activities where halted. Many students who had been looking forward to and preparing for their educational experience abroad were of course very disappointed. But instead of canceling all our activities, we invented someting new together with our partners abroad - virtual mobility - providing our students both work life experience and the new set of skills required in a digitalized future. Our NA approved us to use the organisational support funding in our Erasmus KA1 project for this mobility activity. Due to this, we could continue all through the pandemic to provide our students workbased learning abroad. And for the first time, we could also see a growing interest from students with special needs joining this international learning opportunity. Students who previously never had considered mobility since it required geographical travel. 

However, we were very disappointed when we understood that this great mobility opportunity was no longer a valid activity within the new Erasmus programme 2021-2027. Virtual mobility is the future when we no longer can do as much physical traveling. And many sectors in the society today are already working, doing business and collaborating digitally across boarders. Many big industries are becoming more reluctant to letting their staff travel for short meetings due to climate issues and to save time and money.

In conclusion, I think it'd be wise of the Commission to open up for virtual mobility activities as soon as possible to reach the priorities of the Erasmus programme regarding inclusion, digital and green transformation.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Carrying out an Erasmus+ exchange at the University of Bologna campus Forlì was a great experience that contributed to both my personal and professional growth. From the very beginning, I was thrilled about this opportunity, and, all in all, it didn’t ring hollow: all expectations I had were fully met. Now, I would like to briefly disentangle this experience abroad, aiming at properly conveying the big picture of the main events and processes lying underneath it.

As for the application process, I must say everything was quite smooth and ran like clockwork. Instructions were set forth in a clear and timely manner by the University of Siegen (my home university in Europe) through different means such as webinars, one-to-one consultation sessions during office hours, emails, and the like. In this respect, the International Students’ Affairs office (ISA) was pivotal, their staff members were always willing to provide the required assistance and guidance. By the same token, I would like to bring to the fore the host university’s assistance, which was also vital to complying with some of the paperwork requirements in the due time.

As to my arrival at the host university, everything went well. Instructions provided by the University of Bologna with regards to the step-by-step to be followed once arrived in Forlì were crystal-clear. Their support ran the gamut from migratory-related red tape to administrative affairs. Nonetheless, and by contrast, I also need to acknowledge that some things could have gone better. As a matter of fact, I had to change a couple of courses in my Learning Agreement because the classes were already over when I arrived (not even 10 days had passed from the beginning of the semester when I arrived in Italy). It seems those were express/special courses but were somehow offered as conventional ones. This decanted into a somewhat stressful situation in which I had to cope with more paperwork in order to replace the courses, contact some professors to clarify some of the information, etc. I won’t say this was extremely outrageous, but improving these little details can translate into a better experience for students, which are already passing through some stressful situations in the making. Probably pre-mobility coordination between universities might be enhanced. Or the online available information may be more clear.

With regards to the on-site experience itself (this is, academic and cultural aspects) it was incredibly fruitful. I did bolster my previous knowledge and also developed a meaningful network of new colleagues and friends. Italy, and in particular Forlí, is a great destination for students looking forward to acquiring new experiences within the context of a unique academic system. The University of Bologna, specifically, also offers a unique and amazing experience for international students: a forward-looking environment, top-tier staff, coherent learning materials, and more. Hence, I would, without any doubt, invite anyone who has the chance, to take part in this kind of experience.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I would like to highlight two aspects of the current programme:

IT infrastructure: It is highly regrettable that up to now the IT infrastructure of the new program is very unsatisfying. The Beneficiary Module, which was launched with a long delay, still does not work properly, which makes the work of end-users extremely difficult. This leads to much frustration and is unworthy of an otherwise excellent program with numerous possibilities.

So e.g. looking for Erasmus Mundus Joint Masters, there are only 3 projects listed until now, which obviously does not reflect the number of total projects funded. So please send out selection lists again or make a meaningful overview in the portal.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

 Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tan

initiative: External power supplies – ecodesign & information requirements (review)
feedback_text: While, it seems to be a good idea at first glance for conventional EPS such as computers, mobile phones, wearable, there are many other usage which requires different voltage or combination of Voltage/current which are not provided by USB and Power Delivery protocol. For example, many products needs to have 48V DC external power supplies with high current. This will not be possible anymore with USB as it's limited to 100W/240W. Also many EPS which are located for example in electrical installation dashboard and are powering field buses in home or building automation systems will not be able to comply with USB EPS. At the output of such specific EPS, the data are transmitted on the same pair as the power, this is not possible with a USB connector. These field busses are using other standardised connectors and will not be able to adapt. Moreover, existing EPS were just adapted to the former evolution (2019). industry needs stability, therefore, we recommend option a) No EU change.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Platform for Rehabilitation, EPR, is a network of service providers working with people with disabilities committed to high quality service delivery. Our mission is to build the capacity of our members to provide sustainable, high quality services through mutual learning and training. Our motto is Learning and Innovating together, and a key tool we use to achieve our aims is EU funded projects.

Our feedback from is based on our experience to date running projects under the current funding period. We believe that the new Erasmus Plus 2021-27 has advantages and disadvantages compared to the previous seven years:

Lump sums: the new approach that binds budgets to lump sums on the one hand allows the applicant to request what is really deemed necessary for each activity, thus increasing flexibility within the 3 assigned lump sums.
On the other hand, the calculation of budgets relating to the different activities or to be assigned to individual partners, in the absence of standard figures to be applied, is more difficult.
The disappearance of the 4 types of staff categories and of groups of countries to which different standard budgets were assigned for a single working day tends to penalize some countries. Even if under the previous funding period the set budgets per staff day varied between countries, the actual staff cost varies even more, and combined with the limit of only 3 very different lump sum budgets for the standard projects, this means that it is difficult to assign countries with high wages significant tasks in the project, and thus favours countries where the labour costs are lower. This means mainly favouring organizations based in the South and East of Europe to the detriment of those in the North and West, which risks unbalancing the partnership and of the project, and geographical balances of partnerships in general. *To help mitigate this issue, allowing the inclusion of a variety of wage levels whilst not needing to reduce or greatly increase the scope of the project proposal, we would propose 2 more levels of lump sum: 180,000€ and 325,000€*

New template: compared to the previous version, the new template is more rational and clearer. The return to Work Packages has improved the clarity of the template. The possibility of dedicating a WP and a specific budget to communication and dissemination (and to distinguish it from PMI) was a significant improvement.

Admin requirements before submission: The fact that the Mandates are no longer required before the submission of the project allows the coordinator to save time and unnecessary exchanges with the other partners.

Misalignment between required documents and potential Audit: In the previous program, some documents had to be mandatorily collected by the coordinator and made available in case of Audit (Signatures lists, timesheets, boarding passes for in-person meetings, attendance certificates, etc.). In the current program, however, the indication on the documents to be collected is very vague. The intent to simplify (by eliminating the list of mandatory documents) has instead complicated the work of the coordinator, who does not have clarity on which documents to produce or request from partners.

Continuous reporting system: The new online reporting system seems to be more complete than the old Mobility Tool.

Enhanced inclusion and diversity measures: the new measures are undoubtedly a great step forward. Erasmus Plus represents one of the pillars at European level in terms of education and exchange. These new measures are important from two points of view:
• They promote and facilitate the participation of the most vulnerable groups in project activities, including mobility;
• They increase awareness on the issue of inclusion and diversity among the organizations participating in the projects funded by the programmed, with a potential significant impact on the education system in the EU.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Participating in Erasmus+ projects has provided a very wide range of experiences and broadened the horizon. Both students and teachers have developed communication skills, learn about the education systems and cultures of other European countries. Knowledge of foreign languages, teamwork has improved.
In most cases, cooperation between partners has been effective and constructive. Unfortunately, there is also a very unpleasant (fortunately only one) experience, where the coordinator’s activities were almost close to zero and the partners also had to carry out the work of the coordinator. 
This system, where partners automatically co-finance a coordinator, seems to me to be very good.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The citizens’ association Elektrosmog and Health sees a strong need to protect the environment from waste from electronic equipment (WEEE). Around 21 % of such waste comes from computers, laptops, mobile phones, Wi-Fi routers, etc. There are several high-value raw materials in technology facilities, including gold, silver, copper and iron. As much as 350 kg of silver, 15 876 kg of copper, 34 kg of gold and 15 kg of palladium can be recycled by recycling one million mobile phones. https://theroundup.org/global-e-waste-statistics/ It is horrific that only 42.5 % of WEEE is recycled on average in the EU. The recycling system needs to be streamlined. It is important that individual valuable metals, minerals and chemicals can be extracted from the electronics used and then reused in new electronics. Given the speed and scale of technological developments, the search for resources to produce new electronics in the EU and in the world has significantly increased. Mining of metals and minerals is destructive for the local environment and too often harms the health of local people. All electronics must be easy to repair, easily recyclable and of course have a longer lifetime. In request 13 of the European Citizens’ Initiative, we call for the inclusion of WEEE, other waste products and the environmental impacts of extraction of minerals and precious metals used in electronic devices in the Zero Pollution Action Plan. https://signstop5g.eu/sk/riesenia/ochrana-zivotneho-prostredia/navrh-13 Welcome Petra Bertová Polovková for the Citizens’ Association Elektrosmog and Health This feedback was sent via cable without harmful radiation with less electricity consumption with higher data security.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
Overall, this text still presents too much recycling as a solution to WEEE management. It is imperative that this Directive places emphasis on Member States that recycling is the last resort for WEEE. Many other solutions exist, which do not require as much energy as recycling (the other circular economy cycles).

Article 12 on financing from households should put much more emphasis on the fact that recycling is now far less costly than a few decades ago and that, in view of the financial benefits derived from it, it is unusual for this sector to be financed by the households themselves. This sector must be financed by producers and recyclers who are enriched by waste. Wastes which, in view of their high concentration of strategic metals (Au, Ag, Cu, Zn, Pb) should be considered as resources.
Another option is to require recyclers to recycle – thanks to their benefits from strategic metals – less profitable but equally vital metals (rare metals, rare earths, etc.), and not to prioritise the profitability of the processes at all costs. 
A final solution is to impose the sharing of funding between all national actors working in the circular economy, focusing on the shortest cycles: repair, reuse, repackaging and only, as a last resort, recycling. Member States urgently need to launch pathways for these shortest cycles. 

On the other hand, compensation is not mentioned at all in the article (although it is, for example, in Article 11 of Directive 2008/98/EC). It is essential to give priority to this shortest cycle of the circular economy (as well as the others, which are priorities for recycling) and not to include in the word catch-all ‘re-use’, which reduces the impact of all these sectors. Directive 2008/98/EC adds ‘repair’ following ‘re-use’, which is already a step forward. 
In particular, Article 4 should clearly mention cooperation between producers and circular economy actors (repairers, reuse channels, repackers, etc.) and not just recyclers. Moreover, it is necessary to develop that it is not only “re-use, dismantling and recovery of WEEE and their components and materials” that should be encouraged, but that all stages of the circular economy, well before recycling, and in particular repair, can be greatly promoted at the design stage (see examples such as Fairphone, among others). 

The term ‘recovery’, as defined in that Article (which refers to Article 3 of Directive 2008/98/EC), is not at all appropriate in the case of Article 11. Indeed, its definition in the Directive can be compared to recycling. This Article 11 should take over the other loops of the circular economy.

Annexes II-2 and IV-4 or 5 must also include kitchen appliances (robots, mixers, bread machines, etc.) and other small appliances for cooking and processing food products.
Annexes II-3 or 4 and IV-6 must also include headlamps, which are increasingly common in our waste. 
Annex II-4 IV-4 or 5 must include the plastic-vinyl products (or discs) that have returned to fashion. 
It is also necessary to add to Annex II and Annex IV electronic cigarettes which risk pulling into our waste in the coming years. All disposable electronic devices should also be included here (although they should be banned first).  
Annex VII should specify that this treatment is not applicable in the case of preparation for re-use.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: RREUSE welcomes the possibility to provide input for the evaluation of the WEEE Directive. The WEEE Directive has been an important first step towards increasing the circularity of electronics, but the directive must be updated to reflect increasing quantity of electronic waste and improved by focusing on ensuring correct implementation across member states and on some key aspects. Firstly, EPRs tend to favour material recycling over reuse and preparation for reuse, resulting in suboptimal environmental benefits and less job creation potential, while also directly contradicting the waste hierarchy and the aims of the Directive. The most effective way to solve this issue is to set quantitative reuse and preparation for re-use targets to ensure that incentives are aligned with the waste hierarchy. Eco-modulation of the EPR fee should also be designed to encourage re-use and preparation for re-use before recycling. It is also important that EPR schemes established under the Directive are compliant with the minimum requirements set in Art. 8a of the WFD to ensure the involvement of all stakeholders in the development, governance and functioning of these schemes. Waste prevention aspects should also be addressed by ensuring high ambition in relevant product legislation such as Ecodesign and Right to Repair. Secondly, collection should be improved with a view of safeguarding the reusability of goods, preventing damaging potentially reusable items. Relevant stakeholders, including social enterprises, should be consulted on what technical aspects of separate collection of WEEE should be enhanced in a future revision. Thirdly, accredited operators should be granted access to the waste stream in accordance with Art. 6 of the Directive, which is often not the case, especially regarding takeback schemes operated by distributors who tend to have the highest quality of WEEE and thus the highest reuse potential. Finally, there are also legal barriers hampering the reusability of WEEE. The most important one is the lack of a clear end-of-waste criteria for WEEE that has been prepared for reuse. Accredited operators should be allowed to declare whether an item has reached its end-of-waste status after passing a rigorous examination procedure, similar to what is prescribed by the Austrian end-of-waste criteria. Therefore, a potential revision of the WEEE directive should be conducive to the correct application of the waste hierarchy by: - Setting up a quantitative target for preparation for re-use, separate from recycling Improving separate collection with a view of facilitating preparation for re-use by using standard EN50614 as a baseline to integrate relevant provisions in EU legislation - Ensuring that the implementation of EPR schemes is conducive to the directives aims by guaranteeing the involvement of all stakeholders and aligning economic incentives with the waste hierarchy - Granting access to the waste stream for accredited reuse operators and supporting the establishment of national reuse networks - Considering waste prevention aspects by strengthening the durability and reparability of EEEs in other relevant EU legislation - Establishing clear guidelines to determine the end-of-waste status of WEEEs that have been prepared for re-use Please see the attached document for more detailed feedback.

Relevant stakeholders, including social enterprises, should be consulted on what technical aspects of separate collection of WEEE should be enhanced in a future revision. Thirdly, accredited operators should be granted access to the waste stream in accordance with Art. 6 of the Directive, which is often not the case, especially regarding takeback schemes operated by distributors who tend to have the highest quality of WEEE and thus the highest reuse potential. Finally, there are also legal barriers hampering the reusability of WEEE. The most important one is the lack of a clear end-of-waste criteria for WEEE that has been prepared for reuse. Accredited operators should be allowed to declare whether an item has reached its end-of-waste status after passing a rigorous examination procedure, similar to what is prescribed by the Austrian end-of-waste criteria. Therefore, a potential revision of the WEEE directive should be conducive to the correct application of the waste hierarchy by: - Setting up a quantitative target for preparation for re-use, separate from recycling Improving separate collection with a view of facilitating preparation for re-use by using standard EN50614 as a baseline to integrate relevant provisions in EU legislation - Ensuring that the implementation of EPR schemes is conducive to the directives aims by guaranteeing the involvement of all stakeholders and aligning economic incentives with the waste hierarchy - Granting access to the waste stream for accredited reuse operators and supporting the establishment of national reuse networks - Considering waste prevention aspects by strengthening the durability and reparability of EEEs in other relevant EU legislation - Establishing clear guidelines to determine the end-of-waste status of WEEEs that have been prepared for re-use Please see the attached document for more detailed feedback.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I have been actively involved in the Erasmus+ programme since 2010 in various funded projects in my previous and current job (university advisor international education and acquisition). In the evaluation of the Erasmus+ programme, I hope the following aspects can be taken into account and will be included in questionnaires or surveys for the evaluation itself:

(1) The ease with which the rules and protocols can be followed; especially the complex 'fine print' regarding financial rules and regulations:
- Local circumstances make it often challenging to apply the same rules and regulations leading the increased cost/co-financing and burdening partners.
- This was a challenging experience working in/with South African partners in a capacity-building project where partners had to make the high extra costs for e.g. VAT declarations, or hiring expertise because subcontracting was denied (although the expertise could not be delivered from within the consortium).
- Another example is the strict link between travel (> 99 km) and subsistence for local partners; e.g. local SA partners often had to make extra costs for locations when universities cannot provide this and external locations are required for easy access. Also, local circumstances regarding obstacles in travel and traffic, render it almost impossible to have full working days without using local accommodation. Thus especially, in our case, the SA partners 'suffered' most from this limitation in finances. There seem to have been little understanding of this difficult local situation.

(2) How decisions are made regarding publishing project results on the Project Results Platform. Putting a lot of effort into collecting relevant documentation from various sources, and then noticing less than half is accepted without knowing the reasons behind this, does not seem to be constructive or do justice to the achievements of the project.

(3) Accessibility of the agency and open/transparent communication. In some cases, it was difficult to reach the executive agency and or project officers. Requesting for phone calls seems to work better; better access to help desks on project guidance/counselling would be more constructive and supportive for project coordinators.

(4) Late opening of funding calls in combination with challenging short application windows make it hard to truly work on high-quality joint proposals.

(5) Communication between the executive agency and the national agencies.

(6) More frequent seminars and training for project leaders and coordinators would provide extra support in the post-award management of larger and more complex international projects (only one 2-day training was not enough).

(7) Additional seminars and training for applicants in the pre-award phase would provide extra support; especially if these events/meetings would include specialists from the executive agency and if they would include sharing of experiences, and best/good and bad practices; as a learning community. 

(8) Administrative burden of using the programme successfully. Many times I receive comments from colleagues who are hesitant in using the Erasmus+ programme because of the high and cumbersome administrative burden without having enough working days for management and administration. Internally, partners have to set up a good structure including various experts that can support the programme. The level of co-financing is rather high when taking into account this extra (required) support.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The experience with Erasmus+ in the 2014-2020 period is very positive and has helped to strengthen the organisation, both in terms of content and administration. 
As an organisation (MaMa) we participated in all key actions, as well as in the European Solidarity Corps, where we recruited one person and helped us strengthen our organisation in terms of staffing. We have also been able to create and retain one job through the programmes. Contributing to the development of our organisation is not only at the level of our organisation, but also more broadly, as we involve different youth centres and young people in all projects, as a national network organisation of youth centres. Over the past period, we have included a number of youth centres in K1-mobility of youth workers, which have gained knowledge and were able to apply for an Erasmus+ project and obtain additional funding. In addition, we have included youth centres in the K3 Youth Dialogue and have also trained them to understand the importance of youth dialogue and provide them with a positive experience. 
During the transition period, Erasmus + projects have opened up a number of topics and have also produced strategic documents that upgrade the organisation in substance and open up opportunities for the future. One example is social inclusion in youth centres and digital youth work. Through youth dialogue, we also put two key actions on the political agenda that the Government and the Ministry have adopted. This is a national professional qualification for a youth worker and an increase in funding for the youth sector as part of a public call. 
The effects and results of the previous period are visible at the level of organisations, employees and also youth centres, as Erasmus+ projects, as a support organisation, respond to the needs of the youth sector and indirectly of young people. In the previous programming period, we focused on the quality of youth work, social inclusion and digital youth work, which provided us with good references and a way forward. 
In any case, there were also some shortcomings in implementation, which in the long run brought us a positive experience. 
The epidemic, all of which has reached and touched upon in the past year, must be recognised as having not hitherto had negative consequences due to the nature of the projects. Projects that have already been delayed and are in the process of submitting a report. At this point, it was also thanks for understanding and adapting NA MOVIT and for all the support they offer at times such as the COVID-19 crisis. 
As regards administrative improvements, the proposal to reduce pre-dotation funds or percentages may be proposed, especially in these uncertain situations. They may be limited to only and only certain actions and receive different percentages for different activities. 
Possible improvements in content could also go to smärt Boll’s freedom of choice, at a time when we need to take action in all areas and perhaps not only on social inclusion, mental health, sustainability, digitalisation and desired topics. The proposal is that the priority areas are not so limited and that applicants can choose their own themes, but it would be sensible that they need to cover digital and green themes in one of the activities of the project and not only in general, since youth work alone is a work based on social inclusion. 
With regard to the development of the results themselves, it would be sensible to make a breakthrough in order to build on and develop results either in another key action or in the same one, with a view to making them truly sustainable. 
I also noticed a shortcoming in understanding dissemination and communication, which is not the same and could be separate calls, specifically for the dissemination or dissemination of results after project completion, where the concrete continuation of the project and so-called follow-up of project results could be identified. In the previous period, there was no concrete focus on the continuation and maintenance of project results after project completion

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

Greek Universities are not similar to European standards and it is not easy to match study credits between Greek and European universities and universities in the USA. 
Nor is the procedure for accessing postgraduate degrees and postgraduate degrees clearly defined.

An example of this is the fact that all graduates of the Greek Polytechnics are considered to hold a Master!
In order to work in Greece, an engineer must hold a Master! There is no Bachelor degree in Greece. Angloosaxi engineers work with Bachelor and do ‘uranoxes’, and in Greece to work with a lower working environment, they must have a Master! And to study more years!

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Norwegian Environment Agency welcomes the evaluation of the WEEE Directive and the chance to share our views. We believe that the Directive has been an important instrument in increasing the collection and proper treatment of WEEE. However, we also see that there are room for improvements, based on experiences on the effect of requirements and also as a consequence of changes in markets, technologies and environmental challenges. Also, the Directive should be evaluated in the light of circular economy, including requirements related to extended producer responsibility. Collection targets Meeting the national collection targets have been a challenge, also for Norway. We believe that there is a need to consider if the requirements for collection and the collection targets are appropriate today, and further, to consider if there is a need for changing the requirements. For instance, there is a need to evaluate if a weight based target is appropriate to achieve both the goals of collecting high volumes on one side, but also waste with hazardous substances and also light weight waste, such as mobile phones and tablets, which contains critical minerals. We also believe that using POM (put on marked) and WEEE generated in collection target has proven difficult, and that these data are vulnerable for mistakes. The Norwegian Environment Agency had consultants look into the challlenges of reaching the collection targets in 2018. The report is attached to our comment. Critical raw materials Critical raw materials are key to the green and digital transition. WEEE contains critical raw materials, but the recycling and recovery rate is often close to zero of these materials. Requirements in the WEEE directive do not sufficiently facilitate recycling and recovery of critical raw materials. There is a need for initiatives which optimize recycling of critical raw materials as a contribution to strengthen circularity and the green transition. Technology is still developing, and it might therefore be a need for regulation that speed up this development and subsequent investments in capacity. We recommend that these issues are considered in the evaluation of the WEEE Directive, also in context with the work on an Euopean Critical Raw Materials Act. Illegal export of WEEE Combating illegal export of WEEE is a great challenge. In Norway, illegal export of WEEE has been one of our priorities recent years. The minimum requirements for shipment of used EEE in the WEEE Directive has made it significant easier for authorities to enforce illegal export. However, we experience that there is room for improvements in the Directive to help authorities to uncover and stop illegal activities related to export of WEEE. For instance, there are requirements which we experience as easy to circumvent, like documentation of testing.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
text_raw: The University of Porto has been one of the Portuguese Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) most active in the context of Erasmus + and recognises opportunities for excellence for its academic community and for itself as an organisation. However, I would like to highlight some aspects in the context of the management and implementation of the International Credit Mobility Action that deserve analysis and possible adjustment in order to maximise the impact of projects and simplify their management and implementation: A. We note that, in situations of force majeure duly validated by the NA, participants should not be asked to return the grant (or part thereof), for example in cases of academic inadaptation leading to suspension of the grant and return to home; b. The bureaucratic process involving the Interinstitutional Agreements (IIA) should be simplified, not requiring signature by the legal representative of the institution and only one general agreement (MoU) signed at the launch of the project; c. The final report of the participants presents subjective or potentially misinterpreted issues, such as those related to the “European sentiment” as it is a global/global project and we therefore suggest its removal; D. The final report of the KA1 projects should be reduced, there are many questions of interpretation of statistical data that should be used to comment only in cases where low percentages are identified, and there are many issues that are repetitive from year to year, so a more focused report on the financial part and only a short narrative description of activities, dissemination and impact would be useful; e. A detailed guide to procedures, drawn up by the European Commission or the National Agency, for management and implementation would be most useful in supporting the coordinating institutions of these projects; f. It would be important for the European Commission to engage in joint work with the European Union Delegations, promoting greater knowledge of the Programme and its objectives through meetings and clarifications at the highest level with the national authorities, so as to make it possible to speed up the issuing of visas to ICM fellows.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
We appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback on relevant issues for the impact assessment and  ask to consider the following issues in detail during impact assessment: 

PEF for 100% H2: for Lot 1 
We welcome the possibility to indicate future H2-ready appliance on the upcoming energy label. With regards to energy transition, the PEF should be based on expected sources in the near future rather than steam reforming with current energy mix. This could lead to significantly lower values and could support the technology. 
The H2 targets, especially for green H2, within the EU should be considered and analysed in further detail. European and national H2 strategies are pushing towards renewable H2, the mentioned target values for H2 production could be used as a basis for further developments. Studies on national level (DE: DVGW; IINAS; bdew…) should also be taken into account. 

Modulation: Lot 1
Before introducing a possible requirement for modulation on gas and/or oil appliances, all framework conditions should be considered. In order to create a scientific basis for the discussion, detailed analysis on the number of start-up processes must be carried out for different scenarios. 
Beside the modulation range, other parameters such as the building type and age, the location, the heating behavior of the customer or the control strategy have an influence on the operation of the system and need to be considered. Possible requirements should be derived on the basis of such a comprehensive analysis. With regards to any safety aspect, the smallest resulting output should be limited to a useful value.  

Data monitoring: Lot 1
As these proposed requirements are totally new, and were never discussed in the extensive preparatory phase, they are not based on a feasibility study, currently there are no measurement standards, and hence no possibility to determine the accuracy of the data. A study should highlight the clear advantages, added values and the outlays of additional functionality. The energy consumption shown via  data monitoring functionality should be reflected towards the possible energy savings by end-users. 
Further to appliance characteristics, consumption and efficiency are significantly influenced by general operating condition (like the weather), end-user behaviour and the overall system characteristics. The data from field monitoring of individual heating devices cannot be used for rating of the appliance without detailed knowledge of the application and operating conditions. Additionally, it needs to be further evaluated which accuracy is required to achieve reliable results. Finally, the scope  needs to be reviewed and defined in the light of final energy and rated capacity. 

T peak: Lot 1 
Test conditions for DHW performance of heat pumps are still under discussion. Various proposals can be found from fulfilment of test requirements at 55°C peak temperature to a test temperature of 50°C with or without mathematical corrections by the help of malus percentage points.  The attached document offers an alternative calculation method, applicable to test temperatures between 52°C and 55°C. It provides correction factors for the COP, which better reflect the real behavior of heat pumps compared to a malus system.
text_raw: We appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback on relevant issues for the impact assessment and ask to consider the following issues in detail during impact assessment: PEF for 100% H2: for Lot 1 We welcome the possibility to indicate future H2-ready appliance on the upcoming energy label. With regards to energy transition, the PEF should be based on expected sources in the near future rather than steam reforming with current energy mix. This could lead to significantly lower values and could support the technology. The H2 targets, especially for green H2, within the EU should be considered and analysed in further detail. European and national H2 strategies are pushing towards renewable H2, the mentioned target values for H2 production could be used as a basis for further developments. Studies on national level (DE: DVGW; IINAS; bdew…) should also be taken into account. Modulation: Lot 1 Before introducing a possible requirement for modulation on gas and/or oil appliances, all framework conditions should be considered. In order to create a scientific basis for the discussion, detailed analysis on the number of start-up processes must be carried out for different scenarios. Beside the modulation range, other parameters such as the building type and age, the location, the heating behavior of the customer or the control strategy have an influence on the operation of the system and need to be considered. Possible requirements should be derived on the basis of such a comprehensive analysis. With regards to any safety aspect, the smallest resulting output should be limited to a useful value.
