initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
Women Engage for a Common Future, WECF, welcomes the European Commission’s plan to revise the EU Toy Safety Directive (TSD), in relation to widening the scope of toxic chemicals to be banned for children's toys and products intended for children use. As an international ecofeminist network of 150 women and civil organizations NGOs committed to build a healthy environment and be active at EU level for moving towards a toxic-free future, Women Engage for a Common Future (WECF) calls the EU authorities to stronger regulations in context of harmful chemicals, especially for children. All products used by children should be subject to the precautionary principle. Toys are not justified of having any essential use, that would allow for the use of harmful chemicals or other threats to children`s safety or health. 
The protection of children and the realization of the rights of the child and the right to live in a clean and healthy environmental should be of the most important goals for the EU, as it has been laid down in the EU Strategy on the Rights of the Child.  
As an organisation focusing on gender, women and children's aspects of chemicals safety, protection of vulnerable populations, such as pregnant women and children, must be a priority.  As long as there is no guarantee that only safe products enter the market, WECF raises awareness and informs consumer about better protection from harmful chemicals, including toys.   Children must be protected from potential leaching of chemicals of concerns from toys as they are using them from a very early stage of its development. Products used by children should be subject to the precautionary principle and should not contain toxic chemicals.
Therefore, we welcome the strengthening of the Toy Safety Directive and with it the safety requirements that toys must meet before they can be marketed in the EU. Many extremely hazardous substances continue to be permitted for use in toys, in some cases in even higher concentrations then in the early version of the directive. 
Also, after the UN included the human right for a clean, healthy and sustainable environment in its Resolution 48/12, the future and health of children cannot be compromised.
Products surrounding a baby at this early stage of its development, and in particular their chemical content, and baby’s exposure to it, determine to a large extent how healthy these surroundings are. Toys are developed and sold solely for the education and entertainment of children. Toys are not justified of having any essential use, that would allow for the use of harmful chemicals or other threats to children`s safety or health.
Children are not only exposed to toxins from toys. Furniture, personal care products, clothes, plastic products, food containers and many more are sources for exposure. Combined exposures leading to mixture effects and a sum effect must be considered. Safe levels of chemicals e. g. in toys should not be set by one chemical alone. 
We agree to broaden the scope of the TSD toward all endocrine disruptors and substances that are persistent and bio accumulative. 
Endocrine disruptors (EDCs) are especially for children from high danger because they can disrupt their hormonal status, which affects a healthy growth and development 

Please find more in the file attached.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ 2017-2020 (VET):

KA1 Projects for Accredited Accredited Persons: very smooth and flexible

Ka 2 projects:
— the amounts are very small. With no account being taken of the salaries of mobile teachers, the actual self-financing rate of projects easily increased by more than 50 %. Multiplier events: your organisation is also an important dissemination site.
— funding for management and implementation unfair: EUR 3000 per year is ideally different in Bulgaria or Finland — let alone in Norway.
— project applications were burdensome and opaque for partners. The applications did not support the work of the coordinator.

Erasmus 2021-27 (vocational and general education)

KA1 Projects for Accredited Accredited Persons:
— at the application stage, the applicant should have some budget in order to see what has been applied for. The increased flexibility would otherwise be welcomed.
— the bad idea is to use the same templates of contracts and reports for staff, students and all sectors. When trying to make everyone fit for all, it’s not right for anyone. Participants and NV correspondents in their surprises. The bases are also highly bureaucratic, in particular the learning agreement in the short individual exchanges for high school pupils (2-4 weeks).
—Learning agreement complement as a certificate for the participant not usable
— Beneficiary Modile’s questions are too detailed. Why real mileage or housing? Difficult to fill in important certificates; for example, a Certificate supplement that can be downloaded online by anyone, but what is it? The question related to the recognition process is part of the application, not at this stage — delete! The budget is difficult to understand; the same distribution as for the grant (participation money/all other) would suffice. Too many clicks are required to fill in.
— inclusion support for the participant: shocking acknowledgement — hopefully there will be a change soon. Is unsuitable in its current form for long periods of professional activity at all; shortly, it will lead to a complete cessation of the granting of participation subsidies. This must not be the case — supporting inclusion is so important and good.
— Green travel: many changes have been made to the rules of the game. The idea is good, but the ship should still be green so that we can even be delighted by Finns.
— digital cooperation has its own support category — the digital world is a lot of work, but it could significantly improve the quality of cycles. However, an incentive would be necessary.
— let’s say that vaccination and visa costs, etc., are now also covered. Global opportunities (occupational) are also welcome!
— the minimum duration of student mobility is 5 days for the professional as well. It would allow for flexible organisation of different workshops within the partner network without having to apply for a heavy KA2 project around.
— OLS is now more opaque and heavier for the student than before. If there was too little use before, it is hardly too much now.
— The same amounts of aid for the general and professional sector. There is no surplus of the money, but self-financing is required in both cases.

KA2 projects:

— small-scale partnerships are an excellent type of project! It should be used more widely — it would allow for bilateral cooperation and development. 
— flexibility in funding is welcome — questions remain about how to organise the transfer of money between partners in practice in a fair way?

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
EHPA would like to thank the European Commission and the study team for the work already carried out and welcomes the opportunity given to send feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks. 

Ecodesign Minimum Energy Efficiency Limits
EHPA recommends that minimum energy efficiency performance (MEPS) limit for electric heat pump water heaters (HPWH) at tapping profile L should not be higher than 95%. EHPA suggests aligning the two MEPs tables for Thermally Driven Heat Pump (TDHP), adopting the proposed values indicated in Lot 2 also for lot 1 combi TDHP. 

Material efficiency aspects
EHPA supports the inclusion of relevant, meaningful and verifiable/enforceable material efficiency requirements but has several comments and recommendations. EHPA appreciates that an exhaustive list of spare parts has been defined, referring to the most critical spare parts; accessible only to professional repairers. Only trained and F-gas certified installers are legally allowed to intervene on the appliances. Finally, EHPA recommends setting a requirement ensuring  the maximum delivering time of spare parts within fifteen working days limiting the scope to appliances ≤ 70 kW. 

Calculation Methods & Tpeak
EHPA does not support the proposal for heat pumps regarding the peak temperature. Heat pumps would be penalised for not reaching a temperature (55°C) which is not realistic. We would like to reiterate our compromise on peak temperature sent to the Commission in July 2021, in which we proposed to reduce the peak temperature to no more than 50°C with the current load profile. This would create a level playing field for all technologies, while delivering the necessary sufficient hot water to meet consumer needs today. 

Thermodynamic domestic hot water testing method recommended within regulation 813/2013 and 814/2013 is largely inspired from EN16147 testing standard. This testing standard aims as assessing the energy efficiency of HPWH and combination heat pumps while providing comfort in adequation with the end-user’s need. In particular, tapping profiles and their draw-off mimic domestic hot water daily usage and aim at ensuring that both the water temperature is high enough and sufficient energy is available to fulfil the hot water need. 
There are two draw-offs where Tpeak shall reach 55°C. These draw-off were initially named: bath draw-off and corresponded to dish washing draw-offs which logically occur at 12h45 and 20h30. At the time where EN16147 was originally developed on the basis of Mandate 324 describing the load profiles for all water heaters, it was commonly accepted by experts that 55°C water temperature is not essential to comfort as water at 55°C is generally far too hot to be used without mixing it with cold water. As a consequence, the option for a virtual backup heater was introduced in EN16147 for the dish washing draw-off, and exclusively for the dish washing draw-off. Increasing the water temperature by 3 to 4°C, or increasing the tank heating period frequency will dramatically reduce the overall energy efficiency of the HPWH and combination heat pumps without bringing any additional comfort. 


Avoid setting testing method in the regulation
EHPA would like to share some general recommendations. The legal texts should not contain too many details on measurements and calculations, which should be left to the standards. This may create some unexpected loopholes. Learning from the winter package, it will increase the number of errors and the need for amending regulations. For this reason, we recommend excluding details on test methods/test conditions from the regulation. 

You may find our full position on Lot 2 on EHPA website:
https://www.ehpa.org/fileadmin/user_upload/20211115_EHPA_written_comments_on_Lot_2.pdf

We remain at your disposal to discuss this matter with you in greater details.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
great addition to the programme are the opportunities for short term, BIP. This enhances the changes for fewer opportunities students. Also the amount for inclusion top-up are very effective and should continue. The green travel support could be much higher. Now it is only a small incentive, not making the difference. Instead of giving more travel days, it is earier and less bureaucratic to give more money per participant travelling green.

IT systems that are not in place should not have been introduced yet. e.g. Ben. Module, the new OLS Academy and the EWP. It would have been much less time consuming if all of this is only installed as soon as it is ready, tested and dummy-proof. Now it is a mess and it takes so much hours of work from many people.

The name of "international mobility" as part of KA131 should be reconsidered. It is a confusing term that does not represent what it is. Also the combination of International Mobility in KA131 and Mobility of KA171 is confusing and could be merged to make sure the opportunities are more clear and there are less different processes needed.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: [Part 1/3] The WEEE Directive (Directive 2012/19/EU of 4 July 2012 on waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE)) must evolve to take account of the urgency of preserving resources, protecting the environment and the need to extend the lifetime of equipment through reuse and repair. The intrinsic value of electrical and electronic equipment is also a source of looting and predation, as highlighted by the European Court of Auditors in its report on ‘EU actions and challenges in the field of electronic waste’ (Analysis Document No 04/2021: EU actions and challenges in the field of electronic waste, May 2021 (https://www.eca.europa.eu/fr/Pages/DocItem.aspx?did=58526): waste management that is not virtuous or loose from the legal framework has a clear economic attractiveness. The illegal networks calling for the metallic and critical fractions are sources of pollution and loss of sovereignty for the European Ecosystem, a French operational eco-body approved by the State for the EEE sector, collected 741 000 tonnes of WEEE (out of all 6 categories excluding photovoltaic, household and professional panels) in 2021. Below is our response to the European Commission’s call for input on the evaluation of the Directive, in particular on those aspects where its implementation has been particularly difficult: 1. Achieving WEEE collection targets: The method of calculating the damage to the objectives differs in its application from one country to another, some Member States such as France take into account the tonnes actually collected and processed in accordance with the rules by the approved sector (made up of eco-bodies and individual systems) when other Member States make “reasoned estimates” (in accordance with Article 16(4) of the Directive) taking into account estimated deposits which they cannot guarantee are treated in accordance with the regulatory framework (for example, the proportion of WEEE estimated to be smoothed from a sampling campaign carried out on skippers containing scrap is extrapolated to the whole of the scrap declared on the Member State’s territory without the guarantee). The performance targets of the current Directive do not take account of the reality of the deposit available for collection (GDAC) for operators in the approved sector. The estimated total deposit for the entire territory of the Member State concerned. As illustrated in the diagram below for France (study on the WEEE deposit, carried out in 2019), the deposit available for collection (GDAC) represents all WEEE flows generated in the territory which the approved chain has the capacity to collect, control and influence. This deposit represents 61 % of the total estimated deposit. The balance (39 %) is composed of 25 % WEEE which escapes illegally from the approved sector and 14 % of which information on potential outlets is still very deficient. This is despite a major, but specific, regulatory provision in France (Law of 17 August 2015 on the Ecological Transition for Verte Growth, Articles L.541-10-20 and R.543-200-1 of the Environmental Code), which has made it generally compulsory for WEEE management operators to contract with an eco-body or an approved individual system if they wish to carry out WEEE management activities or, for collection, transit and grouping operators, to demonstrate that the treatment operator to whom they entrust their waste has itself entered into such a contract with an eco organisation or an approved individual system (‘mandatory handover’).

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The ERASMUS+ 2014-2020 introduced our organisation into the ERASMUS world of mobility. As an organisation as well as individual trainers we gained a lot from these mobilities. We took our organisation outside the boundaries of Cyprus, making internationalisation a reality. We met more experienced trainers in EU countries, from whom we gain knowledge, we received experience, we heard about successes, failures and good practices. We had the opportunity to exchange ideas, thoughts and good practices, to share successes and good practices, to discuss and solve problems.

In the professional issue, we became better, we learned and experienced things that we were not aware of, but we gave to our partners our knowledge and experience. As a small NGO organisation, with a very small budged, we could never manage to succeed this without the ERASMUS+ funding. 

We did not make any 2021-2027 mobilities yet and as a result we cannot comment on the new scheme. It is important, however, from the experience gained from the information seminars of our National Agency, that the new 2021-2027 program seems to be more flexible, richer in choices and mobilities, more powerful and more user friendly.

It is also important to point out the huge and immediate support, which our National Agency and the people employed in the Agency provide to the beneficiary organisations. This is done by continoues training seminars in all areas, with fast and full of willingness respod to any questions and problems we have and with the opportunity they give us to use both email and telephone in order to solve problems as soon as they emerge.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
The text in consultation offers only electric heating via heat pumps. This cannot at all be consistent with “green” heating, as advocated in the text.
Electricity says (in France) nuclear, which is not at all ecological, despite the view of its supporters: waste, ore extraction, ageing of reactors, more than doubtful choice of EPR, energy efficiency only close to 30 %, very poor climate protection choices (the effects of which are expected to harm the nuclear sector more than it would help it not to exceed the 2° increase), etc.
There are other ways of heating houses or industrial buildings: energy efficiency, energy saving, wood heating (with waste, not “new” wood), renewable gas, renewable electricity, eco-built buildings, etc.
The choice of all nuclear power is typically French, in contrast to that of Germany, which will do so in the coming years. This is an example to follow. 
Nuclear power is the opposite.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European large families Confederation ELFAC has tried to apply to the program, but our project was rejected. We were told that there was no strict connection between the young people involved and the organizations participating, which is strange, as the organizations are the associations of the young people's families… The young people themselves decided what they wanted to do during the exchanges.

Anyway, we find it very difficult to get ways to help young people of large families to access these kinds of programs: mobility cost is a challenge when there are more children in a family. And so are, in general, the opportunity of education (languages, sports, arts, travelling), for a share of households at highest risk of poverty and social exclusion. Languages, sports, arts, travelling might be considered "not essential", but we all know how important they are for the social development of a person. That's the reason why we applied as a group of European associations, but it seems that there is no "inclusion" for children of large families. Still, almost one out of 3 children in Europe lives in a large family… It's a big share, isn't it?

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
ANEC welcomes the EC’s inception impact assessment, which contains many points we agree with. We agree the aim of this initiative should be to further strengthen the protection of children against possible risks in toys, in particular from chemicals & to further complete the Single Market for toys. Although the Toy Directive certainly brought improvements to child safety in Europe, we are of the opinion that a revision is necessary to improve different aspects.
We agree the main aspects to be tackled are chemical risks, internet-connected toys, and compliance & enforcement. 
We welcome the conclusion of the Commission’s evaluation with respect to deficient chemical provisions. Chemical requirements for toys need to be strengthened significantly. Key point is to broaden the scope of Art 46 to allow for the establishment/amendment of provisions for all kinds of chemicals & all kinds of toys (not only for children under 36 months). The extended Comitology should be used to specify, where appropriate, also essential requirements for safety aspects other than chemicals (i.e. mechanical & physical requirements, e.g. to set noise limit values). We welcome the proposed measures to be assessed, in line with our position paper ‘Toy Safety Directive evaluation and Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability (CSS): Which way forward?’ (April 2021).
We welcome the intention to address risks posed by internet-connected toys. It is essential to address issues we brought to the attention of the European Commission in 2016 & 2017 about unsecure smart toys (Cayla the spying doll). As more & more toys are now connected to Internet or other communication networks/protocols, new rules are needed to effectively protect children. New technologies can pose risks not only because they can have a direct impact on the health & safety of children & their personal data protection, but also because of connectivity, they can be indirectly used as a tool to put at risk their personal security (e.g. kidnapping). Safety obligations of producers need to cover the entire lifespan of the connected toy, not only when it is placed on the market (e.g. software updates). Market Surveillance authorities must have the means & resources to check this evolving aspect of safety. Harmonised Standards have to also reflect it.
EU Safety Gate notifications show that the toy market is far from clean and that there is a continued need for increased market surveillance actions. Necessary resources (budget & staff) shall be made available; the European Commission shall organise & finance joint market surveillance actions on toys.
In the interest of legal certainty, specific requirements for visibility & legibility of warnings on toys (e.g. minimum letter size) need to be introduced, in order to enable Member States to enforce these requirements in a uniform way (there are no specified requirements in the Directive and belonging standards). Warnings on toys are often too small, hidden by other text & sometimes difficult to discover & read.
We welcome the intention to improve reporting obligations by Member States on unsafe toys. In this context, we reiterate the need to revitalise the European Injury Database (EU-IDB) and to create a legal basis to collect accidents & injury data in the EU. The Single Market Programme could provide a sound financial base for this.
ANEC fully supports to convert the Directive into a Regulation.
Finally, we ask for the following aspects to be tackled: better regulate noise levels in toys (set the limit to what is allowed for adults in industry according to Directive 2003/10/EEC, there is no safety-based reason to expose children to higher levels than is allowed for adults); introduce EC-type examination for certain categories of toys (applying the principle ‘the higher the risk, the higher the conformity assessment procedure’, already applied for PPE or Medical Devices); remove CE Marking from the toy or its packaging & relegate it to the technical file.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Erasmus+ programme in which we participate has given us the opportunity to get to know as teachers, people from other countries with the same interests and shared concerns. 
We aim to build a better future for our children through education and to lay the groundwork for the development of tolerance and elimination of racism and intolerance between us and them.
The programme involves six countries: France, Poland, Turkey, Slovenia or Estonia and Cyprus. Title of the Programme “Discover and Share”. We had the opportunity to get to know the history, culture and culture of the participating countries through a wonderful course of 3 years. 
Activities such as dance, food, music, fairy beds, painting and traditional games have enabled children in the six countries to get unique experiences. 
Our video calls brought us very close and links of friendship developed. Through the trips we made and the photos we presented to them, they knew the participating countries and discovered similarities and differences with their country. 
Through the trip to taste, children tried food and products from the different countries we visited. We shared recipes with the parents and made festivals with the participation of parents and children
We adopted painting techniques from other countries.
Children developed critical thinking and asked us to apply what we saw and liked in our school too. In this way we tried to make our kindergarten more humane and to
we give children the opportunity to engage and influence their school environment. 
As teachers, we have gained experience of techniques and knowledge of the education systems of the participating countries and we have tried to apply some of them to our programme as well. 
But we also had many difficulties. The pandemic deprived us of many experiences we had pre-matched to offer children and which were very difficult due to health protocols. The trips were made with many difficulties and with many postponements. It was the first time that we took part as individuals in European programmes, often faced with difficulties. The OPEP and the seminars we attended helped us well 
But now that we came to the end, we understood how much we have gained as teachers and as members of the Erasmus+ society.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Electronic scraps must not be exported to developing countries. They should be treated primarily in the country where they have been recycled or in neighbouring countries. The most important issue is to ensure that products are truly recycled in an environmentally friendly and safe way. They must not cause environmental problems as they have already happened in developing countries. It shall not be allowed to do so. And this is not acceptable.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
We welcome the opportunity to be able to participate in the consultation for the EC’s Inception Impact Assessment concerning the revision of the Toy and Safety Directive. It is important to work with a variety of organisations and to include stakeholders to understand different points of view. 
 
As a responsible manufacturer, we agree with the overall aim of the initiative, which is to strengthen the protection of children against possible risks in toys and we ask the European Commission to consider the following:   

• Converting the Directive into a Regulation would benefit businesses significantly, as it would reduce divergence across countries. It would help achieve the objective of free movement of toys throughout countries.

• Improving reporting procedures and practices is important but these should be followed homogenously across Member States. In addition, it is essential to have a ‘good-practice’ sampling procedure in place. 

• Reviewing carcinogenic, mutagenic and reprotoxic (CMR) limits and improvements on derogations of the general prohibitions are welcome but it is important for these to be determined through an accurate test methodology and with input from third party laboratories. Hence, CMR limits should be relevant to the toy and should not rely on limits that were originally set for Classification Labelling and Packaging (CLP) purposes. The current concentration limits fail to account for possible exposures from extraction or migration.

• We welcome any approach to improve consumer safety and arguments to lower the limits for nitrosamines in teething products, or products intended to be mouthed are supported. However, balloons are not intended to be mouthed; hence, we do not anticipate any changes to limits on balloons where no evidence of harm exists. 

• We support keeping the 36-month age limit as it has been adopted and well understood within the market place.  

• Although the balloon industry has been working hard to ensure all reputable members’ products are compliant through education and using the best possible available resources, we continue to find huge discrepancies at the testing level between different laboratories, and within the same laboratories themselves on chemically identical samples. We believe further work is required on why these discrepancies occur to ensure that tests are robust, reliable and fit for purpose. 

• Suggestions to improve general risk assessments associated to toys are welcome but these must be driven by scientific evidence. Any changes should be fair and should not marginalise or hamper smaller manufacturers. It is important that costs to manufacturers are not unreasonable and unjustifiable. It is also important to consider the time that businesses need to be able to adjust.

• The implementation of the Digital Product Passport could benefit businesses and avoid unnecessary custom delays. Nevertheless, the information shared must be restricted to protect intellectual and proprietary information and sufficient time must be given to allow companies to develop and implement systems. 

• Setting up a fast-track mechanism to address emerging risks is welcome but the criteria used to determine those risks must be clear and the risks must be measurable.  

• It is important to review the role of online market places to protect children from dangerous toys sold online. Clear obligations could be established for online marketplaces to detect, remove and prevent unsafe products being sold. 

• We welcome the intention to address risks posed by internet-connected toys.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The main problem identified by the Association of Municipal Enterprises (VKU) with the WEEE Directive is the statutory collection target of 65 % of the average weight of EEE placed on the market in the three previous years. A revision is therefore welcomed by the VKU. The amended WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU replaced the old EU-wide absolute collection targets (originally 4 kg per inhabitant per year) by relative collection targets, which result in different absolute collection targets from one Member State to another. The new classification established a link between the generation of new EEE on the market and the volume of WEEE to be collected in a given Member State. The aforementioned link between newly placed on the market EEE and the collection of waste equipment implicitly assumes that the purchase of new equipment automatically replaces old appliances in households that are directly disposed of as waste. This automaticity is far from true in reality, as in many cases the appliances have a longer lifetime than the assumed three years, in particular the heavy PV modules used for long periods and then reused, but also refrigerators, washing machines, etc. In the case of heavy PV modules, it is particularly important that, as a result of the energy transition, houses are equipped with PV modules for the first time and therefore no existing PV modules are replaced and become waste. Moreover, older devices often remain in households as replacement appliances, are stored in cellars or garages or are supplied as second-hand appliances, which, from the point of view of resource conservation, is by no means to be criticised, but rather to be welcomed. In practice, the methodological basis for the determination of the survey objectives seems to be unreliable. In particular, the coverage target of 65 % of the average weight of EEE placed on the market in the last three years, which has been in force since 2019, appears to be very high in this context and will be difficult to achieve if the legal means of recording waste electrical equipment are taken into account consistently and exclusively. In particular, there is an urgent need for reform at European level in the formulation of collection rates.

The new classification established a link between the generation of new EEE on the market and the volume of WEEE to be collected in a given Member State. The aforementioned link between newly placed on the market EEE and the collection of waste equipment implicitly assumes that the purchase of new equipment automatically replaces old appliances in households that are directly disposed of as waste. This automaticity is far from true in reality, as in many cases the appliances have a longer lifetime than the assumed three years, in particular the heavy PV modules used for long periods and then reused, but also refrigerators, washing machines, etc. In the case of heavy PV modules, it is particularly important that, as a result of the energy transition, houses are equipped with PV modules for the first time and therefore no existing PV modules are replaced and become waste. Moreover, older devices often remain in households as replacement appliances, are stored in cellars or garages or are supplied as second-hand appliances, which, from the point of view of resource conservation, is by no means to be criticised, but rather to be welcomed. In practice, the methodological basis for the determination of the survey objectives seems to be unreliable. In particular, the coverage target of 65 % of the average weight of EEE placed on the market in the last three years, which has been in force since 2019, appears to be very high in this context and will be difficult to achieve if the legal means of recording waste electrical equipment are taken into account consistently and exclusively. In particular, there is an urgent need for reform at European level in the formulation of collection rates.

feedback_text: Please make the financial management of the project simpler, based on the concept of a lump sum. However, do not predefine specific lump sums. There are no predefined lump sums to implement great ideas. If a great proposal can be implemented with 300K, it shouldn't have to artificially try to fit into 250K or 400K. Also, remove from the application forms any need to define the management structure and decision making (as the new Horizon Europe has done). Everybody by now knows how to handle a project and the sections on project management are more often than not reused from one proposal to the next.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Short-term PhDs
It is very positive that PhD students are considered as a separate target group. 
However, the administration of the programme is too bureaucratic for the individual participants, especially in the SMS track, as the administrative workload is the same as for semester-long stays (eg. IIAs, OLA). This does not meet the requirements for PhD students with the need of short term stays with shorter lead time. It would make sense to adapt the  Short-Term PhD programme and use staff mobility as a model.

BIPs 
It is very positive that this new programme scheme has been established. But here, too, the administrative burden for the individual participants is far too high for a stay of five days, because the guidelines do not distinguish between stays for a whole semester or only for five days.

BIP financial processing:
The amount of  70 EUR per day is too low. Rates that better take into account of the different levels of living costs in the various countries would seem reasonable. Furthermore, travel  support (according to distance bands) would be reasonable as well. The administration of additional support for student with fewer opportunities may be considered being too complex. The quite complex variations of possible funding are also difficult to communicate to students and therefore may appear intransparent.

Administration of Financial Support for Students
The small-scale, very complex financial processing with grant calculation on a daily basis is extremely resource-intensive. Even if the reasons in favour of daily based calculation of the grants are understandable, the administration workload is too high. 

Digitisation Agenda 
In principle, the digitisation of IIAs and OLAs is absolutely something HEIs have to strive for. However, the tools required for this are still not sufficiently functional. As the HEIs are currently facing huge problems to meet the deadlines for the digital extension of all IIAs until 1.1.2023, it is necessary for the Commission to provide guidance on how to deal with the situation through clear and timely communication. 
The difficulties in implementing the digitisation measures should be taken into account in the timelines for the further implementation steps of the digitisation agenda (nomination, transcript). Therefore, this second implementation phase (nomination, transcripts) should be suspended for the time being. 

For the time being, the functionalities of  the Beneficiary Module do not work sufficiently; especially for large HEIs that do not type in mobility data manually, but have to carry out extensive uploads, the functionality is still inadequate.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Response to call for evidence for review of the WEEE Directive (2012/19/EU). EPA (Ireland) is asking for the following to be considered in the review of the WEEE Directive: 1. Use of an EU Regulation rather than a Directive - This will ensure improved consistency in implementation and enforcement of WEEE legislation across the EU. 2. Introduction of a separate collection rate target for PV panel WEEE (and possibly other home-based energy generating EEE) The use of PV panels is increasing rapidly across the EU. The special nature of these products, particularly in relation to their life span, is affecting member states ability to meet the existing overall WEEE collection rate target. Therefore, there is a case for them to have a separate collection rate target. 3. Formal designation of PV panels as B2C EEE There is some doubt and confusion as to the categorisation of PV panels i.e., whether B2B or B2C EEE. This doubt is being exploited by some actors in the sector. Therefore, a formal designation of the panels as B2C EEE would remove this doubt and would improve the ability of environmental authorities to enforce EPR obligations on actors in the PV panel sector. 4. Introduction of a requirement for member states to prepare national plans for management of PV panel WEEE Given various factors, including life span, numbers of products going on the market and availability of guaranteed and sufficient funding to manage them at end-of-life, member states should be required to develop national plans to manage PV panel WEEE. 5. Introduction of a separate collection rate target for B2B WEEE Similar to PV panels, the long-life span (and other characteristics) of B2B EEE is affecting member states ability to meet the existing overall WEEE collection rate target. Therefore, there is a case for B2B WEEE to have a separate collection rate target. 6. Introduction of a requirement to provide take back facilities specifically for B2B WEEE Due to a range of complexities associated with B2B WEEE, the take back of B2B WEEE from users by B2B producers is proving difficult. This is affecting member states ability to meet the existing overall WEEE collection rate target. There would be benefits in requiring B2B producers/local authorities/PROs to provide a national network of take back facilities specifically for B2B WEEE. These facilities would be separate from normal take back facilities for B2C WEEE by households. 7. Prohibition of option for self-compliance by producers with WEEE legislation Self-compliance can facilitate the use of loopholes and weaknesses in EPR legislation and so can be difficult to enforce.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Pharmaceutical Students’ Association (EPSA) is an umbrella organisation representing around 100,000 students in 35 European countries. EPSA welcomes the Erasmus+ as the EU’s programme to support education, training, youth and sport in Europe but calls for attention regarding the promotion and unification of learning mobility opportunities and Operating Funding.

The mobility of students is one of the pivotal points of social and economic development in Europe, creating opportunities for personal and professional growth, promoting intercultural dialogue, developing international cooperation between individuals and institutions, enhancing the quality of higher education and research, and giving substance to the European dimension. 

Mobility and exchange programmes are determinant to achieve the previously mentioned purposes, as it is the example of the Erasmus+ programme. Most pharmaceutical students consider their knowledge about the Erasmus+ programme to be insufficient or medium which may result from insufficient information available at a local level or information not being presented efficiently. They have admitted to lack knowledge on scholarship possibilities that are provided for Erasmus+ exchanges. EPSA concludes that there is room for improvement in numerous aspects regarding mobility, including better information for both students and universities’ staff.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: 1) To what extent was the implementation of the WEEE Directive successful and which were the main problems and the main challenges and why? One of the key Challenges in implementing the WEEE Directive is obtaining clarity in assigning products to the legislation and Product Category, e.g. Car Keys within the legislation. This challenge is in part due to the wording of Art. 2 (4) c which references equipment which is specifically designed, and is to be installed, as part of another type of equipment and therefore introduces uncertainty when assessing equipment which is connected to or intended solely to interact with (physically or wirelessly), but is not installed into other equipment (e.g. vehicle remote key fobs, charging cables / boxes for electric vehicles etc.). This is further hindered by the differing interpretations from Member States. Austria for example considers Car Keys in scope of WEEE even though the EU Commision clarified with the legal service that all components incl. car keys - which are specifically designed for the vehicle - fall under the ELV Directive. Volkswagen expects the implementation of the scope to be consistent Europe-wide and to be clearly communicated so that there is clear boundaries when assessing what legislation applies to specific products. The FAQ Guidance document issued by the EU COM provides useful assistance, including with the matter above, however it is now somewhat dated and has not kept pace with industry developments, e.g. (products accompanying) electrified transport. Therefore Volkswagen suggest that issuance of updated Guidance would further aid Industry and address any problems which still remain in implementing the requirements of the WEEE Directive. Take Back schemes are implemented and financed differently by individual Member States which leads to challenges in understanding the requirements and in implementing a solution for those companies with Europe-wide business. Volkswagen would therefore encourage a consistent approach from Member States, possibly stipulated by EU legislation, with aligned implementation. 2) Did the EU intervention make a difference? VW AG considers that the WEEE Directive has been successful in implementing EPR take-back schemes for EEE. 3) Is the intervention still relevant given developments since its entry into force? VW AG considers that the WEEE Directive remains relevant but that there is now considerable overlap of its goals with other pieces of legislation (e.g. Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation, Battery Regulation) as well as remaining uncertainties (See above). The EU COM should endeavor to avoid overlapping legislative requirements and ensure that clear boundaries exist between product specific legislation.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
type_of_act: Communication concerning the position of the Council
text_raw: The Detergents Regulation should remain a stand-alone Regulation. It is a very well-established sector specific scheme for detergents, well implemented by companies and accepted by the authorities. We therefore prefer an amendment or minor amendments to the Detergents Regulation and no merger with other legal matters. Any elements of the chemicals strategy for sustainability should be integrated, if necessary, as part of such a revision. We see room for improvement in the following aspects: — optimisation of the interface between the medical fiche and CLP, Annex VIII (PCN); — optimisation of the interface with biocidal product law in the labelling of preservatives; — Simplifying labelling and increasing the use of digital solutions.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The EU needs a common position on how to make producer responsibility open to online shops delivering goods to Member States. Different ideas have been taken in this respect. One solution would be for the European Commission to send a demanding notification letter to all online shops operating in the EU and to online shops operating outside the EU that sell electrical appliances and batteries in EU countries. For example, under the WEEE Directive, all importers of electrical and electronic equipment and batteries and accumulators in the EU’s country of destination where they sell products must ensure compliance with producer responsibility obligations, including online shops. If this requirement is not met, the European Commission proposes to ban the operation of non-compliant online shops in EU countries. Stronger action than before is due to the achievement of the green transition objectives and the enhancement of the recycled material resource.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: ZERO - Associação Sistema Terrestre Sustentável is a Portuguese Environmental NGO working on various issues, among them sustainable circular economic models and waste management strategies. As such, we welcome this call for evidence for evaluating the WEEE Directive, supporting the urgent need to revise it extensively and align with its base principles of protecting the environment and human health. From this decade of lessons, the evidence is clear the EU initiative lacks enforcement strength, adaptability to an ever-changing global market and accountability at all levels of the management structure. As such, it fails to support the progress towards circular economy and environmentally sound waste management for electrical and electronic equipment. Not only the quantifiable goals were not achieved by most of the state members, namely the 65% target set for the collection of WEEE, but also the astounding near 50% growth of EEE placed in the EU market in the last years denounces that any strategy in place to prevent e-waste, by stimulating recovery, re-use and circular streams, have failed. For the upcoming and much-needed revision of the WEEE Directive, ZERO would like to make the following recommendations: The revision must bring the Directive up to date, in line with current EU legislation, and consistently applied across all state members. A clear focus on waste prevention and preparing for reuse targets of WEEE, autonomous from the recycling must be a priority. Only then we will have WEEE collection targets that disrupt current e-waste management but also have an effect upstream, directly linked to the aim of preventing EEE production. Overall enforceability, transparency and accountability must be enhanced throughout the entire process of e-waste management. In the age of digital platforms and blockchain methodologies, all records of producers, distributors and carriers must be readily verifiable and accessible to tackle illegal, non-compliant and evasive players - such as the very illusive online market. The Directive should reflect that the core responsibility for any product placed in the EU market must fall to the producer. This would be attainable by the reinforcement of the Extended Producer Responsibility that currently lacks strength and enforceability, being circumvented and deflected with ease or not applied at all, as experienced on the online market. The WEEE Regulation should align with the new Battery Regulation. WEEE legislation should make sure that all waste portable batteries collected undergo preparation for reuse, preparation for repurposing or a recycling process. Minimum common part design requirements should be applied to facilitate these processes. Also the WEEE recovery target for photovoltaics is based on weight and reached with glass and aluminium (90% of PV module). A more focused approach should help to recover metals, especially CRMs such as copper, electrical connections (incl. lead soldering) and polymers (incl. the backsheet containing fluorinated chemicals). Solar thermal and inverters should also be in scope. Strategic horizontal levelling requirements for the treatment, collection and re-use of all WEEE, such as: compulsory deposit return schemes that engage downstream to secure upstream liability; the adoption of standards, such as CENELEC, as mandatory to ensure proper treatment; obligation for disclosure of product design and information, so not to compromise its repair and re-use; and to consider all e-waste, by default, to contain hazardous substances or POPs unless proved otherwise, and not the other way around, ensuring safe handling, treatment and monitoring throughout the management process. The WEEE Directive has proved to be a necessary initiative but for the European Union to respect the Paris Agreement and follow through with the Green Deal road map, the utmost ambitious scenario must be adopted.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The new Directive should address the following topics: Collection targets Collection target defined as a % of POM seems not to be realistic, mainly in growing EEE markets and for some type of EEE (example: PV panels). The WEEE generated model looks better, but an easy and EU wide calculation methodology must be defined. The responsibility for achieving the targets must lie with the actors who perform collection (Local Authorities, Retailers) and the Government (who has the power of control): it cannot lie with the EEE Producers or with the PROs, who have no tool to increase collection All actors principle In a long and complex chain (Consumers, Retailers, Installers, Local Authorities, EEE Producers, PROs, Logistics and Treatment Suppliers), responsibilities must be clearly defined (who is responsible for what; who pay for what). It is essential: all treatment operators dealing with WEEE must declare to the Authorities the quantities of WEEE they receive every month / year (and where the WEEE come from). EEE Producers Register It would be very useful for the EEE Producers usually active in several Member States to have a single European Register; as an alternative, the National Registers should be interconnected On line sales A marketplace must be responsible to declare its own POM as well as the POM of all the retailers who sell through the marketplace. The online sellers must give the consumers the same take back services as the brick and mortar ones. Collection and take back solutions Collection must be made easy, as close as possible to the citizens / consumers. All the bureaucracy must be eliminated; for instance: in Italy, a retailer who makes WEEE collection must have an environmental permit (Albo Gestori Ambientali), record license plates of the vehicles and every single takeback, producing a large amount of paper which are useless, from any point of view. It must be possible to collect WEEE in any shop, in schools, in post offices without any administrative burden. Shipment of WEEE from citizens to (on line) retailers must be possible by post or standard courier services. Take back services for retailers must be possible without any special authorization, with the use of standard trucks. PROs Only EEE Producers should be shareholders of PROs: PROs are the tool with which EEE Producers shareholders fulfill their responsibility, not the way private entrepreneurs do their own business. PROs must be not for profit. Each PROs must publish the list of its shareholders and the contributions requested from them. A multi-PROs system under a clearing house (as in Italy) is the best option to assure a continuous improvement of efficiency and a nationwide service, preventing the PROs from doing any cherry picking of WEEE and avoiding any unnecessary increase in costs of access to WEEE. In case of a multi-PROs system, a minimum size (in terms of POM share) for a PRO would be beneficial. Financing A visible fee financing system is the best way to increase consumers awareness on the WEEE issue: it means that the fee paid by the EEE Producer to its PRO must be the same as the one paid by the retailer to the EEE Producer as well as the same paid by the Consumer to the retailer. Fee eco-modulation is a complex system of little use. It requires simple criteria, easy to measure and verify, and valid throughout Europe; even if this were possible, the fee reduction would be very small compared to the product price: this would vanish the effect of the eco-modulation. Treatment The legislation must assure a levelled playing field across Europe. It means that: treatment standards must be mandatory for all the operators dealing with WEEE. There must be a common conformity verification process (such as WEEELabex) and a pool of trained auditors. Enforcement In order to track all the flows and stop the parallel market a higher level of enforcement is required controlling car shredders, ports, scrap dealers

The WEEE generated model looks better, but an easy and EU wide calculation methodology must be defined. The responsibility for achieving the targets must lie with the actors who perform collection (Local Authorities, Retailers) and the Government (who has the power of control): it cannot lie with the EEE Producers or with the PROs, who have no tool to increase collection All actors principle In a long and complex chain (Consumers, Retailers, Installers, Local Authorities, EEE Producers, PROs, Logistics and Treatment Suppliers), responsibilities must be clearly defined (who is responsible for what; who pay for what). It is essential: all treatment operators dealing with WEEE must declare to the Authorities the quantities of WEEE they receive every month / year (and where the WEEE come from). EEE Producers Register It would be very useful for the EEE Producers usually active in several Member States to have a single European Register; as an alternative, the National Registers should be interconnected On line sales A marketplace must be responsible to declare its own POM as well as the POM of all the retailers who sell through the marketplace. The online sellers must give the consumers the same take back services as the brick and mortar ones. Collection and take back solutions Collection must be made easy, as close as possible to the citizens / consumers. All the bureaucracy must be eliminated; for instance: in Italy, a retailer who makes WEEE collection must have an environmental permit (Albo Gestori Ambientali), record license plates of the vehicles and every single takeback, producing a large amount of paper which are useless, from any point of view. It must be possible to collect WEEE in any shop, in schools, in post offices without any administrative burden. Shipment of WEEE from citizens to (on line) retailers must be possible by post or standard courier services. Take back services for retailers must be possible without any special authorization, with the use of standard trucks. PROs Only EEE Producers should be shareholders of PROs: PROs are the tool with which EEE Producers shareholders fulfill their responsibility, not the way private entrepreneurs do their own business. PROs must be not for profit. Each PROs must publish the list of its shareholders and the contributions requested from them. A multi-PROs system under a clearing house (as in Italy) is the best option to assure a continuous improvement of efficiency and a nationwide service, preventing the PROs from doing any cherry picking of WEEE and avoiding any unnecessary increase in costs of access to WEEE. In case of a multi-PROs system, a minimum size (in terms of POM share) for a PRO would be beneficial. Financing A visible fee financing system is the best way to increase consumers awareness on the WEEE issue: it means that the fee paid by the EEE Producer to its PRO must be the same as the one paid by the retailer to the EEE Producer as well as the same paid by the Consumer to the retailer. Fee eco-modulation is a complex system of little use. It requires simple criteria, easy to measure and verify, and valid throughout Europe; even if this were possible, the fee reduction would be very small compared to the product price: this would vanish the effect of the eco-modulation. Treatment The legislation must assure a levelled playing field across Europe. It means that: treatment standards must be mandatory for all the operators dealing with WEEE. There must be a common conformity verification process (such as WEEELabex) and a pool of trained auditors. Enforcement In order to track all the flows and stop the parallel market a higher level of enforcement is required controlling car shredders, ports, scrap dealers

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The Downstream Users of Chemicals Co-ordination group (DUCC), representing formulators of chemical mixtures for professional and industrial users as well as for consumers, welcomes the opportunity to comment on the inception impact assessment of the Detergents Regulation.

• The Detergents Regulation No 648/2004 is focussed for cleaning products, and its implementation by the Detergents and Maintenance products sector has been a success. It was stated in the Commission Staff Working Document on the evaluation of the Regulation, that the Detergent Regulation has achieved its aims to a large extent.  

• There are however some areas of potential improvement, in particular with regards to improving the coherence of the regulatory framework for detergents by addressing the overlaps and inconsistencies with the REACH, CLP and Biocidal Products Regulations. This is especially true of labelling requirements and solving the duplication and inconsistency between regulations is an important priority. DUCC supports the study being carried out by Commission on simplification of the labelling requirements for chemicals and the use of e-labelling. We refer to the comments made on this topic.

• It is crucial that the revision of the Detergents Regulation is taken in context of the full regulatory framework to ensure there will be no discrepancies and duplications between regulations in future.

The Detergents Regulation remains fit for purpose and we do not see the value of moving the Regulation under the New Legislative framework (NLF). With regards to setting product standards, this would impose a significant burden for industry without any benefits for the Regulation. Also, considering discrepancies between the NLF and other regulations applicable to Detergents is important. 

Regarding the proposal for implementing ingredient restrictions under the Detergents Regulation, DUCC highlights that ingredients used in Detergents must already undergo a risk assessment through the REACH regulation and there are already tools in REACH for the restriction of ingredients in products. In addition, information on detergents is communicated to consumers via labelling requirements of CLP. DUCC, along with its members, has a long history of supporting the implementation of REACH and CLP regulation . This regulatory framework is also under discussion and will be revised under the Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability. The new requirements will also need to be considered to avoid duplication or discrepancy in the future.


*DUCC’s membership represents several important industry sectors, ranging from cosmetics and detergents to aerosols, paints, inks, toners, pressroom chemicals, adhesives and sealants, construction chemicals, fragrances, disinfectants, lubricants and chemical distributors industries. Altogether, their membership comprises more than 9.000 companies across the respective sectors in Europe, the vast majority being SMEs. The calculated turnover of these companies is more than 215 billion euros in Europe. (www.ducc.eu)*

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The market is flooded with electronics and the trend looks likely to continue. In the context of global growth and increasing purchasing power, all kinds of electronics and electronic products are found all over the world, wherever electricity is available. Everyone wants to share the opportunities offered by electronic products and enjoy their capabilities. The advantage has brought new challenges, not only in the development of products but also in their removal when they're no longer used. When the Commission now further develops and fine-tunes the initiative of the 2012 Directive on waste from electrical and electronic equipment Ragn-Sells wants the commission to inquire into the possibility to: Further investigate the conditions for increased reuse, possibly through common guidelines or legislation across the EU. Investigate the illegal flows of WEEE. We know that these are significant in volume and value, but a survey of how big they are, and how the legislation can reduce them, will be important for future increased collection and recycling/reuse.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ programme
The 7-year project funding model now in place is really workable. Over a long period, it is possible to focus on developing activities without burdensome application processes. 

Mobility Tool on the internet was easy to use and work well. With the current BEN system, there is some initial canvas, and in particular the slowness of the programme has been a problem. From time to time, the programme does not seem to be accessible at all and will have to wait for a very long period of time before the programme opens. A good innovation is that all EU portals can be accessed with the same login. 

There is still much less to learn, but a small step is moving towards a more international world.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In brief:
Comenius was the best programme!
Erasmus+ has worsened from programme generation to generation.
Worst novelty: No longer cooperation between vocational and general education schools.
Small-Scale projects are extremely burdensome in terms of the amount of work involved in submitting applications — and only for 2 partners instead of 5 or more partners.
The BiBB Agency does not care me as well as the PAD.
All the changes have only worsened the working conditions for me as project coordinator!
I had to separate myself from long-standing excellent partners! Contacts between schools, teaching staff and SuS and Azubis have been destroyed. 


In detail:
Compared to the Comenius programme, the conditions of the Erasmus+ programme have constantly deteriorated over the course of the “new editions”. For me as a teacher, Comenius was ideal in terms of effort and benefit! The financial support was fully sufficient for the exchange. The Erasmus+ application was much more burdensome. The number of partners was then reduced. In order not to discriminate, I have dissolved my entire long-standing partner group and looked for new partners again. Now I have to separate myself from them too! 

An absolute catastrophe was precisely this new rule that, as a vocational school, we can now only work with other vocational schools, but no longer with secondary schools! At the same time, we offer a diploma from a school-leaving diploma and a university of applied sciences. The general approach was completely negated. In particular, the cross-curricular and cross-curricular horizons, such as our themes of migration, young entrepreneurship, thermal baths and wine, made the projects so interesting for students, Azubis and teachers! The motivation for my coordinating colleague and for me, now only projects with an economic background and with much younger and English-language students/addresses (only Austria still has the dual system), has suffered very much. What is the advantage of my Azubis, who are around the 20, very well speaking English, with cooperation with students aged 16-17? Bearing in mind that in southern European countries 80 % of school leavers and only 20 % of a year go to vocational training, the level can then be reduced. We had guests from a French vocational school here, who were unable to understand neither the project tasks nor the questions of their group members. This was extremely disappointing for both sides. 
For the Small Scale Projects, a huge effort has to be made in the process of submitting an application — for only two partners, instead of the previous six or nine partners. Why? Why are there no longer normal exchange projects? We are accredited as an organisation? 

I have also been separated from my long-standing, highly competent and committed adviser at the PAD and must now manage everything through the staff of BiBB. They are primarily responsible for the professional training profession, but I teach primarily traders — usually with a baccalaureate or specialist — who are not freed by companies to work abroad. On the other hand, a week-long exchange was approved without problems.

Overall, I find the development of the Erasmus+ programme to be absolutely negative. For me, as an applicant teacher, everything has worsened from one programme to another in terms of conditions! 

I want to go back to the old Comenius programme!

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Applia Polska is the only common representation of producers and importers of home appliances in Poland. As part of the economic self-government, we have been actively involved in the legislative process since 2006. Our members are responsible for organizing the collection and financing about 75% of the WEEE market in Poland. We are presenting the below country specific comments in addition to APPLiA Home Appliance Europe general comments. Competition and free market In the situation when only producers are responsible for the organization and financing WEEE market, there is a risk of dominant position of other market players. Retailers, recyclers and municipalities in many cases have strong and growing position on their markets. Commercial and business concentration is intensifying. In case of retailers, two or three companies (traditional or e-commerce sellers) already have hundreds of thousands of tons of e-waste in their hands. The stream of e-waste collection is being used as a tool in negotiations between retailers and producers. Very few recyclers manage thousands of collection points for one-fourth of the country. Natural territorial monopoly of the municipalities also does not support free flow on the goods. All above-described situations limit free competition and causes unjustified growth of the prices of the services. WEEE law puts manufacturers in difficult situation where the demand is artificially defined by regulations and supply of e-waste is controlled by commercial players making speculative tricks. As the result of it, there are higher prices of finished goods for consumers and the position of manufacturers is weakening, especially the smaller ones. Scope The current law is not flexible enough. It does not consider the rapid changes of markets. Of course, the future law will also not foresee unpredictable changes, so it should be open to possible civilization and technological development. New regulations should help to avoid the turbulences and conflicts between different industries grouped in the same categories now. This present unfair law does not consider different lifespan of commodities and establishes joint responsibility for many industries what is not possible to manage. Grey zone When legal regulations are far from market realities (i.e. actual targets based on 65% of POM higher than WEEE generated), the grey zone immediately appears. Simple solution is to increase the transparency of the markets. Local authorities should be obliged to publish aggregated and non-sensitive data immediately. In the digital age, the reports, and statistics should be available online, not with one- or two-year delay. It is the simplest and free way to fight with grey zone and free riders. All actors of the market would use this knowledge to strengthen the competition and economic self-government would indicate possible business abnormalities. It is extremely important to consider the ban of combining various activities of the companies within one capital group, responsible for collecting, processing and recyclers, which certify their obligations at different stages of the cycle. Another area that should be subject to great control is intercontinental trade and the sales of goods by e-commerce companies and internet platforms putting high amounts of products on the market. Definition of the targets The obligations regarding WEEE collection should be verified. They do not fit into changing lifetime of the products, non-registered flow of WEEE and changes among sectors and industries. High and unrealistic targets combined with mixed product categories cause unnormal turbulences on the market. The best are examples of the market of photovoltaic panels and heat pumps where the waste has not appeared yet (!), but at the same time the direct obligations of producers and importers are already in force. As an example, huge artificial demand in categories 1 and specially 4 has been created and is growing every year

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: ECOTIC Association feedback: We believe that a new WEEE Directive or Regulation should take into account the following: 1. EEE design should be pushed for circular economy principles. Durability, reparability, and also high recycled material content should be enhanced. Prepare for reuse operations should be maximum encouraged. Fractions with critical materials should have a control flow to help EU supply chains. 2. Realistic targets we have to admit that the rate of 65% from the average of the three previous years is not fit for the purpose as it was not reached by any EU country (except the ones with verry poor statistics). It is against the circular economy principles that requires long lasting products and push for reuse and it is quite independent from WEEE generated figures (for example with the new product type: photovoltaic panels, scooters and e-bikes). The target of 65% might be taken into account only if it is applied to the average of quantities of EEE put on the market for last 10 previous years. The option with 85% from WEEE generated would be more suitable, with a change in algorithm and proper data in. For example, at this point, the results are independent of current year EEE put on the market figures and we have seen that a strong decrease of the market affect also WEEE available for collection as the rate of replacement for moral depletion is going down. Historical data from WEEE tool, coming from international trade statistics, is far from reality at least in Romania. 3. State collection targets should not be fully transferred to producers. Producers do not have direct access to WEEE and cannot make all actors responsible and to limit the informal flows and exports. In the countries where this is happening (including Romania), and with a pressure with fees for unreached target (0.8 EUR/kg in Romania), we see strong competition for WEEE quantities with the effect that WEEE costs are rising due to speculation, unrelated to the real cost for WEEE. Producers should be responsible for financing all WEEE arising and push for the development of the collection infrastructure and awareness campaigns to strengthen formal collection. 4. Competition for WEEE should not exist between producers (eg PROs). A Clearing House should be mandatory if there is more than one PRO in the country, to make sure that the WEEE managing efforts are related to the market share thus eliminating unfair competition. The Clearing House should be also empowered to coordinate the market with all actors involvement to ensure permanent progress. 5. Mandatory involvement of local authorities with establishment of municipal collection points. We observe that mature collection systems with high collection rate rely on municipal collection points. So, countries that are lagging behind should be pushed for mandatory set up. All EU citizens should have access to proper disposal solutions in their communities. 6. Proper treatment should be enhanced through mandatory conformation to standards EN50625 and EN 50614 for all the recycling chain. Not meeting the standards criteria means not complying with the requirement of the present and future WEEE Directive (improper depollution and focus only on valuable materials). 7. In respect with EPR principles, only producers with no conflict of interest should own and control a PRO. The PROs owned by scrap dealers or other entities than producers are profit orientated with less focus on conformation. 8. Country data should be audited before being published on Eurostat and becoming EU official statistics. We observe very poor statistics in WEEE collection in some states with immature WEEE systems but with record collection rates close to 100%, compared with mature ones that are close to 50%-60%. ECOTIC Association is the main PRO in Romania, with 37% market share in 2021 and working for 600 producers. www.ecotic.ro Dragoș Călugăru, General Manager, Valentin Negoiță, President
