initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
1. The new Erasmus+ mobility accreditation for schools format enables a easier management of school exchanges. This is highly appreciated because I considered unfair that projects including only mobilities for school staff (KA101) were easier to plan and manage than projects including school pupils (KA219/229) in the past. 
School education coordinators carry out a lot of work for the glory in many schools, added to their usual huge workload. Practical arrangements needed for school pupils' mobilities need lots of checks, caution, meetings with families, replacement provisions for accompanying staff... that mean lots of extra work and effort.

2. The new KA120 format also enables to get rid of low-quality partners as soon as they are detected. In KA229, schools were forced to endure such partners up to the end of the project, and to add insult to injury, the coordinator school needed to chase them to be able to submit the final report. 
There are lots of issues that come from the management of such a project, since honest ignorance about the rules of Erasmus+ or key staff withdrawals/health issues to intentional disregard towards training or even profit-making intention by some schools. Some school leaders are not aware about the kind of engagement that Erasmus+ requires from a school, and the kind of professionals needed to carry out a project successfully. 

3. Whenever underage children are involved, cautions must be taken to a maximum level, as the possibility to cover children's temporary stay in a hotel with accompanying adult staff in case of unexpected issues in a hosting family (from sickness to overall unadequacy), also to buy group flights that allow the choice of seats or extra-large/two seats for oversized staff and pupils. Erasmus+ budget is very important to enable quality inclusion, so it should be updated as travel and hosting prices are on the rise, else only schools who can afford to cover money shortages will include exchanges of pupils.

4. National agencies should consider a compulsory training on Erasmus+ procedures for coordinators or school leaders as a requirement to apply. Lack of training is a pain for more experienced partners, that must carry out mentoring of newcomers, added to the usual workload.

5. Excellent projects and good practices deserve better recognition and promotion. 

6. Peripheral countries like Portugal, Spain or Turkey, featuring lower/longer train connections to the rest of Europe, need a special consideration related to low-carbon transportation. Some schools cannot afford to get their staff sent abroad for several days.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
We welcome the initiative of the European Commission and we agree with the overall aim of the initiative to enhance the protection of children through the revision of the Toy and Safety Directive.
 
- We support "the extension of the general risk-assessment approach to be extended to other most hazardous substances as endocrine disruptors or substances that are persistent and bioaccumulative" but we ask the Commission to specify the requirement. Are the extended requirements focused on all the substances classified under the CLP regulation or will a specific list of substances be defined ?
 
- On "allowing the setting of chemical limits values for any toys and not only for those intended for children under 36 months", as a manufacturer, we are already aligned in this direction. However, we ask the Commission to define different limit values to be used, taking into account the risk and its exposure. As an example, the exposure to the risk will be different in between an ingestion and a skin contact exposition.  

- On "establishing positive lists for certain substances such as colorants or preservatives", we welcome this initiative if it helps an effective application in a consistent way. 

- On "setting out requirements for the labelling of chemical composition of toys, including via digital labelling". We ask the Commission to specify the labelling requirements concerning the chemical composition of toys. Is the aim of this requirement to inform the customer of the presence of the substances (even if they are not dangerous), or of their dangerosity ? As an example of a mandatory indication of substances and/ or material that exceeds a certain rate or amount in the product because of their potential negative impact. 

- On "the digital product passport", it’s an opportunity if it permits the digitalization of product information and documentation, as the current EU declaration of conformity. Indeed, “physical” labelling and documentation is a challenge today because of the dilemma with the necessity to reduce packaging waste and at the same time the necessity to give more information to customers to ensure the sustainable management of a product. As a consequence, it will be a real advantage if it permits to remove some of the “physical” labelling on the product. Then, it is important to link the digital passport to other initiatives (nationals or europeans). Linking the initiatives and centralising all the information through a unique digital passport will make it more efficient on accessibility and transparency of the information for customers and will make it easier to implement for industries.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Our organisation has been involved as project leader on four Erasmus+ projects since 2017 and on a few other projects as a project partner. Our experience has been positive and I would certainly recommend participation in the programmes. The Erasmus+ programme is a major contributor to the continued development of learning and culture in the EU and it has a direct impact on the hundreds of thousands of individuals that are involved as organisers, participants etc. 
I would however like to put forward some concrete criticism as to how I believe the application process can be improved, particularly to encourage broader SME participation. Three issues need to be addressed, namely: 1. Segregation of project funding to public and private sector firms, particularly to incentivize SME firms and organisations; 2. Improving the quality of the evaluation process, and not necessarily by involving more evaluators; 3. Recognizing the value of time and resources that need to be invested in submitting proposals for funding and not wasting such efforts and resources.
The following are possible initiatives that would address these issues:-
1. In various EU countries, private sector educational institutions operate in a very biased environment in which public sector educational organisations have an unfair advantage over the private sector firms in getting access to EU funds. The funding of the public sector firms enables them to dedicate more time and resources to sourcing of project funding from programmes such as Erasmus+. Let there be a clear direction by EU policy makers on the proportion of funding that is to go towards each of the sectors. Erasmus+ needs to incentivize SMEs to get more involved, they bring creativity, innovation, resourcefulness and cost effectiveness to the programme.
2. The quality of the evaluation process needs to improve. It is not enough to invest in the training of the evaluators, this is a must. In addition, the project evaluation rubrics need to be more comprehensive explaining how points will be allocated in the process, so that there is no ambiguity as to what the applicant is to provide. It is very frustrating to have an application designed for brevity and with limitations on number of words etc, only to get repeated calls for more details from the evaluator. 
3. The appreciation of the time and resources required to develop a quality funding application needs to be recognised and efforts made by Erasmus+ not to waste such efforts. A two-step process can be introduced, through which there is a preliminary screening of applications to identify the most appropriate and deserving of funding. Those selected would then receive a lump sum funding to complete project proposals in full detail as required for the final evaluation process. 
Erasmus+ has set the bar and more organisations in the educational and cultural sectors should get involved. Europe needs to strengthen its European culture whilst recognizing and celebrating all the diversities in the national and regional environments. Erasmus+ has served to break down cultural barriers, to encourage cross-border collaboration and transfer of knowledge. Its very success to date, means that it has to plan for continued expansion of its operations to maintain the momentum.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Erasmus+ programme plays an essential role in fostering and sustaining European civil society and creates common opportunities and experience that enable us to work towards the commitment to fulfil out common European values. I have personally experienced the Erasmus+ programme as a student, and now am involved with it in the field of youth. The programme provides the resources and framework to support not only transnational, European-wide learning and cooperation but also innovation in terms of developing and implementing new tools to fulfil current needs and engage with new challenges in the field. 

As a beneficiary of the programme, my organisation is supported by professional staff at the level of the National Agency. Compared to some other funding streams, administrative aspects of the programme are relatively straight-forward and do not over burden micro-organisations like the one I work for. 

The increased budget for the period 2021-27 is most welcome and a forward-looking investment, considering the dividends the programme pays in terms of promoting the European Youth Goals and European Values - an added value is the positive light the programme shines on the EU amongst people in partner countries who participate in the programme. 

One aspect that I would suggest reflection and revision is the differing rates of support provided to experts, key staff and professionals within the framework of KA2. The disparities between rates for Germany and Poland, for example, do not reflect contemporary economic realities, on the one hand, and if fact serve to entrench structural inequalities between Europe, East and West, on the other. Here, investment into levelling up would put the programme at the forefront of a drive to iron out structural inequalities that a re a product of a previous era.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We have been involved in two ERASMUS projects of varying sizes. Our proposals for improvement:

1. Flat rates are good as they facilitate project reporting. However, many times the flat rates do not cover the costs incurred. With existing inflation, the standard rates should be increased.

2. A project application should not exceed 20 pages. More than that, we should make this happen:
— we grant aid to make a difference; because we want to make a difference, or
— we do not grant support for job creation for those seeking too much administration.

The administration of Leader projects in Sweden has yet to make improvements, but we still believe that we are well advanced on this work. Erasmus + should look at this for inspiration.

3. Improve the e-service when it is very difficult to navigate, including for the most experienced. This improvement will help to achieve the goals of the 2030 Agenda.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The WEEE Directive was a first step towards environmentally sound management of e-waste and a circular economy. However, since its adoption, the EUs use of electronics has increased exponentially (almost doubling), and therefore so must our action to manage the resulting e-waste. The current directive is no longer fit for purpose and the existing requirements for treatment, collection, re-use and repair of WEEE are outdated and inconsistently applied across EU member states. The EEB thus welcomes this opportunity to call for an extensive revision of the directive. The revision should focus on preventing waste, enabling a circular economy, and reducing the environmental footprint of the electrical and electronics sectors. Preventing as much EEE as possible from entering the waste stream through the encouragement of repair, re-use and preparing for re-use, should be the priority. 1.Set binding targets. Eurostat data shows that only three member states achieved the 65% WEEE collection target. Moreover, the system of setting collection targets based on EEE put on the market is disproportionate (with EEE placed on the EU market double whats collected) and does not sufficiently emphasise reuse and repair. As such, the methodology for setting collection targets needs to be revised, and binding targets for reuse and repair should be set instead. By extension, setting recycling targets based on collected WEEE should also be revised. Quantitative waste prevention targets for WEEE should thus be set as a basis. 2. Prioritise and finance repair and reuse. Once collected, repair, re-use and preparation for re-use must be facilitated through adapting requirements to such operators, ensuring to avoid damage to WEEE during collection, transport, and storage. Improving requirements on WEEE treatment, logistics and preparation for re-use, and introducing a regular review procedure should enable this. As a model for collection and treatment, the CENELEC standards should be made mandatory in all member states.Access to collected EEE should be provided for independent repairers and refurbishers, to facilitate them in their work. Repair and reuse activities could be funded through enhanced producer responsibility schemes through the introduction of modulated fees prioritising waste prevention by earmarking funds to support such work, as is the case in France. 3. Improve EPR schemes. To improve WEEE management, increased producer responsibility is necessary. Producers must be obliged to participate in nation-wide networks for WEEE return for which collection and reuse targets should become legally binding. Implementing a national coordination mechanism for PROs as mentioned in the Waste Framework Directive is also necessary. Online platforms have so far evaded their legal obligations on WEEE take back, and so should also be subject to full liability for third-country sellers. These marketplaces play a leading role in placing non-compliant EEE on the EU market, and so tightening regulation on these platforms is urgently needed. Finally, producers reporting responsibilities should be increased, and collection reports should be publicly available to increase transparency and accountability. This could further mitigate the risks of leakages by ensuring that WEEE is oriented towards accredited actors. Take-back schemes for all WEEE should also be introduced. 4. Align with other EU legislation This revision should align with product policy to counteract enforceability issues. Banning the destruction of unsold EEE and including requirements on product design and information (like design for disassembly, use of secondary materials in production) must be included in EU legislation to have an overall impact on reducing e-waste. Finally, we recommend that this Directive become a Regulation to harmonise and speed up implementation across the EU. Detailed information on these recommendations (developed with ECOS, DUH and RREUSE) is attached.

Once collected, repair, re-use and preparation for re-use must be facilitated through adapting requirements to such operators, ensuring to avoid damage to WEEE during collection, transport, and storage. Improving requirements on WEEE treatment, logistics and preparation for re-use, and introducing a regular review procedure should enable this. As a model for collection and treatment, the CENELEC standards should be made mandatory in all member states.Access to collected EEE should be provided for independent repairers and refurbishers, to facilitate them in their work. Repair and reuse activities could be funded through enhanced producer responsibility schemes through the introduction of modulated fees prioritising waste prevention by earmarking funds to support such work, as is the case in France. 3. Improve EPR schemes. To improve WEEE management, increased producer responsibility is necessary. Producers must be obliged to participate in nation-wide networks for WEEE return for which collection and reuse targets should become legally binding. Implementing a national coordination mechanism for PROs as mentioned in the Waste Framework Directive is also necessary. Online platforms have so far evaded their legal obligations on WEEE take back, and so should also be subject to full liability for third-country sellers. These marketplaces play a leading role in placing non-compliant EEE on the EU market, and so tightening regulation on these platforms is urgently needed. Finally, producers reporting responsibilities should be increased, and collection reports should be publicly available to increase transparency and accountability. This could further mitigate the risks of leakages by ensuring that WEEE is oriented towards accredited actors. Take-back schemes for all WEEE should also be introduced. 4. Align with other EU legislation This revision should align with product policy to counteract enforceability issues. Banning the destruction of unsold EEE and including requirements on product design and information (like design for disassembly, use of secondary materials in production) must be included in EU legislation to have an overall impact on reducing e-waste. Finally, we recommend that this Directive become a Regulation to harmonise and speed up implementation across the EU. Detailed information on these recommendations (developed with ECOS, DUH and RREUSE) is attached.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
text_raw: The University of Porto has been one of the Portuguese Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) most active in the context of Erasmus + and recognises opportunities for excellence for its academic community and for itself as an organisation. However, I would like to highlight some aspects in the context of financing the International Credit Mobility Action that deserve analysis and possible adjustment in order to maximise the impact of projects and simplify their management and implementation: A. whereas insurance is compulsory for participants, the OS should include an amount for insurance, as was the case in the past under the Erasmus Mundus Action 2 programme; b. We welcome the increase in the allocation of OS that brought this programme, since the previous figure was clearly insufficient for the needs identified (full payment of language course, integration activities, insurance, issuing of visas, stop-overs for visa applications, etc.), often forcing HEIs to allocate their own resources with great difficulty in order to ensure the quality of project implementation. We believe that with an increase in this heading, there is room for improvement in the quality of projects. In particular, an increase in the OS budget could make it possible to hold at least one plenary meeting with all the partner institutions of the project. This would be a very important opportunity for capacity building as it would facilitate the presentation of procedures, the applicable rules and the organisation of the details associated with the implementation of the project. At present, HEIs participating in several KA107 projects have very different procedures and different guidelines in different coordination, leading to confusion in the partner HEIs. Based on our previous experience, we believe that the ideal would be to organise a kick-off meeting at the beginning of the project and a final meeting to consolidate the results and implement their dissemination strategy; c. We consider that there should be a OS defined by the European Commission for LCI project partners — a lump sum similar to the previous Erasmus Mundus Action 2 programme — as this is one of the issues that causes the greatest divergence with partners in the Programme partner countries; D. Travel bands have considerable lags in real market values: e.g. travel band between Portugal and Algeria or Morocco (very small linear distance), the value of which is not compatible with the provision of flights, preventing the purchase of air travel within the maximum band values. We think it is important to reflect on the criterion for defining the travel value by separating it from the distance band between the two HEIs. e. There should be a visa heading (a unit value at least for IN students); f. At the level of budgetary management, we believe that it would be extremely beneficial to have full flexibility in the implementation of the envelopes, rather than by country, in order to facilitate the implementation of 100 % of the funding and, above all, to meet the needs identified in the meantime throughout the implementation of the project. Financial management based on respect for values per country is highly rigid and becomes extremely complex for many HEIs; g. The redistribution of the remaining budget between the national HEIs should be done by the National Agency no later than 6 months before the end of the project, in order to avoid unnecessary returns and to allow (for HEIs with capacity) the timely implementation of the remaining funds, not only by allocating new grants but also by allowing extensions of mobilities, which requires more advance notice.

feedback_text:
Dear Concern,

Greetings!

We are an NGO from India and we have participated in many  KA2 Capacity Building projects. We as an organization learned a lot and at the same time, we have seen the great outcomes of these projects for the socia economic development. Nowhere in the world, such cross-border opportunities are available. Personally my self as a person and responsible personnel for the organization it has changed my mind and as well as vision. The respect love and care we received from the ground level of society is tremendously positive. It has strong vibes to change the negativity in society. Bring education and global harmony together. Post pandemic we have seen that the participation in such projects has been removed for Indian organizations. When it was declared we thought it will for sure come back and even a couple of years later there are no signs that the inclusion of Indian organizations will be allowed. We really miss Erasmus+ and the greater impact on socio-economical developments. I and my team appeal to all the decision makers to reconsider our inclusion and develop among all EU member states' beneficiaries. Let us be part of 'Global Harmony'

Regards,

Kerron VAISHNAV

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
EHPA would like to thank the European Commission and the study team for the work already carried out and welcomes the opportunity given to send feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the energy labelling requirements for water heaters and storage tanks. 

Energy Label
EHPA believes that we should maintain the label as simple, clear, and understandable as possible for the end-user. All information on the label should be understandable by the consumers. Consequently, we recommend that all symbols should be described so that they are clearly understood. EHPA also believe that too much information could water down the value of the label for end-users. The QR code is a very easy bridge for customers to retrieve more detailed data when needed. 

Rescaling 
EHPA is supportive to the current process of re-designing the energy label. Within this process, we ask the European Commission and Member States to take into consideration the administrative and operative consequences which this procedure and changes create for the industry, market surveillance authorities and consumers. 
We would like to flag that it is necessary to provide a full 2-year transition period between the entry into force and the applicability of the new label. 

Energy Efficiency Classes
The label thresholds for higher classes are very ambitious for profile M and above and a very different approach is used for class width in the same tapping profile and between the different tapping profiles that we doubt could ensure a fair competition between technologies. 
EHPA has also two major concerns regarding the proposed energy efficiency classes for hot water storage tanks: 
- There is no provision for an empty class and therefore no incentive for hot water tank manufacturers to further improve the performance of their products. The best hot water tanks and PCM thermal stores on the market today achieve an energy label of A+ under the current Regulation, which is to be rescaled to A under the draft regulations. This means that there is no incentive to further improve the energy performance of hot water storage tanks. 
- The introduction of the multivalent tank correction mvc is even more detrimental to further promoting energy efficiency improvements in the hot water tank industry. Based on its current wording, the inclusion of the mvc is going to lead to an improvement by one label class for all hot water storage tanks with an indirect heating coil and a back-up heater on the market today. This means that the proposed factor will lead to a narrowed label gap between the current average of products on the market and the leading products, further disincentivising manufacturers with average performing products from investing in any further improvements in their energy efficiency. 

Product Information Sheet 
EHPA would like to remind that the design of the product information sheet should be up to the manufacturer.

Timing and implementation
EHPA recommends that there should be a period of at least two years between the publication and the implementation of the new rules. 

You may find our full position on Lot 2 on EHPA website :
https://www.ehpa.org/fileadmin/user_upload/20211115_EHPA_written_comments_on_Lot_2.pdf 

We remain at your disposal to discuss this matter with you in greater details.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
All in all, I am really satisfied with the Erasmus+ programme and the opportunities it creates. It has been really remarkable that we have been able to engage in youth exchanges with young people who would otherwise not have the same opportunities to participate in such international activities. I also feel that young people, coming from different backgrounds, experience from projects with a completely different culture and religion than their own and I believe that this broadens young people’s perspectives and understanding of their differences on the face and beyond. Europe has been well divided and nationalist parties have been on the rise in many countries. The confrontation in societies is strong and I believe that Erasmus+ will enable us to have a better Europe and contribute to reducing racism and confrontation. 
The search for funding itself remains truly heavy and the slowness and waiting for decisions, in particular the additional challenges in the COVID-19 era, have made the implementation of projects with limited resources challenging. As an employee, you wonder whether energy and distribution will be enough to make projects in the future. Although young people are much involved in the whole process and their ideas come from them, the implementation of the project will require a lot of support and paperwork. Apparently, when the new project changed, the application form was intended to be slightly lighter, but this has hardly been noticed. However, in essence, all the new changes overwhelmed and, in my view, the new practices and distributions of aid amounts became more complex. I am still of the opinion that project forms are few cans and that it is really challenging for young people to understand the questions. It would be important to have more and more common language rather than official languages. I know that many countries in Europe use projects on the grid and young people are hardly involved in project design. Perhaps this would be useful at EU level to address and harmonise practices.  
However, it is really important that we have such a chance to get support for internationalism and I really hope that it will continue to be.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Our school has developed its Erasmus+ mobilities and meets its needs providing meaning to its educational work. The mobilities have helped our students -  participants in improving their human skills as well as their vocational skills, and at the same time forming a deeper connection with local people and business in Europe.
It is important that in the recent project, individuals with social, economical and other issues have been provided with the opportunity to participate in mobilities given the enhanced inclusion and diversity measures taken in the program of 2021-2027. Subsequently, we believe that this inclusion will assist with the cultivation of common European thinking on the inclusion and integration of weak social groups.
Moreover, we welcome the new simplifications in various procedures of application for programs, reporting, use of the allocated amounts for mobilities as well as simplicity in the use of the beneficiary software. 
Furthermore, we must applause the handling of unforeseen events such as the COVID-19 pandemic by the European Committee where flexibility and alternatives were given. 
Unfortunately, we cannot provide feedback on the program's contribution to mainstreaming climate action, as we live and operate from an island, thus the travel limitations cannot be overcome. Therefore, our only means of travel is the airplane. 
Lastly, we would like to comment that the Erasmus+ programs are improving and provide a variety of choices, thus lay the foundations for a great experience that will last a lifetime for all participants.

The umbrella organisation of the Swiss higher education institutions, Swissuniversities, appreciates the opportunity to provide evidence on the upcoming final evaluation of the last Erasmus+ programme generation (2014-2020) and the mid-term evaluation of the current Erasmus+ programme. We would recommend the following points to be included in the evaluations:
- Analysis of the contribution and added value of non-associated countries (partner countries) to the various actions of Erasmus+.
- Evaluation of how the participation of non-associated countries (partner countries) changed over the course of the programmes and what measures were successful in increasing their participation
- Analysis of the participation of higher education institutions from non-associated countries (partner countries) in the Erasmus+ flagship programmes, such as the European Universities initiative, Centres of Vocational Excellence, and others.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
My Erasmus+ stay was at the University of Aberdeen for two days in the field of Student Counselling/Student Support. The colleagues there have considered a very balanced and good programme for me on these two days. On the first day, there was an interview with the head of the Student Counselling Services, followed by another employee in the Counselling who told me in more detail about a project for start-up students that she was currently developing. After lunch, there were still long discussions with the Head of the Disability Office and with the Deputy Director of People. 

On the second day, I was able to attend a team intervision and a detailed case meeting as well as a team meeting. 
From this technical exchange, I was able to bring with me some insights for our service at the Vienna University of Economics (WU). 
The Student Counselling Service has existed in Aberdeen for 11 years, while at the WU for only three years. The Aberdeen team consists of 11 Counselling staff (for comparison: the Student Counselling Programme at the WU has three members of staff) and sits in its own house at the campus with its own waiting area, one office for the secretariat and seven treatment rooms set up individually and very well. All 11 coaches have a different professional or professional background. The team is therefore very interdisciplinary. It is also a very international team with cooperation from the UK, Venezuela, Poland and Malaysia. The Counselling Team is made up of women only. This broad focus of the team in all respects will allow a large number of students to be well accompanied. 

My wide range of findings have also been taken up with great interest by my rest of the team on the ET, as we are very motivated to continuously develop our service and learn from others to do so. In particular, the administration of appointments and appointments, the Student Counselling Services, provided useful input. 

As regards support for students with physical and psychological impairments, it has once again been shown that universities in the UK have a huge advantage here. In Aberdeen, if students with disabilities are mainly supported by compensation for disadvantages during examinations, there are many opportunities for support, which are much more involved in the day-to-day study (e.g. teaching): Presentations for students are available for download 40 hours before a course, each course is included and published with subtitles. There are ‘pre-readers’ and ‘note-takers’, as well as laboratory assistants, and it is always anticipated that students with disabilities may be present at each course. In doing so, they do not have to seek support individually. In addition, the university is already informed of possible impairments when applying, so that support can be offered from the outset. 

Furthermore, the impact of Brexit on students and the university was very interesting for me. As a result of Brexit, the costs for students from EU countries have risen many times and there is a drop in the number of EU students. 

I definitely strengthened my professional network through this stay. With the Student Counselling Services team, there is a prospect of future, regular, on-line exchanges.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Final evaluation E+ 2014-2020:
Application is always very extensive but feasible. All tools have worked, even though it would have been desirable that the various databases (Mobility Tool, OLS, Europass, etc.) would have been merged into one database in order to avoid double and triple entries.

Interim evaluation E+ 2021-2027:
Application for accreditation is very extensive and unfortunate, poorly usable, in order to define the E+ objectives with the corresponding measures, activities and indicators. The whole is then extremely unclear. Why there is no uploading (or free format) document that defines and feeds the objectives, etc. I doubt that the current format “passes through” experts. 

It is good that the institutions are (willing to) internationalise overall. As a result, in our Chamber of Crafts, the subject of ‘International Vocational Training/International Education Projects’ has become more prominent and the issue is taken seriously and represented by management. 

In fact, everything should become simpler... However, that is not the case. Although the call for funds does not consist of many pages, the fact that no individual mobility with the destinations and countries of destination is recorded in the application or call for funds means that each project promoter uses its own Excel table to generate figures. Then promised and money...many too much money... and again we calculated, by means of an Excel table, how much we would actually need. To return money or not to get such sums at all, but this has not yet been done.

Beneficiary Tool has only recently worked and only rudimentary. Aid amounts are calculated incorrectly (i.e. again an Excel table), TNs appear double and triple for infinite reasons and should not be deleted. TN reports have a button for the language, but there is only English. There is no translation into German, even on the NA-BIBB page. Why not? Our TNs (e.g. craftsazubis) are in any case difficult to understand (in German). We don’t want to talk about English at all.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We are involved in several KA1 learning mobilities as accredited consortium coordinator. Content wise the E+ programme offers a lot of new opportunities for the participants creating better and more modern education.
The Erasmus program however is very bureaucratic, a load of useless and unnecessary paper work, Agreements, Annexes. It has a very slow and badly functioning IT-support. The EU does not offer support for their failing IT-systems.
The new Beneficiary Module is a real disaster and a shame for the EU Commission, it just doesn't work. Participants and coordinators are obliged to use a non-functioning system, but will be assessed later on by the NA's and/or EU if they used the Beneficiary Module well. A contradiction in terminis.
Summarized: The new Erasmus program is  content wise OK, too much useless paperwork and a non-functioning IT-system (Beneficiary Module)
This means 1 star on 5.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Disability Forum represents more then 100 million persons with disabilities. As organisation we have and currently participate in Erasmus+ Project, but the feedback comes also from our members. We have not conducted any structure research, however, what our members and ourselves experienced when it comes to the programme are the following:
Accessibility:  improvements have been done on the Commissions website and an easy to read leaflet has been published, however still not all fims are accessible and many issues arises when it comes to the accessibility of the annexes organisations need to provide, the excell sheets are not inaccessible only but also problematic for persons without disability to use them.
Visibility of inclusion mechanisms for persons with disabilities: There are many measures put in place to support inclusion of persons with fewer opportunities within the Erasmus+ Programme. These measures are still not known by the beneficiaries not doing projects with disadvantaged groups and we receive complaints from young people who were not able to participate in projects because the costs linked to their disabilities were not covered such as: the possibility to take a personal assistant, ann accessibility service, an inaccessible location to name only a few of them. To avoid such discriminatory happenings the possibility to request these costs after a project was submitted and approved could be a solution, or including in the form above the budget a few words indicating that organisations can request for budget to cover costs to support the inclusion of persons with fewer opportunities may decrease the burden upon participants with disabilities or organisations and facilitate the participation with accessibility requirements. 
Lack of data on participants with disabilities: The umbrella term to nominate person subject to a discrimination people with fewer opportunities does not meet the requirements of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities in providing disagregated data on disability. However the application process requires information about participants and beneficiaries must indicate when persons with disabilities participate. This information does not allow disagregated data on disability type and how inclusion is put in place. More indicators in the evaluation process would be helpful to fill in  this  lack. Indicators such as: Did you offer accessibility services? Which ones,  Did you display on your disemination of your activities that they are open for persons with disabilities? The National agencies should also use the information the gather on participans with disabilities and publish their data also on their channels to give more visibility.
Online activities: Blended mobilities and the pandemic situation brought us to choosing for remote activities and online events. Unfortunately the online activities are not budgeted adequately, the preparation time and organisation of an online or remote activity is not financially more advantageous to diminish the budget for this. Travel costs are nonexistent, but these activities need a more thorough preparation, thus more time, accessibility services such as speach to text  sign language interpretation, sometimes personal support are needed to allow an equal and meaningful participation of persons with disabilities. Covering the licenses integrally or partly of conferencing systems is necessary an upgraded version when participants with disabilities are present is necessary as simple accounts may not give access to all features of the programmes that facilitates an accessible participation. 
Person with disabilities in the pool of evaluators: National Agencies should include persons with fewer opportunities, thus with disabilities too in their evaluator pools as these ones are best to evaluate whether an application is indeed meeting the inclusion ccriteria set up by the programme, thus becoming inclusive inside and outside.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Problems encountered in setting up 2021/27:
— implementation of EWP via MOVEON
— dissemination of the Erasmus Charter to the general public, which is based solely on the Department for Academic International Relations. A teaching world with little awareness.
— Measurement of the 20 % outside the EU, which criteria for choosing extra-Community destinations
— the difficult implementation of the reciprocity of certain mobility (e.g.: Balkan, turquy, medium, etc.)
— turnover of Erasmus referrals in the National Agency
— difficult to access mobility to the UK since Brexit
Positive points: very useful webinars

The objectives of the Charter are ambitious and supportive, but operational implementation is complex. The deployment of new non-operational platforms creates an overload of work and generates permanent stress. The ERASMUS Dashboard platforms, Beneficiary Module, could be grouped into a single inter-operable platform.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
Independent Retail Europe considers Directive 2009/48/EC on the safety of toys (Toy Safety Directive / TSD) generally useful and welcomes the clear obligations for distributors. This enables retailers – and especially SMEs – to apply the regulation more efficiently and with legal certainty. 
We do however have concerns about two particular aspects of the Toy Safety Directive: The lack of clarity as regards ‘isolated cases’ as well as the legal coherence regarding the obligations of distributors with the product safety acquis currently under revision.

So-called ‘isolated cases’ refer to particular situations where only one single product or a very limited number of products is un-safe. In such a situation, a distributor is unable to determine with certainty whether a problem with a product is unique to that one product or caused by an inappropriate usage of the product by the consumer, or if the whole production line of the product is faulty. 
Unclear rules on isolated cases can cause conflicts in the supply chain: The manufacturer may then seek damages from the distributor who made an unnecessary notification to the relevant competent authority, thereby also harming the retailer and souring the business relationship.
Diverging reporting obligations within the EU Product Safety legislation will lead to confusion among economic operators and high implementation costs. This will be particularly burdensome for SMEs.
In the light of the ongoing revisions of several legal texts of the product safety acquis, the concept of isolated cases and provisions thereon like the new Article 19 in the proposed GPSR should be embedded consistently in all product safety legislation. To ensure a high level of legal certainty for retailers, we would welcome the revised Toy Safety Directive to include a provision on isolated cases directly in the legal text. 
The proposed Article 19 of the GPSR ensures that there always is a notification, guaranteeing a high level of safety on the EU Single Market whilst safeguarding the business relationship as the notification is always at the initiative of the manufacturer.

initiative: State aid rules for banks in difficulty - evaluation
feedback_text:
The rules in question don't seem to have reduced the risk undertaken by banks who have received help, however, the very existence of so-called bailouts enables the risk, and allows banks to have a safety net in case something goes wrong - thus making risk much more potentially profitable, and making it the only rational option for banks, this is a well known phenomenon called moral hazard.

Although one may feel that it's necessary to provide bailouts in the name of stability, and yes, allowing banks to fail would result in temporary instability, but bailouts only increase the instability in the long run, by enabling risk-taking, and even outright rewarding it. Banks already are required to pay to a fund assuring a lesser impact on customers in case of a bankruptcy, and if this proves insufficient, those payments can be increased, or instead of bailing out a bank, this money could be provided to the victims of the bankruptcy. 

There aren't any clear benefits to citizens when it comes to bailouts - of course, it is seen by some economists as beneficial to the economy, but there isn't any clear indication that it helps the average citizen. I would propose altering those rules, so that in the event that bailouts are provided, they must eventually be paid back (corrected according to inflation and in a bigger sum than what would be gained by the country by lending this money to another entity) specifically as something that benefits the public, it may be a direct donation to the budget, forgiving the debts of the most insolvent private individuals, or anything that can verifiably benefit the public, while ensuring that the repayment doesn't endanger the bank's solvency. This way, banks could still be bailed out and customers wouldn't have to face the consequences of a bankruptcy, but the public could tangibly benefit, and risk taking would stop being the rational strategy to take.

It's also worth noting, that bank bailouts remain a distinctly unpopular policy with many citizens, and is a very polarising topic, so I propose that the future policy should be influenced by public opinion polls and wide-scale public consultations, while banks' interests and their political influence in this matter should be reduced to a necessary minimum.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We are an institution which has only recently, just before COVID, joined the ERASMUS+. Because of the COVID, we did not perform any mobility yet. Our first mobility will take place in a few days. As a result we are not experienced to give any feedback for either 2014-2020, nor for 2021-2027.
We have participated, however in many of our National Agency presentations, seminars and information events. From these participations, we concluded that the ERASMUS+ can take our organisation into the ERASMUS world of mobility. As an organisation as well as individual trainers we are enabled to gain a lot from these mobilities. We are enabled to take our organisation outside the boundaries of Cyprus, making internationalisation a reality. Our trainers can meet and learn from more experienced trainers in EU countries, from whom they can gain knowledge, receive experience, hear about successes, failures and good practices. They can have the opportunity to exchange ideas, thoughts and good practices, to share successes and good practices, to discuss and solve problems.

Our students can get outside Cyprus to a European university or workplace, they can meet new people, new ideas, new ways of living. They can gain and exchange thoughts, knowledge and skills. They can have the opportunity which, ,otherwise it was not possible to have.

We feel that we will not have any difficulties to run the projects we have since our National Agency and its officers are always next to us, helping us and solving any problem we have.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: ECOS welcomes the evaluation of the WEEE Directive to overhaul e-waste rules in the EU and put an end to the e-waste tsunami. 2019 data showed that 24 out of 27 member states failed to collect sufficient WEEE separately and did not reach the EU target of 65% collection. Up to 4.8 million tonnes of WEEE are still improperly disposed of every year and lost for reuse and recycling. The European 2021 study on quality standards for WEEE treatment pointed to the huge environmental, health and social benefits from higher e-waste minimum quality standards at EU level. The WEEE Directive requirements are today outdated, do not reflect the latest technical and scientific progress, and are inconsistently applied across the EU. This calls for an EU WEEE Regulation laying down ambitious minimum quality standards supported by a credible and independent surveillance system. Many EEE products that could be collected for preparing for reuse are actually leaking outside the collection regime. The EU should provide for waste prevention and preparing for reuse targets of WEEE that are distinct from the existing recycling targets. Requirements should also be adapted to preparing for re-use operators, e.g. through simplified reporting and information access. The Ecodesign article from the WEEE Regulation should be better aligned with the EU objectives of the ESPR. Appropriate measures should especially be allowed for EEE reusability, upgradability, reparability, maintenance, and refurbishment, without exception. Moreover, online marketplaces should be fully liable for the EEE they place on the EU market, as well as WEEE take back and proper treatment. The WEEE Regulation should take stock of the new Battery Regulation. Potential gaps on portable batteries could however hamper the way batteries are treated at the end of their first life or their durability. WEEE legislation should therefore address these gaps and make sure that all waste portable batteries collected undergo preparation for reuse, preparation for repurposing or a recycling process. Minimum common part design requirements should be applied to facilitate these processes. WEEE should be treated as containing hazardous substances or POPs unless it can be proved that levels in the item are below the concentration limits of the POP Regulation. The WEEE Regulation should also be better aligned with latest legislative developments (e.g. POP thresholds and conditions) plus it should include more systematic depollution monitoring processes (not only for each step of the process but also for each output fraction likely to contain hazardous substances). 6.3 million tonnes of CO2-equivalent emissions could be saved every year if CENELEC standards for treatment of discarded fridges and freezers were made mandatory across Europe. The WEEE Regulation should use these standards as a baseline and enforce stricter rules. For instance, a more cautious and thorough extraction of refrigerants and foam propellants from old appliances in recovery facilities, and stricter transport rules would lead to fewer leaks in transit. WEEE recovery target for photovoltaics is based on weight and reached with glass and aluminium (90% of PV module). Complementary requirements on individual materials should help recover metals, especially CRMs such as copper, electrical connections (incl. lead soldering) and polymers (incl. the backsheet containing fluorinated chemicals). Moreover, solar thermal and inverters should be in scope. The WEEE Directive obligations apply to boilers in some EU Member States but not in others. This discrepancy prevents coherent treatment of these products at their end-of-life and the liability of producers to finance their collection, treatment, recovery and environmentally sound disposal. This calls for full harmonisation in the EU and further clarification of Annex IV. ECOS look forward to continuing to play an active role in the stakeholder engagement on this important upcoming revision.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Erasmus Student Network is the largest student and alumni organisation supporting the implementation of the Erasmus+ programme and learning mobility experiences in general. Our multilevel structure allows ESN to have a direct engagement with all the key stakeholders involved in the programme. Our more than 500 local associations are connected with more than 1000 Higher Education Institutions active in Erasmus+, and our presence in 41 countries gives a broad perspective of the reality of both programme countries and partner countries, mainly from the Western Balkans and Eastern Partnership.
 
ESN has a unique capacity to gather the voices and experiences of the protagonists of the programme: the students. We combine a strong data collection tradition with direct contact with students on the ground, which allows us to represent their needs, interest and ideas. 
 
Our contribution to the final evaluation of the Erasmus+ is therefore based on a combination of our research and policy initiatives carried out since 2014, and our experience working with students, Higher Education Institutions, National Agencies and the European Commission over these 8 years. ESN´s research and policy outputs can be checked on our website: www.esn.org/policy.

You can read our contribution in the attached document.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Since this year I have worked for Scholarships and the Erasmus + programme. I am delighted that the European Commission sees this programme as a tool to help students gain experience abroad. I also believe that the focus of inclusion on top ups contributes to a more inclusive world. 
However, the introduction of these top ups and their guidance is far from satisfactory. There is no guidance or format that we can draw on as an institution. I made all the calculations and created excel files. Unfortunately, the benificiary module is still not working and so we cannot check whether this is correct. What I find personally very strange is the fact that the email answers are quicker than Yammer while the wish to communicate via the platform is/I think the framework conditions of the Summit Ups are too broad. Although we are probably not about budget, you can ask you whether someone who had a DUO top-up 4 years ago should still be entitled to a top up. Registration of green travel is not possible and medical conditions should not be requested for privacy reasons. We do not want to know either. It is therefore a good idea to me, but implementation has clearly not been thought out or designed in consultation with institutions. I would also point out that my colleague, who has been working for a number of years, is a real couple. I think it is particularly difficult for a well-valued colleague with a lot of expertise to experience such a nuisance because the processes and systems that had to work for a long time are still not working. I can imagine that this is the case for many other people, leaving your exporters with goodwill and flexibility. So my feedback is: it is very good that this is facilitated AND is taking more people who make sure that everything is working in time.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In the new generation of programmes, the inclusive approach and the promotion of individual monitoring is good.
First of all, we very much welcome the fact that the new programme has made it possible for all apprentices to participate and to encourage them to address obstacles. With the new rules, we can easily reduce a variety of obstacles, and at the same time we will take into account our additional costs in caring, in particular in monitoring, evaluating and recognising the learning process. 

However, with regard to the collection and storage of personal data in programme management, particular sensitivity and caution is needed to avoid double discrimination. Especially as young people are a particularly vulnerable group. Here, as an institution, we have a special duty of care vis-à-vis our Adressat:innen.

Although it is basically understandable that the Commission would like to collect data on the participants reached and barriers removed in order to be able to follow up with further initiatives in specific places or for certain groups, we see this as problematic in practice. As a minimum, voluntary data collection should be carried out in separate databases. This means decoupling personal data to manage mobility from any data on inclusion or special needs. But here too, it seems inappropriate or simply impossible to combine reasoning, as many of the lists in the programme guide are far too unspecific. For example, what is meant by ‘people who are not linguistically and culturally integrated’, what is a ‘low standard of living’? The formulations call for concepts that go beyond all scientific and social debates in recent years. In addition, we cannot, and will not, ask the participants for the items listed, nor do we themselves make diagnoses or ask for evidence and file.

The continuing problems with the admistration database continue to be problematic. The announced links between the different tools and databases are pending or not foreseen. The new beneficiary module would therefore need to create at least one API interface for project promoters so that their own open source databases for participant registration and management can communicate with the Beneficiary module.
We are currently working on a participant registration and management module based on the open source database software CiviCRM. It will also update the participant management governance database developed in a previous EU project with a dialogue-based process to develop learning outcomes and identify acquired competences and learning achievements. In addition to the expansion of CiviCRM objects, various CiviCRM extensions are published as free software and are available to the entire community. This will certainly be of interest to other project promoters.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I participated in an Erasmus+ / Mundus two-year Masters program, taking place in France, Finland, and Sweden. This was an amazing, rich, and formative experience I definitely do not regret. Erasmus allowed me to meet people from 20+ countries, discover new cultures and grow as a multi-cultural EU citizen. Thanks to Erasmus+, I am now working in Germany.
I truly believe studying for a semester or more in a different country is a must-have experience for all young citizens, but the costs without the help of Erasmus+ are too prohibitive. We must continue to support the free exchange of culture between academic institutions in the coming years, and develop it further.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In general, 2014-2020
Initially, a very difficult programme, with plenty of cumbersome rules, was gradually facilitated and ultimately quite good. One minus was that information on the budget was often very late and changed from one year to the next, e.g. “Horn of Africa” one year, and the huge amount of money “South Africa” next from being nothing at all. This could be done earlier so that higher education institutions had the opportunity to write better applications for areas that we would otherwise envisage “don’t bother” of South Africa. 

In general, 2021 —
The basic idea of balancing up the ICM with the 20 % in “mainstream” Erasmus is good, but in practice, partly because this has not been widely used, most Swedish higher education institutions will use the 20 % for the UK, which means that they fall. Otherwise many improvements compared to the previous programming period, in particular the application.

Thank you for your work! 

/Niklas Nannskog

This could be done earlier so that higher education institutions had the opportunity to write better applications for areas that we would otherwise envisage “don’t bother” of South Africa. In general, 2021 — The basic idea of balancing up the ICM with the 20 % in “mainstream” Erasmus is good, but in practice, partly because this has not been widely used, most Swedish higher education institutions will use the 20 % for the UK, which means that they fall. Otherwise many improvements compared to the previous programming period, in particular the application. Thank you for your work! /Niklas Nannskog

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Directive highlights the need for separate collection of WEEE from municipal waste in order to facilitate its reuse: while separate collection seems to work, collection efforts in France should be concentrated around conservative collection, in order to maximise the re-employability of WEEE. Indeed, the majority of collecting systems in France remain systems that seriously hamper re-employability, such as the collection of large household appliances with compressors. Thus, efforts should be made towards conserving collection (i.e. collection carried out directly from households), with a view to increasing the national re-use rate. Although the recent AGEC law has allowed for notorious advances (such as the creation of the Repair and Reemployment Funds to make these life-long solutions more attractive), the French re-use targets set by the public authorities for eco-organisations are in the order of 2 %, a rate which is badly missing but explains in the light of current practices. As an example of the large electrical household appliances sector, Ecosystem, an eco-organisation to which almost all new wholesale household appliances market players operating in France belong, only conducts a study for re-use of 6 % of its WEEE deposit. Of these 6 %, only 20 % are re-employed. Thus, only 9000 tonnes (out of a total annual deposit of 664 000 tonnes) will eventually be reused, without mobilising the capacity of the traditional market sector, which would have the operational capacity to repackage large volumes, which would make it possible to offer a sufficiently diversified supply to compete with the nine. Ecosystem reports that it collects 8.5 million large electrical household appliances, about 180 000 (equivalent to 9000 tonnes) are repackaged and therefore 8.3 million go directly to recycling, energy recovery or burial centres, without having been studied for re-use. By denying access to the WEEE deposit to the commercial sector, market players and eco organisations are preventing circular economy companies from finding viable and sustainable business models that alone can eventually generalise the circularity of the economy and its social and environmental co-benefits. By preventing an attractive (diversified and cheaper) offer of repackaged products, France and Europe help to direct consumer purchases towards new goods. However, in the market for large electrical household appliances, there are almost more production dusins in Europe. Virgin raw materials are extracted from Africa, often assembled into spare parts in Asia, even assembled into finished products usually in Turkey and finally transported to Europe. Buying new is therefore tantamount to making the economies of foreign countries work, importing and thus damaging our trade balance, while in France and Europe we have a sufficient power park to produce repackaged goods. And at the same time, by creating skilled local jobs that cannot be relocated, by drastically reducing the carbon content of the product (70 % of the product’s LCA comes from its manufacture, only 30 % of its use), by reducing the volume of waste and by remaining independent in terms of raw material supply! It seems more than necessary for Europe to impose on its Member States an increase and stricter framework for the objectives for the re-use of WEEE, in particular by giving preference to conservative collection systems, and to allow access to the waste deposit for all those involved who are able to repackage them so that the sector can develop.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
— Requirements for preservatives in liquid toys do not limit to children under 3 years of age. There are also toys on the market for older children with frequent dermal contact (grinding and tail clay).
— Extension requirements for nitrosamines, such as NDELA, to other aqueous toys. Now in EN71-12 only a requirement for finger paint is included.

It is difficult that some legislation is regulated by REACH (Annex XVII) and other requirements are set out in the TSD. For compliance, it is desirable that all requirements for toys will be covered by 1 legislation. The main example is the plasticisers (phthalates).

In terms of standardisation, it would be more sophisticated if it was more comprehensible. Standards have been included for some groups of substances (organic substances in Part 9, metals in Part 3). Other standards are product-oriented. However, for finger paints you must also comply with Part 3 tav metals and Part 12 tav NDELA. Parts 9, 10 and 11 are highly obsolete and need to be revised/updated.

To further strengthen the protection of children from chemicals in toys, we strongly support that the TSD is procured, in particular with respect to:
— Allowing the setting of specific limit values for any toys (independent of the age of children and the route through which they are exposed), a.o. in view of the chemicals embodied from squishy toys, sensitising substances and Preservatives in toy smares and in toy modelling Clays, and Allergenic fragrances in toys;
— Strengthening the existing limits for nitrosamines and nitrosatable substances, and extending the limits in finger paint to other aquatic toys such as toy-slime and Putty, 
— Revision the derogations of the general prohibitions of CMRs to allow risk-based limit values.

Parts 9, 10 and 11 are highly obsolete and need to be revised/updated. To further strengthen the protection of children from chemicals in toys, we strongly support that the TSD is procured, in particular with respect to: — Allowing the setting of specific limit values for any toys (independent of the age of children and the route through which they are exposed), a.o. in view of the chemicals embodied from squishy toys, sensitising substances and Preservatives in toy smares and in toy modelling Clays, and Allergenic fragrances in toys; — Strengthening the existing limits for nitrosamines and nitrosatable substances, and extending the limits in finger paint to other aquatic toys such as toy-slime and Putty, — Revision the derogations of the general prohibitions of CMRs to allow risk-based limit values.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
DEKRA welcomes the European Commission’s initiative to review the Toy Safety Directive. 

We would like to support turning the Toy Safety Directive into a Regulation. We believe, that this will ensure consistent application of the toy safety requirements by all Member States.

We also agree with the points raised in the Impact Assessment on new risks on toys that are connected to the internet or toys operating with AI. We are convinced that they should be addressed by the new Regulation. This would take into account security, privacy and the protection of data, if not covered by other legislation. Additionally, these products should be bound to requirements with regard to the availability of software updates to guarantee the product remains secure during its lifetime. We would like to highlight that security flaws have the potential to jeopardize toy safety.

Furthermore, are we in favor of strengthening the chemical requirements for toys and to also set limits for toys intended for children aged above 36 months, as the risk does not decrease above this age.  

With regard to online trade warning signs and other labelling information, we consider that this information should be visible when buying online, similar to when buying toys in a physical shop.

Finally, DEKRA would like to highlight that the Consulting Safety Gate (former RAPEX) reports that many unsafe toys still enter the European single market. This creates an unacceptable risk to the safety, health and privacy of children. Mandatory third-party assessment by independent third parties should be considered for toys with a high-risk potential such as toys intended for the most vulnerable consumers (e.g. children aged 0 – 36 months) and toys connected to the internet.

The Ariston Group supports this initiative and emphasises common regulation at EU level (EU27); the use of a ‘Directive’ logic creates de facto fragmentation of the European market and an obstacle to the free movement of goods.
We therefore request that this Directive be amended into a Regulation with requirements, rules and controls common to all 27 Member States.

Thank you,
Angelo Mancini

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
On ERA KA1
Erasmus has shaped my life and is still doing it. I would not be where I am now and who I am now, as a person and still a convinced European. I am grateful for this. I had to study the lucky in Spain in 1989, with full academic and financial freedom and without being authenticated by tough papers and gold-plating. This is my view as a participant. Pleasure about the opportunity and experience.

BUT: My view as a implementer, administrator, Erasmus University Coordinator In 25 years of professional experience and ‘survivor’ of several generations of programmes: It is fatal to let universities across Europe ‘work’ (or rather not because they do not function) with technical disasters such as the Mobility tool and now the Beneficiary Module and EWP. But what is even more important than technology: regardless of the base, i.e. the people who manage the funds at universities and are the backbone of the programme, innovations will be introduced which make the work involved in implementation so big and complicated that the beneficiaries, young people, can completely fall behind and no longer take care of them and their concerns. Well-established intentions, such as social participation or green travel, lead to “improvements” and are not sufficiently well thought out. The question arises as to whether my university’s participation in the programme is even acceptable in terms of staff. Far from every reality, decisions are taken in Brussels by people who do not seem to be aware of the daily bread and day-to-day work at universities and of the situation of students and staff who want to be mobile and who should and want to focus on (further) education and not on their impervious form and constraints. The actual “spirit” of the programme is completely lost. This is frustrating and disillusioning.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The strengths we see in the Erasmus+ 2021-2027 programmes:
creation of a European Solidarity Corps and demarcation from Erasmus± greater emphasis and opportunities within the ESE
— the creation of smaller strategic partnerships for all who are confronted with strategic partnerships for the first time, as this makes it slightly easier to apply for a project before a collaborative partnership,
— Erasmus+ accreditation, which gives organisations with more experience an advantage in obtaining funding and facilitates applications;
— we warmly welcome additional funding to support young people with fewer opportunities.
— simplifying financial inflows without having to prove everything with invoices (e.g. organisational support, reimbursement of travel, etc.).

Room for improvement of Erasmus+ 2021-2027:
— ECAS — how to make it more transparent and user-friendly (sometimes difficult to find a contact document/opportunity if you do not have a URL directly to opportunity), problems when changing the name of a person registered with the organisation’s e-mail; this can be an extraordinary opportunity for young people to update the functioning of the interface.
— more up-to-date translations before the application deadlines (mainly works where the programme aims at young people applying for a project — a lot of mentoring assistance is still needed to help young people also find themselves on the ECAS interface),
— in order to promote sustainability, consider an additional financial incentive where local providers are involved in the project, small businesses (which are usually more expensive to choose but are more environmentally friendly),

General opinion: 
— when applying for projects (e.g. youth exchanges, mobility of youth workers), it is sometimes difficult to predict in advance how many young people with fewer opportunities will participate (to apply for individual support), how many people will be able to benefit from a green trip (it is understood that due to bureaucratic processes and the allocation of resources it is necessary to do so in advance).

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Association of Portuguese Enterprises for the Environment Sector, AEPSA, is a business association, set up in 1994, which represents and defends the collective interests of private companies intervening in the environment sector and is a key player in the development of the environmental market. The purpose of AEPSA is to represent and defend the collective interests of private companies involved in: Wastewater supply and sewerage water, waste, energy production and utilisation, reusable and/or recyclable materials and products, New technologies, innovation, contaminated soils, among others, and undertakings whose purpose is the recovery of reusable or recyclable products, known as, but not limited to, the processing and preparation of ferrous, non-ferrous, paper, cardboard, ELV (end-of-life vehicles) materials, and all complementary, similar and related materials that can be treated in the context of the circular economy and in pursuit of the Sustainable Development Goals, promoting research, development and innovation, leveraging and encouraging cooperation between the various entities, public and private, national and non-national, intervening both in the domestic and international markets. Thus, AEPSA today represents the whole value chain of the environment in Portugal, which translates into a turnover of around EUR 1,000 million distributed among its approximately 40 members. As part of the initiative on the public consultation carried out by the European Commission (EC) on Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment: Evaluation of European legislation, AEPSA presents its comments and contributions in the attached document.
