initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Electrical and Electronic Waste (WEEE) Directive should aim to extend the lifetime of equipment before addressing its end-of-life and recycling. This extension requires improvements in the reparability of equipment by design, the ability to update the components and to be able to reuse them for other uses after the end of life. Progress in decodesign is also needed to simplify the dismantling of equipment, both to repair and, at the end of life, to recover reusable components and to recycle the rest in an optimal way. In this respect, the Commission could require manufacturers to provide dismantling instructions or even propose standards to standardise certain disassembly steps in order to simplify their industrialisation. Waste deposits need to be better characterised (origin, composition, assembly) in order to simplify and optimise their recycling. Recycled materials must be traceable and their quality monitored. They are an asset for the European Union in terms of reducing dependencies on imported materials and components and must therefore be recycled as far as possible on European territory. This implies that the European Union has full capacities to rise the value chain on its territory, to be preserved if they exist, otherwise to be developed. Recycling needs to be adapted to the needs of the downstream value chain, which implies a reflection on the whole value chain. Finally, reducing the amount of electrical and electronic waste generated also requires softening and leaching, which should be promoted beyond the necessary progress in lifetime extension, reparability, reuse and end-of-life management.

feedback_text:
The programme is very good and the ambition to truly involve young people in the whole process is important, although we know that experience is first needed in order to be part of a team involved in mobility planning.
It is also crucial to actively reach marginalised groups. 
Travel expenses are too low now after Corona and hit us far away from the centre and have costs after arrival in a larger city. 
Food prices have also increased significantly, which will complicate an already very tight budget for the organisers of exchanges. It also fails to take into account the fact that in the spring areas with geographical barriers are very expensive with most (expensive petrol prices in the EU at present) high food prices in general, high transport costs and whether you want to buy a service or material.
Digital systems also require a lot of time and cause great concern when they sometimes do not work or when one does not understand how it is intended, the time to read manuals and familiarise us with the systems is demanding for us small associations making one or a couple of actions per year. 
However, we have great support from the National Agency for this.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Energy Labelling was designed at a time when electricity was mostly fossil based, to help consumers select the most efficient solution to meet their needs, with the lowest energy consumption costs for them. This approach was relevant for comparing similar products, using the same energy, such as two fridges, but far less relevant when comparing appliances offering different services, using different energy vector, like a micro-cogeneration and a heat pump. This is still the case. All dwellings cannot be equipped with all appliances, for technical or economic reasons. If heat pumps can’t be used, customers will have nearly all renewable gas boilers, the best alternative, all in on class, not much of a help.

The heating demand profile is very different from the standard electric equipment ones. It is seasonal, and, extreme cold weeks, occurring a few times in a century, which are essential for public health reasons, are a main design criterion for the infrastructure (grid, storage, dispatchable peak production capacity) of the energy carrier used for it, and therefore, have a major impact on the grid part of the energy bills. 

The  heat pumps’ efficiency is the highest  when the weather is not too cold and for temperature of the heating loop water temperature is under 50°C. This efficiency decreases when outdoor temperatures drop and is at its lowest values during the coldest days, it can then require the action of a  direct low efficiency joule back-up, increasing dramatically the electric peak demand. At such times, it is important to be able to relieve the electric network by “offloading” the heat pump. It must be noted that simply switching off the heat pump for an hour or two, using the building heating inertia, is not a solution, as these extreme cold episodes last for several days. This long peak duration also excludes vehicle to grid as a solution for heating peak demand: for example, a 4 kWe Heat pump with a 4kW back-up, operating 80% of the time, would require, for an average house, 460 kwh for only 3 days of extreme cold, equivalent to 9 fully charged Renault Zoé battery!

Providing genuine flexibility to the electric network during extreme cold peaks, requires switching peak demand to another energy vector. Smart hybrid heat pumps allow it by using the renewable gas boiler back-up during very cold days, or at any moment it could be relevant, when the boiler is more efficient, euro-wise or CO2-wise, than the heat pump. For instance, it could be useful to switch to renewable gas boiler when there is a lack of renewable electricity (if no wind at night), and when meeting the heating demand with electricity would require turbining hydrogen (cumulating losses at electrolysis – around 30% - and losses of electric power plants – around 50%).

The use of a smart hybrid heat pump or a smart cogeneration would therefore : 
- avoid oversizing the electric infrastructure (saving huge amounts of investment), by offering flexibility during extreme cold peaks, 
- get the highest system efficiency possible, considering the upstream electric generation, grid efficiency and the available renewable energy (gas and electric). 

Should only efficiency (and even worse, average efficiency) be recognised in the proposed labelling directive, this will hamper the further development of flexibility (demanded by the Energy Efficiency first principle), enabled by smart-grids and smart hybrid appliances and will increase negative externalities making energy more expensive for all.  

We therefore recommend that smart Heating solutions that improve the energy system efficiency (Energy First Principle), by offering real flexibility enabling the optimisation of renewable energy integration, reducing infrastructure’s costs, and increasing system resilience, such as smart hybrid heat pumps or smart cogeneration, should be given an upgrade in labelling, to bring the best of them to the B class at least and over their nonflexible counterparts.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: As our feedback exceeds the 4000 character limit, we submit it by the enclosed PDF- file. A summary includes: According to ETIRA, representing the European cartridge remanufacturers, in terms of promoting cartridge reuse and reducing (hazardous) cartridge waste, the WEEE has not delivered on its promise. Today, after 20 years of WEEE, fewer cartridges are being reused than ever before, and hazardous and polluting non-OEM and OEM single-use newbuilt cartridges from SE Asia are flooding the EU market in larger volumes than ever before. And as the non-OEM newbuilts cannot be reused, meaning they go straight to landfill or incineration in the EU after 1st use. Cartridges produce much more waste than most IT-products, including mobile phones etc. Printer cartridge reuse has existed for 25 years but never developed to its full potential. Today only 7%-20% of cartridges are reused, while technically up to 80%-90% could be reused. Increase in reuse is hindered by many barriers put up by printer manufacturers, which, only for commercial reasons, want single-use only, and by imports of cheap an polluting single-use non-OEM clone cartridges from SE Asia. The WEEE should help to remve those barriers. For cartridges, ETIRA states that there are many material shortcomings of the WEEE: -Member States can reach the generic reuse / recycling percentage targets set under the WEEE without a single cartridge being reused. But according to EU waste laws and the international waste hierarchy pyramid, reuse has a lower environmental footprint than recycling materials. In the case of cartridges, many studies have shown that reusing a cartridge as a cartridge saves natural resource resources and reduce CO2 emissions by 45%-60% compared to producing a new cartridge. That is why in the WEEE, the activities reuse and recycling should be split into two seperate activities, with each having their own WEEE target. -Because of the great environmental impact they can have, combined with their great reuse potential, printer cartridges need to be seperated from the EEE umbrella product category in which they classified are now, and get their own speciific, cartridge WEEE reuse target. -WEEE implementation is very very different across EU Member States, and even between regions within a Member State !! Environment-friendly cartridge reuse is made impossible as operators are confronted by a plethora of different and often outright contradictory rules and definitions and classifications of used cartridges. Definition of empties range from waste to product. The EU needs to set a definition. -for cartridges, the article 4 WEEE prohibition of specific design features or manufacturing processes that prevent WEEE from being re-used (ecodesign) is a farce because, in 2002 only some, but today all cartridges on the EU market have such illegal features !! The EU should outlaw such design features or manufacturing processes at EU level, and not leave this up to the not-acting Member States -enforcement of WEEE compliance by national market surveillance authorities is non-existent: imports of polluting single-use newbuild cartridges from the Far East by rogue traders without any registration in national WEEE registers and containing illegal chemicals are rampant in most EU countries. The EU should force Member States to act decisively. Importers should provide evidence of correct WEEE registration and REACH/ROHS compliance before importation is allowed. -Today, all too often players do not pay for the waste and wasteful effects they generate. Today, environmental penalties are way too low to really discourage wasting and pollution. To end the soft and varying implementation of the WEEE across Member States, the WEEE should become a regulation.

To end the soft and varying implementation of the WEEE across Member States, the WEEE should become a regulation.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
All the positive points of the programme (such as shorter mobility possible, top-ups for green-travel and fewer opportunities) are not made due to the non-functioning of the systems. Both the Benificiary Module, the exchange of Online Learning Agreements, IIAs and the OLS do not work or do not work properly. This has a negative impact on all those working with Erasmus +. The job satisfaction of many employees in internationalisation has been dramatically reduced. They are doing their utmost to implement all the rules in the best way possible, but Erasmus + has become a Mobstacle in many respects. The EESC recommends that the European Commission carry out more direct consultations with end users on a country-by-country basis and apologise for not operating these systems. The benifiary Module should have been fully operational by August 2021 and the system is more often not functioning or if it works properly until today (spet- 2022). Storage is not possible, stored mobility disappears or partially disappears, each organisation needs to be added manually, while ECHE holders are concerned, so a drop-down should simply be available. Where there are drop-downs, these are particularly bad and appear to be randomly designed (not in alphabetical order or another system as far as I and my colleague have been able to discover). In short, a total flourishing trend.

In addition, institutions are expected to pay out (part of) the scholarship to students prior to the start of their exchange, but the institutions have not yet received money for Call 2022, nor is it possible to introduce mobility into the Benificiary Module.

Erasmus + is at the heart of a beautiful programme, but because too much effort is now being made to implement it at the same time, it is completely wrong. Erasmus without paper has become a sample. Together with a non-functioning Benifiary Module zadel your institutions with the impossible task of managing this.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Please consider our attached document for a deeper analysis, here below you can find a synthesis.
4. Ecodesign requirements 
– extending the rules to cover appliances with a capacity from 400 kW to 1 MW
Having in mind the last version of the draft Regulation presentend, in the future a reversible appliance will be a heating product if its capacity is below 400 kW and starting from 401 kW it will must be declared as a cooling product with different Ecodesign requirements and calculations even if it will be an identical model with only a different declared capacity. 
Considering also a simple energetic analysis, we can state that imposing heating requirements also on the reversible heat pumps will have a non-negligible effect since heat is the most delivered energy by these units during a year.
From a technical point of view, if a unit needs to meet SEER targets would be optimized in cooling mode; if a unit needs to meet SCOP targets would be optimized in heating mode. 
Considering what above, having a unit optimized in heating mode (SCOP target) we believe is in line with the “energy efficiency first” principle. 
Considering also the European Green Deal and the decarbonisation target, reversible units will progressively cover more and more heating loads as primary goals. 
So, measuring them in heating instead of cooling in our opinion also gives the end users the most correct message.

– introducing new space heating categories, in particular hybrid heat pumps 
Assoclima does not support the introduction of any indication of performance and seasonal efficiency at High Temperature regime also as an optional part of the product information and we recommend keeping the Medium Temperature regime testing at rated Tsupply 55°C. 
Furthermore, we re-state our suggestion to carefully consider introducing the intermediate class of 45°C supply water temperature. This, in addition with the low temperature, will represent more the real-life applications of heating heat pumps and will contribute to more energy savings thanks to better-performing applications.
– updating the minimum energy efficiency limits, in particular by accounting for the new primary energy factor of 2.1 and the Green Deal objectives 
As a general consideration, Assoclima would like to confirm its support to the introduction of the proposed PEF correction for the minimum limits.
– new test methods for heat pumps, in particular the ‘compensation-method 
Assoclima believes that the compensation method is not mature enough for being introduced in the current review and thus asks keeping the current test method.
- revised heat pump sound power settings 
Assoclima asks keeping the current approach: sound requirements applying to units up to 70 kW only. We ask keep the Prated as the conditions at which the capacity limits for maximum sound power requirements apply.
– self-monitoring and reporting of in-use energy efficiency for installed space heaters 
It is not possible to currently support the EC proposal and thus Assoclima suggests reconsidering it at the next review (insert it in the review clause). 
– extended resource-efficiency requirements. 
Assoclima asks that only heat pumps below 70 kW are to be covered by specific energy resources requirements. Concerning heat pumps, Assoclima believes that spare parts should be limited to an exhaustive list of critical components to be listed in the Regulation.

3. Energy labelling requirements 
Assoclima welcomes and support the EC proposal of rescaling the energy label to A-G as well as the introduction of the ηs value on the label. 
We’d like also to inform that the proposed label is not clear since has on the same label both MT and LT classes, and so it is still unknown how to deal with it when LT heat pumps don’t reach and consequently don’t show MT performances. 
Last, we’d like some clarification about the packages, whether they shall include a solar device or not.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Dear Madame, Dear Sir,

finally, with the communication on "REPowerEU: Joint European Action for more affordable, secure and sustainable energy" the COM underline again the necessary effort to even reach the climate and energy goals earlier: "30 million newly installed heat pumps installed in 2030, saving 35 bcm in 2030". Heat pump roll out front loading by doubling deployment resulting in a cumulative 10 million units over the next 5 years."

It is therefore important that the ErP Regulation supports these efforts by all means and adjusts the criteria accordingly.

We therefore propose to strengthen the requirements in Annex II - REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY EFFICIENCY OF SEASONAL SPACE HEATERS.

Hybrid space heaters:
The minimum requirements for "Hybrid Space Heaters, MT" are set too low at 110%. The requirements must be at least 125% for one to define a sufficiently high minimum requirement.

If the minimum requirement is adjusted to 125%, the secondary requirement under c) can be omitted. Otherwise, the share of the heat pump in the secondary requirement would have to be set at well over 50%. In the case of a hybrid heat generator, the heat pump must be the dominant heat generator.

Fuel boilers:
Future limit values for boilers should exclude non-condensing technology, therefore a general limit value of 93% could be proposed. 

The need to adapt the energy demand and the energy sources required for the building sector requires that the appropriate adjustments be made in the ErP regulation.

Furthermore, I point to more comprehensive position attached.

Best regards,
Holger Thamm
text_raw: Dear Madame, Dear Sir, finally, with the communication on "REPowerEU: Joint European Action for more affordable, secure and sustainable energy" the COM underline again the necessary effort to even reach the climate and energy goals earlier: "30 million newly installed heat pumps installed in 2030, saving 35 bcm in 2030". Heat pump roll out front loading by doubling deployment resulting in a cumulative 10 million units over the next 5 years." It is therefore important that the ErP Regulation supports these efforts by all means and adjusts the criteria accordingly. We therefore propose to strengthen the requirements in Annex II - REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY EFFICIENCY OF SEASONAL SPACE HEATERS. Hybrid space heaters: The minimum requirements for "Hybrid Space Heaters, MT" are set too low at 110%. The requirements must be at least 125% for one to define a sufficiently high minimum requirement. If the minimum requirement is adjusted to 125%, the secondary requirement under c) can be omitted. Otherwise, the share of the heat pump in the secondary requirement would have to be set at well over 50%. In the case of a hybrid heat generator, the heat pump must be the dominant heat generator. Fuel boilers: Future limit values for boilers should exclude non-condensing technology, therefore a general limit value of 93% could be proposed. The need to adapt the energy demand and the energy sources required for the building sector requires that the appropriate adjustments be made in the ErP regulation. Furthermore, I point to more comprehensive position attached. Best regards, Holger Thamm

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: CDP is the Italian National Promotional Institution and one of the largest Italian financial institutions, with total assets above 410bn. CDP combines financial and industrial capabilities to foster sustainable development in Italy, support growth and boost employment, also spurring innovation, business competitiveness as well as infrastructural and local development. Promoting the circular economy is one of the key target areas of the CDP Strategic Plan for 2022-24, which aims to generate >120bn investment in Italy. CDP intends, inter alia, to support recycling value chains for strategic waste streams, including the development of technologies for the recovery of critical raw materials contained in waste from electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE), which are essential for many technologies contributing to the green transition. In this context, it is worth emphasising that any plan to revise the WEEE Directive should favour investment in the collection, treatment, and recycling of WEEE in order to both prevent adverse impact on human health and environment and contribute to the EU open strategic autonomy.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: PHOTORAMA is a European project dedicated to the development of recycling technologies for PV devices . The project is funded by the European Unions Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 958223. PV panels have been included in the directive 2012/19/EU in the category 4.CONSUMER EQUIPMENT AND PV PANELS (Annex II). The present feedback on the behalf of PHOTORAMA consortium concerns only the PV panels as part of the WEEE under the cat. 4. Solar energy is about to deploy massively in Europe considering the European initiatives announced these last years (Green Deal, Fit for 55, REPowerEU, EU Solar Energy Startegy) to reach the carbon neutrality by 2050. While the market shares of the PV industry may remain with a strong Asiatic influence, the PV waste are and will be on the European ground. PV panels are complex devices that necessitate more specific considerations in the directive. Few recommendations are provided: - Product design referred at the Art.4 The draft of the future Eco-design Directive specific to PV modules and systems has been developed in 2022 and the entry into force is scheduled for 2023. The article 4 currently relates to the Directive 2009/125/EC. A specific mention for the PV panels should be added to link and develop the common incentives. The two directives should be aligned to develop and strengthen circular requirements. The information requirements in the current working document (Art.3 Annex II) related to material contents associated to the EN 45558:2019 should align with the recycling industry needs and so, the WEEE directive requirements (Art.15 section 1). Appropriate codification for PV waste aligned with appropriate codification for PV products will help develop good practices for transparency. - Collection rate referred at the Art. 7 Due to the market volatility and the long lifetime of PV panels, the methodology of calculation for collection target should be adapted specifically (cf. Issues associated to PV panels and compliance with EPR legislation, WEEE forum/ UNITAR report). Including the sub-categorization of PV panels cat.4b in reference to the COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING DECISION (EU) 2019/2193. - Recovery targets referred at the Art. 7 Art.11 -Annex V The recovery targets including recovery and recycling/preparation for re-use should be adapted for the sub-cat. of PV panels. Firstly, the harmonization of a common methodology to assess the recovery ratios to reach the targets should be implemented. In that regard, a methodology for reliable PV waste characterization has been developed in the framework of the PHOTORAMA project and could support further standardization for this purpose. Secondly, the recovery targets calculated by weight expressed as percentage should be complemented. As complex devices containing valuable and critical materials in low percentage by weight, it is easy to reach the current targets without recovering these materials. Considering the high ratio of PV waste, a very large amount of valuable and critical raw materials will be irreversibly lost by operating the current practices. Using down-cycling process and still reaching the recovery targets generates about 4 tons of silver and about 210 tons of silicon lost per 5000 tons treated (capacity treated in France in 2021). At least 10 million tons of PV waste are expected in Europe by 2050 from the IRENA projections. Including a criteria (possibly informative at early stage for the PROs) on critical and valuable to be recovered would incentivize and promote the innovation in the field. Importantly, this will help secure the geopolitical situation of Europe regarding the supply of critical raw materials. Silicon, indium and gallium are in the EC critical raw materials list. The PHOTORAMA consortium with a strong field experience believes to be a relevant stakeholder in further consultations if the revision of Directive is envisaged.

initiative: State aid rules for banks in difficulty - evaluation
feedback_text:
Dear Commission,
Banks in our corporate system are capitalistic for profit, in principle not systemic and therefore do not require state support. People who cocked up, speculates or badly run out. This is how capitalism works now. 
Much more importantly, it is hard to separate the cockers/player banks from the credit banks. This is because banks that only live in the banking sector are not so easy to experience difficulties. The history is evidence. Cockers/player banks, on the other hand, should be subject to 80 % tax, like other casinos. This money can then finance social projects.
Yours sincerely, 
Günter Pahl

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ 2014-20 was great and help a lot the Technical Vocational and Educational Schools in Cyprus. Erasmus+ gave the opportunity to many students and Teachers to travel to other European countries, improve their knowledge in many different topics, skills and European foreign languages, met other European people and cultures, increase their confidence and self-esteem and obtained European Identity and way of thinking.  We owned a great thanks and gratitude to EU authorities for this excessive inspiration the Erasmus+.
However for us here in Cyprus is required must more paperwork comparing with other European countries.  That is unfair and give us the filing that we are not equal within EU countries. Furthermore it seems that for Erasmus+ in Cyprus are applied different rules and regulations than other EU countries. For example our school participated in a KA101 programme. We attended to several seminar that was given by the Cyprus National Agency in Nicosia.  They strictly stated to us that only one teacher at each time can attend to every different seminar.  Therefore during the programme everybody from our school went alone to every different seminar.  However in the seminar that I was attended there was not two but seven teachers from the same school in Malta for the same seminar.  That is completely unfair and against the equality within EU countries.  I believe that the same rules and regulations must apply all over EU countries.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: For the European Commission, UAB EMP recycling is the largest collector and manager of waste electrical and electronic equipment in the Baltic States on the evaluation of the EU rules on waste electrical and electronic equipment. The company collects and processes about 70 %. WEEE generated in the Republic of Lithuania. The company is certified under Weeelabex’s accredited certification scheme. 1. Compliance with EN 50625 and EN 50614 series (Cenelec standards). As shown by our companies and the companies with which we cooperate, compliance with standards, while requiring additional financial, human and technical resources, contributes to the effective and systematic achievement of the main objectives of the WEEE Directive to ensure that as many WEEE as possible is properly collected and treated, while making the WEEE management system more transparent for all actors and waste holders, for producers/importers of EEE and for waste managers and authorities. However, compliance with the standards under the WEEE Directive is currently not mandatory, so only a number of Member States have chosen to make compliance mandatory for waste managers (see Study ON QUALITY STANDARDS FOR THE TREATMENT OF WASTE Electrical AND Electronic EQUIPMENT (WEEE), Reference: ENV.B.3/ETU/2018/0014), which, on the one hand, has created an uneven playing field on the market for the treatment of WEEE in compliance with the standards through better supervision (due to the need to carry out regular internal and external audits in accordance with the requirements of the standards), the use of more advanced technologies, which makes it more expensive, leading to a fraction of WEEE being treated under less stringent conditions and possibly not always in the right way. Therefore, in the context of the revision of the WEEE Directive, we propose: a. As a first step, to assess the feasibility of making compliance with the CENELEC standards a mandatory requirement and to incorporate this into the provisions of the revised WEEE Directive. Second, we propose that the WEEE Directive should be complemented by measures to encourage WEEE managers to implement the standard and, at the same time, to harmonise the auditing of compliance with standards with the permitting and control procedures carried out by public authorities, thereby balancing the administrative burden involved, namely: I. Introduction of a provision in Article 9 of the Directive to allow Member States to provide in their national legislation that waste managers who certify compliance with the relevant CENELEC standards may benefit from simplified procedures for obtaining the permit referred to in Article 9 of the WEEE Directive that is less administratively burdensome, ensuring at least that the competent licensing authorities do not need to re-submit the information contained in the documentation submitted and verified by CENELEC standards to auditors; II. A provision is added to Article 23 of the Directive to allow Member States to provide in their national legislation that waste managers who certify compliance with the relevant CENELEC standards may be subject to less frequent checks by public control authorities if they provide evidence of periodic audits carried out in accordance with the CENELEC standards 2. Transboundary movements of WEEE. We understand that WEEE, like other transboundary movements of waste, is not covered by the WEEE Directive but by the Regulation on Transboundary Movements of Waste, but considering that WEEE is a very specific waste stream and, in principle, the first waste stream, the treatment of which is standardised at EU level, we consider it appropriate to assess, in the context of the revision of the WEEE Directive, the possibilities for the Directive to empower the EC to revise Regulation 1013/2006 within a certain period of time after the entry into force of the Directive, with specific conditions for EEE away from the entry into force of the Directive.

The company collects and processes about 70 %. WEEE generated in the Republic of Lithuania. The company is certified under Weeelabex’s accredited certification scheme. 1. Compliance with EN 50625 and EN 50614 series (Cenelec standards). As shown by our companies and the companies with which we cooperate, compliance with standards, while requiring additional financial, human and technical resources, contributes to the effective and systematic achievement of the main objectives of the WEEE Directive to ensure that as many WEEE as possible is properly collected and treated, while making the WEEE management system more transparent for all actors and waste holders, for producers/importers of EEE and for waste managers and authorities. However, compliance with the standards under the WEEE Directive is currently not mandatory, so only a number of Member States have chosen to make compliance mandatory for waste managers (see Study ON QUALITY STANDARDS FOR THE TREATMENT OF WASTE Electrical AND Electronic EQUIPMENT (WEEE), Reference: ENV.B.3/ETU/2018/0014), which, on the one hand, has created an uneven playing field on the market for the treatment of WEEE in compliance with the standards through better supervision (due to the need to carry out regular internal and external audits in accordance with the requirements of the standards), the use of more advanced technologies, which makes it more expensive, leading to a fraction of WEEE being treated under less stringent conditions and possibly not always in the right way. Therefore, in the context of the revision of the WEEE Directive, we propose: a. As a first step, to assess the feasibility of making compliance with the CENELEC standards a mandatory requirement and to incorporate this into the provisions of the revised WEEE Directive. Second, we propose that the WEEE Directive should be complemented by measures to encourage WEEE managers to implement the standard and, at the same time, to harmonise the auditing of compliance with standards with the permitting and control procedures carried out by public authorities, thereby balancing the administrative burden involved, namely: I. Introduction of a provision in Article 9 of the Directive to allow Member States to provide in their national legislation that waste managers who certify compliance with the relevant CENELEC standards may benefit from simplified procedures for obtaining the permit referred to in Article 9 of the WEEE Directive that is less administratively burdensome, ensuring at least that the competent licensing authorities do not need to re-submit the information contained in the documentation submitted and verified by CENELEC standards to auditors; II. A provision is added to Article 23 of the Directive to allow Member States to provide in their national legislation that waste managers who certify compliance with the relevant CENELEC standards may be subject to less frequent checks by public control authorities if they provide evidence of periodic audits carried out in accordance with the CENELEC standards 2. Transboundary movements of WEEE. We understand that WEEE, like other transboundary movements of waste, is not covered by the WEEE Directive but by the Regulation on Transboundary Movements of Waste, but considering that WEEE is a very specific waste stream and, in principle, the first waste stream, the treatment of which is standardised at EU level, we consider it appropriate to assess, in the context of the revision of the WEEE Directive, the possibilities for the Directive to empower the EC to revise Regulation 1013/2006 within a certain period of time after the entry into force of the Directive, with specific conditions for EEE away from the entry into force of the Directive.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
IBERDROLA welcomes the “Fit for 55 Package”
The Green Deal though the “Fit for 55 Package” offers a unique opportunity to set a proper regulatory framework that boosts momentum on the most efficient renewable alternatives to enable decarbonisation and the achievement of EU climate objectives. 

IBERDROLA calls for and ambitious revision of the labelling and ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks

We believe that the following are the main points that should be considered as part of this revision:

• The electricity Primary Energy Factor (PEF) should be dynamic and forward looking and updated in line with the National Climate and Energy Plans (NECP)

The current ecodesign and labelling class limits are still using the outdated PEF of 2.5 instead of the current one of 2.1 in accordance with the Energy Efficiency Directive 2018/2002/EC (EED). However, updating this value to existing regulation is not enough to reflect the real savings of electrification and implement what the Energy System Integration Strategy stated on this regard: “PEF is an important tool to facilitate comparison of savings across energy carriers. Most renewables are 100% efficient and have a low PEF. The PEF should reflect the real savings brought about by renewable electricity and heat”.

Current default PEF for electricity set by the EED is one of the main barriers to promote the electrification of end uses and, hence, to calculate the corresponding energy efficiency gains. 

A default PEF of 2.1 for electricity seems to be clearly outdated as it does not consider how the increase of renewables in the electricity mix has reduced primary energy consumption, energy dependency and emissions in most MSs. 

The default PEF value for electricity should adequately reflect the future increase of renewables in the electricity mix, it should be dynamic and forward looking, taking into consideration the future energy mix of the MS that is set on its National Climate and Energy Plans (NECP).

Most renewables are 100% efficient and have a low PEF. The PEF should reflect the real savings brought about by renewable electricity and heat”. Current default PEF for electricity set by the EED is one of the main barriers to promote the electrification of end uses and, hence, to calculate the corresponding energy efficiency gains. initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Directive prevented too much divergence in national initiatives that could have occurred but the harmonization is partial and there are very high cost and burden for manufacturers to manage the scheme in each market. To some extent, it helped to improve recycling and traceability. However, the improvement is limited for collection and recycling rates of metallic products. In addition, it does not always encourage the best practice in terms of circular economy. In some countries, the application of WEEE does not give enough freedom to equipment manufacturers to develop the reuse of products and components.

initiative: External power supplies – ecodesign & information requirements (review)
feedback_text:
ANEC-BEUC comments to Call for Evidence on External power supplies – Ecodesign & information requirements (review) 

We welcome the decision to review the Ecodesign Regulation for External Power Supplies. This will allow a complete assessment whether the regulation on EPS is successful in achieving its objectives. - Especially considered there was a long delay between the preparatory work and the adoption of the 2019 regulation, this -will need to be checked against the most updated scientific picture and recent political developments. A review will allow addressing missing relevant Circular economy aspects and respective requirements.  

We agree the review should combine policy options b), c), d), e) and f).  

On the scope: we strongly agree that the scope of the Regulation should be extended to include EPS that are currently not covered by the Regulation’s Annex I (policy option b)) the potential for Ecodesign of other powered products such as headsets, portable speakers, tablets etc., could be covered with a view to also ensure coherence and complementarity with the Ecodesign Regulation on smartphones and tablets underway; and the ongoing Revision of Radio Equipment Directive. In the latter, consumer-relevant improvements1 are made in the context of the ‘common charger’ initiative. 

 It will be important for the revision in fact to ensure consistency between the new Ecodesign rules for EPS and the current discussions about the Radio Equipment Directive with the aim to harmonize charging ports and ensure interoperability of fast charging technology across the board of EPS. 

On information requirements: We also agree with policy options c) and d) to introduce information requirements and require compliance with USB-PD specifications for all or a subset of the EPS under the revised scope.  

On energy efficiency: We support policy option e) and agree the review should assess the feasibility of requirements on minimum energy efficiency at 10% load and whether further efficiency improvements can be achieved at lower loads. Since wireless charging is becoming increasingly popular, we agree that the review should also assess the possibility of introducing energy efficiency and information requirements for wireless chargers.  

On Material efficiency: However, the proposed policy options do not consider explicitly the potential for further material efficiency requirements. We suggest that they should be analysed as part of the policy options as well. These requirements could cover for instance refurbishment/recycling or the total material/weight (the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Working Plan study informs that there is a broad range of weight of EPS).  

Another important point we highly recommend looking at is whether to add minimum quality/safety requirements. In the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Working Plan study, some stakeholders expressed concerns that one universal power supply for a broad range of devices could create risks of low-quality power supplies or be potentially damaging electronic components in high end devices. New Ecodesign requirements could exclude this risk by for instance defining a minimum quality for all EPS.  

Finally, while an informed choice should be clearly given to consumers to buy or not a new charger each time a new device is purchased (so-called or unbundling opt-in), the potential for requirements of default decoupling between the chargers and the products and its impact on overall resource savings in the mid-term future should be analysed. In the context of economic impacts, the impact assessment study should also consider how use patterns of consumers might change in this regard as well as on the potential for purchase of refurbished products, besides used devices on the second hand market.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The WEEE directive has achieved a lot - but it is time to go ahead: - Standardization: Even if there is a common WEEE directive, each country has an own implementing act which causes a lot of problems. There are even countries which weak the EPR concept because they implemented a shared product responsibility which gives municipalities the duty to collect WEEE. There is no compensation of the costs which is linked to the EPR. But there also other problems which cause a big spread in the quality of collection and recycling. There is no level playing field. We recomand to implement a WEEE regulation. - Reduction of bureaucracy and souped up numbers: All countries have in detail an own interpretation of the scope as well as detailed product classifications. That causes high efforts especially for SME and is a barrier to enter other markets. Some countries count also WEEE in scrap as collected, even if they don´t recycle it compliant to the directive. There should be only one central point to register the products for Europe. Of course companies would later have to report per country but only from one point. There would be a common scope and less bureaucracy. Figures would be much more comparable. - Real implementation of the duty to design products in a way that it is possible to remove the batteries without any efforts. - Critical evaluation of the existing recycling targets: Based on changes in the composition of materials in many weee categories and stricter limit values which are based on the zero pollution ambition there are already today big problems to achieve the recycling targets in some categories. There are often no possibilities to improve the recycling rate. - Implementation of recyclingtests for new products: There are sometimes technical developments which cause big problems in the recycling. Producers often don´t know or even care about it. Simple recycling tests could fix that problem for the future. A famous example are batteries today. A mix of LMC and LFP in a battery pack will cause problems which can´t be fixed in all existing or (known) planed recycling lines. - Digital product passport: There should be an implementation of a dpp in the future to connect data from SCIP and many other sources to a single product. There should be a process to develop the correct technology and tools (QR, RFID, NFC, ...) and which data do we need in detail. - Export reporting: There is a lot of export of used equipment which is not reported. For example IT equipment which is often in leasing. That appliances stay in first use for several years. After that they are checked and a lot of it gets a second life in an other country. There is often no reporting about the export from 1st to the 2nd country. The companies in that business field are often not producer. Today there is not even the possibility to report it. In addition to that equipment like motors which are in scope are often used in machines which are not in scope of WEEE. Eol those products don´t end up in WEEE reports. - Minimum recycling content: There should be a minimum recycling content for plastics in new products. Of course it must be a PCR material. There should be a mechanism to find out which share is possible for which category. - Improve the enforcement: Of course the enforcement is the job of the member states. But the enforcement is always a big problem. At least there should be a part in the legislation to set up a kind of cross border working group to agree about a common understanding for: waste declaration, re-use, notifications (cross border transport of WEEE) etc.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The call for evidence for the interim evaluation of Erasmus + 2021-2027 and the final evaluation of Erasmus + 2014-2020 attracted the full attention of our EvaluUE Association. 
Our association brings together evaluators of European programmes. To this end, our members are involved in the European Education Area in three ways: as a European citizen, as education, training and youth professionals, and as evaluators for the Erasmus + programme, mainly in France, but also in Belgium, Spain, Luxembourg and Portugal. 
We are therefore sending you a summary of the first contributions of our members. Despite the tight deadline in summer when many members were on leave, however, we wanted to participate in this call for contributions. This call is fully in line with the momentum of our association to strengthen and improve this agenda. Our members appreciated being able to give their opinions and make proposals.
We recognise the challenge and complexity of managing a European programme at country level. 
We have ensured that the confidentiality rules related to our missions and our commitments to the Commission are respected.  
On the other hand, our association remains available to participate directly or indirectly in the targeted consultation.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: As DKWU I would like to give the following three suggestions to be changed: 1. Separation of preparation for re-use and recycling targets: The WEEE directive should mirror the waste hierarchy laid out in DIRECTIVE 2008/98/EC which prefers preparation for re-use over raw material recycling. Consequently, the currently combined target for preparation for re-use and material recycling (e.g. for category for 2, 5, 6, 7, 8 or 9 of Annex I: 75 % shall be recovered, and 55 % shall be prepared for re-use and recycled) should be divided into a separate target for preparation for re-use and a separate target for recycling. Example: For Office Imaging Equipment (IE) cartridges this could look like this: Target Calculation in accordance with WEEE for IE cartridges: Differentiated by technology: TONER: A: Remanufacturable rate/reusable fraction1: 80% B: Collection rate2: 75% possible re-use rate (A*B): 60% possible recycling ((1-A)*B): 15% current target (preparation for re-use + raw material recycling rate2): 55% INK: A: Remanufacturable rate/reusable fraction1: 73% B: Collection rate2: 75% possible re-use rate (A*B): 55% possible recycling ((1-A)*B): 20% current target (preparation for re-use + raw material recycling rate2): 55% 1 see: Waugh et al. 2018: https://op.europa.eu/de/publication-detail/-/publication/17b7b664-15f0-11e8-9253-01aa75ed71a1 2 see: WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU (https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32012L0019&from=DE) Annex V Part 2 c) 2. Separate EEE category IE cartridges or cartridges containing consumables in general: The collection and preparation for re-use of IE cartridges has grown to a very professional business starting in the early 1990. That means that systems now in place matured for over 30 years. In addition, single-use by design IE cartridges produce a lot of e waste (370 Mio. IJ cartridges1 * 40gr/cartridges + 135 Mio. Toner cartridges1 * 680gr*/cartridge = 106.600.000kg waste per year in the EU!) which could easily be prepared for re-use. Much easier than for example hair driers, toasters and cellular telephones. Printers and consumables such as cartridge have also explicitly been mentioned in the Circular Economy Action Plan (3.1). * Based on average weight of over 100 Mio. collected Toner cartridges. As a consequence, it would make a lot of sense to put IE cartridges or cartridges containing consumables in general into a separate EEE category of their own in WEEE 3.0. This would also make the measurement of target achievements in an IE Ecodesign SRI or an IE Ecodesign regulation much easier. 3. Mandatory marking of all plastics used in EEE in accordance with ISO 11469:2017-01: Example: On most of the Newly Build Cartridges (NBC) / Clone cartridges from the Far East you can hardly find any markings or pictograms and they do not name the plastics/plastic grades used in moulding them. One possible explanation why the plastic used is not named is that because a lot of recycled plastic is used in making the parts the manufacturer simply has no idea of what plastics and additives are mixed in his NBCs as the DecaBDE scandal (https://www.etira.org/posts/tag/decabde/) revealed. Another possible explanation could be that he knows what is inside but believes that it is better for him if his customers and the end user do not know what it is in for obvious reasons. A plastic product without proper identification of the plastics used, cannot be raw material recycled as the plastic is unknown. It can only end up in landfill or incineration. If an IE cartridge does not have a marking then it was purposely produced to be waste that ends up at its End of Life (EOL) in landfill or in incineration. This is an obvious fire-and-forget approach and not very sustainable and far from any kind of circular economy.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Ecoreset SA, supports the position of EERA for harmonization of requirements concerning all the cycles of collection, transportation, classification and treatment of electronic wastes across Member States in the form of an EU regulation. Greece is a fine example of imbalanced operations across Member states. The treatment facilities of electronic wastes are obliged to classify the incoming materials in more than 60 different categories, with subsequent unnecessary handling and reporting costs, without evident benefits for the environment and public health. The collection of electronic wastes in the country is mainly conducted by scrap dealers. Ecoreset SA supports new regulation that enforces the collection of WEEE by Municipalities and b2b networks. The involvement of scrap dealers collection results to wrong handling and scavenging of devices, with high environmental and health and safety risk. The collection should follow CENELEC standards. It is common that the WEEE treatment facilities undergo audits from various Organizations, like EPR schemes, licensing authorities etc and they are obliged to follow CENELEC standards. Similar audits should be implemented by EU regulation for the transporters of WEEE. The purchase of every electronic device in European Union should involve a recycling fee that corresponds to the specific conditions of each region. Spyros Karamoutsos Dr. Chemical Engineer General Manager
text_raw: Ecoreset SA, supports the position of EERA for harmonization of requirements concerning all the cycles of collection, transportation, classification and treatment of electronic wastes across Member States in the form of an EU regulation. Greece is a fine example of imbalanced operations across Member states. The treatment facilities of electronic wastes are obliged to classify the incoming materials in more than 60 different categories, with subsequent unnecessary handling and reporting costs, without evident benefits for the environment and public health. The collection of electronic wastes in the country is mainly conducted by scrap dealers. Ecoreset SA supports new regulation that enforces the collection of WEEE by Municipalities and b2b networks. The involvement of scrap dealers collection results to wrong handling and scavenging of devices, with high environmental and health and safety risk. The collection should follow CENELEC standards. It is common that the WEEE treatment facilities undergo audits from various Organizations, like EPR schemes, licensing authorities etc and they are obliged to follow CENELEC standards. Similar audits should be implemented by EU regulation for the transporters of WEEE. The purchase of every electronic device in European Union should involve a recycling fee that corresponds to the specific conditions of each region. Spyros Karamoutsos Dr. Chemical Engineer General Manager

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: In Europe, unlike in the rest of the world, the rate of growth of volume of WEEE that is properly treated and recycled exceeds the rate of growth of the volume of WEEE that is generated. The WEEE that is collected is also treated to higher standards than in the rest of the world. Thats largely thanks to twenty years of Extended Producer Responsibility legislation across Europe. However, legislation must be redesigned to make it fit for purpose for new market realities. The WEEE Forum will contribute constructively to a thorough evaluation of WEEE legislation with a view to improving its effectiveness, efficiency, relevance and added value. This is our response to the Call for Evidence.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
The European Heating Oil Association (Eurofuel) represents organisations that promote the use of liquid fuels for domestic heating in Europe. Our members are committed to ensuring the competitiveness and efficiency of heating with oil and liquid fuels, while also reducing its environmental footprint. We fully support the objective of Ecodesign to increase the efficiency of products.
We would like to point out that in the EU, 20 million households rely on liquid heating fuel. These households are mostly located in rural, off-gas grid areas, where no other affordable alternative to liquid fuel is available, due to technical, economic or social reasons. With this consideration in mind, we would like to comment on the Call for Evidence.

First, we welcome the recognition of hybrid heating systems, which are efficient and allow GHG reduction. We would suggest having a more open definition. Indeed, hybrid systems are a combination of different heating systems. It should not be limited to hybrid heat pumps.
Hybrid heating systems, which combine heating oil with renewable electricity, solar heat, or biomass, guarantee a reliable supply of heat. These hybrid systems are suitable for the modernisation of old buildings which have higher heating needs. The technology can also be used for well-insulated buildings with less need for heating. In addition, the integration of renewable electricity with innovative power-to-heat technology is also possible. If enough renewable energy is available, it can be converted into heat by means of a heat pump or a heating element in a buffer tank. This further reduces the consumption of heating oil, increases the share of renewable energy in the heating supply and stabilises the electricity grids.

Second, the document mentions also mentions “the appropriateness of specific labelling elements for boilers capable of burning hydrogen”. Indeed, technologies that are able to switch over to renewable energies should not be ruled out. This should be considered when creating the label. Likewise, many oil driven heaters are able to use up to 100% renewable fuels and could benefit from a specific labelling element.
In the same way, the document mentions the “necessary progress and deployment rates for more efficient alternatives to the fuel boilers that currently dominate the market”. Again, any technology are able to switch over to renewable energies should not be ruled out, and liquid fuels boilers can be more efficient (especially condensing ones, using low carbon or renewable liquid fuels).

Third, the document recommends the expansion of the scope for boilers. The threshold established at the rated heat output ≤ 1 MW does not seem appropriate. Heaters above 400 kW and up to 1,000 kW are tailor-made products and differ considerably. What’s more the testing will be difficult for most institutes. Therefore, we do not support the extension of Ecodesign to this product range.
Finally, we would like to make some proposals regarding the key issue of re-scaling the energy labels:

• Additional information on the label in 2023
o introducing a pictogram for the greening of energy carriers, including green gaseous and liquid fuels
o introducing a pictogram for smart appliances and digitalisation to empower the consumer and optimise sector coupling and actual energy use.


• Additional information on the label in 2027
Introduce an energy label with 2 scales:
o the first scale is an energy carrier specific scale, this would allow the differentiation of products within the same product family   
o the second scale is a multi-energy scale, this would still allow the comparability of products of other product families. 

Germany is working on the development of a “Green Fuels Ready Label”. We suggest the same approach at European level. This label would show the technical suitability for greenhouse gas neutral liquid fuels and any mixtures with fossil liquid fuels, and facilitate the selection of future-proof products.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Erasmus+ programme is very important for those who want to pursue an academic career and beyond. It also makes an important contribution to personal career development by offering individuals the opportunity for mobility during their studies and continues through MSCA projects to ERC projects. It is crucial for the development of one's research career. But only if you are at a university with an accredited training programme. Research institutes are excluded in this story and therefore not on par with colleagues employed at universities or faculties. 
In addition, Erasmus offers non-academic staff the opportunity to engage in lifelong learning by participating in staff exchanges, and research institute staff cannot participate in Erasmus actions (i.e. staff exchanges for training ). 
The inclusion of research institutes in the Erasmus programme as eligible institutions would be welcome and necessary. Never the less it would contribute to boosting research careers, to mobility and co-creating the ERA, to excellence in management of EU projects and shared EU values. 
I am at your disposal for further explanations.

Research institutes are excluded in this story and therefore not on par with colleagues employed at universities or faculties. In addition, Erasmus offers non-academic staff the opportunity to engage in lifelong learning by participating in staff exchanges, and research institute staff cannot participate in Erasmus actions (i.e. staff exchanges for training ). The inclusion of research institutes in the Erasmus programme as eligible institutions would be welcome and necessary. initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

 Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Position on reclassification
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Corrections related to the new calculation for packages of solar devices
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Include provisions on multivalent tanks
• Proposed corrections on the Product information 
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Network of Social Integration Enterprises, ENSIE, represents the interests of national and regional networks of ‘Work Integration Social Enterprises’ (WISEs) striving for more inclusive and integrated forms of employment at European level. WISEs are defined by three identifying principles: they are enterprises whose main objective is the social and professional integration of disadvantaged people; they are enterprises at the core of the economic system; and they develop a strong pedagogical dimension.
ENSIE, together with it network members based in different EU countries across Europe, has been participating in several Erasmus+ projects and has noticed several improvements all long the years of implementation.
First, themes and priorities addressed are taking into account the changes of the education and training needs of the civil society, which are also trying to match with the evolution of the labour market. The stress on skills, in particular upskilling and reskilling, as well as on the green and digital transition is key to encourage organisations working with local communities on the ground to take up analysis and findings and transfer them at national and local level. The role of WISEs, in this sense, is crucial, provided that they are implementing both non-formal and informal training through on-the-job tailored adult learning programmes. 
Second, ENSIE noted that more attention is now paid to the social and professional inclusion, especially when it comes to disadvantaged groups, often discriminated on the labour market. ENSIE and its members are, in fact, advocating for more opportunities towards vulnerable groups, who are not able to access the same chances as other groups of the society. Erasmus+, especially in the new programming period 2021-27, has clearly outlined the importance of addressing inclusion of people with support needs in several calls for proposals.
Moreover, Key Action 2 projects focusing on cooperation among organisations and institutions are supporting small organisations, not always used to deal with Erasmus+ projects, to foster networking, exchange of good practises, as well as research and analysis to better acknowledge the needs of the ongoing contexts and boost partnerships at transnational level. 
Simplification in financial management requirements when implementing Erasmus+ projects has been very much appreciated, given that non-profit organisations often struggle in complying with high bureaucracy and administrative levels of management, esteemed as time-consuming and hard, especially when the organisation’s staff is not composed of a lot of members.
In addition, ENSIE very much appreciates the possibility to apply to European level towards EACEA for cooperation partnerships (Key Action 2) besides the one for applications at national level towards the national agencies. In particular, this gives the opportunity for ENSIE, whose geographical scope is at European level, to take up coordination within consortia and to be able to involve new stakeholder organisations in Erasmus+ projects.
Also, the possibility to involve organisations based in third countries (not part of the EU) as partners is a clear added value of the new programming period. ENSIE is trying indeed to better involve its members not based in EU Member States to spread out adult learning opportunities for disadvantaged groups.

For more information, please contact Martina Paterniti, Project Officer, martina.paterniti@ensie.org

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: In 2013, the European Commission requested in a Mandate to European Standardization Organisations (ESOs) to develop European standards (ENs) for the collection, logistics and treatment, including recovery, recycling and preparing for re-use of waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE). Upon this, CENELEC created Working Group (WG) 6 and 7 under the Technical Committee TC111X and released the EN 50625 series and EN 50614 Standards. The WEEE Directive has led to greater waste management controls within Member States (MS) and improvements in the responsible collection, preparing for re-use and treatment of WEEE by diverting appliances to operators who properly remove hazardous components and separate fractions for use as secondary materials within the Union, in an environmentally sound manner taking care of the health and safety of workers. However, the implementation of the Directive in the individual MS has not been consistent leading to an unlevel playing field in environmental activities and treatment regulations, and an increase in the burden of documentation, reporting and enforcement. The European Standards (EN) and Technical Specifications (TS) were published in all MS and in various EU languages. They provide a base for application and certification. Certification organisations provide auditing services to collection, re-use and treatment operators meeting the criteria within the EN standards. Eight MS have incorporated the standards into their national legislation, making compliance against the EN 50625 mandatory by law (FR, NL, CZ, IE, LX, BE, SI, LT), other countries like RO, SE and DE reached agreements where PROs require compliance against the standards by their service providers. The inclusion of the standards into these national regulations and business agreements are critical for increasing the number of compliant facilities. The accredited certification body (WEEELABEX) accounts for 62 certified auditors, and 262 compliant re-use and treatment sites in 16 MS and the EEA. Other audits take place in most MS through certification bodies, approved by industry, e.g. the SE certification body (WEEE AUDITS) accounts for 12 treatments sites. Standards assist producers fulfilling their EPR obligations and treatment-operators of any size in meeting national WEEE regulations. They can be used to support the implementation of legislation and have a harmonizing effect on the EU Directive and to remove trade barriers and enhance economic growth. Their implementation allows operators and wider e-waste community to monitor performance and the efficiency of their processes, to calculate their recycling and recovery rates achieved and to trace waste fractions, all critical points in responsible WEEE management. The process of making and updating standards is transparent and consensus-based. The ENs are reviewed every 5 years and the TS every 3 years, thereby reflecting the state-of-the-art of technologies and market needs WG6 and WG7 gathered over 100 representatives from CENELEC national committees and external experts from the WEEE sector representing EEE manufacturers, waste collectors, preparing for re-use and extended producer responsibility organisations, the recycling industry, NGOs and independent experts and scientists. The production of the 6 EN and 7 TS Standards took 11 years, more than 250 meeting days (ca. 20 experts/meeting) and was supported by numerous external technical studies. Participation in meetings, preparation of texts, data collection, testing of procedures, etc., were covered by the resources of the WG members, who all volunteered their time during the development process. We believe standardisation improves the quality level of WEEE recycling and WEEE re-use and level the playing field. We trust that the evidence for the successes and failures in the WEEE Directive are useful and look forward to working with the Commission in further discussions dealing with the Evaluation. CENELEC TC111x/WG6

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We've been involved in 2 actions and within various calls of the Erasmus+ programme within the new programme phase. And have been active in the programme for many years. The programme is one of the greatest drivers to motivate indidividuals to expand their horizons, and gives way for expansion of institutional profiles within and outside of the EU. In addtion to the programme knowing a lot of "paper work", even with Erasmus without Papers and the new features, we find it very rigid when it comes to meeting the demand at where it is. Renumerations for new features are essentially too low to explore and give little incentive to try if one is not able to cover the real costs. Moreover, in the execution of the programme, there is little recogntioin for the capacity of institutions involved in the roll-out, and seemingly little understanding for operational adhoc choices and changes in practice or limited leniency when external effects play a part beyond the control of the insitutions. Factors can be: local and national laws (or changes thereof), the economic situation per country, the organizational set-up and institutional directions, ad-hoc policy decisions that change directions during a E+ programme phase, available capacity within instutions in relation to the former factors, availability of systems to support the process (at institutional side as well as EU/national agency side, see Benifiery Module issues and the effect the latter has on capacity and culture of a HEI), prioritization, timing and funding of sub actions such within as the EWP implementation versus implementation of E+ new features at institutions side. These factors play a part in the quality of the execution of the Erasmus+ programme within each institution. In short: At times, there seems to be a mismatch in what the programme demands, and what the institutions can utulize and offer with various and time multiiple factors at play. A solution can be to apply a less rigid framework, implying the institution can be trusted to supply and comply, and can account for their choices tailored to their point of departure, rather than having to comply in conformity at all costs. 

Nevertheless the programme has brought a wonderful broadening of our study programming and has lead to many incentives and (new) collaboration in research and education. A unmissable impulse and driver to creating and maintaining the EHEA, international profiles for institutions and competitive postion for the EU in the world.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: During the previous period, I was involved in Erasmus Plus projects, and these projects, from my point of view, are a great leader and have brought about a great change in the level of education, capacity building and exchange of cultures. The Libyan universities participating in these projects have directly benefited from the academic exchange projects, but unfortunately the weakness of the Erasmus representative office in Libya Represented by the Erasmus National Office, it greatly affected the effectiveness of the projects, as the office was not present on the ground and there was no staff, because for four years there was no working office.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
In the previous programming period, we have not worked with the ICM, as we have seen it as a good but, if even more administratively challenging programme than traditional Erasmus mobility. The inclusion in the new programme of a small part of the traditional Erasmus programme, which can be used outside Europe, makes it difficult between the 2 strands. It seems that they wanted to ensure that England could continue to be part of Erasmus even after a possible Brexit. 

The lack of availability of the digital solutions (BM/OLS, etc.) for the new programme by the Commission is a matter of criticism. Similarly, they have underestimated the process of digitisation itself around the other elements where external companies (e.g. MOVEON) are not ready at all, together with the Commission, with the digital solutions to be used by higher education institutions. 

For the programme, which began in 2014, it was the same, so you should have learnt their homework.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Hello, I am Saidqosim, and I am from Tajikistan. Erasmus is in my life since 2011. My Erasmus experience started from being a Student Exchange in Valencia, Spain, completing my master's degree in 2016, Santander, Spain, and making several teaching mobility programs as the teaching staff, Institutional Erasmus plus coordinator at my home university. It changed my whole life, and thanks to Erasmus I am, who I am. Most of my professional achievements, professional skills, and knowledge are related to my studies and experience while on Erasmus. 

Indeed it gives wide opportunities for talented students from around the world, to learn together, build together and live together in harmony. The values, knowledge and behaviour learnt during Erasmus it is unique, and only possible to get them here, none of the other programmes or studies may give them to you.    

I have lived Erasmus student life, and today I am creating opportunities for youth from Tajikistan to experience this unique lifestyle and way of learning. Learning is priceless, living Erasmus is priceless, and being a part of the Erasmus family is priceless - it is the history of 35 years, lives and stories of millions.  

Faithfully yours,
Saidqosim Mukhtorov

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ administration has despite continous efforts of universities become more and more komplex and time consuming, both for the administrators but also for the scholarship holders. Many good ideas such as green travel and inclusion are included in the new programme, however implementing these new possibilities are increasing the administive work load even more. 

The lack of functioning supporting tools, delays by the European Commission to put in place digitationsation tools, etc. result in mobility reporting problems and frustration. With the new programme period being in its second year, the problems with for example Beneficiary Module and OLS implementation are difficult to understand.
