initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The YES Forum is a European network of organisations working with and for young people with fewer opportunities. Since 2002, we work to improve the life chances of vulnerable young people, by promoting their social inclusion and developing their professional skills. The involvement of the YES Forum and its members on EU programmes to support the European youth through education and training dates back to Erasmus+ predecessors. Therefore, we have a longstanding experience with the positive aspects that the Erasmus+ programme brings for socially disadvantaged young people. 

In the past two years, the COVID-19 pandemic brought additional challenges to our work. Youth and social work organisations were not prepared – and, sometimes, unable – to adapt their work to the online environment. Changing the work to online meetings and activities was not as easy in this sector: many youngsters stopped attending the activities as the online format was no longer motivating for them. The so-called disadvantaged young people are a very special target group that needs constant support and motivation to be involved in the activities proposed by the organisations.  

It is regrettable that trainings and meetings that were originally planned to take place in person, but had to take place online, were covered at only 15% of the original cost. This very low percentage assumes that online meetings are less expensive than the ones in person, as there are no costs for accommodation, nor travel. However, this completely ignores that, among others: i) the concept of the training needs to be entirely adapted to the online format, resulting in extra work for planning; ii) the youth and social work organisations might not have enough technological equipment for all the youngsters, which would lead to renting of places for the online activities; iii) during the harder phases of lockdown, not all youngsters had an appropriate learning environment, stable internet connection and all the necessary equipment to follow trainings from their homes and could not receive the support of the youth work organisations.  

We very much welcome the newly introduced “Small-scale partnerships”, aimed at attracting new organisations, specially grassroot organisations, to take part in the programme. Although the administrative requirements aim at being simpler if compared to the Cooperation Partnerships, they are still bureaucratic for newcomers with no experience with EU-funded projects. In our experience, organisations that are totally new to the Erasmus+ projects still needed the support from more experienced organisations in filling the narrative and the financial requirements. This would still place a barrier for organisations that do not have any contact to other involved with Erasmus+ projects. 

We understand that launching a call for evidence should follow a predetermined internal timeline from the Commission; however, we find it disappointing that a call for evidence on such an important and relevant programme was open during summer, holiday time and so very little advertised. We reached out to many members of the network and only one was aware of this evaluation, which was sent to them only two weeks prior the final deadline through the newsletter of the National Agency.  

Together with a number of different organisations at the European, national and local level, we developed recommendations and concrete suggestions from practice for an inclusive implementation of the EU education programmes Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps and published them in 2020. The resulting position paper is attached.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
We welcome the initiative of the European Commission and the opportunity to be able to participate in the consultation concerning the revision of the Toy and Safety Directive. As a responsible manufacturer, we agree with the overall aim of the initiative, which is the protect children better against risks in toys and strengthen the Single Market for toys. Providing the perspective of manufacturer, we ask the European Commission to consider the following:

Improvements to the general risk assessment are welcome, nevertheless to give equal chance for all the manufacturers, they should not be a burden on smaller manufacturers, which could not be able to remain competitive with big companies with greater resources.

The TSD or regulation limits of CMRs and derogation improvements are welcome, but there must be scientific approach and evidence in risk determination, for the intended use of the toy and practical exposure to the risk.

The 36-month age limit should be retained, as it is well established with understandable symbols on the market. The age rating was made based on scientific evidences, including age and mounting behavior. 
This leads also to making distinguish between products intended for children under 36 months or placed into the mouth and balloons, which are not intended for such age and purpose. Recently harmonized norm EN 71-12:2016 distinguishes between this kind of products, providing different limit values for different categories of toys, which were classified based on scientific evidences of and risk assessments for targeted age groups with specific behavior therefore additional changes in limit values are not justified. 

The change from a directive to a regulation is welcome, which would improve efficiency of updates and amendments and also fast-track notification mechanism. Nevertheless but the risk must be clear, evident and measurable, supported by defined sampling procedure, including retain samples taking and storage conditions. The practices and procedures to be followed by every member state to evaluate the existing rules, only then make improvements. 

The Digital Passport program is welcome, but it must be well considered, what kind of information to be shared and how they would be protected. It is very important to understand, which kind of information will need to be communicated and disclosed. Information ensuring safety of products and understandable for consumers is different from technological information, formulas and other documents, which basically manufacturers are obliged to describe in Technical File and make available only for authorities. In other case the information may result in misuse or cyber-attacks.

Information ensuring safety of products and understandable for consumers is different from technological information, formulas and other documents, which basically manufacturers are obliged to describe in Technical File and make available only for authorities. In other case the information may result in misuse or cyber-attacks.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
We are thankful for the opportunity to comment in view of the launch pf the Impact Assessment Study for the revision of the ecodesign and energy labelling regulations for space and combination heaters.

Concerning the proposed scenario for the ecodesign regulation, we are concerned that the proposed efficiency levels are not aligned with the climate target of the EU. We find that the climate mitigation scenarios as well as the interest of European citizens cannot be well served by the proposed levels and we recommend that the minimum requirement for seasonal heating energy efficiency should be set at 110% for all technologies to rapidly shift the market towards efficient decarbonised heating as of 2025. 

We welcome and support the revision of the energy labelling regulation and applaud the effort made to base crucial aspects of the proposal on consumer research. We recommend that the scale should be adjusted to align with minimum requirement for seasonal heating energy efficiency of 110%, which may allow sufficient granularity in the highest classes to fit low temperature regime on the same scale as medium temperature regime. Moreover, we recommend that the promotion of “hydrogen readiness” on the label should be abandoned, and that the shift to the new label should take place as soon as possible.

Detailed comments are provided in the attached document.

- Analysis of the added value and contribution of education institutions from non-associated countries / partner countries in Erasmus+ funded projects, e.g. as associated self-funded partners or as full partners (where possible).
- Analysis of the synergies and compatibility between Erasmus+ and similar national programmes to support European collaboration in education, such as the Swiss programme for Erasmus+ or the Welsh Taith scheme.

It is a prerequisite that young people from across Finland and Europe meet. 

Mobility was very good as long as I received a picture of what the mobility-tool would require me and my project to report.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: APPLiA - Home Appliance Europe welcomes the evaluation of the WEEE legislation which will provide an opportunity to assess fully if the current WEEE Directive remains fit for purpose. We are one of the sectors with most experience of the WEEE Directive. Please find below our initial comments in response to the Commission's evidence call - summary below and a PDF document attached providing further elaboration of our recommendations. We look forward to working with the Commission in the months to come, as the evaluation work progresses. Key Challenges of WEEE Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) concept is outdated Other actors outside EPR channels, driven by commercial considerations, handle significant volumes of waste. These volumes do not enter formal EPR systems. Not all WEEE is being recovered and recycled properly. While producers have taken an active role in fulfilling their legal obligations, other WEEE actors in the value chain also have responsibilities to ensure all WEEE is accounted for and treated properly. Much WEEE is not reported or accounted for While producers and producer schemes are asked to report the WEEE flows collected and treated, other actors handling WEEE can easily by-pass such reporting obligations, even if the WEEE they handle is properly collected and treated. Unrealistic WEEE collection targets Difficulties experienced by almost all Member states in reaching the collection targets opens up the debate about how realistic and fit for purpose the collection targets as defined in the Directive actually are. Raising the bar on treatment of WEEE across the EU There is still not a harmonised approach across the EU on the quality of WEEE treatment. Not all actors handling WEEE are recycling according to the EN treatment standards. Our Recommendation - Bring WEEE legislation in line with market realities and the current situation with WEEE management today "WEEE Are All Responsible" WEEE legislation should target all actors dealing with WEEE and give them the same obligations as producers under the extended producer responsibility principle. The Commission should develop requirements for all actors under the future WEEE legislation. Better monitor WEEE flows and ensure all WEEE is reported All treated WEEE, in line with the quality treatment standards, should be reported and registered as being recycled so that these volumes are reflected in the total collection and recycling results in a given jurisdiction. A shared responsibility between all WEEE actors can only be effective if there is an organised, coordinated system/body in place that is monitoring WEEE management. Review the EU target setting methods In addition to the establishment of a shared responsibility system to support in achieving collection rates, where all actors should contribute to the attainment of the target, the way the collection target is set and calculated should be reevaluated. We would recommend that the Commission investigate the possibility to continue to use a collection target based on the EEE 65% POM based on the average lifespan of the individual WEEE category rather than a blanket 65% based on the average of the previous 3 years POM. Recycling of WEEE according to EN treatment standards To ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of the WEEE Directive, the Commission should ensure that EN 50625 / EN 50614 standards are the reference for WEEE treatment. Enforcement of recycling treatment in line with these standards would help ensure market dynamics are not diverting flows from the formal system towards illegal activities. Empower the role of consumers The role of consumer behaviour is an important aspect to consider in the drive to increase material recovery from WEEE. Engaging consumers in adopting sustainable choices, particularly in the ways they dispose of their electrical products can help in increasing collection and recovery rates.

feedback_text:
The opportunity of job shadowing with other institutions was an invaluable experience to learn different skills, especially with the National qualification database. The training helped us to improve and coordinate the day-to-day user operations. The study visit enabled us to familiarise ourselves with technical aspects of the database and establish more hand experience. We also covered different functions of other work related to other departments with experienced personnel from the visiting institution of their organization. We also gave a presentation on how MFHEA operates within different units of the entity. 

Objective/s of the mission or general overview of the mission

The Erasmus+ job shadowing project shall place the MFHEA in a better position to properly coordinate the day-to-day end user operations the National Qualifications Database as well as to improve and develop further the services rendered to individuals and stakeholders. It is designed to send four MFHEA staff members at a time to visit each of the designated hosting organizations: Estonian Qualifications Authority, Institute of the Republic of Slovenia for Vocational Education and Training, Academic Information Centre in Latvia. Each staff member taking part in the job shadowing exercise had exposure to different facets of the organization's varied departments and related functions. This wasa be done in conjunction with the host organisations to cover all the necessary areas that relate to maintenance of national qualifications databases.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: 1-Collection targets: This is a crucial issue of the WEEE directive. The collection target is either 65% of EEE put on market or 85% of WEEE generated. WEEE generated is the EEE put on market minus exports (EEE & WEEE), minus the EEE stock variation of households and professional users. Exports are probably in the range of 15-20% but need to be better measured. Stock variation in households and professional premises is mostly unknown but could be significantly positive : we are in a rich society able to buy additional equipment to augment functionalities (larger screen, higher definition, ) or duplicate equipment (secondary house, ) or to fulfil a new requirement (monitor for work@home, ). It needs to be measured but it could be in the more than 25% range. This would imply that even if WEEE generated was 100% collected the collection target of 65% of put on market cannot be reached. The logic would be to turn to the 85% of WEEE generated objective. But WEEE generated is not calculated as indicated above, the EU has provided the member states a calculation model (UNU model) which consolidates all past put on the market quantities and based on estimated lifetime for each family of product, calculates what will finish as waste on a given year. It does not take into account exports nor the effect of repair and reuse to extend the lifetime of products. It does not take into account the state of the economy which is essential to both the consumer market and the waste generation. In short, it is a theoretical model with too many parameters to be trustworthy. In France WEEE generated according to the UNU model is roughly the same as EEE put on the market consequently the 65% of PoM objective must be used. Recommendation: The collection objectives need to be reevaluated to be achievable. They should be a percentage of an estimated WEEE generated based on an annual field study rather than a theoretical model. 2-Illegal activities: WEEE can have a positive value from the resale secondary raw materials when treated illegally. In France all operators recycling WEEE are required to have a contract with a PRO whose mission is to oversee that the operator operates legally on all positive value WEEE. The PRO provides a financed solution for all negative value WEEE collected by the operators. This device allows the precise tracking of all WEEE. It requires a close collaboration between the PRO and the Public Authorities to enforce the contractual obligation on the more reluctant operators. Recommendation: Require all WEEE operators to have a contract with a PRO or a producer to track all e-waste. Promote joint actions of PRO and public authorities to enforce legislation.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
I will give my feedback as an EU citizen, even if I represent a school/organisation. In the drop-down menu of the form, it should be possible to select the education sector to be represented by the feedback provider. Now only the higher education sector is represented in the education sector.

In the 2014-2020 programming period, our school implemented two KA1 projects. We really welcome both sending and receiving parties. The organisational support was good at that time (EUR 350) and covered both participants in courses and job shadowing. In the current programming period, the support for those going to the course is only EUR 100. Given that the organisational support was used to cover the salary costs of project management, it is unsustainable that, in the new project period, the organisational support for the person leaving the course is much lower, even though the administrative workload is exactly the same, whether it was a pathway or job shadowing. Please return the larger amount of aid! The increased cost level due to inflation should also be taken into account in future aid amounts. Moreover, it would be good if the host school were automatically reimbursed for a visit by Erasmus.

Unfortunately, the distance calculator does not serve our geographical location from and to Finland. For example, from Northern Finland to Iceland, the aid is only EUR 360, but no flight tickets at that price. Support for expensive travel costs, on the other hand, is so cumbersome to apply for a co-ordinator not even to tell you about this possibility. Support for people travelling through the country should also be better taken into account for Finland, as the absolute minimum is 3 days if you want to travel from Northern Finland to Central Europe through the country. It does not stop the journey, but in practice travels at all times and is acquainted with tide. There is no access to southern Europe within three days. However, there are certainly those who are willing to spend time, e.g. on their summer holidays, to access their Erasmus site through the country. Would it otherwise be possible to attach a reduced Interrail ticket to Erasmus, e.g. 50 % cheaper, which would make it more attractive by means of an easy-to-use ticket?

On behalf of Finnish course organisers, there are concerns about the unequal situation of foreign course organisers. Finnish organisers pay Alvia 24 % of their course, while foreigners do not have to pay anything to Finland. However, there is a clear advantage for Finnish work and the Finnish education system that courses in Finland are provided by those who are part of the education system and not by any of the course organisers who have seen the market ladder of taxation for their foreign companies.

The Erasmus+ programme is great and it is a great pleasure to be involved. I would be grateful if you would take account of my feedback when designing new actions. 

Thank you for Erasmus+!

feedback_text: The Erasmus program is a great opportunity in a student's life. For me it gave the chance to grow, develop and enrich my personality by getting to know new cultures and some great people. However, at the beginning there are lot of difficulties with the paperwork and the slow connections between the universities. The biggest problem was that there were courses listed for incoming students in English and in reality they didn't exist in English, information is sometimes outdated and incorrect. On the other hand, it gives us a variety of choice to better ourselves and have the greatest experience of a lifetime.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Youth Forum is the umbrella organisation for national youth councils and international youth organisations representing collectively millions of young people throughout the European Union and beyond. Our membership encompasses broadly diverse youth organisations led and driven by young people. Both our member organisations and the organisations represented by our members engage with and benefit from the Erasmus+ Programme. 
 
Therefore, we deem it highly relevant to bring our insights into the current call for evidence and overall in the mid-term evaluation of the Erasmus+ Programme. The present contribution is divided in three sections, firstly tackling the process to develop the mid-term evaluation, secondly its overall structure of the Erasmus+, and lastly overall general comments on the implementation of the Erasmus+ Programme so far.
 
PROCESS
 
The overall assessment of the Erasmus+ must be as unbiased and objective as possible. It is therefore essential to ensure that this mid-term evaluation is backed by additional evidence-based reports of relevant stakeholders, including (youth) civil society. In order to achieve this goal, the European Commission, the Directorate-General for Education, Youth, Sport and Culture, the relevant Executive Agency and the Erasmus+ National Agencies need to commit to make raw data accessible and ensure a maximum level of transparency throughout the whole process, from inception to completion. This includes the public availability of datasets on budget breakdowns, statistical data and National Authority reports developed for the mid-term evaluation - and particularly their respective summaries. Regarding the latter, in the previous mid-term evaluation process, numerous EU member states did not make such documents available, which impedes transparency and subsequently accountability.
 
STRUCTURE 
 
The last Erasmus+ mid-term evaluation did not comprehend chapter-specific reviews. Presenting the report in this way hampered the understanding of the actual impact of the programme on chapter-specific matters as well as the identification of issues relevant for each strand – including youth organisations and youth sector stakeholders. In order to produce a more comprehensible assessment on each of the domains covered by the Erasmus+ Programme, we recommend complementary chapter-specific analyses, including the Youth Chapter of the Erasmus+ Programme.
 
CURRENT IMPLEMENTATION 

Although the scope of the current call for evidence is too limited to provide a comprehensive assessment, there are two issues that have considerably impacted the experience of our membership and youth organisations in general in the current Erasmus+ Programme:
 
Centralised actions – the introduction of centralised grants serves tailored to the realities of European NGOs serves as a stepping stone in their acknowledgement as partners towards the achievement of the EU-Youth strategy. However, in the last three years international youth organisations’ applications are increasingly being rejected, including in the call for Operating Grants for in the field of youth. This is restricting the operational and structural capacities of youth organisations, after years of advocating for such grants.
  
Delays in EU Youth Dialogue grants for National Working Groups – National Working Groups are experiencing regularly delays in the receipt of funding to implement their consultations. This is heavily delaying and impeding a meaningful consultation process in the member states, which is an essential part of the EU Youth Dialogue. Moreover, it burdens the National Youth Councils that are the beneficiaries of these grants in most of the cases. 

Find attached the Shadow Report to the mid-term evaluation commissioned by the European Youth Forum in the previous MFF
text_raw: The European Youth Forum is the umbrella organisation for national youth councils and international youth organisations representing collectively millions of young people throughout the European Union and beyond. Our membership encompasses broadly diverse youth organisations led and driven by young people. Both our member organisations and the organisations represented by our members engage with and benefit from the Erasmus+ Programme. Therefore, we deem it highly relevant to bring our insights into the current call for evidence and overall in the mid-term evaluation of the Erasmus+ Programme. The present contribution is divided in three sections, firstly tackling the process to develop the mid-term evaluation, secondly its overall structure of the Erasmus+, and lastly overall general comments on the implementation of the Erasmus+ Programme so far. PROCESS The overall assessment of the Erasmus+ must be as unbiased and objective as possible. It is therefore essential to ensure that this mid-term evaluation is backed by additional evidence-based reports of relevant stakeholders, including (youth) civil society. In order to achieve this goal, the European Commission, the Directorate-General for Education, Youth, Sport and Culture, the relevant Executive Agency and the Erasmus+ National Agencies need to commit to make raw data accessible and ensure a maximum level of transparency throughout the whole process, from inception to completion. This includes the public availability of datasets on budget breakdowns, statistical data and National Authority reports developed for the mid-term evaluation - and particularly their respective summaries. Regarding the latter, in the previous mid-term evaluation process, numerous EU member states did not make such documents available, which impedes transparency and subsequently accountability. initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tanks

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
UL welcomes the opportunity to submit feedback on the European Commission’s Inception Impact Assessment regarding the Revision of the Toy Safety Directive (TSD).

UL has a solid experience with regards to toy safety and is representing one NB under Directive 2009/48/EC Safety of toys.
Sharing the Commission’s assessment of the situation, there are existing and future challenges that need to be addressed by revision of the TSD. We would appreciate it if the Commission would consider the following points in its continuing work.

1/ Derogations and limits for CMR and for other substances that will be added to the revised directive/regulation shall be clearly identified and easy to be applied/interpreted.

2/ UL is in favor of a clear and consistent approach on how to develop chemical safety assessment if this will include more substances (e.g. endocrine disrupters, PBT) and combination effects of chemicals.

3/ Regarding the intention to restrict substances like endocrine disrupters or PBT, a clear classification criteria for endocrine disrupters and PBT substances are needed to make sure that if included in the restriction of the TSD this will be applicable in a consistent way.

4/ Chemical restrictions that will be added in the revised directive/regulation (e.g. in Appendix C or also for toy for children >36 month) should be clear in their applicability and test methods to be applied. In case test methods need to be developed, the standardization process should be aligned with the timing of regulation.

5/ With regards to the intention of setting of chemical limit values for any toys and not only for those intended for children under 36 months, it is necessary to have legal certainty for the chemical requirements and not chemical assessment.

6/ With regards to the Digital Product Passport, UL is of the opinion that the Digital Product Passport could help speed up enforcement, but it is of utmost importance that it is clearly defined which are the documents/information it shall contain and the differences with technical documentation currently requested in TSD or with other compliance docs e.g. Declaration of Conformity.

7/ UL calls for harmonized requirements within EU with, if possible, no national deviations. Therefore, UL supports the ambition of the conversion from a Directive to a Regulation.

8/ UL stresses the need for speed up the timeframe from publication of standards to harmonization of them. 

UL respectfully submits our feedback and look forward to the upcoming public consultation. For further inquiries and discussions, we are always at your disposal.

About UL: 
UL is a premier, global independent safety, sustainability and security science company that has championed progress for more than 126 years. Its nearly 16,000 professionals across 44 countries are guided by the UL mission to promote safe and secure working and living environments for all people. They do so through the application of safety science and hazard-based safety and security engineering. The application of these principles manifests itself in the safety and security evaluation of tens of thousands of products, components and systems for compliance to specific requirements. All our work, from independent research and standards development, to assessment, verification services and certification, to providing analytical and digital solutions, helps improve the well-being of people and places globally.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The European Electronics Recyclers Association (EERA) recognises there have been many advantages in the EU WEEE Directive, which aimed at ensuring the polluter pays, and that the full life cycle of a product is obligated under extended producer responsibility requirements. The following represents our continued call for change. Harmonisation: This has not been accomplished, causing conflicts within the industry and an increase in the burden of documentation, reporting and enforcement. The fact that on average only 45 percent of the WEEE is responsibly collected and treated in the EU, while the target from 2019 onwards has been 65 percent, is an indication that the policy circle is not functioning optimally. EERA calls for harmonised requirements across Member States in the form of an EU Regulation, thereby holding binding legal force to be transposed in every Member State and overruling national laws. Scope: The current divergences between the different countries create imbalanced operational conditions (e.g. Greece uses 63 sub-categories). Furthermore some producer collective schemes still operate using a combination of the previous and most recent WEEE categories, causing confusion and a lack of cohesion, especially for inter-European shipments. More emphasis should be placed on the classifications according to product content, hazards, and treatment requirements rather than size. Collection: As the collection practices are inconsistent across Europe, undocumented routes (especially illegal or less stringent export routes) will be at the forefront of the reasons why the EUs collection targets are not being met. Producers and producer collective schemes should remain responsible for all EEE they put on market, not strictly just WEEE that is available to them. EERA maintains that there should be a mandatory handover of WEEE to approved European WEEE collectors and compliant re-use and recycling operators. Proper Treatment: The WEEE Directive states: ..different national applications of the producer responsibility principle may lead to substantial disparities in the financial burden on economic operators. Having different national policies on the management of WEEE hampers the effectiveness of recycling policies. For that reason, the essential criteria should be laid down at the level of the Union and minimum standards for the treatment of WEEE should be developed. 2012/ 19/ EU (6). The fact that some MS have implemented mandatory certification of WEEE operators is positive, but the leakage across internal European borders to those countries with poorer waste management controls fosters unfair competitive advantages and discourages the stability needed for investing and maintaining existing and new innovative recycling technologies. The continued tolerance of non-compliant routes leads to the loss of critical raw materials (CRMs) and valuable secondary materials. EERA continues to press for mandatory treatment standards to be in place across Europe. Recycling and Recovery Targets: These are overly focused on weight-based metrics rather than quality focused goals that could include the recovery achievements of key CRMs and of the removal of hazardous components (e.g. batteries. Substantiated estimates provided by some MS allow inflated and uncorroborated data that also provides no evidence that correct treatment and recovery has been carried out. EERA continues to advocate that targets should be more tailored to specific product types, with more emphasis placed on other targets within the waste hierarchy beyond solely material recovery, and with recycling and re-use rates being assessed in unison. Substantiated estimates should not be permitted. We remain open to on-going collaboration.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: WEEE Ireland has worked to develop on behalf of the Irish electrical industry a sustainable compliance and takeback system since the first transposition of the WEEE Regulations here in 2005. In the WEEE Compliance Promotion Exercise undertaken by the Commission in 2017, Ireland was highlighted as a small country case study for positive progression with WEEE Directive Implementation, showing high achievements in quantitative criteria and a strong set of qualitative measures. These included formal stakeholder platforms for collaboration, strong collection networks and public collection events, mandatory EN standard WEEE treatment, and visible fees to consumers for some products. Despite the above mentioned, collection targets have been proved difficult to achieve in recent years, in part because there are streams of e-waste outside of the authorised systems through different WEEE Flows not captured under the current reporting system. To change that, the WEEE Forum and WEEE Ireland strongly advocate that all actors must have WEEE reporting obligations. The rise in consumption of EEE is also heavily impacting collection rates under the current 3-year POM average methodology. The most recent national report from Ireland on WEEE collection rates highlights an 11% increase in placed on the market EEE statistics in one year alone. However, the longer life span of certain products that are new to the market (e.g. heat pumps, PV panels), new house builds, and the high levels of repair activity as outlined in our submission, are not taken into account when considering environmental management targets. This should be addressed in the future iteration of the Directive with a strong case for available for collection methodology to be developed. Moreover, there are other areas of the Directive that can be improved for a better outcome when achieving the targets indicated in the Directive. In such terms, WEEE Ireland strongly supports WEEE Forum positions including those related to i) EPR; ii) circular economy; iii) critical raw materials; iv) collection targets; v) recycled content and standards; vi) eco-modulation; vii) consumers; viii) financial policies; ix) enforcement and x) simplified small producers and micro enterprise compliance. Collaboration between national authorities and Producers as well as retailers is important for engagement with consumers. The WEEE like for like, one for one takeback requirement in store and on delivery of new appliances and devices is a very important aspect of the Irish system, where municipal collection points are not at the same level per capita as elsewhere. Most Irish electrical retailers participate in the WEEE Ireland programme Well Take it back where free recycling of all household e-waste and batteries is taken back with no purchase required. 55-60% of takeback is through the retail collection point network. WEEE Ireland has also undertaken comprehensive marketing campaigns with strong calls to action to support changes in behaviours and attitudes to WEEE recycling since the Scheme began in 2005. The public collection day campaigns and associated press releases and media coverage are good examples of this at local community level . National press, radio and digital campaigns focus on different themes, but all aim to deliver increases in takeback and consumer awareness to tackle the e-waste challenge. Some of our recent Consumer Communication Campaigns are a) Dream of Change; b) Small things matter; c) Free recycling and; e) International e-waste day, among others. WEEE Ireland on behalf of more than 1300 Irish industry members, celebrates and welcomes the Commissions initiative to modernise the European Directive 2012/2019/EU on Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: It is said that for every 1 tonne of e-waste Europe recycles, 2 tonnes are a lost opportunity. Therefore, Eurometaux representing the European non-ferrous metals producers and recyclers is calling for actions to establish a level playing field for high-quality recycling. Our key recommendations: WEEE collection: Encourage Member States to increase separate collection of e-waste supported by actions targeting citizens to properly dispose of WEEE. Level playing field conditions: Make the standard EN 50625 on WEEE collection, logistic and treatment and its Technical Specifications legally binding under the WEEE Directive. Coherence of the EU raw materials, chemicals, product & waste legislation: Ensure coherence between on-going & future EU policies developments to maximise circularity and value of metals extracted from e-waste. For more details, please consult the attached document.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
text_raw: The University of Porto recognises Erasmus + opportunities for excellence for its academic community and for itself as an organisation. However, I would like to highlight some aspects in the context of the management and implementation of Credit Mobility that deserve analysis and possible adjustment, as well as to give some suggestions: A. Participants from the various typologies report in their individual reports that they find it difficult to carry out mobility with such a small grant. In addition to a general revision/update of the grant table, we consider that, in the case of students and recent graduates, consideration could be given to the option of allocating a travel support envelope, as is the case at the LCI and even in the KA131 itself for staff and also for students and recent graduates from the outermost regions, thus promoting greater equality of treatment between the different beneficiaries and increasing the participation of more disadvantaged backgrounds students. As a result of the concern about the inclusion priority, UPorto has approved the application of the project KA2 Erasmus mus4All (2021-1-PT01-KA220-HED-000023302), building precisely on the difficulties and financial shortcomings expressed by the participants that have been registered over the years; B. considers that there should be greater flexibility in relation to financial distribution by allowing, for example, the payment of days to the ‘0 grant’ after the end of the mobility, in the event that there is still funding due to final date hits or last-minute withdrawals. This would allow for a more efficient management of funding, also contributing to increased satisfaction from the fellows; C. we suggest creating a top-up for the automatic allocation of a percentage stipulated solely on the basis of the SEN, by category, in the light of the application documents proving this need. This would allow the National Agency (NA) to manage more effectively the amount allocated to this type of complementary support. At present, from the point of view of bureaucratic visa, SEN fellows are disadvantaged in relation to, for example, social action fellows; D. Erasmus + mobility participants for studies or traineeships have increasingly requested mobility of longer duration, so that there should also be an adjustment in relation to the minimum periods that are funded by the NA/EC; E. in order to help mitigate the challenges caused by housing pressure across Europe, a hosting platform linked to the Erasmus + programme, a European database, could be set up, with the various offers available in each city and where registered offers would be permanently available to host mobility students. Students would even be able to assess their stay and provide feedback on the accommodation in which they were staying, and thus there would be an aggregated tool where students could consult offers according to their destination and, through evaluations, already find additional information on, for example, the quality of the accommodation; f. It would be very beneficial to have an information aggregator of the different projects coordinated by each HEI in order to “extract” in detail from MT + (or WB) the number of fellows and the funding granted, per academic year and project duration (e.g. all projects approved in 2019); G. we also suggest creating a single European traineeship platform, or strengthening the erasmusintern.org platform, as a major “E + traineeship offer repository” in the EU to allow for a more dynamic dissemination of SMP opportunities. Applicants have great difficulties in getting a traineeship in the EU, as the market is very large.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
The EU needs to rapidly reduce its dependence on fossil fuels, including Russian gas, to stop financing dictatorial regimes, meet its 2030 climate targets and preserve consumers’ purchasing power.
First, the deep thermal renovation of buildings needs to be accelerated much more vigorously and become an absolute priority for the EU and the Member States. Local biogas production is also a route to be developed.
The revision of the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Regulations for centralised heating is also an essential tool and an urgency. It needs to be finalised very quickly, and given the international context and the ongoing energy crisis, it is now clear that it needs to be much more ambitious than what has been proposed so far.
It should set a clear date before 2030 for a ban on the marketing of new fossil fuel oil and gas boilers. It should also ban inefficient electric boilers as soon as possible and strengthen requirements on heat pumps and hybrid systems to avoid poor quality installations that would lead to uncontrolled electricity consumption peaks. 
It would also be useful to introduce a third party performance certification system in order to ensure upstream verification of the actual quality of the equipment.
Finally, the requirements should apply to all boilers, including very large boilers (above 400 kW).
On the other hand, we have serious doubts about the relevance of a hydrogen sector for heating buildings. Green hydrogen, which will be available in limited quantities, should be used primarily for uses where there are no alternatives (e.g. industry).

CLER — Energy Transition Network 
https://cler.org/

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: I undertook a study year abroad in Germany from August of 2020 to August of 2021. Overall the year was invaluable to me especially as a British national post Brexit as it gave me the possibility to take a graduate job in Germany within the same company I did my Erasmus+ placement with. I do however think there is a problem relating to lack of oversight from Erasmus as the company I worked for did not utilise me as well as they could have done instead opting to use me for cheap labor to do the tasks that they couldn't get company money to fund. That brings me onto my next point. We were told by the placement organisation that to encourage taking students from abroad the German government gave the company €1000 a month as a grant to help pay our wages. However because the company also knew that we got Erasmus+ funding they then opted to only pay us the government grant as our wage. To my understanding the Erasmus+ grant is mainly meant to be used for helping the student move to and from their place of study either to cover travel costs or deposits on accommodation etc. However because of this attitude from the employer to essentially get a year of free labor this fund went entirely on living costs. Additionally the Erasmus grant isn't particularly useful for transport and setting up in the new country when you can't get the first payment until you've started work and can't get the second payment until after you've finished. I do think overall the Erasmus+ scheme is fantastic and without it I wouldn't be living and working in Germany now bot I do think the scheme needs to be reworked and certainly have a higher degree of oversight that holds the placement organisations to account more.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
My daughter had 10 months in Maastricht in 2021-2022. She experienced and preferred a different way of learning to the Irish university she currently attends. The opportunities to learn modules that were otherwise outside of her home curriculum, broadened her knowledge and her choices of further education and topics she was interested in.
The social anxiety she could struggle with was challenged and my daughter came home with friends from at least 10 other countries, had travelled to visit 5 of them. She made the most of her time in mainland Europe and has found that Ireland is too small for her now! She feels very European and travelled for a month, after the Erasmus was over, with 10 people to 6 countries. All the young people were new friends from her time in Maastricht.
I will always be grateful to the EU for her fantastic life changing year abroad.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Programme calls and priorities are well established.
Some comments and recommendations:
1. In nowadays evolution one would expect to see under ERASMUS+ a very open call for any type of proposed innovation regardless of the priorities set by the calls.
2. It will be useful to unify the rules of all calls. 
3. It will be useful for the decentralized programmes to have a common application of rules...different NA apply or demand different rules. Some are minor but are not necessary to be different as an institution may participate in more than one project and has to know how the NA of one project wants certain reporting documents against other NA.
4. Small scale projects of the type of Capacity Building for discovering the needs or creating a needs analysis of neighborhood countries may be useful.
5. the lump-sum rule is excellent but it is not clear if we do or we don't need to keep evidence for the case of audit. If evidence is needed then lump-sum idea is useless and only serves the funding authority in reporting docs while the project coordinator and partners need to continue to collect evidence. Also the lump-sum creates more bureaucracy for the coordinating organization and partners as the budget has to be managed on actual costs (since there are no unit costs) and evidence or proof is needed.
6. More regional activities by the European Commission with local stakeholders may be useful.
7. Thematic conferences for presenting funded project results may be useful for synergies and sustainability. Such conferences can be organized by the EC or can be subcontracted to NGOs and such events can be organized in neighborhood countries also,

ČMOS PŠ welcomes the increased financial support of Erasmus+ on initial and continuous professional development of teachers. initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
The EU needs to rapidly reduce its dependence on fossil fuels, including Russian gas, to stop financing dictatorial regimes, meet its 2030 climate targets and preserve consumers’ purchasing power.
First, the deep thermal renovation of buildings needs to be accelerated much more vigorously and become an absolute priority for the EU and the Member States. Local biogas production is also a route to be developed.
The revision of the Ecodesign and Energy Labelling Regulations for water heaters is also an essential tool and an urgency. It needs to be finalised quickly, and given the international context and the ongoing energy crisis, it is now clear that it needs to be much more ambitious than what has been proposed so far.
It must impose the use of the most efficient technologies, such as renewable energy and thermodynamic water heaters. It must continue to remove fossil fuel water heaters and electric hot water storage tanks from the market for XL capacity and then below it with a clear timetable.
Furthermore, the equipment test must be carried out in ‘normal’ mode and not in a so-called ‘eco’ mode that allows manufacturers to display better performance.
It would also be useful to introduce a third party performance certification system in order to ensure upstream verification of the actual quality of the equipment.
On the other hand, we have serious doubts about the relevance of a hydrogen sector for heating domestic hot water. Green hydrogen, which will be available in limited quantities, should be used primarily for uses where there are no alternatives (e.g. industry). 

CLER — Energy Transition Network 
https://cler.org/

feedback_text:
I was involved in a three year project. This generated a range of benefits related to global competence. We created resources for teachers, academic papers, reports, pupil exchanges in Wales, Belgium and Barcelona.
Through my university it enabled me to develop links with colleagues in different countries, to visit similar institutions and to share good practice on a wide scale. 
The resources we developed during the project can now be used by teachers in the Uk and throughout the EU, this promotes the area of global sustainability.
On a personal level it provided me with a range of challenges - from presentations and the creation of resources from schools, to organising a large multiplier conference with more than a hundred attendees. 
The project was such a positive experience for all involved and the benefits will continue to be seen as the resources created are used and shared to a greater extent in schools and universities.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Other so bad times in the organisation of the Erasmus programme, information and workflows are not remembered. The 2021-27 period opened with an unprecedented delay, lacked (and so far) detailed, transparent, unambiguous instructions for the implementation of new activities. 
The toolkit (Beneficiary Module, then BM) became available with a delay of more than half a year and is so far unusable (more than one year from the start of the project in 2021). It is incomprehensible that the Commission does not provide universities with information, apologies or timetable in this regard. There is no possibility to provide direct feedback and the NA requests do not provide answers or solutions. 
The use of funds for the reception of Ukrainians is the only decision, but it is unclear why it is organised through K131 projects where the incoming (incoming) student/employee financing and paperwork are not organised under K171. Also without dedicated funding, creating only flexibility in the use of financial instruments. This makes it difficult to manage and plan projects. It would be helpful to respond to the new situation at national level with increased resources and to coordinate the processes and support activities related to the support and adoption of the new target group. It is not conceivable that the existing human resources will be able to provide sufficient support to universities. 
Short-term blended mobility is certainly a welcome opportunity for both participants and universities. However, they could have been viewed separately — mobility in mobility projects and organisational support for the organisation of BIP on a stand-alone basis. The organisation of BIP in the mobility workflows is not fit and can therefore affect the quality of the overall project reporting and the future opportunities for mobility. BIP promoters or developers and performers are specialised units which should be able to report on their project themselves, but all of them carry out the same project in BM. This is a significant obstacle in larger universities. There is a very uneven level of awareness among (foreign) universities of the requirements for organising the BIP (as well as NA instructions), while the requirement of a minimum of 15 students (marked through BM) may lead to the reimbursement of many BIP organisation grants. Although the courses are carried out and the costs incurred, but (a) whether the participants abandon the last minute and there is no coercive mechanism for universities to force them to participate in a changing world, (b) sending universities do not indicate (BM does not work, no awareness, actually sends the organiser’s BIP identifier and the information does not run together) to the mobility.This is not an overly flexible scheme, and the level of detail of the information collected in BM is unreasonable. 
Blended mobility grants are low for students and do not cover costs. The documentation required for participation in a small-scale course (3-6 ECTS) is disproportionate, from the perspective of both the participant and the university. For the latter, the data set in Benecifiary Module is incomprehensible. Obtaining precise guidance on the requirements has been an undescribed, undescribed, query-based process. 
The possibility of using up to 20 % of the budget for mobility projects with non-programme countries — once K171 has been created, it would only be possible to simplify and increase flexibility for this programme, rather than grafting it to another programme. How can professional entities be guided in large universities to conclude cooperation agreements, while ensuring that the total amount of mobility funding for all activities in the respective project does not exceed 20 % of the budget? 
OLS — prior to the completion of the new system, the previous system was closed, while participating students maintained the obligation to carry out an OLS test in the main language of instruction prior to their mobility. The EC sent a text for participants to the National Agencies (for transmission to universities), which did not mention the obligation to sit a test in the main language of instruction before the mobility, but in advertising how this portal can learn what happened. The universities had to incorporate the EC’s requirements into the EC text and the NA was not required to do so by communicating it as necessary.  
Universities need more interlocutor rather than an auditor.

feedback_text: As an association, we have been participating for many years, since 1992 in all forms of mobility. initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Feedback from KR 103/107 or 131/171
The Erasmus programme remains an excellent opportunity for students and recent graduates to gain experience abroad and to develop further academic, professional and personal development.

However, despite all promises to the contrary, the management of the programme to facilitate this exchange has become more complex and more cumbersome since 2015.
This is due, on the one hand, to the excessive documentation requirements imposed on universities: Documents required in original versions, annexes which must be completed not only by e-mail, but as a contractual arrangement, and a wide range of solutions and funding in the same cases. In addition, a sending organisation is actually responsible for delays of a receiving organisation.

The announcement of a digitalisation plan in the programme should have made all this easier, but in reality, since 2019, everything should be double-documented and fallback systems found, as the announced systems do not work or communicate. The purpose of digitisation cannot be to duplicate paper-based documentation at the same time in order to be prepared in the event that the system does not function. This approach earmarks an extremely large number of resources, which must be diverted from the provision of advice and care to mobile people, thus achieving the opposite, less support.

It should also be noted that the evaluation/consultation of mobile participants often fails to reach the target. The participant reports are too long, students often lose interest in questions that do not concern them and no longer give real feedback, but only click on the questionnaire with moderate answers.

It would be great if the new generation of programmes created a stable introduction of the announced measures before reverting to new priorities, systems and targets. This is the only way to ensure that the organisation of a stay is truly easier and hurdles removed.
text_raw: Feedback from KR 103/107 or 131/171 The Erasmus programme remains an excellent opportunity for students and recent graduates to gain experience abroad and to develop further academic, professional and personal development. However, despite all promises to the contrary, the management of the programme to facilitate this exchange has become more complex and more cumbersome since 2015. This is due, on the one hand, to the excessive documentation requirements imposed on universities: Documents required in original versions, annexes which must be completed not only by e-mail, but as a contractual arrangement, and a wide range of solutions and funding in the same cases. In addition, a sending organisation is actually responsible for delays of a receiving organisation. The announcement of a digitalisation plan in the programme should have made all this easier, but in reality, since 2019, everything should be double-documented and fallback systems found, as the announced systems do not work or communicate. The purpose of digitisation cannot be to duplicate paper-based documentation at the same time in order to be prepared in the event that the system does not function. This approach earmarks an extremely large number of resources, which must be diverted from the provision of advice and care to mobile people, thus achieving the opposite, less support. It should also be noted that the evaluation/consultation of mobile participants often fails to reach the target. The participant reports are too long, students often lose interest in questions that do not concern them and no longer give real feedback, but only click on the questionnaire with moderate answers. It would be great if the new generation of programmes created a stable introduction of the announced measures before reverting to new priorities, systems and targets. This is the only way to ensure that the organisation of a stay is truly easier and hurdles removed.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

 Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tan

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
A revision of the Toy Safety Directive is unanimously to be welcomed. It would be desirable for limit values to be justified directly in the text of the Directive (e.g. in the Annex) for pollutants in and under the scope of the daily exposure of users (e.g. formaldehyde, which is also present in significant quantities in foodstuffs), on the basis of publicly available sources. This should help to ensure that decisions are traceable.

It would also make sense — particularly in the case of biocides — to make a meaningful assessment of the burden and the benefit to the user. There are some applications where the application alone limits the choice of biocides.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: EUCOLIGHT, herewith raises a series of issues for consideration in the context of the WEEE Directive evaluation and makes detailed recommendations in the annexed pdf: 1.- WEEE definitions. In several MS (e.g., CZ, PL, RO), integrated LED luminaires are considered as lamps, which is not accurate, nor in line with the reference Guidelines by EWRN on the WEEE open scope. The annexes of the WEEE Directive should better define luminaires in categories 4 and 5 as to include integrated LED luminaires. 2.- Subcategories for lighting. MS use different WEEE subcategories. This implies considerable administrative burden for both international producers and registers. Harmonising subcategories in the next WEEE legislation is desirable. 3.- Reinforced data transparency requirements and control mechanisms for all actors of the supply and waste management chain beyond producers affiliated with producer take-back systems. This is a must for the proper enforcement of extended producer responsibility obligations for WEEE. We make recommendations e.g., transparency of data from national registers, including mandatory POM reporting, better cooperation, and coordination between authorities. 4.- The online-free riding issue. The level of non-compliant products supplied through online marketplaces in the EU market is still alarming notably for light bulbs and other WEEE. EUCOLIGHT just updated a study undertaken in 2019 whose results in 8 countries (CZ, BE, DE, DK, ES, IT, RO, PT) show that the non-compliance figures are still very high. We urge the EU to establish, once for good, the appropriate framework to tackle this unfair situation. We make practical recommendations e.g., compulsory verification, or marketplace deemed to be the producer, rendering sales for non-compliant product impossible, mandatory registration number in all commercial transactions 5.- Collective vs individual take-back systems. In some MS, individual take-back systems are not subject to the same rules as collective ones, leading some producers to opt for individual models to avoid extended producer responsibility obligations. Consequence: unfair advantages and increased burden on other agents. Solution: clear criteria and same obligations for individual and collective systems. Some MS have a solution (AT, CZ). 6.- Requirements for producers leaving a take-back system. Challenges about how to calculate the collection rate when a producer leaves during a given year have been reported. In RO a take-back system has no collection target for the first year, which generates compliance and financial issues. Solution: regulate producers departing conditions at EU level. CZ regulations offer a solution. 7.- Waste available for collection. Part of the waste available is not handled by established producer take-back systems due to e.g., sorting errors by (household) waste holders. It is the time to think about another approach considering waste held by all involved actors. 8.- Visible fee. It is a fine tool for WEEE financing transparency, and helps solving several issues at national level e.g., discounts on waste management fees from some take-back schemes to some big producers. The visible fee situation varies across the EU (from obligation to prohibition). A lack of clarity of national laws is reported (e.g., in CZ). Consequently, EU legislation to be more specific when MS render the visible fee mandatory. 9.- Eco-modulation of the fees. We call on the Commission to be cautious with the adoption of any criteria in this field. We undertook a study that demonstrated the absence of real benefit in modulating WEEE fees for lighting products. A comprehensive study by WEEE Forum and other stakeholders has further demonstrated that there was very little room to reduce technical treatment costs through treating greener products and huge limitations to delivering a product or consumer change through eco modulation.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: On behalf of the Digital Poland Association, an industry employers' organisation which brings together the largest high-tech companies operating in Poland, including manufacturers, importers and distributors of electrical and electronic equipment, we are submitting a position paper on the "WEEE directive - evaluating the EU rules". Although the WEEE directive has been published and transposed to the EU Member States many years ago there are still some challenges: 1. Non-achievable WEEE collection targets in many European countries 2. Mandatory visible fee in a few countries 3. The problem with free-riders and grey area 4. Different country implementation Details of the position in the attachment.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
Please consider our attached document for a deeper analysis, here below you can find a synthesis.
4. Ecodesign requirements 
– extending the rules to cover appliances with a capacity from 400 kW to 1 MW
Having in mind the last version of the draft Regulation presentend, in the future a reversible appliance will be a heating product if its capacity is below 400 kW and starting from 401 kW it will must be declared as a cooling product with different Ecodesign requirements and calculations even if it will be an identical model with only a different declared capacity. 
Considering also a simple energetic analysis, we can state that imposing heating requirements also on the reversible heat pumps will have a non-negligible effect since heat is the most delivered energy by these units during a year.
From a technical point of view, if a unit needs to meet SEER targets would be optimized in cooling mode; if a unit needs to meet SCOP targets would be optimized in heating mode. 
Considering what above, having a unit optimized in heating mode (SCOP target) we believe is in line with the “energy efficiency first” principle. 
Considering also the European Green Deal and the decarbonisation target, reversible units will progressively cover more and more heating loads as primary goals. 
So, measuring them in heating instead of cooling in our opinion also gives the end users the most correct message.

– introducing new space heating categories, in particular hybrid heat pumps 
Assoclima does not support the introduction of any indication of performance and seasonal efficiency at High Temperature regime also as an optional part of the product information and we recommend keeping the Medium Temperature regime testing at rated Tsupply 55°C. 
Furthermore, we re-state our suggestion to carefully consider introducing the intermediate class of 45°C supply water temperature. This, in addition with the low temperature, will represent more the real-life applications of heating heat pumps and will contribute to more energy savings thanks to better-performing applications.
– updating the minimum energy efficiency limits, in particular by accounting for the new primary energy factor of 2.1 and the Green Deal objectives 
As a general consideration, Assoclima would like to confirm its support to the introduction of the proposed PEF correction for the minimum limits.
– new test methods for heat pumps, in particular the ‘compensation-method 
Assoclima believes that the compensation method is not mature enough for being introduced in the current review and thus asks keeping the current test method.
- revised heat pump sound power settings 
Assoclima asks keeping the current approach: sound requirements applying to units up to 70 kW only. We ask keep the Prated as the conditions at which the capacity limits for maximum sound power requirements apply.
– self-monitoring and reporting of in-use energy efficiency for installed space heaters 
It is not possible to currently support the EC proposal and thus Assoclima suggests reconsidering it at the next review (insert it in the review clause). 
– extended resource-efficiency requirements. 
Assoclima asks that only heat pumps below 70 kW are to be covered by specific energy resources requirements. Concerning heat pumps, Assoclima believes that spare parts should be limited to an exhaustive list of critical components to be listed in the Regulation.

3. Energy labelling requirements 
Assoclima welcomes and support the EC proposal of rescaling the energy label to A-G as well as the introduction of the ηs value on the label. 
We’d like also to inform that the proposed label is not clear since has on the same label both MT and LT classes, and so it is still unknown how to deal with it when LT heat pumps don’t reach and consequently don’t show MT performances. 
Last, we’d like some clarification about the packages, whether they shall include a solar device or not.
