initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Aligning Member State interpretation and adoption of the Directive, improving its enforcement, standardising the metrics and definitions are all necessary improvements but they will not be sufficient. Not sufficient because the almost total loss of critical raw materials (CRMs) used in many categories of electronic and some electrical devices that become WEEE is now recognised as having consequences for EU economic security. The 2020 EU study "Critical Raw Materials for Strategic Technologies and Sectors in the EU" provides evidence enough but the urgent shift toward investment in more renewable energy (RePowerEU) since February 2022, implies a renewed focus on asking why we accept the near total loss of CRMs within the WEEE management system. Work by T U Delft is especially noteworthy in this regard. A significant, yet uncertain, quantity of CRMs are required during the manufacture of many categories of electrical and electronic devices. At the end of the useful working life of a device, if collected, the used products are typically fragmentized for material recycling. Meeting the recycling targets is an aim but during the process the effective capture rate for CRMs is Zero. There is for the most part no reason to change since only tiny quantities of CRMs, with an economic value of a few euro cents per device, are used to provide the functionality required. The CRMs are then largely mixed into the melted ferrous and non ferrous streams from which it is practically impossible to recover them. Where they become less accessible than they were in the earths crust, their brief period of usefulness within a device is followed by an accelerated entropy. An alternative approach would be to extend the useful working life of the devices and/or their components. Some products have already implemented systems to achieve this outcome. Aircraft and truck tyres for example, where there is a clear economic driver to do so, but systems of site registration and tyre identification for quality control had to be implemented and agreed through international standards. Trade association ETRMA can provide details of how this was achieved. The WEEE Directive may not, by itself, be an appropriate instrument with which to achieve an outcome in which many more electrical and electronic products have an extended working life through processes such as remanufacture and refurbishment. But as presently framed, the WEEE Directive is not aligning with other instruments in preparation to address the need to make better use of the CRMs in these devices. The single most useful reform would be to adopt a combined recycling and remanufacturing target. Others have argued for separate targets, we disagree. By combining the targets, manufacturers can choose to what extent remanufacturing is achievable for their product category. The certified tonnage of products remanufactured would be counted as equivalent to 5 times the material tonnage collected for recycling. Policy makers would subsequently have the option of changing this ratio to suit future circumstances. We are optimistic about the forthcoming regulatory changes to implement the Circular Economy Action Plan. Equally, we are optimistic about the direction of the proposed Critical Raw Materials Act. The Revised Blue Guide (2022) and developing rules from EFRAG on company reporting for sustainability are equally helpful for those companies involved in remanufacture and refurbishment. We will be glad to work with your technical consultants to expand on our brief contribution here and to direct them to leading companies that are already active in the 30 billion remanufacturing sector in Europe. Our member companies represent many product sectors, mostly for industrial, commercial and health uses but also some consumer-facing products such as imaging equipment and cartridges, personal IT and medical devices. The Conseil europeen de remanufacture is grateful for this opportunity to contribute.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: [Part 2/3] It is understood here that Member States have the necessary resources to rigorously control WEEE operators and shipments of waste. In addition, measuring collection and recycling performance is better suited to the objectives of extending the lifespan and desegregation of resources. The Directive does not take into account the need for the implementation of the circular economy, in particular the increase in the use and repair of equipment, which helps to extend the lifetime of EEE and thus delay their transition to waste status, or the impact of major technological breaks (such as the arrival of LED lamps, which has multiplied the average lifetime of the lamps by 8). French legislation and regulations have introduced specific provisions, but the main European objectives remain high collection and recycling rates without taking into account the need for re-use and repair. Finally, we observe a very significant impact of health and/or economic crises which weakens the relevance of the method for calculating collection targets based on placing on the market (65 % of the average placing on the market in the previous three years which are not representative) without taking into account the duration of the equipment and the length of time the equipment is held. France is not an isolated case and despite 17 years deffort and almost 48 million tonnes collected (Source: Eurostat) within the European Union, the majority of Member States fail to meet the collection targets set by the Directive. The WEEE Forum carried out an ‘An enhanced definition of EPR and the role of all actors’ study (https://weee-forum.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/EPR-and-the-role-of-all-actors_final.pdf) analysing collection performance in each of the Member States and proposing policy levers to improve WEEE collection. 2. Ensuring appropriate treatment of WEEE: The notion of scarcity or criticality of fractions in recycling performance is not taken into account. However, in terms of resources, it is more difficult and critical to capture one ton dor than one tonne of steel. The current Directive does not encourage the capture of resources with the greatest environmental impacts. There is therefore a lack of a depletion of mineral and fossil resources vs. a simple recycling indicator and an indicator of tonnes of CO2 avoided. Some fractions need to be covered by a common common European treatment policy, particularly when deposits are large but in the run-up to extinction. The alignment of practices is necessary in order to ensure appropriate mutual treatment, to avoid the proliferation of unsustainable industrial pain and to ensure harmonisation of recycling solutions between Member States: example of glass sorting and treatment of screen and cathode ray tubes (CRT). The environmental protection requirements laid down by France make it impossible to put in place a solution for recycling lead glass from cathode ray tubes in France and prohibit its use. As a result, lead glass is burned onerous as hazardous waste and France has not met the recycling targets for the ‘screens’ category. Furthermore, in order to ensure treatment and de-pollution in line with European level and to avoid distortions of competition between Member States (heterogeneous levels of requirements create ‘Dair calls’ for illegal shipments of waste), the Directive must lay down the framework and level of requirements, in particular by means of the mandatory application of standards (implementing act).
text_raw: [Part 2/3] It is understood here that Member States have the necessary resources to rigorously control WEEE operators and shipments of waste. In addition, measuring collection and recycling performance is better suited to the objectives of extending the lifespan and desegregation of resources. The Directive does not take into account the need for the implementation of the circular economy, in particular the increase in the use and repair of equipment, which helps to extend the lifetime of EEE and thus delay their transition to waste status, or the impact of major technological breaks (such as the arrival of LED lamps, which has multiplied the average lifetime of the lamps by 8). French legislation and regulations have introduced specific provisions, but the main European objectives remain high collection and recycling rates without taking into account the need for re-use and repair. Finally, we observe a very significant impact of health and/or economic crises which weakens the relevance of the method for calculating collection targets based on placing on the market (65 % of the average placing on the market in the previous three years which are not representative) without taking into account the duration of the equipment and the length of time the equipment is held. France is not an isolated case and despite 17 years deffort and almost 48 million tonnes collected (Source: Eurostat) within the European Union, the majority of Member States fail to meet the collection targets set by the Directive. The WEEE Forum carried out an ‘An enhanced definition of EPR and the role of all actors’ study (https://weee-forum.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/EPR-and-the-role-of-all-actors_final.pdf) analysing collection performance in each of the Member States and proposing policy levers to improve WEEE collection. 2. Ensuring appropriate treatment of WEEE: The notion of scarcity or criticality of fractions in recycling performance is not taken into account. However, in terms of resources, it is more difficult and critical to capture one ton dor than one tonne of steel. The current Directive does not encourage the capture of resources with the greatest environmental impacts. There is therefore a lack of a depletion of mineral and fossil resources vs. a simple recycling indicator and an indicator of tonnes of CO2 avoided. Some fractions need to be covered by a common common European treatment policy, particularly when deposits are large but in the run-up to extinction. The alignment of practices is necessary in order to ensure appropriate mutual treatment, to avoid the proliferation of unsustainable industrial pain and to ensure harmonisation of recycling solutions between Member States: example of glass sorting and treatment of screen and cathode ray tubes (CRT). The environmental protection requirements laid down by France make it impossible to put in place a solution for recycling lead glass from cathode ray tubes in France and prohibit its use. As a result, lead glass is burned onerous as hazardous waste and France has not met the recycling targets for the ‘screens’ category. Furthermore, in order to ensure treatment and de-pollution in line with European level and to avoid distortions of competition between Member States (heterogeneous levels of requirements create ‘Dair calls’ for illegal shipments of waste), the Directive must lay down the framework and level of requirements, in particular by means of the mandatory application of standards (implementing act).

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: 2nd November 2022 The harmonised collection and recycling of WEEE in Europe is critical as it helps retain resources, avoid unnecessary CO2 emissions, and protects people from the potential impact of hazardous substances. The current WEEE Directive, and its highly variable implementation depending on each member state, often with poor enforcement and the absence of a level playing field for collection and treatment, is not at present providing the ideal regulatory context for sustainable WEEE management. The full life cycle of an electrical or electronic product is obligated under extended producer responsibility requirements. To encourage and enforce these responsibilities equally in all member states mandatory hand over of WEEE should form a significant change. Each and every citizen in member states has the equal right to have their WEEE recycled to the highest standard. This right should not vary due to their location. Their aspirations for a clean environment and best use of the resources contained in WEEE can only be fulfilled if collection and treatment processes are standardised and enforced. Re-use is part of the life cycle but ultimately quality recycling will be needed. Concerning preparation for re-use; provisions need to be strengthened to avoid loopholes that allow illegal export of WEEE. Only a strong WEEE Regulation will ensure harmonisation. Only a strong WEEE Regulation will support the Circular Economy Action Plan by providing confidence for producers and recyclers with the access to all WEEE, and consequently the recovery of critical and valuable resources while at the same time providing for the removal of undesirable substances and items. These two enforceable aspects throughout all member states will bring about the creation of collection, treatment, and recovery capacities for the circular economy. The essential development of much needed treatment capacities for the recovery of raw materials in Europe can only be achieved when a level playing field is mandatory and enforced. Kurt M. Kyck KMK Metals Recycling Ltd, Ireland

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Repair&Share welcomes the opportunity to give input for the revision of the WEEE Directive. Experience from different projects on repairing EEE learned that the current way of collecting and treating WEEE is focussed too much on recyling and does not correspond to the European waste hierarchy. Current targets, infrastructure, logistics, rules regarding access to waste streams, product passports and data on EEE, EPR schemes, fees and financial incentives for the treatment of WEEE are mainly geared to recycling. This leads to the early recycling of goods that could be repaired and reused. Not only do many opportunities to prevent WEEE remain untapped. Structural barriers and mechanisms in the collection and treatment of WEEE preclude the ecologically and economically more interesting solutions of repair and reuse of EEE.Therefore we believe that in the renewed WEE Directive, prevention of WEE through repair and reuse should take a central place. We put forward the following recommendations: Clear and legally binding targets for repair and reuse; Clear requirements for the collection, storage and transport of WEEE to avoid additional damage of discarded goods; EPR fees that also finance the promotion and enhancing of repair and reuse; Financial incentives that correspond with the EU waste hierarchy; Facilitating the harvesting and use of second hand spare parts for repair and reuse actors; Representation of repair and reuse stakeholders in Producer Responsibility Organisations; Linking the WEEE Directive with ESPR, Right to Repair and SPI. See our paper attached for more details.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
EPEE, the voice of the air conditioning, heat pump, and refrigeration industry in Europe, welcomes the opportunity to provide comments to the Commission’s proposals for reviewing ENER Lot 2 (ecodesign Regulation 814/2013 and energy labelling Regulation 812/2013 for water heaters). We welcome the Commission’s considerations of the industry comments following the last Consultation Forum of 28 September and we support the need for reviewing the requirements for water heaters.

Nonetheless, we believe that certain aspects could be further optimised. 

In light of the REPowerEU Communication and the Fit-for-55 strategy, which are a result of the ever-increasing pressure to decouple our economic future from the use of fossil fuels, the Commission has put forward the goal to accelerate the rollout of heat pumps to 10 million newly installed units by 2027 and 30 million by 2030. There is a clear need to facilitate the uptake of heat pumps, especially in the legislative framework provided by the ecodesign and energy labelling regulations for water heaters (ENER Lot 2). 

In view of these developments, EPEE welcomes transitioning to an A – G energy labelling scale as fast as possible. This is not only welcome but also needed to move towards higher efficiencies in the market by allowing for a proper comparison between the energy efficiencies of the various water heating technologies. 

EPEE calls for further investigation on whether the currently proposed additional requirements for heat pumps in ENER Lot 2, such as the additional testing requirements for Tpeak and sound power, are not imposing a too large burden on heat pump suppliers entering the market and inadvertently creating an unlevel playing field with fossil fuel-powered space heaters. 

Furthermore, the potential consideration of immature test methodologies, such as compensation methods, are not helpful in fostering the uptake of heat pumps in the market. 

In addition, test methodologies, such as Tpeak, must be considered carefully in view of facilitating the market towards lower temperatures. Although the need for test modification is recognised, any considerations of applying the test or adding penalties should be balanced and reasonable. 

Please see our recommendations with more detailed explanation in our attached position paper (dated 15 November 2021).

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The PSPKD fully supports the Commission’s conclusion: “the aims of the Detergent Regulation are relevant, the rules are consistent and complete, the Regulation has achieved it’s ideas to a large extent”. 

We would like to stress that the Detergents Regulation is crucial for the further making available on the market of cleaning products and should provide the basis for possible future regulations on cleaning products, and, importantly, operate with voluntary initiatives that allow for the flexibility needed in the field of innovation.

Key issues to be considered in the planning of the revision:

1.Improving regulatory consistency
The removal of its identified inconsistencies with REACH and CLP should be addressed in order to avoid significant discrepancies and duplication of requirements in the future. The Biocidal Products Regulation should also be taken into account, given that many detergents are also biocidal products. 

2.Card for Sustainable Cleaning
This project has shown how voluntary industry schemes can foster sustainable development in the production and use of cleaning products. 

3.Scope of the Detergents Regulation
The scope of the detergent regulation should remain unchanged. In the opinion of the PSPKD, this also applies to micro-organism-based cleaners. 

4.Labels relevant to the consumer
The inclusion of all chemical names on the packaging results in overloading of labels, as well as a lack of readability and correct understanding by the consumer. We support the coordination of this topic in the Commission study on the simplification and digitalisation of chemicals labels.

5.Restrictions on ingredients
The risk assessment and reduction of detergent ingredients should be regulated through horizontal legislation (REACH, BPR and the General Product Safety Directive) in order to ensure maximum consistency in legislation.

6.Restrictions on phosphates
Restrictions on consumer laundry products and dishwashers have been successfully implemented by industry. And where phosphates are still used – niche applications, in particular industrial and institutional applications – are key components to achieve lower energy and water consumption. 

7.Selling of consumer detergents in bulk
This practice is an increasing trend among products intended for the general public. This form of sale is a positive initiative from the point of view of environmental protection and sustainability. In the view of the PSPKD, clarity can be provided through guidelines/guide on how to implement the already existing regulatory requirements for this form of sale. Extending the use of digital labels will be a key element in ensuring that products comply with labelling obligations.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
Women Engage for a Common Future, France (WECF France) welcomes the European Commission’s plan to revise the European Toy Safety Directive. As the French office of the international ecofeminist network WECF, which brings together 150 women and non-governmental civil organisations involved in building a healthy and active environment at EU level with a view to a noxious future, WECF France calls on the European authorities to strengthen regulations on dangerous chemicals.
We also strongly encourage the European community to broaden the scope for toys and products intended for children over 36 months of age. 
We agree to extend the scope of the Directive to all endocrine disruptors or to persistent and bioaccumulative substances. WECF France and other NGOs across Europe have repeatedly demonstrated the presence and/or contamination of toys with persistent organic pollutants (POPs) and endocrine disruptors (PE). 
It has been demonstrated by product tests conducted by WECF France, Arnika, HEAL, IPEN and other European organisations that products, including plastic toys, on sale in Europe contain brominated flame retardant chemicals, including polybrominated diphenylic ethers (PBDE), tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBA) and a number of new IFB (i.e. 1,2-bis (2,4,6-tribromophenyl) diphenyl (PBDE).
We want the use of flame retardants, whether or not chemically linked to fibre, to be banned in toys
Problematic materials (i.e. PVC) requiring the application of various additives to persistent, bioaccumulative (PBT or vPvB) and endocrine disrupting properties should also be covered by the Directive.
We want all phthalates to be banned/substituted in toys. 
Several studies carried out by NGOs, including WECF France, have shown that the scope of the Toy Safety Directive is insufficient to protect children. Children are often exposed to very dangerous substances not only through toys, but also through sports accessories, clothing and shoes or hairdressing accessories, kitchen utensils and office (artistic) supplies. All these products are often designed for children, but excluded from the scope of the Directive. 
The EU Toy Safety Directive should cover in its scope all toy related items (sports and art accessories or hair supplies designed and used by children as toys). In addition, all persistent organic chemicals and endocrine disruptors must be restricted without derogation, as in electronic products. 
All threshold values shall be based on the total concentration of chemicals in the product. 
Nanomaterials must be strictly banned, regardless of whether they are added intentionally or not. 
The same standards should be applied to articles made from virgin and recycled materials.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: FEAD, the European Federation for Waste Management and Environmental Services, welcomes the evaluation of the WEEE Directive and supports the objective of the European Green Deal and the new Circular Economy Action Plan. The WEEE management sector is crucial towards sustainability, and even more so today, where recent events are highlighting the strengths and weaknesses of the world's state when it comes to raw materials and supply. WEEE is one of the fastest-growing waste streams which, furthermore, contains precious and critical raw materials, the recovery of which is crucial for a more circular economy and potentially also for EU open strategic autonomy. FEAD knows how delicate and important the role of the waste management industry is in order to achieve ambitious goals and meet the challenges of the present and the future. Therefore, to contribute to the public consultation aiming at evaluating the WEEE Directive, FEAD sets out below its observations, comments and proposals. The current WEEE Directive, and its highly variable implementation in each Member State, often with poor enforcement and the absence of a level-playing field, is not at present providing the ideal regulatory context for sustainable WEEE management health and the environment. One of the major issues raised during the consultation with our members is the need for more harmonisation in Europe. The current WEEE Directive leaves room for different interpretations by individual Member States on how to transpose the legislation at their national level. As a result, the obligations and conditions recyclers face in each Member State vary, contributing to an uneven playing field in a variety of points. Product design is also a key aspect, and it should be directly linked to the ability to carry out proper recycling at its end-of-life. At present recyclers face a number of prominent issues which obstruct adequate processing and thus the fulfilment of expected recycling and recovery targets. With reference to this aspect, consistency with other legislations is crucial to improve the efficacy of the provisions on WEEE and reach the highest recyclability. Another major aspect in which shortcomings and deficits that need to be improved are noted is collection. The improvement and regulation of particular details could help Member States and companies to reach the Europeans collection targets, but also and above all to improve the re-use and recycling of WEEE. WEEE treatment standards are needed to ensure a level playing field within and among Member States and to increase the environmental benefits through a high-quality recycling. Recovery targets regulated in Article 11 of the current WEEE Directive should be more tailored to specific product types and should grant flexibility around the methodologies implemented to reach and promote greater recovery of key CRMs and a higher capture and removal of toxic and hazardous substances and fractions. The entire position of FEAD with more details can be read in the attached document.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European Erasmus programme is, in my view, the most important programme that currently exists in education at all levels. It is a programme that gives multiple opportunities, both for personal and educational and professional development. 

With my involvement, I gained tremendous experiences, knowledge, skills. I was aware of other places and people. Even any negative difficulties I have encountered from my course to date and my involvement in it have helped me at every level of my life. I broke stereotypes and prejudices and I saw the world with another lens, from a different perspective. 

The topics with which it is swallowed are so crucial, topical, interesting that you can learn, act, create, know, acquaint you with. You can combine, engage with each other, innovate and finally succeed.

Demanding and time-consuming requests for involvement in the programme, final reports, etc., hamper the involvement of more organisations in the programme. Many colleagues wish to be involved, but their procedural dissuades, which I fully share. To conclude, to underline the need for a revision of the economic tables, as well as the cost of living, also due to recent global events e.g. the impact of the war in Ukraine and fuel prices, it has risen. The economic tables need to be re-examined as their prices no longer reflect today’s reality, which are much higher and follow continuous upward trends.
text_raw: The European Erasmus programme is, in my view, the most important programme that currently exists in education at all levels. It is a programme that gives multiple opportunities, both for personal and educational and professional development. With my involvement, I gained tremendous experiences, knowledge, skills. I was aware of other places and people. Even any negative difficulties I have encountered from my course to date and my involvement in it have helped me at every level of my life. I broke stereotypes and prejudices and I saw the world with another lens, from a different perspective. The topics with which it is swallowed are so crucial, topical, interesting that you can learn, act, create, know, acquaint you with. You can combine, engage with each other, innovate and finally succeed. Demanding and time-consuming requests for involvement in the programme, final reports, etc., hamper the involvement of more organisations in the programme. Many colleagues wish to be involved, but their procedural dissuades, which I fully share. To conclude, to underline the need for a revision of the economic tables, as well as the cost of living, also due to recent global events e.g. the impact of the war in Ukraine and fuel prices, it has risen. The economic tables need to be re-examined as their prices no longer reflect today’s reality, which are much higher and follow continuous upward trends.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Many positives, many opportunities for which we thank ERASMUS. Nevertheless we note some drawbacks in order to help the program become more flexible in the future.

The application process requires a huge investment of time. This can mean that the program tends to favor the organizations that have learned to write, to present their ideas better and not necessarily the best ideas.

The application form has many questions where repetition is the main element. In certain cases I had to write four times the same things. The application forms in 2021-27 did not actually change and in no case they are simpler.

Thank you.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The lack of harmonisation of the Directive would force me to go through a burdensome and expensive registration procedure in all Member States or to hire an even more expensive service provider. As this is not economically viable for a small company, I have had to limit shipping to Germany.
As a result, the scheme excludes small businesses from markets, to the detriment of entrepreneurs and – by reducing betting – of consumers.

I am in favour of the WEEE2 rules, however, a central body should be set up, similar to the OSS procedure, through which reports are processed. This also concerns the Packaging Ordinance, etc.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
Feedback: Waste electrical and electronic equipment – evaluation of EU rules
In our view, the WEEE Directive is a great success because it has helped to eliminate hazardous substances from the products and because it has transported the collection and recycling of WEEE products across Europe. 
From the point of view of a producer of WEEE products with a pan-European distribution, an even greater harmonisation of EPR obligations across Europe is desirable. 
•National registration needs to be harmonised. 
•Harmonised staggered exemption limits should be introduced in each EU country so that only a few devices with a relatively small total weight of less than 100 kg (second phase 1 000 kg) per year per country can be registered free of charge, with a low system contribution of e.g. EUR 50 (EUR 200 for the second phase), without incurring further obligations and costs such as authorised representatives, participation and funding in national collection and recycling schemes. This will make it possible for products to be sold across the EU in small quantities at an acceptable cost without infringing the law. With annual reporting to the relevant national authority of the total quantities of WEEE distributed in a harmonised manner across the EU, with a maximum breakdown by WEEE product category. 

Note: In cross-border intra-European B2B distribution, quantities of WEEE are mostly not recorded in the destination country. The distributor imposes on his foreign customer the legal obligations for WEEE in his country, and the distributor is often not registered there because he does not sell to final consumers. However, the foreign B2B customer is mostly unaware of this obligation as a national importer and will therefore not register these imported quantities with its national authorities in the majority of cases in the destination country. It is therefore appropriate to encourage companies to be aware of and comply with their obligations under the WEEE Directive and the other Directives on batteries and packaging and packaging waste when purchasing cross-border within Europe.

feedback_text:
•complex registration and application procedures — young young people or associations who do not have experience in the project face greater difficulties in the programmes and, in part, in project management
•especially in Youth Participation Activities (and Solidarity Projects): more flexibility and deadlines would be desirable. Few application deadlines per year and high number of participants for a project make the YPAs unattractive. So long periods and (administrative) effort for youth groups (especially in the case of Fewer opportunity) is not realistic.
•Who want to expand a tool when introducing new tools/platforms. Clustering for participants on a platform (e.g. all administrative steps, financial, language learning, networking, communication, etc. in an app), bundling the beneficiaries on a platform (as is already done in part with the Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps page): Administrative, insurance, financial, etc.
•Create tools more intuitively
•More budget for ESC: Structures and partnerships that have been set up in recent years cannot be continued due to lack of funding.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text: During the previous programming period and given to the alterations required during implementation with regards to COVID restrictions, we were enforced to implement most of our activities by distance tools. Although this was done in  cooperation with the managing authorities, the programme itself was not as flexible as it should be with transferring funds between budget lines or even including additional outcomes so that funds would not be lost. I therefore propose that new projects should be given the opportunity to be implemented in a more flexible manner as even now new measures (such as COVID related)cannot be foreseen. Additionally there is an almost  horizontal rule in rejecting budget lines related with translation of material produced during a project, which to me seems illogical and  at the same time limits down the dissemination of what is perceived amongst general public. The programme is required to rule out the assumption that English language is the spoken language of all EU countries.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ 2014-2020 final evaluation.

During the 2014 -2020 period our school, B Technical and Vocational School “Gregoris
Afxentiou” Lemesos, carried out five KA102 VET learner and staff mobility projects and
participated in one KA2- Cooperation for innovation and the exchange of good practices
project.
The most important benefits of the Erasmus+ program were:
 It gave the opportunity to students and teachers from our school to be trained and
update their knowledge and skills in their respective fields.
 It also gave the opportunity to students and teachers from our school to learn about the
utilization of new technologies that are either recently evolved or are used in other
countries but not in Cyprus.
 All participating students were able to connect the theory learned at school with practice
in an actual live working environment.
 Students and teachers had the opportunity to meet people from other countries and immerse in
cultural activities.
 More than 40 percent of the participants were people with fewer opportunities. We believe that
these participants especially gained a lot from such an opportunity and have also increased their
employment opportunities upon leaving school.
On the downside:
 Every year we had to submit lengthy applications.
 The budget awarded was 50% or less than the budget we requested.

Nevertheless, the benefits gained outweighed any problems occurred.

Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation

For the period from 2021 afterwards, the benefits of the new Erasmus+ program are as
follows:
 Our school benefited by gaining Erasmus accreditation in the field. of vocational
education and training. This feature gave us the opportunity to set up a 6-year School
development plan where Erasmus+ mobilities and cooperations between
organizations will play a key part to our effort of our school development.
 The budget awarded to our school, finally matched the budget requested. This is
giving us the opportunity to implement our school development plan to its full extent,
without any deviations due to budget limitations.
 The Erasmus program’s emphasis to diversity and inclusion, benefits our school and
our efforts, because the majority of our students are students with fewer
opportunities.
What need to be improved though is the new Beneficiary Module tool, that has replaced
Mobility tool+. It is very slow and unresponsive and has many problems. 
We have spent many hours communicating with our National Agency for a multitude of problems.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
We are grateful for the opportunity offered by the KA121-VET mobility project to carry out student and staff mobility and welcome visitors to our school. As an accredited actor, we see the flexibility of the programme as very positive. The trainings organised by the NA have been good and the Howspace platform has provided support in the organisation of the project activities and enabled peer-to-peer support. We would like to see an active update and use of the platform in the future.
Erasmus mobility periods develop language skills, open up the world, both professionally and in general, and bring an experience of autonomy and trust to students and staff members. We welcome the increase in the amount of individual grants for Erasmus+ in 2022. The grant will allow almost all those willing to go to a mobility period. Shorter exchanges and group mobility are seen as a good reform, as they lower the threshold to go to a period of kV, which may be the first international experience of the individual. We hope that for VET group mobility, the minimum duration of the student mobility period will be reduced and the documentation related to mobility will be reduced, e.g. by making social documentation available to the group as a whole. We would also like to see lighter documentation when a teacher becomes a student supporter or assesses evidence. Supporting inclusion as a idea is good, but has led to more bureaucracy and therefore little use is made. 
We feel that, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the programme was commendable, and the development of virtual cooperation and digital connectivity brought improvements to practices that will make work easier, even when the pandemic recedes. We hope that in the future, the documents of the mobility period will be further simplified and reduced and will become fully electronic. It would be useful to have an electronic signature available on all documents, including Europass Mobility, which we want to be filled in and signed on the Europass portal (https://europa.eu/europass).
In the new project period, opportunities for global mobility and support for visa and vaccine costs are of particular interest. 
A network of VET actors organising KA1 mobility would be welcome, and we could find partners through this network to organise mobility and cooperation in Europe or globally.
The organisation of mobility has traditionally functioned free of charge through the principle of student exchanges between institutions or through cooperation between the institution and the working partner. The proliferation of commercial service providers as providers of ancillary activities such as accommodation and ancillary services is a concern, as we fear that it will increase the cost of mobility and make the programme commercial. On the other hand, as a host organisation, the involvement of the service provider serves its purpose by concentrating on the core task of the institution. We would like to see joint discussions and guidelines for the use of external service providers. 
It is challenging to understand the budget at the application stage and after the decision. While the programme promises flexibility for accredited applicants, the grant agreement includes precise numbers of activities. We did not receive any justification for the number of activities, although they differed from those requested. Nor is there any justification for the budgetary ratio to the number of activities. 
Improvements would be needed in keeping the project schedule. The 1.6.2022 project started on 1 June 2022, but its grant agreements were not received until the end of August. The reporting platform “Beneficiary Module” has still not become operational in mid-September. The new OLS language course platform is also not operational according to the promised schedule. 
Overall, we see Erasmus+ KA 1 mobility projects as a very important and important system for VET with great potential.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: 1/Pour the French market, perceived by GIGREL, is hardly collected because of the lifespan and reuse of products not necessarily in the EU. Consequently, generating sets follow the application of French law deriving from Directive 2012/19, whereas those products are specifically called upon to run for a long period and are re-used elsewhere. In doing so, the WEEE reporting schemes in place impose a far from negligible administrative and technical burden on businesses. 2/La the question arises as to whether the placing on the EU market follows the same legal requirements of the framework of the Directive in the different countries where the transposition of the Directive applies. More specifically, this second point concerns the insufficient perception of the application of the transposed texts: producers are likely to place products that are not registered on the market, which could put compliant producers at a competitive disadvantage compared to others who do not comply with them. 3/Le point 2/a, which is all the more pressing with regard to online sales, in particular from sales sites outside the EU. How can we ensure that products comply with EU law for the WEEE part?

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ 2014-2020 final evaluation.
As a coordinating school Kuressaare Ametikool participated in two Erasmus+ projects – international cooperation between schools. “Recording our Training Experiences in Documentaries” was followed by “Yellow European Stars”. In our feedback we focus on challenges our partnership encountered during the implementation of the project.
1.
The biggest challenge or difficulty our partnership encountered during the implementation of the project was the COVID-19 pandemic or coronavirus pandemic. As we all know the pandemic triggered severe social and economic disruption around the world. Educational institutions and public areas were partially or fully closed, and many events were cancelled or postponed. So was the situation with our project that had just started. The new situation caused us to be uncertain because we did not know about our future. We could complete two mobilities successfully, but the third mobility we decided to have a virtual mobility. Months later, we could complete the objectives of the third mobility (the virtual one) with a Short Term Joint Staff Training Event as only adults were allowed to travel in September 2021 due to covid restrictions and vaccination policies in the UE.
The initial planned time of the project was 21 months, and its implementation period was from 01.09.2019 to 31.05.2021. After our virtual mobility all partners agreed to prolong the implementation period of the project goals and activities for six months, from 01.09.2019 to 30.11.2021. Because of the COVID-19 pandemic the duration of the project had been agreed to extend for one more time at the online meeting of the coordinators of all partner schools on 30.08.2021. Finally, the duration of the project was 01.09.2019-31.05.2022.
2.
Before COVID-19 pandemic during the mobilities the hosting school students’ families used to host students from all partner schools. In that way our budget allowed for more students to participate in mobilities. During the COVID-19 restrictions we gave up housing students in host families for security reasons. Therefore, we had to take more into account the budgetary possibilities because the students stayed in hotels.
3.
According to the budget of “Yellow European Stars”, one teacher and four students could participate in one mobility. We would like to point out that when you are travelling with teenagers, in the future we ask to give the opportunity for at least two teachers to travel with students. All kinds of situations occur in our life and then it is easier if there are at least two adults with the students. For example, the student needed to be taken to the hospital and both teachers were asked to go with him.
4.
The Turkish partner school could not attend the meeting held in Italy for teacher training in September 2021 and could not attend the meeting held in Spain Feb 2022 and the Italian partner school could not attend the meeting held in Estonia in the autumn 2021. There were some restrictions and number of Covid-19 cases were very high. Both schools participated virtually. Things worked, sometimes the Internet connection was not so good. However, something was still missing which is present during face-to-face meetings.
5.
Covid-19 has also affected people psychologically. People have lost their motivation. During the project work, students and their families hesitated to participate in the activities, and it took time to convince some families. This process took some time and caused extra effort to ensure the participation of students and teachers in the activities.
6.
The economic crisis, which started with the start of the normalization processes after Covid-19, deepened with the effect of the war.
7.
Despite the difficult situation due to the COVID-19 pandemic and the war in Ukraine, we can say that with the joint effort of our team we have achieved the goals of the project and we would like to continue our cooperation in the future.

There were some restrictions and number of Covid-19 cases were very high. Both schools participated virtually. Things worked, sometimes the Internet connection was not so good. However, something was still missing which is present during face-to-face meetings. 5. Covid-19 has also affected people psychologically. People have lost their motivation. During the project work, students and their families hesitated to participate in the activities, and it took time to convince some families. This process took some time and caused extra effort to ensure the participation of students and teachers in the activities. 6. The economic crisis, which started with the start of the normalization processes after Covid-19, deepened with the effect of the war. 7. Despite the difficult situation due to the COVID-19 pandemic and the war in Ukraine, we can say that with the joint effort of our team we have achieved the goals of the project and we would like to continue our cooperation in the future.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
The TÜV Association welcomes the European Commission’s intention to revise the Toy Safety Directive 2009/48/EC. Given that children are the most vulnerable consumers in the EU Single Market, the future Toy Safety Directive/Regulation must set a blueprint for a modern and ambitious EU product safety legislation. 

In light of the growing number of connected toys, the Toy Safety Directive must keep pace with digitalisation and the new risks that are emerging thereof. Security and privacy risks are not covered by the risk analysis of the current directive. The general safety requirements (Article 10(2)) and the particular safety requirements (Annex II) of the directive only focus on the functional safety of toys such as mechanical, electrical and chemical safety. What is missing are clear cybersecurity protection objectives and requirements such as an adequate protection against external attacks and measures to protect the right to privacy. This regulatory gap is crucial and has to be closed. 

Moreover, a revision of the current directive is needed given the low compliance level of toys in the internal market as shown by the Safety Gate numbers year by year. This is due to the fact that toys are placed on the market through a mere self-declaration of conformity and market surveillance authorities are only able to identify the tip of the iceberg of non-compliant toys. 

In our view, the European legislator should remedy this situation by strengthening the precautionary principle. Toys must be subject to a mandatory ex-ante assessment by independent conformity assessment bodies if 
1) toys pose a potential risk to life, limb, privacy or other substantial legal interests of the child, or, if 
2) toys are used by children up to three years of age.

Please find attached a detailed position paper on the subject.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Participation in international projects is a good opportunity to create new international contacts, develop language skills, learn different cultures, see the world and develop in different fields. This will increase the democratic participation and inclusiveness of the participants.

International projects are particularly important for young people on the periphery because of their social and geographical isolation. Thanks to the projects, we have established a lot of contacts in different EU countries. 
The projects provide an opportunity to network contacts among both teachers and students. Students’ friendship is expressed in peer visits and creates opportunities for studying in foreign universities. For example, thanks to our project, a Czech young man enrolled in a university in Estonia. 

Our school will have two projects from 2014 to 2020:
KA201 Understanding and critical media Literacy (2016-2019)
KA229 Music don’t need to know any languages (2018-2021)

The Gymnasium of Hiiumaa has played both a coordinating role in the strategic cooperation project and a partner in the school-to-school project. 

KA201 Understanding and Critical Media Literacy (2016-2019)

There was a lot of positive results in this project. An interesting theme and the possibility for students to participate in the role of curriculum designers provided added value to the project. 
Through the project and various meetings, students’ knowledge of foreign languages increased both at communication and vocabulary level. Presentations, presentations and representation of your country have honoured our school and students. 

Colleagues from partner countries were very generous and the project was successfully implemented through effective cooperation. 

In its role as project coordinator, it was difficult to take responsibility for the financial resources and use of the grant from 9 different countries. 
It was difficult for partners to understand that only one country is responsible for the financial part of the grant and that the Agency’s requirements apply. 
There was the impression that different countries had different requirements for the use of support. The differences between the KA201 and KA229 projects also caused confusion. 

KA229 Music don’t Need to Know any Languages (2018-2021)

COVID also affected our project. 
* as a result of the coronavirus pandemic, we lost financial gaps (the flight took place in the partner country and the school did not accept us);
* it was difficult to organise video meetings as teachers also worked in evening exchanges and evening school
* problems with the Internet
* it was difficult to mobilise students in home settings
* students’ motivation declined as no real meeting took place
* teacher motivation declined
* video meetings were remote and did not deliver the result we expected

Video meetings are a good tool for organising project meetings. They can be used both as coordinators’ meetings and for personal communication among students. If the project is only online, it is difficult to implement the project’s objectives. 



However, project writing, submission and reporting have become easier and easier to understand in order to use grants and justify unit-specific amounts for accounting purposes. Sometimes there is a feeling that the Ministry of Finance (national secondary school) is not familiar with the ERASMUS+ principles. 

Writing and implementing a project is an important work. 
Writing and submitting a project application will be prepared in cooperation between different partner countries and will require a lot of time and commitment. There are a large number of applications, but few funding is available. This discourages people from submitting applications. 

Projects could also provide for the implementation of grant projects.  

In view of the future and as the cost of living increases, both travel allowances and individual support per person should be increased.

initiative: External power supplies – ecodesign & information requirements (review)
feedback_text:
The IARU represents national amateur radio societies and their members in over 100 countries which include Member States of the European Union (EU) as well as the European Economic Area (EEA). 
The national members of IARU welcome regulations to adopt a “universal EPS” and the unbundling as set out in the Common Charger initiative.
However IARU feels it appropriate to draw attention at this time to the potential of power supplies, specifically switching power supplies, to contribute to spectrum pollution.
The recent evaluation of the EMC Directive covers this topic in detail [1]. Furthermore the last-published [2] report by EMC ADCO showed that approximately half (56%) of the devices tested met the technical emissions compliance tests. It is to be hoped that a more recent market surveillance report due to be published soon will show greater compliance.
Switching power supplies generate unwanted emissions and the attached cable can often make an excellent radiator (antenna) so that the combination can add to spectrum pollution. Unfortunately a lack of comprehensive standards for cables can result in poor quality cables being used in the marketplace. In our view, testing requirements should reflect typical use cases of EPS with associated cabling.

[1]Study on the evaluation of the Electromagnetic Compatibility Directive 2014/30/EU (EMCD): final report, Publications Office, 2021
[2] Report on the Fifth Joint Cross-Border EMC Market Surveillance Campaign on switching power supplies (2012/2013)

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
My experience as citizen of Greece that deals with IKY, the Erasmus+ national agency, is rather disappointing. It seems to me there is no transparency in their way of handling things and the evaluation of programs is not really based on the text of the evaluation but on other things (e.g., if a school is already a partner in another project, etc.). Unfortunately, it is no secret that corruption is rather high in this part of the EU. Thus I strongly believe that it is a big mistake to have a decentralized evaluation scheme of applications. The EU should establish a really independent body of evaluators, ask applicants to write their applications in English, French, or German and submitted them to Brussels for evaluation. I  am not really sure about this, but I have the impression that other programs (e.g., the HORIZONS2020 project) used this evaluation scheme. This way, I believe, applicants would be sure their applications have been evaluated in the best possible way. 

Another thing that I would like to mention is that any country that directly participates in this project should accept and respect the basic principles of the EU. Currently, Turkey is a program country which is really an oxymoron. First, the president of this country always about the EU in a disparagingly manner and the same time there is no democracy in Turkey. Journalists are jailed just for saying the truth, the human rights of minorities are violated, etc. I just wonder if Turkey is member country of the program, why Russia and maybe Iran re not members countries? There are no differences between these countries.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Although the WEEE Regulation is a good theoretical concept, in practice it makes it very difficult, or almost impossible, for small and medium-sized enterprises to participate in the trading of electrical appliances. Expensive fees, complicated registration and notification procedures and lack of exemption for small quantities are just a few examples. At the same time, it supports unfair competition from the Far East, where suppliers are impracticable and can freely bring truly dangerous products to the market without fear of consequences.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Bvmde calls for: ‘Take waste from electrical and electronic equipment’ (WEEE), other waste products and the environmental impact of the extraction of rare earths and metals used in electronic equipment in the ‘Zero pollution action plan’. Europe recycles the highest share of WEEE in the world, but it is only 42.5 %. It should be a top priority that we have effective recycling systems in the EU where all e-waste is considered a valuable resource and properly recycled within EU borders. It is crucial that the individual valuable metals, minerals and chemicals can be recovered from the used electronics and then reused into new electronics. The further extraction of rare earths and other metals, minerals, etc. and the associated environmental degradation must be stopped. Not to mention the sometimes miserable circumstances for workers in the mines (often children). Action is needed to protect the fundamental rights of these people. It is also very important that the EU requires all electrical equipment produced and imported in the EU to comply with the RoHS Directive, which restricts hazardous substances in electrical and electronic equipment. The RoHS Directive currently restricts the use of 10 substances: Lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, polybrominated biphenyls (PBB) and polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDE), bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and diisobutyl phthalate (DIBP). https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/waste-and-recycling/rohs-directive_en

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Perhaps a more rigorous auditing process for new program should be implemented, at least vis-à-vis the administration and scholarship related issues. In my personal experience, accountability was an issue in one of the Erasmus Mundus programs.

I was in an Erasmus Mundus Program (History in the Public Sphere MA) between 2020-2022. As part of the first cohort, we anticipated administrative challenges. However, what we didn't expect was a sudden change in scholarship amount right before we were due to graduate. In essence, the duration and scholarship amount stated in the offer letter AND student handbook was not honored. The final scholarship amount was two months short. Not only so, it was not communicated until the very end of the program, two days before the final presentation of our thesis projects. 

Furthermore, the scholarship was also not deposited on time in a two-month window. Between Sept-Oct 2021, no scholarship was deposited, until late October. We were due to travel between consortium universities then, and had to depend on one another to be able to afford subsistence costs. We did receive the full amount for the two months in the end, despite the delay. 

In both instances, students contacted the administrators of the program and filed complaints. In both instances, the administrator replied that it was an administrative error, without ameliorative action. Throughout the program, there were also multiple feedback sessions where students were invited to share their commendations and complaints. All this to say that students have been actively providing feedback to the program.

I hope that new programs are able to get the administrative support they need. I also hope that agreements between students and the Erasmus+ programs can be honored. I would have loved to recommend the program to other students in my country, as there were aspects of the curricula I enjoyed. However, I cannot do so in good conscience, given the lack of accountability in basic administrative work. 

Thank you so much.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
EPEE, the voice of the air conditioning, heat pump, and refrigeration industry in Europe, welcomes the opportunity to provide comments to the Commission’s proposals for reviewing ENER Lot 1 (ecodesign Regulation 813/2013 and energy labelling Regulation 811/2013 for space heaters). We welcome the Commission’s considerations of the industry comments following the last Consultation Forum of 27 September and we support the need for reviewing the requirements for space heaters.

Nonetheless, we believe that certain aspects could be further optimised. 

In light of the REPowerEU Communication and the Fit-for-55 strategy, which are a result of the ever-increasing pressure to decouple our economic future from the use of fossil fuels, the Commission has put forward the goal to accelerate the rollout of heat pumps to 10 million newly installed units by 2027 and 30 million by 2030. There is a clear need to facilitate the uptake of heat pumps, especially in the legislative framework provided by the ecodesign and energy labelling regulations for space heaters (ENER Lot 1). 

In view of these developments, EPEE welcomes transition to an A – G energy labelling scale as fast as possible. This is not only welcome but also needed to move towards higher efficiencies in the market by allowing for a proper comparison between the energy efficiencies of the various space heating technologies. 

EPEE calls for further investigation on whether the currently proposed additional requirements for heat pumps in ENER Lot 1, such as the additional testing requirements for Tpeak and sound power, are not imposing a too large burden on heat pump suppliers entering the market and inadvertently creating an unlevel playing field with fossil fuel-powered space heaters. 

Furthermore, the potential consideration of immature test methodologies, such as compensation methods, are not helpful in fostering the uptake of heat pumps in the market. 

In addition, test methodologies, such as Tpeak, must be considered carefully in view of facilitating the market towards lower temperatures. Although the need for test modification is recognised, any considerations of applying the test or adding penalties should be balanced and reasonable. 

Please see our recommendations with more detailed explanation in our attached position paper (dated 15 November 2021).

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Right to Repair Europe welcomes this opportunity to provide evidence on the revision of the WEEE Directive. Given the exponential growth in our consumption of electronics since the entry into force of the WEEE directive, the current rules for managing WEEE are outdated. E-waste is now the fastest growing waste stream and without decisive action the problem will worsen. Preventing e-waste is enabled by prolonging product lifetimes through repair. This revision is an opportunity to consolidate repair as the primary solution for WEEE, in accordance with the EU waste hierarchy. As a result of improper e-waste management, millions of tons of WEEE are improperly disposed of in the EU (in nature, residual waste streams, or illegal exports). Without the possibility to easily repair devices, their hazardous components will continue to enter the waste stream and cause health and environmental damage.To unlock the potential of repair as a solution, this revision should contain the following policy options. Current targets for collection and recycling of WEEE do not encourage e-waste prevention through repair. We therefore recommend shifting the focus to setting legally-binding targets for e-waste prevention, and for repair and reuse activities. To facilitate repair, this revision must ensure that rules on the collection and treatment of e-waste are more robust. In 2019, only three EU member states met the WEEE collection target. Concretely, collection targets could be improved by unlinking the targets to the EEE placed on the market. Setting collection targets in this way is disproportionate and does not place sufficient emphasis on reuse and repair. Once collected, preparation and treatment of WEEE for reuse and repair must be prioritised. Measures should be taken to avoid damage to WEEE during collection, transport, and storage - for example by making its storage in waterproof facilities mandatory. Waste management companies should be obliged to allow professional repairers, refurbishers and members of community repair initiatives access to reusable WEEE. Deposit return schemes for all WEEE should be introduced, with collection points widely available at local level. Finally, to ensure the overall effectiveness of the revised directive, we advise that the CENELEC standards for collection, treatment, transport and preparation for re-use of WEEE be made mandatory in EU member states. Enhance producer responsibility models.This revision can prioritise repair through improving producer responsibility schemes. Producer participation in nation-wide networks for WEEE return should become obligatory. Further, producer fees should be modulated to prioritise repair and refurbishment. For example, the funds from EPR schemes could be earmarked to promote repair practices, like funding the collection of used spare parts that could be reused. Such modulation of fees can be seen in the producer responsibility model in France. EPR schemes should also be used to monitor substandard practices surrounding WEEE shipped outside the EU, through funding the traceability of these products. Producers reporting responsibilities should be increased, and collection reports should be publicly available. In addition, policy-makers should remember that the ability to properly treat devices reaching their end-of-life is contingent on how the product is designed. Waste policies should be aligned with an increase of the ambition of the proposed ESPR which should, ideally, ban the destruction of unsold EEE and result in solid requirements on product design and information (such as a general obligation to design for disassembly). Finally, given that even the existing rules on WEEE are difficult to enforce across the EU, we urge that this directive be turned into a regulation, thus ensuring that the rules are directly implementable, harmonised and binding.
text_raw: Right to Repair Europe welcomes this opportunity to provide evidence on the revision of the WEEE Directive. Given the exponential growth in our consumption of electronics since the entry into force of the WEEE directive, the current rules for managing WEEE are outdated. E-waste is now the fastest growing waste stream and without decisive action the problem will worsen. Preventing e-waste is enabled by prolonging product lifetimes through repair. This revision is an opportunity to consolidate repair as the primary solution for WEEE, in accordance with the EU waste hierarchy. As a result of improper e-waste management, millions of tons of WEEE are improperly disposed of in the EU (in nature, residual waste streams, or illegal exports). Without the possibility to easily repair devices, their hazardous components will continue to enter the waste stream and cause health and environmental damage.To unlock the potential of repair as a solution, this revision should contain the following policy options. Current targets for collection and recycling of WEEE do not encourage e-waste prevention through repair. We therefore recommend shifting the focus to setting legally-binding targets for e-waste prevention, and for repair and reuse activities. To facilitate repair, this revision must ensure that rules on the collection and treatment of e-waste are more robust. In 2019, only three EU member states met the WEEE collection target. Concretely, collection targets could be improved by unlinking the targets to the EEE placed on the market. Setting collection targets in this way is disproportionate and does not place sufficient emphasis on reuse and repair. Once collected, preparation and treatment of WEEE for reuse and repair must be prioritised. Measures should be taken to avoid damage to WEEE during collection, transport, and storage - for example by making its storage in waterproof facilities mandatory. Waste management companies should be obliged to allow professional repairers, refurbishers and members of community repair initiatives access to reusable WEEE. Deposit return schemes for all WEEE should be introduced, with collection points widely available at local level. Finally, to ensure the overall effectiveness of the revised directive, we advise that the CENELEC standards for collection, treatment, transport and preparation for re-use of WEEE be made mandatory in EU member states. Enhance producer responsibility models.This revision can prioritise repair through improving producer responsibility schemes. Producer participation in nation-wide networks for WEEE return should become obligatory. Further, producer fees should be modulated to prioritise repair and refurbishment. For example, the funds from EPR schemes could be earmarked to promote repair practices, like funding the collection of used spare parts that could be reused. Such modulation of fees can be seen in the producer responsibility model in France. EPR schemes should also be used to monitor substandard practices surrounding WEEE shipped outside the EU, through funding the traceability of these products. Producers reporting responsibilities should be increased, and collection reports should be publicly available. In addition, policy-makers should remember that the ability to properly treat devices reaching their end-of-life is contingent on how the product is designed. Waste policies should be aligned with an increase of the ambition of the proposed ESPR which should, ideally, ban the destruction of unsold EEE and result in solid requirements on product design and information (such as a general obligation to design for disassembly). Finally, given that even the existing rules on WEEE are difficult to enforce across the EU, we urge that this directive be turned into a regulation, thus ensuring that the rules are directly implementable, harmonised and binding.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
The Danish industry of detergents represented by the Association of Cosmetics and Detergents, "Kosmetik- og Hygiejnebranchen", strongly welcomes this initiative from the European Commission.

The Detergent Regulation should only regulate cleaning products
- The Detergent Regulation should continue to regulate the sector as a separate regulation. Such a modification would not grant any benefits.
- The scope of the Detergent Regulation should remain for cleaning products only, including microbial cleaning products.

Overlaps and inconsistencies should be addressed
- Double obligation has resulted in overlaps particularly between the Detergent Regulation and CLP in regard to labelling of ingredients, specific
perfuming substances and preservatives. Such discrepancies should be addressed to accommodate a transparent and unambiguous
consumer guidance.

Information on pack should be legible, consistent and useful
- CLP should regulate the information required on the label of the product, as is the case for chemicals in general.
- The Detergent Regulation should only apply to the sector specific conditions of detergents not already regulated by other pieces of legislation.
- Digitalization provides an opportunity to finally take care of the challenges connected to the requirements of CLP and the Detergent Regulation 
by making specific information elements from the label available online instead.

Evaluation of ingredients should exclusively be done through REACH
- Restrictions on specific substances by the detergent regulation may accidentally result in consumers losing their confidence in other regulations such as REACH.
- To support the aim of "one substance, one evaluation", substance specific restrictions should therefore remain a topic addressed only by REACH.

The Detergent Regulation should comply with the real world
- Historically the adoption of the Detergent Regulation included the aim of protecting the environment against environmentally harmful substances. This thoughtfulness and consideration for the environment is fundamental for the Detergent Regulation and should be retained as an essential feature when the regulation is revised.
- For example, laundry detergents should be able to address specific conditions such as the cleaning temperature or dosage for a specific amount of clothes.
- The Detergent Regulation should also comply with the real world – a typical washing machine is capable of washing at least 8 kg of clothes, whereas the Detergent Regulation defines the standard washing machine load to equal 4,5 kg.
- A growing trend is the sale of detergents in bulk and refillable packaging. While such initiatives may lead to potential benefits by reducing the amount of packaging required, the legislation should ensure proper consumer safety and hazard communication.

Please see the attached file for the detailed feedback.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The new Erasmus + programme offers more opportunities, fewer opportunities, outside Europe. This is seen as positive by consortium I-CNN (Alfa College, ROC Friese Poort and Landstede). However, as a consortium, we have faced a number of challenges since the launch of the new Erasmus + programme:
1. The Beneficiairy Module was only made available one year after the start of the programme.
2. The consortium’s individual partners are still not able to log in.
3. The Beneficiairy Module still has several bugs (e.g. the Beneficiary Module can often not be opened or stuck) and is slow.
4. Despite the fact that the OLS would become part of the Beneficiairy Module, a separate system has again been chosen. A system that is very cumbersome (create its own ECAS account, the language test can be done through many steps).
5. The Beneficiairy module and OLS have been introduced without instructions.
6. No extension of the contract period can be requested within the Beneficiairy Module, which prevents participants from being registered for the contract period. 
7. As the tools do not work well, we face a lot of additional administrative work. Resulting in unnecessary financial risks.
All in all, expectations (more accessible, less administration, more digitalisation of notifications and forms) have not been met at all.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
IKW represents the interests of more than 430 firms in the beauty and household sector at national and European level. The IKW member companies employ approximately 50.000 people and cover a turnover of more than 95 %.

The Detergents Regulation is still relevant from the IKW’s point of view and should be maintained as a separate regulation. In particular, in the view of the IKW, the labelling requirements in Part A of Annex VII should be updated in order to:
(a) terms such as ‘anionic surfactants’, ‘amphoteric surfactants’, etc. are no longer mandatory as label elements because they are incomprehensible to the vast majority of people using detergents and are not useful for the safe and economical use of detergents;
(b) delete overlaps and duplications with regard to labelling in accordance with Regulations (EC) No 1272/2008 and No 528/2012. These Regulations entered into force under the Detergents Regulation. 
In principle, only those label elements necessary for safe use and correct dosage of detergents should be required.
