initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The Swedish certification body for EN 50625 and EN 50614 WEEE AUDITS welcomes a revision of the WEEE Directive and provide the following input: The WEEE Directive and the EN 50625-series have had a major impact on how WEEE is handled in Sweden. The WEEE Directive 2002/96 did require a major shift in the collection of WEEE and a non-profit collective collection scheme El-Kretsen AB was established in order to provide collection of WEEE from private households in the whole of Sweden. Separate collection is coordinated from >600 municipal collection facilities in the 290 Local Governments in Sweden. El-Kretsen AB did fund the major part of the Swedish participation in the development of the CENELEC standards EN 50625 and EN 50614 and requirement for compliance with these standards was introduced by El-Kretsen AB in all new contracts with treatment operators. Following the work with the EN 50625 and EN 50614 standards the Swedish certification body WEEE AUDITS was established in 2016. There are currently twelve WEEE AUDITS certified treatment facilities in Sweden and a total of 41 municipal collection facilities that has been audited based on the Collection and Logistics standard EN 50625-4. The WEEE legislation has to be coordinated with several other legislations. WEEE AUDITS has therefore been active in providing information and comments on POP, IED/BAT, Reach, Rohs, WSR, etc. and have presented studies on CRM, PFAS, preparing for re-use and WISH illegal products. Also, proposing additional EcoDesign requirement in order to support recycling of secondary materials and de-pollution - targets for secondary materials in different EEE categories and de-pollution aspects in the products design phase. Member States have problems reaching collection targets and in Sweden the main two actions should be to focus on professional EEE and EEE that is incorrectly classified as without producers responsibility. There are problems with a correct interpretation of the legislation at all levels/actors from the Competent Authorities to waste holders. A revised Directive/Regulation has to clarify a large number of issues, that is described in the added document. WEEE AUDITS would also like to address the time it takes to make major changes in the WEEE collection and treatment eco-system. This applies to all actions from MS collecting accurate data, modification of separate collection, implementing changes in transport routes, improving treatment based on EN 50625 (including building new facilities, implementing new processes, introducing new treatment operators and new down-steam final treatment), etc. Not to mention the time it takes to recast all European legislation and to implement it in national legislation, followed by an update of the CENELEC standards. With this said, WEEE AUDITS would like to clarify that obtaining CRMs from WEEE may be interesting from the Commissions point of view, but is extremely complex and expensive and will not be able solve any short terms demand. Some CRMs can however be obtained from recycling of batteries and mining wastes, or is preferable obtained by traditional mining. There are many initiatives from the Commission with generic objectives Green Deal, Circular Economy, etc. WEEE AUDITS, with experience from having boots on the ground are happy to share experience from audits and findings related to existing legislation and have assembled a comprehensive document that are added to our response.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Concerning the new Erasmus+, I strongly disagree with the end of KA 229 and its adaptation to KA 1. It makes no sense that the most popular action disappears - it's the end of Comenius spirit. If the Commission had remarks about it, it could have modified it, but never cancelling it. It prevents structured and common goals' partnerships between schools and terminates the possibility of young children and teenagers to actually promote and benefit from the European values of cohesion, intercultural development and citizenship.
Then, we have now a clear separation between School Education and VET. Previously, under Comenius and E+ 2014-2020, students from both systems could take part in international mobilities together. Now it is not allowed. This prevents VET students to increase their sociocultural skills, to say the least.
The simplification of procedures has not reached NAs. They can still ask for any document they believe necessary, which could not be a problem, unless it is not clear from the very beginning.
External assessment of projects, before approval and on their final report, are quite frequently ridiculous in their remarks. To give an example, if an organization follows "by the book" the recommendations given on a non approval analysis and the following year re-submits the project, with the same partners, it will not be assessed by the previous evaluators and most definitely will have a different scope under the new evaluators, leading again to a non aproval for different reasons.

initiative: Protecting children from unsafe toys and strengthening the Single Market – revision of the Toy Safety Directive
feedback_text:
FPS Economy welcomes this assessment which contains good proposals.
Some comments/proposals:
• There is a need to tighten up chemical requirements. The Committee procedures under Article 46 of the Toy Safety Directive 2009/48/EC (TSD) should be more widely applied, namely not only for toys intended for children under 36 months and other toys intended to be placed in the mouth, but for all toys, AND not only for chemical requirements but for all types of requirements. 
• Toy with Internet connection presents specific risks that are not included in the current TSD. The EESC welcomes the inclusion of this in the review. This should certainly take into account the evolving digital technologies where it is important that the product is safe not only when it is placed on the market but also throughout the period during which it can be used (e.g. software updates). However, checks on this by the market surveillance authorities are also difficult. This should be taken into account during its implementation. 
• Improving the reporting obligations of Member States on unsafe toys and the application of toy safety rules, and identifying indicators and related data needs for future monitoring and evaluation, is a good proposal. However, the data that Member States already have to collect and transmit via ICSMS, Safety Gate and via the TSD evaluation questionnaires should be taken into account here. The aim should be to reduce and simplify the administrative burden.  
• European control campaigns organised and funded by the European Commission remain essential.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Bavarian Crafts Day (BHT), as the leading organisation of the Bavarian Chambers of Crafts and Associations, represents the interests of Bavarian crafts with around 209 500 businesses. 

Brief description of the position (detailed opinion attached)

1.The importance of Erasmus+ for crafts

The Bavarian Crafts Day welcomes the continuation of the Erasmus programme and the significant increase in funding. It is particularly important to increase and support the participation of apprentices and workers from crafts and small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). This will make a decisive contribution to the equivalence of vocational and academic education in mobility and address the shortage of skilled workers in the craft sector. 

2.Implementation of Erasmus+ from a crafts perspective

•Erasmus accreditation tool
We see the possibility of accreditation very positively. The participation of crafts in Erasmus+ can be increased through accreditation, e.g. a Chamber of Crafts.

Suggestion: It would be important for all relevant documents/tools to be provided by the administration in good time in the future so that project promoters can act with legal certainty. 

• Green Erasmus
We welcome the link between Erasmus+ and the European Green Deal. This gives us the opportunity to take up these issues more closely in mobility in the craft sector. We also support the approach of increasing the promotion of climate-friendly travel (Green Travel).

Suggestion: It would be helpful for project promoters to provide a tool kit with approaches and examples for the implementation of Green Erasmus. 

• Flat-rate travel costs
In our view, the subsidy is too low. It is also calculated on the basis of the crow flies. For example, the distance between Munich and Madrid is indicated as approximately 1 484 km. A total of EUR 275 would be paid for the trip and EUR 320 for Green Travel. This does not approximate to cover travel costs. Young people in vocational education and training, whose income is not so high, will then find it difficult to participate. In our view, this contradicts the idea of wider social participation in the programme.

Suggestions: With regard to vocational training in particular, we are in favour of a significant increase in the flat-rate travel allowance. Another option would be to adapt distance measurement and distance ratings. 

•Inclusion
We support the principle of including participants with fewer opportunities in all actions. The craft industry in particular provides important support in this regard. 

Suggestion: The concept of inclusion is very broad in the programme. It would be helpful if the European Commission provided concrete examples of the increased eligibility for funding. Indicators could include, for example:

—Missing or low foreign language skills
According to our experience, insufficient language skills are often a barrier to Erasmus+ for young people from crafts and SMEs. Higher funding could, for example, be used to enable specific language courses for the target group.

— Young people with a migrant background and refugees
According to our experience in the craft sector, these target groups are less likely to participate in Erasmus+. There is a need for targeted outreach services and measures, including with regard to parents. It is proposed that funds from the programme may be used for this purpose, e.g. through a lump sum.

•Bureaucracy
Erasmus+ is a growing and therefore very complex programme. Many aspects need to be taken into account in the application/accounting process. 

Suggestions: For us, it would be helpful if the European Commission were to give more examples of how to understand individual concerns and prepare answers to frequently asked questions (FAQs). The Programme Guide or the Financial Manual should be made even clearer and more practical.

This will make a decisive contribution to the equivalence of vocational and academic education in mobility and address the shortage of skilled workers in the craft sector. 2.Implementation of Erasmus+ from a crafts perspective •Erasmus accreditation tool We see the possibility of accreditation very positively. The participation of crafts in Erasmus+ can be increased through accreditation, e.g. a Chamber of Crafts. Suggestion: It would be important for all relevant documents/tools to be provided by the administration in good time in the future so that project promoters can act with legal certainty. • Green Erasmus We welcome the link between Erasmus+ and the European Green Deal. This gives us the opportunity to take up these issues more closely in mobility in the craft sector. We also support the approach of increasing the promotion of climate-friendly travel (Green Travel). Suggestion: It would be helpful for project promoters to provide a tool kit with approaches and examples for the implementation of Green Erasmus. • Flat-rate travel costs In our view, the subsidy is too low. It is also calculated on the basis of the crow flies. For example, the distance between Munich and Madrid is indicated as approximately 1 484 km. A total of EUR 275 would be paid for the trip and EUR 320 for Green Travel. This does not approximate to cover travel costs. Young people in vocational education and training, whose income is not so high, will then find it difficult to participate. In our view, this contradicts the idea of wider social participation in the programme. Suggestions: With regard to vocational training in particular, we are in favour of a significant increase in the flat-rate travel allowance. Another option would be to adapt distance measurement and distance ratings. •Inclusion We support the principle of including participants with fewer opportunities in all actions. The craft industry in particular provides important support in this regard. Suggestion: The concept of inclusion is very broad in the programme. It would be helpful if the European Commission provided concrete examples of the increased eligibility for funding. Indicators could include, for example: —Missing or low foreign language skills According to our experience, insufficient language skills are often a barrier to Erasmus+ for young people from crafts and SMEs. Higher funding could, for example, be used to enable specific language courses for the target group. — Young people with a migrant background and refugees According to our experience in the craft sector, these target groups are less likely to participate in Erasmus+. There is a need for targeted outreach services and measures, including with regard to parents. It is proposed that funds from the programme may be used for this purpose, e.g. through a lump sum. •Bureaucracy Erasmus+ is a growing and therefore very complex programme. Many aspects need to be taken into account in the application/accounting process. Suggestions: For us, it would be helpful if the European Commission were to give more examples of how to understand individual concerns and prepare answers to frequently asked questions (FAQs). The Programme Guide or the Financial Manual should be made even clearer and more practical.

initiative: Detergents – streamlining and updating the EU rules
feedback_text:
SMEunited believes that:
The Detergents regulation is a well established, sector-specific regulation for detergents and cleaning agents. It is well implemented by companies and accepted by the authorities. Therefore, it should remain as a separate regulation.
Improvements of the interface to the CLP regulation concerning the medical data sheet and CLP, Annex VIII are necessary.
Improvements of the interface to the biocidal product legislation in relation to the labelling of preservatives are necessary.
Digitization and simplification of the labelling are necessary.

SMEunited prefers an amendment or minor changes to the detergent regulation and not a merger with other pieces of legislation.
Moreover, SMEunited thinks that any necessary elements of the Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability should be integrated as part of an amendment of the detergents regulation.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Feedback on Erasmus+ Call for Evidence — Youth Social Work
The Bundesarbeitsgemeinschaft Katholische Jugendsozialarbeit BAG KJS, as the national association of Catholic youth social work providers in Germany, is convinced that the Erasmus+ youth and education programme, especially for socially disadvantaged young people, opens up access to European learning experiences and thus makes it possible to participate more in society. It is all the more important that the Erasmus+ programme is inclusive so that all young people in Europe can benefit from it, regardless of their social background and existing individual constraints. We therefore warmly welcome the re-emphasised focus of the programme on social inclusion.
The complex application process continues to be a major hurdle for organisations. It is necessary to simplify the applicable, complicated and confusing programme guide and the burdensome application forms. User-friendly and targeted information processing needs to be implemented as a matter of urgency. The consistent use of simplified cost options is positive, which significantly reduces the burden on organisations in project planning and management. For the newly introduced “Small Partnerships”, a simplified application procedure has been implemented and the financial administration has been significantly streamlined by two overall flat rates, which in particular makes it easier for new entrants to apply and implement projects. There is an urgent need to increase the management flat rates in order to be able, as an organisation, to meet the high quality and policy objectives of the programme.

The distinction between cooperation partnerships and small-scale partnerships is in itself helpful and useful. However, it is necessary to closely evaluate whether the overall project flat rates set at their various levels are sufficient and appropriate.

While the newly introduced accreditation for mobility projects is appropriate for organisations established in the programme, this procedure is a further hurdle, especially for smaller organisations or those who wish to participate only sporadically in the programme. In order to allow mobility, especially for smaller organisations and new entrants, the implementation of short-term projects should be possible not only on a limited scale, but as an alternative of equal importance to accreditation. It is also necessary to simplify access conditions in the case of mobility of skilled workers (youth sector) and to reduce the programme’s objectives and expected effects to a more realistic level. 

We welcome the introduction of Discover EU Inclusion for young people with fewer opportunities. However, such offers need to be made more flexible in order to reach everyone. Youth social work providers are unable to participate in Discover EU as youth social work providers are unable to participate in Discover EU, as youth social work offers and projects almost exclusively accompany mixed groups.

We also support a stronger link between the Erasmus+ programme and other EU funding programmes, in particular the national and regional ESF+. There would be an opportunity to add a European or transnational dimension to EU-funded national or regional projects. Consideration should also be given to how more synergies could be created between the transnational ESF+ programme “ALMA” and Erasmus+.

Together with a number of different organisations from the European, national and local levels, we have produced practical recommendations and concrete suggestions for an inclusive implementation of the EU’s Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps education programmes and published in 2020. The resulting position paper is attached to this contribution.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
A comment should be made in advance: The Erasmusplus programme offers great opportunities for vocational education and training. It is very important that it follows the principle of ‘leave no one behind’ and that people who receive the support they need to be able to move. But in some places the programme should be better suited to reality, i.e. improved. Some aspects that we have noticed in the concrete implementation:
1 Green Travel
+ For GreenTravel trips, there must be at least the increased support rates for outward and return journeys, in addition to the first 14 days. If intermediate nights are also required for a return journey of several days, it should be possible to obtain a higher daily allowance for these days. As a general rule, hotel nights are to be planned and these are more expensive.
+ Indeed, it must be noted that the increased flat rate for transport does not compensate for the additional costs incurred in the case of more distant journeys by rail. On the other hand, the flights are still so favourable that the lump sum is sometimes not fully needed. 
+ Anyone who is not travelling Green Travel should be obliged to make a carbon offsetting payment. This is above all to raise awareness!
+ For trainers’ preparatory visits, the rate of EUR 575 is too low, at least if it does not go to the neighbouring country. There must also be a Green Travel version here. If, for example, Bremen — Vienna — Bucharest travels by rail and takes a single sleeve compartment, then the cost is approximately EUR 700.
+ If apprentices travel Green Travel, it must be possible to also subsidise an accompanying person, even if there are no minors in the group. A green travel trip to Ireland, i.e. by train OHZ-Bremen-Köln-Brussels-London Holyhead ferry to Galway, onward to Galway, is also difficult for young people (18-22 years old), who sometimes have little or no experience of travelling or having no experience with the train, and is so challenging that they will refrain from doing so. 

2 participant report: It is very unfavourable that the promoter cannot return poor or incomplete TN reports to the TN for correction. This was done in Leonardo, but unfortunately it is no longer possible with Erasmusplus.

3 Why have the learning agreements been so inflated? However, those from 2014-2021 were also ok. 

4 In the current Erasmus period, accreditation focuses on the impact of Erasmus mobility on the institution/company. This should be reconsidered. For a vocational school, a lot is really conceivable and useful. But a bakery that some apprentices want to send, and planning certainty through accreditation, for which it will be difficult to think about truly useful objectives for the Erasmus plan.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
2014-2020
It was positive that the Commission was flexible with solutions so that students were not hit economically during the outbreak of the pandemic.

Positive with traineeship scholarship. For us with a large number of professional training courses, without mobility windows for mobility, traineeships are a lot of work in the higher education institution.

MT + was so functional, but it was often closed to updates, delaying our administrative processes.

Commission document templates are in the wrong format and difficult to work. Updating them between calls is time-consuming.

While we should all follow the same Erasmus + framework, several receiving HEIs do not sign the learning agreement before mobility for our outgoing SMS. It is also noticeable that there are higher education institutions which, for their departure, do not retain credit or that students need to study full-time (equivalent to 30 ECTS).

2021-2027
The fact that the project period now extends beyond 31 May is positive as we can, for example, budget SMS during the spring term (these study futures usually end after 31 May).

The allocated budget continues to increase and the new possibilities in the programme are assumed to increase the use of the budget, but this is unlikely to be the case as we cannot always implement the new elements. 

With more new elements in the programme, the administrative burden increases, learning all the elements and keeping track of all the evidence to be collected. The administrative burden increases not only for us IROs but also for mobile participants.

The EWP was promoted towards higher education institutions as reducing the administrative burden on our higher education institutions, so we all thought that this would not be the case. Several years in, the EWP still suffers significantly. The Commission’s digital solutions are not in place, while informing higher education institutions that we will be ready by a certain date. The work on the EWP is something that we can add to any other work we are already doing, so the administrative burden continues to increase.

The fact that the Commission has not learned a lesson and makes the same mistake by implementing a completely new system (Beneficiary Module) that does not work is frustrating. They are bugs and other errors in the system, so I cannot trust that the funds I calculate in the BM are correct. Nor do we have access to the projects. I can sit and calculate funds by hand and hope that the calculations are correct. All this leads to a high frustration and administrative burden. The new OLS also does not work two years into the programme and the information received is sparse. Overall, it is a digital disaster.

We hoped that shorter mobility would be possible in the new programming period, as requested by many higher education institutions. However, this possibility came as a blended mobility, where a good solution is first to be found academically and then this exchange should be credited to the degree, which will be difficult. While blended mobility would be a good solution for our professional training courses, it would be difficult to find solutions that could actually be taken into account. Instead, it would have been desirable to simply reduce the length of an ordinary SMS, that it could take place for a shorter period and that it should not have any crediting requirement either.

The focus on sustainability and inclusion is positive. However, with regard to inclusion, it may be difficult because legislation differs from one country to another. We also feel uncomfortable to save evidence for children and disability, among other things.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Eurodiaconia specifically appreciates the EU's willingness to invest in the sphere of education and youth as seen by the significant funding increases for the Erasmus+ program. The program's goals are highly valuable to Europe and especially relevant in these unsettling times. Positive aspects of the program include its increased focus on diversity and reversing climate change.

Project planning and administration were significantly made easier by the regular use of simpler pricing options. The extensive program rules that apply to everyone and the challenging application processes, however, work against the program's efforts to simplify itself. It is necessary to eliminate duplicates and further simplify the application instructions and forms.

An increase in the organization and management fee for mobility projects as well as a rate option for human costs that is graded according to income categories are necessary to be able to achieve the program's high standards for quality and political aims. Additionally, it must be carefully considered if the various amounts of the specified total project flat rates are sufficient and appropriate. Hence, we emphasize the importance of reviewing and adjusting the cost options accordingly to the demands of the different beneficiaries. 

The fact that inclusion plans have been established and that inclusion in Erasmus+ is being taken more seriously in this new funding period is really encouraging. Every EU funding program must be created so that everyone can take part in initiatives notwithstanding their particular differences by making up for disadvantages. Therefore, we stress the significance of including these goals in all financing programs.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: Dear Sir or Madame, we are answering your call for evidence from the perspective of a manufacturer selling in many member states and producing some products which are falling under WEEE. We believe that the directive overall had a positive impact in the market. In general we have seen a considerable increase in the quantities of collected electronic waste.Some member states have developed legislation that implements an open system in the management of WEEE, which means that the quantities managed by the Extended Producer Responsibility Systems and those managed directly by managers are accounted for, a system has not been established to account for the quantities managed by the managers in each member state. The main problem is the fulfilment of collection targets without counting the quantities that are managed by the managers without going through the Extended Responsibility Schemes, this is very important in categories 4, 5 and 6 in domestic and especially in professional. On the other hand, in the case of certain EEE such as air conditioners, the level of waste generation is much lower than that calculated in the objectives, although it is true that the sale of these products is high, the majority of these sales (we estimate that around 70%) correspond to new installations and therefore no waste is generated. The same problem applies to photovoltaic panels and other products for professional use. Although both the WEEE Directive and the RD allow the creation of individual systems, it makes no sense to allow the creation of individual systems for the management of domestic EEE waste, since a producer has no control over the point of generation of this waste in the domestic environment, the collection at municipal points is avoided and unfair competition is generated. If the this directive is going to be changed, it is very important from our point of view to keep some points untouched: - keep 6 categories as they are - keep open scope to be prepared for future developments Overall we think that the intervention of the EU has been decisive to start working on the idea of urban mining in several member states. In our opinion this directive is still relevant and needed, either to solve the problems mentioned above, the Directive establishes a shared responsibility Producers - Distributors - Users and administrations, it is not acceptable that the administration wants to put all the responsibility on the producer and force them to finance the municipal collection of these wastes when they are already being financed through taxes.

On the other hand, in the case of certain EEE such as air conditioners, the level of waste generation is much lower than that calculated in the objectives, although it is true that the sale of these products is high, the majority of these sales (we estimate that around 70%) correspond to new installations and therefore no waste is generated. initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text:
In Spain, the current legislation is not causing all electronic waste to pass first through a preparation for re-use centre as indicated in the Royal Decree.

It would be interesting to set more ambitious targets in terms of reuse as original manufacturers do not want re-use and if they are not met due to barriers they introduce themselves, fines should be imposed. Printer cartridges are a clear example. 

It would also be good that the rules will be adapted to be more specific as regards preparing for re-use as it is not the same to reuse a washing machine as a cartridge.

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The EcoPoint Association is fully committed to making an important contribution to protecting the surrounding environment by reintegrating valuable components into the economic cycle, thus facilitating the transition from a linear economy to a circular economy, which is much more beneficial from an environmental point of view. We consider that the new rules adopted and implemented through the transposition into national legislation of Directive 2012/19 on WEEE, supplemented by Commission Decision 2019/2193, have contributed to a better use of resources from the recycling of materials from electrical and electronic equipment waste, with a positive impact on the circular economy based on the re-use and reuse of equipment and component materials from equipment and components with a life span exceeding or becoming waste. We believe it is important for government bodies to better facilitate the reuse and/or recycling chain of recovered components up to the point where they re-enter the industrial production chain. This concerns, in particular, harmonisation and simplification of the procedures applied to operators involved in the treatment, recycling and recovery of valuable fractions, in particular non-ferrous and pretious metals, rare elements, etc., so as to make them much faster in the form of raw materials in production processes.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ KA131
Erasmus+ is a fantastic programme, offering fabulous opportunities and enriching the lives of so many people, however, the implementation of KA131 is currently very challenging: 

EWP 
The EWP tools and communication are not working well. In fact all dysfunctionalities of EWP, which the International Offices are facing and struggling with for almost 2 years can be summed up in “EWP = Erasmus without Progress”.   
Instead of simplification, the IIA administration has increased multiply. The IIA exchanges are extremely tedious and time consuming and it is a highly discouraging situation which takes up too many recourses at cost of universities (mostly publicly supported) for very little satisfactory result. Instead of focusing on mobilities, the mobile persons and the many great aspects of the fabulous Erasmus+ programme, International Offices have no other choice but to prioritize dealing with technical problems.
It seems that the EWP connecting point was not ready for a feasible and successful data transfer in such high numbers and did not anticipate the complexity of the HEI systems and the Erasmus programme regulations itself. 
The interoperability between the different IT tools/systems is not functioning well and the different providers are very slow in making the data exchange work. The EWP functionalities are delivered late and not communicated in time to the universities. Work already invested needs to be repeated over and over again, without any results. It is a shame to put so much burden and monetary costs on tax money over such a long period with so little workable results.
Looking back at more than 30 years of Erasmus, EWP is so far the biggest flop of the programme since its beginning. Very frustrating and disappointing indeed.

Beneficiary Module
The fact that users can't even enter the system properly shows the lack of functionality of this tool. Basic functions such as logging in, having constant access to the projects or being able to import mobilities don’t work. Mobile persons who have completed their mobilities months ago cannot receive/submit their reports. 
The same as mentioned with regard to EWP applies to the Beneficiary Module: Instead of focusing on mobilities and the mobile persons, International Offices have no other choice but to prioritize dealing with technical problems. In most of the cases, IT specialists must be consulted for basic project/mobility implementation steps and this is a very dissatisfying condition that has become predominant in the current work with Erasmus+.

Erasmus+ KA171
The adaptation to the application process for KA171 in comparison to KA107 – applying for funding by region instead of per country – has somewhat simplified the application process and is, therefore, to be welcomed.

Erasmus+ KA2
The unit costs approach for the budget used in most of the initiatives in the Erasmus+ Key Action 2 had already been well established in the program 2014-2020. It remains to be seen whether the recently introduced lump-sum approach will indeed be a simplification for the project consortia. While preparing a project proposal based on the lump-sum approach is definitely more time-consuming than with the unit costs approach, time will show whether it will make the project implementation easier. This will depend on how checks and audits for these projects will be handled by the European Commission and – by delegation – the EACEA and the National Agencies and what the related requirements will be.
The first call in the Erasmus+ program 2021-2027 was published with a considerable delay, leaving little time for the preparation of applications in Key Action 2. In the second call, the change from the unit costs approach to the lump-sum approach for Cooperation Partnerships came as a surprise, with application forms being available only at a later stage in the process, and the information remained vague for applicants, even for those consulting with the National Agency.

initiative: Energy efficiency – ecodesign requirements for space and combination heaters (review)
feedback_text:
EHPA would like to thank the European Commission and the study team for the work already carried out and welcomes the opportunity given to send feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for space heaters and combination heaters. 

Ecodesign Minimum Energy Efficiency Limits
EHPA does not support the increased values for low-temperature heat pumps which are higher than the PEF/CC correction. EHPA suggests correcting the value to 150%, which is in line with previous proposals and takes into account only the new PEF of 2.1. We do not understand either the increased values for the thermally driven heat pumps for medium temperature (MT). 
We also find that the proposed minimum efficiency requirements for hybrids are not ambitious enough. Based on a PEF of 2,1 and according to EN 14825 test methods, we would like to suggest an increase of the requirements for hybrids in tier 2 to 125% in the near future. 

Material efficiency aspects
EHPA supports the inclusion of relevant, meaningful and verifiable/enforceable material efficiency requirements but has several comments and recommendations. EHPA appreciates that an exhaustive list of spare parts has been defined, referring to the most critical spare parts; accessible only to professional repairers. Only trained and F-gas certified installers are legally allowed to intervene on the equipment and systems. Finally, EHPA recommends setting a requirement ensuring the maximum delivering time of spare parts within fifteen working days limiting the scope to appliances ≤ 70 kW. Our full position is available on EHPA website*.

Requirements related to monitoring
This is a completely new proposal from the Commission and has never been studied beforehand, therefore we question the practicability and impacts of such proposal. EHPA is now participating in the technical meetings organised by VHK to further detailed the concept, practicalities and our concerns on the scope and requirements of this proposal. We recommend the Commission to follow the discussions and read the position papers shared with the consultant on this issue. 
Finally, EHPA is against comparison between measured and declared values and uploading on EPREL or EC website. 

Compensation method and alternative solutions
While we welcome and support BAM investigations on the compensation method, EHPA strongly disagrees with the assumption that the compensation method is ready to be employed, and not even with the optional approach proposed in the draft regulations. The compensation method is still not fit for short-term introduction. Our full position is available on EHPA website*.

Calculation Methods & Tpeak
EHPA does not support the proposal for heat pumps regarding the peak temperature. Heat pumps would be penalised for not reaching a temperature (55°C) which is not realistic. We would like to reiterate our compromise on peak temperature sent to the European Commission in July 2021, in which we proposed to reduce the peak temperature to no more than 50°C with the current load profile. This would create a level playing field for all technologies while delivering the necessary sufficient hot water to meet consumer needs today. Our full position is available on EHPA website.

Avoid setting testing method in the regulation
EHPA would like to share some general recommendations. The legal texts should not contain too many details on measurements and calculations, which should be left to the standards. This may create some unexpected loopholes. Learning from the winter package, it will increase the number of errors and the need for amending regulations. For this reason, we recommend excluding details on test methods/test conditions from the regulation. 

You may find our full position on Lot 1 on EHPA website: https://www.ehpa.org/fileadmin/user_upload/20211115_EHPA_written_comments_on_Lot_1.pdf

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: We support initiative to improve this area by establishing clear requirements for producer and importer (PI) responsibility and representatives of their collective organization (CO). Despite the set ambitious management goals, their achievement is often complicated due to the imperfection of the national regulation of individual states and its implementation, determining that, for example, only some waste managers (operators) (WO) have the ability to confirm the completion of tasks, although a larger number of WO have the right to manage such waste. It is the extremely oppressive state control that creates the conditions that only some WO participate in the system, although as mentioned, there are a sufficient number of WO handling electronic waste. This also results in a small number of processing (in the sense of performing tasks) and insufficient control of all participants in this sector. Therefore, we believe that it would be appropriate to regulate clear requirements for WO at the level of the entire Community, so that national institutions cannot artificially limit them. It is also important to clearly regulate that electronic waste can only be purchased by recyclers (WO) or preparers for re-use (with clear requirements for such entities, such as reports on reuse, which should also be allowed to be included in the achievement of national tasks). All other persons shall not have the right to collect and buy used electronics, if it is intended that the latter waste be properly managed. Accordingly, it is important not only to have a clear and uniform registration of PI throughout the Community, but also to determine the requirements for WO, especially as far as exports to third countries are concerned, which, unfortunately, is not recognized as a proper performance of tasks according to national requirements, although legally is possible. Therefore, a large part of the waste is exported to third countries, and all these quantities cannot justify the proper implementation of PI responsibility (performance of tasks). It is important that also the state's responsibility for the achievement of tasks must be established (because many aspects of the implementation depend on its regulation, control and administrative actions), considering PI participation in collective management as an assurance of their responsibility in organizational systems and without requiring additional financial guarantees. In LT, there is an extremely sensitive problem of the cancellation of documents proving waste management, which justify the realization of the responsibility of PI and the performance of tasks, when due to the sometimes criminal actions of WO, after the cancellation of these documents, responsibility arises on innocent PI, in this case it would be appropriate on the scale of the whole Community regulate these issues as well, so that PI are not responsible and do not face consequences for the illegal actions of WO. We believe that it must be clearly established that the principle of producer responsibility cannot include the legality of the WO activities, which neither PI nor their CO can ensure as effectively as state supervision. It is important to make it possible to implement one category of task by another, because i.e. there is a shortage of refrigerator waste, and an excess of other waste, neither refrigerator waste (in the absence of it) nor other waste (because only as much as is needed for the implementation of tasks) is managed. National regulation also limits the ability of PI to collect electronic waste free of charge, setting requirements to announce about it in the press and to place reception points in rare populated areas, while in large cities it is allowed to exploit only several collection points, although these areas are where most of the waste is collected, and in rare populated areas simple direct collection from homes is more efficient.

initiative: Energy efficiency – labelling requirements for water heaters and tanks (review/rescaling)
feedback_text:
Solar Heat Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide additional feedback on the call for evidence for the impact assessment on the Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and storage tanks, while thanking the European Commission services and the experts contributing to this work. 

A detailed Solar Heat Europe’s contribution to the Call for Evidence on Labelling and Ecodesign requirements for water heaters and tanks can be found in the attached file.

Solar Heat Europe has already contributed with its comments to the draft proposals presented at the Consultation Forum. The previous contribution from Solar Heat Europe can be found online. Additional links in the attached document.

The current review is a unique opportunity to align this regulation with the renewed ambition from the European Union to reduce our greenhouse gas emissions by 55% by 2030. The Fit for 55 package, and notably the Renovation Wave, will open new opportunities to change European homes, making them more energy efficient and/or equipped with efficient and renewable based solutions. Making our homes future proof means that we must address heating and cooling, as space heating accounts for 65% of energy consumption in homes.  And we would like to stress the fact that water heating represents a similar level of energy consumption as power appliances do (15%), which adds to the relevance of the energy labelling and ecodesign of space and water heaters, as a tool to steer change in our energy system. This regulation will be essential to make European citizens active players in the effort to decarbonize heating and cooling, creating awareness and supporting informed decisions.

 Besides the effects in terms of energy savings and reduction of CO2 emissions, promoting renewable and efficient solutions will also help our economy. Taking as example our sector, solar thermal, we are proud to have a strong manufacturing sector that produces most of the over 10 million systems that we have currently in Europe. It creates jobs in manufacturing, commercialization, installation, maintenance and operation. More than half of the added value remains locally. It creates value and jobs while helping to cut emissions and to reduce our dependency of imported fossil fuels.

Key points included in Solar Heat Europe’s contribution:
• Improvements proposed to the product label
• Suggestion regarding package label preparation
• Proposal for a Retrofit Package Label
• Inputs for the package label layout
• Support to the proposed calculation of packages with Solar Devices
• Need to consider thermosiphon systems in equivalence to a package label
• Position on reclassification
• Include Solar Device in definitions under Art. 2
• Adapt obligations of suppliers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of dealers regarding packages
• Adapt obligations of internet hosting platform, regarding packages
• Recognition of PVT panels
• Specificities of Thermosiphons and ICS
• Comment on Lookup tables for solar devices
• Implications of new demand profiles
• Support to Multivalent tan

initiative: Waste from electrical and electronic equipment – evaluating the EU rules
feedback_text: The European Recycling Industries Confederation (EuRIC) represents the recycling industry at a European level. Uniting national recycling federations from EU/EEA Member States, the Confederation represents more than 5,500+ recycling companies from large industry to SMEs generating an aggregated annual turnover of about 95 billion EUR by treating various waste streams such as household or industrial & commercial waste including ferrous and non-ferrous metals, end-of-life vehicles (ELVs), electronic waste (WEEE), packaging (paper and plastics), end-of-life tyres and textiles. EuRIC strongly supports the European Commissions initiative to evaluate the Directive on Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) which goal is to protect the environment and human health, contribute to sustainable production and consumption as well as ensure that resources are used efficiently in an EU circular economy. In terms of sustainability, although EuRIC believes that a significant step forward has been made since the Directives entry into force in 2012, European recyclers believe that there is room for improvement in order to realise the objectives of the EU Green Deal and the new Circular Economy Action Plan (CEAP). The entire position of EuRIC can be read in the attached document.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The European University Association (EUA) and its members welcomed the Erasmus+ programme 2021-2027, notably for its increased budget and new actions.  

However, Erasmus+ 2021-2027 started with some delay. A majority of action lines are now about a year into implementation. Some, including the third country programmes, have yet to commence. Hence, the attached contribution to the call for evidence summarises initial observations and evidence gathered from EUA’s member institutions. It also outlines certain key issues, some of which have already been addressed in EUA’s report for the 2016 Erasmus+ midterm review. 

These issues will be further explored in a membership consultation survey in autumn/winter 2022 that will inform EUA’s contribution to the EU’s public consultation in spring 2023.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Following the experiences with Erasmus, this is my return.

it is not clear to find all the information necessary to complete the various documents in the final report. When mobility has been carried out in the end class, learners tend to take their time to complete their assessment, when they forget to do so.

There are many documents to be provided and once requested at the end of the project, which could be provided as the project progresses.

The headings are not necessarily explicit and for some we feel that we are asked for the same information but in a different way. 

The very positive point is that the agency’s staff are very available and always help us with the final report.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
The Erasmus programme is an important and essential part of our university’s overall and internationalisation strategy. It allows both students and staff to open up new horizons, so that they can learn and see through the plateside, both professionally and personally. 

The 2014-20 generation was a full success despite some inconveniences in the administration and was well received by all stakeholders. The tools and online platforms made available, although not always intuitive, have generally worked and have generally facilitated administration. 

Unfortunately, this cannot be observed for the new generation 2021-27. Unfortunately, the Commission’s honing and eager objectives — to facilitate administration by means of a large-scale digital plan (EWP, OLA, etc.) and to encourage green travel and inclusion — have so far only resulted in more work, as the online tools made available either do not work at all or only work very partially. As a result, many processes need to be documented twice in order to keep the overview, as there is no reliance on online platforms and must always be prepared in case something does not work. In addition, the deadlines set up by the EC seem somewhat unrealistic given the lack of IT infrastructure. The “new” generation of programmes has now been running for almost two years and, for example, the Beneficiary Module is not fully operational, but is to be used on a compulsory basis. It would be desirable for the tools to be used to function properly before deadlines are installed, thus avoiding unnecessary pressure on the International Bureaus.

In addition, the promised increase in the E+budget is not yet felt by universities. The new application forms for KA131, which require only persons but no period of stay, mean that National Agencies have to estimate the number of students going abroad for a full year or only for a two-week Summer School on the basis of distribution keys and past performance that are not publicly available. In the case of KA171, too, the details of the approved budgets, e.g. inclusion support, are not clear. The ultimate effect is that fewer students are mobile than at present. 

The fact that the new aspects of the 2021-27 generation of the programme are welcomed by all stakeholders is undisputed, but unfortunately the additional effort has so far been out of proportion and unnecessarily binds capacities that need to be withdrawn from other projects.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Ah, ERASMUS, this famous student programme, which has been enriched with a formal education and training component now well identified by the public and beneficiaries, but also with a “youth” (and sport) section which severely lacks visibility, recognition and promotion The youth strand remains almost invisible in the media, the institutions (even the most European institutions), which still and always identify Erasmus with student/academic mobility only!

When Erasmus became + and it included Youth in Action (which started to find its place in the ecosystem of youth programmes), the maps blurred. 
it would be said that Youth is always the poor brother, who is unaware or neglected and who has to fight to exist! 
What damage has been suffered by Avoir out of Erasmus the ‘European Voluntary Service’ (well identified as the mobility scheme for young volunteers) and transformed it into a European Solidarity Corps, with a little ‘vendor’ name and, finally, having reduced it to a small programme in terms of financial resources, which once again struggle to become established...

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
- New systems are introduced before they are tested and fully functional. Beneficiary Module is one example and the new OLS is another. This creates a heavy workload. We would be glad to test new systems and functionalities. 

- Paper-less agreements have created many difficulties and thus a very heavy workload. Dashboard and third-party providers are not always in-sync and the transfer of data is not always working to full satisfaction. 

- New rules are introduced with short notice and projects start running before NA have introduced the new guidelines and rules. Hence it is hard to administer projects correctly from the beginning. News are spread across the organisation and to students. 

- Very small details separate different kind of activites, making them difficult to promote in a correct way. Why can a PhD student do a 30 day and 60 day mobility but not one of 45 days? Or why is there a sum for days 1-14 and another 15-30 days, it makes the payment process more complex. 

- We would appreciate if our NA would be invited to forward-looking seminars and workshops about changes and new developments.

initiative: Erasmus+ 2021-27 interim evaluation & Erasmus+ 2014-20 final evaluation
feedback_text:
Erasmus+ 2014-2020:
• Implementation is mostly smooth — only sometimes the EU tools have failed to match the application, reporting or nomination deadlines.
• SMS: Minimum stay of 3 months not feasible for some programmes (mainly health programmes)
• Flexibility with regard to: Excellent COVID-19
• Good support for Brexit, even though the situation is difficult and unclear
• Great that mobility to UK has been extended to 3 years, but project timing (31.5.) is not well-selected as it is in the middle of the semester
• Damage the fact that there were no short Term Mobilities
• Inclusion support through actual costs and own application is very burdensome and a barrier for some students


Erasmus+ 2021-2027
• Toll that there is a return to short-term mobility — allows mobility in courses where it has not yet been possible for curricular purposes
• Toll that there are blended devices and that the focus on digitalisation is also reflected in the programme itself.
• Increased digitalisation leads to more redundancy in resolution; universities are at very different levels
• Unfortunately, some EU tools are not yet fully adapted to the new generation of programmes or not yet operational (e.g. Beneficiary Modules)
• More support or impetus for the implementation of the programme priorities would be helpful
• National implementation of Top Up Fewer Mobilities Call 2021 on student grants has led to double funding — good that this is being implemented differently in Call 2022.
• Comprehensive training via well-structured webinars from the National Agency (OEAD).
• The possibility of a flat-rate top-up for disability or chronic illness represents a much smaller hurdle than processing through a separate application for the costs actually incurred. Super also in line with the programme priority. 
• Good that Green Travel is now being promoted. This should be further developed, e.g. by introducing distance bands (to get more for a longer journey). 
• GDP is a very good idea, especially for students who cannot carry out long mobility.
• The Beneficiary Module would need to be further developed — very unfriendly and very slow... 
• Toll that 20 % of the funds can now be spent on international mobility. This flexibility in the use of funds gives us new opportunities to implement the higher education strategy and allows us to provide financial support to a much larger proportion of students.  
• The possibility of simple reassignment to the interim report is very positive.

Super also in line with the programme priority. • Good that Green Travel is now being promoted. This should be further developed, e.g. by introducing distance bands (to get more for a longer journey). • GDP is a very good idea, especially for students who cannot carry out long mobility. • The Beneficiary Module would need to be further developed — very unfriendly and very slow... • Toll that 20 % of the funds can now be spent on international mobility. This flexibility in the use of funds gives us new opportunities to implement the higher education strategy and allows us to provide financial support to a much larger proportion of students. • The possibility of simple reassignment to the interim report is very positive.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The projected European Opensource should not restrain the free-software, those that exists and those that may be invented. And on the other side, the opensource initiative should respect the patents. The equilibrium that need to be found must at least respect the author rights, which is already the case in the free-software and by definition in patents. I hope, as a EU-citizen and also as a business owner, that all the initiatives will tend to use KISS methods (Keep It Stupid Simple). And I wait from the Europe administration that more simple standards are used. The electronic invoicing system which will be in use from this year in many countries should be simplified, in order that SME and associations can invoice their work easier. More simple software is hard to produce but it should be prioritized over complex software. Less digitization is suitable to avoid the mandatory use of computers and portable appliances (phones, tablets, etc). About the storage of information at the Europe scale, the duration of data retention should me minimized at the shortest possible, RGPD is part of it but it should be improved on the definition of retention duration. i.e., there exists such duration for administrative documents in France for example. It should be harmonized in Europe. There should be a European directory of open-source software, free-software and proprietary software, like the well-known site "alternativeto" or like the French ANSSI lists. If there are subsidiaries from Europe for software companies, that should involve a complete liberation of source code of such software for European users : one may provide an eID to access such code, but the idea behind is to maintain access to what Europeans pay for in taxes. About identification, one may not force the use of smartphones. As it can easily be stolen or lost, it should only be used as a two-factor authentication system. The eID card should be the main authentication system instead, but not in a electronic-only way. I hope that the future of digitization will be a lot easier and respectful than now : it is actually a real mess that just tends to use lesser and lesser digital things. And stop winter/summer time also : that's a reminder.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: I shall explain why the wording: ‘assist countries in upholding these principles’, the ‘Strategy for the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 2030’ reads sarcasm and veils from persons with disabilities. The EU ratified the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities on 6 November 2009 by Council Decision 2010/48/EC. More than a dozen years have passed and the rights enshrined in the Convention continue to be violated with impunity. Even young people with disabilities still experience segregation due to disability and are forcibly sent to institutions to take care of their lives instead of dying there. This is because, for example, personal assistance of persons with disabilities is not implemented or is implemented seemingly to a significantly limited extent, which prevents self-determination, living outside institutions, education, work, social integration. In Poland, for example, legislative work on systemic personal assistance has been blocked for years, including by the Ministry of Finance. At the same time, care services provided by municipalities are often fictional. For example, a person with a four-terminal failure is proposed by a municipality, for example: 2 hours per week of care services, i.e. as much as nothing and what it means to be sent to institutions and to be vegetated. Poland ratified the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities in 2012 by law (Journal of Laws 2012, item 1169) and to this day, the provisions of this law are not being complied with, without any consequences for those responsible. Addressing discrimination and segregation of persons with disabilities in the EU by helping Member States will not work. In the face of reality, it sounds like a joke. Only harsh penalties for violations of the UN NCP can force countries such as Poland to end discrimination and segregation of persons with disabilities and to respect the rights of persons with disabilities. Urgent, concrete and real action is needed to bring this barbarism to an end as soon as possible and restore the rule of law in the European Union, as failure to respect human rights, including the rights of persons with disabilities as enshrined in laws and ratified international agreements, is also a lack of rule of law.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: IPPF EN Contribution to the Consultation on Better Regulation Simplification of EU law cannot become a justification for weakening democratic safeguards. In the current context of threats to democracy and shrinking civic space, the EUs regulatory system must remain firmly anchored in evidence-based policymaking, independent expertise, meaningful democratic participation and comprehensive impact assessments. These elements are not procedural obstacles, but essential conditions for effective, legitimate and resilient EU governance. Claims of consultation fatigue are not supported by civil society experience. On the contrary, CSOs consistently call for more inclusive, accessible and accountable consultation processes, including clear feedback on how civil society input has been used, and justifications when it is not. The Commission must not normalise Omnibus procedures or dilute Better Regulation safeguards. Upholding participation, impact assessments and transparency is essential for democratic governance, the legitimacy of EU decision-making, and protection of the common good against private interests.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: This contribution by CONFLAVORO PMI presents the perspective of small and medium-sized enterprises on the revision of the EU Better Regulation framework, highlighting the central role of regulatory quality in supporting competitiveness, resilience and sustainable growth. From an SME viewpoint, effective regulation must reconcile evidence-based policymaking with the need for timely action, without generating disproportionate administrative or compliance burdens. The document argues for a more modular and proportionate use of Better Regulation tools, distinguishing between structural initiatives and urgent or corrective interventions, and strengthening the ex post evaluation of EU legislation. Particular emphasis is placed on the systematic application of the think small first principle as an operational criterion for assessing impacts, designing obligations and ensuring that regulatory solutions are compatible with SMEs organisational capacity. Furthermore, the contribution calls for a more integrated and efficient stakeholder consultation system, inspired by the once-only principle, capable of reducing consultation fatigue and better capturing cumulative regulatory effects across policy areas. It also stresses the need to translate the Simplicity by design principle into concrete choices regarding legal instruments, delegated and implementing acts, and the use of digital tools. Overall, CONFLAVORO PMI advocates for a smarter, more predictable and SME-friendly Better Regulation framework, capable of improving the practical applicability of EU legislation while safeguarding policy objectives and reinforcing trust between European institutions and the productive fabric of the Union.

feedback_text: The European Trade Union Confederation (ETUC) represents 45 million workers as members across 94 national trade union confederations in 42 countries, plus 10 sectoral European trade union federations. ETUC is a recognised cross-industry social partner to the EU under the Treaties. ETUC calls for the revision of the EU Public Procurement and Concessions Directives to ensure that public money goes to economic operators that respect workers and trade union rights, that negotiate with trade unions and whose workers are covered by collective agreements. The respect and promotion of these key conditions must be an integral part of any public procurement process, from planning to award and performance of contracts. To this end, the revision must strengthen the use of social clauses, promote collective bargaining (including by giving advantage to tenderers whose workers are covered by collective agreements) and quality jobs, ensure respect for ILO Convention C94 conditions, end lowest-price awards in favour of MEAT including mandatory criteria connected with quality jobs and collective bargaining, regulate subcontracting (joint and as well as several liabilitylimiting chain length), ensure exclusion of companies breaching labour standards, reinforce enforcement and sanctions, and safeguard public, public/public and in-house service provision. For more details, please see the attached Annex, summarising the ETUC demands for the revision of the EU Public Procurement Directives.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: Over-regulation has long been a structural challenge in the EU, as overly complex and numerous rules undermine competitiveness, investment, and innovation. Regulatory self-restraint and systematic assessment of the economic consequences of EU legislation are therefore welcome and should be applied consistently by all EU institutions in legislative programming. A core objective of the forthcoming Communication should be to prevent unnecessary burdens in new initiatives and to prioritize making existing rules work better, before introducing new ones. To reconcile evidence-based policymaking with the need for urgent action, the BR Toolbox should apply stronger proportionality in the depth of analysis. This implies a risk- and impact-based approach, focusing resources on initiatives with the highest expected impacts, distributional sensitivity and uncertainty. For time-critical proposals, the Commission could formalise an expedited track based on a short and transparent impact scan that identifies the problem, objectives, and feasible policy options (including non-regulatory ones), maps key economic, social and environmental impacts, and presents uncertainty explicitly through ranges, scenarios, and assumptions rather than fragile point estimates. Such acceleration should be balanced by stronger follow-up requirements. For proposals with impacts on trade relations with third countries, the Commission should harmonize the assessment of trade effects and include mitigating measures to enhance legal certainty and reduce regulatory friction. Evaluation planning should be defined at the time of adoption, including indicators, data sources, timelines, and supported where appropriate by review clauses. Evidence standards could be strengthened through the systematic use of administrative and registry data, empirical evidence and reproducible methods, alongside transparent documentation of data gaps and methodological limitations. Stakeholder input remains valuable for identifying implementation challenges but is often insufficient on its own to support robust quantification of impacts or causal analysis, especially when based on non-representative samples. The BR Toolbox could therefore provide clearer guidance on prioritising routinely collected data, linking datasets where appropriate, and transparently presenting assumptions, data limitations and uncertainty. A more holistic approach to stakeholder consultations based on a single consultation strategy per initiative could reduce consultation fatigue while improving evidentiary value by combining open feedback, targeted expert input, and structured evidence requests. Where feasible, a once-only principle should apply, supported by shared repositories of prior stakeholder submissions and analytical materials. Consultation feedback is often dominated by inputs that are difficult to validate or interpret quantitatively (e.g. opinion surveys with limited representativeness or unclear counterfactuals). While such feedback provides useful context, it cannot substitute for credible estimates of costs and benefits. Finally, simplicity by design should become an operational requirement rather than an aspiration. This includes assessing the cumulative burdens, removing overlaps and redundant reporting, and ensuring that new initiatives do not re-introduce complexity elsewhere. Instrument choice and drafting should be implementation-oriented with standardised requirements, limited exceptions, and cross-references. The delegation of competencies to the Commission via delegated and implementing acts should be limited to indispensable cases. Compliance should be designed to be digital-ready by default, with interoperable reporting formats that support the once-only principle and reduce costs. Regular review clauses and simplification scorecards (e.g. the number of reporting duties, exceptions and cross-references) could help ensure that EU rules will be readable, proportionate, and enforceable.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Agricultural cooperation, which represents 800 French agricultural and agri-food cooperatives engaged in Bio, welcomes the targeted reopening of the Bio Regulation. It is appropriate to focus only on a few points which particularly penalise farmers and operators who hinder the development of Biological Agriculture, which must maintain a high degree of requirement. We identified 6 major topics: 1. Return to the 48-hour time after the use of allopathic veterinary treatments, including parasitic treatments. This severely penalises egg and milk producers (mainly goats), who sometimes have to discard Bio products when there is no health justification (when the deadline is 0 hours) and breeders do not have an effective alternative. 2. Maintain the long-term use of up to 5 % of non-organic proteins to lalimate piglets of less than 35 kg and poultry under 36 weeks of age, as no satisfactory solution to 100 % has been found by operators in the various Member States or by the technical institutes after several years of experimentation. 3. Allow access to organic food industry co-products to feed animals, bearing in mind that most of the authorised processing aids for organic human food are not on the list of those authorised for animal feed. 4. To allow the use of vegetable fats and vitamins in organically slab foods because the use of vegetable oils and vitamins are essential for the manufacture of such foods (dilution of milk powder in fat) and for animal health (vitamins). 5. Review the labelling of organic feed by simplifying it to allow the designation “organic feed” for any feed produced in accordance with Regulation 2018/848 6. Resume with the wording of the previous Regulation, which allowed animals to be finished in buildings in a framed manner. It is essential to ensure regular production with controlled quality in order to maintain the remuneration and thus the sustainability of farmers. Moreover, the majority of the animals were required to leave with ‘The final stage of lowering adult bovine animals intended for meat production may take place indoors, provided that the period spent inside does not exceed one fifth of their life and, in any event, a period of three months.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As a professional registered engineer -with the commitment to advance and protect society- it is needed to testify the following: - After having been using Open Source Software for 30+ years, in economic sectors that matter to societal needs, it is completely clear that OSS is a solid step forward in any dimension and especially so when it comes to transparency and digital sovereignty. - Furthermore, it is needed to testify that OSS alone is not the endgame: standards that enforce a certain level of compliance, such as GDPR/CRA/DSA & DORA-like standards, will become increasingly important in order to enforce a regulatory regime which promotes accountability - especially when they are harmonized. - Open standards need to exist and evolve in order to create the best possible incentives in a competitive market This is a plea to treat software systems -when used for societally meaningful purposes- as engineering products, exactly as anything else that falls under engineering domains in order to account for: * Systemic risks * Public safety and well-being * Irreversibility & long time horizons * Information asymmetry in adequate assessment * Ethical duties beyond employment horizons * Trust in shared infrastructure It is imperative to make the best of Open Systems and in the right way.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Coverage to improve the welfare of organic pigs and better N-circulation while preserving the open-air nature Theme: Annex II, Part II, 1.6. Housing and husbandry practices, 1.6.5: Open-air areas may be partially covered. [...] Proposal for improvement: The entire Part II of Annex II should be separated into rules for individual livestock species. The unclear term ‘outdoor area’ should be deleted without replacement. Possible cover must be designed according to the type of livestock, region and weather conditions. For pigs, up to 90 % of the outdoor climate area should be allowed to be covered and only the excretion area should remain free. Justification: Pigs are not steppe animals like cattle or horses, but are derived from wild boar living in the forest. Domestic pigs are often not pigmented and should therefore be protected against heat and sunburn with cover for animal welfare reasons. Roofing also protects the environment from unnecessary ammonia emissions, not only piglets or young pigs from pneumonia or other cold diseases. Nitrogen from organic livestock farming is a valuable fertiliser for organic farming!

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: Feedback on the EU Disability Rights Strategy 2021-2030 Submitted by Tukena Foundation, Finland 1. Early Childhood Intervention (ECI) Following the Guidance on independent living and inclusion in the community, it is crucial to focus on the early years key in preventing segregation, institutionalisation and fostering inclusion. Evidence from Finland: Our foundation works directly with families of children with disabilities. We observe that children with less visible support needs face significant delays; families navigate complex professional networks alone; the coordination burden falls disproportionately on mothers; and transition points create service gaps when children turn 18, support often disappears despite continuing needs. The Commission should conduct an EU-wide study on ECI systems and develop EU Guidelines in the form of a Council Recommendation. 2. Disability-Specific Framework for Social Services The voluntary SSGI framework must be actionable. We need a dedicated framework for disability-specific social services recognising specific needs. A cross-social services approach risks overlooking critical nuances and weakening impact. Co-design with all stakeholders is essential. 3. European Strategy for Welfare Technology and Accessibility A coordinated approach to accessibility, assistive technology, and AI can foster social inclusion, create unified European markets for assistive technology, and reduce long-term public expenditure. Assistive Technology Centres can play a crucial role in de-institutionalisation by providing tailored solutions for independent community living. 4. European Data Resource Centre on De-Institutionalisation A data centre collecting information on institutionalisation levels, policy effectiveness and progress would enable evidence-based policy and reform monitoring. 5. Legal Capacity and Supported Decision-Making An EU-wide study should assess legal capacity frameworks and best practices in supported decision-making. In Finland, families report that current systems do not sufficiently support the voice and self-determination of persons with disabilities across the life course. 6. EU Social Funding Strengthening enabling conditions and monitoring of EU social funding is essential. Earmarking funding at least for social inclusion is necessary to achieve the Strategy's objectives. (full feedback is in the attachment)
