initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Three specific aspects that could help strengthen the single market framework and boost procurement in the Member States: 1o/choose a Regulation (no directives) which is directly applicable, allowing, where appropriate, a considerable margin of adjustment for the Member States. The transposition of the directives has, de facto, led to overregulation that hampers efficient public procurement in Europe. 2°/in the case of minor contracts, harmonise the maximum and minimum amounts at European level and allow maximum agility. Expenditure below the minimum threshold should be considered (minor costs exempted from tendering procedures). In the case of expenditure between the minimum and maximum lower recruitment threshold, in order to increase the possibility of competition and participation, establish one of the following two options: (a) the possibility of tendering the minor with advertising; (b) the possibility to include models of adherence to the formal conditions established by the contracting authority in certain sectors in order to have a pull of potential suppliers. 3o/be explicit in the taxonomy of contracts excluded from the scope of public procurement. E.g. science, research and knowledge transfer contracts, intellectual property contracts, etc.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: L'open source è alla base di internet, e dei software che girano su quasi tutti gli hardware del mondo (proprietari e non). Molte big tech vendono come prodotti progetti open source (software as a service, ad esempio database come postgresql e redis) guadagnando senza finanziare chi mantiene il progetto (si faccia riferimento a cosa è successo fra Google e FFMPEG). Molti di questi progetti open source (FOSS) sono stati sviluppati in europa (Linux kernel penso sia uno dei più rilevanti) e possono creare il cuore fondante dell'europa. Suggerisco di creare una commissione di "esperti di open source" che unendo le loro menti possano trovare un modo di rendere l'open source una pietra miliare dell'UE su cui costruire una società indipendente dalle big tech, magari trovando un modo di finanziare l'open source. Cose che penso fondamentali diventino open source perché ad oggi tutto il nostro sistema software si basa su opzioni chiuse molto spesso a pagamento: SUITE: Text editor presentazioni fogli di calcolo note CLOUD: spazio di archiviazione potenza di calcolo password manager mail social media chatbot AI SOFTWARE: computer smartphone home assistant WEB: domini url motore di ricerca browser Io sto diventando abbastanza pessimista verso la piega che sta prendendo il web ultimamente, ma penso che l'UE abbia il potere di cambiare le cose (magari partendo da riappropriarsi dei domini .ue, .it ecc... che ad ora mi pare siano in mano a compagnie non dell'unione) e con sacrifici iniziali creare un mondo del software per esseri umani, non per clienti. Per arrivare a ciò bisogna come quasi sempre partire dalle scuole e dalla formazione: possiamo lavorare quanto vogliamo all'open source, ma se a scuola si insegna solo ad usare google drive, power point ed excell non cambierà mai niente. All'inizio ci vorranno sacrifici e fatica ma sono sicuro che il mondo sarà migliore quando le nuove generazioni sapranno usare linux invece di windows (anche perché dopo essersi assicurati il monopolio degli utenti i servizi diventano sempre peggiori per ottenere il massimo guadagno). Mi chiedo: e se potessimo costruire un nuovo paradigma del software dove invece di voler mostrare pubblicità si cerca di massimizzare il benessere dell'utente? Io ci credo, spero che anche voi capiate che per quanto difficile sia staccarsi da questa realtà, ne esiste una migliore.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: European DIGITAL SME Alliance supports the Commissions initiative to create a cohesive strategy for European Open Digital Ecosystems, at the condition that this does not become exclusive of portions of the existing market. DIGITAL SME supports the boost of made-in-Europe solutions in AI, digital public infrastructure and all the critical tech layers that are key to attain tech sovereignty, including via open-source solutions, to reduce vendor lock-in and foster better interoperability. DIGITAL SME particularly looks forward the publication of the Tech Sovereignty Package, and in particular the Cloud and AI Development Act, its core pillar, as a crucial opportunity to cut the dependency on extra-EU hyperscalers and boost European homegrown alternative solutions. Please find attached an initial response by the European DIGITAL SME Alliance on the European Open Digital Ecosystems Strategy. A full response will be published after further consultations with its members.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Independence from American and foreign companies will allow for a smoother European Union, attracting new nostrane minds to important projects, fostering development and growth. Open source, as also demonstrated abroad by China, for example, allows almost simultaneous and spontaneous work without the time and money restrictions that would bind each and every one of us. Driven by the idea of doing good and seeking a common and healthy independence, I think it is very important that we Europeans come together to increase our identity, working on our image, and making us respect as a global power. Being European is a pride, and this can only be confirmed by one’s own quest for solidarity between our nations, abandoning dependence on those who might leave us if time goes wrong.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: As a nationally representative employers' organisation, the Bulgarian Industrial Association submits this document to contribute constructively to the European Commission's ongoing efforts to strengthen the Better Regulation framework. From the perspective of industry, the quality, coherence, and predictability of EU legislation are decisive factors for Europe's competitiveness and for the ability of our enterprises to operate, invest, and innovate within the Single Market. We emphasise the need for a more rigorous and transparent evidence base underpinning all legislative initiatives, including systematic competitiveness checks and cumulativeburden assessments. For our members, particularly SMEs, reducing administrative and reporting obligations is essential. This requires the consistent application of digitalbydefault principles, the onceonly approach, and streamlined administrative procedures that facilitate investment and accelerate strategic projects in key sectors. From the BIAs standpoint, meaningful stakeholder engagement must occur at the earliest stages of policy development, with balanced representation of business in expert groups and consultation periods that allow for the collection of technical evidence. We also underline the importance of limiting the excessive use of delegated and implementing acts, ensuring that essential political and economic decisions remain with the primary legislators. We further highlight the need for realistic transition periods, full coherence across product legislation, and strict adherence to established standardisation processes. These elements are indispensable for maintaining legal certainty and avoiding disruptions that could hinder industrial activity. At the same time, we caution against regulatory overreach and call for a stronger role for the Regulatory Scrutiny Board to ensure proportionate, evidencebased interventions. BIA also stresses the importance of eliminating national goldplating and administrative overregulation, integrating digital tools to reduce consultation fatigue, and safeguarding the autonomy of social dialogue as a core element of the European social model. These principles are essential to ensuring that EU regulation remains practical, proportionate, and conducive to longterm industrial competitiveness. A detailed version of our opinion, including all recommendations, is provided in the attached document.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Hello, in order to save the Bio sector, there is an urgent need to simplify its rules. The latest amendments now bring technical performance and thus economic losses for laying hen farmers. This means that many of them will stop farming where possible. Here are the options to be put in place to respond to ANIMAL ETRE BIEN: — Organic food containing 95 % and anti-parasitic treatment without waiting time. — Leaving the hens outside 1/3 of their life (chicken rearing + laying hens). Go back to batches of pullets with 12 000 places to promote single strips and thus limit health risks... The sole purpose of these measures is to provide comfort to the animals, as required by the BIO specification.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: "Stowarzyszenie QGIS Polska" is a Polish NGO focused on open source GIS (QGIS) and open geospatial standards. We consider this initiative critically important and we are ready to support the EU with practical, hands-on expertise. * The EU has, for years, reinforced a dangerously high dependency on software ecosystems controlled outside Europemainly the United States and increasingly China. * The most acute lock-in is on mobile platforms (iOS/Android and their service layers), but similar dependence exists for desktop operating systems and office productivity suites. * This is not only a cost problem (recurring licenses paid largely outside EU markets), but a direct security and sovereignty risk in todays geopolitical context. * Mature alternatives exist now: open source stacks such as Linux, LibreOffice (ODF), PostgreSQL and open-source GIS (e.g., QGIS) can cover a large share of public-sector needs today. * The initiative is at least 10 years overdue and must move from declarations to fast, measurable commitments and practical rollout. For years the EU has effectively rented its digital foundations from external vendors. A political decision, regulatory change, or commercial shift made elsewhere can quickly affect the EUs ability to operate public services, update systems, and maintain cybersecurity across millions of devices. At the same time, recurring license fees and vendor lock-in drain public budgets and transfer substantial spending outside the EU, while limiting competition and slowing innovation in European service markets. The EU does not need to invent new proprietary platforms. It should scale what already works: open standards for documents and data, and open source where functionally sufficientLinux for desktops and servers, LibreOffice with open formats, robust open databases, and open-source GIS such as QGIS for a major share of public-sector geospatial workflows (supporting INSPIRE-style interoperability). This enables a real exit option, strengthens transparency and auditability, and redirects spending from licenses and lock-in to European service capacity: migration, integration, maintenance, cybersecurity hardening, training, and long-term support. We urge rapid implementation with clear targets and timelines: open standards first; an Open Source First procurement rule with documented exceptions only; mandatory exit plans for critical systems (data, workflows, integrations); EU reference baselines for desktops/servers and core applications; funding for migration and skills; and systematic supply-chain security (SBOM, audits, and trusted EU build and repository infrastructure). Mobile dependency must be treated as the weakest link: support European device and service-layer initiatives, ensure a trusted app distribution path for public services, and require that publicly funded apps are buildable and verifiable from complete source code and open standards. We submit the full document as a PDF attachment as more detailed feedback.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I have been using OpenSource tools for my personal use for 15 years. During this period, solutions have been considerably simplified and it is now easy to do everything necessary for the digital life of all days for citizens and businesses with exclusively open source software. A device is not really owned if its software is closed and immutable (see discreet data capture). Any device with software sold in the EU (from mobile phone to IRM scanner to computer) should allow users to (easily) install their own alternative software without loss of functionality. Otherwise, a significant part of the value of the device remains with the owner of the software for no consideration.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: To enhance the clarity, usability and practical implementation of EU legislation, several improvements could be considered at both the drafting and implementation stages. 1. Improve the readability and structure of legal acts. EU legal texts often contain complex formulations, lengthy sentences and non intuitive drafting structures, which can impede understanding on first reading. Greater emphasis on plain language, logical sequencing of provisions and concise sentence construction would increase legal certainty and facilitate implementation by national authorities and stakeholders. Drafting should aim to ensure that obligations are immediately comprehensible without requiring extensive interpretative efforts. 2. Reconsider the use and length of recitals. While recitals provide important context, their increasing length makes acts more difficult to navigate. Analytical or explanatory material currently included in recitals could be transferred to accompanying documentssuch as explanatory memoranda, staff working documents or impact assessmentsallowing the operative provisions to remain focused, streamlined and easier to apply in practice. 3. Ensure an appropriate balance between binding obligations and practical guidance. Legislative acts frequently set out detailed and binding requirements (including strict deadlines and procedural steps) while deferring essential operational guidance to future delegated or implementing acts, or to guidelines that may be delayed or lack sufficient specificity. This may leave Member States with obligations that cannot be fully implemented in the absence of timely and comprehensive secondary instruments. Key implementation elements should therefore be integrated directly into the main act, or the necessary secondary acts and guidance should be developed in parallel and adopted within clear timelines. 4. Strengthen implementation planning from the outset. Responsibilities are sometimes assigned to bodies that are not yet designated or operational, creating uncertainty during the initial implementation phase. Better coordination during the drafting processparticularly regarding institutional responsibilities, administrative capacity and national implementation structureswould help ensure that obligations are realistic, proportionate and aligned with Member States readiness. 5. Enhance the use of digital tools to support implementation. Digital instruments could be used more extensively to improve accessibility and comprehension of EU rules. Examples include user friendly digital guidance, interactive implementation tools, consolidated digital versions of acts and structured information platforms. These should complement, rather than substitute, clear and coherent legislative drafting.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The Swedish Forest Industries Federation (SFIF) welcome the opportunity to contribute further to the 2026 review of the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS). The Swedish pulp and paper industry fully supports the EU objective to reach climate neutrality by 2050. As active participants in the ETS since its inception, the Swedish paper and pulp industry have already reduced our greenhouse gas emissions by over 65% compared to 2005 levels and 80% compared to 1995 levels. In this period, production of paper and pulp has increased over 1% per year in average. Emission reductions have mainly been achieved through energy efficiency and replacement of fossil fuels with biofuels. Protection against carbon leakage must be technology neutral 25 of 45 paper and pulp installations, representing 75 % of the paper and pulp production in Sweden will be excluded from ETS due to the so called 95%-rule. Sweden has chosen not to compensate energy intense industry for indirect carbon cost why these installations will no longer be covered by any carbon leakage protection even though they produce the same products as competing installations that have not invested in replacing their fossil fuels to the same extent. The 95 %-rule is counterproductive when it comes to achieving the objectives of the ETS-directive as it a) incentivize operators above 95 % to revert to fossil fuels and b) de-incentivize operators below 95 % to invest further in the climate transition. It also sends an unfortunate message to operators that have invested heavily in reliance of the ETS-system, for instance Swedish pulp mills whose investments have contributed to reduced GHG emissions with 65 percent since 2005. The 95 %-rule demonstrates that EU-policies can be altered during the investment horizon rendering long term climate investments unprofitable. Carbon leakage protection needs to be technology neutral and not discriminate against the use of biofuels. All installations that produce a product that is on the carbon leakage list as previously applied in Phase IV (2021-2030) should be included in ETS and receive protection from carbon leakage. Therefore the 95%-rule must be abolished, and installations with 100 % biogenic emissions should also be included in ETS. Indirect cost compensation will remain necessary also after 2030 to protect against carbon leakage. Countries should use the higher limit for ETS revenue allocation to ensure adequate compensation and protect industry competitiveness. Indirect cost compensation should be funded and managed centrally in EU to create a level playing field. Clearly differentiate between fossil and biogenic CO2-emissions The pulp and paper industry has great potential to contribute to permanent removals through bio-CCS. Fossil carbon dioxide should not be equated with biogenic carbon dioxide, why support systems and control measures should be kept separate for biogenic and fossil carbon dioxide. This also applies to recycled fossil carbon dioxide. Fossil carbon capture should be treated with a focus on ensuring the integrity of the EU ETS, while biogenic carbon capture should be incentivised more strongly. Biogenic emissions capture can result in carbon removals in products, especially in the context of CCU technologies, and contribute positively to the renewability of fuels and materials. This approach would support the storage of carbon in products, thus encouraging sustainable innovation. To avoid underpayment or double payment, the accounting methodology should clearly differentiate between these two types of carbon, ensuring that biogenic carbon capture is appropriately rewarded for its long-term climate benefits, without undermining the carbon price signal for fossil-based emissions. This distinction would also encourage the development of more sustainable and renewable carbon removal solutions.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Minimum total area and functional areas instead of minimum indoor area and minimum outdoor climate area! Themes: EU Implementing Regulation 2020/464 laying down detailed rules for the application of Regulation (EU) 2018/848, Annex I, Part III, specifies stocking densities, minimum indoor areas and minimum outdoor areas for pigs referred to in Article 10. However, the terms ‘indoor area’ and ‘outdoor area’ do not correspond to the ethological needs of pigs or to the newly established new pig husbandry systems. The term ‘stable’ is not defined anywhere in Austrian building laws. Furthermore, terms such as outdoor area, outdoor climate area, open air area (open air area?) are controversial in specialised bodies, need to be precisely defined and make advice and control unnecessarily complicated. Proposal for improvement/simplification: The terms indoor and outdoor areas should be grouped into total area and the current minimum areas of both should be summed up. The term indoor area should no longer be used, but terms that are clearly covered should be used. All terms should be coordinated with specialist experts in the field of organic pig farming and checked for their practical applicability across Europe. Justification: The term ‘outdoor area’ implies an indoor. Both concepts are controversial in livestock farming and need to be defined in each text in a complicated way. Instead, species-appropriate animal welfare yards are now organised using functional areas, i.e. lying area, fres area, activity area and defecation area, thus adapting to the ethological needs of pigs. Internal and external separation by doors is not strictly necessary in open front systems or in multi-channel installations, such as modern animal welfare houses are constructed. Here, the EU Regulation should allow simplification instead of complex definitions that do not improve animal welfare! Coverage to improve the welfare of organic pigs and better N-circulation while preserving the open-air nature Theme: Annex II, Part II, 1.6. Housing and husbandry practices, 1.6.5: Open-air areas may be partially covered. [...] Proposal for improvement: The entire Part II of Annex II should be separated into rules for individual livestock species. The unclear term ‘outdoor area’ should be deleted without replacement. Possible cover must be designed according to the type of livestock, region and weather conditions. For pigs, up to 90 % of the outdoor climate area should be allowed to be covered and only the excretion area should remain free. Justification: Pigs are not steppe animals like cattle or horses, but are derived from wild boar living in the forest. Domestic pigs are often not pigmented and should therefore be protected against heat and sunburn with cover for animal welfare reasons. Roofing also protects the environment from unnecessary ammonia emissions, not only piglets or young pigs from pneumonia or other cold diseases. Nitrogen from organic livestock farming is a valuable fertiliser for organic farming!

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: Better regulation: uphold and strengthen health protective, science-based EU lawmaking The Health and Environment Alliance (HEAL) - representing over 70 member organisations across the WHO European Region welcomes the opportunity to contribute recommendations. However, we do not consider that there is a contradiction between evidence-based policymaking and urgent action and thus a need for better reconciliation. The growing evidence of health harm from the triple planetary crisis calls for urgent and increased policy action. Instead of trying to weaken the existing health protective framework (with eroding civil society and independent scientific input into EU law making processes) the EU Commission should step up implementation and enforcement of existing legislation for better health. This includes increased financing of pollution prevention and climate resilience action. Transparency, civil society involvement, and health costing (of inaction) should be principles by design, and will result in simple development and implementation of EU policies, tackling complex issues. 1. TRIPLE CRISIS: URGENTLY PROTECT HEALTH AND PRIORITISE VULNERABLE GROUPS Europe is facing a public health crisis driven by climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution. The health impacts and cost of inaction on pollution are unequal, unacceptably high. Vulnerable groups such as children, pregnant women, the elderly, people living with disease and those facing socio-economic health inequalities are most at-risk and most impacted. Evidence underlines that EU policies and laws urgently need to reduce this environmental, climate health burden as today 180 billion annually are linked to poor implementation of EU environmental law. The EU needs to uphold the overriding public interest to prevent and reduce health costs and health inequalities, not deregulate key environmental policies. Environmental, climate legislation which results in health benefits are a return on investment, not a cost. 2. UPHOLD AND STRENGTHEN EVIDENCE-BASED POLICYMAKING WITH HEALTH COST ASSESSMENTS The current Better Regulation approach and the omnibus processes lack the proper accounting of health costs (externalisation of health, environmental cost). Impact assessments still need to be the default for EU lawmaking, and need to fully consider health impacts and cost, in the short and long term, especially for vulnerable groups. Independent science is key to these assessments. As required by the Treaties and EU case law, any legislative proposal has to comply with the principles of a transparent, evidence-based and inclusive law-making process. 3. ENSURE CIVIL SOCIETY INVOLVEMENT AND TRANSPARENCY The omnibus processes are not efficient, as they deliberately excluded civil society consultation, justified with an urgency notion which was not defined. A clear, consistent and non-arbitrary application of the EU law making rules has to be ensured, as well as transparency on the legislative process. *** More information on HEALs position: https://www.env-health.org/press-note-health-groups-warn-eu-deregulation-drive-on-pesticides-and-biocides-will-put-peoples-health-at-risk-and-increase-costs/ https://www.env-health.org/eu-cosmetics-simplification-risks-prolonging-exposure-to-carcinogens/ https://www.env-health.org/environmental-omnibus-risks-increased-pollution-health-costs-and-inequalities-warn-health-groups/

Evidence underlines that EU policies and laws urgently need to reduce this environmental, climate health burden as today 180 billion annually are linked to poor implementation of EU environmental law. The EU needs to uphold the overriding public interest to prevent and reduce health costs and health inequalities, not deregulate key environmental policies. Environmental, climate legislation which results in health benefits are a return on investment, not a cost. 2. UPHOLD AND STRENGTHEN EVIDENCE-BASED POLICYMAKING WITH HEALTH COST ASSESSMENTS The current Better Regulation approach and the omnibus processes lack the proper accounting of health costs (externalisation of health, environmental cost). Impact assessments still need to be the default for EU lawmaking, and need to fully consider health impacts and cost, in the short and long term, especially for vulnerable groups. Independent science is key to these assessments. As required by the Treaties and EU case law, any legislative proposal has to comply with the principles of a transparent, evidence-based and inclusive law-making process. 3. ENSURE CIVIL SOCIETY INVOLVEMENT AND TRANSPARENCY The omnibus processes are not efficient, as they deliberately excluded civil society consultation, justified with an urgency notion which was not defined. A clear, consistent and non-arbitrary application of the EU law making rules has to be ensured, as well as transparency on the legislative process. *** More information on HEALs position: https://www.env-health.org/press-note-health-groups-warn-eu-deregulation-drive-on-pesticides-and-biocides-will-put-peoples-health-at-risk-and-increase-costs/ https://www.env-health.org/eu-cosmetics-simplification-risks-prolonging-exposure-to-carcinogens/ https://www.env-health.org/environmental-omnibus-risks-increased-pollution-health-costs-and-inequalities-warn-health-groups/

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Local authorities need a practical set of procurement rules and more flexibility to achieve local objectives. A renewed focus on markets and competition is therefore necessary. For the upcoming revision, the VNG stresses the need to streamline and consolidate public procurement provisions, which are currently scattered over more than 60 EU sectoral legislative acts. A review should be started to eliminate inconsistencies, redundancies and conflicting obligations that are unmanageable for contracting authorities.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Please find attached our detailed recommendations, and below a summary. Public procurement is one of Europes most significant economic levers. The 2014 Public Procurement Directives established a strong framework, but inconsistent implementation across Member States has prevented their full potential from being realised, limiting cross-border participation and European competitiveness. Many inefficiencies stem from national and sub-national barriers rather than flaws in the directives themselves. Fragmentation of tender requirements and compliance rules increases administrative burdens, particularly for SMEs and bidders from other Member States. A targeted revision focused on harmonisation, enforcement and better procurement tools will be more effective than an overly complex overhaul. Overcomplicating procurement with excessive policy objectives would only introduce more bureaucracy and undermine European competitiveness. Instead, the reform should prioritise simplification and scale: Joint procurement is a key mechanism for achieving economies of scale, yet remains underutilised outside crises. Expanding voluntary, strategic joint purchasing mechanisms would enhance Europes industrial base whilst preserving national autonomy in procurement decisions. EU preference in procurement has become more pressing as global competitors increasingly favour domestic suppliers. However, blanket EU preference would inflate costs, limit innovation access and weaken Europes position in critical technologies. Instead, the reform should refine most advantageous tender selection to prioritise reciprocity, sustainability and resilience criteria in procurement. Enhancing public procurement requires better digital tools. Further standardising e-procurement tools is necessary to reduce administrative burdens and improve efficiency. Strengthening collaboration between public authorities and industry including innovation procurement pilots would further drive process improvements and joint procurement efficiency. Europe has an opportunity to transform public procurement into a strategic driver of competitiveness, innovation and resilience. Procurement reform must be pragmatic and future-proof to ensure that European companies have the scale, access and tools they need to compete across the single market and on the global stage.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Dear EC, I am one of the leaders for a large and venerable open source project called Inkscape and I wish to share with you the experience of running an organisation that has contributors from the EU and the rest of the world. The Inkscape project founded in 2003 is a vector image editor useful for making designs, logos, pdfs, prints and other useful made materials. It was founded by a group of USA citizens as volunteers with no company or employees. Despite not having many resources the project has millions of users and is well known in various industries and academic settings. Over time, I have notice that the contributions from volunteers in the USA have waned, while those from the EU have increased dramatically. This change in who is willing to spend time building such a project feels like it is due to the change in culture within the Free Software and Open Source communities themselves, moving from a libertarian culture to a more social democratic one and I believe these slow shifts have changed us and who does this work. The EU is in a partially strong alignment with this new open source spirit and I believe you will be best placed to build many great things if you do it right. The main concern is, as it always was, resources. Our project is run on a shoe string and while we are able to pay people through our 501c3 parent organisation to provide technical labour, we do not have the stability or sustainability of some of our sister projects. What marks our project out differently is that we are still joined to a US organisation which is misaligned to industrious objectives so are limited in what we can do. While our fellow projects in the EU such as Blender, Krita and Godot have found better success through grants, industry partnerships and direct user funding. Although they have struggled to find consistency in various EU country legal frameworks for the types of organisation that they are, something I think you should think about making a consistent type of organisation EU wide. Improving the economic and labour cultural values within the Free and Open Source community has always been one of my objectives and I'm keen to see the EU recognise this deficit in the open source model and what can be done to keep projects healthy, independent and capable. Thank you for your time.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The UTPF shares the European Commission’s ambition to revise the framework of the 2014 Directives to achieve the EU’s strategic objectives. The UTPF is the professional organisation representing public urban transport operators (operators of 170 bus, metro and trams networks), rail operators (passengers and freight) and infrastructure and railway station managers in France. Since many members of the UTPF are entrusted with public service tasks, the urban and rail public transport sector devotes a significant part of its budget to public procurement. We would therefore like to make three key recommendations to the European Commission in order to: (1) deepen resilience and security of supply to contribute more broadly to the strategic autonomy and economic development of the European Union; (2) launch a major project to simplify the Directives; (3) consider public procurement as a powerful lever to contribute to the objectives of the green transition.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: The market for organic products has been developed by organic farmers. It is also the organic farmers who have worked extensively on public relations for decades, thus ensuring credibility and building consumer confidence in organic products. Organic farming is a vibrant organic system, which necessarily depends on room for manoeuvre in order to respond to different natural and sociological conditions, regional specificities and structures, and also in relation to climatic changes, with adapted and changing forms of farming in order to achieve its objectives. Organic farming, which is intended to be passed in detail into irreconcilable paragraphs that are considered far from the realities of the farms and downgraded from the top, is condemned to death for a short or long time. The rules on how organic farming can and may be used are, as far as possible, part of the self-governance of practising farmers.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Opinion on the simplification of the EU Organic Regulation for organic poultry farming 1. In future, in accordance with the Organic Regulation, grassed open air areas should be provided at each organic poultry house for pre-breeding, even if the animals are not yet able to use it physiologically. This is impractical and creates unnecessary burdens and endangers animal welfare, as the young animals without glucke cannot safely use the open-air area and, if necessary, cool and fall ill. Requests: Open-air areas need to be available only when the soil and animal status allow the animals to be fully fidered. Broilers: pullets from around 28.35 days of life: from approximately 56.63 days of the day of life of the housing house without open-air areas, it should be permitted to continue to operate without restrictions until full feathering. Justification: Prevention of health risks due to weather conditions, practical implementation for different age groups, using old buildings and indoor locations to promote small-scale agriculture 2. In future, the Commission intends to allow only one fattening poultry house of 1 600 m² per farm. This jeopardises the existence of many farms. Requests: Several houses of 1 600 m² per farm must be allowed, as is currently the case. Justification: Good practice for decades ensures animal welfare, the environment and cost-effectiveness advice confirms compliance with organic law 3. For broilers, a 2.5 m² open-air open-air range should be sufficient for broilers, both in mobile houses and in fixed houses with alternating open-air areas. 4. In addition to the above, further adjustments are needed to make organic poultry farming work in practice. Requests for a change in the interpretation of organic legislation: The dismantling of the 3 rd elevated level in poultry houses from 2030 onwards shall not be required; it should be possible to offer it as a luxury additional area. The open-air area should be allowed to remain closed during the laying break for a maximum of seven weeks if eggs are marketed under barn conditions. The maximum daily increase for extensive breeds in all EU Member States should be uniform; to this end, a list of lines at EU level will be drawn up to request changes to organic legislation: We need a uniform rule on the open-air area for young poultry: 1 m² of open-air areas for animals up to an average weight of 1.5 kg to ensure animal welfare and high performance require more flexibility in the rearing of pullets. The pullets must be removed from the age of 18 LW in order to allow a slowly developing flock to remain reared for longer. Justification: Practical adaptations ensure animal welfare and economic viability avoiding unnecessary bureaucracy and costs for farms to harmonise standards within the EU to increase competitiveness. These changes are crucial for a practical, animal welfare-oriented and economically viable organic poultry farming.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Towards full Federal Digital Sovereignty through Open Source and Industrial Independence. As a European citizen, I welcome this initiative, considering it a fundamental pillar for the maturing of the Union. My contribution focuses on three key strands. 1. Independence of Infrastructure and Software (Full-Stack): Europe must pursue absolute digital sovereignty that goes beyond the current geopolitical spheres of influence. This requires a concrete commitment to the development and adoption of ecosystems based on Linux and open standards at all levels: from cloud computing to desktop and mobile operating systems, ensuring that the Union’s critical infrastructure is transparent, verifiable and autonomous. 2. Accessibility and UX (User Experience): The Open Source must not remain a tool for practitioners alone. For a true digital transition, it is necessary to invest in the “usability” of free software. Open systems need to become intuitive, modern and less cumbersome, so that citizens and businesses can adopt them without technical barriers, making choice more natural and simple. 3. Industrial production in Scala: Software sovereignty is incomplete without hardware sovereignty. There is an urgent need for a serious industrial project for European hardware production, both for the Cloud/Data Center sector and for the consumer market. The ability to produce its own devices and servers is a prerequisite for, and an adjuvant to, the security and economic growth of a Europe that aspires to a strong and mature federal configuration. In short, Open Source must be the engine of a Europe capable of building its own digital future without external dependencies, combining technical excellence with user-friendliness for all its citizens. Best regards, Ferdinando Mongillo

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Being sovereign, in terms of information systems, means, in my view, mastering the bricks of those systems, for a population of standard professionals and not just experts. That is why I think we should not seek to achieve sovereignty over systems as complex as clouds (from OpenStack to Kubernetes), but over information systems based on less complex technologies (virtualisation, software storage). To give a concrete example of supporting initiatives such as Proxmox that can be implemented by a very large number of entities, rather than pushing for the use of cloud operators. This type of infrastructure is sufficient for a very large number of use cases.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Im submitting input to the European Commissions Call for Evidence for the upcoming Communication Towards European open digital ecosystems (Q1 2026). Given the 4,000-character limit, Im sharing here a concise summary and attaching a full PDF with detailed evidence, examples, and proposed EU-level measures. In short: Europes key strength is high-quality open technologies and strong institutional trust. The core weakness is the maintenance and security stewardship gap (funding, governance, succession, LTS support). The biggest adoption barriers are procurement/compliance built for proprietary vendors, plus operational friction (deployment, documentation, integration) and uneven software supply-chain practices (SBOM, signing, coordinated vulnerability handling). Open source delivers concrete lifecycle benefits: reduced lock-in, improved auditability and resilience, stronger competition and SME participation, and better sovereignty outcomes. Priority areas where EU action can move the needle: EU-funded stewardship (maintenance + security + governance operations) EU-centered foundation infrastructure (trusted repos, secure CI/CD, signing/attestation, SBOM pipelines, vulnerability intake/triage) Procurement reform with lifecycle-ready support contracts and audit-based accountability Focus domains: cloud, AI infrastructure, cybersecurity, edge/IoT, internet technologies, open hardware, and industrial stacks (automotive/manufacturing) Full submission is provided in the attached PDF. #OpenSource #DigitalSovereignty #Cybersecurity #FOSS #OpenStandards #OSPO #EUDigital

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Summary Relying on foreign proprietary technology primarily optimizes for short-term efficiency. Adopting open source strategically optimizes for long-term optionality, resilience, and digital sovereignty. Open source should be treated as a preferred strategic path for public and critical systems, provided that organizations invest in competence, governance, and maintenance. While it requires more upfront effort, it reduces long-term dependency, systemic risk, and exposure to external jurisdictional control. Detailed input Open source should be considered a preferred strategic path, even though it requires more initial effort. Its long-term benefits materialize when organizations invest deliberately in technical competence and governance. Open source is not free: costs are shifted toward expertise, infrastructure, and maintenance rather than licensing. In return, it offers greater independence, resilience, and long-term control. Security in open source systems is based on transparency rather than blind trust. Vulnerabilities can be inspected and addressed by those with the appropriate expertise and incentives. This contrasts with opaque, proprietary systems where risk assessment depends on trusting external vendors whose incentives may not align with long-term sovereignty, public interest, or risk management requirements. Governments and organizations retain the ability to fork open source projects to introduce specific functionality, strengthen security, and comply with local requirements. Sensitive systems and data can be self-hosted, improving data sovereignty and reducing exposure to external jurisdictional or contractual constraints. In conflict or crisis scenarios, reliance on proprietary collaboration or infrastructure platforms introduces systemic risk. Such services can be remotely restricted or shut down, potentially disrupting large parts of the European public and private sectors. In addition, it is difficult to fully verify how data is processed or accessed when it resides within closed systems subject to non-EU legal frameworks. Open source platforms enable cooperation at the EU level while allowing individual member states to adapt implementations to local laws, operational needs, and cultural contexts. This supports shared infrastructure without enforcing uniform dependency.

Open source platforms enable cooperation at the EU level while allowing individual member states to adapt implementations to local laws, operational needs, and cultural contexts. This supports shared infrastructure without enforcing uniform dependency.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: One of the weaknesses that affect the EU open source sector is lack of funding and weak community-building around the interest/field. Many smaller open source components struggle to make an impact without proper advertisement and funding. Advertising allows projects to find experts in their specific fields and funding allows them to focus their personal time using their expertise for development and to seek talent. Platforms like GitHub, which allows developers to publish their code and annotate it with tags for simplified discovery somewhat help reduce the fragmentation and helps with discovery of the project, but the projects are still required to find own ways to advertise beyond this. If projects can acquire funding, they can attract interest of other experts and join together to develop the software as a group, thus strengthening the support for the code and the developers behind it. Software developed as open source can be easily audited by 3rd parties. Any auditor can be hired or freely choose to go through the source code which is open to review it and find potential software bugs or other kinds of errors/mistakes. This allows critical code to be easily reviewed, which allows anyone to validate the safety of the software used in anything from vehicles to banking systems. Software in these fields is often well audited, but the actual audit reports are rarely public. This would increase the transparency and trust in the critical systems that power and assist our daily lives. Open source code outlives the counterparts that are developed by corporations and always outlives their initial developer's interest or development interest. When organizations behind the development fail, the development most often ceases without any plan to continue. Sometimes this development is passed on to a buyer/stakeholder. It is rare that the source of the code that is not developed anymore is published as open source. Open source can be redistributed, modified, integrated and as such even if the main "copy" of the software source is deleted, due to it's licensing the rest of the copies can continue to live under a different maintainer/organization and possibly name/trademark. To summarize, open source reduces dependency and increases the review count of the code produced, thus reducing the risk of using a component that will possibly not exist in a few years or decade. It also reduces development costs in the case that the developer of a component cannot maintain it anymore due to any reason, the development can be passed to another developer. It allows anyone to find mistakes and either report them and fix the issue themselves, allowing timely reaction to any security issues, no matter the developer/vendor. Now, EU can help in the following ways: - Providing funding to groups which organize to develop open source software together - This already happens and has a strong impact, yet there is room for improvement - Providing a platform to aggregate or advertise at EU-level the need of developers in specific fields - Creating platforms for professional exchange between groups and individuals - Providing development groups with assistance in creating a financial plan to become self-sustainable - Maintaining recommendations of financial strategies - Encouraging developers behind critical software which isn't open source to publish their work - Requiring organizations behind critical yet private code to publish their work before ceasing to exist or stopping development

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: InfluenceMap welcomes the stated objectives of this initiative, but we are concerned by the direction of travel with regards the application of the Better Regulation framework, which already appears to have opened the door to outright policy capture in the EU. The European Ombudsmans finding of maladministration in relation to Omnibus I should be taken as a warning: without protecting transparency and accountability, trust in the EU policymaking process will suffer. Already, polling shows less than half of Europeans (48%) tend to trust the EU (a decline of 4 points since Spring 2025). See InfluenceMap's full response attached.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open-source software underpins the vast majority of the European technological sector, but its impact reaches beyond that. Software has "eaten the world" and now exists in every sector, from agriculture to retail. As a senior software engineer at Ingka, the largest IKEA franchisee, I see the impact first hand: relying on open-source technologies has directly enabled us to hire co-workers that speak the same technical language regardless of their background, to work faster, to deliver better experiences for customers and employees across the world, and all that a lower cost. Thousands of Europeans have jobs at Ingka thanks to, in part, open source software. Likewise, as an occasional contributor to open-source software, I continue to be amazed at the impact even a small contribution can make. A couple of hours of work on, for example, implementing a device driver, can result in tens of thousands of people being able to use products they were previously unable to. Other contributions can result in rescuing perfectly usable hardware, abandoned or unilaterally declared as "obsolete" by the manufacturer, thus significantly reducing e-waste and saving European citizens money. Open source work is driven by volunteers and enthusiasts, often in their spare time. Their work directly benefits everyone, from regular citizens to international corporations. But, they rarely get compensated. They are under pressure to build more, patch issues, resolve support requests. They are often passionate developers, but lack the skills and resources to promote their projects, conduct user research, build intuitive and attractive user interfaces, and achieve mass market adoption. As it stands, there are very few incentives for open source users to contribute back - either as individuals or through various organizations. Financial contributions (sponsorships, donations) require navigating the complexity of tax, labor and other regulations. For example, if I wish to pay someone to develop a feature for an open-source project, is this a labor agreement, a purchase, or something else? Do I have to pay VAT? Does the developer have to report this income, and how? Even if this was made clear, there would still not be any reason for a company to contribute. This is why companies using open-source components in their systems should be incentivized to do so, either financially or by hiring individuals to work on a project. This could take the form of tax credits for certain contributions. And there should be an additional incentive for companies to open-source their products under a copyleft or permissive license, either in form of a further tax benefit or a subsidy. We have plenty of foundational software; we need real products, built for non-technical users, that will be better in quality from their proprietary counterparts. We cannot expect the vast majority of users to use software that's worse or does not fit their use case simply out of ideological reasons. But through funding and protections for open-source software, we can build a future that is better than the one tech mega-corporations have in stock for us. I hope we get there.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: FESI welcomes the European Commissions initiative to modernise and strengthen the Better Regulation framework and thanks the Commission for the opportunity to contribute. As long-standing participant in the EU decision-making process, FESI supports open, transparent and evidence-based policymaking, grounded in meaningful stakeholder engagement and legal certainty. The Better Regulation framework has historically been a core asset of EU policymaking, ensuring transparency, predictability and proportionality while supporting ambitious policy objectives. However, recent legislative experience shows that shortcomings in its application risk undermining EU competitiveness, investment confidence and the credibility of the regulatory process. During the 20192024 mandate, several major initiatives progressed hectically under very tight timelines and with insufficient impact assessments, in some cases despite negative opinions from the Regulatory Scrutiny Board (e.g. CSDDD). This has weakened the evidence base of legislation and contributed to a growing number of unwanted post-adoption revisions and omnibus initiatives. While simplification is welcome in principle, repeated and premature changes to legislation have created regulatory uncertainty, delaying or withholding investment decisions in Europe. From FESIs perspective, better regulation should not mean deregulation. It should mean thoughtful, outcome-driven regulation: legislation designed around its measurable real-world impact and systematically balanced against the concrete administrative and economic costs imposed on companies. Impact assessments should therefore place greater emphasis on mandatory, outcome-based costbenefit analysis and on prioritising initiatives that can demonstrably deliver meaningful benefits while minimizing the burdens required for compliance. Some Commissions own analyses, including work by the Joint Research Centre, show that some measures under consideration may deliver only limited real-world gains despite significant compliance efforts. Such assessments can greatly help the policymaker to discard ineffective potential measures. This approach should become a standard foundation of policymaking. FESI also wishes to highlight the importance of realistic consultation timelines. Stakeholders are given only 4 weeks to comment on highly complex proposals spanning hundreds or thousands of pages. This challenge is often compounded by the publication of major consultations during holiday periods, when internal coordination and expert availability are significantly reduced. Such timelines do not support high-quality, evidence-based feedback and risk leading to implementation problems and subsequent corrective legislation. Allowing adequate time at an early stage would improve legislative quality and legal certainty. Shortened timelines should remain the exception and be clearly justified. Better Regulation would also benefit from clearer communication of policy objectives and expected outcomes. Stakeholders should be informed from the outset about the concrete problems addressed, the benefits expected for citizens, companies and the environment, and how success will be measured in practice. In addition, to allow for effective compliance planning, fully developed implementation guidelines should be published well in advance of application, following transparent stakeholder engagement and allowing sufficient time for clarification and, where necessary, the publication of FAQs. Finally, FESI stresses the need to improve the accessibility and readability of EU legislation. Highly complex and fragmented legal texts hinder understanding and effective implementation, particularly for non-legal audiences. The Commission should develop more interactive tools to navigate legislation and systematically publish consolidated versions of amended legislation. Improving accessibility is essential for transparency, compliance and trust in the process.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Red Hat*, the worlds leading provider of open source solutions, uses a community-driven model to deliver high-performing Linux, cloud, automation, container, and Kubernetes technologies, as well as open source AI frameworks that power the full machine learning lifecycle. Though a wholly owned subsidiary of IBM since 2019, Red Hat operates as a distinct legal entity, maintaining neutrality to serve as a catalyst for open innovation across the global hybrid cloud. Our "upstream-first"** development model ensures that we do not merely consume open source code but actively harden, secure, and contribute back to the projects from around the world that power the modern digital economy. Instead of "forking" code into proprietary silos - which creates technical debt and incompatibility - we submit every bug fix, security patch, and feature enhancement to the community repository first. This model represents one of the largest R&D budgets in industry, as a percentage of revenue, and is widely recognised as key to sustain thriving communities as well as optimising a secure, stable and standardised public good for all. To complement various commercialisation/monetisation models, public intervention can sometimes be helpful for projects which have not attracted the same level of commercial support and thus need public and/or steward support for underfunded or potentially fringe projects. This EU call for evidence is a timely initiative to level-set about the success of open source and the importance of understanding core drivers which underpin this success as well as its critical role to realise many EU policy objectives such as European competitiveness and digital sovereignty. Doing so in a comprehensive way will also better position Europe to co-develop and unlock the next wave in open source innovation and break-through emerging technologies. Given our 30+ year role of developing open source software and defending open in the public policy field, we welcome this important EU consultation and would like to share some preliminary perspectives - attached - as a basis for further dialogue and collaboration. *https://transparency-register.europa.eu/search-register-or-update/organisation-detail_en?id=741186216712-07 **https://www.redhat.com/en/resources/open-source-participation-guidelines-overview#section-5

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: IFUT, representing researchers and academics across the Irish Higher education sector, welcomes the plan to put the ERA objectives into EU law. The ERA should make the free movement of researchers, knowledge and technology real, and it should keep researchers safe. The Act shall protect research as a public good. Policy must serve the public interest, support democratic governance, and ensure fair working conditions for future and present researchers and academics. Public investment needs to be reliable and long term. The European Semester shall push Member States towards the 3% R&D target, with transparent decision making with relevant stakeholders, including the trade unions, and open reporting. Funding must also protect and promote independent and curiosity-driven research within all scientific disciplines. Long-term institutional funding should be shielded from short-term political and commercial pressure. We support a more ambitious benchmark for funding and call for at least 4% of GDP for publicly and private funded research. Research may support innovation and the labour market, but it must not be led by narrowly defined market needs. Better coordination between the EU and the Member States can reduce fragmentation only if there are safeguards. The Act should link EU research funding to social conditionalities, including respecting academic-freedom, quality job of the staff, trade union rights, and collective agreements. Governance and evaluation should be transparent, with strong regulation on conflicts of interest and on effective social dialogue with trade unions. Access to infrastructures should be fair across regions and disciplines to avoid widening gaps of funding. Fundamental values must be guaranteed in practice. The Act should set out clear protections for academic freedom, ethics and integrity, gender equality and equal opportunities, and provide clear standards. Protections should cover research, teaching, research dissemination, participation in governance and institutional autonomy, and should shield staff from harassment, censorship and interference. Openness should help, not hinder, research: open access to publicly funded results and proportionate, rights-respecting rules for data and AI are essential for trust and collaboration. Research careers must be attractive and secure if Europe wants to keep and attract talents. Open-ended contracts should be the norm for core research and teaching ensuring fair pay, social protection and manageable workload. Assessment and promotion should recognise teaching, supervision, teamwork, open practices and work with society, not only narrow metrics. Early-career and female researchers need targeted support and leadership opportunities. Mobility must come with rights: faster recognition of qualifications, portability of pensions and benefits, and predictable residence and long-stay visa procedures for researchers and their families. Good governance depends on social dialogue. Any EU work on researchers careers must respect national collective bargaining and social dialogue. Researchers and academics trade unions should have a formal role in the ERA Forum, ERAC and the boards of infrastructures and partnerships. Involving unions in the design, implementation and monitoring of research policies and investment having impact on research and academic jobs is the best way to end structural precarity and to build fair, attractive and transparent career paths. In short, the ERA Act should bind Member States to ambitious public investment in independent science; align policies without handing the research agenda to market interests; protect academic freedom and ethics with enforceable rules; provide secure jobs for the staff, fair assessment and mobility with rights; ensure equitable infrastructures across regions and disciplines; and embed social dialogue with the unions that represent Europes researchers.

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: European standards (ENs) play a crucial role in promoting economic integration and facilitate cross-border trade. By establishing standards for emerging technologies, ENs enable innovation and encourage the adoption of environmentally friendly practices. When ENs gain international recognition, they help bolster the EU's strategic position as a leader in regulatory frameworks. Reviewing the European standardization system, its development processes in terms of acceleration and accessibility should however preserve the fundamental principles, including the national delegation principle. As CEN and CENELEC develop solutions to speed up standards development, the EC should implement measures to improve the speed of citation. Important elements to consider during the revision of Regulation 1025: 1. Using alternatives within the existing functional system other deliverables (TS, TR, CWA) that CEN, CENELEC & ETSI may offer could fulfil the needs of a variety of stakeholders and provide timely compliance. 2. European impact in international standardization. European industry strongly benefits from international alignment in standardization, allowing for global market access and reduced costs as products do not have to be manufactured differently for different markets. Via their participation in ISO and IEC, the National Members of CEN and CENELEC ensure that European expertise has a strong impact internationally. 3. European standardization is the key component of the European quality infrastructure. ESOs not only provide the standards against which products are assessed, but also the standards for the architecture of the quality system itself. Without these standards, the European and national quality infrastructures could not perform their activities. Standards should be continued to be recognised as an essential element of the European quality infrastructure. 4. Need for a coherent European standardization system. Use of standards from sources other than the ESOs should be subject to the same requirements as standards developed by the ESOs. The use of common specifications and standards from outside the ESS risks a proliferation of competing technical requirements on the European market and a less coherent ESS. 5. Sustainable European Standardization system. Free readability access to harmonized European standards must be carefully managed to preserve the integrity and sustainability of the ESS, in particular the financial sustainability of NSBs. Securing existing IP rights is essential to protect the financial sustainability of the standardization system. Copyright enables NSBs, many of which are not-for-profit, to develop value-added products and services that facilitate implementation, generate revenue streams, and support long-term sustainability. Appropriate and proportionate remuneration should accompany free readability access to standards. 6. Preserve the essential values of standardization. Faster processes must not come at the expense of quality, inclusiveness, trust or weakening of the national consensus model. NSBs act as crucial links between national stakeholders and the European level as they channel input from the national level to the development of European standards. 7. New generations of standardization experts. NSBs have cultivated a strong network of technical experts from industry, SMEs, public administrations, academia and societal organizations. However, like in many sectors, securing expertise is a major challenge. The revised Regulation should acknowledge the importance of integrating standardization into vocational education and university curricula to form and recruit future experts.

By establishing standards for emerging technologies, ENs enable innovation and encourage the adoption of environmentally friendly practices. When ENs gain international recognition, they help bolster the EU's strategic position as a leader in regulatory frameworks. Reviewing the European standardization system, its development processes in terms of acceleration and accessibility should however preserve the fundamental principles, including the national delegation principle. As CEN and CENELEC develop solutions to speed up standards development, the EC should implement measures to improve the speed of citation. Important elements to consider during the revision of Regulation 1025: 1. Using alternatives within the existing functional system other deliverables (TS, TR, CWA) that CEN, CENELEC & ETSI may offer could fulfil the needs of a variety of stakeholders and provide timely compliance. 2. European impact in international standardization. European industry strongly benefits from international alignment in standardization, allowing for global market access and reduced costs as products do not have to be manufactured differently for different markets. Via their participation in ISO and IEC, the National Members of CEN and CENELEC ensure that European expertise has a strong impact internationally. 3. European standardization is the key component of the European quality infrastructure. ESOs not only provide the standards against which products are assessed, but also the standards for the architecture of the quality system itself. Without these standards, the European and national quality infrastructures could not perform their activities. Standards should be continued to be recognised as an essential element of the European quality infrastructure. 4. Need for a coherent European standardization system. Use of standards from sources other than the ESOs should be subject to the same requirements as standards developed by the ESOs. The use of common specifications and standards from outside the ESS risks a proliferation of competing technical requirements on the European market and a less coherent ESS. 5. Sustainable European Standardization system. Free readability access to harmonized European standards must be carefully managed to preserve the integrity and sustainability of the ESS, in particular the financial sustainability of NSBs. Securing existing IP rights is essential to protect the financial sustainability of the standardization system. Copyright enables NSBs, many of which are not-for-profit, to develop value-added products and services that facilitate implementation, generate revenue streams, and support long-term sustainability. Appropriate and proportionate remuneration should accompany free readability access to standards. 6. Preserve the essential values of standardization. Faster processes must not come at the expense of quality, inclusiveness, trust or weakening of the national consensus model. NSBs act as crucial links between national stakeholders and the European level as they channel input from the national level to the development of European standards. 7. New generations of standardization experts. NSBs have cultivated a strong network of technical experts from industry, SMEs, public administrations, academia and societal organizations. However, like in many sectors, securing expertise is a major challenge. The revised Regulation should acknowledge the importance of integrating standardization into vocational education and university curricula to form and recruit future experts.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: When it comes to the rights of persons with disabilities the EU faces significant problems: The segregation of disabled people has increased. According to Eurofound, the number of disabled people confined to institutions has increased by 29% in the last decade. Disabled people are excluded from work. Between 2010 and 2023 the disability employment gap has increased from 18,6% to 24,5%. Disabled people experience significantly higher poverty rates and are much more likely to be homeless. According to Eurostat, in 2024 28,8% were at risk of poverty, compared to 17.9% of non disabled people. According to the OECD, in some member states a quarter of the homeless population report to have physical impairments and up to 60% to have a psychosocial impairment. We call on the European Union to use its competences to the fullest to improve the situation because: The EU is a state party to the UN CRPD and obliged to implement it faithfully. The exclusion and discrimination against disabled people is causing immense economic costs to society. According to the OECD, some member states are spending up to 4,4% of GDP on long-term care with more than 80% going into nursing homes. Disability exclusion is producing 0.84-1.42 billion in annual GDP losses and 302-493 million in lost tax revenue EU-wide. We as ENIL have prepared detailed recommendations to address the enormous problems we are facing. Our recommendations are linked to the Concluding Observations of the UN Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD-Committee) to the Combined Second and Third Periodic report of the European Union. Improve implementation and monitoring of the UN CRPD In its Concluding Observations the CRPD-Committee criticised the legal and institutional arrangements for the implementation of the UN CRPD. We call on the EU to: Ratify the Optional Protocol to the Convention. Reform the current CRPD monitoring framework at the EU-level. We need a framework that is independent, well structured and adequately funded. Issue guidance highlighting the crucial roles of disabled peoples organisations in EU policy making and the need to provide financing of operational costs. 2. Use EU Funds to support Independent Living In its Concluding Observations the CRPD-Committee criticised the fact that the EU is spending large amounts of resources on institutions which under UN CRPD is not allowed. Under the new MFF and the National and Regional Partnership Plans Regulation political steering of the spending of EU Funds will be enhanced which creates an opportunity to direct member states to redirect funding from services that segregate into Independent Living Services. We call on the EU to: Ensure that the Country Specific Recommendations and the European Semester contain clear instructions for member states to substantially revise their use of EU Funds. Ensure there are milestones in place which set concrete targets for a reduction of spending on institutions or sheltered employment and an increase of spending for services such as personal assistance, personal budgets and supported employment. A formal procedure needs to be installed which ensures that the Disability Platform and especially the Disabled Peoples Organisations who are members receive a co-production role in drafting disability related recommendations of the Country Specific Recommendation, the European Semester and milestones and targets. 3. Use state aid to improve labour market inclusion EU State aid legislation is frequently used to support social objectives. For example the SGEI-Decision was reformed to support the Affordable Housing Plan. We call on the EU to use its competences in this area to boost the labour market inclusion of disabled people: Redirect subsidies from institutions and sheltered workshops and into personal assistance and supported employment. Reform the General Block Exemption Regulation to remove 2(f) from Article 34, thereby reducing the subsidisation

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: CLEPA, the European Association of Automotive Suppliers, welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the revision of the Public Procurement Directive. Public procurement is a powerful policy lever to support Europes industrial base, accelerate the green and digital transition, and strengthen the resilience of strategically important value chains, including the automotive ecosystem. 1. Prioritising European value creation through non-price criteria CLEPA strongly supports a more systematic and meaningful use of non-price criteria in public procurement procedures. Award decisions should not be based predominantly on the lowest price, but should give substantial weight to industrial, sustainability, and resilience dimension, including made-in-Europe criteria. European goods and services should be prioritised in public procurement processes, particularly for the automotive sector where the Europe has a strong industrial footprint and clear competitiveness and resilience interests. To be effective, such criteria should: Be mandatory for a defined set of strategic products, including vehicles Be clearly defined, objective and legally robust, Be aligned with existing EU legislation 2. Made-in-Europe criteria for vehicles in public procurement CLEPA believes that vehicles procured through public procurement, including under the Clean Vehicles Directive, should be subject to mandatory made-in-Europe criteria based on a clear and enforceable definition of European value creation. CLEPA encourages the Commission to apply the following criteria when defining a made-in-Europe vehicle: Final assemble takes place within European Union; European component content (excluding the battery) represents at least 75% of the ex-works price of total components; European critical component content, including the e-powertrain and electrical and electronic systems, meets progressive thresholds, starting at 50% in 2026 and rising to at least 70% in the medium term. Applying such criteria would better reflect the reality of automotive value creation in Europe and prevent situations where limited assembly operations are sufficient to qualify a vehicle as European. 3. Addressing unfair competition while remaining open to trusted partners CLEPA underlines that the objective of made-in-Europe criteria is to strengthen local production and ensure fair competition. Public procurement rules must prevent automotive industries benefiting from heavy and distortive foreign subsidies from gaining an unfair advantage in EU-funded tenders. In this context, CLEPA sees strong complementarity between made-in-Europe requirements in public procurement and the effective enforcement of the Foreign Subsidies Regulation. CLEPA data indicates that more than 100,000 job losses in automotive component manufacturing have been announced over the past two years. Targeted procurement measures are therefore needed to preserve critical industrial capacities Europe. Introducing made-in-Europe criteria in public procurement vehicles would deliver concrete tangible benefits for the EUs automotive ecosystem. At the same time, CLEPA stresses the importance of maintaining close cooperation with like-minded and trusted trade partners, reflecting the integrated and globally connected nature of automotive supply chains. Procurement rules should therefore remain compatible with the EUs international commitments, including WTO obligations, while safeguarding Europes strategic interests, and take into account applicable Government Procurement Agreement commitments, as appropriate. Conclusion The revision of the Public Procurement Directive is a timely opportunity to align procurement policy with Europes broader industrial and competitiveness objectives. By embedding clear and mandatory made-in-Europe criteria for vehicles, the EU can ensure that public spending actively supports innovation, resilience and high-quality industrial employment in Europe.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: This document presents feedback from DigitalTrade4.EU, a pan-European consortium of trade, logistics, and technology experts, to the European Commission. It advocates for aligning digital trade solutionsparticularly interoperable standards like MLETR and eIDAS 2.0with the EU's cybersecurity and defense initiatives. The proposal emphasizes decentralized systems and dual-use technologies to simultaneously strengthen civilian supply chains, military readiness, and cyber resilience, while reducing administrative burdens for businesses. Key recommendations include harmonizing digital frameworks, investing in secure infrastructure, and extending Digital Product Passports for defense applications. To read more, download the full document.
