initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open source software (OSS) is at the heart of the Internet and todays digital society: it underpins critical infrastructure, innovation, and the services that citizens and businesses rely on every day. I have been actively developing and contributing to OSS for more than 30 years, and I have seen firsthand how it enables collaboration, resilience, and European competitiveness. Over this time, I have also built several companies that together created jobs for around 300 peoplegrowth and impact that would simply not have been possible without the openness, shared foundations, and rapid innovation made possible by OSS.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The revision of the Public Procurement Directive offers the opportunity to streamline processes and modernise guidelines to enhance Europes position. We as Maritime & Offshore NL welcome this oppertunity. Hence, public procurement policies within a strong Single Market are not only a guideline for correct and uniform processes, but they can serve as a driver for strategic investments to fully utilise the potential of valuable technologies, products and services within Europe. The vision of Maritime & Offshore NL The Dutch trade association for (manufacturing companies in) the maritime and offshore sector, Maritime & Offshore NL, supports the goals of the Single Market Strategy, as it is crucial to balance competition with non-European countries. The European maritime and offshore sector has been under severe pressure for years, with a rapid loss of its market share to (especially) Asian countries. Europe must strengthen itself in order to consolidate, or rather increase, its market share and to prepare on capitalising future earning opportunities. National actors benefit from a collective approach from a European context and cooperation. The aim should be to create a global level playing field, to reduce current price gaps with countries outside the EU and to minimise dependencies on third countries (e.g. for energy and raw materials). A leading role of public buyers in establishing and organising lead markets for sustainable and innovative solutions is essential to remain economically and socially relevant as a sector. Procurement as a foundation for strategic industrial policy Maritime & Offshore NL sees the revision of the Public Procurement Directive as an important foundation for sector-specific strategies (such as the proposed European Maritime Industry Strategy) to promote fair competition and agility within the Single Market. Our priorities for the review focus on: establishing a European and global level playing field; emphasis on strategic interests in line with other national and EU policies; reduction of administrative burdens for businesses. In this context, Maritime & Offshore NL makes the following recommendations: 1. Include qualitative (non-price) criteria that focus on overarching strategic interests to be applied in procurement processes. The revised procurement rules provide a framework with a minimum standard for the application of a qualitative (non-price) selection mechanism with regard to strategic objectives, allowing sectors - within a European context - to select and apply sector-specific criteria themselves. 2. Increase transparency and harmonise procurement conditions to facilitate European cooperation. The revised procurement rules set a clear framework and limits the possibilities to apply (implicit) local preference requirements within tenders in order to ensure fair competition. 3. Reduce the administrative burden by introducing a uniform and accessible system for the admission of European vendors. The revised procurement rules are supported by more efficient use of digital means for sharing and storing data, reducing the administrative burden and providing a better overview on available capacity. 4. Provide a flexible legal framework that can be swiftly adapted to changing circumstances. The revised procurement rules provide a generic legal framework for procurement policies that focuses on economic security, autonomy and resilience, but leaves sufficient flexibility for strategic sectors, such as the maritime and offshore industry, to address unforeseen conditions or exploit opportunities when they arise. A detailed version of our position is attached as an appendix.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: (better formatting attached) With almost 14 years of professional experience in the open-source PC-firmware domain, I'd like to report on this often ignored but important topic. PC firmware, aka. BIOS/UEFI, is used in many devices from small network appliances over laptops to huge servers. Important about this firmware: 1. PC firmware is the most privileged software running on a vast amount of devices in our digital ecosystem, more privileged than our operating systems (OS). It can undermine any security mechanisms of our OS. 2. PC firmware operates as the trust anchor to verify digital signatures of our OSs (i.e. secure boot). It is the key element that ensures we actually run the untampered software that we intend to run. Most proprietary PC firmware is developed by a small oligopoly of three non-EU companies, AMI, Phoenix and Insyde, in co-operation with the x86 silicon vendors AMD and Intel. These silicon vendors also offer hardware root-of-trust technology. If used, the device manufacturers key is burned into the hardware. Then, not the device owner but the manufacturer controls what firmware the device can run. And if secure boot is enforced, what software can be run. Such technology is used by all top PC manufacturers. Most PCs and similar devices offer the option to use custom secure-boot keys. However, this can be changed remotely by the device manufacturer as long as the device downloads and installs firmware updates. We depend much on these updates, as vulnerabilities are found regularly. A hardware root-of-trust, effectively limits the possibility to fix vulnerabilities after the manufacturer stops their support. With it, it's even possible to lock a device to specific keys for good. Projects like coreboot.org show that open-source can provide secure alternatives. It ships with all current Google Chromebooks and also some high-security products for processing classified data. While providing such open-source firmware is possible for manufacturers, there are high obstacles for the open-source community to provide this for devices that shipped with proprietary firmware. The biggest obstacle is the lack of hardware documentation. PC firmware is generally divided into two parts: 1. hardware initialization, 2. the boot loader and security features. The latter can be developed and re-used for any device just like software. The former however, depends on chip-specific information from the silicon vendor, and how chips are connected on every device. This is mostly kept confidential without good reason. The hardware initialization is specific to each silicon vendor's chips. Their business is hardware and their part of the firmware does not add any business advantage. How chips are connected, usually documented by board schematics, can be derived from the product. This is easily affordable by anyone with a business interest, but unfeasible for open-source communities to do for every device. AMD has already started an open-source effort called openSIL that will open their part from 2027 on. As this should be the standard, I strongly suggest to make open-source hardware-initialization a hard requirement in all public tenders. And in the long run, part of legislation for every device sold in the EU. Board schematics should be part of any device's manual, as they once were. This too should be a requirement in all public tenders, and eventually to sell a device within the EU. This information would not only enable sovereign firmware development but could also be used for education. The EU should foster academic courses for PC firmware, as this is currently a topic with little EU expertise. The use of hardware root-of-trust technology should be forbidden, unless there is a clear process how the owner can take control of it to continue firmware updates. I'm happy to answer any questions on the topic. The opensourcefirmware.foundation can also be queried, and I can suggest contacts at the German BSI who are familiar with the topic.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Feedback on the EU ETS from the Project Carbon2Chem®  The project Carbon2Chem® welcomes the opportunity to give stakeholder feedback within the 2025 consultation on the EU ETS. Comments in a nutshell Clear and unambiguous definitions and usage of key terms in the EU ETS are essential. Introducing and defining an independent term for CO2 formation without CO2 release would facilitate the proper description of CCU and CCS in the context of EU ETS activities. Definitions should be harmonized throughout the EU ETS and preferably also with related legislation like the Renewable Energy Directive. All language versions of the EU ETS legislation should describe the same legal requirements. A temporary state of carbon as CO2 in a production process leading to a carbon-containing product should not be regarded as a fictitious CO2 emission from that product in the EU ETS, in order to create a level playing field with products containing virgin fossil carbon. The capture and storage of zero-rated CO2 would generate negative emissions which are not recognizable in the current EU ETS. For precisely this reason, the EU ETS currently does not provide a legal basis to require the capture of a larger quantity of CO2 than could be recognized and deducted in the EU ETS. A corresponding clarification in the MRR appears to be necessary. Clarity of language and terminology Clear language and unambiguous terminology are of paramount importance in any legal instrument. Missing terms for proper differentiation The term emissions, which refers to the release of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere according to the UNFCCC (1992), has been used in the EU ETS to refer also to the generation of the greenhouse gas CO2 without its subsequent release into the atmosphere. Within EU ETS legislation, the term carbon may refer to elementary carbon or to other carbon-containing material, even to CO2, sometimes with different meanings within a single phrase. A clear and unambiguous use of different terms for different objects would be desirable. Uniform requirements in all language versions of the legislation Identical legal provisions in all language versions of EU legislation are essential. e.g. Article 49a(1) of the MRR refers to CO2 originating from non-zero-rated carbon in most language versions of the MRR, whereas the German version of this paragraph relates to CO2 from zero-rated carbon. Harmonized definitions in related legislation Capturing CO2 will be a common feature of CCS and CCU activities. Article 2 of the Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/2620 covers captured CO2 as a feedstock for the manufacture of products. For this reason, it does not seem advisable to have a definition of CO2 capture in Article 3 of the MRR that only refers to activities aiming at permanent geological storage of CO2, which will not be compatible with CO2 capture in the broader sense. Removing fundamental barriers to the adequate consideration of CCU production pathways in the EU ETS The release of greenhouse gases from the use of products, insofar as this use is not an activity explicitly covered by the EU ETS, is in principle outside the regulatory scope of the EU ETS. This applies to all carbon-containing products. According to the general principles of the EU ETS, the production of carbon-containing products should not be subject to the pricing of GHG emissions that will be generated and released into the atmosphere only during the use of the products, unless that product use is an ETS activity by itself. Problematic specifications in the EU ETS regarding the handling of mixed C input (partly zero-rated) with a view to cost-effective and economically efficient GHG emissions reduction The current MRR specifies several requirements for ETS activities with carbon input that is partly zero-rated. Further information in the attached document.
text_raw: Feedback on the EU ETS from the Project Carbon2Chem® The project Carbon2Chem® welcomes the opportunity to give stakeholder feedback within the 2025 consultation on the EU ETS. Comments in a nutshell Clear and unambiguous definitions and usage of key terms in the EU ETS are essential. Introducing and defining an independent term for CO2 formation without CO2 release would facilitate the proper description of CCU and CCS in the context of EU ETS activities. Definitions should be harmonized throughout the EU ETS and preferably also with related legislation like the Renewable Energy Directive. All language versions of the EU ETS legislation should describe the same legal requirements. A temporary state of carbon as CO2 in a production process leading to a carbon-containing product should not be regarded as a fictitious CO2 emission from that product in the EU ETS, in order to create a level playing field with products containing virgin fossil carbon. The capture and storage of zero-rated CO2 would generate negative emissions which are not recognizable in the current EU ETS. For precisely this reason, the EU ETS currently does not provide a legal basis to require the capture of a larger quantity of CO2 than could be recognized and deducted in the EU ETS. A corresponding clarification in the MRR appears to be necessary. Clarity of language and terminology Clear language and unambiguous terminology are of paramount importance in any legal instrument. Missing terms for proper differentiation The term emissions, which refers to the release of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere according to the UNFCCC (1992), has been used in the EU ETS to refer also to the generation of the greenhouse gas CO2 without its subsequent release into the atmosphere. Within EU ETS legislation, the term carbon may refer to elementary carbon or to other carbon-containing material, even to CO2, sometimes with different meanings within a single phrase. A clear and unambiguous use of different terms for different objects would be desirable. Uniform requirements in all language versions of the legislation Identical legal provisions in all language versions of EU legislation are essential. e.g. Article 49a(1) of the MRR refers to CO2 originating from non-zero-rated carbon in most language versions of the MRR, whereas the German version of this paragraph relates to CO2 from zero-rated carbon. initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: It was high time that legislators also recognised the need to increase the share of open source software. Such a measure can be easily implemented in the area of public administration. Municipalities, schools, boarding schools, hospitals, armed forces, charities and churches are all areas where the use of commercial software would be unnecessary or at least limited. Today, the software and network needs of these areas can be fully covered by free software offering more cost-effective, independent, faster and safer solutions than proprietary products. The current situation benefits only large US corporations. Profits are realised there and these companies often use their market power to influence decision-making in their favour. Moreover, their data protection standards are often not as strict as they would be expected to be in Europe. I, personally, have for many years been exclusively using the Hungarian operating system and blackPantherOS software solutions, which provide a stable working environment, a carefully curated selection of applications, high security and comprehensive solutions to all challenges, be it in the area of publication design, noteset processing, document management or cloud synchronisation. However, it is particularly worrying and, in my view, unlawful that certain undertakings or statutory provisions de facto compel users to use Microsoft products. As a result, I have to compromise in some cases, for example, I am unable to use services such as TelekomTVGo or SkyShowTime. The providers concerned have shown no interest in this issue and are deliberately hindering the use of alternative solutions. The European Union should take decisive action against such practices through appropriate legislative measures.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: CERMI stresses that the EU must consolidate itself as a social Europe, where the inclusion of the 101 million persons with disabilities is not treated as a sectoral policy but as a fundamental pillar for prosperity and democracy. The Strategy must be unequivocally grounded in the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD) and must follow the observations issued by the UN Committee. To ensure effective implementation, CERMI calls for a structured civil dialogue under the principle nothing about persons with disabilities without us, including the establishment of a focal point in all EU institutions (including the European Parliament and the Council) and an independent monitoring mechanism with its own dedicated budget. The document identifies accessibility as a key enabling right for participation. It proposes the creation of a European Agency for Accessibility to oversee technical compliance, along with binding legislation to eliminate the current voluntary approach to accessibility in the built environment. In the field of transport, CERMI calls for the revision of Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 to explicitly prohibit denied boarding in air transport and ensure full liability for damage to mobility equipment, as well as for legislation on mutual recognition of assistance dogs, supported by a European database. CERMI also requests that the forthcoming regulation on digital mobility ensure full accessibility of single ticketing platforms. In the internal market, it calls for a review of the European Standardisation System to balance industry influence and for broadening copyright exceptions to cover audiovisual works and museum materials. CERMI advocates for a comprehensive Non-Discrimination Directive extending protection beyond employment and recognising the refusal of reasonable accommodation as discrimination. The text calls for ensuring accessible and secret voting, removing any restrictions based on legal capacity, and applying an intersectional approach addressing priority groups such as children, older persons and refugees. A specific section is dedicated to women with disabilities, demanding coordination between disability and gender strategies and the collection of disaggregated data to expose multiple discrimination. CERMI also calls for criminalising non consensual sterilisation and conducting a European macro survey on violence against these women. To make free movement effective, CERMI proposes expanding the European Disability Card so that it covers long-term relocation and ensures portability of disability and social security benefits. It also calls for a European compensation mechanism to balance the costs of personal assistance across Member States and for guarantees of continuity of support during transitions of residence. In the field of employment, the document demands an Employment Guarantee, dedicated ESF+ funding to combat poverty and a quota system for hiring persons with disabilities within EU institutions, including reforms to EPSO selection processes. The document is explicit in demanding a European Deinstitutionalisation Strategy that prohibits the use of EU funds for building or renovating segregated institutions. It calls for a binding Council Decision requiring Member States to set timelines for the transition to community living, supported by annual Eurostat data to monitor progress. This is complemented by a call for a European Accessible Housing Plan. CERMI demands ethical AI that does not discriminate in employment processes and insists that accessibility be a mandatory condition for operating in the Single Market. In relation to the European Green Deal, it calls for a just transition protecting persons with disabilities from energy poverty and for the recognition of a disability constituency in international climate negotiations. Finally, in external action, CERMI requests that the accession of new countries be conditioned upon full compliance with the CRPD

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Waste-to-energy (Wte) is a key component of modern circular economy systems. It ensures the safe, hygienic and environmentally sound management of non-recyclable waste, while at the same time contributing to the supply of energy in the use of waste heat, in particular through the provision of electricity and district heating. It is a reliable, locally available energy source that contributes to regional energy independence. Unlike weather-dependent renewable energies, it is available throughout the year and in line with needs, even in the absence of wind and sun. In doing so, it strengthens not only security of supply, but also resilience to geopolitical risks and energy import dependencies. In Munich, for example, the Wte systems supply the heat for about 20 % of the municipal heat network. The discussion on the possible inclusion of Wte installations in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) is too short. Emissions trading is not an appropriate tool for managing this sector. Such a measure would not only fail to achieve the environmental objective, but would also entail significant risks for waste management, social fairness and European competitiveness. The inclusion of thermal waste treatment in the EU ETS is not environmentally appropriate. It would create disincentives, exacerbate social inequalities and weaken the circular economy. Instead, a cross-sectoral, technology-open and socially balanced approach is needed, recognising the role of Wte as part of the solution. Emissions trading must operate where it cannot develop its strengths where it is structurally ineffective.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: VNG sees open-source software as a strategic enabler of digital autonomy, interoperability, and public values for local governments across Europe. Municipalities are committed to reducing vendor-lockins, enabling flexibility and reducing costs by facilitating re-use. This is in part organised via Common Ground. Common Ground is an information management vision and architecture paradigm that transforms municipal information provision from a closed, application-oriented landscape into an open, data-driven, and component based flexible system. From the viewpoint of Dutch municipalities, several EUlevel measures would strengthen the EU opensource ecosystem, reinforce technological sovereignty, and improve cybersecurity across local governments. Municipalities rely on interoperable, secure, and sustainable digital public infrastructure. The EU can play a crucial role in enabling this by: (1) Establishing long term, structural EU funding for open digital infrastructure and ensure municipalities can directly benefit from such funding streams; (2) Supporting crossborder municipal collaboration through shared opensource components and digital commons (for example via EDICs and NGI initiatives); (3) Invest in European led auditing and certification of open source software used in public administration; (4) Create an EUwide procurement and compliance framework that favors open source; (5) Strengthen the European open source skills and community ecosystem; (6) Proactively research the risk AI poses to the open-source sector and act accordingly in order to protect the use of open-source technologies within the public sector; (7) Contribute to researching different governance and management forms such a steward ownership or the beehive model, for the public sector. Please see the attached file with our answers to questions posed by the European Commission in this Call for Evidence.

feedback_text: 1. We focus on technologies that are essential for the further digitalisation of the European economy. 2. We are establishing standards that are not just open-minded, but completely free from third-party rights and claims. 3. We pool our resources only on OSSs that are supported by independent foundations. 4. We produce on the basis of 2nd and 3 rd Software catalogues/stacks required for use in tax-funded projects. 5. We validate these software stacks periodically and ensure a long life cycle. 6. We knowingly accept that there will always be better solutions on the market.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Yara welcomes the initiative to update and simplify the EU Organic Framework. Yara would like to bring the following points to the attention: 1) addition of definitions for certain key terms, 2) introduction of simplified authorisation procedure for inputs based on EGTOP recommendations and stakeholder submissions, 3) preparation for the inclusion of microbial inoculants and plant bio-stimulants, and 4) expansion of the scope of circular materials as alternatives to fossil/mined materials. The details about those points can be found in the attached paper.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am going to address questions 2 and 3, as outlined in the "C. - Consultation strategy" section. 2. Open-source software provides many benefits over proprietary software, especially in the public sector. Given the open nature of the software, it is often interoperable with other competing products, reducing lock-in. This goes hand-in-hand with another important pillar open-source software brings to the table: digital sovereignty. In proprietary software, the customers (governments, companies, individuals) often do not have a choice about where their data is stored, and even if they have, the data is usually still handled by whoever is providing the software. If they handle your data incorrectly (e.g., sharing it with a foreign government), you have a choice. By contrast, in open-source software, customers are usually free to decide where and how software is hosted, ensuring their data is safe from external actors. Additionally, given that anyone can inspect and analyse the source code of open-source software, security researchers from all over Europe and the world can discover, report and fix security vulnerabilities that could otherwise go unnoticed in proprietary software. While this is not a silver bullet, and there have certainly been cases of open-source software being vulnerable (see OpenSSL Heartbleed, XZ compromise, and the recent React2Shell vulnerability), it can be argued that security vulnerabilities are handled in a much more transparent way in open-source projects, and those that have been found could have remained undetected if it were not for independent security researchers. Finally, while cost savings are also a factor to consider, it is far from the most important. Given one of the main struggles of open-source software is funding, the savings from switching to open-source software should be invested back into funding and donations to the projects being used. 3. As mentioned, the main problem with open-source is the lack of funding, which means there are not many developers that can focus on those projects outside of their free time. If those developers could work on open-source as part of their job, the open-source software offerings would substantially improve quality, the lack of which has in some cases been a major barrier to adoption. As a concrete measure, the EU should fund open-source projects that are important to its sovereignty and create incentives for the private sector to do the same.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: Reconnaître la neurodiversité chez les individus et ne plus stigmatiser les personnes par un handicap, troubles ou déficits mais de valoriser la différence qui doit jouer un rôle essentiel dans nos sociétés, autant que la biodiversité. Créer une école Européenne accueillant chaque enfant dans le respect de son développement personnel révélant ses propres talents et lui permettant de construire la société de demain dans la collaboration et non la compétition pour répondre aux défis qui nous attendent. En effet, les dispositifs mis en place en France ne respectent pas suffisamment la personne dans son auto détermination, et sa réelle inclusion dans la société car ils sappuient sur les « troubles » ou « déficits » à corriger au lieu de développer les « talents » et les capacités hors normes de la personne. Trop de personnes sont laissées pour compte au fil du parcours scolaire et de leur intégration sociale. Souvent rejetées à cause de leurs différences, elles finissent exclues et isolées à leur domicile sans activité ou dans des lieux daccueil qui sont aujourdhui la seule solution que lon ait pu créer. La force de lUnion Européenne réside dans la richesse de ses cultures différentes unies pour des valeurs communes dont lhumanité est une priorité. Cest grâce à lUE que nous pouvons rassembler les forces de tous nos individus sans discrimination, sans hiérarchie, dans la liberté dêtre soi-même et de vivre heureux dans une société qui est la nôtre, à tous! La puissance de lunion permettra de développer les techniques dapprentissage adaptées, de proposer une pédagogie actualisée, de considérer lensemble des possibilités de parcours de développement pour que chacun trouve sa place dans la société. Les enjeux européens seront pris en considération et les futures générations dactifs pourront apporter les bonnes réponses. Si chacun est considéré à sa juste valeur et construit son parcours personnel avec une éducation adaptée, ouvrant toutes les possibilités, alors les citoyens européens nauront plus besoin dautant daides pour compenser ce manque ni de soins psychiatriques pour soulager les souffrances et nous pourrons trouver une harmonie et une vraie égalité des chances pour tous.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The Shipyards & Maritime Equipment Association of Europe (SEA Europe) represents the maritime technology industry across 17 European countries. This includes the full spectrum of shipbuilding and maritime equipment manufacturingranging from the design, construction, maintenance, repair, retrofit, and conversion of all types of commercial and naval vessels, to the entire supply chain of maritime systems, equipment, materials, and services. Recognising the critical connection between the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) and the Innovation Fund in strengthening the competitiveness of Europes maritime manufacturing sector, SEA Europe is preparing a single, consolidated response to both ongoing calls for evidence. This comprehensive input is currently being finalised and will be submitted to the European Commission without delay. SEA Europes contribution is fully aligned with its broader vision for a future Industrial Maritime Strategyone that promotes a competitive, sustainable, and resilient European maritime industry. The following key recommendations can be noted: A. Evaluate maintaining EU ETS for a competitive, sustainable and efficient European waterborne transport and ensure coherence between the IMO Net Zero Framework and the FuelEU Maritime Regulation. B. Extend EU ETS to cover emissions from smaller ships (i.e. between 400 GT and 5.000 GT). C. Enhance Innovation Fund accessibility by introducing deployment flexibility for the projects concerning investments on ships. D. Differentiate maritime projects involving building/retrofitting of ships and projects involving alternative fuel production and (port) infrastructure within Innovation Fund. E. Flow back the revenues from the inclusion of shipping into EU ETS to the maritime sector.
text_raw: The Shipyards & Maritime Equipment Association of Europe (SEA Europe) represents the maritime technology industry across 17 European countries. This includes the full spectrum of shipbuilding and maritime equipment manufacturingranging from the design, construction, maintenance, repair, retrofit, and conversion of all types of commercial and naval vessels, to the entire supply chain of maritime systems, equipment, materials, and services. Recognising the critical connection between the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) and the Innovation Fund in strengthening the competitiveness of Europes maritime manufacturing sector, SEA Europe is preparing a single, consolidated response to both ongoing calls for evidence. This comprehensive input is currently being finalised and will be submitted to the European Commission without delay. SEA Europes contribution is fully aligned with its broader vision for a future Industrial Maritime Strategyone that promotes a competitive, sustainable, and resilient European maritime industry. The following key recommendations can be noted: A. Evaluate maintaining EU ETS for a competitive, sustainable and efficient European waterborne transport and ensure coherence between the IMO Net Zero Framework and the FuelEU Maritime Regulation. B. Extend EU ETS to cover emissions from smaller ships (i.e. between 400 GT and 5.000 GT). C. Enhance Innovation Fund accessibility by introducing deployment flexibility for the projects concerning investments on ships. D. Differentiate maritime projects involving building/retrofitting of ships and projects involving alternative fuel production and (port) infrastructure within Innovation Fund. E. Flow back the revenues from the inclusion of shipping into EU ETS to the maritime sector.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: In the current context of European higher education, the shift to Open Source solutions addresses several policy, economic and regulatory challenges. First of all, lopen Source enables institutions to strengthen their independence by avoiding excessive subjection to suppliers, often from outside Europe, while retaining control over digital tools and strategic choices. This autonomy also facilitates future developments and limits the risks associated with commercial or technological policy changes imposed by third parties. On the other hand, in financial terms, Open Source solutions offer better cost control. The lack of proprietary licences makes it possible to redirect budgets towards more useful expenditure, such as supporting users, maintaining or improving skills and tools. This approach is particularly adapted to the budgetary constraints of public institutions and promotes more stable long-term financial planning. As regards data protection, lopen Source also contributes to better compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Institutions have increased visibility on how data is processed and can choose solutions hosted in Europe, reducing legal risks and increasing the trust of users, be they students, teachers or administrative staff. With regard to digital security, Open Source solutions facilitate compliance with the requirements of the NIS2 Directive. The same solutions also allow for a better understanding of the systems used and greater responsiveness in the event of an incident, as institutions do not rely exclusively on a supplier to correct or develop their tools. This transparency enhances the overall resilience of digital infrastructure. Lopen Source also promotes cooperation between higher education institutions. They can pool their efforts, share developments and jointly build solutions tailored to their common needs. This collective dynamic strengthens the European academic ecosystem. Finally, the use of lopen Source is consistent with the core values of higher education, such as knowledge sharing, openness, ethics and social responsibility. It is fully in line with the European objectives of digital sovereignty and sustainable development.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The ERIC Forum brings together 30 European Research Infrastructure Consortia (ERICs) to strengthen coordination and enhance collaboration across Europe. ERICs are established under a dedicated EU legal framework and are recognised as strategic assets of the ERA. Operating across borders and legal systems, they encounter many of the challenges the ERA Act seeks to address, making them both affected and well placed to contribute solutions for strengthening research careers and securing Europes talent base. We propose that the new EU legal instrument include: (1) A standardisation of employment rules and contractual regimes across the ERA; (2) Adoption of a Europe-wide harmonised framework of job titles and job descriptions, which would enhance the mobility between states and also between sectors (Research and Academia, the commercial, and the productive sector); (3) Measures to promote greater uniformity in the level of social security protection for employees moving between countries, supporting the concept that employees who work in research and innovation are citizens of Europe and not single member states. Supporting arguments: ERICS transcend national boundaries, pool resources, and deliver scientific, technological, and societal impact. It is the highly skilled professionals who operate, maintain, develop, and enable open access to these infrastructures that transform them into engines of discovery, innovation, and competitiveness, empowering Europe as the innovation leader in the Global landscape. More than two decades of efforts in promoting research professionals mobility and improvement in employment conditions have not removed significant obstacles: (1) Portability of employment across Europe is hindered by divergent social security and pension systems; (2) A Wide variety of employment rules and contractual regimes across Member States; (3) Recognition of qualifications remains inconsistent across Member States. Given the lack of full EU legislative competence, efforts to harmonise certain labour law elements, for example, through a 28th legal regime, would be welcome. These issues are further explained in the attached document.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: KickCancer is a Belgian foundation with an international scope. Its non-profit mission is to support scientific, clinical, and fundamental research in paediatric oncology, and more broadly to foster all activities aimed at curing childhood cancers and improving the medical follow-up of treatments, both during and after recovery. We actively finance research projects to ensure that children with cancer gain access to the most innovative and effective treatments, while also reducing the long-term toxic effects of current therapies. Paediatric cancer is the leading cause of death by disease in Europe for children over one year of age, and two-thirds of survivors suffer long-term side effects from toxic treatments. Science and innovation hold the promise of safer, more effective therapies. KickCancer welcomes this Call for Evidence, as a strong European Research Area (ERA) is essential to deliver breakthroughs in paediatric oncology. 1) The need for cross-border collaboration Multi-country clinical trials (MCCTs) are crucial to accelerate drug discovery and development. Cross-border data sharing, scientific collaboration, and structural funding must underpin high-quality paediatric oncology trials. Harmonised digital tools - such as AI, the European Health Data Space (EHDS), Real World Evidence (RWE) platforms, and consistent data standards - will strengthen MCCTs and speed up drug development. 2) Europes potential in paediatric oncology KickCancer underlines the competitive advantage of a strong EU Single Market R&I ecosystem. Europe can become a global leader in patient-centred innovation for children with cancer, attracting investment and talent. Yet paediatric oncology remains marginal within the health R&I landscape, with persistent barriers slowing drug development. 3) The role of academia Because paediatric cancers are rare, commercial research offers little return on investment. Academic centres are essential to bridge this market failure. Global leadership in this field requires collective efforts to: - Support fundamental and translational academic research for first-in-child treatments. - Build trial-ready infrastructures to accelerate access to innovation. - Create alternative business models for ultra-rare diseases (e.g. joint procurement contracts). Such investments would speed access to effective medicines with reduced toxicity, improving survivors long-term health. 4) Advanced therapies Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (ATMPs), such as CAR-T and omics-based therapies, hold great promise in paediatric oncology. Academic centres increasingly drive their development. Explicit recognition of academias role alongside industry in the ERA Act would reinforce the EUs commitment to supporting all actors in the R&I ecosystem and accelerate childrens access to cutting-edge therapies. 5) Investment and sustainability Ensuring sustainable access to child-centred therapies requires an investment-friendly environment that combines public and private funding. This is vital to reduce financial risk in ultra-rare fields like paediatric oncology, which do not attract traditional venture capital. Investing in paediatric oncology R&I will benefit not only patients but also society and the economy: lowering mortality, improving workforce productivity, expanding knowledge, attracting talent, and cementing Europes leadership in patient-centred innovation. 6) The case for a strong ERA Paediatric oncology clearly illustrates the need for a strong European Research Area. Only a single, borderless R&I market can deliver the scale required for cross-border trials, genomic data sharing, and researcher mobility. By prioritising investment, boosting the uptake of innovations, and ensuring equitable access to excellence, the ERA Act can transform Europe into a global leader in paediatric cancer research and deliver life-saving therapies to children across all Member States.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The Austrian Federal Competition Authority refers to the opinion submitted during the public consultation on the evaluation phase of this initiative (https://ec.europa.eu/info/law/better-regulation/have-your-say/initiatives/14427-Public-procurement-directives-evaluation/F3521959_en), which highlighted aspects at the interface between competition law and public procurement law that should be taken into account in the revision of the European public procurement directives: 1.) Insofar as public procurement law regulations oblige bidders to cooperate with the investigating authorities and the tendering authorities (in particular to restore professional reliability - self-cleaning), care must be taken to ensure that the requirements imposed on such cooperation do not jeopardize the effectiveness of antitrust investigations or undermine the protection of leniency statements and settlement submissions provided for under EU law in accordance with Directives 2014/104 and 2019/1. 2.) Procurement procedures (in particular the bids received) should be documented in a standardized and uniform format according to specified criteria in a central database in order to enable both contracting authorities and investigating authorities (e.g., competition authorities, law enforcement agencies) to identify conspicuous patterns that indicate prohibited contact between bidders (bid rigging). Investigative authorities should have comprehensive access to these databases by default.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The GÉANT Association welcomes the European Commission initiative to advance the European Research Area (ERA) through the ERA Act. As the provider of Europes high-capacity trusted digital infrastructure for research and education, GÉANT, together with National Research and Education Networks (NRENs), is deeply invested in building the fifth freedom for the free movement of researchers, scientific knowledge, and technology. GÉANTs solutions and expertise in high-capacity networking, Trust & Identity, and innovative technology have supported Europes R&I ecosystem for over two decades. Together with the other four key European e-infrastructures in the e-Infrastructures Assembly EUDAT, EGI, OpenAIRE, and PRACE we are at the forefront of digital infrastructure for R&I. We emphasise the following priorities for a successful ERA: Establishing the fifth freedom will generate unprecedented volumes of research data. This would have a lasting impact on European research and competitiveness in strategically important technologies. However, a realisation of the fifth freedom will require solutions that respect and safeguard public research as a public good, as in the EuroHPC context. Europe must ensure that its digital tools for research are state-of-the-art. When researchers Choose Europe for Science, their experience should be as seamless as possible. Quick onboarding of researchers is a must and facilitated through Europe-grown solutions like the ones GÉANT and the NREN community deliver. At the same time, research itself relies on research-enabling organisations like GÉANT. The growing demands for data repository management, digital applications, cybersecurity, and interoperability require the upgrade of supporting digital infrastructure to avoid fragmentation of technology. A fully integrated European Research Area needs pan-European solutions that can be adapted to national research cultures and traditions, respecting national differences while fighting fragmentation. We believe the success of the ERA Act is contingent on leveraging cross-border collaboration, coordination, and interoperability, and the GÉANT Association exemplifies a proven model for effective European collaboration to create an innovative research infrastructure ecosystem. Please see the attached file for our full statement.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am in favour of proactively increasing the use of Open Source software within the European Union, in particular as a credible alternative to the solutions of the major non-EU platforms (GAFAM). This is a strategic, economic, democratic and cultural challenge. In terms of sovereignty, the massive dependence on non-European proprietary solutions creates a lasting vulnerability: technological, legal and contractual dependence. Lopen Source, through the auditability of the code, reversibility and control of technical choices, is a key lever for resilience for European administrations and businesses. In economic terms, lopen Source does not mean free but better value allocation. It limits rents, promotes local investment (integration, support, training) and supports a European ecosystem of SMEs, dESN and cooperative projects. In the long term, this is a factor for competitiveness and productivity. In terms of innovation, lopen Source has historically been a major driver of digital progress. The European backlog is less due to the quality of the technologies than to their diffusion and industrialisation. Encourage lopen Source, speed up the pooling of efforts, incremental innovation and the dissemination of good practices, particularly in public services. Democratically, digital tools structure the relationship between the state and the citizen. Transparency, auditability and lack of opaque dependency are legitimate requirements. Lopen Source also facilitates the upskilling of staff and the development of learning organisations. However, this reinforcement must be pragmatic and not dogmatic: based on maturity, security and sustainability criteria, accompanied by gradual migration strategies, investment in training and clear governance. Lopen Source has a cost, but a cost that is better controlled and more virtuous. Finally, the European Union must take on a coherent political strategy: integration of lopen Source in public procurement, funding of European digital commons, interoperability and reversibility requirement. Strengthening Lopen Source in the EU is not an ideological choice, but a rational choice of sovereignty, economic efficiency and democratic maturity.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: This contribution is submitted by Albioma, a renewable electricity producer active in the French Outermost Regions. It is made in the framework of the Commissions consultation launched on 15 December 2025 under Article 31(5) of the Renewable Energy Directive (EU) 2023/2413 (RED III), which requires the review and, where justified, revision of the values and methodologies laid down in Annexes V and VI through a delegated act. The present submission focuses exclusively on Annex VI (19), and in particular on the fossil fuel comparator used for greenhouse-gas calculations for electricity produced from solid biomass in the EUs Outermost Regions (ORs). The current fossil fuel comparator does not adequately reflect the permanent structural and operational conditions of electricity systems in Outermost Regions, including their isolation from continental grids, specific climatic conditions, grid configuration constraints and fuel-supply logistics. As a result, the fossil fuel comparator applicable to solid-biomass electricity in ORs does not correspond to performance levels that are technically achievable under real operating conditions, contrary to the objective of Article 31(5) RED III. Article 29(13) of RED III enables Member States to define different GHG savings criteria for Outermost Regions, however in such case, biomass is considered a fossil fuel. Consequently, biomass being structurally the only technology available for the production of decarbonized dispatchable power in the Outermost regions (such dispatchable power being indispensable to ensure grid stability and end-consumer security of supply), the current fossil fuel comparator defined in Annex VI makes the effective decarbonization of the Outermost Regions de facto impossible. This contribution therefore proposes a targeted and technically substantiated revision of the fossil fuel comparator set out in Annex VI of RED III for electricity produced from solid biomass in Outermost Regions, adjusting it from 212 gCOeq/MJ to 366 gCOeq/MJ, in order to align the pathway value with documented structural constraints and actual operating conditions, without compromising the environmental integrity of RED III. The justification is set out in the main body of this submission and further substantiated in a dedicated Technical Annex.

This contribution therefore proposes a targeted and technically substantiated revision of the fossil fuel comparator set out in Annex VI of RED III for electricity produced from solid biomass in Outermost Regions, adjusting it from 212 gCOeq/MJ to 366 gCOeq/MJ, in order to align the pathway value with documented structural constraints and actual operating conditions, without compromising the environmental integrity of RED III. The justification is set out in the main body of this submission and further substantiated in a dedicated Technical Annex.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I discovered the open-source tools 15 years ago and now I am 100 % open-source. In addition to being reliable and respectful of privacy, they are often much closer ‘to the ground’ than the big proprietary solutions that have only to do with users and the way they use them again. Many too many people are not even aware of these tools, where there are many prejudices when they are installed and used. In the same way as awareness-raising campaigns on tobacco, alcohol, road safety, etc., it is also vital to deploy means of communication to promote these tools. Creating them is one thing, but having them adopted is another. Cybercrime has become endemic in our societies and I do not even talk about deepfakes, which are a poison for our democraticians. Citizens continue to remain largely passive in their relationship with technology and only those who have convictions or a certain sensitivity are proactive in their use of digital tools. It is also necessary to spread propaganda for the free tools (in the right sense of the term), if they will remain confidential as they are still the case. Ehut does not.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: ECOFI welcomes the Organic Omnibus and urges the Commission to use it to simplify and clarify the rules governing fertilisers and soil improvers in organic farming. Organic-based fertilisers are essential for maintaining soil fertility, nutrient availability and stable yields in organic systems, yet Annex II of Regulation 2021/1165 remains incomplete, inconsistently interpreted, and difficult to updatecreating barriers for farmers, manufacturers, and ultimately consumers. ECOFI calls for a streamlined and science-based process to update Annex II; clearer, harmonised guidance for certification bodies; inclusion of missing technical additives and safe circular nutrient sources; alignment with related EU frameworks (FPR, ABPR, circular economy); and removal or clarification of unworkable concepts such as factory farming origin. A more coherent, predictable system will strengthen organic farmers soil-management practices, reduce administrative burden, support nutrient recycling, and reinforce consumer trust and the competitiveness of the EU organic sector. You can find more details in the PDF attached to this submission.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The FS Group welcomes the European Commission’s proposal to revise the public procurement directives. Application of the special sectors directive: with reference to Directive 2014/25/EU, it is necessary to clarify Article 19, specifying that the rules on special sectors apply only to assignments that are functionally instrumental to the performance of the activities referred to in Articles 814, in a direct relationship of means at the end. Such clarification would make it possible to avoid broad interpretations that make contracts only indirectly linked to the special sectors subject to the Directive, with a disproportionate impact on the organisational autonomy and freedom of public undertakings or holders of special or exclusive rights. Application of the Mixed Procurement Directive: it should be clarified that contracts intended partly for activities covered by Directive 2014/25/EU and partly by Directive 2014/24/EU are governed by Article 6 of Directive 2014/25/EU. The legislation should also be supplemented to cover cases where the contract relates to activities covered by several directives or even not covered by European law, where it is not objectively possible to identify the principal activity or separate the services. With regard to contracts excluded from the scope of the directives, we propose clarifying that such awards are not subject to the application of the principles of the TFEU, transposing the guidance of the Court of Justice and preventing situations in which a Member State of the European Union transposes a European directive in a more onerous or restrictive manner. Integrity: it is suggested to extend the grounds for exclusion to include the beneficial owner of the economic operator, as well as any relevant trustees, trusts and settlors. It is proposed to consider the grounds for exclusion of Union sanctions or restrictive measures as well as the violation of trade compliance rules. It would also be useful to regulate the extent of exclusionary infringements and whether or not self-cleaning applies to them, preventing costly overlaps in national transposition. Public-public partnerships and in-house awards: greater organisational flexibility should be granted to intra-group contracts between companies belonging to the same industrial group, even if they are active in different sectors, and forms of joint procurement should be allowed. In this context, it is appropriate to clarify the concept of similar services for the purposes of calculating the turnover percentages laid down in Directive 2014/25/EU and to define a clearer framework for the participation of non-EU undertakings, simplifying the rules for countries that are parties to the Agreement on Public Procurement. Procurement of technological innovation and research: it is proposed to introduce more flexible provisions for the acquisition of innovative products and services, adapting the rules to the digital revolution (and coordinating them with the rules on the transfer of intellectual property rights). It is also appropriate to define more precisely the conditions for awarding contracts without publication of a contract notice, including by reference to EU programmes such as Horizon Europe, while also clarifying the meaning of the prohibition on profit-making or recovery of R & D costs. Public undertakings operating in the market in perfect competition: finally, when public undertakings compete on the market with private operators following public and transparent procedures, they should be treated in the same way as the latter and be covered by the exemption provided for in Directive 2014/25/EU, namely that private operators who have won a tender for a public service are not obliged to launch further tenders for the purchase of goods and services necessary for the performance of the contract.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Hello, as a mere consumer of organic products, I am surprised by this revision of the Regulation. A revision of such an important regulation only 3 and a half years after its implementation seems to me premature when organic producers expect public support and consumers need to be sure of the true environmental and social value of organic farming. Here I reiterate my commitment to the founding principles of organic farming, which make it all of its value and relevance: regeneration of soils by maintaining biological life (earthworms, protozoa, bacteria, algae, fungi, etc.), prohibition of genetically modified organisms/New genomic techniques, animal welfare and access to later (ban on industrial farming), 100 % organic food. I count on your understanding and commitment; Thank you.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Bioenergy Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback to the EU ETS and MSR review. As a representative of the European bioenergy sector, we strongly support the EUs 2040 climate target and the transition to net-zero by 2050. We call for the inclusion of permanent carbon removals in the EU ETS, in line with the "like-for-like" principle. Only high-integrity removalscertified under the Carbon Removal and Carbon Farming Certification (CRCF) frameworkshould be eligible to offset fossil emissions. Solutions such as BioCCS and biochar must be supported to unlock investment and scale up deployment. A phased approach with an intermediary buyer, strict MRV, and a limited number of removals under the ETS cap is key to preserving ambition and market stability. We also caution against lowering the 20 MW threshold for ETS1. ETS2 will already cover smaller emitters under a more suitable framework. Lowering the threshold would impose disproportionate burdens on small operators, particularly in the bioenergy sector, without major climate benefits. We urge the Commission to focus on simplifying the ETS framework, ensuring coherence with RED III, and supporting investment in both emission reductions and permanent removals. The full feedback paper is attached for further details.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Towards Open Source 2.0., from open source software to open source resources? Research work is needed to understand the definition(s) of open source. (https://opensource2.cc) The meaning of « open source » remains confused and with the democratization of other openness movements (open science, open education, open hardware, open data, etc.) we may be near a paradigm shift in the very meaning of the concept leading to a change in its nature. Rather than « open source software », it may be key to move towards « open source resources » to develop open technologies in other sectors than software (research, education...). Apprehending this subtlety of « open source resources » may be critical to support the emergence of a robust open digital ecosystem in plenty of domains, where there is the need to develop specific environments to collaborate around various typologies of digital resources (text, research article, educational content, objects blueprints, datasets, images, video or audio resources or whatever). Dedicated infrastructures to build open resources at scale are still waiting to emerge. Rather than open source software, software with available source code, open source resource seems to be about digital (and "open") resources where source files are provided. Raw and editable elements that are needed to modify the final resource. Examples: docx/odt/pptx/LaTex of a pdf, markdown of a website, svg/psd file of an image, etc. With other types of open resources outside software, it's common to provide them under an open license granting the right to use/modify/share them but source files are regularly poorly handled preventing the ability to do so. Current digital solutions are not designed for these open practices contributing to a poor structure for the management of source files and related collaborative practices around open resources undermining the ability to develop them in depth. This stronger understanding of « open source » could increase consideration around source files of digital resources and therefore reduce obstacles to modification and collaboration such as in (open) science and (open) education. With orientations and policies evolving towards digital commons, with open source software, open science, open education and other open models more broadly, we lack an overall vision of the (public ?) infrastructure that needs to be developed to fully enable their existence. As long as we do not truly understand the meaning of « open source », we can't really know precisely what a related policy should be.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Strengths and weaknesses of the EU FLOSS sector: The greatest Fortaleza is pure meritocracy (developers lead according to their contributions to the code, and if someone disagrees, they can do a fork freely). Moreover, innovation is very rapid in established communities such as Debian/GNOME, all with full transparency. It is usually developed for pleasure and to improve the code. In many cases it is altruistic. Weaknesses: there is ‘AS IS’ volunteering that can go as far as ‘burnout’, strict legislation (CRA/GDPR) exposes them to demands, fragile cybersecurity because no tests are hired. Users who do NOT contribute to the code but demand a lot also do not support the development of the Free Software. Barriers (i) Inercia of people to switch technology to Free Software because they are flawed with proprietary programs from small ones. Free Software does not invest in Marketing campaigns to advertise as proprietary sotfware companies do. (ii) Sustainable contributions: building a community is complex and should be incentivised from education. Without a Community minimum it is difficult for projects to be sustained. Added value: Reduces lock-in, accelerates innovation through collective reviews and improves safety through global scrutiny. Nextcloud: Data sovereignty and privacy. It is used by universities, research centres and private companies. — Debian Linux: Community development of free software as an operating system. Tens of thousands of IT equipment in Galicia use Debian as a youth operating system. Companies such as Librebit use and support Free Software at events. Concrete EU actions: * Funding should be channelled through business or user associations that have as their social objective the promotion of free software. Examples such as GALPon.org or AGASOL.gal (associations with experience in code/events that help the Free Software Community). * Sponsorship of events specialised in Free Software, explicit requirement for the topics to be displayed at the event to be Free Software. Both proprietary code and non-free solutions should be excluded. * Setting up OSPO from the Member States to help individual developers or strategic projects avoid legal problems, improve cybersecurity issues and generally provide aid to developers who are merely technical and not experts on the legal side. Priority areas: * Cybersecurity: FLOSS communities like OpenSSL have patched critical vulnerabilities (e.g. Heartbleed) via fast meritocratic forks, something impossible in a closed owner. EU priority: CRA requires SBOM/audits; individual developers need a legal shield to encode ‘AS IS’ without fear of lawsuits, but want their code to be safe as well. * Verifiable AI: Open models, any AI application on a citizen’s data that affects their rights, must be verifiable by a third party so that it can replicate ALL the AI ecosystem. * Oblige hardware manufacturers to be compatible with Free Software (especially its drivers) so that they can be sold in Europe. This will allow them to fully exploit without dependence on software manufacturers. It allows for greater resilience and avoids planned obsolescence (at least at software level), thus helping to reduce waste by extending the lifetime of IT equipment.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Question 1: Strengths, Weaknesses & Barriers Strengths: The EU has a vibrant open-source community delivering mature, production-ready solutions across critical sectors (networking, storage, security, AI). Weaknesses & Barriers: Fragmented documentation and lack of standardized deployment guides hinder enterprise adoption Public procurement optimized for proprietary vendors creates artificial barriers Hardware compatibility issues, particularly for AI accelerators and specialized processors Critical projects often rely on volunteer maintainers without guaranteed funding Absence of EU-hosted infrastructure (repositories, CI/CD platforms) creates non-EU dependencies Unclear business models for open-source companies Question 2: Added Value of Open Source Public Sector: Sovereignty: Full control without vendor lock-in Transparency: Auditable code enables security verification Cost efficiency: 40-60% cost reduction versus proprietary alternatives Interoperability: Open standards facilitate cross-border collaboration Private Sector: Supply chain security: Verifiable components reduce cybersecurity risks Flexibility: Customization without vendor permission Competitive advantage: SMEs compete equally with established players Market access: Build differentiated solutions on shared foundations Question 3: Concrete EU Measures Development & Sustainability: Dedicated funding for maintenance and security updates beyond R&D grants EU-hosted infrastructure services (code repositories, container registries, CI/CD) Support for open-source foundations providing professional governance Adoption & Integration: Reform procurement to mandate open-source consideration with interoperability requirements Certification programs for open-source solutions in critical sectors Standardized migration toolkits for transitioning from proprietary systems Ecosystem Growth: Accelerator programs for open-source startups and SMEs Professional support networks around key open technologies Educational programs integrating open-source development into curricula Open-source driver development and hardware certification programs Question 4: Priority Technology Areas AI/ML infrastructure: Open models, training frameworks, inference engines Cloud & edge computing: Alternatives to hyperscaler dominance Cybersecurity: Open security tools, intrusion detection, identity management Network infrastructure: Open networking stacks, DNS systems IoT & industrial control: Open protocols for Industry 4.0 Data management: Open databases, data space infrastructure Question 5: Sectors for Increased Competitiveness High-priority sectors: Public administration: Digital government, cross-border interoperability Healthcare: Electronic health records, medical imaging Manufacturing: Industrial IoT, supply chain management Automotive: Software-defined vehicles, autonomous systems Finance: Payment systems, compliance tools Energy: Smart grid, renewable energy management Open-source adoption simultaneously improves cybersecurity (transparency, auditability), reduces costs (eliminating vendor lock-in), and enhances competitiveness (rapid innovation, customization). Critical infrastructure sectors particularly benefit from verified security and long-term control over essential systems.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The German Steel Association welcomes the European Commissions consultation on the revision of the EU Public Procurement Directives. This revision represents a strategic opportunity to transform public procurement from a predominantly price-driven instrument into a value-based policy tool that strengthens Europe’s industrial base, supports strategic autonomy, and accelerates the transition of the steel industry towards climate neutrality. Public procurement must be explicitly recognised as an industrial-policy steering instrument. By creating predictable lead markets for low-emission and strategically important basic materials such as steel, public demand can significantly stimulate investment opportunities, support first movers, and enable the large-scale deployment of climate-neutral production technologies. To achieve this, procurement frameworks must systematically integrate non-price criteria such as emissions performance, circularity, innovation and transformation progress. A central pillar of the revised directives should be the introduction of mandatory sustainability criteria. Only binding requirements can provide the legal and planning certainty needed to trigger investments and rapidly scale up markets for low-emission steel. These criteria must be based on a harmonised European framework for low-emission steel, aligned with existing EU product legislation, to avoid market fragmentation and excessive administrative burden. Established methodologies such as the Low Emission Steel Standard (LESS) * using key performance indicators (KPIs) such as Transformation Progress Level (TPL), Product Carbon Footprint (PCF/EPD) and Recycled Content (RC), stood ready to enable a practical, technology-neutral and legally robust basis for implementation. In addition, the revision must introduce EU preferences (European-content criteria) as binding elements in public procurement for strategic steel-intensive sectors. Steel is a strategic material underpinning Europe’s infrastructure, security of supply and industrial competitiveness. In areas such as hydrogen and CO infrastructure, European-content criteria would directly strengthen resilient value chains, safeguard critical industrial capacities within the Single Market and ensure that public investment supports Europe’s long-term economic and climate objectives. Overall, the revised Public Procurement Directives should establish a coherent, legally sound and WTO-compliant framework that enables contracting authorities to fully leverage procurement as a strategic tool for sustainability, resilience and competitiveness, while ensuring legal certainty and avoiding fragmentation within the Single Market.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: VÖWG welcomes the revision of the EU public procurement framework as an opportunity to strengthen practicability, competition and legal certainty. The current system remains overly complex, discouraging participation and leading to reduced bidder numbers, particularly in specialised and critical infrastructure markets. The reform should prioritise genuine simplification, realistic thresholds with indexation, flexible procedures, and clearer rules on contract modifications, in-house cooperation and concessions. Security and resilience must be embedded as core procurement principles, ensuring that transparency does not create risks for critical infrastructure. Strategic objectives such as sustainability and Made in Europe should be enabled, but not imposed through mandatory overlays that further restrict markets. A practical certification-based approach is preferable to additional control burdens for contracting authorities. Finally, procurement rules for social services and reserved contracts should be adapted to provide longer planning horizons and safeguard continuity of essential services. The full VÖWG position is set out in the attached document.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: We understand the need to enable the European Union to respond more swiftly to a rapidly changing reality, and we endorse the need to remove barriers to the philanthropic entities and civil society organisations acting in the single European market. However, in our opinion, the proposals presented in the Communication on Better Regulation create a risk of violating the fundamental principles on which the entire Union is based. First and foremost, this concerns the principles expressed in Article 11 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) which commits EU institutions to maintain an open, transparent and regular dialogue with representative associations and civil society. This provision is also reflected in international law standards. Recently, this principle has been developed by the European Commission in its Civil Society Strategy. At a time of declining trust in representative institutions and faith in democracy, we believe it is very important for EU institutions to maintain consistency in their proposals and policy documents, while working on improving its delivery on the citizens' needs. In this respect we would like to underline our belief that effective regulation and policymaking must be grounded in solid evidence and inclusive, participatory processes. Policymaking should draw on robust data and reflect the perspectives of diverse societal actors, while upholding democratic principles, the rule of law, and fundamental rights. Even in the face of urgent societal or geopolitical challenges, established consultative mechanismssuch as impact assessments and stakeholder consultationsshould not be undermined. Only in clearly defined, exceptional circumstances, and based strictly on necessity and proportionality, should deviations from these processes be considered. The European Commission should ensure that the better regulation framework is applied in a predictable, consistent, and non-arbitrary manner. Further detailed comments are provided in the attached document.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The typical contractsof the existing directives and the concessions directive have the effect of consolidating models of private public parking. Community legislation should innovate and create regulatory spaces for certain types of activities, in particular technlogics, linking the industrial technological development of the European Union. This approach fits into the broadest spectrum of achieving digital sovereignty. The principles of the TFEU must be and be compatible with new ‘purchasing’ or contracting models. EU law can and must innovate in order to create legally sound solutions that would stifle problems beyond free market and competition.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Dear Agriculture Commissioner Hansen and dear Commissioners, I am writing to draw your attention to important aspects of the planned simplification of the EU Organic Regulation. The proposed changes mainly affect organic poultry farming and have significant implications for animal welfare, economic viability and the future of many farms. The aim must be to simplify the rules without jeopardising established practices or unduly increasing the administrative burden. 1. Production units in organic poultry fattening In accordance with the current proposal of the European Commission, only one broiler house with a maximum area of 1.600 m² per farm will be allowed in the future. Such a limitation poses existential challenges to several established and economically stable farms. I would therefore strongly recommend that, as before, more houses, each with 1.600 m², are still permitted per farm. This practice, which has proved successful for decades, ensures animal welfare, environmental protection and economic viability. Expert reports also confirm the compatibility of this structure with the applicable legislation on organic farming. 2. The planned requirement to provide access to the outdoor area in all organic poultry houses, even if the animals are not yet physiologically ready with feathered cloves to use it, is hardly practicable. It would be much more practical to require access to outdoor use only when soil conditions and animal health allow it, i.e. when birds have reached the full flesh. For broilers this would be from around days 28-35 and for turkeys day 49. Existing buildings without access to outdoor use should therefore continue to be allowed to be used. This not only protects animals from weather conditions, but also helps to maintain small-scale farming, especially in older buildings or farms located in residential areas. 3. Protein supply in organic livestock farms From 2027 onwards, a complete organic feed supply is planned. However, high quality organic protein sources are currently not available in sufficient quantities. A complete transformation is therefore not realistic in the short term without increasing imports or risking animal health. An extension of the existing derogation allowing the use of up to 5 % conventional protein feed for suckling and young poultry is therefore ideally necessary until 2030 or, depending on the development of practical and economically viable alternatives. In addition, regulatory adjustments, investment incentives for the development of new protein sources and targeted research funding for domestic GM-free alternatives are needed. Equally important are clear and practical rules for insect protein and sustainable fishmeal production. I would like to warmly ask you to fight for practical, animal welfare and economically viable solutions in the political process. Organic poultry farming in Europe needs clear, realistic and European-harmonised framework conditions to ensure its continued existence and growth. Thank you very much for your attention and commitment. With friendly hill Melanie Bonde, organic broiler producer Vestervang 7, DK-6270 Tønder, mobile: 0045-28247514.
