initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: In this aggressive geopolitical context, and economic threats addressed by certain declarations and/or actions, it becomes obvious to refocus on the interests of the EU, to provide a sound market for its citizens, and its industries. It is so simple to give the priority to European manufacturers on public procurement. A simple gesture with a lot of impact. We are protecting the European industry, and its consumers. When dealing with health topics, whether it concerns food, or material in contact with food or water, it becomes obvious that our system in the EU is not designed to track every imported good and check whether or not they are in compliance with our rules. This creates an imbalance, because for sure, the goods that are manufactured within the EU have to face a lot of challenges in terms of standards, social responsibility and of course, in terms of protection of the environment. This makes for an unfair comparison between European producers and non-EU manufacturers, that are often playing with this slack in our process and are sometimes able to downgrade the overall quality and pass through the EU specification threshold. Most of the time, public procurement clerks are out of solution and do not understand what tools are at their disposal to opt out certain supplies from outside the EU. Reciprocity rules for instance, are most of the time not applied or not even known by the public procurement in charge of tenders, and result in open highways for wars on price against non-EU low quality manufacturers who only goal is to kill European industries. Innovation and sustainable aspects that will bring revolution on the different market segments are only being developed by European companies. If they are weakened, there will be no more innovation and the most useful technical solutions, (electric cables, water ductile iron pipeline, train tracks) will become commodities imported from outside the EU and will most probably decrease in terms of quality and durability. The choice of the made in Europe should not be tabooed in public tenders! Every other large economy in the world is protecting the access to its public market, (USA, India, China, Iran, Turkey) why not do the same for Europe? Is there any explanation to this open-door policy on public procurement? Only countries who signed bilateral reciprocity agreements should be able to participate to public tenders. We cannot expect low price criteria to prevail anymore. It is not a smart choice, as most of the time for infrastructure, it is the total cost of ownership that should be compared. Not the purchasing price of the material. In terms of sustainability, no real criteria or law can be mentioned to protect the choice of the public procurement. So they do not feel obliged to comply with this, even though it is an emergency to change the way we function. The voluntary GPP is not well applied. By pushing these criteria, the impact could be multiple. Implementing a procurement framework could attract more secure investments to European industry, which will result in generating social, economic, and environmental benefits, as well as continuing to promote innovation. It will support the necessary industrial ecosystem that fosters the development of European defence and other critical industrial sectors. It allows European industry to advance towards decarbonization and the circular economy, reducing strategic dependence on goods and services. In crisis situations, it ensures the continuity of service of Europe's critical infrastructure, such as water supply networks, electricity and transport. Compared to others, the European industrial regulatory framework ensures the application of high social and environmental standards in the production of goods, which also contribute economically to local development within the EU. Otherwise, what would be the point of European legislation in the industry?

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: Amazon welcomes the opportunity to provide input to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on modernisation of the Better Regulation framework. This Call for Evidence is itself a testament to the Commission's commitment to ensuring that its legislative proposals are evidence-based and meaningfully informed by the views of affected stakeholders. We share the Commission's interest in improving and applying the Better Regulation framework, ensuring that it remains fit for purpose and workable for future challenges. As Europe faces intensifying global competition, the need for a regulatory environment that protects consumers whilst also enabling innovation has never been more critical. The Draghi report's call for an additional 750-800 billion in annual investment underscores the urgency of creating conditions that attract and retain long-term capital. Amazon is a key contributor to this vision. Since 2010, we have invested 225 billion in Europe, generating 41 billion in additional GDP impact and supporting 150,000 direct and 450,000 indirect jobs. More than 127,000 European small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) sell on Amazon, generating 15 billion in export sales in 2024. We have many data centres in Europe for our EU customers and are deeply rooted in European industry, buying products and services from companies like Daimler Truck, Maersk, and Ariane Espace. However, our experienceand that of the thousands of European SMEs we work withdemonstrates that regulatory complexity remains a significant barrier to realising the Single Market's full potential. It is crucial that these stakeholders views are taken into account through the legislative and regulatory process. We therefore applaud the Commissions commitment through the Better Regulation process to address not only the substance of new regulation but the process through which it is developed. We welcome the chance to share our experience from past and ongoing legislative initiatives. This includes identifying examples where we think that adjustments to the legislative process would lead to better outcomes for European consumers and businesses by reducing business uncertainty and generating a favourable investment environment across Europe. Our key recommendations are to: 1. Ensure rigorous impact assessments that are founded on current and relevant data. 2. Ensure that the launch timing and feedback periods of consultations allow for meaningful input. 3. Address the gap between legislative adoption and implementation readiness. Please see the attached paper for further detail on these recommendations. Amazon remains ready to engage constructively in further dialogue on these important issues and to support the Commission's modernisation efforts. Together, we can ensure that Europe's regulatory framework serves as a competitive advantageattracting investment, enabling innovation, and strengthening the resilience that will secure Europe's prosperity for generations to come.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The Kaleidos project (GA no 101132079), working on the systematization and implementation of quadruple-helix-based knowledge valorisation best practices to foster the transition towards an Open Science innovation management model across European academic and research centres, welcomes the European Commissions initiative to prepare a legislative proposal for an ERA Act. The challenges identified regarding persistent fragmentation of research and innovation systems, together with disparities in R&D investment levels, the insufficient coordination between national and EU priorities, and the limited cooperation between higher education institutions, research organisations, and businesses are in line with the challenges encountered in the implementation of Kaleidos. Our project contribution focuses on block 3 of the proposed policy measures, regarding the improvement of the framework conditions for researchers and research organisations in Europe, essential for creating better opportunities for knowledge valorisation. We consider (i) ERA should actively encourage universities, research centres, enterprises, and public actors to define long-lasting collaboration relationships within defined, durable and formalised frameworks. Systemic cooperation around shared challenges ensures that research addresses real societal needs while delivering tangible social and market value. Sustained collaboration of this kind requires both physical infrastructures and organisational resources that merit targeted investment. (ii) Local governments should dedicate specialised teams to support collaborative initiatives, while enterprises should be directly involved in framing both challenges and potential solutions to guarantee the relevance and applicability of outcomes. (iii) Academic careers need to evolve so that contributions to participatory processes, innovation activities, and societal engagement are valued and incentivised alongside traditional outputs such as publications and competitive funding. Ongoing initiatives to revise and complement evaluation criteria and indicators should be maintained and their contributions considered to best capture broader impacts of research and reward the alignment with societal needs. Kaleidos supports the core objectives of the ERA Act and recognises its potential to strengthen coordination and impact across the European research landscape. We urge, however, that knowledge valorisation and systemic collaboration and co-creation practices are explicitly included, ensuring that long lasting alliances between universities, research organisations, enterprises, and public actors are encouraged and rewarded.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Tendering (customised) Software/Applications just because the contract is ending: don’t do it! If a completely new need for customised software (or an application) arises within an organisation, there is nothing wrong with letting the market develops an offer based on competition by issuing a (European) tender. When choosing the right procedure and using the right purchasing technique, this can have positive consequences for the price/quality. Things are different when no new need arises but when the contract for the current software/application has expired. In many cases, a lawyer, purchaser, contract manager, controller or accountant will then state: a new tender must be issued. This may be correct from an accounting (legal) perspective, but it is anything but socially responsible (effective & efficient). The core of this lies in the fact that customs software is not subject to wear and tear. Due to continuous Management & Maintenance, the tendered product is still ‘brand new’ at the end of the contract term. Especially if, as is usually the case these days, adequate contract management has tasks place for a number of years. Even the improvement of look and feel that is often used as an argument could be managed within the term of the contract. If the expired contract term is the only trigger for re-tendering, then in my opinion that falls under the heading SIPC (Socially irresponsible Purchasing and Commissioning). You are actually putting an as good as newproduct in the trash and that is of no benefit to anyone. The only thing you can hope for is that the tender is won by the current supplier/developer, but that is actually a Kafkaesque situation.
text_raw: Tendering (customised) Software/Applications just because the contract is ending: don’t do it! If a completely new need for customised software (or an application) arises within an organisation, there is nothing wrong with letting the market develops an offer based on competition by issuing a (European) tender. When choosing the right procedure and using the right purchasing technique, this can have positive consequences for the price/quality. Things are different when no new need arises but when the contract for the current software/application has expired. In many cases, a lawyer, purchaser, contract manager, controller or accountant will then state: a new tender must be issued. This may be correct from an accounting (legal) perspective, but it is anything but socially responsible (effective & efficient). The core of this lies in the fact that customs software is not subject to wear and tear. Due to continuous Management & Maintenance, the tendered product is still ‘brand new’ at the end of the contract term. Especially if, as is usually the case these days, adequate contract management has tasks place for a number of years. Even the improvement of look and feel that is often used as an argument could be managed within the term of the contract. If the expired contract term is the only trigger for re-tendering, then in my opinion that falls under the heading SIPC (Socially irresponsible Purchasing and Commissioning). You are actually putting an as good as newproduct in the trash and that is of no benefit to anyone. The only thing you can hope for is that the tender is won by the current supplier/developer, but that is actually a Kafkaesque situation.

feedback_text: Edison supports the European Commission in launching a consultation on the revision of the EU Emissions Exchange System (EU ETS) and the Market Stability Reserve (MSR), confirming its willingness to contribute constructively to the process. This document presents the main recommendations and policy proposals drawn up by Edison, with the aim of strengthening the coherence, ambition and consistency of the system, in line with the European Union’s climate neutrality objectives.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: Assistenzhunde Helfende Pfoten gGmbH is a non-profit organisation working in the field of assistance dogs and long-term support of persons with disabilities. A particular focus lies on independent living and practical participation of persons with disabilities through the use of specially trained assistance dogs, as well as on structural and cross-border challenges within the European Union. We welcome the European Commissions intention to further develop and strengthen the Strategy for the Rights of Persons with Disabilities up to 2030. The clear commitment to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UN CRPD), the identification of persisting implementation gaps, and the renewed focus on independent living and participation represent important and necessary steps. From a practical perspective, however, many barriers to participation persist not because rights are formally lacking, but because appropriate support and assistance structures in particular the use of specially trained assistance dogs are not sufficiently recognised, assessed and operationalised in everyday life. Independent living requires reliable assistance systems as well as functioning structures for assessment, recognition and quality assurance. Where these are lacking, self-determination remains limited in practice. This affects not only individual forms of support, but also assistance providers delivering such services on a professional and long-term basis. In some Member States including Germany clear and reliable assessment and recognition mechanisms for assistance dog teams are still missing. This structural gap leads to legal uncertainty and de facto discrimination, both for persons with disabilities and for the assistance providers responsible for supporting them. These challenges become particularly apparent in the context of mobility and freedom of movement. Although the strategy rightly emphasises accessibility and mobility, persons with disabilities continue to face significant obstacles when their established assistance and support systems are not recognised. Fragmented recognition of assistance systems undermines the practical exercise of freedom of movement within the European Union and negatively affects access to education, employment and social participation. Assistance dog teams and the assistance providers supporting them are a clear example. Internationally established quality frameworks such as those applied by accredited member organisations under the umbrella of Assistance Dogs International already provide robust standards for training, team assessment and ongoing quality assurance. Nevertheless, assistance dog teams that meet these standards frequently experience legal uncertainty and exclusion, both within their own country and in cross-border contexts, due to the absence of coordinated recognition mechanisms. In this context, the European Disability Card represents an important instrument for promoting mobility and mutual recognition within the EU. Its practical effectiveness, however, depends on whether it consistently reflects the assistance systems and services that enable independent living in everyday life, including professionally supported assistance dog teams. Without such consideration, the card risks falling short of its potential to facilitate genuine participation and mobility. We therefore see a clear need for stronger coordination at EU level. Without encroaching on national competences, the Commission could support Member States through guidance, minimum quality principles and a structured exchange of good practices related to assistance systems. This would increase legal certainty, reduce discrimination and contribute to a more coherent implementation of the UN CRPD, particularly with regard to independent living and freedom of movement. Finally, we encourage the Commission to complement quantitative indicators with qualitative elements that better reflect the lived experienc

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: El Grupo Social ONCE es una de las organizaciones sociales dedicadas a las personas con discapacidad de referencia mundial y también uno de los mayores grupos ejemplo de la economía social. Somos el tercer empleador de España, y el primero en el mundo de personas con discapacidad, con más de 80.000 trabajadores. Nuestros más de 85 años de experiencia y trabajo en España nos han servido para contribuir a la mejora de las oportunidades de inclusión y la plena autonomía de las personas ciegas y con otras discapacidades de Europa, América Latina y otras partes del mundo. Ahora, bajo el marco de la Convención de Naciones Unidas sobre los Derechos de las Personas con Discapacidad e implicados en la construcción de la Agenda 2030 de Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible, nuestro modelo no tiene fronteras. Desde nuestra organización acogemos con satisfacción el compromiso de la Comisión Europea de reforzar la Estrategia de la UE sobre los Derechos de las Personas con Discapacidad hasta 2030. Consideramos esencial garantizar la correcta aplicación de las acciones desarrolladas durante la primera mitad de la Estrategia, pero también pedimos nuevas actuaciones e iniciativas que, ante todo, aborden aquellos ámbitos a los que no se haya logrado llegar durante este primer periodo. Varias de las iniciativas emblemáticas llevadas a cabo durante estos primeros cuatro años han servido para construir un entendimiento común en toda la UE e intercambiar buenas prácticas entre diferentes actores y partes interesadas. Estas constituyen una excelente base sobre la que desarrollar políticas concretas y normas vinculantes para los Estados miembros, siempre con el apoyo de la financiación de la UE. Las nuevas iniciativas deben basarse en el trabajo realizado hasta la fecha en el marco de la Estrategia, transformando las acciones no legislativas y no vinculantes en medidas que tengan un impacto más directo y concreto en la vida de las personas con discapacidad. El Grupo Social ONCE ha preparado un documento de posición exhaustivo, con ejemplos concretos de iniciativas emblemáticas y acciones en las que nos gustaría que la Unión Europea y los Estados miembros trabajaran durante los próximos cinco años. Este documento se adjunta a nuestra respuesta.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Enedis, the electricity distribution system operator in France, is a major contracting entity with an annual procurement volume of approximately EUR 6.3 billion, 97 % of which is sourced in Europe from more than 3 000 suppliers and service providers, around half of whom are micro, small and medium-sized enterprises. As an operator of critical infrastructure, Enedis contributes to European industrial value chains. In a context of growing geopolitical and technological tensions, Europe must strengthen its economic security and the protection of strategic undertakings, in particular critical infrastructure operators. European electricity network operators remain dependent on non-European, raw materials, hardware, software and data components. These developments raise concerns in terms of security of supply and system resilience. Against this background, the revision of the EU public procurement framework represents an opportunity to enhance sovereignty, efficiency and flexibility, while preserving transparency and competition. Maintaining a specific procurement regime for the energy, water, transport and communications sectors remains essential. These sectors involve critical infrastructures operated under long-term concession arrangements and are characterised by structurally limited competition resulting from special or exclusive rights granted by Member States. Directive 2014/25/EU provides appropriate tools, such as negotiated procedures and qualification systems, which are essential for technically complex contracts and should be preserved. At the same time, current rules remain too rigid for contract execution and for managing economic or technological contingencies. Greater flexibility is needed to allow easier adaptation of contracts during their performance where objectively justified. This should go hand in hand with lighter information and documentation requirements (such as award notices), higher thresholds, and wider use of direct awards, especially for IT procurement and highly specialised digital solutions. Simplification is also a key condition to facilitate access to public procurement for SMEs. Legal certainty should be reinforced through full harmonisation of exclusion grounds at EU level, including a single list of exclusions, a European eligibility certificate and a common register of excluded companies. European preference should guide procurement policy in light of persistent asymmetries in international market access. Made in Europe clauses and qualitative non-price criteria, such as durability, innovation, security of supply and resilience, should be more systematically considered, on the basis of European value added, production location and ownership of key intellectual property. Where justified by market conditions and strategic considerations, Enedis proposes that contract notices published in the Official Journal of the European Union explicitly include Made in Europe eligibility requirements. Finally, strategic autonomy must also be understood in the digital sphere. European data and digital infrastructures are strategic assets that should be protected through cybersecurity, data localisation and, where relevant, interoperability and technological reversibility requirements, in line with Regulation (EU) 2024/1735 (Net-Zero Industry Act). As a mission-driven company, Enedis supports the integration of environmental and social objectives into public procurement through incentive-based mechanisms rather than rigid mandatory obligations. // Main recommendations: preserve the specific procurement regime for strategic sectors; increase contractual flexibility and reduce administrative burden; harmonise exclusion grounds at EU level; strengthen the European preference in contract notices; ensure strategic digital autonomy; require interoperability and reversibility in digital procurements; promote environmental and social objectives through incentives.

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: The revision of the standardisation Regulation is highly anticipated. Standards should provide aceess to the market, while stating the state-of-the-art. In order to meet this, standardisation must become faster and adoption on european level to a harmonized standard has to be enabled more quickly. Despite the urgent need for inclusion of all stakeholders in standardisation process, the before mention of speed of adoption must be maintained or improved, this provides a challenge, as more voices usually mean more time for consens, which we dont have in a fast moving economy. Therefore a way needs to be found for SMEs or others to participate, but increase the speed of standard development and adoption to provide faster acess to the market and maintain safety to the public. The initative of SMART standards has to be followed with urgency. Standards with clear and precise requirement units will ultimatly enhance adaptability by manufacturers and transparency in surveillance by notified bodies. This will create an even faster access to the market and therefore increase the attractive ness of the sectors and markets. Which is in alignment with the newly formulated European strategy. I hope this adoptions will create the desired effects and strengthen the market.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: In view of the call for a revision of the EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve review and the call for evidence opened by the COM, the Directorate General for Merchant Shipping of the Ministry of Transport and Sustainable Mobility of Spain presents comments relating to the maritime sector in an attached document. We understand that, in view of the recent approval and foreseeable adoption of the measures in the International Maritime Organisation (IMO), it is considered necessary to amend the directive. This Unit and the Ministry have stated that international maritime transport needs to be regulated internationally within the framework of the IMO. In this sense, the IMO, through the Zero Net Emissions Framework (NZF) to be adopted in October, ensures a homogeneous application without dysfunctions at international level. The summary of the document is as follows: 1.We Call for alignment with the IMO and the ETS directive. 2.We call for a more accurate and proactive treatment of carbon leakage to avoid loss of competitiveness, including a statistically robust analysis, as well as accompanying measures to avoid leakage of traffic and carbon out of the EU. 3.We are not in favour of the extension of the ETS for ships between 400 and 5000 GT to encourage IMO to react as a consequence of such amendment. We do not oppose that these ships are in the IMO framework using all the necessary efforts excluding the development of regional measure

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The BioMed Alliance welcomes the European Commissions intention to improve research in Europe through the development of a new European Research Area Act. We hope that the Act will play a role in supporting the fifth freedom, the free movement of researchers, scientific knowledge, and technology in the EU single market, but it needs to be fit for purpose and not add unnecessary regulatory complexity. We support the intention to bring up national commitments to reach the 3% R&D target: by developing new legal mechanisms to support and incentivise public and private-sector R&D spending, alongside national plans to achieve the 3% of GDP investment target. We believe that national investment must be matched by strong funding at EU level, including for the next Horizon Europe, to support collaborative and cross-border research. In addition, funding for health research must be ringfenced due to its importance for European wellbeing and the economy. Strong health research funding is also necessary to strengthen European resilience against health threats and challenges such as population ageing, anti-microbial resistance, an increasing incidence of chronic diseases, climate change geopolitical tensions, and the emergence of new pathogens with pandemic potential. We also welcome the second policy action to reinforce the alignment of investments, policies and programmes around strategic priority areas between the EU and Member States and across Member States, by ensuring political coordination to promote joined-up EU and national-level investment in R&D and synergies with related policies. We believe that this is of particular importance in the health field, due to its unique nature and its impact on the health of European citizens and the strategic importance of the health and life science sector for the European economy and competitiveness. The COVID-19 pandemic has been a wake-up call, and in response to the crisis the EC implemented many new instruments and activities to foster collaboration and coordination among member states. The ERA Act should ensure strategic coordination across all health programs at national and European level based on the experiences and expertise of the different actors, by involving and consulting academia, patients, and private sector actors, as carried out in part by the EU public-private partnership program Innovative Health Initiative (IHI). Different stakeholders have been calling for strengthened coordination for years, starting with earlier support from BioMed Alliance and the Scientific Panel for Health for a unified oversight structure. The various stakeholders should continue the work to enhance coordination related to health and societal challenges, especially considering the increasing challenges that are on the horizon for the EU. BioMed Alliance also agrees that improvements in framework conditions for researchers can help promote a better circulation of researchers and scientific knowledge. However, the impact of any proposed changes on the sector must be carefully evaluated so that they facilitate research and not create any unnecessary barriers. For instance, improved legal frameworks and strengthening ethics and integrity must not add to regulatory complexity and put an additional burden on researchers. For more information, please also see the BioMed Alliance recommendations for FP10 as published in FEBS letters here: https://febs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1873-3468.70079

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: In professional CAD workflows, the dominant DWG format is proprietary and non-ISO-standardised. This restricts interoperability between tools, limits reuse and development of European and open-source software, and creates long-term vendor lock-in. Addressing file format standardisation and interoperability aligns with the EUs strategic objectives for open digital ecosystems, technological sovereignty, and fair competition.
text_raw: In professional CAD workflows, the dominant DWG format is proprietary and non-ISO-standardised. This restricts interoperability between tools, limits reuse and development of European and open-source software, and creates long-term vendor lock-in. Addressing file format standardisation and interoperability aligns with the EUs strategic objectives for open digital ecosystems, technological sovereignty, and fair competition.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: QGIS.org submits this contribution as the steward of QGIS, a widely deployed Digital Public Good forming a core component of public and private-sector geospatial infrastructure. Drawing on more than two decades of real-world use across administrations, SMEs, and critical sectors, we highlight that Europes digital sovereignty depends not only on adopting open source, but on sustainably maintaining and governing it. The submission provides concrete, experience-based recommendations on procurement, funding, cybersecurity, and ecosystem support to ensure that open source remains a secure, resilient, and competitive foundation for Europes digital future. See the attached PDF for the full submission

feedback_text: The European Consortium of Innovative Universities (ECIU) is calling upon the European Union and its Member States to harness the full potential of the upcoming European Research Area Act (ERA Act) to further develop the European Research Area (ERA). In times of geopolitical uncertainty and the need for competitiveness and technological advancement, we cannot afford to continue as before. We must strengthen our European 'research house': reinforce its foundations, make it more attractive to talent and adapt it to evolving needs. Barriers to the free circulation of researchers, scientific knowledge and technology continue to contribute to the fragmentation of the ERA. Addressing these barriers is crucial to building a more integrated and resilient European research ecosystemone that is capable of tackling challenges that extend beyond national borders. The ERA Act must be Europes answer to the fifth freedom as proposed by Enrico Letta (2024). The ECIU supports the overall aims of the ERA Act, particularly its potential to: enhance knowledge valorisation in Europe; support the societal impact of research; invest in the latest technologies; strengthen the link between education, research and innovation (R&I); promote societal engagement. In this context, it is crucial to acknowledge the significance of tackling global challenges such as the energy transition, digitalisation and the increasing use of artificial intelligence, which require collaborative efforts that transcend national borders. The ERA Act should ensure that Europe continues to lead the way in innovation while responding to urgent societal needs. Additionally, ECIU underlines the importance of national commitments in achieving the 3% R&D target, as well as the need for stronger coordination of policies and investments between Member States and the EU. Coordinating research efforts is essential for aligning EU strategic priorities with national funding strategies. It is equally important to ensure that this coordination includes all countries participating in Horizon Europe to foster a truly international and inclusive ERA. The ERA Act is key to strengthen European competitiveness, and strong synergies with the Start-up and Scale-up Strategy and the upcoming EU Innovation Act are crucial. A more integrated ERA in which knowledge can flow freely across borders, sectors and disciplines will drive innovation. In strong synergy with the Innovation Act, the ERA Act is a key opportunity to boost Europes competitiveness by advancing knowledge valorisation. To support knowledge valorisation, the ERA Act must promote an entrepreneurial culture in Europe. For example, by strengthening the collaboration between universities and businesses, and recognise entrepreneurial achievements. The ERA Act is an opportunity to strengthen the links between research and education, and link to the European Education Area to foster more integrated and effective knowledge ecosystems, with universities as central actors. A higher level of awareness on and improved complementarity between research and education is needed to strengthen cooperation between academic, public, and private entities, this will help aligning research and innovation with broader societal goals. The ERA Act is an opportunity to increase the integration of societal impacts into research evaluation frameworks. The ERA Act can help working towards a much-needed European approach to citizen science, as engaging citizens boosts the societal uptake of implementable solutions to societal challenges. ECIU welcomes the European Commissions ambition to strengthen Europes global position in artificial intelligence (AI) in the AI Act. The ERA Act must support this ambition by empowering universities, researchers, innovators, and their communities to advance science and key technologies in an interdisciplinary way.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: This submission focuses on open-weight AI, i.e. systems whose parameters are publicly available for download and adaptation, rather than open-source technologies broadly. We draw this distinction because open-weight AI presents governance challenges that traditional open-source software does not: once model weights are released, they cannot be recalled, safety guardrails can be removed with minimal effort, and thousands of safety-stripped variants already circulate freely. Simply transplanting broader open-source policy arguments to advance AI models could undermine the specialised governance these systems require. The geopolitical context makes this urgent. Today's open-weight landscape is dominated by Chinese models (DeepSeek, Qwen), while American frontier systems (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) remain closed. While Europes Mistral also has open-weight general-purpose models, it does not compete at the frontier. Any strategy framing open source as a path to European sovereignty must reckon with the fact that uncritical adoption of Chinese open-weight models simply substitutes one dependency for another. Meanwhile, Europe's own position is precarious: it hosts only 56% of global AI compute versus roughly 75% in the US, faces acute shortages of advanced AI engineers, and captures just 6% of global AI startup funding. We argue that Europe needs a dual-track strategy. The first track builds societal resilience: the safety infrastructure, governance standards, and defensive capabilities needed to manage misuse risks from powerful AI models Europe does not control. The second track reduces strategic dependence: directing the EU's approximately 2 trillion in annual public procurement toward European open-weight developers to build a sustainable domestic ecosystem, without relying on direct subsidies. Neither track works alone. Resilience without alternatives means permanent dependency; alternatives without resilience risk a single misuse incident discrediting openness altogether. Concretely, this means developing tiered release standards where openness is granted in proportion to demonstrated safety, with AI Safety and Security Institutes (AISIs) leading risk threshold development. It means sustained investment in technical safeguards tamper-resistant architectures, machine unlearning, and restricted-access channels for frontier models. It means context-aware procurement that prioritises European open-weight solutions where they offer clear sovereignty benefits cybersecurity, public services, SME adoption, research without mandating openness where closed models remain materially superior. And it means societal preparedness: AI-specific incident monitoring, cross-border threat intelligence, and targeted support that raises the floor of resilience for businesses and critical infrastructure operators alike. The window for establishing governance frameworks that enable safe openness is narrowing as model capabilities advance. Openness and safety are not mutually exclusive, but pairing them requires deliberate investment in both tracks, starting now.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The objective of adapting Directives 2014/23/EU, 2014/24/EU and 2014/25/EU should be to allow contracting authorities to design tender conditions in a way that preserves the diversity of suppliers and avoids shortages of supply (in particular pharmaceutical products). We also call for a separate sector-specific procurement directive, analogous to the defence industry, with sector-specific regulations as a key industry that would underline the purpose of geostrategic independence.

We also call for a separate sector-specific procurement directive, analogous to the defence industry, with sector-specific regulations as a key industry that would underline the purpose of geostrategic independence.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: It should be mandatory for all EU countries, due to sovereignty concerns, to either use local (European-made and owned) proprietary Operating Systems, or, ideally, Free and Open-Source ones. You cannot start thinking about sovereignty and freedom without the base software being something you can inspect and/or control. There's obviously more that can be done in terms of hardware, but that's considerably harder to accomplish, even if not less important. There's also the need for making cloud software alternatives to the huge US and Chinese companies who virtually own and control all of them. You can attract europeans to build these alternatives with grants (or actual jobs) distributed to individuals and software companies (not big agencies who will subcontract other smaller agencies and freelancers) who can be vetted as capable of doing so.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Capacity: Open source contributor (25 years), Former board member of open source advocacy April and of the open source project Open edX, Founder of open source edtech company OpenCraft, French citizen SUMMARY Open source is inherently borderless and transparent. The EU's strategy should fund the maintenance of proven projects rather than creating new ones, and position open source as the federation point for all actors seeking digital independence. KEY RECOMMENDATIONS I) Fund Maintenance, Not Just Innovation EU funding has historically favored new project creation over maintenance. This is counterproductive: - Maintenance is where security happens: vulnerabilities are addressed and dependencies updated through ongoing work. - The open source ecosystem already innovates. The EU's highest-impact intervention is to sustainably fund and help to further develop existing projects that benefit to European interests. Current grant criteria often require "novelty," excluding maintenance of established critical infrastructure. In addition, it is easier for a public institution to identify established open source projects and provide them with maintenance & ongoing development funding for their roadmap, than to try to design new software or fund specific features. Recommendation: Create funding streams for long-term, recurring maintenance supportnot just one-off innovation grants. II) Embrace Open Source's Borderless Nature Open source licenses guarantee the EU's sovereignty over all open source software, regardless of the countries that contribute to it. It opens the possibility of international collaboration, where non-EU countries also contribute to EU sovereignty. - Transparency eliminates hidden risks. Code can be audited by anyone; security depends on the code itself, not on trusting foreign entities. - Free software freedoms legally guarantee rights to use, modify, and redistribute. No foreign actor can revoke access. Recommendation: Collaborate on international open source projects, whether they were created in Europe or not. Fund European developers on international projects, increasing the European influence in those open source projects. - Open Source as Federation Strategy To counter proprietary foreign platform dominance, position open source as the rallying point for digital independence. It allows any country, company, or individual to participate without ceding control. The EU can lead while welcoming global collaboration. III) Prioritise Education European schools depend on US platforms (Google Classroom, Microsoft Teams) with access to student data. Open source alternatives like Open edXserving 100 million learners globallyprovide auditable platforms hostable on European infrastructure. Recommendation: Make education technology a priority vertical for open source adoption. IV) Scale Proven Models Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund demonstrates an effective approach: fund existing critical infrastructure based on impact. It has supported 60+ projects with over 24 million since 2022. However, its scale remains insufficient. Recommendation: Create an EU-wide mechanism modeled on the Sovereign Tech Fund, significantly scaled up. V) Support Governance Capacity Healthy projects need sustainable governance. Models like the Apache Software Foundation demonstrate transparent community governance. Recommendation: Fund governance capacity-building: legal support, foundation establishment, certification programs. ---------- WHAT TO AVOID - Funding new projects that duplicate existing solutions - One-off grants without long-term commitment - Geographic restrictions on project nationality - the important bit is to be open source! - Opaque selection processes CONCLUSION The EU can become the global leader in sustainable open source infrastructurenot by building a walled garden, but by becoming the most reliable long-term supporter of the open source commons.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Good evening, I would ask for relaxation or a reasonable interpretation of the grazing obligation in organic farming. As I am in a single location, I cannot comply with the full grazing obligation. We are organic in 1997 and I can also imagine anything else, but Germany wants to interpret this Regulation far too strictly. Please inform you that only some of the cattle on pasture are sufficient if the rest has an open-air area and is fed with herbaceous forage. I am very desperate and thank you for my understanding and commitment. Yours sincerely, Prey Martin

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: UFE welcomes the EC intention to address the excessive prescriptiveness of public procurements procedures, to streamline the framework and to better align it with EUs strategic priorities, including economic security and sovereignty. For activities related to energy, the revision of the public procurement directives should indeed be an opportunity to preserve and reinforce the more flexible treatment granted to the energy sector, given its closed and security-sensitive nature, and to design tools to strengthen Europes energy and industrial sovereignty. 1. Ensure competitiveness by simplifying the legal framework: The revision of the public procurement directives, in particular the Utilities directive, must maintain a dedicated regime for contracting entities operating in the energy sector, which should be able to maintain and strengthen the more flexible treatment they benefit from. Among its key priorities, UFE calls for: - Maintaining the format of a directive, rather than transforming it to a regulation, to give Member States the opportunity to adjust national specificities. - Raise the application thresholds. - Clarifying that contracting entities, when acting in the context of competitive calls for tender, must be exempted from public procurement requirements in order to compete on an equal footing with private economic operators. The case-by-case logic of article 34 of Utilities Directive should be thus overcomed, by exempting ab initio contracting entities operating on the competitive market of electricity generation and wholesale. This procedure is lengthy, applied unevenly across Member States, and no longer reflects todays reality, as these activities are already fully exposed to competition. - Preserving the negotiated procedure. - More flexibility in framework agreements. - More flexibility to amend ongoing contracts, without having to restart the process. 2. Strengthening EU energy and industrial sovereignty: It is essential that the revision of the public procurement directives build on and go beyond the Net-Zero Industry Act (NZIA) by fostering the development and reshoring manufacturing value chains in the EU, while taking into account the reality of value chains and avoiding being overly prescriptive. This can be achieved by: - Promoting environmental sustainability. For contracts relating to net-zero technologies, or involving work that includes such technologies, those provisions could be modified to be aligned with provisions of the NZIA on environmental sustainability in public procurement (art. 25, §1-5). - Promoting European preference. Several levers can be considered, for instance: 1) In case of equal bids, award the contract to the European offer. 2) Possibility for buyers to open their contracts only to European operators (understood as EU, UK, EEA and Switzerland). 3) Consider a European content criterion that would incentivise the production of made in Europe products by granting companies that integrate part of their value chain(s) in Europe with extra points in procurements. 4) Strengthen requirements relating to the location within the EU of the means of production of the products covered by the contract (art. 78). Contracting entities should be able to include this requirement into technical specifications (art. 60) or in conditions relating to the performance of contracts (art. 87). 5) In general, beside direct European preference, the directives could be more demanding with regard to non-price criteria in order to promote virtuous operators (electrification criterion, carbon footprint, location of production facilities). Last but not least, the revision should address the growing importance of cybersecurity and digital sovereignty, for instance by allocating part of public procurement contracts to operators demonstrating their data centers are located in Europe. Please find attached more details on our position.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Practical experience with open source in an SME context I make extensive use of open-source software in a commercial setting, primarily for running an online webshop and related data analysis. My experience is that open source is not an ideological alternative, but a key economic enabler that allows small and medium-sized enterprises to remain competitive. 1. Cost reduction and market access For my infrastructure and webshop, I rely on: Linux (server and hosting environment) WordPress and WooCommerce as the e-commerce platform A combination of open-source plugins and paid commercial extensions This stack: structurally lowers fixed operational costs avoids dependency on closed SaaS platforms enables scalability without high upfront investment Without open-source software, many SMEs would simply not have a viable digital business model. 2. Hybrid ecosystems work in practice My use case demonstrates that open source works best in hybrid ecosystems: an open core complemented by commercial plugins, extensions and paid support combined with custom development where needed This hybrid model supports both innovation and sustainability. EU policy should enable and strengthen this model, rather than framing open source and commercial software as opposites. 3. Community contribution is valuable but fragile In addition to using open source, I contribute back where possible: reporting bugs developing my own code and tooling, mainly focused on data analysis and SQL processing of large datasets These contributions are typically made alongside regular work and without structural support. As a result, many core projects remain vulnerable, particularly in areas such as: long-term maintenance security documentation This highlights a clear role for the EU in supporting sustainable maintenance, not only innovation projects. 4. Open source as infrastructure, not a niche Open source is often discussed as a niche or ideological choice, while in reality it functions as: foundational digital infrastructure a critical layer for web services, cloud, data and cybersecurity a prerequisite for digital autonomy EU policy should therefore treat open source as strategic digital infrastructure, comparable to energy or telecommunications. 5. Where EU action can make a difference Based on practical experience, EU-level impact would be strongest through: public procurement rules that require open standards measures to prevent vendor lock-in structural support for maintenance and security of core open-source components explicit recognition of open source as a valid and scalable foundation for SME digitalisation Conclusion Open source is not an end in itself, but a strategic and economic instrument. In practice, it directly contributes to competitiveness, innovation and cost efficiency within the European internal market.
text_raw: Practical experience with open source in an SME context I make extensive use of open-source software in a commercial setting, primarily for running an online webshop and related data analysis. My experience is that open source is not an ideological alternative, but a key economic enabler that allows small and medium-sized enterprises to remain competitive. 1. Cost reduction and market access For my infrastructure and webshop, I rely on: Linux (server and hosting environment) WordPress and WooCommerce as the e-commerce platform A combination of open-source plugins and paid commercial extensions This stack: structurally lowers fixed operational costs avoids dependency on closed SaaS platforms enables scalability without high upfront investment Without open-source software, many SMEs would simply not have a viable digital business model. 2. Hybrid ecosystems work in practice My use case demonstrates that open source works best in hybrid ecosystems: an open core complemented by commercial plugins, extensions and paid support combined with custom development where needed This hybrid model supports both innovation and sustainability. EU policy should enable and strengthen this model, rather than framing open source and commercial software as opposites. 3. Community contribution is valuable but fragile In addition to using open source, I contribute back where possible: reporting bugs developing my own code and tooling, mainly focused on data analysis and SQL processing of large datasets These contributions are typically made alongside regular work and without structural support. As a result, many core projects remain vulnerable, particularly in areas such as: long-term maintenance security documentation This highlights a clear role for the EU in supporting sustainable maintenance, not only innovation projects. 4. Open source as infrastructure, not a niche Open source is often discussed as a niche or ideological choice, while in reality it functions as: foundational digital infrastructure a critical layer for web services, cloud, data and cybersecurity a prerequisite for digital autonomy EU policy should therefore treat open source as strategic digital infrastructure, comparable to energy or telecommunications. 5. Where EU action can make a difference Based on practical experience, EU-level impact would be strongest through: public procurement rules that require open standards measures to prevent vendor lock-in structural support for maintenance and security of core open-source components explicit recognition of open source as a valid and scalable foundation for SME digitalisation Conclusion Open source is not an end in itself, but a strategic and economic instrument. In practice, it directly contributes to competitiveness, innovation and cost efficiency within the European internal market.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: This contribution is submitted by a European open source e-learning and knowledge infrastructure provider with long-standing experience in the adoption, governance and sustainability of open source platforms across public administrations, universities, research organisations and defence-related contexts. From an operational perspective, European open source software demonstrates its greatest strategic value when it is treated not merely as a technical solution, but as a shared digital knowledge infrastructure. In practice, open source platforms are increasingly used not only for learning, but also for content and document management, institutional communication, governance, auditability and long-term preservation of organisational knowledge. Experience from large-scale public sector deployments and complex EU-funded projects shows that well-governed open source platforms can significantly reduce dependency on fragmented proprietary tools, improve continuity of knowledge beyond project lifecycles, and strengthen security, interoperability and regulatory compliance. These aspects are particularly relevant in domains such as education, research, public administration and defence, where institutional memory and long-term access to knowledge are critical. Despite these benefits, several structural challenges continue to limit the impact of European open source software. A persistent misconception equates open source with zero cost, leading to underinvestment in maintenance, security and governance. Public procurement frameworks often remain focused on short-term products rather than long-term infrastructures, disadvantaging open source solutions designed to evolve over time. In addition, European open source ecosystems face economic and regulatory asymmetries when competing with large non-European vendors. While policy discussions increasingly address data and cloud sovereignty, knowledge sovereigntythe ability to control, preserve and reuse institutional knowledge over timeremains insufficiently recognised. As a result, valuable knowledge generated within EU-funded projects is often fragmented or lost after project completion due to the absence of shared, sustainable knowledge infrastructures. To address these challenges, the European Union could consider: - supporting structural funding mechanisms for the long-term maintenance, security and evolution of strategic open source infrastructures; - adopting procurement policies that recognise lifecycle costs, governance models, interoperability and community-based development; - promoting guided adoption of open source solutions within public administrations where functionally equivalent; - investing in skills development and professionalisation related to open source software and governance; - strengthening institutional communication around mature European open source ecosystems and their role in digital sovereignty. European open source software is a key enabler of digital sovereignty, institutional resilience and democratic governance. Treating open source platforms as shared European knowledge infrastructuressustained by active communities and professional ecosystemscan reduce dependency on external vendors and contribute to a more sustainable and autonomous digital future for Europe. A more detailed contribution is provided in the attached document.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The application of the Emissions Trading System (ETS) to incinerators or landfills is an innovative measure for municipalities to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and promote more sustainable behaviour. This system, which has already been successfully adopted in various industrial sectors, offers many environmental and economic advantages in a sector such as waste, where there is considerable scope for improvement and the possibility of spreading innovations, with numerous patents waiting to be industrialised, but which are currently hampered by contracts of more than 10 years, which oblige municipalities to keep obsolete incineration plants in place, especially in temperate countries such as Italy, where most of the installations built do not have a district heating network. We therefore propose to allocate ETS revenues paid by incineration plants to local authorities, to finance projects to improve separate collection and to promote industrial chains for the recovery of secondary raw materials.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Given that all or almost all applications are based on a layer of persistence, I believe that facilitating the adoption of free software would be easier if there were economic incentives for companies to equip themselves at home with specific staff to manage databases. For example, a company purchasing an ERP system or even a small manager can often choose which database to use. How is the choice made? Obviously, the company wants to limit the costs and therefore chooses the database at the lowest cost of the licences and the maintenance contract. We know that free software has no licensing costs but maintenance. However, this is still offered by a few companies and is likely to have higher costs than the counterpart with proprietary software. One solution would be to employ as an employee, or as an external consultant, a person or persons at a low cost as a result of the incentive. Or to train new or existing staff through low-cost training courses. This would be added to the balance plate, in favour of choosing the free software. Indirectly increasing employment and knowledge dissemination on free databases such as PostgreSQL, MariaDB. Another area on which the Commission could act is that of meetings between developers and users. These meetings strengthen communities, connect people, show what’s new. In some cases they are organised directly by companies, in others there is some volunteer who does so, including by looking for corporate sponsors. These are never events that require huge budgets, and volunteers often take care of everything, so that what would be needed is a method of publicising them, paying for classrooms and perhaps even a network transmission service, and reimbursing the expenses of speakers. Unfortunately, we are far behind on this front, especially in Italy: events such as pgDay are missing from all those who took part. Others, such as LinuxDay, could be better organised. Another point of view is to push the adoption of free software from above: the Commission could finance nations, regions or even individual municipalities so that public administrations truly adopt free software. This is not a simple thing: in the past, with large European cities (see below a retrospective examination of how Munich City Council adopted the free software from around 2006 to 2017), attempts have been made to abandon Microsoft Office’s products, but this attempt has found many critics among both managers and users, who are normally opposed to any change, until after a few years Microsoft has offered some kind of agreement, such as granting free licences for two years, and the municipality has recovered. The municipality boasted the savings, even if temporary, Microsoft initially sent us back a little, and everything went back as before. To avoid falling into these pies, the Commission could set up centres of expertise with qualified staff familiar with free software and the domain of public administration, and these centres should facilitate the adoption of free software in public administrations. (Examples of abandoned roads in Turin: the (first?) decision of the Municipality of Turin to adopt the free software in 2017; the note from the CSI Piemonte of 2019 settling the free software and providing it for ascertainment and availability; a second decision to adopt the free software in Turin in November 2022). initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The Association for Financial Markets in Europe (AFME) and the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) welcome the opportunity to respond to the European Commissions public consultation on the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and on the Market Stability Reserve (MSR). In this submission, we have chosen to focus on a select number of questions, particularly those concerning carbon removals (Section 5.7.1) and linking with other carbon markets (Section 5.8.3), where we believe our members can provide meaningful input. Given the character limitations of the online questionnaire, we are using this accompanying document to expand on the rationale behind the positions expressed in our response.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: As a regular supplier to public authorities, and as a citizen who wishes to see public funds used efficiently and transparently, I would like to offer the following suggestions for consideration in the ongoing revision of EU public procurement rules. a) Track record of suppliers Evaluation criteria should include a transparent assessment of bidders past performance, including adherence to budget, timeliness, and quality of delivery. Contractors who have repeatedly exceeded costs or failed to meet deadlines should have this reflected in their evaluation, while companies with a proven record of efficiency and quality should be positively recognized. An EU-wide database of contractor performance, shared among Member States, would strengthen accountability and make it easier for reliable firms to participate in cross-border projects. This approach would promote value for money and better lifecycle cost management in public spending. b) Common EU guide and digital platform A harmonized EU-wide framework, building on existing eProcurement and eForms systems, could provide clear guidance on suitable evaluation criteria for different procurement procedures. A shared digital platform would make procurement data transparent, comparable, and accessible across the Union. Such a platform would simplify access for suppliers, reduce administrative burden, and foster fair competition in the single market. c) Capacity-based limits on concurrent projects To ensure quality and timely delivery, limits could be defined on how many projects a contractor can undertake at the same time. These limits should be flexible and proportionate to the companys financial strength, production capacity, and staffing levels. This would prevent overextension while still allowing efficient, well-resourced companies to compete fairly. d) Financial transparency and creditworthiness Financial transparency should be enhanced to make evaluations more efficient and objective. Linking credit ratings or financial health indicators to the level of bank guarantees required could help mitigate risk. However, such measures should be applied proportionately to avoid disadvantaging small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), ensuring that responsible smaller suppliers remain competitive. Conclusion By improving supplier evaluation criteria, encouraging transparency, and harmonizing practices across the EU, public procurement can become more efficient, accountable, and competitive ensuring better value for public money and greater trust in how it is spent.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The European Electronic Recyclers Association (EERA) supports the European Commission's initiative to modify the EU's public procurement rules. Public procurement is a key tool for achieving EU goals like environmental sustainability, re-use, the circular economy, innovation, and strategic autonomy. For the electronic recycling industry, procurement rules greatly affect market access, investment choices, and the quality and reliability of waste management, re-use and recycling services across the Union. EERA emphasises that public procurement should implement the waste hierarchy by prioritising preparation for reuse of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) and waste EEE (WEEE) as the default option. Recycling should be applied when reuse is not technically or economically viable. Professional assessment, repair, and refurbishment deliver the most resource efficiency and should therefore be treated as a core objective of the revised procurement framework. PLEASE see our full response in the attached document.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: The growing desire for simplification and acceleration of policymaking processes must not compromise standards of good governance (accountability, transparency, participation, ev-idence-based, legal clarity) as these are indispensable to enhance democratic legitimacy, quality of policy design, and effective implementation. The review of the Better Regulation Guidelines should consider two aspects that improve public participation and streamline decision making, without compromising standards of good governance. First, public participation could be advanced by defining and applying best-practice standards, providing a comprehensive review of the existing EU participation ecosys-tem, and establish a single digital point of access to existing mechanism. This would help transform the currently fragmented landscape into a more coherent system. Second, expedited procedures could streamline and speed-up policymaking processes in specific, clearly defined cases (technical, lower-level modifications & high urgency), respecting minimum standards of transparency, evidence consideration, participation and accountability. These points are further elaborated and substantiated in the attached document.

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: Tarkett is a global leader in flooring and sports surface solutions, committed to sustainable innovation, circular economy practices, and environmental performance. With a strong industrial presence in Europe, Tarkett actively contributes to standardisation processes aligned with its industrial activities and sustainability goals. Tarkett supports a targeted revision of the Regulation (EU) No. 1025/2012 on European standardisation to: - Preserve a system based on transparency, inclusiveness, and innovation that has proven to be robust, market oriented and efficient. - Strengthen European industrial competitiveness - Accelerate the ecological and circular transition. Key principles of the revision should : - Maintain and reinforce the industry-led model, ensuring strong involvement of companies in standard development. - Recognise the central role of harmonised standards (hEN) as the main tool for presumption of products' conformity, especially refering to construction products harmonised regulation for example. - Ensure legal clarity regarding the role of standards and Common Specifications (CS), which must remain exceptional. On Common Specifications (CS), Tarkett shares the view that CS should: - Be used only in cases of proven failure of the standardisation process. - Respect strict criteria for transparency, inclusiveness, and coherence. - Not undermine the role of European Standardisation Organisations (ESOs) or create legal uncertainty. On Sustainability and Innovation, the EU should promote international alignment while advancing its environmental and social values. Moreover, the Revised framework should ensure sustainability related innovations, supporting effective transition to circular economy and climate neutrality are rapidly addressed in standards/ harmonised regulations revision process. Today, incomplete or inconsistent normative frameworks, rigid legislative framework not allowing the integration of innovative approaches, and lack of legal basis for experts/consultants/verifyers for decision making, may block or at least delay market uptake of more sustainable products, discourage investment in circular solutions, and as a result weaken European industry competitiveness. The attached document provides real case of road blocks faced by Tarkett and its business partner for scaling a low-carbon innovation.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: [Answer 3] What concrete measures and actions may be taken at EU level to support the development and growth of the EU open-source sector...? Europe can convert open source from a promise into a strategic pillar but it needs concrete, implementable EUlevel actions. Key measures are straightforward: define clear eligibility and sovereignty criteria; embed those criteria across funding programmes; provide public developer infrastructure; fund longterm maintenance; operationalise automated quality and security checks; and reform procurement to reward portability, governance and sustained contribution. Here are three concrete, practical examples: Public infrastructure + attestations: an EUhosted registry with CI runners and SBOM storage where projects publish machinereadable attestations (passing tests, dependency scans, license checks). Initiatives like openCode aim in this direction; security best practices and tooling can draw on work by the OpenSSF to operationalise supplychain hygiene. Procurement pilots with enforceable maintenance commitments: adopt OSB Alliancestyle procurement guidance and run pilot tenders that require explicit maintenance line items, contribution obligations, and scoring for community governance and portability. This makes public money = public code operational and financially sustainable. Stewardship + secure baselines: create a stewardship fund that pays core maintainers for critical libraries and commissions independent audits; pair this with secure baseline projects such as national secure container initiatives (examples exist at regional level) to provide readytodeploy, reviewed templates for government use. Legal & businessmodel clarity EUPL and practical safeguards: The EUPL 1.2 already provides a solid European licensing foundation that balances copyright protection and reuse. It should remain a central legal instrument, but it can be usefully complemented with practical guidance to protect viable monetisation paths for OSS maintainers. A key risk today is that mature components are commercialised by third parties as SaaS without upstream subscription or support agreements, which can starve maintainers and incentivise a drift back to proprietary models (examples in the ecosystem include licencechanges around major database and middleware projects). Additionally, legal constraints in some jurisdictions (for example, limits on formal transfer of copyright under national law) make constructs like public domain problematic in practice and can introduce unexpected liability or rights gaps. To reduce these risks, the EUPL framework could be extended with bestpractice guidance for mixedlicense stacks, recommended contractual patterns for SaaS provisioning, and clarity on mechanisms that preserve maintainers revenue while guaranteeing public reuse. Small wins matter: publiccode.yml is a case in point a simple, standardised format for software catalogue entries that is easy to implement and yields immediate discoverability and comparability benefits. Practical, interoperable standards, a minimal but trusted EU infrastructure, and a funding model that pays for maintenance will together reduce risk, lower total cost of ownership, and give European industry the footing to outcompete alternatives through superior, sovereign solutions.
text_raw: [Answer 3] What concrete measures and actions may be taken at EU level to support the development and growth of the EU open-source sector...? Europe can convert open source from a promise into a strategic pillar but it needs concrete, implementable EUlevel actions. Key measures are straightforward: define clear eligibility and sovereignty criteria; embed those criteria across funding programmes; provide public developer infrastructure; fund longterm maintenance; operationalise automated quality and security checks; and reform procurement to reward portability, governance and sustained contribution. Here are three concrete, practical examples: Public infrastructure + attestations: an EUhosted registry with CI runners and SBOM storage where projects publish machinereadable attestations (passing tests, dependency scans, license checks). Initiatives like openCode aim in this direction; security best practices and tooling can draw on work by the OpenSSF to operationalise supplychain hygiene. Procurement pilots with enforceable maintenance commitments: adopt OSB Alliancestyle procurement guidance and run pilot tenders that require explicit maintenance line items, contribution obligations, and scoring for community governance and portability. This makes public money = public code operational and financially sustainable. Stewardship + secure baselines: create a stewardship fund that pays core maintainers for critical libraries and commissions independent audits; pair this with secure baseline projects such as national secure container initiatives (examples exist at regional level) to provide readytodeploy, reviewed templates for government use. initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: This submission responds to the Call for Evidence on the Communication on Better Regulation from the perspective of an independent civil society organisation working at the intersection of business, human rights, and sustainable development, with practical experience in EU and national implementation contexts, including Central and Eastern Europe. PIHRBs core message is that the effectiveness of the Better Regulation framework should be assessed not only in terms of efficiency and administrative burden, but also in terms of its capacity to deliver legally robust, implementable, and socially legitimate EU legislation. In areas affecting fundamental rights, environmental protection, and corporate accountability, procedural quality is a precondition for effective outcomes. The submission highlights three interlinked priorities: 1. Urgency and evidence-based policymaking While recognising the need for swift EU action in certain circumstances, PIHRB stresses that the absence of clear criteria for invoking urgency risks undermining legal certainty, predictability, and trust in EU decision-making. Clear definitions, safeguards, and proportionality requirements would allow the Commission to act swiftly while preserving the integrity of the Better Regulation framework and reducing institutional and litigation risks. 2. Holistic and balanced stakeholder participation Meaningful consultation is essential to democratic legitimacy and policy quality. Without practical measures to address structural participation gaps including differences in resources, language access, and administrative capacity consultation processes risk systematically privileging well-resourced actors. This, in turn, weakens the evidentiary base of EU legislation and may negatively affect implementation at national level. 3. Simplification focused on clarity and enforceability, not deregulation From an implementation perspective, overly abstract or under-specified EU obligations tend to shift interpretative and enforcement burdens to Member States, courts, and civil society. Simplicity by design should therefore prioritise legal clarity, guidance, and enforceability, while preserving minimum harmonisation, preventing regulatory regression, and strengthening enforcement frameworks. Overall, PIHRBs contribution aims to support the Commission in strengthening Better Regulation as a tool for resilient, rights-respecting, and future-proof EU lawmaking particularly in politically sensitive and high-impact policy areas.
