initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I'm an individual developing open-source software. My most popular product is raylib, a C graphics library intended to draw graphics in mostly any kind of screen, used for videogames development, tools, web applications, embedded devices, automotive displays, academia simulation/research software and more. raylib has become very popular in the last 12 years, thanks to its portability, wide range of applications supported and its free and open source license. For 12 years I've been working on raylib almost on a daily basis, while managing to live by getting some contract work and working as a University teacher. For some periods of time I've also been able to work full-time on this project, living from my savings. I'm sharing this feedback from my personal experience working as a solo-developer on a popular open-source project. I defined the main challenged I found as the THREE-Ms [MMM]: - Maintenance: Despite trying to keep the project as simple and small as possible, to date it has grown to support +20 platforms and has been adapted to +50 programming languages. Maintaining such a big project is not easy. Considering it is an open-source project, contributors are expected to help with its maintenance but many times contributions are limited to adding some new features or solving some issues. There is rarely a long-term commitment to the project or even code-ownership from many contributors. It implies work and burden for the maintainer of the project. - Motivation: Working on a project solo for so long has an impact on motivation. Even being very passionate about the project, the fact of solving issues for months with barely any retribution could feel like a full-time unpaid job. Additionally, working as a solo developer on a big project can be very isolating, generating an impact on motivation. - Money: I think this is the biggest problem with open-source. It's effectively very difficult to make money or even build a business around it, especially for a solo-developer. Even in the case of getting some money thanks to user-donations or some institutional fund like NGI0, there many other concerns: * Uncertainty: It's uncertain how long those donations could last or if there will be further funds. * Legal entity: Some countries could not have a legal regulation that considers donations/funds received for open-source development, specially operating as an individual. * Life stability: It is very difficult to plan for the future as an individual developing open source software. For all those reasons, I think many amazing open-source projects developed by individuals get abandoned after some years. Possible actions to improve that situation: 1. Create a Directory of Certified/Verified/European-supported/recommended open-source software. Projects included in the Directory can be directly supported by the European Union, with 5-year maintenance agreements to ensure stability and long-term commitment. So, institutions willing to use OSS can check available verified options and implement them with some guarantee that the software will be supported by a certain time with fixes and improvements. 2. Create an European OSS Association to work as an umbrella and support for small/individual OSS development groups, specially on legal multi-country matters. Also as a meeting point of open-source developers, to connect with others and share knowledge and opportunities. The association could also provide some physical space for small teams or individuals wanting to work in an office, even sharing equipment. 3. Connect with per-country governments to use already available resources. Many countries/cities have institutions to help startups/entrepreneurs but, in my experience, those institutions could not be aware of the impact and potential of open-source software. Consequently many of their services are not target to open-source developers. Thank you very much for this opportunity to provide feedback!

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: The Syndicat National des Accouveurs (National Association of Accouveurs (SNA)) brings together all breeding and access companies in France covering all the poultry species produced: broilers, laying hens, turkeys, palmipeds (ducks and geese), guinea fowls, game trees (pheasants, partridges, mallard ducks), pigeons and quails. The NAS requests that the possibility of using non-organic chicks of less than 3 days old be maintained. The NAS considers that it is not possible to generalise the use of organic chicks throughout Europe. This would pose a huge number of problems and end organic poultry production in several countries. Restriction of access to an outdoor range for breeding animals is contrary to a number of existing regulations or health measures (e.g.: Salmonella regulation in France and biosecurity measures). Parental farms are specific holdings and breeders produce parents of the same strain for different productions: organic, fermier, Label Rouge, open-air, given the too limited volumes of organic production, including for countries producing large organic poultry/eggs such as France, it is not possible to dedicate parent farms to the production of organic breeding animals. Similarly, it is not possible to dedicate hatcheries to organic production. Limiting breeding holdings and hatcheries to a few operators will lead to the end of the local supply and will significantly increase the time taken to transport chicks. Finally, there is a very wide diversity in Europe of slow-growing strains currently used in the production of organic flesh poultry and strains suitable for outdoor farming for organic egg production. Imposing the use of organic chicks will prevent this diversity in view of the small number of parent herds dedicated to organic production.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: ICTU is concerned about a resurfacing EU deregulation agenda, as clearly reflected in the 2024-2029 Political Guidelines of the Von der Leyen II Commission[i] and mainstreamed across the Mission Letters[ii] of all incoming Commissioners. A narrative on EU legislation as burdensome is reductionist and represents a threat to the European project. This EU bashing undermines trust in institutions, feeding Euroscepticism and the far right. A deregulatory approach to competitiveness has no place in a Europe where protection gaps and violations of workers, trade union and social rights persist, as a result of non-compliance, lax enforcement, impunity and regulatory loopholes. The push for reduced administrative, reporting and regulatory costs and burdens prioritises business interests over democracy and societal needs. The general interest is not only composed of businesses, but also workers and trade unions, consumers, citizens and communities, regions, public authorities and the environment. By almost exclusively focussing on the quantification of short-term economic costs for compliance and adjustments, the Better Regulation Agenda ignores positive social, environmental and economic impacts of quality legislation as a long-term investment for society. As much as the 2020 pandemic demonstrated the need for regulation to act in the public interest and manage a crisis, likewise the 2008 financial crisis illustrated how failure to regulate can even cause a crisis, and in what ways deregulation and marketisation can have devastating consequences for the European social fabric and public services. The EU cannot afford a return to austerity. Over the years of European integration, the cost of non-Europe has proven to be much greater than the regulatory investments for a more Social Europe.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Lottery The establishment of the priority of lottery in the EU public procurement directives is an effective approach to increasing competition and participation opportunities for SMEs as well as avoiding subcontracting chains in public contracts. This is also in line with a European Parliament resolution of 9 September 2025, in which it supported, by a large majority, the inclusion of the priority of the award of lots in the public procurement directives. Article 46 of Directive 2014/24/EU allows contracting authorities to award a contract in several lots, but not in the sense that public contracts must in principle be divided into lots. So far, therefore, the award of lots has not been effective. The provision in German public procurement law (Section 97(4) GWB) could serve as a model. Under that provision, services are to be divided up in quantity (sub-lots) and awarded separately by type or field of expertise (specialist lots). Several lots may be awarded jointly for economic or technical reasons. With regard to public works contracts in particular, the priority given to the award of lots is of particular importance, as the construction sector in Europe is strongly dominated by small and medium-sized enterprises. The Federal Court of Auditors also concludes in its analysis of the economic viability of construction measures that the expectations of an overall award are often not met. In addition, the use of subcontractors regularly leads to additional costs, which the Federal Court of Auditors puts on average at around 10 %, but in some cases up to more than 20 %. Where the contracting authority also awards a works contract in lots, it shall entrust the execution of various works to a number of specialised contractors. The specialist companies regularly carry out the vast majority of the work in their trade as their own services, thereby limiting subcontracting chains. On the other hand, restrictions on subcontracting must not be included in EU public procurement rules. In Germany, as in other EU Member States, the construction industry is subject to a prohibition on the hiring-out of workers. Capacity constraints can therefore only be mitigated by the use of subcontractors. By means of variants, contracting entities can procure works in a more innovative, rational and cost-saving manner, as this significantly increases the range of companies offering them and the range of execution options. To date, contracting authorities must explicitly allow variants in the procurement documents (Article 45(1) of Directive 2014/24/EU). Variant solutions should therefore in principle be allowed, unless the contracting authority explicitly excludes them (opt-out). Such a provision should therefore be included in the EU public procurement directives.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Feedback on the initiative for a targeted amendment to Regulation (EU) 2018/848. Südwestdeutsche Salzwerke AG strongly recommends that sea salt and other salts for food and feed be completely removed from Annex I of Regulation (EU) 2018/848. Salt is an inorganic and mineral compound that is typically extracted from underground salt and brine deposits or seawater. It is not an organic or agricultural product. For years, Südwestdeutsche Salzwerke AG (Heilbronn, Germany), one of Europe's largest salt producers, has been following regulatory developments regarding the inclusion of salt in the scope of the regulation and the unsuccessful efforts to draft production rules, for which no consensus has been reached. In 2023, the European Parliament rejected the legislative act on the proposal for production rules by a clear majority. The proposed rules were discriminatory - they would have excluded over 90% of all European salt producers from the possibility of applying for certification. From the perspective of Südwestdeutsche Salzwerke AG, the European Commission should make it a priority in its targeted amendment of Regulation (EU) 2018/848 to completely remove sea salt and other salts for food and feed from Annex I to clarify the status of salt with regard to the organic regulation and framework.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: OpenForum Europe welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commissions Call for Evidence on the forthcoming Communication Towards European open digital ecosystems. We welcome the growing recognition across EU institutions of the strategic importance of open source as a foundational component of Europes digital infrastructure, as well as the emerging convergence of policy initiatives that place software ecosystems at the centre of Europes digital and industrial strategy. The Communication is timely and well aligned with recent and forthcoming policy instruments, including in cloud, data and AI, and signals a more coherent approach to addressing Europes structural dependencies in critical technology domains. The Commissions acknowledgement that Europe combines strong developer communities with persistent barriers to scaling, sustainability and market integration reflects a shared understanding of the challenge. This submission seeks to build on this positive momentum by offering a structured analysis of open source as an under-leveraged policy tool and by setting out policy recommendations aimed at strengthening competitiveness, resilience, and long-term European capability. Please see the attached document for OpenForum Europe's detailed feedback.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: Alliance for AI, IoT and Edge Continuum Innovation is providing the following feedback: One of the key tasks of the Cybersecurity Act (CA) is to create the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework ECCF. In this context, we agree that the Cybersecurity Certification should be based on technical standards, which can be measurable and assessable with measurable and EU Member States aligned cybersecurity criteria. The Cybersecurity Certification driven by non-technical risk factors is not based on technical standards and therefore, is not assessable with measurable and EU Member States aligned cybersecurity criteria. In this regard, we propose not to include the non-technical Cybersecurity Certification requirements in the CSA revision.

feedback_text: Hello, I am writing to you on two aspects: I am surprised by the revision of the Regulation, only after 3 years. Stability is needed. — we must defend and even develop organic farming. This is about the health of Europeans, because we are all eakers, consumers. Organic farming also protects the diversity of life. This is absolutely essential, especially since day after day, ticience tells us that we are just a link in a chain of life that is made up of thousands of plant and animal species on which we depend. It is therefore essential that the Regulation be consistent with the protection of life. There is a need for organic farming to maintain a link with the soil, that GMOs remain prohibited, that there is no use of synthetic products against pests and weeds, that livestock farms remain at self-manageable sizes, etc. We count on you. Cordially

This is absolutely essential, especially since day after day, ticience tells us that we are just a link in a chain of life that is made up of thousands of plant and animal species on which we depend. It is therefore essential that the Regulation be consistent with the protection of life. There is a need for organic farming to maintain a link with the soil, that GMOs remain prohibited, that there is no use of synthetic products against pests and weeds, that livestock farms remain at self-manageable sizes, etc. We count on you. Cordially

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As a scientist specializing in bioimaging and computational biology, I strongly support the European Open Digital Ecosystems initiative and urge explicit prioritization of open-source software (OSS), open data, and open standards as foundational research infrastructure. OSS constitutes 70 to 90% of modern software, yet lacks sustainable funding, while proprietary alternatives fragment research efforts and hinder reproducibility.  In data-intensive fields like microscopy image analysis, critical OSS tools enable reproducible pipelines and cross-disciplinary reuse across academia and industry. However, these assets remain fragile, maintained by underfunded volunteers despite continent-wide dependency. The initiative correctly recognizes that research programs alone prove insufficient for scaling OSS solutions. EU research grants must mandate OSS and open data (FAIR principles: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) as requirements, with explicit evaluation in peer review. Successful models exist: Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund has deployed 24.6M since 2022 supporting critical open infrastructure; NumFOCUS awards ~200k annually for scientific software sustainability; and the European Open Science Cloud partnership mobilizes 500M EU funding plus 500M in-kind contributions. Funding must allocate dedicated budget lines (15 to 20% of project funds) for maintenance, documentation, security audits, and governance preventing "grant cliff" abandonment when projects end. Three Strategic Gaps Requiring Action: 1) Sustainability: Research tools serving Europe-wide communities need long-term funding beyond project cycles, supporting foundations, cooperatives, and small teams. 2) Interoperability: OSS and open data must integrate with European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) and sectoral data spaces via funded standards, enabling cumulative scientific progress. 3) Security and Resilience: Shared OSS dependencies underpinning institutional stacks require coordinated funding for supply-chain audits and incident response critical for digital sovereignty. To unlock science's potential for European competitiveness: Recognize research software and open data as strategic infrastructure, equivalent to physical laboratories. Require OSI-approved licensing and FAIR principles for all EU-funded digital R&I outputs. Launch dedicated funding streams within Horizon Europe successors or EDICs for long-term maintenance of research-origin OSS and data assets. Align public procurement to favour research-originating open-source, avoiding vendor lock-in. By embedding open-source and open-data as binding funding requirements with dedicated maintenance support, this strategy transforms open ecosystems from aspiration into enduring research infrastructure, strengthening Europe's technological sovereignty, scientific competitiveness, and innovation equity.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The Swedish Library Association is a non-profit and partisan unbound member and interest organisation that brings together and works for all types of libraries. We drive opinion and create a debate to ensure that libraries are best placed to be a force for change in society. We call for changes in legislation and resource reinforcements for libraries, we are working on reading promotion and cultural heritage issues, and we are working to ensure that research is accessible to all. All to strengthen democratic society. The libraries are the hub of academic publication. They support researchers in publishing matters, are responsible for subscriptions to magazines and sometimes run higher education institutions’ own publishers. As a result, they often become, involuntary, copyright experts without the support of neither lawyers nor institutions. In order for researchers from higher education institutions in which our members are active to operate effectively and freely, in an open access environment, we see the need for harmonised EU legislation on secondary publishing rights. Therefore, when such a provision is inserted in the ERA Regulation, it needs to require that a final version of an article can be published immediately in a scientific resort, without an embargo. This is crucial to ensure that timely research needs to benefit society quickly. We also see it as an obstacle today that there is insufficient metadata on research, such as standardised models of metadata on copyright on articles. This should also be included in the ERA Regulation. Another obstacle for HEI libraries today is access to available e-learning materials and materials in languages other than English, including Swedish. Today, educational material producers have a disproportionate power over access to digital teaching material at Swedish higher education institutions and effectively block students from digital learning materials in Swedish. The right to learning materials regardless of language should therefore be strengthened by the ERA Regulation.
topic: Employment and social affairs, Research and innovation, Single market

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: Contribution of the SVOL to the European Commission’s public consultation on the draft Delegated Directive amending Annexes V and VI to Directive (EU) 2018/2001 (RED): The association of owners of municipal, private and church forests in the Czech Republic (SVOL) considers the energy use of forest biomass to be an essential part of sustainable forest management, particularly in Central Europe, which has been significantly affected by climate change and widespread disturbances of forest stands in recent years. The energy use of biomass is a key outlet for extractive residues, salvage wood and non-materially used wood. These revenues are then used to finance forest restoration, stabilisation of stands and adaptation measures to climate change. Any reduction in these outlets therefore has a direct impact on the ability of forest owners to respond actively to climate and environmental challenges. 1. Setting the transport distances of biomass The proposed methodology includes extreme categories of transport distances of biomass (e.g. above 10 000 km) that do not correspond to the realities of forestry in the Czech Republic, where biomass is predominantly used in regional supply chains, typically up to 200 km. The inclusion of the following extreme categories in one methodological table: it leads to an overall stricter and more conservative benchmark setting, increases the risk that even locally sourced biomass ceases to meet the minimum emission savings requirements due to a combination of other factors, weakens the location principle, which is key for the sustainable use of biomass. We therefore propose the removal of categories of transport distances above 10 000 km and the introduction of a finer distinction between local biomass supply (e.g. < 250 km, 250 500 km). 2. Biomass moisture reference values Proposed biomass moisture reference values (e.g. 30 % for wood chips) are highly dependent on climatic conditions, seasonality and harvesting patterns. Without a clear definition that these are exclusively methodological assumptions, there is a risk of their interpretation as a mandatory requirement, which could lead to the exclusion of commonly available forest biomass from RED. 3. CSTOR Correction Factor Introduction of the CSTOR (biomass storage) correction factor is an additional administrative element, without demonstrating that biomass storage is a systematic problem requiring a new control mechanism. Unclear conditions for its application may lead to divergent interpretations in controls and increase the administrative burden on forest owners. The proposed revision of the methodology as presented may limit the availability of energy use of forest biomass and thus indirectly reduce the ability of forest owners to regenerate forests and adapt forest ecosystems to ongoing climate change. We therefore ask the European Commission to take our observations into account.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Syensqo supports of the initiative to revise the Public Procurement Directives (Directives 2014/23/EU, 2014/24/EU and 20214/25/EU), and of the ongoing efforts by the Commission to review its industrial and trade policy tools to provide greater economic and supply chain security for European companies. initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As an architect/systems and networks engineer in a local authority, we are in the process of migrating from our VMware virtual infrastructure to Proxmox VE 9.1. In all objectivity, Proxmox offers agility and leadership comparable to that of VMware, while offering almost unlimited compatibility with open standards. Storage performance and resilience: The change from a VMware datastore on traditional hardware RAID bay to a ZFS storage (RAID-Z) on Proxmox has reduced the duration of snapshots from several minutes to a few seconds. Unlike the proprietary hardware RAID, RAID-Z offers detection and correction of silent data errors (bit rot) through checksums, flexibility in volume management without fixed configuration constraints. Containerisation and sovereignty: The native integration of the LXC containers into Proxmox allows us to doubt the way to directly host our Docker and Podman workloads in a light and secure manner. This hybrid approach offers several strategic advantages: — Almost instantaneous start of application services as soon as the hypervisor is energised – Optimised insulation between application containers and the host, enhancing security – Reducing complexity: no longer need full VMS for each containerised service – Manage resources thanks to the fine management of CPU quotas/hot provisionable container memory This architecture allows us to reconcile digital sovereignty, performance and operational agility, while significantly reducing our proprietary licensing costs.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: "1. () What are the main barriers that hamper (i) adoption and maintenance of high-quality and secure open source; and (ii) sustainable contributions to opensource communities?" Many open-source projects start as passion projects. Taking a huge risk, taking up a loan, and founding a company the traditional is a big leap to make, since most developers who start their passion project can continue to work on it on the side without taking such risks. Funding them through easily accessible grants allows them to start with a smaller step in that direction. At the same time, knowing that the EU Commission actively supports open-source, encourages citizens and public procurement to give open-source adoption a try. "3. What concrete measures and actions may be taken at EU level to support the development and growth of the EU open-source sector and contribute to the EUs technological sovereignty and cybersecurity agenda?" Keep funding the NGI initiative. A very effective way of supporting the open-source ecosystem are easily accessible grants, such as the ones offered by NLnet and the Next Generation Internet programs. Most projects already exist, but are done on the side by their developers. With easily accessible grants, such developers can work on their projects without needing a separate full-time job to pay the bills and their projects can grow into something larger. Provide financial incentives for public procurement to take a look at open solutions. Government offices are often struck for money and have little to no wiggle room to try something new. There are plenty of small and large companies who can install and maintain all kinds of open tools and infrastructure, it frequently just seems less risky to many procurement officers to go with huge overseas companies like Microsoft because everyone else does and nobody will blame the procurement officers if something goes wrong. "4. What technology areas should be prioritised and why?" The health sector. It is very hard for open-source to gain foothold in the health sector, because of extremely high upfront cost of developing and certifying many health sector related products. On the other side, proprietary health sector products are often extremely expensive, unsecure, and restrictive; A common example would be CT machines which need a completely outdated and insecure Windows XP computer because they stopped receiving support from the manufacturer decades ago. Open-source technology would allow third parties to support such machines even when the original manufacturer stopped doing so. "2. What is the added value of open source for the public and private sectors?" Open-source solutions are typically superior to proprietary ones, as they are cheaper, put more power into the hands of the consumer, and are resilient to monopoly, price hikes, and companies going under. As a consumer, I can use the smart home sector as an example: My Panasonic air conditioning unit offers the ability to control it via Wi-Fi adaptors which cost 180 total for both my units. At the same time, they can only be controlled by Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa or Panasonic's smartphone app; all of which require an internet connection to Panasonic's servers. This system is relatively expensive, is fully dependent on Panasonic providing updates and not shutting their servers down, and doesn't even integrate with most smart home systems (including Home Assistant, which I am using). Alternatively, there is an open-hardware and open-software solution available, which costs less than 50 for both units, doesn't require an internet connection, receives permanent security and feature updates through the open-source ecosystem it is based on, and integrates perfectly into my smart home system. Disclaimer: I am an open-source developer, and my passion project (an open-source social-VR platform) grew into employment for me, with a jump-start from NLnet and Next Generation Internet.

feedback_text: Hello, I think it is essential to develop Open Source in Europe because this is the only way to successfully emancipate ourselves from the US GAFAM and the Chinese. This would allow free, serious alternatives to be developed that do not depend on a country’s policy. Without this initiative, we risk being dependent on and suffering fake news and other cultural and societal influences from the United States, which are taking a worrying authoritarian turn. I am myself in the IT world and trying to find these alternatives today, which sometimes exist but which require investment and support from us all. You Kevin DOYET A citizen concerned

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Feedback Initiative “Towards European Open Digital Ecosystems” 1. Strengths and Weaknesses of the EU Open Source Sector The European Open Source Sector has clear strengths: a mature technological base, active development communities and broad adoption in critical layers of digital infrastructure (virtualisation, networks, storage, cybersecurity). These solutions allow for a high degree of transparency, auditability and control, which are essential for technological sovereignty and cyber resilience. However, structural weaknesses remain. These include difficulties in scaling up European projects against dominant global suppliers, limited access to public procurement due to proprietary technology-oriented tender specifications, and the absence of homogeneous certification and reference frameworks for open source solutions. In addition, the regulatory and compliance burden can be disproportionate for SMEs and emerging projects, affecting their competitiveness. 2. Open source added value for the public and private sectors Open source brings differential value in both the public and private sectors by significantly reducing the total cost of ownership, eliminating vendor lock-in dependencies and allowing for greater budgetary predictability. The possibility to audit the source code improves risk management and response to cybersecurity vulnerabilities. In the area of public administrations, open source solutions facilitate the deployment of sovereign infrastructure, both on-premises and hybrid, aligned with the principles of data protection, continuity of service and supply chain control. In the private sector, they enable SMEs to access advanced technologies without excessive economic barriers, fostering innovation and competitiveness. 3. Measures and actions to be taken by the EU The European Union should take concrete measures to strengthen the European open source ecosystem, including: Incorporate technology neutrality criteria in public procurement, prioritising open source solutions where technically feasible and sustainable. Establish European certification and reference programmes for open source solutions, especially in critical infrastructure and cybersecurity. Support sustainable business models based on European partners’ services, support and ecosystems. Reduce regulatory barriers for SMEs and emerging projects, avoiding disproportionate compliance requirements. Promote training and capacity-building programmes in strategic open source technologies for public administrations and businesses. 4. Technology areas that should be prioritised To move towards digital sovereignty, the EU should prioritise key layers of digital infrastructure, including: Virtualisation and infrastructure management. Cloud and sovereign hybrid environments. Backups, disaster recovery and high availability. Edge computing and distributed systems. Cybersecurity embedded in the infrastructure. Strengthening these layers makes it possible to build trustworthy digital services on a controlled and transparent technological basis. 5. Sectors with the greatest impact on competitiveness and cyber resilience More use of open source can significantly increase competitiveness and cyber resilience in strategic sectors such as: Public administrations. Health. Education Industry and advanced manufacturing. Energy and critical infrastructure. Telecommunications. In these areas, open, auditable infrastructures without external dependencies are key to ensuring operational continuity, data protection and European technological autonomy.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The use of free software is not only vital to avoid technological lock-in but is also an important source of innovation. Access to previous developments to learn and build on them has a multiplier effect similar to that seen in the world of science, also based on building on prior knowledge and the ability to challenge and improve it. Nowadays, scientific or supercomputing infrastructure is in fact already based on free or open software. However, the benefits of these developments are not always shared with those who bear the costs of them (development of functionality, maintenance, correction of faults, etc.) and this is why the European Union should articulate mechanisms to promote more equitable development. Technological foundations in Germany or the Netherlands, financially supporting improvements in strategic free software, can be interesting models. Open software-based procurement should also be favoured.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am a user, contributor, and developer of open source software. I am also a mother hoping to see my children growing up digitally literate, and not captured early into the strategic incompetence that proprietary software giants such as Microsoft, Apple, Meta, Alphabet, and others build into their platforms. It is my sincere hope that the political turmoil results in a more sovereign European digital ecosystem freed largely from US-based political influence. Cory Doctorow recently gave a talk at the Chaos Computer Club conference: https://media.ccc.de/v/39c3-a-post-american-enshittification-resistant-internet In it, he argues for the EU to repeal anti-circumvention law, which I find a worthwhile argument to repeat here. In practice, anti-circumvention laws grant US-based software vendors de facto control over interoperability, repair, modification, and competition. Dominant firms can use DRM to block rivals without relying on copyright infringement claims, reinforcing market concentration. Open source software values interoperability to build compatible, secure, and innovative alternatives, often requiring reverse engineering. Anti-circumvention laws criminalize these activities even when no copyright is violated, making it legally risky or impossible for European open source projects to compete with proprietary US platforms. By removing legal barriers to interoperability, the EU would enable homegrown open source alternatives in cloud computing, operating systems, productivity software, and infrastructure. This reduces dependency on US vendors, lowers public procurement costs, and improves long-term technological sovereignty. Anti-circumvention laws function as an industrial policy favoring incumbent US software firms. Repealing them would unlock competition, strengthen European open source ecosystems, and restore balance between copyright protection and the public interestwithout legalizing infringement. The EU should consider repeal as a strategic step toward digital sovereignty and a more competitive, innovative and open software market. At the same time, I want to urge political decision makers to keep education and digital literacy in their mind. While we need open source software, we also need a digitally literate population that uses them competently. Dominant software platforms often display what superficially looks like better user friendliness, but in practice often means forbidding the user to understand basic computing principles. This is a severe threat to digital competence that hinders adoption of open source alternatives, which often provide users with more technical freedom at the expense of appearing less user friendly in comparison. This can be remedied by educating students and adults alike in the marketing strategies behind market domination and user lock-in to proprietary platforms, early introduction of open source alternatives (e.g., mandating the use of FOSS operating systems and tools in schools, in public administration), and digital education that targets understanding computing principles instead of teaching use of specific proprietary tools.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: EU is one of the biggest hubs of open source software. And today, for EU to sustain its relevancy in the global market, it should invest in the projects and organizations that produce public domain software and services. This will easily resonate with the community, as we already have the root here. Namely, KDE project, ActivityPub servers (like Mastodon, Pleroma, PeerTube), software registries and git remotes (e.g. SourceHut), NextCloud, Lemmy, etc. The public money should be spent on public software, instead of funneling money into Microsoft, Google and other service providers. Funding FOSS projects is very important for preserving democracy and leveling the playing field for all participants. These programs keep the internet alive, prevents it from becoming an oligopoly market, and provides the means to a democratic environment. Most importantly, privacy and anonymity - which are the basis for free speech - can only achieved by publicly auditable code.

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: ERF is the Spanish association of waste recyclers, and we have been involved in standardisation since 2010. Please find out the following: In view of the upcoming revision of the European Standardisation Regulation 1025/2012, the following elements should be considered: It is essential for the European Commission to speed up the deadlines for citing standards in the Official Journal of the European Union: — European industry, in collaboration with other interest groups, is investing heavily in the development of standards to ensure that they support the evolution of the state of art and the delay by the Commission in its revision and subsequent citation in the OJEU leads to a loss of competitiveness and legal uncertainty. European leadership in international standardisation developed in international bodies ISO and IEC should be strengthened: — Production chains are becoming increasingly global and European industry is investing heavily in the development of international standards to align requirements in the internal market with those of destination markets outside the EU. The European regulatory model based on the New Legislative Framework (NLF), complemented by Market Surveillance, has proven to be an ideal way to foster innovation and the placing of safe products in the Internal Market. The certainty and flexibility it brings to the industry have been key to making it a success model. It is imperative to preserve the criteria and pillars of the NLF while maintaining the fact that the rules must reflect the state of the art and should be an option to comply with the legislation. — Any departure from this regulatory model, such as the so-called Common Specifications, should be properly justified and treated exceptionally in order to avoid creating legal uncertainty in the market. Common Specifications should therefore only be considered as a last resort and if: * Harmonised standards are not feasible within a reasonable period of time. * An open, transparent and inclusive consultation process is in place. * These specifications are temporary in nature and will be replaced by a future harmonised standard. * Are aligned with international standards and their technical developments. It would be desirable for the European Commission to put in place the necessary mechanisms to ensure coordination in its regulatory initiatives and standardisation tasks, particularly in the areas of digitalisation and sustainability. The European standardisation regulation should ensure the sustainability of the European standardisation system: — This is particularly relevant in the context established by the EU Court of Justice ruling obliging the European Commission to facilitate access to standards requested from it, which represents a serious risk to the funding model of European and international standardisation. Mechanisms to protect intellectual property from standards need to be articulated.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: 1. What are the strengths and weaknesses of the EU open-source sector? What are the main barriers that hamper (i) adoption and maintenance of high-quality and secure open source; and (ii) sustainable contributions to open-source communities? For individuals: - There is no one go-to place for supporting open-source software. Everything is fragmented, every project manages their finances separately, every tool authors ask for money. It's hard to decide what to fund and how much. - Many consumer devices comes with an operating system preinstalled. On computers it's usually MS Windows. Users are unaware of included costs and are often unable to choose alternatives. For companies: American giants are de-facto standard in some industries. Office from Microsoft, Photoshop from Adobe, AutoCAD from Autodesk and so on. Breaking those monopolies requires funding into developing viable alternatives and bulding new companies, that would sell licenses and support. 2. What is the added value of open source for the public and private sectors? Please provide concrete examples, including the factors (such as cost, risk, lock-in, security, innovation, among others) that are most important to assess the added value. The biggest added value is avoiding vendor lock-in. A clear example was not so long ago, when Microsoft blocked access to its services from International Criminal Court after issuing arrest warrants against Benjamin Netanyahu. With open-source software hosted on their own servers it would not be possible. 3. What concrete measures and actions may be taken at EU level to support the development and growth of the EU open-source sector and contribute to the EUs technological sovereignty and cybersecurity agenda? At the level of public institutions: - Declare a list of open file formats for use (meaning files like .doc should not be allowed to be used), with a specific end date. - Provide funding for companies willing to implement or expand existing tools, with a clear requirement: "public money = public code". For consumer products: - Strengthen the ability to choose preinstalled operating systems and applications. Require, that the cost of included licenses to be explicitly visible. Give users freedom to choose alternatives. - Fund development of alternatives. Again "public money = public code". 4. What technology areas should be prioritised and why? - Operating systems - Office suites - Collaboration platforms Why: (I already wrote it above) breaking monopolies and vendor lock-in. 5. In what sectors could an increased use of open source lead to increased competitiveness and cyber resilience? Everywhere, where are monopolies and vendor lock-ins.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: (For the BDF, pending validation of the declaration made in the Transparency Register) The Belgian Disability Forum asbl (BDF) brings together 18 Belgian organisations representing persons with disabilities. The BDF represents Belgian persons with disabilities at European and supranational level. https://bdf.belgium.be/fr/%C3%A0-propos-de-nous.html The BDF’s priorities are as follows: The BDF calls for the development of an effective strategy with tangible effects for citizens with disabilities, their families and their carers. Accessibility first: The BDF emphasises accessibility as a vector of rights, autonomy and equality. It calls on the European Union (EU) to develop concrete actions in the following areas: the creation of a European Accessibility Agency, legislation on the affordability of assistive technologies, ensuring their free movement within the EU, the revision of the Air Travel Regulation, the revision of legislation or new legislation ensuring the accessibility of infrastructure and vehicles, the revision of the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) to include accessibility requirements, legislation on accessible labelling and binding universal accessibility planning dici 2050, legislation on equal access to essential services ensuring their access through digital AND non-digital means, legislation planning general accessibility on the basis of Design Universel to be achieved in 2050, with binding intermediate steps. Freedom of movement and economic inclusion: The BDF calls for a directive guaranteeing full freedom of movement for persons with disabilities. He also calls for disability mainstreaming in the European Semester process to guide the use of EU funds. Quality of life and employment: The BDF calls for measures to ensure resources to enable people with disabilities to live in dignity, the employment and skills guarantee for people with disabilities, along the lines of the Youth Guarantee, a directive regulating the use of algorithms in employment, recommendations to the Member States on the possible combination of disability benefits and other forms of income, a study on disability-related additional costs, the use of EU funds to support families of people with disabilities, the strengthening of reasonable accommodation and the development of a European package on the choice of place to live. Equality and non-discrimination: The BDF calls for binding measures against forced sterilisation of women and girls with disabilities, recognition of sign languages, awareness-raising campaigns on women with disabilities, a recommendation on autonomy and supported decision-making, a study on hate speech and the integration of people with disabilities into migration and asylum policy. Rights of persons with disabilities globally: The BDF recommends a European action plan for people with disabilities in the European Union’s external action, inclusive climate action, integration into humanitarian aid and the protection of people with disabilities in armed conflicts. Implementation and governance: The BDF emphasises the analysis of the implementation of the strategy, the clear definition of responsibilities, the improvement of representation in the Disability Platform, the establishment of an annual forum on the rights of persons with disabilities and the handistreaming of disability issues in all European strategies.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Associazione Italiana Internet Provider (AIIP) is the oldest and largest association of ICT operators in Italy and has been representing for 30 years more than 60 Italian SME operators, who have been and still are committed to create an open, distributed, and independent telecom network, including by setting up colocation data centres and offering and/or using them to provide housing, hosting, and cloud and edge services. AIIP welcomes the opportunity to submit its contribution to the European Open Digital Ecosystems initiative. Open-source and sovereign data management represent two fundamental pillars for building a more open, efficient, and independent Internet, enabling innovation, trust, and resilience across digital services and infrastructures. By promoting open digital infrastructure and reducing reliance on non-EU proprietary technologies, this initiative can help secure a more sovereign and secure, digital future for citizens, businesses and public administrations alike. Please find our contribution in the attached document.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Since 2022, the City of Paris has been implementing its third Sustainable Food Plan, with a target of 100 % sustainable food in collective catering by 2027, including 75 % organic products and 50 % local food, produced and processed within a radius of 250 km. With 28.5 million meals served each year in 1300 municipal establishments, the Parisian mass catering service is a major lever for transforming food systems. The share of sustainable food thus increased from 49 % in 2022 to almost 60 % in 2024 (in terms of value excluding tax on purchases), reflecting the ability of public procurement to guide agricultural and agri-food practices. Beyond Paris, these objectives contribute to the structuring of resilient local sectors. The Parisian mass catering service potentially involves 11 100 hectares of agricultural land, and 45 000 hectares on the scale of AgriParis Seine, confirming the strategic role of public procurement as a lasting outlet for producers and as a tool for territorial food sovereignty, in a context of a decline in the number of farms. Established in July 2023, AgriParis Seine embodies territorial cooperation at the level of the Seine basin. It brings together nine public authorities and operators representing 8.4 million inhabitants, 100 million annual meals and 175 000 hectares of agricultural land. Acting as a trusted third party, the structure of local sectors is consolidated as part of the ‘Seine feedicière’ demonstrator, through strategic tools (observatory, matchmaking, marketing approaches), support for processing units adapted to mass catering and the development of shared low-carbon logistics solutions. This approach aims to establish sustainable reciprocity between urban and rural areas, securing outlets and contributing to better agricultural remuneration. However, despite these dynamics, the achievement of the targets faces persistent structural bottlenecks. In 2024, the share of local products reached 17.8 %, far from the target of 50 %. The constraints of the public procurement framework, the complexity of procedures, the weight of volumes, the difficulties of integrating territorial criteria and the lack of knowledge between buyers and producers continue to favour dominant operators. While matchmaking initiatives improve the readability of markets, they are still insufficient to remove these lock-ins. Proposals 1. Securing the European framework for location criteria: legally integrating location criteria into public procurement and recognising the strategic specificity of food supply in order to facilitate the use of short and local supply chains, strengthen the food supply capacity of regions, secure sustainable agricultural outlets and more profitable local sectors. 2. Open up the free choice of procedure for 50 % of the annual volume of purchases of foodstuffs To allow the free choice of procedure for up to 50 % of the annual volume of purchases of foodstuffs, on the basis of a public food strategy drawn up in the context of multi-stakeholder governance. This would make it possible to adapt procedures to the state of local supply, and to strengthen the bargaining power of public buyers. 3. Allow the economic and social externalities of purchasing to be taken into account Legally acknowledge that public purchasing is a structuring economic act, allowing positive economic and social externalities to be taken into account, particularly in the area of agriculture and food, in order to align European public procurement with the objectives of sustainability and territorial cohesion.

However, despite these dynamics, the achievement of the targets faces persistent structural bottlenecks. In 2024, the share of local products reached 17.8 %, far from the target of 50 %. The constraints of the public procurement framework, the complexity of procedures, the weight of volumes, the difficulties of integrating territorial criteria and the lack of knowledge between buyers and producers continue to favour dominant operators. While matchmaking initiatives improve the readability of markets, they are still insufficient to remove these lock-ins. Proposals 1. Securing the European framework for location criteria: legally integrating location criteria into public procurement and recognising the strategic specificity of food supply in order to facilitate the use of short and local supply chains, strengthen the food supply capacity of regions, secure sustainable agricultural outlets and more profitable local sectors. 2. Open up the free choice of procedure for 50 % of the annual volume of purchases of foodstuffs To allow the free choice of procedure for up to 50 % of the annual volume of purchases of foodstuffs, on the basis of a public food strategy drawn up in the context of multi-stakeholder governance. This would make it possible to adapt procedures to the state of local supply, and to strengthen the bargaining power of public buyers. 3. Allow the economic and social externalities of purchasing to be taken into account Legally acknowledge that public purchasing is a structuring economic act, allowing positive economic and social externalities to be taken into account, particularly in the area of agriculture and food, in order to align European public procurement with the objectives of sustainability and territorial cohesion.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I welcome the initiative for European Open Digital Ecosystems. However, as a citizen concerned with public safety, infrastructure resilience, and the rule of law in an era of hybrid warfare and shifting geopolitical alliances, I urge the Commission to anchor this ecosystem in strict reciprocity and pragmatism. True digital sovereignty cannot exist if governments act as 'Free Riders' on the work of open-source communities, nor if we remain dependent on volatile external powers for our digital foundations. Therefore, I strongly suggest the following pillars for a robust strategy: 1. Mandatory Reciprocity & Upstream Obligation The usage of Open Source Software by public institutions must come with a mandatory 'upstream obligation.' If public funds rely on existing open libraries, kernels, or frameworks, there must be a facilitating contribution in returnbe it financial support, developer hours for maintenance, or infrastructure. 'Public Money, Public Code' should not just be about transparency, but about the active maintenance of the digital foundation that guarantees our collective security. This directly supports the EUs goal of Strategic Autonomy by securing the supply chain against neglect and external sabotage. 2. A Decentralised, Scalable 'EuroStack' The Commission should facilitate the creation of a decentralised 'EuroStack': a software and infrastructure standard that is scalable from supranational institutions down to SMEs and the individual self-hosting citizen, prioritising 'local-first' principles and edge processing. This stack must ensure individual data sovereignty independent of hyperscalers, European or not. Furthermore, given the reality of fluctuating Atlantic policies, the EU should not act in isolation but form a 'Coalition of the Willing' with stable, like-minded OECD partnerssuch as Japan, South Korea, Switzerland, Ukraine, and Canadato create a global standard for democratic, open technology. 3. Extended Right to Repair & Legalised Reverse Engineering In alignment with the objectives of the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), reverse engineering must be explicitly legalised in cases where manufacturers fail to provide a proper End-of-Life exit strategy for IoT or networking devices. Abandoned hardware is not just e-waste; it is a potential beachhead for hostile botnets and state-sponsored cyberattacks. If a vendor abandons the security of a device, the community must have the legal right to inspect the firmware and maintain the 'maintenance contract' themselves. Additionally, manufacturers who commercially free-ride on open-source software without honoring the license terms (e.g., GPL violations) must face active penalties. 4. A European Legal Structure for Open Source To support this, the EU needs a dedicated legal vehicle tailored for open-source foundations, akin to the Societas Europaea, serving as an EU-wide alternative to the Belgian AIBSL or U.S. 501(c)(3). This will streamline cross-border collaboration and funding, essential for retaining talent and innovation within the Single Market. These steps are vital to maintain the open-source ecosystem as a high-trust community through a zero-trust approach to source code auditability. This strategy provides Europe with the opportunity to develop a healthy, sovereign tech culture that upholds the international rule of law and European values.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The Dutch taskforce negative emissions started in 2022, we now bring together 40+ innovators, scale-up, established companies, NGOs, knowledge institutions and public-private carbon/CC(U)S partnerships in our shared aim to develop the policy, regulatory and market conditions for a mature direct carbon removals ecosystem in the Netherlands and the European Union. We call for the inclusion of direct carbon removal in the EU ETS-system to help kickstart an EU-carbon economy leading to negative emissions. Thereby creating an additional pathway to not only reach emission reduction goals in 2030, 2040 and beyond- but also offer a solution for hard-to-abate industries (such as steel on the production side or concrete on the demand side) and strategic carbon management in unsure geopolitical times. It contributes to all three key themes of the Competitiveness Report: innovation, decarbonization and security. Like the Commission proposal for 2040 says: future architecture should include incentives to build a business case for domestic permanent carbon removals, e.g. through the ETS directive. All CDR technologies, whether is it BECCUS, DAC, DOC or mineralization, benefit from such an inclusion: being part of the ETS-mechanism brings value to CDR that helps bridge the gap to the use of grey CO2. To ensure that CDR doesnt replace, but adds to, emission reduction, we can imagine the use of safeguards -like different regimes for grey / green CO2. After all, we aim for negative emissions which eventually include offsetting historical emissions to stay within the planetary bounds for 1,5-2 degrees Celsius. We also support the Dutch governments non-paper on the consideration of carbon removal procurement through revenues from the EU ETS (https://open.overheid.nl/documenten/7e2c29d7-47f7-4a23-a879-9b2ef128316f/file). We believe it is crucial to have uncertainty-reducing instruments in the market-creation phase. EU ETS and procurement both contribute to this through predictability for investment decisions, while leaving ample space for the market to develop itself.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I think that Call for Evidence correctly identifies some of the key challenges for Open Source adoption. In my opinion there is one major omission, which is thinking in terms of companies, industry, etc., while forgetting that open-source started as a bottom-up movement of people who believed in the core principle of freedom. I think this individual component of open-source is crucial. At the moment, majority of high-profile open source software is being developed by tech giants: companies such as Google, Facebook or Huawei are among the top contributors to Linux kernel. This has to be kept in check by ensuring that the maintainers have funding that does not come directly from tech giants. Otherwise, a conflict of interest arises. As someone who has worked on open-source, the biggest challenge is precisely that lack of funding for individual contributors. I have to balance full-time professional work and working on open-source in my free time. This is a major challenge and the amount of work open source work I am able to carry out is small. I would love to become a full-time open source developer, but that requires access to funding that is available either to individuals or to projects, not just to companies. The primary added value of open source is safety and security. Currently, we don't know what majority of our "intelligent" devices do. What exactly are the cars doing? Are they spying? Are they faking low NOx emissions in laboratory testing? The 2015 Volkswagen emission scandal would not have happened if car makers were obligated to publish all the code that runs their cars. There are documented cases of IoT devices having either poorly implemented security, exposing their users to cybersecurity threats, or outright actively spying on their users and sending data to producer (and also having killswitches that allow the producer to disable the device remotely). Requiring that all such devices have their code published (+ means of verifying that the published code is actually running on the device) would increase public safety end help enforce law compliance. Adoption of open-source varies by sector. One area where I think it is particularly bad is the public administration. This, in my opinion, results from the fact that people working administrative jobs are not trained IT specialists and are thus unaware that avoiding a vendor lock-in is possible. A common example are public tenders for software delivery that only require deploying the software, but not transfer of ownership of its source code. Another interesting case is the gaming industry and, more broadly, the digital art industry. Many programs used by artists for 2D and 3D art have no viable open-source alternatives. If EU funded creation of software that could replace closed-source programs, such as Adobe, that would help a whole branch of the EU industry.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Aviation accounts for 2% of global CO emissions and 3.84% of total EU-wide emissions. It contributes 851 billion to European GDP (5%) and generates a strong multiplier effect on job creation and GDP per capita, +1.6% and +0.5%, respectively, for every 10% increase in connectivity. Yet, aviation has been included in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) since 2012, as the first transport mode. It is also the first industry to implement a global scheme to limit CO emissions at the international level through CORSIA (Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation), which is effective since 2021 and will become mandatory for most ICAO members in 2027. Unilateral EU climate policies have significantly increased the cost of flying to, from, and within the EU, impacting connectivity across the region. According to the Draghi Report on EU Competitiveness, decarbonising aviation could cost up to 61 billion annually between 2031 and 2050. The document attached outlines A4Es general position on the topics referenced in the call for evidence for the evaluation and impact assessment of the EU ETS for aviation. It emphasizes the need for a policy framework in which the ETS can balance environmental ambition with the need to maintain competitiveness, connectivity, and socioeconomic contributions of European airlines.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Proposal to amend Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/464 splitting the minimum indoor area and the minimum outdoor area for rearing piglets into two sections of weight to date: In accordance with Annex I to Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/464, after weaning up to 35 kg live weight piglets have at least 0.6 m² of indoor area and 0.4 m² of outdoor surface available. This means that piglets weighing between 10 and 12 kg immediately after weaning up to 35 kg live weight have the same space requirements. Proposal: We propose to divide the minimum indoor and outdoor surface for weaned piglets into two weight sections. The limit should be in the middle of the rearing phase. Justification: The aim of organic pig farming is to keep the animals as close as possible to their specific needs. This includes, in particular, designing the house in such a way that the pigs, or in this case piglets, can divide the existing area into functional areas. In addition to a littered area for resting, the pigs need a fres area. Both areas are generally kept clean by pigs, so that they are not boiled. In addition to the two areas mentioned above, pigs need to have a faeces area, ideally in the outer surface. Piglets need sufficient space to experience their species-specific behaviours. However, if too much space is offered, the animals no longer separate the functional areas in the desired form. In many cases, piglets boil and hare, especially at the beginning of rearing in the house, and do not look for the open-air area for this purpose, as they still have sufficient clean lying area. However, this significantly affects hygiene and worsens the indoor climate, as harmful gases without active ventilation cannot escape from the house. Harmful gases can even accumulate in conjunction with openings to the open air and frequent air exchange, which is particularly undesirable in the winter half-year. If only 10-12 kg of very small piglets are available at the beginning, it makes it easier for them to perform functional separation. At the end of rearing, however, the minimum levels mentioned above prove to be appropriate. In particular, the thermoregulation of piglets is still very limited immediately after weaning. In view of the large volume of houses, they are not able to heat the house with their per-heat. Although the small-climate areas generally available to piglets use a heating system (e.g. lid heating panels) to heat the rest area, the feeding area may be cold during the winter months despite wall insulation. If the piglets accept the outer surface as a faeces area, this large area carries a risk of high emissions, as the small piglets are not initially able to accept part of the outer fan as a clean outdoor lying area and the rest as the excretion surface. For practical reasons, it is also possible to divide the two weight ranges in the middle between after weaning and up to 35 kg, i.e. around 20 to 22 kg. The smaller piglets would be provided with half of the existing area, i.e. 0.3 m² in the house and 0.2 m² in the open-air area.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: * Article 41 provides that the prior involvement of tenderers in the preparation of the procurement must not distort competition. Although the principle is understandable, it remains unclear which participation is actually considered problematic and what are the objective criteria for assessing whether there is a potential distortion of competition. We recommend that the Commission develops a guide or additional definition in a directive that clarifies this. * The third subparagraph of Article 42(1) allows the contracting authority to specify in the technical specifications whether a transfer of intellectual property rights (IP) is required. In practice, however, the formulation of IP terms and conditions is one of the most complex aspects, especially in innovation-oriented procurement. Contracting authorities often lack a clear understanding of when and to what extent the preservation, sharing or transfer of IP rights is justified, how it affects: (I) the motivation of companies to innovate, (ii) the country’s long-term independence from suppliers (vendor lock-in), (iii) and compliance with EU State aid rules. We recommend that the Commission complete Article 42 or prepare a guide containing a typology of IP terms and conditions with explanations describing: (I) for which types of procurement it is reasonable to require a full transfer of IP rights (e.g. standard solutions, off-the-shelf software); (II) when it is reasonable to use limited licences or shared rights to balance the public interest against the commercial interest of the provider; or (iii) when it is justified to fully preserve IP rights at the provider, e.g. for the purpose of commercialisation or further development in the private sector. * While the Directive allows and encourages the division of contracts into lots in order to facilitate access for small and medium-sized enterprises and innovative market participants, the current regulation remains too weak and is mainly applied formally rather than in substance in practice. Contracting authorities often justify the non-division by a general sentence (project integrity, efficiency), the actual content of which is often not controlled by the review bodies. Consequently, the objective of the splitting rules, which was to open up the market to new and smaller players, is not achieved. We recommend that the Commission formulate the splitting as a default obligation, with the exception of the possibility not to implement it, only if: (I) it would be objectively impractical (e.g. market structure, technical inseparability); (II) this would jeopardise the achievement of the procurement objectives (e.g. excessive increase in coordination risk). To require a more specific and substantive justification where the contract is not subdivided into lots, including a technical, legal or economic justification with consideration of alternatives. Article 68 allows, but does not require, contracting authorities to take into account the full life-cycle costs of a product or service. This means that many buyers still rely solely on the purchase price, ignoring the real costs that may arise from energy consumption, maintenance, waste management or environmental impacts. The result may be an inefficient use of taxpayers’ money, as purchases are made cheaply but with long-term extra costs. We recommend that the Commission make life-cycle costing mandatory at least for certain types of procurement, such as construction works and infrastructure (e.g. buildings, roads, energy facilities), energy- and resource-intensive products (e.g. IT equipment, means of transport), services with a high environmental footprint (e.g. waste management, catering, washing, cleaning). The public sector does not buy the process, but the outcome. However, the purchase price does not reflect the total cost. Low cost may mean high later costs. Life-cycle costing would foster innovation and environmental friendliness by highlighting companies whose solutions offer better value in the long term.

Consequently, the objective of the splitting rules, which was to open up the market to new and smaller players, is not achieved. We recommend that the Commission formulate the splitting as a default obligation, with the exception of the possibility not to implement it, only if: (I) it would be objectively impractical (e.g. market structure, technical inseparability); (II) this would jeopardise the achievement of the procurement objectives (e.g. excessive increase in coordination risk). To require a more specific and substantive justification where the contract is not subdivided into lots, including a technical, legal or economic justification with consideration of alternatives. Article 68 allows, but does not require, contracting authorities to take into account the full life-cycle costs of a product or service. This means that many buyers still rely solely on the purchase price, ignoring the real costs that may arise from energy consumption, maintenance, waste management or environmental impacts. The result may be an inefficient use of taxpayers’ money, as purchases are made cheaply but with long-term extra costs. We recommend that the Commission make life-cycle costing mandatory at least for certain types of procurement, such as construction works and infrastructure (e.g. buildings, roads, energy facilities), energy- and resource-intensive products (e.g. IT equipment, means of transport), services with a high environmental footprint (e.g. waste management, catering, washing, cleaning). The public sector does not buy the process, but the outcome. However, the purchase price does not reflect the total cost. Low cost may mean high later costs. Life-cycle costing would foster innovation and environmental friendliness by highlighting companies whose solutions offer better value in the long term.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: My feedback is given in the attachment both in polish and english. RESPONSE TO THE INITIATIVE TO ENHANCE THE STRATEGY FOR PEOPLE WITH DISABILITIES TO 2030 In connection with the announcement of measures aimed at enhance the Strategy for the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, I would like to make some comments on the right of access to the labour market and self-employment. According to the draft Joint Employment Report 2026 , the employment gap for persons with disabilities of working age in many Member States is not narrowing, and in some Member States it is widening (see graph on p. 56). In my opinion, the employment gap is a failure (imperfection) in the internal market that can be overcome at European Union level by liberalising the rules on state aid for creating jobs for people with disabilities, maintaining jobs for people with disabilities and benefits for employees with disabilities in the workplace. The same should apply to persons with disabilities who wish to become professionally active through self-employment. I therefore propose extending and liberalising the catalogue of permitted state aid in accordance with Commission Regulation (EU) No 651/2025 of 17 June 2014 declaring certain categories of aid compatible with the internal market in application of Articles 107 and 108 of the Treaty ("GBER Regulation"). The GBER Regulation considers aid for subsidising wages for the recruitment of persons with disabilities (Article 32), aid for subsidising the wages of persons with disabilities (Article 33) and certain costs related to the employment of persons with disabilities to be compatible with the internal market. The catalogue of public aid for benefits for employees with disabilities distributed through employers funds may be extended to include, for example, health counselling and medical and rehabilitation care, or career counselling and further training to improve the skills of persons with disabilities in the labour market. Aid for the self-employment of persons with disabilities, including for starting up and continuing such activities, and public aid for improving the qualifications of self-employed persons with disabilities should be considered compatible with the internal market. Aid that removes barriers to entry and continued participation in the competitive market for self-employed persons with disabilities, such as subsidies for compulsory social security or health insurance contributions and contributions to professional organizations to which membership is compulsory under the law of the Member State, should be considered compatible with the internal market. Next, tax incentives related to the employment and self-employment of persons with disabilities should be considered. This should apply to both indirect harmonised taxes within the European Union and incentives for preferential taxation of the work of persons with disabilities. The strategy should clearly indicate that the employment of persons with disabilities is supported both in the open market and in sheltered employment, provided that it does not lead to social isolation. Finally, the European Union should encourage Member States to enact and interpret domestic law on the employment and self-employment of persons with disabilities in such a way that it does not create or constitute an administrative barrier to the creation and maintenance of jobs for persons with disabilities, both in the open market and in sheltered employment.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: I believe (and many of my suffering concur) that the concepts and status and duration of guardianship and guardianship must be called into question, as must all, without exception, legal, psychological and medical measures. It is these provisions of good conscience and legalised exclusion that cause harm. It only helps physically non-able-bodied people who are severely unable to be autonomous and independent. This is far from what is usually referred to as ‘disabled’. Not all of us are without potential, life and capacity, we have strength and energy to resell and a brains. It is up to you to take control, train and lead: this is fully in line with the spirit and letter of this global initiative.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: We have been an association of organic producers since 1991 in the Pays de la Loire region. We are made up of 1500 producers. Our Board is very attentive to the image and confidence of consumers in our products. For example, if there is a lack of fodder to feed our animals, we facilitate relations between members so that they can turn to each other’s hay. We do not want consumers to think that animals eat anything other than food produced on our farms. The same applies to cereals for our poultry or pigs. For animals to be removed from the outside, this is not always easy depending on access to pastures and the weather. May if it is necessary to make efforts to distinguish itself well from labels or brands which also promote ‘grazing milk or hay milk’. We can do no less than labels! It would therefore be catastrophic if the organic regulation were too light. We urge you to open the revision of the Organic Regulation only on the issue of imported products, as required by the EU Court of Justice.
