initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: IDM facilities perform a public health function. They are not responsible for the nature of the inputs and cannot reduce their GHG emissions. They manage the waste produced by households on their own. The inclusion of UEVs in the ETS leads to a distortion of the incineration sector compared to the waste sector, while it avoids GHG emissions through the recovery of waste heat and emits less GHG per tonne treated than the storage of waste. Inclusion in the ETS will significantly increase the treatment of municipal waste; financial burden borne by public services and by citizens through their household waste tax and the price of heat for homes/businesses connected to an EVU. Financially penalising installations involved in material recovery and the avoidance of residual waste, in accordance with the hierarchy of management methods (Waste Framework Directory 2008) makes no economic and environmental sense. The methods used at national level (CITEPA), for example for drawing up demissions reports dating back to the European level, show their limits when comparing them with the removal measures carried out on site. This is the case, for example, for methane emissions from waste storage facilities. Estimation methods without calculation should take into account the following parameters, in order to refine the results: composition and quantity of incoming waste; technologies implemented in the installation; quantity of gas captured; seasonal products: Inclusion in the EU ETS would risk undermining the investment capacities of the entire value chain of the waste sector, at a time when these are more than necessary to achieve the ambitious emission reduction measures planned by the sector as part of the waste sector. To prevent emissions from waste management, it would be more effective to strengthen design and recycling requirements, as well as incentive or taxation mechanisms to place non-recyclable, unnecessary or short-lived products or packaging on the market. Two studies (see below): “Decarbonisation objective: reconciling carbon reduction and waste treatment in Europe” (FNADE, 2024). “Inclusion of Waste Energy Valuation Units in the European carbon market” (Starting, 2024). These two French studies shed light on the impact of this inclusion on the cost of waste management: By 2050 a 34 % increase in the cost of waste management in UVE (+ 75/tdéchets) is expected; applied to the heat sale price, this would contribute to increases of + 68/MWh for users in the case of a combination of TGAP (French taxation) and EU ETS. In the case of a non-TGAP scenario, the increase by 2050 is predicted by 28 % of the total waste management in UVE (+ 60/tdéchets), which, when applied to the heat sale price, would contribute to increases of + 54/MWh. This additional cost to households would have a marginal impact on the reduction of GHG emissions in the non-hazardous waste sector, and thus a disproportionate marginal cost of CO2 reduction (> 930/tCO2e in 2028 and up to > 4300/tCO2e in 2050).

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: “free as a bird not like a beer” Europe’s contribution to open source software & hardware must be made for all open source players worldwide because it is Europeans who will gain thanks to the general interest. By providing financial support to the foundations and players of the free world, the positive impact will be felt not only by Europeans but by the world as a whole, which will also be able to make free use of this software for worldwide postage: this seems to me to be in line with the values of Europe. Limiting itself to the European ecosystem would only bring a limit and completely sterile and costly wheel reinventions. If software exists free of charge and it is valid: resources must be provided to strengthen them, improve them and enable them to move beyond closed-source solutions. As regards potential foreign interference, it is insolvent without requiring an open and published code. Another role for Europe seems to me to be to support the security audit of these published codes to ensure that they are good & secure. Accusations against each other, with no visibility on the code/material, will always only be unfounded allegations, impossible to prove, always at risk. Nothing will prevent a one-day closed system, for a QUELQUONQUE reason to trigger a malicious code whether on 5G antennas, inverters, firewalls, telephones, cars, etc. Any closed equipment will always be at risk of having a deliberate or unintentional fault. This includes ALL European & foreign companies because making this distinction is in any case impossible in the long term (buy-out/shareholder/interests/...) For very sensitive services, it could require that the code be compiled at the user’s premises and that this source code not be published but that in no case be just the binary that is just installed. In cloud time, it could also require the autonomous operation of certain systems. When military vessels are seen under Windows 95 and trade secrets are invoked: it’s to cry! The Linux core and its design would be less secure than these closed systems: let’s be serious (and in addition you can audit the code to check)! Open source software is much more adaptive in a digital ecosystem because if a link is missing, it can be developed and then published to be taken over by others. With closed software, you remain dependent on the publisher’s goodwill. An interesting edge effect of open source software is that it also contributes against planned or ‘unplanned’ obsolescence: how many electronic devices are kept only 1-2-3 years before their manufacturer disappears, switches to a new version. Do we have what of this connected object without a support? Without a security update? Is it thrown away because it does not support SSL xx? Almost all connected objects (IOT) are: this represents a major security risk: millions of items of equipment that are added day by day, all over the world and that are potentially vulnerable, probably not when they leave, but tomorrow: it is a real time bomb to paralyse a country from seeing a continent. With regard to technological areas, it is incredible that Europe has been able to equip itself with the GDPR for the benefit of our personal data, but that not all systems that process them have a free and open code: we have identified what is sensitive, but we put it in a cardboard box! Europe’s role is therefore to provide financial support to all contributors of open-source software on the basis of the quality/usefulness of the software/framework, to avoid a monopoly of a single software (free or not), to encourage companies to publish their code, to inform companies about the types of licences in the open-source software.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: In this feedback I will be focusing mainly on the question of benefits of FOSS: The benefits of FOSS are numerous and interconnected. > Sovereignty >> Exploitation In the example of Microsoft vs IBM, Microsoft showed that software platforms dominate markets, and closed-source, for-profit platforms stifle competition. At some point, for-profit companies reach a plateau of new customers and simply need to charge existing customers more to achieve growth. For-profit companies also disregard possible long-lasting damage to society. For example, Meta (formerly Facebook) suppressed internal research showing that its platform leads to various mental health harms. >> Politics Technology cannot be separated from politics. This is evident in cases such as Microsoft suspending services for ICC judge Karim Khan. Foreign for-profit companies obey the actions of foreign leaders. For example, Trump threatened to pull the broadcasting license of CBS. Currently, with such high dependence on foreign software and services, the EU is indefensible against hostile foreign acts. It is important to note that possible hostile actions by foreign for-profit companies will be commanded by foreign leaders, not by CEOs. When assessing trust in a foreign company (for example, Microsoft), it is not enough to consider only its for-profit motives; we must also consider the foreign leader who might command hostile actions (for example, Trump). This means we should not ask ourselves if we trust Microsoft, but if we trust Trump. > Cost Effectiveness Open source is a long-term, cost-effective solution. The narrative of for-profit companies is that the total cost of ownership is lower with a subscription, but in the long term, these companies exploit users as the cost of switching becomes prohibitive. Using FOSS in corporate environments is not necessarily free, as there are maintenance and support costs, but software subscription costs are greatly reduced. For example, the mandatory upgrade from Windows 10 to Windows 11 rendered millions of PCs obsolete or a security risk due to a one-sided decision by a for-profit company. Such actions do not align with EU environmental goals or basic consumer protection. A clear example of cost effectiveness is the use of FOSS by large hyperscalers like Google and Amazon. These companies use Linux as the basis for their services because the costs of depending on for-profit OS manufacturers would be exorbitant. They also benefit from the ability to adapt the OS to their exact needs. > Personal Mental Freedom The digital tools we use become an extension of ourselves. Time invested in learning to use digital tools can be wasted if users lose access to them. Creative software now costs an exorbitant amount for starting artists or non-professionals. If an artist loses a job and thus access to such tools (usually paid for by their employer), they lose their main creative instrument. In contrast, if the education system relies on FOSS tools, artists always retain access to their learned skills. The years invested in personal education cannot be taken away by a company raising prices or changing the work environment. > Shared Infrastructure FOSS should be viewed as shared infrastructure. The EU should not be breeding future predators, but should instead develop efficient digital infrastructure that everyday citizens can use in their daily work and that companies can use to run their businesses efficiently and safely: without the risk of spiking costs or foreign interference. The EU should strive for interoperability and open formats, providing future innovative companies with a larger pool of users.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am an academic AI researcher in Ireland. Being in this field makes me keenly aware of how much digital sovereignty the EU is giving up by relying on privatised technology infrastructure. Especially infrastructure from outside the EU. The decisions made by EU citizens and organisations are being shaped by tech giants using data they collect from all levels of our increasingly digital infrastructure. AI especially is a technology where it is critical to have transparency in training, in injected automatic prompts, and in potential bias. As EU citizens and institutions become more dependent on AI, the ability for companies which control the AI to subtly influence people and organisations will grow massively. This can be used to swing elections and for these private AI companies to silently usurp power and control our understanding of the world to their benefit. Requiring AI be open source and transparent with open training is critical to ensuring the AI revolution works for the EU instead of creating private institutions (inside or outside the EU) which begin to control it.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: Iberdrola is happy to provide feedback on this Call for Evidence as a proof of its involvement in EU processes, offering its perspectives to make the EU better regulation more rigorous and efficient, to create high quality and better applicable EU laws. Here follows a summary of the arguments of the attached file. Q#1. The Commission must ensure that urgent action does not erode the foundations of evidence based EU policymaking: The regulatory acquis has grown excessively complex, generating overlaps, contradictions, and high compliance burdens. Streamlining and Single Market integration must become core priorities. Despite improvements to the regulatory toolbox, many recent proposals have bypassed essential steps such as impact assessments and stakeholder consultations, undermining proportionality, applicability and legal certainty. Urgency, as demonstrated during the energy crisis, does not justify abandoning structured and data driven methods. Even accelerated procedures must respect minimum transparency and analytical requirements. A two year rolling regulatory plan, updated quarterly, should be adopted: with early stakeholder input secure time for drafting robust policy options ensure all proposals are supported by an impact assessment be reviewed by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board (RSB), whose opinion should be public and followed by Commission adjustments In the ordinary legislative procedure, substantial amendments introduced by co-legislators should be impact assessed before trilogues. Regulatory acts should include systematic review clauses enabling adaptation to technological and market developments. Q#2. Holistic stakeholder engagement is essential for regulatory quality and stability: When defining the regulatory plan, the EC should identify horizontal impacts across DGs and organise single, integrated coordinated consultations with a common core complemented by thematic modules. Every legislative initiative requires a high quality, methodologically sound impact assessment, combining qualitative and quantitative analysis of all options, including the baseline. The RSB must systematically verify methodological compliance. A specific urgency procedure should be added to the Better Regulation Toolbox ensuring: abbreviated but transparent consultations proportionate impact assessments including reality and competitiveness checks clear justification of the exceptional procedure. Once an urgent measure is adopted, a continuous fitness check must immediately follow. Monitoring should be transparent, public and overseen by the RSB. Q#3. Simplifying and improving the implementation of EU law requires disciplined use of legal instruments and stronger design principles: The increasing use of delegated and implementing acts for substantive policy elements is problematic. Although these instruments offer agility, they reduce transparency, stakeholder input and impact assessment rigor. The shift from Directives to Regulations must not lead to broad Regulations subsequently detailed through extensive secondary legislation. Delegated and implementing acts should be strictly limited to genuinely technical matters. Excessive reliance on secondary legislation creates temporal misalignment: delegated or implementing acts are often delivered so late that they are outdated because the basic act has been amended (a situation already observed across multiple recent files). This generates legal uncertainty and undermines coherent implementation. Clear boundaries, tighter drafting discipline and better sequencing between primary and secondary legislation are essential. The Commission should systematically apply simplification by design from the outset. Simplification should be an ex-ante design obligation, not an ex post corrective exercise through omnibus packages. The RSB should explicitly verify whether simplification principles are respected in all proposals. See attached Iberdrola's response in more detail.
text_raw: Iberdrola is happy to provide feedback on this Call for Evidence as a proof of its involvement in EU processes, offering its perspectives to make the EU better regulation more rigorous and efficient, to create high quality and better applicable EU laws. Here follows a summary of the arguments of the attached file. Q#1. The Commission must ensure that urgent action does not erode the foundations of evidence based EU policymaking: The regulatory acquis has grown excessively complex, generating overlaps, contradictions, and high compliance burdens. Streamlining and Single Market integration must become core priorities. Despite improvements to the regulatory toolbox, many recent proposals have bypassed essential steps such as impact assessments and stakeholder consultations, undermining proportionality, applicability and legal certainty. Urgency, as demonstrated during the energy crisis, does not justify abandoning structured and data driven methods. Even accelerated procedures must respect minimum transparency and analytical requirements. A two year rolling regulatory plan, updated quarterly, should be adopted: with early stakeholder input secure time for drafting robust policy options ensure all proposals are supported by an impact assessment be reviewed by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board (RSB), whose opinion should be public and followed by Commission adjustments In the ordinary legislative procedure, substantial amendments introduced by co-legislators should be impact assessed before trilogues. Regulatory acts should include systematic review clauses enabling adaptation to technological and market developments. Q#2. Holistic stakeholder engagement is essential for regulatory quality and stability: When defining the regulatory plan, the EC should identify horizontal impacts across DGs and organise single, integrated coordinated consultations with a common core complemented by thematic modules. Every legislative initiative requires a high quality, methodologically sound impact assessment, combining qualitative and quantitative analysis of all options, including the baseline. The RSB must systematically verify methodological compliance. A specific urgency procedure should be added to the Better Regulation Toolbox ensuring: abbreviated but transparent consultations proportionate impact assessments including reality and competitiveness checks clear justification of the exceptional procedure. Once an urgent measure is adopted, a continuous fitness check must immediately follow. Monitoring should be transparent, public and overseen by the RSB. Q#3. Simplifying and improving the implementation of EU law requires disciplined use of legal instruments and stronger design principles: The increasing use of delegated and implementing acts for substantive policy elements is problematic. Although these instruments offer agility, they reduce transparency, stakeholder input and impact assessment rigor. The shift from Directives to Regulations must not lead to broad Regulations subsequently detailed through extensive secondary legislation. Delegated and implementing acts should be strictly limited to genuinely technical matters. Excessive reliance on secondary legislation creates temporal misalignment: delegated or implementing acts are often delivered so late that they are outdated because the basic act has been amended (a situation already observed across multiple recent files). This generates legal uncertainty and undermines coherent implementation. Clear boundaries, tighter drafting discipline and better sequencing between primary and secondary legislation are essential. The Commission should systematically apply simplification by design from the outset. Simplification should be an ex-ante design obligation, not an ex post corrective exercise through omnibus packages. The RSB should explicitly verify whether simplification principles are respected in all proposals. See attached Iberdrola's response in more detail.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: The German Social Insurance Agency (DSV) is committed to ensuring that new, ambitious measures are adopted by the European Commission for the period 2026-2030 to further improve the lives of persons with disabilities in the European Union. initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open Source Software (OSS) is more than just ensuring we stay independent from non-EU actors. As a citizen, using closed third-party solutions makes it impossible for me to know whether a provider complies with GDPR, and being dependent on a closed third-party means that we can get locked-in to non-open standards, which other parties can never offer a compliant solution if we ever want to move away from the first provider. This is not only a question about independence from foreign actors, but also about being able to have a truly open internal market where providers can compete with fairness, and about minimizing the opportunity cost of switching between them once a provider no longer satisfies the conditions or needs of the union. It is not so much a question of software technologies as a question about fairness, and the only way to ensure fair competition in this space is through open standards and open source software being the foundation for everything in the union. Ensuring open standards also mean that it's possible to ensure compatibility between local municipalities all the way up to the global level, and minimizing time and resources spent on fixing the chafing between different systems and standards. Time has shown that to ensure open standards it is imperative that there is a common open source base as a foundation. Otherwise dominating parties will simply choose not to follow the standard, or claim to follow it without actually doing so. This has been clear through various office document standards, web browsers and other technology over the years, with the goal of locking in their user-base so that no alternatives are viable. By eliminating the non-open source software platforms from choice, we can ensure that the open standards are respected, and interoperability between different providers is ensured, meaning they compete by merit rather than lock-in.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) is a key instrument to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and aims to achieve climate neutrality by 2050. Under the Fit for 55 package, review clauses were added to ETS Directive 2003/87/EC. By 2026, consider possible adaptations for stationary installations, aviation and maritime transport, as well as an extension to new sectors such as waste management. The aim is to increase emissions reductions, improve market efficiency and cross-sectoral coherence. The VÖWG is clearly committed to the climate objectives of the Fit for 55 package and supports the further development of CO reduction incentives. From the point of view of services of general interest, it considers the planned extensions of the ETS at the same time with caution, in particular with regard to thermal waste recovery, heat supply and inland waterway transport. Given the tight economic situation, rising cost of living and high investment needs, climate action needs to be socially balanced, technically feasible and economically viable. The revision of the ETS should include regional supply conditions, existing structures and cost-effective design. Public companies make key contributions to decarbonisation, for example through waste recycling with heat recovery, low-emission logistics, climate-friendly mobility or investments in district heating networks. These services need to be made visible in the ETS and targeted support is needed to avoid one-sided burdens that would disproportionately affect municipal capacity or socially weaker groups. The VÖWG calls for an inclusive understanding of climate policy that recognises public service tasks as a lever for sustainable transformation. The ETS should take into account sector-specific differences, national specificities and regional responsibility and ensure a fair distribution of transformation costs. VÖWG positions as an overview of polluter-based CO pricing in waste management are not possible; instead, producer responsibility and a fair-level playing field is needed for all disposal routes. Flat-rate ETS pricing for thermal waste recovery offers little effect and puts at risk safe disposal. Progress in recycling and use of waste heat from incineration must be recognised in the ETS and supported by compensatory measures in order not to jeopardise the circular economy and heat transition. CCUS requires clear EU-wide evidence, a level playing field and targeted support for permanent carbon removals. Recognise CCU in the ETS only if there is evidence of emission reductions; avoid double carbon pollution in the waste sector and make biogenic CO countable. ETS extension to shipping must not lead to competitive disadvantages; there is a need for a clear distinction with IMO, fair implementation and consideration of the efficiency of inland waterway transport. For a more detailed explanation of the above-mentioned priorities, please refer to our attached opinion.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: The AGRIAL goat milk branch brings together 42 organic goat farmers for an annual organic milk production of 6 million litres. The reopening of the European Organic Regulation allows us to raise several issues that penalise organic goat farmers. These points severely hamper the development of the organic goat dairy sector. This is why we ask for a relaxation on the following 3 topics: 1- review the deadline of 48 hours (before the milk is marketed) when certain veterinary treatments are used, while the legal period under the agreement is null. This is particularly the case for parasitic treatments. The grazing of animals, which is essential in organic livestock production, leads to significant free-riding problems in goat farming. The use of anti-parasites is essential and must often be carried out collectively on the herd’s animals. This 48-hour deadline therefore has a significant impact on milk production, with large volumes of milk discarded to meet this deadline. Economic climate is high. 2- authorise the use of non-animal materials in the composition of organic slab replacers. Indeed, limiting the direct transmission of diseases between the goat and its small goat is a major challenge from the moment of misdemeanour. The use of milk powder remains an interesting solution for rearing the kids. This requires a balanced slab food that meets the needs of the animal. It should be possible to include ingredients of plant origin in the formulation. The composition of this slab feed also requires additional ingredients, whether for technical reasons for drinking (such as fluid properties for the disposal of milk) or to prevent health concerns on young animals. Examples include vitamins, essential oils, yeasts and probiotics 3- Ensure the temporary keeping conditions for goats in order to manage the herd’s health. The management of grazing free riding remains delicate in goat farming, and free-riding can seriously undermine animal health. Keeping animals in buildings can in some cases reduce parasitic and health pressure on animals and better monitor vulnerable animals.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: It is a feedback on EU ETS directive by the National Centre for Emission Management (KOBiZE, Poland). It is widely observed by various stakeholders that policy instruments currently in place are unlikely to deliver the Unions emission reduction targets on their own, without any flexibilities. The EU ETS is undergoing profound changes and in this evolving landscape, with new measures being put in place, it is expected that market liquidity and stability will be deeply affected, potentially causing price fluctuations and volatility as supply and demand adjust. The planned reform of the EU ETS and other climate policy measures must focus on strengthening the competitiveness and resilience of EU industry in line with climate policy objectives. That is why it is so important to implement legal solutions that, on the one hand, will highlight the different situations in which Member States are, on the other hand, will be understandable and acceptable to the Union society and thus strengthen joint action within the Union. Actions to protect the competitiveness of EU industry and address carbon leakage should be based on existing mechanisms. The phase-out of free allocation should be postponed, and 100% allocation should be implemented for goods at risk of carbon leakage, alongside CBAM reform and simplifications. This approach will reduce the financial pressure on industry, which invests in reduction measures and is obliged to purchase missing allowances to cover current emissions. We recognize the need to account for residual emissions and ensure environmental integrity. The integration of carbon dioxide removals (CDR), under strict and transparent criteria, may contribute to that aim, provided it supplements and does not replace essential emissions reductions. The inclusion of municipal waste incineration plants in the ETS should be carried out in such a way as to create incentives for investors to use this technology. The key issue is the level of free allocation. If incinerators are to be included, they should have a CL of 80-100% to compete with heat produced from fossil fuels. Linking the EU ETS to other global schemes or integrating offsets could bring significant economic and environmental benefits. This would be possible if the design and governance met the highest standards. Such measures could reduce compliance costs. They could also stabilise carbon prices. Furthermore, they could enhance international cooperation on climate change. While linking promotes a more balanced global market for emission allowances, offsetting scenarios provide additional flexibility, making carbon neutrality targets more achievable for the EU and its partners. One possible solution to incorporate removals into the EU ETS and revise MSR mechanism is the establishment of a European Carbon Central Bank (ECCB). The European Carbon Central Bank (ECCB) could be a dedicated, independent institution mandated with the power to govern, stabilize, and strategically steer the development of the EU carbon market in the post-2030 landscape. While the ECCB would not replace the markets core logic, it would enhance it by providing the institutional capacity required to maintain stability, protect environmental integrity, and deliver social legitimacy. Directive established a pecuniary fine for failure to surrender an appropriate number of emission allowances by the operator, aircraft operator and shipping company on time (Article 16(3) and (3a)). In order to mitigate the punitive nature of this fine, while not impairing the effective functioning of the EU ETS, it is worth considering some changes in this respect in the event of incidental difficulties with timely performance of this obligation. Please see the attached file for more details.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: The Cool Heating Coalition, the civil society voice on heating and cooling, supports the update to the pathway values for biomass. We want to further highlight the opportunity to shift to healthier homes and improved land use. Research by the Joint Research Centre concludes that biomass for space heating has "detrimental effect on air quality" , both inside our homes and outdoors. We therefore urge the Commission to be a strong voice for public health and environmental resilience. The most effective and permanent solution is to accelerate the shift to clean technologies like heat pumps, geothermal, solar thermal, aquathermal, electric cooking, and renewable-based district heating.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Hydrogen Europe is the European association representing the interests of the hydrogen industry and its stakeholders, while promoting hydrogen as a key enabler of a zero-emission society. The association brings together more than 600 members, 40+ EU regions, and 30+ national associations. The revision of the EU Public Procurement Directives represents a relevant and timely opportunity to support Europes decarbonisation, industrial competitiveness, and resilience objectives, provided that public procurement is used in a targeted, proportionate, and system-aware manner. Across the hydrogen ecosystem, including energy producers, infrastructure operators, technology and project developers, industrial manufacturers, and financial investors, there is broad recognition that public procurement alone cannot deliver the hydrogen transition. Nevertheless, it can play a meaningful role in creating lead markets, reducing risk for first movers, improving investment visibility and support more decarbonised and European supply chains. The central theme emerging from the discussions is the importance of fairness and symmetry: where public money or public ownership is involved, requirements related to sustainability, resilience, local content or origin should be applied in a way that avoids creating unequal conditions between public and private actors operating in the same markets. For regulated infrastructure operators in particular, cost impacts must be carefully managed to avoid unintended effects on network tariffs, energy prices, and security of supply. Hydrogen Europe underlines that, where hydrogen applications form part of the subject matter of public tenders, procurement should be used in a targeted and proportionate manner. Priority should be given to segments where hydrogen delivers clear added value, notably hard-to-electrify transport, hydrogen molecules and hydrogen-based materials such as clean steel and fertilisers, and strategic fuels such as e-fuels for aviation and maritime use, as well as related technologies, including electrolysers, fuel cells, and key components. At the same time, Hydrogen Europe raise caution against blanket or rigid procurement approaches that risk increasing system costs, distorting competition, or slowing down infrastructure deployment. Finally, stakeholders underline the need for coherence with existing EU frameworks, notably the Net-Zero Industry Act (NZIA), the European Hydrogen Bank (EHB), the Clean Industrial Deal (CID), and the upcoming Industrial Accelerator Act (IAA). Public procurement is seen as a potential operational complement to these instruments, not as a parallel or conflicting layer of obligations.

Across the hydrogen ecosystem, including energy producers, infrastructure operators, technology and project developers, industrial manufacturers, and financial investors, there is broad recognition that public procurement alone cannot deliver the hydrogen transition. Nevertheless, it can play a meaningful role in creating lead markets, reducing risk for first movers, improving investment visibility and support more decarbonised and European supply chains. The central theme emerging from the discussions is the importance of fairness and symmetry: where public money or public ownership is involved, requirements related to sustainability, resilience, local content or origin should be applied in a way that avoids creating unequal conditions between public and private actors operating in the same markets. For regulated infrastructure operators in particular, cost impacts must be carefully managed to avoid unintended effects on network tariffs, energy prices, and security of supply. Hydrogen Europe underlines that, where hydrogen applications form part of the subject matter of public tenders, procurement should be used in a targeted and proportionate manner. Priority should be given to segments where hydrogen delivers clear added value, notably hard-to-electrify transport, hydrogen molecules and hydrogen-based materials such as clean steel and fertilisers, and strategic fuels such as e-fuels for aviation and maritime use, as well as related technologies, including electrolysers, fuel cells, and key components. At the same time, Hydrogen Europe raise caution against blanket or rigid procurement approaches that risk increasing system costs, distorting competition, or slowing down infrastructure deployment. Finally, stakeholders underline the need for coherence with existing EU frameworks, notably the Net-Zero Industry Act (NZIA), the European Hydrogen Bank (EHB), the Clean Industrial Deal (CID), and the upcoming Industrial Accelerator Act (IAA). Public procurement is seen as a potential operational complement to these instruments, not as a parallel or conflicting layer of obligations.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As a PhD student, the entirety of my work is focused on open source systems, specifically the Linux Kernel. The EU should expand their contributions in this sector as currently there are areas of the technology stack that are fully closed. Examples of these are within the hardware/firmware of devices, the BIOS software, the actual design of the hardware. For the main barriers on the adoption of the open source software there are missing feature parity, a different workflow for many programs, and a missing support service. Software that is provided "as is" has no guarantees, that are sometimes needed in specific fields. The added value for open source is that it avoids a lot of repetition and rewriting of the same code over different competing products. Having the code also be open, and with a copyleft license, means all possible upgrades are always available to the public. A security flaw being fixed would not be able to reappear in competing products, as the single product can provide for everyone. This also allows a very open stance, as lock-in can be virtually non existent. Proprietary formats cannot exist within an open source program, and therefore a program that can translate data from one program to the other can always exist, giving a guarantee of interoperability. The EU should both financially and legally support these open source projects, defending them from possible company takeovers, or namesquatting, an example being OBS being "Open Broadcasting Software" being almost taken over by a company using the same name for their own fork of the project "Streamlabs OBS", which ended up replacing a lot of the traffic towards the original, due to SEO optimization tricks, fundamentally removing support from the original, especially as this version was closed source. The areas to focus first are on the mainstream software, programs that reduce the dependency on US based companies, for example Office and Windows. Continuning to support projects like Linux and LibreOffice/OnlyOffice means widening the support for the average citizen to have freedom of computation, in order to actually being able to own their machine and the software running on it. Open Source software needs extra guardrails over the security, as every single bit of code needs peer review and various guarantees, but on the opposite side, the fact that the code is open, means it's open to public scrutiny, and more likely to be more secure than closed counterparts, even if a lot of projects do not have the means to hire security consultants and audits, this could be another area in which the EU could support these projects.

feedback_text: Many of our customers are spread across southern France, with small units ordering between 250 and 1500 chicks and different strains depending on the needs of their markets. If our sales volume does not justify a batch of breeding animals, these customers will no longer be delivered. They will have to take hundreds of km or more to recover chicks, which will all be the same without special features depending on the production area, with enormous logistical constraints on their supplies. Of course, I am to maintain the possibility of using non-organic chicks

feedback_text: (1) many open-source projects do not depend solely on input at code level. Funding is sometimes needed to advance project maintainers, which are sometimes companies that switch to a fee-paying licensing system after several years when the use of the project is free of charge. Many businesses are not willing to pay and are therefore not prepared to take this risk. There is also a lack of awareness of the life of an open-source project, which sometimes depends on the personal resources of a single person. 2) – possibility to improve a product if needed, my company has already contributed multiple times to the Proxmox project to add functions we needed internally. — Control costs by repeating once again the example of Proxmox vs Vmware. Proxmox has a reduced license cost that allows supporting the project first and foremost. And the different licence levels depend on the desired level of support. — In terms of security, being able to audit the code and easily understand how it works is essential in terms of security. — The open-source aspect also helps to avoid technology lock-in by making it possible to forker projects when the undertaking behind a product makes questionable choices such as Elastic Search and OpenSearch, for example. (3) the establishment of an exceptional financial contribution could help certain projects, the provision of basic accommodation to avoid that small projects have to bear the infrastructure costs, the establishment of a European entity to contribute to the open source could also be considered (provision of developer on projects for bug correction and adding functionalities) (4) The priority areas in my view are: — Virtualisation: To compete with many giants of US virtualisation – Hosting: Many companies house their infrastructure in hosts such as AWS, GCP, Azure. Apart from data security, the profit generated by these companies makes little contribution to Europe. — Information systems: Many European companies depend on the Microsoft 365 environment, the day when Microsoft stops operating in Europe let’s imagine, thousands of companies, large groups of which will be paralysed for days or weeks while European solutions that can be hosted in Europe exist. Generally, all communication channels and the storage of documents depend on this product, which is an essential part of a business. — Operating systems, the use of Windows in a company but above all in public administration is an aberration, we are funding a US company to open the door to it in all our environments. While this choice was historically viable, in the current context this choice is more than debatable, especially with the progress of Linux’s accesibility compared to 20 years back and its arrival in more and more homes thanks to initiatives to replace Windows with Linux free of charge. (5) in all sectors depending on the IT tool. (5) hosting, authentication,

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: The disaggregated default values in Annex V, Part D play a central role in the RED GHG accounting framework by forming the basis for compliance calculations in the absence of actual data. The draft exhibits multiple material inconsistencies and apparent calculation errors, which significantly affect the accuracy and credibility of the methodology and must be addressed without delay. The calculation methodology in Annex V part C remains insufficiently transparent limiting stakeholders ability to understand the drivers behind the revised values. Overall, the updated values change only marginally, yet domestic agricultural crops once again appear disadvantaged compared with sugar cane, raising questions about the neutrality of key assumptions. Recital 4 states that feedstock transport distance pathways should be incorporated into Annex V, where appropriate, in line with the approach adopted in 2018 for Annex VI of Directive (EU) 2018/2001. Unlike Annex VI, which explicitly reflects differences in transport distances, the draft delegated annex V does not establish comparable typical or default values showing disparities in transport-related emissions between long-distance imports and locally sourced feedstocks used for biofuel production. We ask the Commission to introduce a transport-distance pathway into the disaggregated default values in Parts C and D of Annex V as it would accurately reflect the GHG intensity of non-EU-produced feedstocks used for biofuels. While the Commission encourages investment in renewable energy, its current approach effectively discourages the use of biomass for energy production. This is evident in the growing divergence between scientifically grounded values and the default assumptions proposed by the Commission. In many parts of Europe, forest biomass is the only economically viable outlet for low-quality wood and plays a key role in sustainable forest management. We note that fossil fuel reference values remain unchanged and recommend clarifying the rationale for this approach. We are particularly concerned that consolidating typical and default values into a single table appears to remove disaggregated default values previously available in Annex V. We strongly request that the values in Part D (Disaggregated default values for transport and distribution of final fuel only) remain available and updated if necessary. These values are essential for operators who wish to declare actual values for feedstock transport only. Without these disaggregated defaults, operators would be required to calculate actual values for both feedstock transport and final fuel transport and distribution. In practice, calculating actual values for final fuel transport is often not feasible, as it requires detailed information on final user locations, transport routes, modes, storage and intermediate steps, which are rarely available to fuel producers. In addition, the requirement to apply worst-case values when aggregating lots creates a disproportionate burden and leads to results that are not representative of real supply chains. Removing disaggregated default values would therefore eliminate the possibility to use actual values for feedstock transport, undermining incentives to reduce emissions in parts of the supply chain that producers can directly influence. This would weaken the effectiveness of the GHG accounting framework. The Commission must improve transparency, retain key disaggregated default values, and ensure that the methodology continues to reward measurable emission reductions where operators have direct control. The range of crops that can be grown as raw materials for biofuel production includes intermediate crops. However, crops as such are not named. The Commission must supplement the draft by including or regulating the basic requirement that the GHG value of intermediate crops must be calculated using the same calculation method as for the crops listed in Annex V.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: The European Commission is required to review Annexes V and VI under Article 31(5) of the Renewable Energy Directive. While the revision aims to simplify the structure of these Annexes it is important to maintain the existing GHG default values which are used by several operators in the renewable energy value chains as specially small operators might not have the ability to calculate the actual GHG values. It is also important to avoid setting unnecessary provisions with respect to the GHG default values to ensure practical implementation of the GHG default values. Also applying harmonised terms throughout the Directive and its Annexes is important to avoid confusion and possible misinterpretation. Neste welcomes the revision of the RED Annex V. However, Neste wishes to express concerns on specific parts of the revised Annex V and recommends to modify these parts as suggested in the file attached.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: While I welcome the EU's decision to pursue this, I simply want to encourage the Commission to avoid reinventing the wheel. There are so many wonderful open-source communities around the EU focusing on everything from technical development to policy, from small groups of hackers to larger commercial entities (i.e., Nextcloud). This includes national Internet communities, technical and standards development bodies, groups like the Chaos Computer Club, and countless other developers, engineers, techies, and everything in-between. So, I encourage you to proactively seek them out, request best practices, and create an alternative Big Tech movement together. As an example of some of these communities and alternatives, see the following websites: 1. https://european-alternatives.eu/ 2. https://www.goeuropean.org/ 3. https://www.opensourcealternative.to/ I also encourage close collaboration with Eurostack and other similar initiatives. Do not mistake the obvious: American Big Tech is NOT your friend! This is a great opportunity for the EU to unite all kinds of different groups and stakeholders, ranging the full spectrum of political and ideological beliefs, to collaborate on building and enabling a different path for European technology going forward.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) operate under strict budgetary, operational and human-resource constraints. For them, open source is not an ideological preference, but a pragmatic and strategic tool to remain competitive, resilient and independent in an increasingly digital economy. Why Open Source Matters for SMEs Cost predictability and affordability Fully open-source solutions eliminate licence fees and hidden costs. SMEs can deploy enterprise-grade infrastructure while maintaining predictable budgets and healthy cash flow. Example: platforms like Proxmox Virtual Environment provide virtualisation, clustering and high availability without per-CPU or per-VM licensing. Freedom from vendor lock-in Open-source software runs on standard hardware and across different environments, allowing SMEs to avoid forced migrations, price increases or dependency on hyperscalers. Example: Proxmox can be deployed on-premise, at the edge or in hybrid setups, using standard Linux kernels and open formats. Operational simplicity for small IT teams Integrated open-source stacks reduce complexity by replacing multiple proprietary tools with a single, coherent platform. Example: Proxmox combines virtualisation, storage management, backup integration and clustering in one interface, reducing operational overhead for small teams. Transparency and trust Auditable source code increases confidence in security, compliance and long-term sustainability, especially when handling sensitive business or customer data. Tangible Benefits in Daily SME Operations Lower total cost of ownership (TCO) while retaining enterprise-level features such as snapshots, replication and high availability. Strong security posture through rapid, community-driven vulnerability detection and patching. Interoperability via open APIs and standards, enabling integration with existing ERP, CRM, monitoring or backup tools. Scalable adoption, allowing SMEs to start with a small setup and grow without re-architecting or renegotiating licences. What SMEs Need from EU Policy To unlock the full potential of open source for SMEs, EU action should focus on enablement rather than compliance burden: Sustainable funding for critical open-source infrastructure projects that SMEs rely on as foundational technologies. Tax incentives or financial support for SMEs adopting or contributing to open-source platforms (including testing, documentation and small patches). Procurement frameworks accessible to SMEs, explicitly recognising fully open-source solutions such as Proxmox-based stacks, without requirements tailored only to large vendors. Targeted training and up-skilling programmes focused on practical infrastructure software used by SMEs. Proportionate regulation that ensures security and compliance without discouraging small companies or early-stage open-source projects. Infrastructure First: A Pragmatic Path to Digital Sovereignty for SMEs For SMEs, digital sovereignty starts at the infrastructure layer, not at consumer-facing services. Open-source virtualisation and backup platforms enable SMEs to retain full control over data, meet EU data-residency requirements, and deploy resilient on-premise or hybrid systems at sustainable cost. By supporting and standardising proven open-source infrastructure solutionssuch as Proxmoxthe EU can ensure that SMEs access the same technological foundations as large enterprises, without the financial, legal or strategic constraints of proprietary ecosystems. High-Impact Sectors for SMEs Open-source infrastructure can significantly increase competitiveness and cyber resilience in SME-driven sectors such as public services, healthcare, manufacturing and Industry 4.0, telecommunications, energy and local utilities.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: UCPH welcomes the European Commissions ambition to reduce fragmentation in the European R&I ecosystem. We can without hesitation support the aim of actually achieving the target of investing 3% of GDP in R&I. It is therefore also very encouraging to see that the proposal for the Multi-Annual Financial Framework shows a significant budget increase for R&I. We acknowledge that some framework conditions for collaboration be-tween higher education institutions, research institutions, and businesses pose a challenge. While we understand the current focus on the more technology-oriented research and innovation domains, we want to stress that broad support and collaboration remain a vital part of the European re-search landscape. This is a prerequisite for long-term sustainability for the R&I ecosystem, and thus also for long-term sustainability regarding competitiveness and overcoming the very severe global societal challenges. A better alignment of EU and national R&I policies would enable more coherence and meaningful collaborations in funding support of R&I; such an alignment exercise must not only be a question of the council of relevant ministers, the European Parliament, and the European Commission agreeing on a modus operandi. It must take the form of real co-creation involving all relevant target groups, including universities. As stated, framework conditions for researchers and their various institutions, can and should be improved. Researchers are already very mobile, but it would be benefit all if any current stumbling blocks can be cleared. Here too, it is important that an ERA Act is founded on first-hand insight into the concrete challenges faced by researchers and research institutions. The challenges that an Act needs on to address on this particular subject are complex on a national level, and even more daunting at the EU level. Universities are already some of the most open organisations when it comes to sharing knowledge and results; we, as well as many other universities, are continuing to develop new ways of sharing and exploiting our results, very often in close collaboration with businesses, public institutions, NGOs, and civil society in general. When addressing this issue, we want to under-line that this type of activity is currently often not supported by any kind of dedicated funding, be it from the national governments or the EUs various programmes. This hard nut also needs to be cracked. This is linked closely to the wish for better conditions for valorisation of research results and knowledge, which we can endorse. Most researchers across research domains do in fact want their work to be used in some way or another, and this includes utilisation outside academia. But as with other activities aimed at knowledge sharing, valorisation takes time, and often needs further development in collaboration with lead users before it can be further exploited and lead to social impact. Such activities can be costly and the Act needs to take this into account. We can only welcome a transparent and workable legal framework for sharing access to research infrastructures, be they local at an individual research organisation or pan-European such as ESS or CERN. The issue here is to ensure that any kind of legislation is manageable, especially for locally owned/run infrastructures. If openness and easy access is the aim, it will only be achieved if owners and users is not burdened with bureaucracy or additional unfunded expenses. Research today is an international ecosystem, and we would welcome greater alignment between the member states on how to ensure that collaboration with third countries finds the optimum balance between openness and the necessary level of security to protect critical data and research. Much can be won if the EU and the member states can agree on a common approach that achieves this balance and that can be implemented locally at institutional level.

We acknowledge that some framework conditions for collaboration be-tween higher education institutions, research institutions, and businesses pose a challenge. While we understand the current focus on the more technology-oriented research and innovation domains, we want to stress that broad support and collaboration remain a vital part of the European re-search landscape. This is a prerequisite for long-term sustainability for the R&I ecosystem, and thus also for long-term sustainability regarding competitiveness and overcoming the very severe global societal challenges. A better alignment of EU and national R&I policies would enable more coherence and meaningful collaborations in funding support of R&I; such an alignment exercise must not only be a question of the council of relevant ministers, the European Parliament, and the European Commission agreeing on a modus operandi. It must take the form of real co-creation involving all relevant target groups, including universities. As stated, framework conditions for researchers and their various institutions, can and should be improved. Researchers are already very mobile, but it would be benefit all if any current stumbling blocks can be cleared. Here too, it is important that an ERA Act is founded on first-hand insight into the concrete challenges faced by researchers and research institutions. The challenges that an Act needs on to address on this particular subject are complex on a national level, and even more daunting at the EU level. Universities are already some of the most open organisations when it comes to sharing knowledge and results; we, as well as many other universities, are continuing to develop new ways of sharing and exploiting our results, very often in close collaboration with businesses, public institutions, NGOs, and civil society in general. When addressing this issue, we want to under-line that this type of activity is currently often not supported by any kind of dedicated funding, be it from the national governments or the EUs various programmes. This hard nut also needs to be cracked. This is linked closely to the wish for better conditions for valorisation of research results and knowledge, which we can endorse. Most researchers across research domains do in fact want their work to be used in some way or another, and this includes utilisation outside academia. But as with other activities aimed at knowledge sharing, valorisation takes time, and often needs further development in collaboration with lead users before it can be further exploited and lead to social impact. Such activities can be costly and the Act needs to take this into account. We can only welcome a transparent and workable legal framework for sharing access to research infrastructures, be they local at an individual research organisation or pan-European such as ESS or CERN. The issue here is to ensure that any kind of legislation is manageable, especially for locally owned/run infrastructures. If openness and easy access is the aim, it will only be achieved if owners and users is not burdened with bureaucracy or additional unfunded expenses. Research today is an international ecosystem, and we would welcome greater alignment between the member states on how to ensure that collaboration with third countries finds the optimum balance between openness and the necessary level of security to protect critical data and research. Much can be won if the EU and the member states can agree on a common approach that achieves this balance and that can be implemented locally at institutional level.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The European Research Area (ERA) must be shaped as an inclusive framework that actively integrates the enlargement countries, particularly the Western Balkans, ensuring they are not left at the periphery of European research and innovation developments. From the POLICY ANSWERS project and the Western Balkans Info Hub perspective, a core recommendation is that tailored support is essential for Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Kosovo, Montenegro, North Macedonia, and Serbia so they can share fully in ERAs benefits and objectives while acknowledging their specific regional contexts. Recent ERA country reports for 2024 illustrate that while these economies are indeed committed to ERA principles, despite not formally signed up. Of course, challenges in research funding, participation in EU programmes and policy alignment persist. Across the region, institutional fragility, limited infrastructure and low participation in European research projects are recurring issues that make convergence with EU standards difficult. Therefore, the ERA Act should incorporate concrete mechanisms that account for these disparities, offering bespoke policy support, knowledge transfer and dedicated funding streams to address the identified gaps. Emphasis must be placed on capacity-building, regional cooperation and greater policy coordination, enabling these economies to improve their position within ERA structures and the wider European R&I ecosystem. Ethics, equality, gender considerations and citizen engagement must be anchored at the core of the European Research Area if the ERA is to be truly transformative for the Western Balkans and enlargement countries. Advancing these priorities is not only essential for fostering a research and innovation environment that is both inclusive and responsible, but also vital for increasing public trust in science and its outcomes. The ERA must support greater citizen engagement by encouraging open dialogue between researchers and the wider public, offering transparent information and opportunities for meaningful participation in the co-design and evaluation of research. Building a culture of trust and inclusion in research helps ensure that societal challenges unique to the region are addressed in a manner that reflects local values and needs, thereby reinforcing the ERAs relevance and legitimacy. Robust monitoring, consistent measurement standards and well-coordinated statistical practices are critical for tracking progress and identifying persistent gaps in ERA integration for the Western Balkans. As outlined in the POLICY ANSWERS projects policy brief on monitoring and reporting, data collection systems across the region remain fragmented, with limited alignment to ERA indicators and with variable data quality and frequency. This hinders the comparability of research and innovation performance against EU benchmarks and makes it challenging to target policies and funding effectively. To address these challenges, there is a pressing need for enhanced coordination on data strategies, capacity-building for statistical offices and support for the systematic adoption of international standards and ERA-specific monitoring frameworks. These improvements will enable evidence-based policy-making, facilitate the benchmarking of progress towards ERA priorities, and ensure that the specific needs and achievements of the Western Balkans are visible and valued within the broader ERA context. Overall, we want to emphasise that the regional context, such as persistent socio-economic disparities, talent mobility challenges, etc., should be reflected in ERA implementation instruments. Only with targeted interventions can the ERA advance its vision of a truly integrated European Research Area and ensure the meaningful participation of the Western Balkans and other enlargement countries in line with shared European values and ambitions. More information about the initiative: www.westernbalkans-infohub.eu

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Quoting Poul-Henning Kamp: At the most fundamental level, the EU has three options: 1. Pick and bless a set of winners, consisting of: a) Operating system, portable to any reasonable computer architecture. b) Text-processing, suitable for tasks up to a book. c) Spreadsheet d) Email client. e) Web Browser f) Accounting software, suitable for small organizations. and fund organizations to maintain, develop and support the software for the future as open source, turning that software into infrastructure like water, power and electricity, free for all, individuals, startups and established companies alike, to use and benefit from. 2. Continuously develop/pick, bless and meticulously enforce open standards of interoperability, and then "let the competition loose". 3. Both. By providing a free baseline and de-facto reference implementations for the open standards, "the market" will be free to innovate, improve and compete, but cannot (re)create walled gardens. To everybody, me included, option two seems the ideologically "pure" choice, because we have all been brought to believe that "governments should not pick winners". But governments have always picked winners. Today all of EU has 230VAC electrical grids, because EU picked that as a winner, thereby leveling the market to everybody's benefit. Therefore I will argue, that the wise choice for EU is option three. First, it will be incredibly cheap, as in just tens of millions of Euro per year, to provide all EU citizens with a free and trustworthy software platform to run on their computers. Second, it can be done incredibly fast: From EU makes the decision, the first version can be release in a matter of months, if not weeks. Third, it will guarantee interoperability of data.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: To truly strengthen the European open source ecosystem, it is not enough to invest only in major industrial projects or research programmes. it is crucial to support communities, associations and social infrastructure that make source lopen live, accessible and adoptable every day. Associations such as Fedimedia Italia play a key role in promoting and disseminating Fediverse and open source technologies: accompany users, public administrations and private organisations in the adoption of open platforms; offer expertise on governance, moderation, interoperability and migration; they help build trust and awareness around digital models that are alternative to Big Tech. One of the main obstacles to the adoption of open source is the lack of stable resources to cover structural costs: hosting, security, maintenance, user support, training, documentation and community coordination activities. Without continued economic support, many open source realities remain fragile, despite producing high public value. This is why the EU should: formally recognise the role of open source associations and foundations as digital civic infrastructures; provide for structural and recurrent funding mechanisms, not limited to experimental or short-term calls; facilitate their access to public procurement, including through criteria that enhance interoperability, lack of lock-in and open governance; support hybrid and sustainable business and associative models, combining services, support, donations, membership and public-private partnerships; invest in European support networks for Fedivere and digital commons, fostering cooperation between Member States. The value of open source for public and private organisations is not only economic, but also strategic and democratic: reduction of external dependencies, increased transparency, verifiable security, control over data, resilience of infrastructure and adaptability over time. Supporting these realities therefore means investing in European technological sovereignty, strengthening an open digital ecosystem that is not dominated by a few global players, but collectively built and governed in the public interest.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: The establishment of an EU scheme for certifying the cybersecurity of cloud services (EUCS) has been under discussion for years. However, debates about the inclusion of so-called sovereignty requirements in an EUCS are delaying an agreement. In a comprehensive publication attached, the Centre for European Policy (cep) outlines ways out of the deadlocked discussion surrounding the EUCS. In particular, it presents several ideas on how to adapt the EU Cybersecurity Act and presents proposals for a future resilient cloud policy.

However, debates about the inclusion of so-called sovereignty requirements in an EUCS are delaying an agreement. In a comprehensive publication attached, the Centre for European Policy (cep) outlines ways out of the deadlocked discussion surrounding the EUCS. In particular, it presents several ideas on how to adapt the EU Cybersecurity Act and presents proposals for a future resilient cloud policy.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Hello, both young farmers set up in organic farming in France and farm workers on a mixed organic farming farm, I would like to express my opinion on the amendment of the European rules on organic farming. First of all, I think it is pointless and fastuous to go back just 3 years after the regulation we have deja, it means spending Europe’s money unnecessarily, there are much more challenges today if we want to have farmers in the long term. This is very good for consultancies who will charge a maximum but not for European farmers. Organic farming is a long-term game, changing the rules every 3 years means pulling a ball on the foot to make a race, exhausting farmers and their money instead of helping them to focus on their work. Secondly, organic farming is a strength in Europe, not only the quality of production we have, the diversity of production that we have thanks to the diversity of land, but also the skills and knowledge of farmers who have knowledge, who are also trained and taught. European leaders should be aware of this, and we could travel on the organic market around the world, instead of being a consumer of organic farming outside Europe, which is a scandal. Our organic economy must be qualitative, as we will have the world market. A good organic product is products that have gout, are good for health, do not kill farmers, it is what people are looking for when they eat, the current constraint is only the price because we do not support organic farming enough in Europe, compete with low-quality imports. Third, some of the changes that the Commissioner for Agriculture wishes to initiate must be minimal and restricted to sectors which are genuinely concerned. Farmers who produce organic farming convincingly do so because we believe that we have an ancestral way of cultivating, respecting soils, animals, farmers and those who consume them. There is a package of conventional farmers who have switched to organic farming and who would prefer to stop all going back to the conventional system. There is a pillar that should not be touched or no longer needed to be touched or no longer wanted to appear and coerced to produce organic. First of all, we have to produce in the soil, the above-ground is a scandal, has no gout and is a pollution scandal. On the seed side, we need to look for natural diversity and be able to produce our seeds in Europe. The GMO does not have a place that is expensive and pointless, as the nature provided is an infinite panel of varieties, not to mention that GMOs make bioaggressors resistant and thus it serves all agriculture (except for pesticide suppliers who make money on our back). On the farm side, see the intensive farms on concrete with closed-bones that flush silage and die. The animals must have access to form and make good meat and milk, they must feed on the basic healthy diet, grass, hay, etc., no hyper processed mixture that is disguised and gives farmers attached to the cooperatives. I am talking about only topics that I know because I practice, but there is a lot to say, instead of trying to change the regulations, to support farmers engaged in organic farming directly, to help them have a normal life, to ensure that subsidies and margins do not go to industrialists and cooperatives, to help them to learn and to start listening to them before they want to change their lives from offices through regulations.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As the open-source committee chair of the Danish Data Science Community, I would like to make the following points: Trust and mutual contribution are the strengths of open source in the EU. We give back to each other across countries and public institutions. Not many private companies are able to contribute due to competition considerations and misunderstandings about what open source is and its quid pro quo nature. Even public owned companies have problems with releasing open-source due to legal restrictions. For organisations: Legal barriers are the greatest barrier, questions around liability of releasing open source and a missing legal foundation make them bigger. GDPR and the EU AI Act are important but also killing open source in especially AI ML data science due to the lack of released data from the private sector. The EU needs to help member states create a legal foundation for organisation to release open source including datasets for building AI and sharing knowledge. Furthermore, we and you need to share the good examples such as HuggingfaceHub, MistralAI and other companies who are open source first. And higlight companies who release and let there employees contribute to open source as good examples. We have found this is the best way in our community. Another good way is talking about digital soveriegnity, but we need to have a donate first approach and accept whatevers on the internet is up for grabs. Thats the different way we can do it in the EU, trusting each other and giving to each other, meeting in the process and not playing a zero sum game. This will also help center tech and smart minds around europe and our values of freedom and a rulebased society. Initiatives such as OS2 https://www.os2.eu/ and the Coral project https://huggingface.co/CoRal-project who has released great software and gold quality datasets can be used as good examples from the public sector in Denmark. The EU needs to teach how to do it safely, with risk, and a strong legal foundations that can not be used to punish contributors, but protect them. Side note: AI Generated code shows the main vulnerability with open source code. Dependcy injection can be done as a man in the middle attack on liberaries that are not accesible or do not seem to exist. EU can help manage opensource code and data for the use by all europeans if they can accept decentralisation. Some centralisation maybe accepted for increased security. The EU needs to help create a secure supply chair for open source, but it cannot lock down the internet. Organization website: ddsc.io

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Digital sovereignty should be an essential goal of the EU now more than ever. Both because of the current geopolitical context but also because private companies outside of the EU are gathering large training data from European citizens for AI models without proper consent as EU citizens don't have much knowledge about open source alternatives. The current open source infrastructure seems at an ideal stage to transition most of the EU's digital infrastructure to open source alternatives. As a personal example, I mainly use free and open-source softwares without being an expert in informatics. I also helped quite a lot of relatives to adopt these tools (e.g. Ubuntu, libreoffice, nextcloud...). The only time I needed a proprietary software recently is to fill a form from the EU about a grant I received. I believe this shows both that the transition is possible and that for now the EU is mostly acting against open source solutions at least in it's administration
