initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: At the moment, some specific companies profit for the situations we are in right now. Like Microsoft being pushed onto students in all kind of places and recently caused a lot of harm to the environment with making perfectly working devices obsolete, with the end of life of Windows 10. Those devices still run perfectly fine, but people don't know or have no other option, because of the push to use one software from one company on every device. Furthermore, should money from the public be used for causes that help everyone and not one company in specific. This includes operating system, office suit and many more. While there are already are open source projects that do those things, Libreoffice, OnlyOffice or for operating system OpenSUSE and CachyOS with KDE when you look at Germany alone. But those can often not compete with bigger scale corporations because of their limited funding. While as example I have a PC from 2011 in my house, which wouldn't be supported under windows, but I was able to install Linux on it and still use it Then there is the problem with locked ecosystems like iOS from apple or soon to be android from Google. There are barely any options and they try to limit the options of usage you have in those devices even more. Google plans on only allowing to run Google approved programs, which would kill a lot of open source projects Then there is the problem of monopolies, that aren't able to be taken down, like WhatsApp, which ignore all senses of privacy, but you can't really get rid of it, because there is no open standard that everything has to use, which is also secure and end to end encrypted and if you use another messaging app it means you won't be able to write with people that don't have those. Which can mean you are excluded of updates for something like Work, Uni or other things Open source also gives options. As an example, if something like instagram introduces that they will use all posts to train AI you are still locked in. There is no option to leave, while with open source you can have something like mastodon, which is decentralized and if they go in a direction you don't like there will probably be a fork in the near future to go back to how it was before Or let's go to hardware. CMF Watch 2 Pro can't be used without an account. Means you spend money on hardware that you can't use on your own terms. At least not without an open source alternative, like Gadgetbridge (probably also at risk with the new Android rules) Which means you are either being spied on, limited by choice, locked in to an ecosystem, with only a theoretical option being available, as if you use that you are basically out of society Open source also means more local work. A lot of open source projects are at home in the eu, like CachyOS, OpenSUSE, KDE, Tuta, Filen, Tuxedo and so many more. And honestly maybe if we are lucky also fewer Monopolies, which would be better for everyone After all open source isn't just about freedom, it is about community, transparency, ownership and not for corporate greed And after all that, I have a request. Please put all the EU apps open source alternatives to the app stores of the devices, and if possible make them open source as well. That is how you show support

Google plans on only allowing to run Google approved programs, which would kill a lot of open source projects Then there is the problem of monopolies, that aren't able to be taken down, like WhatsApp, which ignore all senses of privacy, but you can't really get rid of it, because there is no open standard that everything has to use, which is also secure and end to end encrypted and if you use another messaging app it means you won't be able to write with people that don't have those. Which can mean you are excluded of updates for something like Work, Uni or other things Open source also gives options. As an example, if something like instagram introduces that they will use all posts to train AI you are still locked in. There is no option to leave, while with open source you can have something like mastodon, which is decentralized and if they go in a direction you don't like there will probably be a fork in the near future to go back to how it was before Or let's go to hardware. CMF Watch 2 Pro can't be used without an account. Means you spend money on hardware that you can't use on your own terms. At least not without an open source alternative, like Gadgetbridge (probably also at risk with the new Android rules) Which means you are either being spied on, limited by choice, locked in to an ecosystem, with only a theoretical option being available, as if you use that you are basically out of society Open source also means more local work. A lot of open source projects are at home in the eu, like CachyOS, OpenSUSE, KDE, Tuta, Filen, Tuxedo and so many more. And honestly maybe if we are lucky also fewer Monopolies, which would be better for everyone After all open source isn't just about freedom, it is about community, transparency, ownership and not for corporate greed And after all that, I have a request. Please put all the EU apps open source alternatives to the app stores of the devices, and if possible make them open source as well. That is how you show support

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Opinion on the covering of outdoor climate areas in organic pig farming: Annex II, Part II, Section 1.6 Accommodation and husbandry practices, in particular 1.6.5 (open air areas may be partially covered) Proposal to adapt the legislation to the separation of rules by species of livestock: The entire Part II of the Annex should be broken down into species-specific rules in order to meet the different biological and ethological needs of the species. Deletion of open air areas: The term is technically blurred and leaves room for interpretation in practice. A clear distinction between outdoor climate areas, open-air areas and pastures would be more appropriate. Open air areas should be deleted or clearly redefined. Making the roofing arrangements more flexible: The possibility of covering should depend on: Animal species Regional climate conditions Animal welfare and environmental objectives for pigs should be explicitly allowed to cover up to 90 % of the outdoor climate area, while the excretion area remains open. Justification for biology of pigs: Unlike cattle or horses, pigs derive from wild boar living in forest habitats. Domestic pigs are often not pigmented and are therefore particularly vulnerable to heat stress and sunburn. Extensive cover is therefore essential for animal welfare reasons. Health benefits: Roofing reliably protects piglets and young animals from wet, draught and weather stress. This reduces the risk of respiratory diseases, such as pneumonia, which can pose serious problems in organic farming without routine medication. Environmental and climate protection: Roofing minimises soil humidification, thereby reducing the formation and release of ammonia. At the same time, nitrogen from excrements can be better bonded and used as a valuable organic fertiliser in organic crop production. This strengthens regional nutrient cycles and reduces environmental pressures. Maintenance of open-air nature: A covered outdoor climate area remains an open-air area as long as fresh air, daylight and movement are guaranteed. Adapted cover is therefore not an obstacle to the nature of organic livestock farming.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: 1. What are the strengths and weaknesses of the EU open-source sector? What are the main barriers that hamper (i) adoption and maintenance of high-quality and secure open source; and (ii) sustainable contributions to open-source communities? 1i. Adoption requires support until a critical user mass is reached. Maintenance also requires support or dedicated individuals. 1ii. Sustainable contributions require the software to have enough value and support to attract open source developers, as well as good project management by maintainers. 2. What is the added value of open source for the public and private sectors? Please provide concrete examples, including the factors (such as cost, risk, lock-in, security, innovation, among others) that are most important to assess the added value. Having good open source software readily available can act as enabler for development: People can use them as tools for no cost to support their own projects (academic and commercial), so long as the licensing is suitable. In addition, open source safeguards the availability and security of critical software, which reduces risks: Open source software cannot be taken away or provided at high cost, and compromising it is much harder. 3. What concrete measures and actions may be taken at EU level to support the development and growth of the EU open-source sector and contribute to the EUs technological sovereignty and cybersecurity agenda? * Support critical open source projects with funding * Focus on supporting/building alternatives to software that is closed-source and market-dominant. 4 &5. What technology areas should be prioritised and why? In what sectors could an increased use of open source lead to increased competitiveness and cyber resilience? * Smartphone hardware & software; Phones are very locked-down computing machines, and not controlled by EU-based companies. * Internet services (cloud services, search engines) * Communications, Instant Messaging, Social Media * Machine learning

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The Malta Maritime Forum (MMF) reaffirms its full commitment to climate action and maritime sustainability. However, the success of maritime decarbonisation efforts depends fundamentally on a single, globally coherent regulatory framework. The shipping industry is inherently international. Unilateral and regional measures like the EU ETS and Fuel EU Maritime, risk creating unfair competition, regulatory overlap and investment uncertainty that undermine the very objectives they seek to achieve. The EU ETS was extended to the maritime industry, in order to spur the International process in IMO. Now that in April 2025, the IMO has reached an ambitious agreement for a global framework, the MMF strongly urges the European Commission and EU Member States to unequivocally support its formal adoption in October 2025. Once adopted, it is crucial that all existing regional and unilateral measuresincluding the EU regimes under the ETS and FuelEU Maritimeare replaced to ensure full harmonisation and legal coherence. The aim must be clear - a single, predictable international regime is the only viable pathway for fair and effective decarbonisation across the global shipping sector Impact Between EU and Non EU Ports: The lack of a globally harmonised framework creates competitive distortions for EU-flagged vessels, exacerbates investment uncertainty, and imposes administrative burdens that disproportionately affect smaller and medium-sized shipping operators. This submission also outlines the disproportionate impacts of the EU ETS maritime on EU ports in the Mediterranean, with specific emphasis on small island Member States like Malta. It documents early evidence of: Business leakage: major shipping lines shifting transshipment operations to non-EU ports (e.g., Tangier Med, Damietta, Abu Qir), Carbon leakage: increased emissions due to longer sailings and added feeder legs, Loss of maritime connectivity: a 13.2% decline in ship calls at Malta Freeport, and Undermining of EU principles: including competitiveness, cohesion, fair competition, and socio-economic resilience. These risks compromise not only port competitiveness but the broader viability of EU shipping as a whole. Recommendation ad interim of global framework In the interim and as long as the EU ETS remains in effect, it is essential that: Revenues generated at EU and national levels are reinvested directly into the maritime sector, particularly to bridge the cost gap between conventional and zero-/low-carbon fuels; The cost recovery mechanism embedded in the IMO agreement is fully and unconditionally transposed into EU law to ensure fairness and certainty. No duplication or overlap of compliance obligations exists between EU and international regimes. There is an urgent reassessment of the ETSs design and implementation. Any revision process must consider both ex-post evidence and ex-ante risks, especially the strategic build-up of transshipment capacity in neighbouring non-EU ports. A transition to a global, harmonised framework under the IMO must be the guiding objective. PLEASE SEE FULL POSITION IN ATTACHED DOCUMENT

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I'd like to provide two pieces of feedback, besides my general support for open-source software and hardware (such as 3d printers): * First, I've heard people from various background, including someone in charge of a hospital, say that they could not afford open-source. Not because it is difficult to use, not because it is failing often, the reasoning could never reach that point: the problem is, when there is an issue, they need to be able to call someone and have it fixed soon. This creates a dependency on closed-source technologies, and often, when the company fails, hospitals end up with an unsupported mess and without the funds to replace it. This is true at small scale for specialised software and at larger scale for general software (unsupported and insecure windows XP systems in critical environments, for example). But when they are big enough, these software become infrastructure, and could be managed as such. Instead of every public service paying for windows licenses, these millions (billions?) of euros could be used to provide support for open source technologies, through organisations similar to the ones responsible for electricity infrastructure. This would naturally help improve the open-source software public services use, keep competent devs happy and in the eu, enable widespread adoption by companies as the projects become more common and standard, reduce dependency on closed-source, and decrease costs through scale and federation of efforts. Companies could probably also pay for that support. * Existing funding solutions for open source are mostly for mature projects, but which opportunities help new projects launch? Which hackathon follows up with a way to fund a bunch of new open source projects sustainably, long enough for them to become big enough? Startups need capital investment before launching. Open source software, which enables everyone to be productive, requires passionate people sacrificing their free time. How much more would we have if some people could pay rent for moonshot projects? Could we get an open-source secure sms system for example (an open source implementation of Rich Communication Services, which should also probably be infrastructure) ? Linux and a lot of key projects started very small and were already useful. Significant projects nowadays require much more investment to be truly independent (see the ladybird browser), because there is simply much more existing hardware and standards to support. We need to think of how the next generation of open source software will be funded, not just how current software can survive.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: While I fully recognise the long-established importance of open source projects such as Linux and the broader open source ecosystem, I would like to draw attention to a strategic area that is, in my view, even more critical for Europes digital sovereignty: the governance of internet access technologies, particularly web browsers and browser engines. Today, effective control over how citizens and institutions access the internet is concentrated in the hands of a very small number of predominantly US-based companies, notably Google, Apple, and Mozilla, through their dominance of browser markets and underlying engine technologies. This concentration raises concerns not only from a competition perspective, but also in terms of technological sovereignty, long-term security, and democratic accountability. Recent developments in the US political and regulatory landscape further underline the risks of such dependency. In this context, I believe the EU should consider reigniting and strengthening efforts to support genuinely open, independent browser engine alternatives through sustained and serious public funding. Such efforts would help diversify the technological foundations of the web, reduce systemic dependencies, and ensure that core internet infrastructure aligns with European values and legal frameworks. One promising example is the Servo browser engine project, which places a strong emphasis on memory safety, modern architecture, and long-term maintainability, while remaining truly open source. Targeted EU investment in initiatives of this kind could play a key role in improving both the safety and resilience of the digital ecosystem. Supporting alternative open source browser engines would be a concrete and impactful step toward breaking the current de facto monopolistic structure of browser technologies and strengthening Europes technological independence in a domain that underpins almost all digital activity.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Agronomes and Véinaires Sans Frontières (AVSF) is a French association of international solidarity which has been operating for more than 40 years with certified organic farmers’ organisations in developing countries. As part of our field operations with organisations of small producers certified as organic under the 2018/848 group of operators, we have made the following findings: o The new rules (more numerous inspections, systematic sampling, creation of legal structures for mixed cooperatives or more than 2000 members), motivated by a requirement of reliability and credibility of the control systems assumed to be cheaper in the southern countries, lead to an explosion in costs, increased administrative burdens, a high risk of de-structuring cooperatives, decertification for fragile organisations and disruptions of supply to Europe.o The risk of circumvention of a regulatory step that finally recognises collective certification, in particular by requiring that the certificate should henceforth belong to producer groups and not to export companies. This right of ownership recognised on paper may in fact encounter resistance and attempts to circumvent organic businesses. In the light of these findings, we welcome the European Commission’s efforts to simplify the regulation, which is intended to remove these difficulties for producers, their organisations and their European partner companies (risks of disruption of supply and inflation of organic products). We therefore request that: — Postpone the date of application of the rules to 31/12/2026 in order to allow operators to adapt to the new requirements and thus avoid serious legal uncertainties for operators and control bodies which would lead to waves of decertification and supply disruptions. — Remove the legal entity for groups of operators with more than 2000 members and the ban on mixed cooperatives, as these criteria require an artificial restructuring of cooperatives with the creation of separate sub-groups that go beyond the administrative procedures and costs involved, weaken the collective dynamics, the economic trajectory and the investment projects of cooperatives. Many cooperatives react to this new criterion by excluding producers who produce the least products or by reducing the number of organic products marketed. — Base the fixed rates of inspection and sampling on the risks and whether or not control plans based on residue analyses carried out by POs or purchasers exist. Imposing the sampling of 2 % of producers by the certification bodies for pesticide residue analysis is not relevant, as analyses often add to analyses already carried out during control plans that already apply and thus lead to unjustified overruns. — Remove the turnover limits of EUR 25 000 which are not adapted to the current market situation. Cocoa prices, which have been stagnating for years between 3 000 and 4 000 USD/tonne, have now reached USD 8 000/tonne. Finally, this increase makes it possible to pay producers better. It would be inconsistent to exclude small producers from collective certification on the grounds that their income has increased as a result of this price improvement. — Maintain ownership of the organic certificate on behalf of producer groups, as this is a key condition for strengthening their autonomy and enabling them to freely choose their trading partners. It is essential to act for the strict application of this rule, so that organic certificates are systematically issued on behalf of groups.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The Global Electronics Association (formerly IPC) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the revision of the EU public procurement rules through this call for evidence. Public procurement offers a meaningful, strategic instrument for shaping Europes long-term industrial, technological, and security capabilities. In the electronics sector in particular, procurement decisions play a decisive role in determining whether Europe will retain the capacity to design and build the sensitive and cutting-edge systems on which its security, resilience, and competitiveness depend. Please see attached our Position.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The European Conference of Transport Research Institutes (ECTRI) welcomes this public consultation on the European Research Area (ERA) Act. The development and full realisation of the ERA is critical to advance the free movement of researchers, scientific knowledge, and technology in the EU. To further strengthen this fifth freedom, ECTRI recommends addressing the following topics in the Impact Assessment (IA) of the future ERA Act: 1. EU Member States should be engaged in boosting the ERA in critical research areas through their national R&I plans, and improving the use of scientific knowledge in decision-making. For instance, in the transport sector, such plans, together with the setting up or empowerment of scientific advisory groups, would accelerate the transition towards multimodal, smart, and sustainable mobility, including decarbonisation, user-centric mobility services and advances on the circular economy. Additionally, the ERA Act should aim at reducing imbalances within the EU, as disparities in research capacity, infrastructure, salaries, and access to funding weaken both cohesion and competitiveness. 2. To materialise the fifth freedom, there is a need to dismantle pervasive administrative and legal barriers. A simplified and agile framework, with a more consistent and coordinated approach across Member States is necessary, preventing the EU from lagging behind in global competition, while fostering cooperation among countries, companies, and research centres. This includes the empowerment of research infrastructures and the facilitation of free access to them by EU researchers. 3. Retaining talents is critical for Europe. Research careers need to be better valued in EU Member States, especially in Southern and Eastern Europe, and salaries should be harmonised. Improvements in a common framework applicable to researchers and research organisations in Europe is of utmost importance. Such common framework should also promote gender equality and equal opportunities. Addressing these through flexible career paths, mentorship, and targeted support is essential to ensure that the ERA fully benefits from diverse talent and perspectives. 4. The need for formal ratification at both EU and national levels are factors slowing down decision making and limiting the ERAs agility in responding to emerging challenges. Enhanced coordination mechanisms and simplified decision-making rules in EU research policy are necessary. 5. European researchers should benefit from Pan-European programs facilitating knowledge transfer, and skills enhancement in key sectors, including transport. To unlock public-private cooperation, one important measure is to merge all existing platforms of collaboration where industry and enterprises, SMEs, and other stakeholders cooperate and make better use of these connections in the future Framework Programme, including sound exploitation plans of the patents/products/services developed. It should be complemented by National R&I Plans in all Member States fully aligned with the common agreed priorities. In the transport sector, cities might play a central role in this regard, as active partners to ensure that research outcomes deliver tangible benefits for citizens and support the green and digital transitions. 6. The ERA Act and its impact assessment is understood by ECTRI as an instrument that can mitigate geopolitical risk and re-ignite international cooperation, with measures to enable critical partnerships with key third countries. 7. The Impact Assessment should be complemented by a business plan providing the indicators needed to advance towards an efficient, fair, and resilient ERA. Such business plan should ensure a fair distribution of benefits and costs across all parties during the implementation of the ERA Act.
topic: Employment and social affairs, Research and innovation, Single market
text_raw: The European Conference of Transport Research Institutes (ECTRI) welcomes this public consultation on the European Research Area (ERA) Act. The development and full realisation of the ERA is critical to advance the free movement of researchers, scientific knowledge, and technology in the EU. To further strengthen this fifth freedom, ECTRI recommends addressing the following topics in the Impact Assessment (IA) of the future ERA Act: 1. EU Member States should be engaged in boosting the ERA in critical research areas through their national R&I plans, and improving the use of scientific knowledge in decision-making. For instance, in the transport sector, such plans, together with the setting up or empowerment of scientific advisory groups, would accelerate the transition towards multimodal, smart, and sustainable mobility, including decarbonisation, user-centric mobility services and advances on the circular economy. Additionally, the ERA Act should aim at reducing imbalances within the EU, as disparities in research capacity, infrastructure, salaries, and access to funding weaken both cohesion and competitiveness. 2. To materialise the fifth freedom, there is a need to dismantle pervasive administrative and legal barriers. A simplified and agile framework, with a more consistent and coordinated approach across Member States is necessary, preventing the EU from lagging behind in global competition, while fostering cooperation among countries, companies, and research centres. This includes the empowerment of research infrastructures and the facilitation of free access to them by EU researchers. 3. Retaining talents is critical for Europe. Research careers need to be better valued in EU Member States, especially in Southern and Eastern Europe, and salaries should be harmonised. Improvements in a common framework applicable to researchers and research organisations in Europe is of utmost importance. Such common framework should also promote gender equality and equal opportunities. Addressing these through flexible career paths, mentorship, and targeted support is essential to ensure that the ERA fully benefits from diverse talent and perspectives. 4. The need for formal ratification at both EU and national levels are factors slowing down decision making and limiting the ERAs agility in responding to emerging challenges. Enhanced coordination mechanisms and simplified decision-making rules in EU research policy are necessary. 5. European researchers should benefit from Pan-European programs facilitating knowledge transfer, and skills enhancement in key sectors, including transport. To unlock public-private cooperation, one important measure is to merge all existing platforms of collaboration where industry and enterprises, SMEs, and other stakeholders cooperate and make better use of these connections in the future Framework Programme, including sound exploitation plans of the patents/products/services developed. It should be complemented by National R&I Plans in all Member States fully aligned with the common agreed priorities. In the transport sector, cities might play a central role in this regard, as active partners to ensure that research outcomes deliver tangible benefits for citizens and support the green and digital transitions. 6. The ERA Act and its impact assessment is understood by ECTRI as an instrument that can mitigate geopolitical risk and re-ignite international cooperation, with measures to enable critical partnerships with key third countries. 7. The Impact Assessment should be complemented by a business plan providing the indicators needed to advance towards an efficient, fair, and resilient ERA. Such business plan should ensure a fair distribution of benefits and costs across all parties during the implementation of the ERA Act.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: As Krakowski Holding Komunalny SA, we strongly oppose the inclusion of Waste-to-Energy (WtE) facilities in the EU ETS 1. In our view, the current ETS system is not the right tool to reduce GHG emissions in the waste sector. Only a thorough structural reform could make it effective for this purpose. WtE plays a vital role in treating non-recyclable waste and provides an essential public service. We cannot influence the composition of waste it is determined by consumers and local systems. Many plastic products still cannot be recycled due to harmful additives and must be safely destroyed in WtE plants. Inclusion of only WtE in the ETS, without addressing landfilling or MBT plants, risks pushing waste toward less sustainable options. Any regulation should cover the entire residual waste sector, be proportionate, and avoid putting excessive financial pressure on municipalities or citizens. We believe the Polluter Pays Principle should target producers of unrecyclable fossil-based materials. The ETS would increase gate fees and impact the entire waste chain, including recyclers. The MRV rules for WtE must reflect the complexity of waste and allow more flexibility. One-size-fits-all monitoring requirements do not work in our diverse sector. Training is essential, and early implementation years should allow room for adaptation. We see no evidence that ETS inclusion would significantly boost recycling or separate collection. A better way forward is to focus on Extended Producer Responsibility for fossil-based product manufacturers.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Public procurement remains an underutilised strategic policy instrument in the EU, particularly in its capacity to support social enterprises, impact-driven enterprises and innovative startups. As highlighted in Impact Europes contribution to the Commissions evaluation of the Public Procurement Directives in March 2025, these actors continue to face structural barriers to accessing procurement opportunities, including administratively complex procedures, limited engagement with contracting authorities, insufficient data on social and environmental outcomes, and the continued predominance of price-based award decisions. While the existing framework allows for the integration of social, green and innovative considerations, their voluntary and uneven application across Member States has resulted in limited impact in practice. The current consultation on the revision of EU public procurement rules marks a critical opportunity to move from evaluation to action. Impact Europe considers it essential to better align public procurement with the EUs strategic objectives on competitiveness, innovation and strategic autonomy, while ensuring the delivery of measurable social and environmental value. Public procurement can play a key role in modernising public services and scaling social and impact-driven innovation, particularly in a context of fiscal constraints and demographic change. Building on its previous contribution, Impact Europe puts forward a set of targeted recommendations to strengthen the strategic use of public procurement, including value-based award decisions, improved market access, greater transparency and participation, measurable targets, and the coherent integration of social, green and innovative criteria across procurement stages. These proposals aim to support a more coherent and future-oriented EU procurement framework that enables fair competition, improves cross-border participation and allows social enterprises, impact-driven enterprises and innovative startups to compete based on the added value they bring forward. Read Impact Europe's recommendations further in the attached submission.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The EU in the past initiated multiple instruments (cf. Gaia-X, IPCEI-CIS, ...), many EU countries invest into local digital sovereignty initiatives, somewhat not coordinated EU-wide yet (cf. numerique.gouv.fr, zendis.de and many more). There is not a SINGLE above initiative that does not highly rely upon neutrally governed open-source software (OSS), predominantly the ecosystem around the Linux Foundation, CNCF, etc. but of course also Eclipse and Apache. Hereby, the most relevant consideration is that open-source ownership must be held by a neutral foundation, a digital commons, where funding is jointly governed by interested parties using that open source in commercial products/services. Beware: there are many single vendor owned oss projects, often marketed under the OSS-Flag and thereby benefitting from public funding and developer trust. Such open source should be explicitly flagged, and special scrutiny must be applied by the EU for consideration, as myriad negative examples (license rug-pulls, acquisitions by hyperscaler, etc.) prove that with increasing adoption, companies ditch the digital commons in favor of monetarization. We have a positive, forward-looking example in the EU: While IPCEI-CIS, for example, "welcomed" participants to contribute their efforts into open-source, no obligations were contractually required. Nonetheless, a number of participants (some of the largest European enterprises, together with many smaller partners with highly specialized expertise) recognized the failure and created the necessary "open-source foundation infrastructure" to jointly and freely share core efforts: see https://NeoNephos.org/introduction . NeoNephos' mission statement is highly relevant for the 8RA multi-provider cloud-edge continuum goal (https://youtu.be/8_GUj1tLdPo). A recent landmark study by The Harvard Business School (https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4693148) quantified the massive financial impact of OSS: * Demand-Side Value: Estimated at $8.8 trillion. This represents the replacement cost if every firm using OSS had to develop that software internally from scratch. * Supply-Side Value: Estimated at $4.15 billion, which is the direct cost required to recreate the most widely used OSS packages once. For the EU it would be FUTILE not to consider OSS as the most important tool for its digital sovereignty ambitions. The EU could employ a pattern in technology business & economics commonly known as commoditize your complement, only on an EU-wide scale, by contractually obliging all beneficiaries of EU-funding towards contributing towards foundation-led OSS. (Also, in lieu of technology disruption with AI, most commercial and dominating players double down on OSS, cf. Linux Foundation led AAIF.io).

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: The European Energy Information Sharing and Analysis Centre (EE-ISAC) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commissions consultation on the revision of Regulation (EU) 2019/881 (the Cybersecurity Act). EE-ISAC is a trust-based, industry-driven information-sharing network that brings together stakeholders from the energy sector, academia, public authorities, and cybersecurity providers to enhance the cyber resilience of Europes energy critical infrastructure. Since its foundation in 2015, EE-ISAC has actively promoted cross-sectoral and international collaboration, including with ISACs in Japan and the United States, and maintains strong engagement with relevant EU-level initiatives. EE-ISAC supports platforms like the Malware Information Sharing Platform (MISP) and ENISAs Nextcloud instance as important tools for enabling near real-time exchange of threat intelligence. These instruments should be further institutionalised under ENISAs mandate to support secure and efficient information sharing among trusted stakeholders. EE-ISAC welcomes the Commissions initiative to revise the Cybersecurity Act, particularly the efforts to clarify ENISAs mandate and reinforce the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework. However, EE-ISAC highlights a critical operational gap regarding ENISAs role as an effective hub for cybersecurity information exchange. While Articles 3(2) and 7 of the current Act mandate ENISA to facilitate cooperation and information sharing, this role has not been fully implemented in practice. This was evident during recent cross-border energy sector incidents (e.g., those affecting Spain and Portugal), where ISACs and other stakeholders were not supported by coordinated EU-level situational awareness or timely dissemination of threat intelligence. EE-ISAC believes the revised Cybersecurity Act must address this gap decisively. To this end, EE-ISAC recommends that the revised Act should: - Explicitly strengthen ENISAs operational role as a central EU clearinghouse for cybersecurity threat intelligence, especially in cross-border and multi-sector scenarios; - Establish clear legal and procedural mechanisms that allow ENISA to coordinate and share threat information actively with national authorities and trusted communities, including ISACs, in line with the NIS2 Directive (Directive (EU) 2022/2555), notably Articles 7 and 8; - Ensure that ENISA is not only granted the necessary mandate but also the resources, authority, and technical capabilities to serve as a trusted operational node for structured, secure, and timely information exchange. - Enhancing ENISAs proactive coordination capabilities would improve the EUs collective cybersecurity preparedness, build trust across sectors and Member States, and ensure more effective responses to cyber threats affecting critical infrastructure such as energy. EE-ISAC stands ready to support this process and contribute its expertise to a stronger European cybersecurity framework.

feedback_text: I am deeply concerned about the absolute dominance of digital products of big techs from outside the EU. Microsoft and Adobe to name a few. I believe the EU should focus on suverenity in this aspect, before focusing on the suverenity in material aspects. And open softwares are a perfect opportunity for this. Becoming less dependent on Microsoft's Windows and Office are used and viewed as exclusive tools in offices and schools. They are not. EU needs to use it's own resources which are already available and perfectly able to give viable alternatives. There are dozens of free, stable and user friendly Linux operation systems such as blackPanther OS, Debian, Ubuntu, Linux Mint, Fedora, Zorin OS. All of them provide familiar interfaces, ready to use in offices, schools after installation. They offer free to use office program package Libre Office by deault, there is no compatibility issues with each other. All of them has long history (blackPanther OS is 23 years old, continuesly developed and updated, very stable and fresh Linux, Debian is one of the oldest Linux with big and stable community, Ubuntu has the biggest community and hardware compatibility, Fedora focuses on being always fresh and stable, Zorin OS and Linux Mint focuses on familiarity to Windows). By switching offices and schools from Windows and Microsoft Office would greatly reduce producing e-waste and corruption on government level, because Linux is not lobby oriented as Microsoft is and Linux is not interested buying politicians and lawmakers as Microsoft is. blackPanther OS is focusing to offer free availability of softwares for communities, schools and handicapped people since its birth in 2003.

feedback_text: Payment systems such as Mastercard that operate globally. Applications such as Google Pay or Samsung Pay. Open source search engines that respect privacy. Do not miss out on Mistral and severely restrict the purchase of shares by foreign companies in strategic European companies. Device security certifications not monopolised by Google or a few companies with conflicts of interest on operating systems, thus involving European and non-European entities such as GraphenOS. Create new open source operating systems for augmented reality glasses and smartphones. Adaptive drivers for operating systems such as Linux or impose open source drivers for new hardware. Encourage innovative start-ups in the sector without creating zombie companies that live off welfare and squandered public funds. A browser that truly respects privacy. Limit the data that applications such as TikTok etc. can read and send. Provide more funding to the Matrix Foundation and DeltaChat by bringing in experts to have a greater impact on the market. WhatsApp should be truly obliged to be interoperable with Matrix, Signal and DeltaChat etc. without applying its own privacy conditions in hybrid chats, but rather predefined European rules. Google is taking over everything, which is not good at all.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: Inclusive education is one of the key areas for ensuring the full participation of persons with disabilities and special needs in the society and everyday life. ETUCE and EFEE's most recent research on inclusion of persons with disabilities and special needs in education and teaching profession (project 'Inclusive education for all needs: European Sectoral Social Partners in Education promoting inclusion of persons with special needs in education: https://www.csee-etuce.org/en/item/4413) shows that systemic barriers to inclusive education persist such as - Inadequate education funding in general and insufficient dedicated investment in inclusive and special education; - A significant and growing recruitment and retention crisis in the teaching professions; - Limited professional development opportunities for education personnel in inclusive and special education strategies and methods. Therefore, ETUCE and EFEE policy recommendations for enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities would be: 1. Prioritise sustained, long-term investment in inclusive education to strengthen social cohesion, reduce discrimination and advance inclusive societies. 2. Finance the continuing professional development of education personnel in inclusive and special education, and ensure that provisions for CPD are included in collective agreements. 3. Support the professional autonomy and discretion of education professionals, enabling them to determine the most effective approaches to support students with special educational needs and disabilities. 4. Strengthen cross-sectoral collaboration between government and non-government actors across education, health and social care at local, regional and national levels to ensure integrated and equitable service provision. 5. Invest in enhancing the attractiveness of the teaching professions to improve supply of appropriately qualified teachers, specialists and support personnel. 6. Reduce the bureaucracy and administrative burden in inclusive and special education, enabling education personnel to dedicate their time and expertise to supporting all students. 6. Reframe inclusive education as a fundamental human right of all children, young people and adults, and monitor progress on the policy frameworks. 7. Engage students, parents and the wider educational communities to ensure that their voices are heard in policy development. Furthermore, ETUCE and EFEE highlight that disability representation within the education workforce remains low, and education personnel with disabilities face considerable obstacles in professional preparation and practice which are outlined in the attached documents. Therefore, we recommend the following policy adjustments for disability-inclusive employment in education sector (more recommendations in the attachment): 1. Develop inclusive teacher policies with education social partners, actively including disabled voices. 2. Invest in disability equality and anti-discrimination training for education personnel to prevent discrimination and promote inclusive workplaces. 3. Support disability-awareness and knowledge exchange by establishing and actively engaging in networks, forums and events where disabled persons organisations can lead and shape discussions. 4. Ensure that educational workplaces receive adequate funding to invest in resources to support the full inclusion of education personnel with disabilities, including access to all necessary physical, digital and technological accommodations. 5. Embed and monitor disability rights in social dialogue mechanisms to ensure they inform policies and agreements. 6. Collect disability data on education personnel in compliance with national data protection legislation and use it to monitor employment practices. 7. Invest in the physical and digital learning environment to ensure that new educational institutions are inclusive by design and that existing institutions are fully accessible to all employees.

Ensure that educational workplaces receive adequate funding to invest in resources to support the full inclusion of education personnel with disabilities, including access to all necessary physical, digital and technological accommodations. 5. Embed and monitor disability rights in social dialogue mechanisms to ensure they inform policies and agreements. 6. Collect disability data on education personnel in compliance with national data protection legislation and use it to monitor employment practices. 7. Invest in the physical and digital learning environment to ensure that new educational institutions are inclusive by design and that existing institutions are fully accessible to all employees.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The European Union should establish a unified portfolio of sovereign, opensource digital products to strengthen Europes technological independence and resilience. This initiative should focus on strategic alternatives to dominant nonEU technology providers, with a clear priority roadmap. Key focus areas could include: Developing a European alternative to Cloudflaretype network and security infrastructure. Creating scalable, EUbased cloud platforms to reduce reliance on hyperscalers such as Google Cloud, AWS, and Microsoft Azure. Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace replacements Windows commands 73% of EU desktop OS, while Android (Google) and iOS (Apple) near 100% of mobile; Google Search exceeds 89%. Browsers, social media (Meta platforms), and marketplaces like Amazon further entrench US control over consumer and data flows. Identifying and replacing other critical U.S.based tools currently monopolizing EU markets (e.g., communication platforms, productivity suites, analytics services). To reduce fragmentation, this effort should be coordinated by a central body or visionary leader from the opensource community, ensuring coherence and momentum,perhaps under the mentorship of figures such as Linus Torvalds or equivalent European innovators. The EU should maintain a dedicated and transparent online repository showcasing these sovereign products, encouraging both public and private sector adoption. Awareness campaigns, including targeted digital advertising, should promote these projects to build recognition and trust among EU citizens and enterprises. Moreover, the EU should ensure that these solutions are costcompetitive with U.S. alternatives, offering financial and regulatory incentives for EU governments and companies to adopt and contribute to them.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Question 1: The EU IT industry primarily consists of smaller players with SAP being the notable exception. This applies also to the OSS sector. The IT industry is full of network effects creating "winner-takes-it-all" markets where only one or a very small number of players can succeed. We see that situation with cloud infrastructure providers. Likewise, OSS players are relatively small in Europe. Thanks to the ability to collaborate on open standards and open source code, they can fight above their weight class, but still fail to bolster real competition in the larger platform markets. Meanwhile, big tech companies in the US have understood how to leverage Open Source technology to their advantage, using the rights that non-reciprocal (permissive) OSS licenses give to enhance software without contributing back. That said, they actually do strategic investments into OSS where it suits them and this way contribute more than large companies in Europe do. The latter have largely failed to understand the lever Open Source gives to them. Barriers in Europe is a highly fragmented market with fragmented regulation. And too many companies with traditional business culture (in risk-averse society) avoiding the risks of a fast moving software industry. There is no capital market that understands and supports startups in this space to a sufficient degree. Later stage funding for successful IT startups in Europe required for scaling more often than not comes from the US. Question 2: A success story is the Sovereign Cloud Stack (SCS) where cloud providers have agreed on common standards for user-facing APIs and also collaborated on open source engineering work to deliver a resilient platform together. This avoids customers to become locked-in to a single provider, as switching inside (or combining) a network of coopeting providers is technically very easy. The ByCS.de (Bavarian School Cloud) is a successful workload on top of an SCS cloud, serving more than a million pupils, teachers and parents. Question 3: The funding projects need to ensure not to be too fragmented, creating too disconnected pockets of innovation which may not fit together. That said, funding is a great way to provide the initial open source investments to build successful business models. Funding may also be a requirement for infrastructure project maintenance, where the common access is so beneficial that value extraction with a commercial model may not be desirable. For most areas, open source should be created and maintained by a network of collaborating companies. These should fund the development from customers that buy their services such as support, maintenance, trainings, consulting. The EU should thus have procurement policies in place that prefer EU open source companies. The contribution to the (global!) open source projects needs to be an important criterium for the selection of the vendor, this way ensuring that the digital public good does benefit from its use. Procurement needs to measure & prioritize true sovereignty, The EU has strict regulation on data protection (GDPR) as well as DSA and DMA. However the adequacy decision subverts GDPR. The regulation is only marginally enforced causing a moral hazard for those that want to follow the laws and creates little incentives to work with smaller (and initially less capable) European vendors that do comply. Question 4: The foundations of our IT infrastructure are what most other projects depend upon and are thus the most important. - The ability to design and manufacture computer chips needs to be built. Takes decade and needs heavy investments. - We need good open source cloud and container platform technologies and a network of operators that provide services. Much easier. - The situation in Operating Systems is better with Linux. - The collaboration space would benefit from escaping the M365 monopoly with public sector initiatives such as La Suite and openDesk. Question 5: (see answer to 4)

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: As a citizen and family member of a person with a disability, I believe that support should be provided both to caregivers and to organisations that take care of the most complex cases that cannot be handled within the family, but always with a vision and mission of universal access to the rights of persons with disabilities and the development of the greatest possible autonomy and independent living. Ensure support for these organisations (associations, cooperatives), not only in economic terms, but also in terms of training and finding competent human resources (pool of available staff to be recruited), through targeted European calls for proposals.
topic: Consumers, Employment and social affairs, Justice and fundamental rights

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: I believe Europe needs strong cybersecurity, but also clear, balanced rules that support innovation and openness. Regulation should protect users, but also help businesses grownot hold them back. A practical, fair approach, regardless of the country of origin, will strengthen security while keeping Europe competitive in the digital world.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: It is expected by the market to include the incineration of municipal waste in the ETS with a mechanism to ensure that the costs of emission allowances are covered by waste producers rather than passed on to consumers in fees, so that this gives the right signal to companies that introduce waste in order to achieve the 1st objective. EU and Member State policies should aim to reduce waste production, increase recycling and reduce the incineration of municipal waste. Passing on the costs of emission allowances to packaging manufacturers will contribute to this objective.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The EU should mandate free+open standards for all government procurements. This allows everyone to compete at the same level and to benefit from integrations that otherwise would not be possible. It will also reduce the walled-garden push that big-tech is performing. It is essential to approach IT systems as essential infrastructure similarly to water, sewage, and electricity.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: The Japan Business Council in Europe (JBCE) welcomes the opportunity to provide its feedback on the revision of the Public Procurement directives as part of the Call for Evidence launched on 3 November 2025. Indeed, while acknowledging the strategic imperative of the European Union (EU) to secure its own competitiveness and economic security agenda, JBCE has been actively promoting since Spring 2025 a Made with Common Values approach as an alternative to a Made in EU proposal. This approach would enable the EU to achieve its objectives while respecting its commitments towards its trade partners, including Japan. As a cross-sectoral organisation, JBCE also wishes to highlight a few concerns related to the functioning of public procurement schemes throughout the EU. Please find attached our position paper.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: The Confédération des Petites et Moyennes Entreprises (CPME) represents French SMEs across all sectors and regions. In its response to the consultation on the EU Cybersecurity Act, CPME supports the objective of strengthening cybersecurity, but stresses the need for a more accessible, consistent and proportionate framework. In particular, CPME calls for better alignment between the Cybersecurity Act, NIS 2 and related regulations, in order to reduce unnecessary compliance burdens on SMEs and support their digital resilience. Please find enclosed our full contribution for your consideration.

In its response to the consultation on the EU Cybersecurity Act, CPME supports the objective of strengthening cybersecurity, but stresses the need for a more accessible, consistent and proportionate framework. In particular, CPME calls for better alignment between the Cybersecurity Act, NIS 2 and related regulations, in order to reduce unnecessary compliance burdens on SMEs and support their digital resilience. Please find enclosed our full contribution for your consideration.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: EU should strongly support not only in declaration but in action the development and widespread adoption of free software and copyleft licenses, like Affero GPL and EUPL. Public funding must contribute to increasing the common good in a way that makes it difficult for Big Tech to exploit it without contributing back. AGPL and EUPL licenses as opposed to non-copyleft licenses like MIT or BSD have been proven effective in that, as Googles AGPL policy shows. Such policy would realize the principles of the EU, such as the protection of human rights and sustainable development. Meanwhile the current model of operation, in which public money supports the development of Big Tech companies, contradicts this idea. We are facing the absurdity products from Big Tech are dominant even in public institutionsand as a result public employees are forced during work hours to create value (such as data) for private companies. Companies, lets notice, that are subordinate to the authorities of non-EU country, whose political stability and dedication to human rights (or even human life would be fair to say) and to democracy are today questionable. Unfortunately, as we can see, these political and commercial impact are intertwined in the US today, and the authorities do not hesitate to use all means of influence to maintain the oligopolies of their businesses. One would like to see the same determination in the EU authorities. The last decade has also shown how difficult it is to enforce European laws on non-European companies let us learn from this and draw conclusions for the future. Free software also allows for more efficient use of resources money, labor, talent, and time (which is at essence in a race against the growing power of digital oligopolies): effort that has already been done is not repeated, but rather the general intellect is developed and thanks to copyleft licenses, it becomes secured and equally accessible to everyone. The EU and MSs should implement the requirement for free software and copyleft, together with the principle of interoperability, in their public procurement. This will ensure the rational management of public funds. The same rules should apply to the requirements for granting programs from EU, member states, and associated countries funding opportunities. Public funds public ownership of the code. The EU should fund the infrastructure that is critical for Europe's digital sovereignty from data servers and clouds, through open APIs, to software for public institutions. Free source operating systems for mobile devices is particularly important for the security of data generated by children, who begin their digital lives with tablets and smartphones. Publicly funded, easily accessible range of services and products based on free software and copyleft should eventually replace proprietary systems. Finally, the issue of securing one of the key pillars of democracy, namely public debate. Since ancient times, the agora has been a place where discussions took place, different positions were presented, and it was only through their clash that the best decisions and ideas emerged. Today, unfortunately, it is terrorized by algorithms they dictate which voices are amplified and which cannot break through. Instead of the development of general intellect and the ability to think critically,from multiple perspectives, they reinforce extreme opinions. Democracy cannot exist without freedom of debate (which is more than just freedom of expression for individuals) it is the task of EU institutions to ensure this freedom by supporting alternative spaces for discussion, such as social media based on FOSS. The fight against disinformation cannot be won when all power is handed over to the owners of companies whose business model is based on spreading disinformation. Funding FOSS SM would be in line with the protection of minorities' rights to participate in debate & respect for science based policies.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Comments from Tekniska verken i Linköping AB regarding the consultation on the EU Emissions Trading System for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and the Market Stability Reserve Tekniska verken i Linköping AB hereby submits the following comments in response to the consultation on the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and the Market Stability Reserve. Tekniska verken owns and operates the Gärstadverket waste-to-energy combined heat and power plant, located near Linköping in southern Sweden. Approximately 600,000 tonnes of residual waste from households and industry are treated at the facility each year for energy recovery, resulting in around 270,000 tonnes of CO equivalents, which are currently included in the EU ETS. This waste originates from various parts of Europe, including Norway, the United Kingdom, and Iceland. Tekniska verken would like to highlight the following key points: Harmonised inclusion of waste incineration: At present, only Sweden, Denmark, and Lithuania have voluntarily included waste incineration in the EU ETS because of their interpretation of the regulation. This approach is not applied in the rest of the EU. It is essential that all waste incineration plants within the EU are subject to a harmonised regulatory framework to ensure fair market conditions. Therefore, waste incineration should be incorporated into the EU ETS across all Member States as soon as possible. Such inclusion would also create uniform incentives for investments in carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and utilisation (CCU) throughout the EU. Avoiding distortion of waste flows: If only a limited number of Member States include waste incineration in the EU ETS, rising allowance prices may lead to a diversion of residual waste to countries not covered by the system. EU-wide inclusion would maintain a level playing field and ensure that waste is directed to the most efficient energy recovery facilitiessuch as Nordic plants integrated with high-efficiency district heating networks. Need for coordinated policy if EU-wide inclusion is not implemented: If the EU ultimately decides not to include waste incineration in the EU ETS, Sweden, Denmark, and Lithuania should revoke their opt-in inclusion. Otherwise, energy-efficient facilities in these countries will be at a disadvantage compared to less efficient plants in Member States outside the scheme. Opting out could allow these countries to redirect financial resources toward CCS/CCU development or enhanced waste sorting infrastructure, thereby reducing plastic incineration and encouraging material recycling. Recognition of CCU within the EU ETS: CCU is one of several critical solutions to mitigate the climate impact of necessary waste incineration. Many CCU applications involve non-permanent carbon storage while displacing emissions from fossil-based products. It is vital that such activities are recognised within the EU ETSfor example, in the production of e-fuels, chemicals, or food industry inputs. Without recognition, waste incineration plants engaged in CCU risk being penalised, which would hinder innovation and climate progress. To strengthen investment signals, the EU ETS should treat captured and utilised carbon equally, regardless of its source. Strategic benefit of CCU: One positive side effect of CCU is increased EU self-sufficiency in hydrocarbon production. Including CCU-enabled waste incineration plants in the EU ETS in a way that does not acknowledge this benefit would reduce the EUs strategic autonomy in this area. Continued role for waste-to-energy beyond 2039: Energy recovery from waste will remain necessary even after 2039, when the free allocation of allowances under EU ETS 1 is scheduled to end. This applies, for instance, to contaminated, hazardous, or non-recyclable waste. As it will not be technically or economically feasible to retrofit all plants with CC

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I wish for the EU to rely on open source technologies, by which I explicitly mean technologies that are resilient from having their license changed by a company to their own benefit. The EU should be able to do their computing without being reliant on the US. Linux and BSDs are the only operating systems that currently allow such. Besides general Laptop/Desktop software (Desktop Environments such as Gnome/KDE, Kernels such as the Linux Kernel), which is already fairly decently supported, there are various pain points. - We need support for an office suite that can be used across the EU based on open source technologies to the benefit of everyone. Microsofts monopoly here is an everlasting blackmail opportunity by the US - We need to support the creation of competing mobile operating systems such as SailfishOS such as it is provided by for example the jolla phone. Only this way can be have a chance to not be spied upon by the US through google, have trade secrets stolen and Privacy infringed - We need to support the open source technologies we already rely upon and that often are operating on shoestring budgets. - We need to improve accessibility across the open source software stack. A lot of work has already been done here on Linux and Wayland, but more remains to be done by the various Desktop Environments. The german sovereign tech fund is already a great idea in that regard and has done fantastic work. I believe using this model and scaling this up to be EU wide with appropriate funds could be something to look into.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: The EU Organic Regulation introduced for the first time Europe-wide control rules for organic farms in 1992. I switched my farm to organic farming in 1991 and the EU Organic Regulation has seen the EU Organic Regulation as a very good basis providing clarity and certainty to our farmers. Framework legislation that clearly defines the boundaries of the system and nevertheless allows for the greatest possible freedom of management within the limits. The Regulation summarised very well the level of knowledge of organic production at the time. This was mainly developed and defined by farmers, consultants and academia in the 1980s. This knowledge was based on the level of knowledge at the time and the technology available at the time! Both the state of knowledge and technology on the arable land and in the stables have evolved fundamentally since then. At the end of the 1980s, there were no animal housing concepts developed for organic farming! Basically, the existing houses were taken, a little reconstructed and more space was given to the animals. Innovative animal housing systems with separate functional areas and different climate zones allowing optimal animal welfare have mainly been developed over the last 20 years by farmers together with consultants and academia. What we need to address as a matter of urgency is an adapted breeding system to ensure that the whole runs for the benefit of humans, animals and the environment. So much! What happened to the EU Organic Regulation during this period? It has essentially not evolved in technical terms since 1992! Although new interpretative details have always been developed and implemented, in principle the Regulation is based on knowledge from the start-up period. By way of example, this is the case of livestock farming, which, as described, has changed massively for the better. The old, rigid space requirements in the Regulation no longer fit into the new findings implemented in modern innovative housing systems. On the contrary, too much space in the house leads to a much worse housing climate, with all the negative consequences for humans, animals and the environment. And yet, a Commission advisory body recently published a pamphlet that insisted on compliance with the outdated space requirements! This is contrary to better knowledge and purely administrative arbitrariness. This urgently needs to be revised and made more flexible. Another example is the insistence on full grazing for ruminants. First, the Regulation does not define grazing in any way. This leads to the absurd situation where an area of the size of a football field for 100 cows is recognised as pasture. The same 100 cows may then be kept in a closed house 80 % of the year, as there is grazing. An innovative housing system with year-round access to open air areas, with climate irritants and functional areas and partial pasture is not recognised. Perhaps we should ask cows and not decide on consumer ideological expectations. It is undisputed that closed, old houses can only be eco-friendly with summer grazing. However, as I said, the development went further outside the Regulation. To send unfamiliar chicks to the outside, hopefully, we don't have anything to say. There is no need for such interpretations of the Regulation that cruel humans and animals! They are simply superfluous. If organic production is to remain competitive in the EU, innovation needs to be recognised. It is important to have an innovative, evidence-based framework law, rather than a permanent new interpretative detail based on outdated knowledge! If the announced animal husbandry interpretations were to come in such a way, this would mean leaving many farms. The EU is pushing organic farming back to the absolute niche!

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: Legrand, global specialist in electrical and digital building infrastructures, welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the public consultation on the revision of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 on European standardisation. We strongly believe thatthe proposed provisions on common specifications wouldlead to the emergence of a parallel structure. This will further undermine the public-private partnership that has successfully and consistently contributed to an open, inclusive, transparent and industry-led European standardisation system. We in addition submit further comments for your kind consideration in the attached file.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: SDBN fights for the privacy rights of European citizens. We are alarmed by the current use of omnibus packages to bypass proper consultation and weaken fundamental rights (including the right to privacy) under the guise of simplification. Urgency should not be used as a rationale for bypassing impact assessments. Moreover, impact assessments cannot be reduced to merely assessing the costs of compliance and should instead consider broad impacts on society at large. The current tendency to focus predominantly on business compliance costs provides an incomplete picture of regulatory impact. It furthermore penalizes companies that have spent time and resources complying with existing law. For instance in the case of the GDPR, changes to the current regime would penalize especially smaller companies that have spent tremendous efforts to comply with the regulation. Finally, a comprehensive impact assessment must evaluate effects on among others fundamental rights, social cohesion and the environment, and it needs to cover the entire omnibus, not merely individual regulations. The consultation of civil society organizations is vital to democratic governance and effective policymaking. Urgency cannot be a rationale for bypassing these consultations or shortening their timeframes. Meaningful participation requires adequate time for organizations to analyze proposals, consult their constituencies, and formulate substantive responses. Over the past year, we were confronted with a huge amount of consultations with four week deadlines (including this one). Furthermore, consultations cannot be reduced to one-off exercises. Effective stakeholder engagement requires ongoing dialogue throughout the legislative process, from initial conception through implementation and review. The Digital Omnibus package illustrates the risks of inadequate civil society consultation. The changes made to the GDPR almost exactly reflect the demands by Big Tech companies, as reported by the Corporate Europe Observatory, while not at all reflecting the concerns and demands made by civil society. This imbalance undermines the legitimacy and quality of the resulting legislation. Simplification should never mean deregulation. These are fundamentally different concepts that must not be conflated. True simplification enhances clarity and usability while preserving protections; deregulation weakens or removes those protections. As we are seeing in the Digital Omnibus, the current proposed changes go far beyond clarification or providing legal certainty and instead go to the very heart of privacy rights, and go against the Charter of Fundamental Rights. Alterations to our fundamental rights cannot be characterized as mere technical simplification and cannot be fast-tracked. This cannot happen and should not be normalized as an acceptable approach to regulatory reform. Moreover, while we understand the need to avoid duplicating compliance efforts, the burden of proof should be on industry to demonstrate real compliance costs, rather than relying on general assertions. Such evidence should subsequently be made public and weighted against the harms of under-compliance. As an alternative to the Commission's current approach, real simplification can often be achieved through unified enforcement and clarifications can be provided on the enforcement level, rather than through changes to the law itself. Specifically, streamlining enforcement procedures and ensuring consistent application across Member States could reduce complexity without altering substantive protections; Detailed guidance provided by regulatory authorities could clarify how requirements apply in practice; Better coordination between authorities could eliminate duplication and inconsistency.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: AERRL welcomes the Commissions call for on the European Research act. We are particularly sensitive to the objective of reducing fragmentation. In the rail sector, fragmentation is a scourge that we are fighting in order to achieve the simplification and harmonisation of standards and procedures at European level. This is essential in order to finally build the single rail market. The Association of European Rail Rolling Stock lessors is a representative body of the railway sector at EU level. In 2023, a record 3650 locomotives were leased in Europe. This represents 20% of the total main-line vehicles circulating. Lessors are at the forefront of the railway sector development, by investing in the most in modern & cross-border motorised rolling stock in EU. Within the last decade they bought 42% to 45% of newly manufactured locomotives. Lessors are the first investors in locomotive procurement in Europe with ~800 million acquisition per annum for the past 10 years. As these amounts increase, we expect an investment of one billion euros a year by 2027. These numbers greatly surpass the volume of incumbent operators. Lessors own almost half of the ERTMS European fleet. If we consider the European locomotive fleet as 31/12/2023, we see that 61 % of the lessors fleet is electric, while railway companies achieve only 47 %. Considering the 2025 leased fleet, 80% of the newly ordered are electric. We urge the Commission to consider the following factors: - Innovation is important to ensure the future of European rail, but standardisation and simplification are much more significant priorities, as they are a prerequisite for the commercial success and enhance the survival of today's railways. Without standardisation, the cost of European rail becomes untenable as a competitive mode of transport, risking an ever-growing reverse modal shift. In this context, innovation could be of a strategical asset. The adoption of a different deployment plan for key technologies like ERTMS, based on the deployment of a single and stable technology at a European level, would be a great strategic innovation that would provide a significant boost to the European rail sector , putting an end to the unsustainable fragmentation undermining the whole sector. - The preparatory work for the deployment of new technologies must in any case take into account the interests of all European stakeholders. On-board migration strategies should be designed based on product strategies, developed by OEMs, with backwards compatibility guaranteed to existing ETCS equipped locomotives, as a key principle. Stable technologies are needed to ensure standardisation and an economic viable model for the deployment of rolling stock. We need Technical Specifications of Interoperability regulations that support the use of existing locomotives and not limiting it, therefore innovations shall be on an optional basis and not binding to all vehicles
