initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Public procurement is one of Europes strongest demand-side levers, but for many startups and scaleups it remains a high-cost, low-probability channel. When procedures favour incumbents, taxpayers miss better solutions and young firms lose the quickest route to reference customers, revenue, and scale. The revision should prioritise startup-first access: not protectionism, but rules and incentives that make it rational for public buyers to take measured risks on new suppliers. Main barriers we see are front-loaded compliance costs, disproportionate financial guarantees, track-record and turnover thresholds that exclude young firms, prescriptive specifications that lock in known vendors, fragmented documentation across portals, long and asymmetric timelines, and slow payments that create cash-flow risk. Digital too often means uploading PDFs into different national systems rather than a single, reusable set of credentials. Proportionality and simplicity should be hardwired into the mainstream regime. Contracting authorities should justify any eligibility, certification, reference, or financial-capacity requirement that is not strictly linked to delivery risk, and use modular contracting or smaller lots where appropriate. A mandatory startup/SME access check would help prevent unnecessary administrative barriers. Suppliers should have a clear right to correct minor administrative mistakes before exclusion. Two-stage procedures should be promoted so that a short initial proposal is evaluated before full documentation is requested from shortlisted bidders. Speed and predictability matter. If bidders are given short deadlines to prepare full offers, authorities should be bound to similarly short evaluation and award timelines. Faster payments and, where appropriate, pre-financing should be normalised for smaller innovative suppliers, especially for R&D services and first deployments. Guidance should confirm that capacity can be demonstrated by alternatives to turnover (e.g., investor backing, staged milestones, insurance). To unlock innovation, rules should favour functional, outcome-based specifications and meaningful pre-market engagement, so buyers describe problems and performance targets rather than the solution. The framework should also close the pilot-to-scale gap by allowing validated pilots to flow into follow-on procurement through transparent, stepwise scaling and re-competition at sensible thresholds. Procurement should reward strategic value, not only price. We support a more economically strategic approach that captures total cost of ownership, interoperability, security of supply, resilience, and innovation impact, with clear legal guidance, model clauses, and training so buyers can use such criteria without fear of ex post challenge. Consider risk-sharing tools, such as standard milestone-based contracts and targeted guarantees, to de-risk both buyer and supplier, where justified. Member States should be encouraged to set measurable targets for spending with innovative SMEs and to build procurement competence centres. Finally, procurement must work as a Single Market. Apply the once-only principle to supplier evidence; require interoperability so credentials, corporate IDs, and performance references can be reused across borders; improve cross-border visibility of below-threshold opportunities; and reduce language frictions by permitting English for key documents in innovation-heavy procurements. Better tender and award data would help smaller firms plan and would support evidence-based policy. The revision should also enable joint cross-border innovation procurement through standard templates and shared frameworks. Where Member States operate regulatory sandboxes, authorities should be enabled to act as early customers of validated solutions via faster, transparent pathways. Turning public buyers into credible first customers is one of the fastest ways to translate European innovation into competitiveness.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: It is important for us, European and national, to be able to develop our own digital and IT resources that are independent of their American counterparts (windows, android, ios, apple, etc.). It is unacceptable that our data, for the purpose of backup, should be duplicated on servers which follow a different legal framework from our and which can be used to feed systems other than notres. I also consider it essential that each country develops its own independent IT system; good on gateways to exchange information must exist but not become the common standard. It is possible to create a common platform that can be fully customised by the state authorities of the Member States.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The concept of European Open Source seems unsustainable if we focus on which countries provided the authors of the code. An EU only open source project would not be able to gain the community by excluding/limiting input outside of the EU. Linux, Kubernetes etc. are not worth duplicating. Focus the efforts on wide scale deployment of open source and building EU based consultancies rather than restricting contributions.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Germany and the European Union demonstrate substantial strength in open-source ecosystems through large-scale community knowledge projects and public-sector Linux adoption, showing that open digital ecosystems are built on sustained, mostly voluntary contributions that merit formal recognition as Ehrenamt (civic volunteer work). Germany hosts one of the largest and most active Wikipedia communities worldwide, with the German-language edition ranking among the top language versions by number of articles and active editors. Wikimedia Deutschland, a non-profit with over 111,000 members, develops free software, maintains the open data project Wikidata, and supports volunteer editors who provide a critical pillar of Europe's digital public knowledge infrastructure. German public institutions have a long track record of adopting Linux and open-source software to strengthen digital sovereignty. Examples include Linux use by the German Bundestag's server infrastructure, large-scale deployments in federal agencies such as the Federal Employment Office (Bundesagentur für Arbeit), and municipal migrations including Munich's LiMux project and Schleswig-Holstein's ongoing transition to Linux and LibreOffice. A significant share of this infrastructureWikipedia, Wikidata, and core open-source software used in public administrationis built and maintained by unpaid volunteers acting in the public interest. However, Germany's legal framework does not generally recognize open-source development and maintenance as Ehrenamt under tax and funding law, prompting recent petitions calling for such recognition. For the European Open Digital Ecosystems initiative, these German examples illustrate that Europe already hosts mature, high-impact open-source and open-knowledge communities delivering public goods at continental scale. Recognizing contributions to Wikipedia, Wikidata, Linux distributions, and strategic open-source components as Ehrenamt (voluntary work with accepted tax or similar benefits) would align legal and financial support with the EU's objectives of digital sovereignty, resilience, and trustworthy digital infrastructure. References: - European Commission Better Regulation Portal - European Open Digital Ecosystems Initiative: https://ec.europa.eu/info/law/better-regulation/have-your-say/initiatives/16213-European-Open-Digital-Ecosystems/ - Wikimedia Deutschland: https://www.wikimedia.de/ - Wikipedia - German Wikipedia: https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deutschsprachige_Wikipedia - Wikipedia - Open-Source-Software in öffentlichen Einrichtungen (German): https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open-Source-Software_in_%C3%B6ffentlichen_Einrichtungen - Wikipedia - LiMux: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LiMux - Linux Foundation - Open Source as Europe's Strategic Advantage (2025): https://www.linuxfoundation.org/research/world-of-open-source-eu-2025 - Recognition of open source work as voluntary work in Germany: https://www.web-vision.de/en/petition-anerkennung-von-open-source-arbeit-als-ehrenamt-in-deutschland.html

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The current state of scholarly publishing presents challenges of multiple kinds, including the adequacy of established research evaluation systems. Market realities in academic publishing create difficulties for the research community, ranging from the devaluation of scientific quality in the publish or perish culture, through systemic bias in the evaluation of the humanities, social sciences and low-resource languages, to the chronic underfunding of academic libraries. 1. A Harmonised Secondary Publication Right One of the main obstacles to full public access to knowledge produced with public resources lies in copyright law. A 2024 ERA report commissioned by the European Commission concluded that a major barrier to the creation of a genuine single market for research, innovation and technology in the EU is the imbalance in the current copyright framework (https://data.europa.eu/doi/10.2777/633395). Copyright and related rights, in practice, benefit publishers disproportionately, limiting accessibility, circulation, and reuse of research outputs. Licensing obligations imposed by traditional publishing models prevent broad access to scientific publications, even among researchers themselves. Institutions are uncertain about the legal limits of introducing rights retention policies, while the absence of a harmonised research exception further restricts cross-border collaboration. To resolve this, the ERA Act must introduce a harmonised EU-level Secondary Publication Right and ensure that no legal obstacles stand in the way of rights retention initiatives. It must also establish a mandatory and flexible research exception. 2. Reform of Research Evaluation to Safeguard EU Science Research evaluation is not a neutral tool but a social technology shaping the reality of science, determining which research is funded and which careers are built. Evaluation practices are generally centred on the journal article, dominated by quantitative metrics, and heavily reliant on indexing in global proprietary databases such as Web of Science and Scopus. This fuels two systemic issues: first, a toxic publish or perish culture privileging quantity over quality; and second, dependence on proprietary data analytics platforms which threatens the sovereignty of European research. To guarantee autonomy of EU science, the ERA Act must include legislative measures to modernise research evaluation and to insulate it from dependence on large proprietary platforms. 3. Addressing Market Concentration and Gatekeeping in Scholarly Publishing The global academic publishing sector is highly concentrated. As of 2023, the Big Five Elsevier, John Wiley & Sons, Taylor & Francis, Springer Nature and SAGE controlled at least 25% of all academic journals, while the top ten publishers produced nearly 50% of all articles. Revenues of the Big Five exceed 50% of total industry income, with profit margins sometimes reaching 40%. These publishers are not only producers but also intermediaries in the dissemination of knowledge, controlling both distribution and reputation-building mechanisms. Elsevier, for instance, owns Scopus and ScienceDirect, while Clarivate owns Web of Science, impact factor metrics, and ProQuest databases. This vertical integration concentrates publishing, aggregation, commercialisation and evaluation within global structures that control much of the value chain of academic publishing. As a result, large publishers act as gatekeepers in scientific communication. Their market power and control over bibliometric infrastructures resemble the role of gatekeepers addressed under the Digital Markets Act. Therefore, the ERA Act should include legislative measures of competition law nature to restrict the dominance of big scholarly publishers and safeguard the openness of the European research ecosystem.
topic: Employment and social affairs, Research and innovation, Single market
text_raw: The current state of scholarly publishing presents challenges of multiple kinds, including the adequacy of established research evaluation systems. Market realities in academic publishing create difficulties for the research community, ranging from the devaluation of scientific quality in the publish or perish culture, through systemic bias in the evaluation of the humanities, social sciences and low-resource languages, to the chronic underfunding of academic libraries. 1. A Harmonised Secondary Publication Right One of the main obstacles to full public access to knowledge produced with public resources lies in copyright law. A 2024 ERA report commissioned by the European Commission concluded that a major barrier to the creation of a genuine single market for research, innovation and technology in the EU is the imbalance in the current copyright framework (https://data.europa.eu/doi/10.2777/633395). Copyright and related rights, in practice, benefit publishers disproportionately, limiting accessibility, circulation, and reuse of research outputs. Licensing obligations imposed by traditional publishing models prevent broad access to scientific publications, even among researchers themselves. Institutions are uncertain about the legal limits of introducing rights retention policies, while the absence of a harmonised research exception further restricts cross-border collaboration. To resolve this, the ERA Act must introduce a harmonised EU-level Secondary Publication Right and ensure that no legal obstacles stand in the way of rights retention initiatives. It must also establish a mandatory and flexible research exception. 2. Reform of Research Evaluation to Safeguard EU Science Research evaluation is not a neutral tool but a social technology shaping the reality of science, determining which research is funded and which careers are built. initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The EU has an enormous human capital in the field of computer science and software engineering. Many of the most important FOSS (free and open source software) initiatives originated in the EU, Linux being one notable example. We must understand, however, that the word "free" in "free software" means free as in "free speech", not "free beer". In other words, there should be incentives (financial but not only) for FOSS developers to make good, solid software which could then be used by enterprises, public institutions, and individuals in the EU. Grants for FOSS developers (without too much red tape!!) would be one solution; another idea might be to support European companies writing FOSS open source products are not necessarily a steady source of income, which can bring down small enterprises building them. I think the biggest and most important challenge is achieving "feature parity" with equivalent non-EU products used in strategic areas, so: software powering cloud platforms (load balancers, firewalls, orchestration tools, etc.), secure communications tools, collaborative office suites, and so on. Definitely *NOT* chasing the latest fads "not to be left behind".

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: As identified by the Clean Industrial Deal, public procurement is a tool to support EU competitiveness and decarbonisation simultaneously. Furthermore, public procurement is a key lever to boost Europes circular economy, a strategic goal. However, as found by the 2025 evaluation report, its potential is underused. Considering the need to simplify business operations and reduce burdens for public authorities, supporting contracting authorities throughout the procurement process including pre-tender, tender, and post-tender phases is a key goal of this revision. Harmonised rules should simplify operations in the Single Market while legal certainty provided eliminates fear of litigation. Simplification should not lower ambition, where the EUR 2.6 Tn value of EU public procurement must deliver the best value by fostering lead markets, resilience, and competitiveness. Simplification should entail support that empowers contracting authorities to design and monitor procurement more efficiently. In the context of competitiveness, sustainability must be embedded as core principle of public procurement. This reinforces Article 18(2) of Directive 2004/18/EC, which the Court of Justice ruled establishes sustainability as a cardinal value in case C-395/18 - Tim. Committing to competitive sustainability signals that Europes economy can generate value while addressing the triple planetary crises. Green investments are investments into long-term resilience. Minimum requirements in sectoral legislation accompanied by harmonised monitoring tools signal market demand for taxonomy-aligned products. Using labels, digital tools, and Digital Product Passports would collect high-quality data about taxonomy-aligned economic activities, crucial to central banks who are required to manage transition risks and assess vulnerabilities across the economy. ACR+ supports Made in Europe criteria in public procurement for strategic sectors as buying green supports sustainable EU operators. A value-chain approach is key; products and services cannot be circular unless their entire value chains are circular. However, this should not be a gateway for protectionism. The revised act should include targets for green procurement (GPP). The 2025 evaluation found that 14 Member States reported only 25% of their contracts held green criteria; a suggested target could be that 100% of public contracts incorporate an environmental clause by 2030. A monitoring system should track uptake across the EU. Targets on mandatory social clauses should be investigated, with France as an example. Mandating targets across the EU would increase integration of the single market; at present <5% of contracts are awarded directly to firms established in other Member States or third countries, though indirect cross-border procurement represents 20% of the total procurement. This is mainly due to differences in national legislation, language, and administration. The Commission should support joint procurement and aggregated demand across regions and Member States via cost- and risk-sharing among contracting authorities, clarified joint bidding rules, and a facility for portfolio procurement. Aggregated demand via joint procurement fosters scalability, generates value across borders, and enhances supply chain resilience. Representing local and regional authorities across the EU and considering our cumulative learnings from EU-funded projects on public procurement, ACR+ offers 9 recommendations: Prioritisequality:moving away from the lowest priceonly Introduce mandatory minimumGPPrequirements Coherence of the legal framework Promotemarket engagement Contract managementand monitoring Strengthen economic securityandsovereignty EnablingEUFundingfor contracting authorities in public procurement A professionalisationframeworkforcontracting authorities EU andNational Action Plans forStrategicPublic Procurement

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations (EFPIA) contribution to Better Regulation Call for Evidence EFPIA represents the research-based pharmaceutical industry operating in Europe. EFPIA welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the Commissions reflection on Better Regulation. For the pharmaceutical sector, a strategic sector for the European Union, essential to public health, security of supply, innovation and competitiveness, Better Regulation must ensure coherence, predictability and proportionality, particularly considering increasing regulatory complexity. 1) Reconciling evidence-based policymaking and urgent action EFPIA recognises the need for timely regulatory action and highlights that urgency should not compromise evidence quality or proportionality. To better reconcile these objectives, we suggest that the Commission should: systematically apply competitiveness and health security in impact assessments to all new legislative initiatives, including cumulative effects on innovation, manufacturing capacity, supply chains and medicine availability [1]; use adaptive implementation approaches, including review clauses and phased timelines, allowing adjustment as new evidence emerges [1]; conduct priority Reality Checks with affected stakeholders where assumptions are uncertain or feasibility has not been sufficiently tested[1]; ensure early engagement by relevant regulators e.g. involve the EMA in any legislative proposal affecting medicines, supporting innovators through scientific advice, given differing agency mandates. EFPIA analysis has shown that the pharmaceutical sector was affected by up to 26 new EU legislative initiatives within the last five-year period [1]. Insufficient coordination risked undermining medicine availability. For example, an unforeseen consequence under the synthetic polymer microparticles legislation would have restricted all oral dose medicines in clinical trials in Europe, while a broad PFAS restriction could affect over 600 essential medicines [2]. 2) Ensuring a holistic approach to stakeholder consultations Stakeholder consultations should be more holistic and cross-policy, reflecting interactions between environmental, chemicals, health and industrial legislation. Consultations should start early, continue through delegated and implementing acts, and be supported by transparent data, assumptions and methodologies. The Urban Wastewater Treatment Directive (UWWTD) illustrates the need for this approach. Industry analysis indicates that the Commissions cost estimates significantly underestimated real-world impacts [3], while uncertainties remain regarding the methodology used to assess pharmaceutical contributions to micropollutant loads [4]. Earlier and broader engagement could have improved evidence quality. 3) Making EU law simpler and easier to implement In line with President von der Leyens objective to make Europe simpler and faster, EFPIA supports: clearer and more predictable use of legal instruments, including delegated and implementing acts; stronger action against gold-plating and divergent national interpretations; realistic implementation timelines reflecting sector-specific regulatory processes; effective use of interoperable digital tools to reduce administrative burden [1]. For a strategic sector such as pharmaceuticals, Better Regulation should ensure coherent, evidence-based and workable legislation that safeguards patient access to medicines while supporting EU competitiveness. EFPIA stands ready to continue engaging constructively with the Commission.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Contribution from U.Di.Con. In response to the European Commission’s consultation on open digital ecosystems, the Committee views the issue, from the point of view of consumers, as central to freedom of choice, safety, the quality of digital services and the protection of fundamental rights online. It also highlights the criticality of the EU’s dependence on non-EU technologies and providers and the need for a European strategy on open source. The document acknowledges the strengths of open source (dynamic ecosystem, transparency and interoperability), but points to structural weaknesses such as fragmentation, lack of governance and stable funding for maintenance and security, as well as still widespread perceptions of lower reliability compared to proprietary software. Proposed actions include: stable financial support to strategic projects, sustainable governance models, integration of open source in public procurement, investments in audit/certification and technical support, together with digital literacy and accessibility and security requirements for software for citizens. Cloud, operating systems and networks, cybersecurity, AI, digital identity and platforms for digital public services shall be identified as priorities; greater use of allopen source is considered useful for competitiveness and cyber resilience in digital public administration, health, education, smart cities and the data industry.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: 1. In animal production: delete the 48-hour standstill obligation for processing operations with a legal deadline of 0; delete the 3/4 cattle live-in rule; review the rules on the renewal rate for non-organic animals (no possibilities to buy between 10 %/20 % depending on the species and 30 % despite the derogation); on the other hand, there is a very significant difference between the management of endangered breeds and other breeds, which creates a significant distortion between breeders; authorise the conversion of laying hens; maintain the 5 % non-organic protein feed in young poultry and pigs. 2. In crop production: the variety allowed for varieties which can be differentiated with the naked eye is not an objective criterion; clarify the rules on crop rotation; clarify the possible mix of seed production. 3. Into processed products: clarify the rules for authorising E535 in salt (acceptable criteria?); authorise certification of non-food products (hydrolats, balsams, floral waters, etc.). Clarify what constitutes a suspicion of non-compliance (Articles 27--). Consolidate all texts in a single regulation.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Towards appropriate and legally consistent proportionality in the accounting of biogenic CO2 capture under the EU ETS Annex to the questionnaire completed by the German Cement Works Association (Verein Deutscher Zementwerke, VDZ) for the ETS1 review of the European Commission. EU ETS installations are required to significantly reduce atmospheric GHG emission over the next decade in order to meet the projected ETS targets of net zero emissions in 2039/2040. Especially sectors with unavoidable CO2 emissions in their processes are therefore planning and preparing to employ all available of efficiency measures in their value chains and additionally to significantly invest in carbon capture and storage (CCS) technologies. The most relevant sectors with substantial amounts of unavoidable CO2 emissions within the EU ETS are the cement and lime industries, while the inclusion of waste incineration is under consideration. In all three sectors, the CO2 generated includes significant amounts of biogenic CO2. Especially cement plants early on have substituted traditional fossil fuels with the use of alternative fuels sustainably generated from wastes with significant shares of biogenic carbon. Early on, this has delivered fossil fuel and climate savings. It includes investments in the pre-processing and handling of mixed municipal and industrial waste streams, to source waste fractions suited for industrial fuel use. CCS technology has to be employed in the industrial processes of the cement and lime sectors, which is clearly expressed in all carbon neutrality roadmaps. It is a decisive step on the path towards climate neutrality, as it is able to prevent about 95% of remaining atmospheric CO2 emissions. Technically, the capture process will encompass fossil and biogenic CO2. Economically, both require significant additional operational costs and investments. Thus, the strategic final investment decisions (FID), which now have to be made in the first CCS projects, require critical assessment of scaling and refinancing for the CO2 capture process. The EU ETS is the fundamental predictable source of economic incentive. However, last year the EU ETS accounting of capturing biogenic CO2 has become subject to significant legal uncertainty and limitation on the recognition of it in the EU ETS. The accounting depends on the interpretation of the reference for a proportional share of biogenic and fossil fractions of the CO2 captured. The question of interpretation of MRR Article 49(6) is whether the fraction of non-zero rated (fossil) and zero rated (biogenic) CO2 is referred to a. the total amount of CO2 generated in an ETS installation (fossil and biogenic) or b. only to the amount of CO2 captured. In the first case (case a) the principal condition of the ETS must limit the maximum subtracted fraction to not exceed the reported fossil CO2 released to the atmosphere, so that no negative emission can be generated , but effective scaling of CO2 capture to also cover zero-rated/biogenic CO2 sources is recognised. Such interpretation would be in line with EU policy, the Industrial Carbon Management Strategy (ICMS) and the Net Zero Industry Act (NZIA), which make an urgent call for capturing and storing biogenic CO2 as well. It would support early action for effectively preventing (biogenic) atmospheric GHG emissions and build a necessary element to achieve industrial and Europes climate neutrality. However, in the current interpretation of the Monitoring and Reporting Regulation (MRR) by the European Commission (case b), a complete lack of recognition of the capture and permanent storage of biogenic CO2 in the EU ETS has been forwarded. This interpretation is discouraging current projects from the effective scaling of CO2 capture. In consequence, the contradiction between the two interpretations (case a/b) is now hindering ongoing industrial CCS project development and FIDs subject to fundamental uncertainty regarding the accounting..

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am a co-founder of a video game company started in Belgium that uses open-source game engines instead of the paid game engines. We not only wanted to split less profit with the paid game engines (i.e. Unity & Unreal), but we wanted more reliability and less "what if the company gets sold" etc. We also didn't want to rely on paid American tech. I've also moved to strictly open source (as much as possible) for music composition which would have been more difficult 20 years ago. Open-source is important to me as a business owner and person because more people can benefit from our work. We even intend as a company to make our source code available for free to all when possible. We hope to be a guide for the rest of the video game development industry on how to produce large games using open-source tech. Creating open-source repositories and contributing to existing open-source projects will create a higher quality talent pool to hire from and will eventually create a stronger open-source tech ecosystem to draw inspiration from. Not everything has to be about IPs. Not everything paid is better. Just look at Blender-it's so good and is still an industry standard for the past 15 years or so-and it's open-source and free! My other two co-founders and I moved from the United States to Belgium so we could make it a Belgian company as we felt our company values would thrive within the framework of the EU open-source initiative and our egalitarian values would be better accepted in Europe. Oh, and we love GDPR thanks EU for having data privacy. The "right to be forgotten" digitally is a beautiful thing.
text_raw: I am a co-founder of a video game company started in Belgium that uses open-source game engines instead of the paid game engines. We not only wanted to split less profit with the paid game engines (i.e. Unity & Unreal), but we wanted more reliability and less "what if the company gets sold" etc. We also didn't want to rely on paid American tech. I've also moved to strictly open source (as much as possible) for music composition which would have been more difficult 20 years ago. Open-source is important to me as a business owner and person because more people can benefit from our work. We even intend as a company to make our source code available for free to all when possible. We hope to be a guide for the rest of the video game development industry on how to produce large games using open-source tech. Creating open-source repositories and contributing to existing open-source projects will create a higher quality talent pool to hire from and will eventually create a stronger open-source tech ecosystem to draw inspiration from. Not everything has to be about IPs. Not everything paid is better. Just look at Blender-it's so good and is still an industry standard for the past 15 years or so-and it's open-source and free! My other two co-founders and I moved from the United States to Belgium so we could make it a Belgian company as we felt our company values would thrive within the framework of the EU open-source initiative and our egalitarian values would be better accepted in Europe. Oh, and we love GDPR thanks EU for having data privacy. The "right to be forgotten" digitally is a beautiful thing.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: Zuboff Third Law: ‘Any digital application that can be used for surveillance or control shall be used for surveillance or control.’ The slogan who has nothing to hide should not fear: seemingly harmless data such as enjoyment or access to a service can also be collected, aggregated and used to create invasive personal profiles. Mass surveillance is thus carried out on the basis of metadata, which is justified on noble grounds, but which essentially threatens the individual and lanonimated freedom of all citizens. The institutions often call for practicality and use limited to the individual case, but de facto extend data retention rules and time limits to make them permanent control tools on a macroscopic basis. The node is not in technology itself, but in the absence of a widespread digital culture, which leads citizens to deliver their data without questioning the scope of what happens afterwards. European law provides for precise limits on the storage of and access to data. However, the practical reality remains much less reassuring. Citizens almost never know who is accessing their data, how long they are stored and under what safeguards they are transferred outside the EU. The best prevention narrative becomes a pretext for introducing permanent and pervasive tracking systems, which are often invisible. Biometrics and state trojans, although presented as advanced technologies designed for security, are not infallible and easily prone to error or abuse, turning them into a controlled entity regardless of whether it is dangerous. The underlying issue remains a democratic problem: if every access to a service requires verification of identity or age, if each connection generates long-stored metadata and if digital techniques synchronise data of various kinds, a form of generalised preventive control shall be put in place. Yet European law provides that any processing of data must be transparent, proportionate and subject to supervision by the Supervisor or the citizen itself. It is therefore not a matter of paranoia, nor of a clear rejection of the safety or protection of minors, but of calling for reflection on a fundamental balance. It is essential that every intervention is accompanied by real, transparent and controllable safeguards, and that citizens develop digital awareness to account for the choices made in their name. Protecting does not mean giving up freedom, including on the most marginal services, cannot become the price to be paid for an alleged safe well-being.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: eBay welcomes the European Commissions Call for Evidence on the forthcoming Communication on Better Regulation, which aims to modernise the EUs Better Regulation principles to support a simpler, faster and more effective policymaking process. We believe the principles can be central to the Unions democratic process, competitiveness and economic resilience, particularly so in a context of heightened geopolitical uncertainty, rapid technological change and intensifying global competition. A well-functioning set of Better Regulation principles is essential to ensure EU policymaking remains evidence-based, coherent, proportionate, and capable of delivering tangible benefits. For eBay, Better Regulation is also critical for EU competitiveness, SME growth, and sustainability, as regulatory design choices directly impact real-world outcomes for the millions of consumers and small businesses who use our services. In this context, the modernisation of Better Regulation principles represents an opportunity not only to streamline procedures, but also to strengthen policy coherence and improve implementation and ensure that new and existing rules work together effectively in practice.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: LONGi welcomes the European Commissions initiative to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the Public Procurement Framework, recognising its crucial role in accelerating clean energy adoption and deployment. As a leading global manufacturer of solar products and solutions, LONGi is committed to driving the transition towards a low-carbon economy. Our expertise lies in producing high-efficiency monocrystalline solar wafers, cells and modules, which we integrate with battery storage, to provide hybrid solutions that hold the key to rapid renewable energy deployment in Europe and worldwide. Through our continuous investment in research and development, we are advancing solar efficiency and affordability, fostering innovative solutions for our global clients. Attached, we included a detailed list of key considerations that we believe the revision of the Public Procurement Framework should address.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: A proposal to improve the strategy for the rights of students with visual impairment and blindness, with a particular focus on specialised support centres and schools, in order to sustainably strengthen the educational and participatory opportunities of students with visual impairment or blindness, a targeted further development of specialised schools and support centres is key. These institutions have technical expertise, technical equipment and pedagogical concepts, which are often not available to the same extent in the mainstream school system. The following proposal therefore pays particular attention to the development and promotion of these specialised education. 1. The development of specialised schools and support centres specialised schools for blind and visually impaired children should be secured, modernised and their structure strengthened in the long term. Extending locations and offerings can improve local coverage and reduce long access routes. Sufficient funding is needed to ensure small learning groups, individual support and highly qualified staff. 2. The specific pedagogical competence of these institutions, such as Braille, mobility training, life-speaking skills and assistive technologies, must be further developed and maintained in the long term. Specialised teachers need stable framework conditions, attractive training opportunities and recognition of their additional qualifications. 3. Upgrading the technical equipment of support centres should act as competence centres for assistive technologies, equipped with up-to-date hardware and software such as Braille lines, screen readers, magnification systems, digital learning platforms and tactile materials. Schools can also transfer their expertise to mainstream schools, thereby improving the quality of support across systems. 4. Freedom of choice for families should ensure that parents and pupils are free to choose whether the educational pathway takes place at a specialised school or inclusive in a mainstream school, without any interdependence of resources, quality or support. Both routes must be of high quality. Specialised schools are an important alternative that promotes individual strengths in a particularly good way. 5. Cooperation between specialised schools and mainstream schools can strengthen specialised schools as advisory and support centres that support mainstream schools with their knowledge. Joint projects, training and exchange programmes promote understanding, inclusiveness and transfer of skills. 6. Ensuring social participation in specialised institutions provides not only technical support but also a protected environment in which children and young people with similar experiences can experience community and build self-confidence. This social component is an important building block for personal development and should be explicitly strengthened. Specialised schools for blind and visually impaired pupils are an indispensable part of a diverse and equitable education system. Their expansion, modernisation and long-term security are instrumental in meeting individual needs and ensuring real equal opportunities. At the same time, they complement the inclusive regulatory system by providing expertise and increasing the quality of education for all children and young people concerned.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Towards “SOCIAL EFICIENCY”, as an objective of public procurement: QUALITY, INTEGRITY, STRATEGY AND AGILITY. The new EU legislation should deepen the objectives of the 2014 Directives, strengthening mandatory practical mechanisms to achieve them and simplifying the EU regulation itself and its implementation by the Member States. The means to achieve the aims and objectives of public procurement need to be more precise and focused not primarily on the award phase, but on the preparation and monitoring and control of implementation, the “dark zones” in the words of the OECD.

The means to achieve the aims and objectives of public procurement need to be more precise and focused not primarily on the award phase, but on the preparation and monitoring and control of implementation, the “dark zones” in the words of the OECD.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: More efficient and proportionate EU regulation is achieved through a change of approach: — proportionality must become an operational and verifiable criterion throughout the regulatory cycle; — consultations must produce useful and comparable evidence, testing the feasibility of regulatory options; — texts need to be clearer, more straightforward and easier to implement in order to reduce interpretative ambiguities and compliance costs. In this context, the divergence between the professions of order and non-order should also be treated as a problem of the quality of regulation: when differentiation is not supported by a direct link with the general interest and a strict demonstration of necessity, it ends up functioning as a market barrier, increases burdens on users and businesses, and fuels unjustified segmentations. The aim must be a European law that protects, but which at the same time remains viable: less unproductive bureaucracy, more legal certainty and more capacity to generate concrete results without passing excessive costs on to final recipients.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open source helps mitigate lock-in and security risks that lead to reduced sovereignty. Operating systems and user-facing software that we can build, run, and maintain ourselves allows retaining control over our devices without having to build a European OS from scratch. Examples could include Android and Ubuntu, both with a vibrant ecosystem of freely available and open source apps that provide most functions needed in modern daily life, except for mostly (semi-)government software such as national transport providers (Deutsche Bahn) or national authentication methods (DigiD). National software typically requires some amount of lock-in to Google or Apple, not just in ecosystem and convenience but also in cryptographic signatures. In 2025, Samsung, among the most popular mobile device vendors in Europe, stopped allowing installing software on smartphones except when it is cryptographically signed by themselves. There are only a few remaining options and even fewer European ones. Positive examples would be Fairphone, Shiftphone, and Murena, but their market share is such that we would basically have to restart society with pre-smartphone technology if Apple and Google get an order from their elected leader to disable devices and disallow future unlocking via an update. If users could, instead, choose to always unlock their devices, install a Google- or Apple-free operating system, and software would not actively thwart these open systems, daily life could recover expediently. GrapheneOS provides an example of how simple the installation procedure can be using WebUSB: I would trust an average citizen to do it in an hour so long as (a copy of) website remains available in their language. Beyond sovereignty: requiring openability of general computation hardware also strengthens article 8 ECHR (the right to private and family life) by not being subject to Google's, Microsoft's, or Apple's policies and algorithms as a fact of modern life.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: OVHcloud welcomes the revision of the Cybersecurity Act as a timely opportunity to both ensure secure and resilient IT services and strengthen ENISAs mandate, especially in the context of recent geopolitical challenges that have exposed Europes strategic vulnerabilities. OVHcloud encourages EU efforts to bolster strategic autonomy, reduce dependence on non-EU providers, and safeguard the Unions competitiveness and economic security, as underscored by recent EU initiatives and reports. In line with the initial objectives of the Cybersecurity Act to foster a resilient EU Digital Single Market and considering the current international context, the revision should take into account these strategic factors. Indeed, as highlighted in the Commissions recent Internal Security Strategy, cybersecurity and technological sovereignty are closely interlinked, and addressing technological dependencies as well as data protection must be a priority. OVHcloud therefore welcomes the Commissions intention to review the framework defining an appropriate level of cybersecurity - considering not only technical risks but also strategic threats and dependencies across the value chain. Earlier publications, including the Draghi Report (2024) and the Letta Report (2024), have also warned against excessive technological reliance on non-EU players. Both reports emphasize the need for a robust EU-wide security policy for sensitive data, underlining the risks of continued dependence on foreign digital services providers. Without targeted policy interventions, our dependency will continue to deepen, posing long-term challenges to Europes autonomy and security. The forthcoming revision of the Cybersecurity Act presents a timely opportunity to address existing gaps, streamline the EU cybersecurity certification framework to serve as a strategic asset to concretely bolster the EUs strategic autonomy and cybersecurity, and clarify ENISAs mandate. In this position paper, we set out the following key recommendations to help advance these objectives: I. Addressing risks arising from the EUs technological dependencies through the Cybersecurity Act: - By extending the scope of the CSA beyond non-technical factors to tackle risks around technological dependencies that could represent a threat to European entities. - By introducing a vulnerability analysis for each ICT service or technology to understand each sectors specificities and main risks to be tackled by the CSA and certification. - By applying evolutive strategic instruments in coordination with the certification schemes to adapt the level of security required by the ICT service. II. Confirming ENISAs mandate as a strategic actor fostering trust, coherence, and guidance for more secure digital services for EU organizations: - By extending ENISAs mandate and role beyond non-technical issues to analyze and tackle risks around technological dependencies for EU organizations. - By confirming ENISA as a strategic actor in certification.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: Unem welcomes the opportunity granted by the European Commission to comment on the revision of the Annex V and VI of the Renewable Energy Directive 2018/2001 (RED II). It appears from the draft Annex V to the Delegated Directive that typical and default values of the greenhouse gas emissions savings for renewable fuel ethers ETBE, TAEE (part A) and bio-MTBE (part B) have been removed. Renewable fuel ethers (ETBE, TAEE and bio-MTBE) are produced from bio-alcohol (ethanol and bio-methanol), transported, distributed and used across the world as high-quality and high-efficiency petrol components. They are blended with petrol due to their high-octane number and oxygen content, which improves the fuel combustion, supports fuel efficiency and decarbonizes transportation; their introduction in petrol has allowed, since 80s, the removal of lead additives. Ecofuel recommends the European Commission to maintain the references to bio-ethers with the corresponding mention equal to that of the ethanol production pathway used for ETBE and TAEE, as ethanol is incorporated, and equal to that of the bio-methanol production pathway used for bio-MTBE synthesized from bio-methanol. Maintaining bio-ethers in Annex V and VI ensures consistency between the different Annexes of the Renewable Energy Directives (RED I, RED II but also RED III), in which ETBE, TAEE and bio-MTBE, are listed as biofuels, e.g. Annex III Energy content of fuels.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The EU has strong open-source software (OSS) communities and a long record of public support for open digital technologies through initiatives and programs such as Next Generation Internet, FIWARE and investments in open hardware ecosystems (e.g., RISC-V). The EUs goals for competitiveness, cybersecurity, and technological sovereignty align with OSS practices such as transparency, auditability, and shared development. A recurring issue is that value created by European OSS communities is often captured outside the EU, and market structures in public and private sectors can favor dominant incumbents through network effects and procurement patterns. Key barriers to adoption and long-term maintenance of high-quality and secure OSS include insufficient resourcing for maintenance and security hardening, limited capacity for coordinated vulnerability handling, and gaps in deployment and integration (documentation, usability, operations, and legacy system integration). Strategic dependencies can arise where core development, hosting, CI/CD, and package infrastructure are controlled outside the EU. Fragmentation across many projects and uneven sectoral maturity complicate convergence on widely reused components. Sustainable contributions are limited when organizations use OSS without incentives to contribute upstream. Common corporate barriers include missing internal KPIs for contribution, limited staff time allocated to upstream work, and legal or compliance uncertainty. This reduces the capacity of communities and maintainers to sustain critical components. In the public sector, OSS can support interoperability and reuse across administrations and cross-border services, and it can strengthen transparency and supply-chain visibility relevant to cybersecurity. In the private sector, OSS can reduce lock-in risks, enable switching and multi-vendor strategies, and improve innovation speed through shared building blocks. Assessing added value should include total cost of ownership (including operations and migration), exit options and lock-in risk, security and supply-chain risk management, and interoperability with open standards. A concrete measure to increase reuse and public value is Public Money Public Code: software developed with public funds should, by default, be released under an open-source license with public repositories and adequate documentation, with clearly defined exceptions (e.g., specific security constraints or data protection requirements). Procurement guidance and templates can standardize these requirements and support reuse across Member States. To increase sustainable corporate contributions, EU-level measures can include procurement evaluation criteria that recognize upstream contributions, security maintenance, participation in open governance, and long-term stewardship of dependencies. Complementary actions include targeted funding for maintenance and security work on widely used components and practical guidance that reduces legal and operational friction for companies contributing to OSS. To strengthen the contributor pipeline, EU-level programs can incentivize students and young professionals through paid fellowships or internships linked to priority areas (cybersecurity, cloud infrastructure, AI, edge/IoT). Priority areas for OSS support include cybersecurity tooling and secure-by-design as well as privacy-by-design components, cloud and digital infrastructure building blocks, AI frameworks and related tooling, edge/IoT platforms, core internet technologies, and open hardware ecosystems. Sectors where increased OSS uptake can improve competitiveness and cyber resilience include public administrations and digital government services, critical infrastructure domains with high security requirements, industrial stacks such as automotive and manufacturing, and EU cloud and data infrastructure.

Complementary actions include targeted funding for maintenance and security work on widely used components and practical guidance that reduces legal and operational friction for companies contributing to OSS. To strengthen the contributor pipeline, EU-level programs can incentivize students and young professionals through paid fellowships or internships linked to priority areas (cybersecurity, cloud infrastructure, AI, edge/IoT). Priority areas for OSS support include cybersecurity tooling and secure-by-design as well as privacy-by-design components, cloud and digital infrastructure building blocks, AI frameworks and related tooling, edge/IoT platforms, core internet technologies, and open hardware ecosystems. Sectors where increased OSS uptake can improve competitiveness and cyber resilience include public administrations and digital government services, critical infrastructure domains with high security requirements, industrial stacks such as automotive and manufacturing, and EU cloud and data infrastructure.

feedback_text: I would like to point out that in practice, 100 % of people in the EU are at least temporarily ‘disabled’ in their lives. It is true that the official definition of disability does not cover infants (necessary complete help), children and adolescents whose brains and motors are not yet fully developed, periods of frailty during illness, accident, pregnancy, under the influence of medication or alkphol/drugs, signs of failure during chemotherapy and in old age... Nevertheless, all these groups of people benefit from accessibility. Yours sincerely, Almut Siebel

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: The European Sea Ports Organisation (ESPO) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the Commissions ongoing consultation processes and remains committed to providing constructive, evidence based input. 1. Consultation workload and stakeholder fatigue ESPO greatly values the opportunity to provide input, but the large volume of simultaneous initiatives contributes to stakeholder fatigue. A more strategic scheduling of consultations would support higher quality contributions from stakeholders. 2. Timing and duration of consultations Several consultationsparticularly those with four week deadlines during the summer periodmake it difficult for stakeholders to respond comprehensively. ESPO recommends: Avoiding major holiday periods when setting consultation deadlines. Allowing longer consultation periods for complex or highly technical files. 3. Transparency and meaningful engagement In some cases, the short interval between the closure of consultations and the publication of consolidated summaries has led to a perception that stakeholder feedback was not fully considered. ESPO therefore recommends: Ensuring sufficient time for proper analysis of all contributions. Evaluate the results and also prioritize the input according to the relevance of the participant Explaining more clearly how the feedback has influenced the Commissions final approach. 4. Need for more bilateral and small group exchanges General consultationsespecially those structured around yes/no questions, rating scales, or very large stakeholder meetingsoften fail to capture the full complexity of issues affecting ports. This is certainly the case when a legislation is not port specific, but covers different areas, different types of stakeholders. ESPO would prefer: More bilateral discussions between the Commission and stakeholders. Small, focused group meetings to allow for deeper technical exchanges. These settings foster more meaningful dialogue and richer insights. 5. Strengthening field insight through direct engagement ESPO believes that Commission officials should again be allowed (time and budget wise) to participate in in-sector conferences, site visits, and thematic workshops, helping them to: Gain a clearer understanding of operational realities. Build stronger insight into the challenges and constraints faced by ports. This would enhance the evidence base and reduce reliance on overly broad questionnaires. 6. Balancing time pressure with quality outcomes ESPO recognises the time pressures faced by Commission services. However, implementing the improvements above would ultimately help deliver informed, realistic, and more effective policy outcomes, to the benefit of both policymakers and stakeholders.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Europe depends heavily on foreign technology especially US cloud and software services. This access to and control over our data is concerning, especially when considering that the Trump administration has demonstrated repeatedly that they are willing to exploit economic dependencies to force their policies on other nations. It will be difficult to replace US software services but the open source software model is very well suited for this transition. The need for technological sovereignty is shared by many nations within and outside Europe. Instead of wasting resources by independently developing solutions for the same problems open source allows for collaboration by reusing and extending existing solutions. Additionally private companies face many of the same technological challenges as public institutions and would also benefit from and contribute to the development of these solutions. While a lot of investment into the development of European alternatives is necessary this investment will benefit the domestic economy instead of going to large foreign tech corporations. The open source software development model has been proven extensively as a lot of the foundational software powering the internet and used by most companies and institutions is open source (e.g. Linux, OpenSSL, LLVM, GCC, chromium, etc.). The open source model is also beneficial for security. While being open source in itself does not make software secure, it allows anyone who used to software to audit and improve its security. With closed source software users have to trust the software vendor to ensure security with no way to perform independent audits. Having established open source standards and solutions also makes collaboration easier by reducing fragmentation between incompatible solutions. One of the biggest problems of open source is that the maintainers of widely used software are often overwhelmed and face burn out because they are maintaining these projects in their free time while working a full time job. Providing funding to allow them to make a living by working on open source projects would solve this issue. The EU should heavily invest into the development of open source solutions especially for public administration. Ensuring the independence of public administration is most important and would also serve as an example to other institutions that open source solutions are viable alternatives. Existing regulations for software development (e.g. in aerospace and automotive applications) also need to be adapted to accommodate open source software as in their current form it is often impractical or even impossible to achieve the compliance requirements when using open source solutions. These regulations were often written with the assumption that there exists a single software vendor that develops and sells the software which breaks down when using open source software. Other barriers for open source adoption should also be removed by requiring companies to provide open interfaces to their services. This is especially important companies providing essential services. For example most online banking applications rely on closed source solutions from Apple and Google which means they do not work on alternative open source operating systems. This means it is practically impossible to use these alternatives for most people as they are reliant on these services.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: The recent EU consultation on the modernisation of the Better Regulation framework clearly shows that the key problem is not a lack of tools, but the relationship between procedures, time, responsibility and human perception. Many existing processes are formally designed correctly, but are less effective because they do not take sufficient account of the essentials: how decisions are understood, remembered, transmitted and applied in people’s daily lives. The existing consultation system also needs to be expanded rather than deepened. The increasing fatigue of stakeholders does not primarily result from the frequency of participation procedures, but from the lack of feedback on what has become of the contributions made. Consultations will only be effective if they are placed in a comprehensible context which, in the short term, identifies which anreg_toggle areas have been picked up or discarded and, in the long term, makes visible which contributions have subsequently proven to be relevant. Participation should not be understood solely as data collection, but as a process that creates responsibility on both sides: contributors as well as the institutions that process these contributions. In this sense, a holistic approach does not mean gathering as many perspectives as possible at the same time, but rather listening contextually and assessing the respective contributions in their context. The aim of simplicity in the design of EU legislation must not be reduced to a purely formal reduction in complexity. Clarity is not only provided by shorter texts or standardised tools, but by rules that can be understood and accepted in the specific application environment. The choice of legal instruments should therefore take greater account of who implements them, the organisational, cultural or digital conditions under which they are implemented, and the implicit assumptions about knowledge, resources and room for manoeuvre. Digital tools can contribute to simplification, but at the same time carry the risk of new opacity, especially for smaller actors and citizens, if they are not deliberately designed to be user-centric. One often overlooked aspect of legislation is institutional management of time. Deadlines are a necessary steering tool, but may lose their legitimate function if they are used to demonstrate power, secure responsibilities or simulate workload. Efficient management is not reflected in the maximum use of pre-defined deadlines, but in an appropriate, factual speed of response. Further development of better regulation should therefore take into account qualitative criteria that not only measure formal adherence to time limits, but also assess the appropriateness and traceability of action. Overall, the next stage in the development of better regulation is not so much the introduction of additional instruments as a change in attitude towards impact, context and policy follow-up. Legislation is not just a formal act, but a relational process that requires trust and must generate trust. It takes effect where rules are not only decided but supported internally. Modern, future-proof policy-making therefore needs less repetition, more responsibility and the courage to take seriously those aspects that cannot be measured immediately, but whose importance is evident in the long term. ...... initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Europe must stand firm on digital sovereignty. For me, this has two parts. First, we must build a truly European and high-tech digital ecosystem. Second, we must apply rules and laws in a serious and consistent way across EU. On the ecosystem, digital sovereignty needs to first focus on the basics of our daily digital life: email and communication tools, social networks, cloud services, operating systems, office software and AI-based productivity tools. Today, Europe has many small, fragmented and underfunded solutions and companies. This does not allow us to compete with global players. We should move away from this fragmentation and instead support the emergence of a limited number of strong European companies, well financed and technologically solid. The key tool for this should not be subsidies. What European digital companies really need is not more grants, but customers and firm orders. Public procurement must therefore become a strategic instrument. It is time EU institutions and national administrations pave the way and lead by example ! They are the one that should start to use European digital tools whenever they are good enough. This would create real demand and help European solutions to scale to adoption develops across corporates, smaller industries and private individuals. This will not be easy. Moving public administrations to European tools is technically difficult and operationally demanding, EU cloud services for storage and emails, EU solutions for laptop operating systems, web browsers, office suite and phone communication or social media. It requires strong technical skills inside administrations, clear leadership, and the willingness to commit to a long-term transition. This is a 3 to 5 year journey, not a quick win. European digital innovators need customers, not subsidies. Public administrations need skills, leadership and courage to make this change. The second part is law enforcement. EU digital rules must be applied in a firm and credible way, especially to large platforms and social media. Services that do not comply with EU laws should not be allowed to operate until they do. Without enforcement, digital sovereignty remains only a word. I also believe Europe needs a stronger and more coherent governance at EU level, possibly through a dedicated digital authority, similar in spirit to what exists for food safety or medicines. And we must avoid two weights, two measures. Traditional media are subject to strict rules, while some social media accounts with hundreds of thousands of followers are not, often under the argument of freedom of expression. But when an account reaches such scale and influence, it effectively becomes a media actor and should be subject to appropriate rules and responsibilities. Digital sovereignty cannot work with such asymmetry. In my view, Europe will not secure its digital future with more fragmented initiatives or more subsidies. We need a strong European digital ecosystem, built through demand and public procurement, and we need firm and fair enforcement of EU law. This requires technical competence, leadership and political courage. Without this, Europe will remain dependent in areas that are now central to our economy, our democracy and our strategic autonomy.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Here is why European development policy fail, and the US development policy sometimes works. If you are working on some strategically important technology, at a university or in a shed somewhere in the US, some day a man you never seen will appear and ask some questions. With out saying who they are they will end the conversation with a "Keep working on this", and then a few weeks later a million dollar check will show up in the mail from some agency you have never heard of. Eventually the technology starts working and is adopted by others. Mean while, the EU has also recognized that this is an important technology, so they will not spend a measly million euros, they will spend 100 million euros! And they will do so by putting out a call for anyone who might be interested in this technology, pulling in every university that wants a grant, and none of the people in sheds who actually are working on it. Then the EU says "We must leverage all 500 million Europeans and all 28 countries" so its puts together a group of every possible stakeholder, and tells them to come up with a plan. 5 years later they have written a 3000 page plan, but have no implementation. Then to support this "important European endeavour" the EU will mandate that everyone uses the new European standard, in order to get EU support. But since the new standard is just a paper product, it doesn't solve anyone's problems, so European companies now have a choice: Adopt the EU standard, and eventually fail, or adopt the working standard coming from the US and lock them selves out of EU support. The wise ones choose the later. You want to help Open source? Great, It takes about ten minutes of googling to find the biggest opensource projects, and who the Europeans behind them are. Dont ask industry what they want, ask them what they are already using. Dont ask for people to apply for grants. The people you want to support, will never apply for them. You have to find them. They are busy people and if you are taking time away from what they are doing, you are not helping. The processing of the grant applications probably cost more than the grant size needs to be. 2 people in a tiny office could spend 10milion euro a year and make EU a haven for open source. That's 100k per year for the top 100 projects in Europe. That's the difference between doing a project on the side or full time. Saying you have money to give out attracts the wrong people. You have to find them yourself, and put your trust in them because you can see that they are willing to do the work without any support. Dont think you can create new projects, there are so many projects already, support the ones that are taking off. Dont pick winners, see who the winners are. Linux, Curl, Python, Blender. Dont write big checks, write small checks that lets people keep doing what they are doing. Europe is full of amazing projects by amazing people, but EU is a institution that only deals with institutions, so it never bothers to find us.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: We operate two pig fattening houses with partial coverage of the outside runs. These buildings were constructed approximately 10 years ago, in consultation and subsequent approval by the control body and control authority. The cover is approximately 90 % of the open-air area. The high proportion of cover is deliberately chosen, as it significantly reduces the input of rainwater, which is very beneficial for animal welfare and animal health. The cleanliness in the house is significantly improved by much lower outdoor moisture inputs. Furthermore, the environmental impact is severely reduced by significant reductions in external and indoor emissions. Structural changes are subject to the permit law with regard to building law and emission legislation and are counterproductive to animal welfare and animal health. Moreover, it is not clear that poultry houses operated over years of EU compliance will lose their eligibility for approval, even though the circumstances have not changed. This disregards the high level of legal certainty and the protection of legitimate expectations. Furthermore, such measures no longer provide investment certainty if such changes are required within the depreciation period. We therefore call for a legally certain procedure to keep existing and approved buildings in operation. A final EU Eco-Basic Regulation (EU) 2018/848 would be very helpful for us and would allow our pig farming to continue to operate. MFG organic farms in Lower Saxony
