initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: Introduction Childrens Rights Over Flights is a campaign by parents, grandparents, and other concerned people, calling for children's rights in the context of the climate emergency to be prioritised over aviation growth. We are volunteers who have come together due to our huge concern on this issue, and have worked together as a group on this submission. ETS review We call on the ETS review to recommend the following: * Meaningful action at EU and international levels to reduce aviation emissions. The current UN CORSIA scheme is not fit for purpose. CORSIA allows offsetting of fossil fuel emissions through land use or forestry-based offsetting schemes, which are highly problematic on multiple fronts. Fossil fuel emissions need to be reduced directly, not offset in this way. * Make the EU Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS) more effective in reducing aviation emissions and emissions generally by ensuring - No free permits: airlines should no longer be allocated free emission permits but must pay for all permits, Steep reductions in permits: the total quantity of permits issued should decrease steeply, in line with climate targets, - Include cargo/freight: ETS should be applied to cargo-only flights as well as passenger flights, - Include outside EEA: ETS should apply to flights outside the European Economic Area (EEA) as well as flights inside the EEA. We note that the consultation states While in principle the ETS covers emissions from all flights landing in and departing from the European Economic Area (EEA), the EU has temporarily limited the scope to intra-EEA flights. This is to encourage the development of an effective global carbon pricing scheme by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). The EU should not wait for international efforts, which are not moving with the needed speed and effectiveness. Rather the EU must lead by example to avert the climate crisis by putting in place effective measures now, while also encouraging ICAO to also do so. - Include all climate-related emissions: ETS should include the substantial non-CO2 climate effects of aviation as well as CO2 emissions. * Remove jet kerosene tax exemption in the EU: Ensure that jet kerosene tax exemption is abolished EU-wide as in commitments made in 2021 in the Fit For 55 package, and that the abolition of the exemption is not postponed. * Ban advertising of aviation, flight-based holiday packages, and products transported by air freight, throughout the EU. Advertising promotes and normalises patterns of consumption that are destructive to the climate, including frequent flying and air-freight-transported consumer products.
text_raw: Introduction Childrens Rights Over Flights is a campaign by parents, grandparents, and other concerned people, calling for children's rights in the context of the climate emergency to be prioritised over aviation growth. We are volunteers who have come together due to our huge concern on this issue, and have worked together as a group on this submission. ETS review We call on the ETS review to recommend the following: * Meaningful action at EU and international levels to reduce aviation emissions. The current UN CORSIA scheme is not fit for purpose. CORSIA allows offsetting of fossil fuel emissions through land use or forestry-based offsetting schemes, which are highly problematic on multiple fronts. Fossil fuel emissions need to be reduced directly, not offset in this way. * Make the EU Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS) more effective in reducing aviation emissions and emissions generally by ensuring - No free permits: airlines should no longer be allocated free emission permits but must pay for all permits, Steep reductions in permits: the total quantity of permits issued should decrease steeply, in line with climate targets, - Include cargo/freight: ETS should be applied to cargo-only flights as well as passenger flights, - Include outside EEA: ETS should apply to flights outside the European Economic Area (EEA) as well as flights inside the EEA. We note that the consultation states While in principle the ETS covers emissions from all flights landing in and departing from the European Economic Area (EEA), the EU has temporarily limited the scope to intra-EEA flights. This is to encourage the development of an effective global carbon pricing scheme by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). The EU should not wait for international efforts, which are not moving with the needed speed and effectiveness. Rather the EU must lead by example to avert the climate crisis by putting in place effective measures now, while also encouraging ICAO to also do so. - Include all climate-related emissions: ETS should include the substantial non-CO2 climate effects of aviation as well as CO2 emissions. * Remove jet kerosene tax exemption in the EU: Ensure that jet kerosene tax exemption is abolished EU-wide as in commitments made in 2021 in the Fit For 55 package, and that the abolition of the exemption is not postponed. * Ban advertising of aviation, flight-based holiday packages, and products transported by air freight, throughout the EU. Advertising promotes and normalises patterns of consumption that are destructive to the climate, including frequent flying and air-freight-transported consumer products.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: We are a group of developers working on open-source reverse engineering and disassembler tools (Rizin and Capstone). Tools like these are prerequisites in the offensive and defensive cybersecurity field, as well as in the archiving and restoration of software with lost source code. *We see open source as the fastest and most reliable way to:* - Save enormous amounts in licensing costs in institutions, the economy, and for EU citizens. - Keep support for older formats and computers. - Exceptionally enhance the cyber defense of, and trust in, IT infrastructure. - The EU and its citizens depend almost entirely on Microsoft, Alphabet (Google), Amazon (AWS) or Meta products. US companies have a de facto monopoly on most software used in the EU. These US companies are forced to provide any data the US government sees as relevant to an authorized investigation to protect against international terrorism or clandestine intelligence activities. The requests, executed via National Security Letters, do not require a court order and are not published. This enables the US government to extract exceptionally cheap intelligence. - Software without public source code is inherently untrustworthy. There is no technical way for companies to prove that their delivered software is free of malicious functionality. Even limiting access to source code to certain auditing parties wont increase trust, since these parties can be corrupted. - The only known way to produce trusted software is to make it open-source. Even if the source code contains security flaws or unwanted functionality, it can be removed by users or companies. - Making code public enables audits by any party, giving high confidence that the audited version is secure and works as intended. If it doesnt, this fact will also be known-contrary to proprietary software, which prevents either. - Replacing proprietary software with open-source solutions raises the price significantly for any actor to extract intelligence from IT systems or render them unusable. This is possible because: - It withdraws software development and control over it from the jurisdiction of any single nation. - It raises the cost of intelligence extraction from open-source-operated systems immediately. Instead of using national security laws, intelligence agencies have to rely on exceptionally expensive malware. - Open-source code also allows the supply chain to be tracked. Knowing what was used to create certain software enables risk management and investments at critical points. Both are impossible with proprietary software due to trade secrets and potentially uncooperative providers. - Open source is the fastest and most reliable way to grow a sovereign digital infrastructure. - The EU can enter this software market immediately, without the decade long groundwork, by supporting talented developers and their open-source tools, may of them located in the EU. The skill is there; the groundwork has already been done; it just needs funding. - A profit-oriented industry can be built by businesses providing support for open-source solutions. *We strongly encourage the Commission to define a strategy that:* - Enforces public institutions to use open-source solutions. - Defines and enforces EU-wide open standards for documents, protocols, and databases for any software used within the EU. - This prevents lock-in effects for organizations. Switching to open-source alternatives is often seen as too risky because the perceived cost of getting rid of proprietary formats (like .docx) is too high. Enforcing open standards EU-wide can change this significantly. - Enforces open standards on every software company operating within the EU market. This will incentivize non-EU companies to implement the open standards in their proprietary software, which in turn keeps EU nations compatible. - Additionally, the EU should promote these open standards globally.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Linux Foundation Europe welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the public consultation on the European Open Digital Ecosystems Strategy. More detailed feedback is provided in the accompanying document, while the key points are summarised as follows: 1. Open source is a basis of successful business ventures and is widely used across industries, with most modern software built on open source components, whether proprietary or not. It is a diverse ecosystem spanning multiple governance, sustainability, and commercial models, and the Linux Foundation welcomes the Call for Evidences acknowledgment of this reality and its pursuit of pragmatic, evidence-based approaches to strengthen EU competitiveness and technological autonomy. 2. Europe should build on and influence the global open source commons rather than pursue isolated notions of European Open Source, as existing global projects already underpin cloud, AI, and emerging digital infrastructure. Strategic upstream investment and participation in these commons - alongside scaling local commercial open source companies through funding instruments, market-access initiatives, and updated procurement practices - offers the most realistic path to technological sovereignty, innovation, and talent retention. 3. Critical open source infrastructure should be hosted under neutral governance to ensure balanced decision-making, mitigate single-vendor and lock-in risks, and foster rapid de facto standardisation.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open source underpins the internet, and software that runs on almost all hardware in the world (proprietary and non-proprietary). Many big tech sell open source projects (software as a service, e.g. databases such as PostgreSQL and redis) as products, earning without funding those who keep the project (reference is made to what happened between Google and FFMPEG). Many of these open source projects (FOSS) have been developed in europa (Linux kernel penso is one of the most relevant) and can create the founding heart of europa. I suggest creating a committee of “open source experts” that by combining their minds can find a way to make open source a cornerstone of the EU on which to build a society independent from big tech, perhaps finding a way to finance open source. What I think is crucial becomes open source because today our entire software system is based on very often paid closed options: SUITE: Text editor presentations spreadsheets notes CLOUD: computing power storage space password manager mail social media chatbot TO SOFTWARE: smart phone home assistant WEB: I am becoming rather pessimistic about the recent web bubble, but I think the EU has the power to change things (perhaps starting with reappropriating EU domains, etc. which I think are currently in the hands of non-union companies) and making initial sacrifices to create a world of software for humans, not for customers. In order to achieve this, it is necessary, as in most cases, to start with schools and training. we can work as much as we want in open source, but if you teach at school only to use google drive, power point and excell, it will never change anything. In the beginning, sacrifices and fatigue will be needed, but I am sure that the world will be better when new generations know that they will use linux instead of windows (also because after having secured the monopoly of users, services are getting worse to get maximum profit). I am wondering: what if we can build a new software paradigm where, instead of advertising, we try to maximise the well-being of the user? I believe, I hope that you too understand that although it is difficult to detach from this reality, there is a better one.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: City of Turku welcomes Commissions initiative and emphases that the EU procurement rules should be simplified and administrative burden should be reduced. Contracting authorities should be provided flexibility in carrying out public procurement. It is likewise essential that the regulatory framework enables the integration of environmental and social considerations into procurement processes. However, the procurement rules should remain procedural in nature and should not incorporate substantive legislation. Contracting authorities must retain sufficient discretion to define the content and requirements of the subject matter of the procurement. The regulatory framework should also ensure that procurement procedures are accessible to companies of all sizes. The following presents more detailed perspectives on the matter: 1. Administrative burden The administrative burden on contracting authorities should not increase as a result of the reform of the directives. On the contrary, the provisions of the directives should be simplified. For example, the introduction of eForms has increased the administrative burden. These forms have complicated the work of procurers. Procurement directives should also remain purely procedural rules. Contracting authorities are increasingly required to comply with sectoral legislation, the substance of which requires resourced to understand. 2. Simplifying Procurement Procedures for Suppliers Procurement procedures are also burdensome for suppliers. One concrete example is the ESPD form which is difficult to understand and challenging to complete for suppliers who do not regularly participate in tenders. Simplifying procurement procedures would facilitate the work of both suppliers and contracting authorities. 3. Share of Foreign Suppliers In contracting authorities so-called cross-border procurements, where suppliers would come from other EU countries, might be very few. Usually, the needs of contracting authorities are such that it is challenging for foreign suppliers to participate in tenders. 4. Other Considerations Attention should be paid to updating CPV codes. The code system is outdated and does not reflect current needs. Even if the directives are lightened, it is already evident, for example, in the procurement law reform being prepared in Finland, that procurement legislation is being tightened

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: As a European citizen, it's important that we strengthen our industrial capacity and strategic autonomy. I believe that the presented proposals presented here appeal to me because I believe it contributes to a fair competition and a more sustainable future. 1. I believe the reciprocity clause should be applied more strictly. I have the impression that European companies often have to comply with rules that don't apply to competitors from third countries. That doesn't feel fair. If other countries don't open their markets, why should we do so unconditionally? 2. I believe it's essential to consider the total cost of ownership. Cheap often turns out to be expensive. As a taxpayer, I want public funds to be spent wisely, and that means considering not only the purchase price, but also lifespan, maintenance, and sustainability. 3. A preference for "Made in Europe" seems logical to me. I notice that much essential infrastructure depends on imports from countries that don't always apply the same standards. It's important to me that the products we use for our water and energy supply are reliable and meet European standards. Therefore, it seems reasonable to me that the origin of products carries more weight. 4. Finally, I believe that products from companies convicted of dumping or subsidy abuse should not simply be allowed into our market. It makes sense that we protect our industry from unfair practices. 5. Making non-financial criteria mandatory seems like a good idea to me. Environmental, social, and local impact are at least as important to me as price. If we take these values seriously, they should also be factored into tenders.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: [ Barriers that hamper adoption ] Throughout our educational system, it is taught that there is basically one kind of computer, and that computer runs Windows. Unless youth is particularly curious, or happen to go into IT, they will never know about open source software in general, and Linux specifically. This knowledge barrier is maintained by lucrative contracts that Microsoft and others have with the public sector. If we want adoption, we need to start with awareness. And that starts with education. [ Added value of open source in public sector ] The added value of open source in the public sector overlaps a lot with the added value in industrial or even private use: - The guarantee that software will not disappear or have unforeseen license costs - The freedom and ability to change software as needs come up In addition, using open source in the public sector aligns nicely with the mission of most public sectors: to be of benefit to the public, not to one closed corporation. [ Concrete measures that may be taken at the EU level ] - Recommend that all government-developed or sponsored software needs to have an OSI-approved license - Education: Re-introduce computers away from being "magic boxes that Microsoft or Apple control" to "sophisticated machines that we can control ourselves". This can totally be done from day 1 in school, see for example the Raspberry Pi (yes, a US initiative, but the concept can easily be repeated on e.g. RISC-V). - Education: Create a software awareness of the "state of the art" of open source. Things that have been accepted as a "solved problem", exist as open source software that is slowly taking over their respective industry. Examples of solved problems are operating system (Linux), document writing (multiple alternatives), 3D editing (Blender), Audio editing (Ardour), and many more. [ Prioritization of technology areas ] Prioritization shouldn't be based on industry or technology areas, but rather on maturity of available open source solutions. See the list above, operating systems, document writing tools, etc. [ Open source for increased competitiveness and resilience ] This question is somewhat conflicting in its premise. Quality open source emerges, because multiple parties find that competition is undesirable on a certain product area: everybody needs an operating system, everybody needs a standard way to exchange documents, etc. So these are no longer areas one can compete on. Quite the opposite: By embracing open source in ANY industry to solve problems that make a certain company NOT unique, resources can be spent on the actual unique selling points of a company.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As an EU citizen, I believe open source is not just a technical choice, but a strategic imperative for Europes future. It offers a unique opportunity to reduce our dependency on non-EU tech giants, enhance our cybersecurity, and foster innovation that aligns with European values. The EU must act decisively to remove barriers to adoption, invest in skills and infrastructure, and create a regulatory environment that supports open-source growth. I urge the Commission to: Prioritize public procurement reform to ensure open-source solutions are given fair consideration. Invest in sustainable funding and business models to support the long-term maintenance and scaling of open-source projects. Strengthen governance and community support to ensure open-source projects are secure, reliable, and aligned with EU priorities. Focus on critical sectors like cloud, AI, cybersecurity, and public infrastructure to maximize the impact of open-source investments. Concrete Measures and Actions at EU Level To unlock the full potential of open source, the EU should focus on the following actions: Policy & Funding: EU Open-Source Strategy (Q1 2026): Develop and implement a comprehensive strategy that supports the entire open-source lifecycle, from development to market integration, with a strong emphasis on sustainable governance and scaling Public Procurement Reform: Update procurement rules to mandate open-source components (e.g., 70% by 2030) and establish clear cybersecurity certification pathways for open-source solutions Public-Private Partnerships: Encourage public sector adoption and contribution to open-source projects, ensuring financial sustainability through partnerships with industry and civil society Regulation & Standardization: Cyber Resilience Act (CRA): Enforce baseline cybersecurity standards for all digital products, including open-source software, to ensure security and reliability Open Source Programme Offices (OSPOs): Establish OSPOs in public administrations to drive institutional learning, collaboration, and governance Standardization: Support the creation of a Fourth European Standardisation Organisation (ESO) to promote open development and interoperability across the EU Investment & Skills: Targeted Funding: Direct EU funding (e.g., Digital Europe Programme, Horizon Europe) to open-source projects in critical sectors such as cloud computing, AI, cybersecurity, and automotive Skills Development: Invest in training and certification programs to address the skills gap and support digital transformation in both public and private sectors 4. Priority Technology Areas The EU should prioritize open-source development in the following areas to maximize impact: Cloud & Edge Computing: Reduce dependency on non-EU providers and enhance data sovereignty by supporting open-source alternatives Artificial Intelligence (AI): Foster ethical, transparent, and compliant AI development, in line with the EU AI Act, to ensure Europe leads in responsible AI innovation Cybersecurity: Develop open-source tools for secure software, identity management, and infrastructure resilience to protect critical digital infrastructure Open Hardware & Industrial Applications: Support open-source solutions in automotive, manufacturing, and energy sectors to strengthen competitiveness and digital sovereignty Public Digital Infrastructure: Invest in open-source solutions for government services, health, transport, and public administration to ensure secure, interoperable, and citizen-centric digital services By taking these steps, the EU can harness the full potential of open source to achieve technological sovereignty, competitiveness, and cyber resilienceensuring that Europe remains a leader in the global digital economy.

Priority Technology Areas The EU should prioritize open-source development in the following areas to maximize impact: Cloud & Edge Computing: Reduce dependency on non-EU providers and enhance data sovereignty by supporting open-source alternatives Artificial Intelligence (AI): Foster ethical, transparent, and compliant AI development, in line with the EU AI Act, to ensure Europe leads in responsible AI innovation Cybersecurity: Develop open-source tools for secure software, identity management, and infrastructure resilience to protect critical digital infrastructure Open Hardware & Industrial Applications: Support open-source solutions in automotive, manufacturing, and energy sectors to strengthen competitiveness and digital sovereignty Public Digital Infrastructure: Invest in open-source solutions for government services, health, transport, and public administration to ensure secure, interoperable, and citizen-centric digital services By taking these steps, the EU can harness the full potential of open source to achieve technological sovereignty, competitiveness, and cyber resilienceensuring that Europe remains a leader in the global digital economy.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The following feedback focuses on open-source silicon and its ecosystem. "Open silicon" denotes an integrated circuit whose complete design (from the abstract description to the layout) is publicly available and can be implemented using open-source EDA tools, enabling full auditability and technological sovereignty. The text is largely based on the following documents: Recommendations and roadmap for the development of open-source silicon in the EU (2023) https://wiki.f-si.org/images/1/19/Recommendations_and_roadmap_open_silicon_2023_11_03.pdf White paper for the EC, January 2020 https://wiki.f-si.org/index.php?title=White_paper_for_the_EC,_January_2020 Question 1: Strengths, Weaknesses, and Barriers Strengths: Europe has excellent chip design education and historically contributed core Electronic Design Automation (EDA) algorithms. Pioneering open-source EDA companies exist (YosysHQ, ChipFlow), and IHP Microelectronics released Germany's first open PDK, a replicable model. Weaknesses: The know-how for developing (not for using) EDA tools has nearly disappeared from EU universities. Academia lacks incentives for open-source contributions -publications count, open-source projects don't. Most professors are users, not developers, of proprietary EDA tools, creating cultural bias against open-source. Barriers to adoption: Mainstream EDA vendors prohibit publishing any outputs from their tools, killing open hardware at its root. Students face NDA burdens (e.g. restricted access or inability to study the EDA code) before lectures, discouraging careers in chip design. Mainstream tools are unfriendly and expensive. Barriers to sustainability: EU dependence on foreign EDA tools creates existential extraterritorial risk, as demonstrated when Cadence shut Moscow offices overnight. Permissive licenses allow publicly-funded innovations to be absorbed by proprietary vendors effectively undermining the economic sustainability of the open-source ecosystem. Patent threats loom over open-source EDA and hardware developers. References: Roadmap §3.3, §7, White Paper §3-4 Question 2: Added Value Cost: Open-source EDA tools eliminate license fees exceeding 1M/year per company. OpenROAD development cost ~$3M/year total -less than three commercial licenses. Lock-in: Open-source silicon enables foundry portability. Foundry PDKs written in proprietary formats lock entire ecosystems to foreign EDA vendors. Security: Open silicon (open all the way to layout) enables public verification that fabricated chips match published layouts (genuine auditability). See the ROCA vulnerability in Estonian ID cards for an example of the problem. Innovation: Open-source enables massive design synthesis parallelization impossible with costly proprietary licenses. OpenROAD achieved 24-hour RTL-to-layout synthesis and 12nm-node capability by 2023. Community-developed PDKs can arguably contain fewer errors through public scrutiny. Concrete examples: Prof. Pretl (JKU Linz) runs complete open-source chip design courses. TinyTapeout democratizes silicon access commercially. IHP's open PDK enabled the first fully-open European chip designs. References: Roadmap §3.2, §4.2, §4.3, §12.1, White Paper §2 For better formatting and for the full text, see attached .pdf

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: We would like to propose that importers be granted the ability to directly update the information in Box 17 of the COI certificate, which refers to the vessel(s) used for transport. In practice, changes to the means of transport often occur during shipmentfor example, due to delays or congestion in major portsresulting in the goods being transferred to different vessels. These changes frequently happen at short notice and across multiple time zones, making it administratively inefficient for only the suppliers control body to be authorized to make updates, and can be costly in increased delays and fees in ports because of delays. Since importers already have the right to complete Box 20 of the COI certificate, where receipt of the shipment's arrival date is confirmed, it would be logical and consistent to also allow importers to update Box 17. This would improve traceability, reduce the risk of inaccuracies, and simplify the administrative process surrounding the COI certificate.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Symfony welcomes the opportunity to provide evidence for the European Commissions Open Digital Ecosystem Strategy. As a European-born open source PHP framework that now powers over 20% of the professional web and supports a global community of 600,000 developers, our contribution is attached and focuses on five key areas: 1) we advocate for open source as a foundational infrastructure for European digital autonomy. 2) we propose contribute-back procurement mandates and a permanent EU FOSSA program to ensure long-term maintenance. 3) we suggest investing in the mid-stack frameworks where European business logic lives to avoid vendor lock-in. 4) we address the risks that AI poses to the developer talent pipeline and the professional craft. 5) we call for direct financial and legal support for the developers who are the architects of Europe's digital autonomy.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I'm VP of Community at Zig Software Foundation (ZSF from now on), an American non-profit foundation (501c3, EIN 84-5105214) dedicated to developing Zig, an open source programming language. ZSF lives entirely off donations (see 2025 financials https://ziglang.org/news/2025-financials/ ), has been operating for about 8 year now and we're starting to see mainstream adoption of the language by tech companies all over the world. In my experience the funding model we adopted is extremely effective for big-enough open source projects, but it doesn't seem it would be possible to replicate it in EU. More specifically, I'm an Italian citizen and I don't believe it would be possible for me to create a tax-exempt foundation comparable to an American 501c3. Likewise, US taxpayers can deduct donations to ZSF (and other 501c3 non-profits) while in EU, as far as I'm aware, tax deducibility of donations to non-profits can only happen within the same country (so I, an Italian citizen, can only deduct donations to Italian non-profits). The fact that ZSF can pay developers to work full-time on its open-source project shows that the tech industry is interested in this particular open-source solution, to the point of being willing to pay for its development. This is testament to the value that the public attributes to open source projects such as Zig. As stated above, unfortunately it doesn't seem possible for EU citizens to capture that interest in the same way that American companies can under tax code 501c3. In my opinion, first and foremost there must be a straight-forward way for EU citizens to setup non-profit, tax-exempt foundations to host big open source projects. Secondarily EU citizens should be able to donate to these foundations and get tax benefits. In terms of industries that would benetit the most from increased usage of open source software, I think that the automotive sector is the one that would benefit the most. I know of plenty of horror stories where car manufacturers are effectively held hostage by closed source software, sold to them by vendors that often go out of business, leaving them with unmaintainable software solutions.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: Contracts awarded for purposes of resale or lease to third parties. It is proposed to align the regulation and to also provide for the possibility of excluding the contracts for the purpose of resale or leasing to third parties from the Directive 2014/24/EU. Explanation: Contracting authorities may also carry out activities related to the resale or leasing of the subject of the contract to third parties, and they may not have a special or exclusive right to sell or lease the subject of such contracts, so the exclusion for resale or lease to third parties should not be associated with to the status of undertakings under the Directives. So it is proposed to align the regulation and to also provide for the possibility of excluding the contracts for the purpose of resale or leasing to third parties from the Directive 2014/24/EU.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: We can only agree on the comments by Kahls Kaffe and Arvid Nordquist about box 17 in the COI related to green coffee import. As the bill of lading is issued from origin to final destination, without stating all the feeders or truck plates, it does not make sense to list all means of the transport between POL and POD in the COI. It just adds a lot of complexity, delays and costs which does not benefit the cause of Organic import. If we as importers were allowed to make the changes in box 17, based on the tracking data, even that would be a good improvement.
text_raw: We can only agree on the comments by Kahls Kaffe and Arvid Nordquist about box 17 in the COI related to green coffee import. As the bill of lading is issued from origin to final destination, without stating all the feeders or truck plates, it does not make sense to list all means of the transport between POL and POD in the COI. It just adds a lot of complexity, delays and costs which does not benefit the cause of Organic import. If we as importers were allowed to make the changes in box 17, based on the tracking data, even that would be a good improvement.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The European Brain Council welcomes the Commissions initiative to further structure the European Research Area and strongly supports its ambition to strengthen Europes competitiveness, foster integration and make the EU the most attractive destination for researchers and innovators. For brain research in particular, long-term commensurate and predictable investment is essential. Limited resources as well as segmented funding mechanisms remain a major barrier to progress. The ERA Act should ensure that national commitments to R&D are backed by clear monitoring, accountability and mechanisms that promote stability. Brain disorders neurological and mental alike are widespread, highly disabling and often difficult to treat, representing the leading cause of disability and the second leading cause of death in the world. The brain is the most complex organ of the human body. Understanding how the brain works, how brain diseases progress and finding treatments and cures for these diseases is a long-term endeavour. Brain research is highly interdisciplinary, requiring the use of a wide range of research techniques, based on different models human, artificial, digital and, predominantly, animal which remains essential for progress in this domain. We therefore encourage the inclusion of targeted measures that recognise the unique challenges of brain research, which is inherently interdisciplinary and requires cooperation across science, technology, healthcare and social domains. Reducing administrative burdens will also be critical to accelerate therapeutic innovation and the translation of research into clinical applications, particularly in the life science ecosystem. Harmonisation of regulations and simplification of procedures would strengthen Europes ability to compete globally while supporting researchers on the ground. Moreover, knowledge valorisation should be at the heart of the ERA Act, ensuring that discoveries are effectively translated into innovations and policies that deliver societal benefits. Involving patients and caregivers in this process will help close the innovation gap and ensure that outcomes meet real-world needs. Stronger coordination between the EU and Member States is crucial to ensure that investments and policies are aligned with the strategic priorities laid out in the 10th Framework Programme for Research & Innovation, the Life Sciences Strategy, and other key files for the European research and innovation ecosystem. Brain health and brain capital should be embedded as cross-cutting priorities in research and innovation roadmaps, guaranteeing synergies with health, digital, industrial and social policies. Equally, improving mobility and research careers, promoting open science and ensuring equitable access to infrastructures will be necessary to create the right framework conditions. The ERA Act offers a timely opportunity to build a coherent, resilient and globally competitive European research and innovation ecosystem. By ensuring stable investment and removing barriers to collaboration, the EU can reinforce its position as a global leader in science and innovation while addressing the growing societal and economic challenges posed by brain disorders. The European Brain Council (EBC) is a network of key players in the Brain Area, with a membership encompassing scientific societies, patient organisations, professional societies and industry partners. A non-profit organisation based in Brussels, its main mission is to promote brain research with the ultimate goal of improving the lives of the millions of Europeans living with brain conditions, mental and neurological alike.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: There must be exemptions from full grazing for dairy cattle and cattle holdings. The word ‘full’ must be removed from the law. Holdings which, for structural reasons, cannot carry out grazing or which individual groups of animals cannot graze, need a perspective. The grazing principle should be interpreted as follows: Grazing, where possible, otherwise access to open-air areas and roughage. I would also like to refer to various legal opinions which conclude that the current EU Organic Regulation does not require grazing.
text_raw: There must be exemptions from full grazing for dairy cattle and cattle holdings. The word ‘full’ must be removed from the law. Holdings which, for structural reasons, cannot carry out grazing or which individual groups of animals cannot graze, need a perspective. The grazing principle should be interpreted as follows: Grazing, where possible, otherwise access to open-air areas and roughage. I would also like to refer to various legal opinions which conclude that the current EU Organic Regulation does not require grazing.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: As an academic, it seems to me that the support mechanisms for initiatives in the field of free or open-source software are not sufficient and that this creates difficulties in bringing about innovative and promising projects. For example, in my University of Technology, we are developing two open-source software packages. The first is a software to help with problem solving and feedback. This software is used by several companies (ETI-type), particularly in the aeronautics sector. It therefore corresponds to a real need and could be disseminated on a larger scale if we had the means to do so, which is not the case today. The second is software resulting from a pedagogical innovation that consists of developing scenarios in which students play the role of characters in an adventure punctuated by puzzles that need to be resolved in order to progress. We have developed a collaborative platform to easily create, play and share such scenarios. But here too, we are struggling to find funding to accompany and promote this initiative. I therefore suggest that calls for projects be put in place very quickly to enable the emergence and consolidation of initiatives in the field of open source software (at all stages of maturity). Finally, a more personal reflection: as a daily user of open source software, I believe that the European Commission should provide more resources for open source environments, which often run after relatively low funding despite being very useful. The laudable political ambition to develop open-source software must be accompanied by means to make it a reality.

I speak in full knowledge of the facts as a person with a disability who often says: “I’m sorry for me because asking for help from the institutions is complex and overly long.”

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: PRIMIGEA hereby submits its comments in response to the public consultation launched by the European Commission on the review of the Public Procurement Directives. From the perspective of the mineral raw materials sector, PRIMIGEA considers that this review represents a key opportunity to reposition public procurement as a strategic instrument in support of sustainability, industrial resilience, competitiveness and the European Unions open strategic autonomy. Mineral raw materials constitute the essential material base of all industrial value chains, and any reform of the public procurement framework has a direct and systemic impact on their functioning, security of supply and investment conditions. In this context, PRIMIGEA advocates a shift away from procurement models predominantly focused on price, towards approaches based on the overall quality of tenders, legal certainty, proportionality and regulatory coherence. PRIMIGEA underlines the need to ensure a level playing field, effective protection against dumping and abnormally low tenders, meaningful access for SMEs, and greater harmonisation in the application of procurement rules across Member States. The Confederation also supports the introduction of clear and legally robust European preference mechanisms, including for mineral raw materials and intermediate products, based on substantive Made in Europe criteria. Through these comments, PRIMIGEA seeks to contribute constructively to the development of a modern, coherent and strategic public procurement framework that strengthens European industry and guarantees the security of supply of critical and non-energy mineral raw materials.

A more coherent and practicable EU procurement framework is essential for efficient public spending, healthy competition, and the strategic use of procurement to support Europes broader policy goals. A more coherent and practicable EU procurement framework is essential for efficient public spending, healthy competition, and the strategic use of procurement to support Europes broader policy goals. Best regards, Esapekka Kuikka Procurement Director Senate Properties

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The EU has opportunities to develop the open source ecosystem and build it up as a sovereign alternative to the traditional "big tech" model used by many companies. An ideal scenario would see a continent working together in a non-zero sum economy, everyone contributing together to build a more secure, useful, and local technology environment. Ultimately, a functioning digital economy needs to treat software not as a source of rental income, but as a basis on which every other useful job and service is built, and this requires a more collaborative approach without gatekeepers. Open source forces this to be the case. The collaborative approach offered by open source allows people and organisations to share their own resources, and to collaborate on developing them as they need, without having to wait for investment from the company that owns the technology. This applies to everything from full operating system environments such as those offered by KDE, organised by a group in Germany, to smaller suites focused on specific tools such as LibreOffice, run by The Document Foundation, also based in Germany. A fully open source workspace offers better security as there is no central authority that can "retire" a tool, preventing workers from using it, and even if maintainers stop supporting it, another team can step in and take ownership. Many large tech companies such as google are noted for their propensity to stop offering products that people rely on (see killedbygoogle.com), or for companies such as adobe to take working products and force companies into an expensive cloud subscription model which takes control of their own work away from them. Open source can also refer to data - be this datasets in open access science, databases such as mapping and geo data (such as OpenStreetMap, developed initially in the UK), and resources such as educational material (often seen with open code education groups such as Code Your Future in the UK). There are some challenges to ensure that an open ecosystem can function better than closed source technologies: 1. Anti-circumvention laws prevent organisations in the EU from developing alternatives that can work with or compete with closed source technologies and if the EU is serious about supporting open source and a modern digital ecosystem, these laws should be repealed. 2. There needs to be much stricter requirements ensuring that closed systems adhere to interoperability, whether this is from a data aspect (such as allowing transfer of data between services) or a systems aspect (allowing users of different, even competing, services the ability to program them to talk to each other). 3. There needs to be an EU-wide approach to the concept of "public money for public code" - normalising the idea that code is a public good and that any investments should see the output shared with everyone. This should also extend to EU institutions only using open source solutions in their work and communications.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: EU Procurement legislation shall leave room for local discretion The Association of Finnish Cities and Municipalities (AFCM) considers that the most important goal in the revision of the Public Procurement Directives shall be to simplify the current procedural rules. We consider that simplification means, in practice, less rules and more flexibility to local authorities. 1. Fewer procedures with less detail Rules on the scope of directives, applicable procedures, and procurement announcements should be simplified for both contracting authorities and tenderers. The Concessions Directive could serve as a model for such streamlined rules. - There is no need for detailed descriptions of procedural phases; a simple distinction between one-phase and two-phase procedures would suffice. In two-phase restricted procedures, contracting authorities should retain flexi-bility to define the process. - EU contract notices should include only the essential information neces-sary for tenderers to assess business opportunities. - The current MEAT criterion should be replaced with three distinct options: a. Lowest cost with minimum quality requirements b. Lowest cost combined with scored quality criteria c. Quality-only evaluation with a fixed price These options provide greater clarity and predictability for contracting au-thorities and suppliers, reduce confusion caused by the current MEAT inter-pretation, and allow more transparent alignment between cost and quality considerations. 2. Horizontal objectives as voluntary tools Public procurement legislation should remain a procedural framework, not a ve-hicle for mandatory policy goals. Horizontal objectivessuch as green and so-cial sustainability, innovation, safety, and preparednessshould stay voluntary tools. Local authorities must retain the flexibility to select objectives that best suit individual procurements, keeping the core purpose of procurement focused on public buying and satisfying local needs. The growing number of sector-specific regulations and directives makes it in-creasingly difficult for local authorities to manage compliance. These rules should be reviewed and streamlined to ensure simple, coherent mechanisms compatible with the procurement context. 3. European preference like IPI The policy goal of European preference should remain secondary to simplifying the procurement framework. Any preference for certain European industries should be implemented through a dedicated instrumentsimilar to the Inter-national Procurement Instrument (IPI)activated by the Commission. Such an instrument could define the sector, timeframe, and contract value reserved for companies of genuinely European origin. Importantly, the administrative burden of verifying company or product origin must not fall on contracting authorities. It should also be recognized that prioritizing European markets will likely in-crease costs due to limited supply within the EU. In the current economic cli-mate, careful consideration is needed before imposing additional costs on local and regional authorities. 4. Local activities outside the scope Local and regional authorities should continue to have the ability to organize services in cooperation with each other. The in-house and horizontal coopera-tion exemptions must remain simple and clear to ensure practical applicability. Furthermore, purely local services with no cross-border interest should be ex-cluded from the scope of future procurement legislation. Services such as youth and elderly care, housing, sports and fitness facilities, school and daycare catering, and maintenance tasks like snow ploughing should not be subject to burdensome EU rules. AFCM is a member in the Council of European Municipalities and Regions (CEMR) and supports the views of CEMRs upcoming position paper of January 2025.

initiative: Standardisation Regulation – revision
feedback_text: Medical device law in the EU needs to better align with global regulations when it comes to exemptions from complex and burdensome regulatory approaches. US 510 (k) and China Clinical Evaluation exemptions for well established medical devices should be implemented in order to harmonize global standards. This will ensure early market access of innovative technologies and maintenance of supply for patient care in the EU. It will prevent products, manufacturers and jobs disappearing from the EU market.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Feedback Response to Ares(2026)69111 from ElaadNL Strengths and weaknesses of the EU open-source sector The EU opensource sector benefits from strong collaboration, alignment with EU values, and a need for digital sovereignty. However, the ecosystem faces notable issues: Chronic underinvestment in open-source systems, leading to maintenance and sustainability risks Limited awareness and availability of open-source alternatives for critical systems Incompatible tendering favoring proprietary solutions, reinforcing vendor lock-in These issues increase dependency on proprietary solutions, hinder the modernization of digital grid systems, and slow down transitions toward open, interoperable alternatives. Added value of open source Open-source reduces cost, lowers vendor lock-in, and strengthens digital sovereignty. Its open and community-driven nature improve security through faster vulnerability detection and remediation, while shared R&D accelerates innovation. EU level measures to strengthen technological sovereignty and cybersecurity The EU must adopt a systemic, end-to-end view of digital dependencies. Open-source software alone does not guarantee digital sovereignty if critical layers, such as hosting, cloud, compute, and operational control, remain under non-EU influence. Digital sovereignty should not imply building internally but ensuring that organizations retain effective governance, oversight, and decision-making power over the digital systems on which society depends. EU level support should prioritize the following: 1. Open source first procurement. Align procurement frameworks to prefer open source solutions, ensure consistent regulatory guidance, and direct investment toward sovereign EU open source based services. 2. Reduce structural dependencies. Ensure credible substitution paths to open-source alternatives for essential systems, especially in critical sectors. 3. A risk-based roadmap for adoption of open-source. Prioritize open-source adoption in critical systems first, where resilience and security gains are highest. Scale capability across the EU through skills development, training, and organizational structures. Priority technology areas The EU should prioritize foundational technology domains where dependencies create systemic risk: Open-source cloud and edge infrastructure Open-source AI and shared data infrastructures Critical sectoral technologies (e.g. open-source grid and energy system and eMobility software) Sectors where OSS boosts competitiveness and cyber resilience Energy: Interoperable, vendor independent open-source tools strengthen the resilience and sovereignty of critical energy infrastructure. Cloud & edge: Open platforms reduce dependence on non EU cloud and edge providers and create growth opportunities for European alternatives. AI: Open, trustworthy open source AI models support compliance with EU regulatory and ethical standards.

initiative: The EU Cybersecurity Act
feedback_text: At this time, we believe it is important not to make changes to the current text of the TUCA. We propose that ENISA's capacities and resources be strengthened, so that it can consolidate its current mandate and facilitate the effective implementation of existing legislation, contributing to the reduction of barriers to market entry and achieving a minimum level of maturity and adaptation before considering further expansions of the framework. In this sense, our recommendation is to strengthen the effectiveness of the current framework, optimize existing resources that allow a real implementation of the CFS. These proposals are articulated around five points that we consider essential to consolidate a more robust, inclusive European cybersecurity ecosystem adapted to the capabilities of the market. Implementation of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework (ECCF)EU5G remain in the draft phase, without a defined operational framework. Before assigning new competencies, it is essential to concentrate the available resources on the effective implementation of the ECCF. This will allow the market to progressively adapt and the framework demonstrate its operational maturity, thus providing a solid basis for companies to operate with technical and regulatory certainty, supported by proven processes and effective cooperation mechanisms. Only once this level of consolidation has been achieved will it be possible to evaluate the results and data obtained to improve the effectiveness of the current mechanisms. Increase ENISA's resources This includes strengthening its role in the coordination and delivery of key services, such as incident response and post-incident investigation, in order to provide stronger and more effective support to Member States. Only by structurally strengthening ENISA will it be possible to ensure a coherent and effective implementation of cybersecurity policies across the European Union. Avoid over-regulation European cybersecurity certification should be maintained, to a large extent, as a voluntary mechanism, avoiding the imposition of disproportionate barriers to market entry or innovation. This certification should be based on objective, measurable and risk-focused technical criteria, thus ensuring its neutrality and effectiveness, and avoiding the creation of unnecessary regulatory burdens that could hinder the development of the European digital ecosystem. Provide implementation assistance and capacity building It is especially important for actors with limited resources, such as SMEs, which often face greater difficulties in adapting to regulatory requirements. Through training initiatives, clear guidance, accessible standards and support platforms, ENISA can make a significant contribution to reducing compliance costs and fostering wider and more equitable participation in the European cybersecurity ecosystem. Encourage the participation of the industrial ecosystem in cybersecurity governance. Ensure that strategic and technical decisions are made with adequate representation of the private sector, ensuring that policies reflect market realities and foster public-private collaboration

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: I have only noted that the number of organic poultry producers has been decreasing over the last 5 years in order to maintain a sustainable and rational agriculture that provides a decent standard of living for farmers, while taking into account animal welfare and the welfare of livestock breeders. Working and feeding conditions have become so cumbersome that farmers prefer to stop rather than having to produce organic products that lack the animals. To that end, I support the simplification of the UAB rules on the obligation of 95 % of organic feed for all poultry. On the other hand, the definition of a withdrawal period of at least 48 hours (where there is no legal withdrawal period) for allopathic treatments undermines the financial health of farms (for example, which can no longer market eggs) but also the health of animals and consumers, since this may limit the use of treatments on farms. I therefore support the return to the 2008 rules. Finally, because the breeding link is a small link, it is impossible for operators to specify it to organic farming without compromising the competitiveness of businesses and the biodiversity of the genetics offered in organic farming. It therefore seems important to me to remove the deadlines for organic poultry breeding animals.

Working and feeding conditions have become so cumbersome that farmers prefer to stop rather than having to produce organic products that lack the animals. To that end, I support the simplification of the UAB rules on the obligation of 95 % of organic feed for all poultry. On the other hand, the definition of a withdrawal period of at least 48 hours (where there is no legal withdrawal period) for allopathic treatments undermines the financial health of farms (for example, which can no longer market eggs) but also the health of animals and consumers, since this may limit the use of treatments on farms. I therefore support the return to the 2008 rules. Finally, because the breeding link is a small link, it is impossible for operators to specify it to organic farming without compromising the competitiveness of businesses and the biodiversity of the genetics offered in organic farming. It therefore seems important to me to remove the deadlines for organic poultry breeding animals.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: With the EU Organic Regulation, we in Europe have a set of rules that, on the one hand, protect the interests of consumers by defining organic farming standards. On the other hand, the EU Organic Regulation also lays down rules and requirements which provide a basis for farms to organise and develop their holdings. Business development requires long-term and reliable planning certainty. With the process of interpreting the rules on grazing, first in Austria and subsequently in Germany, the Commission has fundamentally changed the framework conditions for countless dairy farms in Germany. Farms that are one of the pioneers of the organic sector and which have been ecologically managed for decades have been imposed overnight on insolvent requirements. With a lot of commitment, the farms have in the past achieved solutions that respect animal welfare by means of individual solutions with open-air areas and green feed. In particular, the current interpretation denies farms which are restricted due to structural requirements (indoor areas, roads, etc.) the possibility of organic farming. This is particularly critical given that various legal opinions have shown that the Commission’s current interpretation is not supported by the legislative text. The current process does not take account of the fact that organic farms have always worked on the further development of housing systems over the years in order to improve animal husbandry, increase profitability and promote climate protection. On the contrary, many dairy farms are forced to reschedule as a result of a change of interpretation. The Commission is consciously accepting the destruction of long-standing livelihoods. In order to be able to further develop organic farming and in particular organic livestock farming, holdings did not need backward-looking designs that would prevent innovation. The negative effects of such a restrictive approach are currently also evident in the market. Although the demand for organic products has recently increased significantly, no farms are willing to switch to organic farming. Without flexibility within the organic farming system, we will not be able to achieve any significant growth in the organic sector in future.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: There are several aspects to consider here: * First, to me it is obvious that any public service should use open source software to provide the aforesaid service. A common service shouldn't be dependent on a single private entity, within EU or not. * When private actors are unavoidable, interoperability should always be enforced. EU services and EU citizens should always be in control of the data, and be able to switch provider if needed. * It is important to understand that open source software does have a cost and a lifecycle. Developers should be paid, contributions/modifications should be proposed to the main project. Without this, even a wildly used opensource software can become unmaintained and die. * If the data is stored outside of EU, using opensource won't solve anything: we still won't be in control of our data. Facebook and Microsoft use tons of opensource softwares, nevertheless they own the data, and that data is under US legislation rule, not EU. Opensource and interoperability are the way to go (and should have been since day one), but it also must be done properly.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: avenues for simplification: — grouping the various annexes to the European regulation into one as before. — offer unified tools for operators to register their activities: production, cultivation and rearing specifications – for producers: link CAP declarations directly to the piecemeal tools of each country. In France, for example, at the time of the CAP declaration, Cartobio has been directly updated. The same applies to the removal or conversion of new parcels when the CAP is updated. — make it compulsory to send the supplier operator’s up-to-date certificate to the customer operator at the time of each transaction. — for livestock farmers, abolish animal conversion for large animals (horses, cattle), which is a burdensome standard for both breeders and auditors, and define a duration as for conversions of small animals (pigs, sheep, goats, etc.). — certification of companies manufacturing UAB products to avoid continuous monitoring of product approvals and changes in UAB substances and not UAB substances. — not allow remote audit for the documentary part of the audit as this adds a layer of complexity for the operator and for the CB (auditor) for the interpretation of the data. — not to change the standards of animal buildings too frequently. Once every 20 years if revised and leave 10 years for standard-setting. — not to impose a balancing and traceability exercise drawn up by the auditor, which is just annotated to the report and requested more specifically during the internal audits of the CBs. — require customers who have access to it to the updated ledger on the day of audits. — reduce permitted cases of mixity (producers) in order to facilitate the work of certifying bodies and have consistent production systems at farm level. Thank you for taking these elements into account.

feedback_text: I am posting this as a private EU citizen, but I own an IT shop since many years here in Italy. We, as in the EU population, NEED to get rid of anything that isn't open source or directly tied to us europeans. We need social and financial independence from the other powers of the world that are closing in, we need to stand as the last place where freedom truly exist, and all of this cannot happen as long as we are dependant on USA's, China's etc and their technology. I work with PC and fix them everyday, for a living; I can tell you that the average person will not see any difference between Windows and Linux Mint, between Office and Open Office. The transitional period could last a month at most, since as of now Linux and Open Source alternatives are pretty much the same as paid for private apps. This is what I would expect if no EU state (or an EU private company) steps in and decides to create or modify an existing linux OS or an existing open source office alternative. Please, for our future, let the EU be as indipendent as possible, only this way we can fight the rise of extremists in the other parts of the world that keep threatening our ways of life.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Opensource founding, support and help is widely underdone and underestimated at EU level. 1. The largest opensource projects are created, maintained by USA citizens. There are a lot of root causes for this, and to pick one, the connections between research, business, public and private foundations are much more efficient than in EU. EU grants are hard and complex to obtain ; 2. Opensources projects are hosted mainly in USA : because of Github. That means a lack of EU sovereinty on a core value of IT : its source code. See for example how Microsoft (that acquired GH in 2018) has created the AI agent Copilot thanks to the hosted GH code. Codeberg.org (de), or framagit (fr) are maintained mainly by benevolants ; 3. EU citizen initiatives (even the more ethical ones) are not supported by EU : see how dns0.eu (a curated and EU based DNS service) has closed few months ago ; 4. There is no EU strategy to build a democratic, transparent, secure IT system. We host an email service. We have 10th of attacks every day. We are buying lists of malicious IP's to abuseIPDB (US based) to proactively blocks those IP's. We had to build the protection system by our own. It's like there was no rule of law for vehicules and we had to buy some vehicules licence plate lists to protect ourselves in the public space. Cyberspace is a "farwest". The consequences are multiple. The cost is huge for the EU companies that are under multiple threats of bad actors on the net (rogue states, opponent states or criminal organizations). We see everyday public services, or private companies that are hacked. For example, it wouldn't be expensive (because not technically hard to implement) to have a public based malicious IP service provided by EU. This would have three benefits : protect EU companies, maybe more than EU if it is well done, that means building a digital sovereinty, and having a Cyber-Security observatory. The issue with abuseIPDB (apart from being in USA) is that there are no (known) followings (classify, geo-political reflexion or diplomatic pressure on bad actors to make the root cause of the threats disapear). The digital power is US based. If Trump cuts the access to AWS, all our EU financial services are down. If Trump cuts the access to Microsoft, French health services are down (and probably a lot more). EU is economically down in few hours. If we buy F35 planes and Trump cuts access to backend plateforms they are down. The defense is built by the US techno autoritarian stack : Palantir, Anduril... We are buying these technologies to USA. That means we are loosing money and power in favor of USA. See https://www.authoritarian-stack.info/ And when IT people are audited by EU parliament or commission, and exposes those threats (sovereinety, business, ethic, sustainability), EU politics are anwsering AI and global surveillance. That means exactly the dystopic world that USA is building now. Everyday we hear about so-called "delay of EU tech compared to China and USA". CSRD and double materiality was a great progress, even China took inspiration from it. It was a EU soft power. There was the green deal that was a good start. It is dismantled. And yes we lack a digital global strategy to use IT as a tool, not goal. There should be another way of building a desirable future for our children. I.e. do the contrary of what is done oversea : create a sustainable, democratic, respectful, transparent world. EU has the power and size to build it. Opensource software has all the required properties for sovereinty, auditability to provide a strong and independent digital infrastructure for EU.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: HOP - Stop Planned Obsolescence is a French environment protection association. Since 2015, it advocates for long-lasting products. It is directly linked to the construction of circular economy tools such as the repairability and durability indexes as well as the French repair bonus. It represents a community of more than 60.000 people. HOP is also part of the Right to Repair campaign's steering group. HOP started in 2025 the "No Windows Tax" campaign which mobilized 22 organizations to denounce the end of free Windows 10 updates and raise awareness about software obsolescence. The end of Windows 10 free security updates Microsoft announced that the end of free security updates for Windows 10 will take place on October 14, 2025. This decision pushed users to upgrade to Windows 11. Due to the multinationals hardware requirements, 400 million computers worldwide were incompatible with Windows 11 as of summer 2025, some being only five years old. The estimated consequences were particularly alarming: Economic impacts: over 10.4 billion in additional costs for users to pay for extended updates over one year, not including the costs of replacing computers. Environmental impacts: the emission of over 70 million tons of CO², the extraction of nearly 32,000 Eiffel Towers worth of raw materials, and the production of at least 800,000 tons of electronic waste. This case also highlighted our collective dependence on non-European digital multinationals. In response to these challenges, the coalitions petition won strong public support, gathering 50,000 signatures in just one month. The campaign demonstrated a convergence of interests among public, private, and civil society actors in seriously considering the transition to free and open-source software. The citizen mobilization secured a concession from Microsoft: eased access to update extensions until October 2026 in Europe. However, this is an underwhelming victorythis extension is only a reprieve, and in one year, users will face the same problem again. Furthermore, Microsoft requires users to log in with an account and does not allow automatic activation. The company also communicated for nearly nine months about the end of updates, including messages encouraging the replacement of incompatible computers, and many people had already done so. Software obsolescence: a recurring problem to restrain Software obsolescence refers to all software techniques that push for the premature disposal of equipment. In the case of Windows 10, this encouragement to replace devices is due to the end of security updates, the lack of user information about alternatives to computer replacement, and the significant hardware requirements for upgrading to Windows 11. Many other forms of software obsolescence exist, such as mandatory updates that can cause malfunctions or overburden device memory, or part-pairing that digitally ties a part to the device (preventing its replacement or refurbishment), among others. Switching to free and open-source software: a viable alternative to promote Transitioning to free and open-source software like Linux allows users to: Free themselves from the unilateral decisions of Microsoft and other non-European software publishers, Significantly extend the lifespan of their devices, Do so securely and without license purchase costs. However, installation requires technical skills that are not yet widely accessible, and may involve adapting certain uses. This challenge has already been identified in the recommendations of French public authorities. GNU/Linux already equips the vast majority of the Gendarmerie nationale's devices. As already highlighted in a 2012 circular from the French Prime Minister, free and open-source software is driven by actual needs, minimizing unnecessary changes. It is high time these recommendations were put into practice. These points were echoed in a collective opinion column calling on French public administrations and individuals to se

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: This feedback is submitted on behalf of the sugarcane producing companies in response to the Commissions public consultation on the proposed revision of the typical greenhouse gas emission values for sugarcane ethanol as set out in Annex V of the Directive. On the basis of the information and evidence provided in the attached document, the following recommendations are submitted for the Commissions consideration: That the typical emission value for sugarcane ethanol in Annex V, with respect to the cultivation stage, be adjusted to 14.69 g COeq/MJ; That emissions from transport and distribution be disaggregated into feedstock transport, inland transport, and intercontinental transport, in order to support a region agnostic methodological approach; That the typical emission value for sugarcane ethanol in Annex V, with respect to the transport and distribution stage, be adjusted to 4.99 g COeq/MJ, comprising 1.62 g COeq/MJ for feedstock transport, 1.17 g COeq/MJ for inland transport, and 2.20 g COeq/MJ for intercontinental transport (with BrazilRotterdam used as the reference route); That the Commission provide a detailed description of the methodological approach and data sources used to derive the emission estimates proposed in the revised Annexes to the Directive; That Table D. Disaggregated Standard Values for Biofuels and Bioliquids be corrected with respect to the sugarcane ethanol entry, as it appears to contain a formatting inconsistency.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: When software runs hospitals, energy grids, public services and the devices we rely on every day, it should be treated like any other critical infrastructure. Open-source software is a strong foundation because it allows independent inspection, shared improvemen and real choice - but openness alone is not enough. We also need clear, enforceable standards that ensure security, accountability and fair access, so that no single company can quietly control systems we all depend on. Done right, open systems combined with strong standards reduce hidden risks, prevent lock-in and help keep Europes digital infrastructure trustworthy, resilient and under democratic control.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Proxmox VE: European Excellence in Virtualization When discussing technological sovereignty and open-source innovation in Europe, Proxmox Virtual Environment deserves special recognition. This Austrian-developed virtualization platform perfectly embodies what the EU's digital strategy aims to achieve: independent, transparent, and high-quality technological solutions. At SOKOM SRL, we've been relying on Proxmox since 2008, and this long-term relationship speaks volumes about the platform's reliability and evolution. Over these years, we've witnessed Proxmox grow from a promising solution into a mature, enterprise-grade virtualization platform that consistently meets our evolving needs. What makes Proxmox truly special is its European DNA. Developed in Vienna by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, it represents a success story of how European companies can compete with global tech giants while maintaining values of transparency and user respect. In today's geopolitical landscape, having a trusted European alternative matters more than ever. The open-source nature of Proxmox is its greatest strength. Built on Debian Linux, KVM, and LXC, the platform offers complete code transparencyno hidden surprises, no vendor lock-in, just pure freedom to adapt and modify according to your needs. This transparency is crucial for security audits and regulatory compliance, particularly important for European organizations. From a practical standpoint, Proxmox is remarkably versatile. It handles both full virtualization and lightweight containers effortlessly, making it suitable for virtually any workload. The web-based interface is intuitive enough for quick deployment yet powerful enough for complex enterprise scenarios. Features like high-availability clustering, integrated backup systems, and live migration work seamlessly out of the box. Security-wise, Proxmox takes no shortcuts. Regular updates, robust access controls, and two-factor authentication ensure your infrastructure remains protected. Our experience since 2008 confirms this platform's stability and security credentials. Proxmox proves that European open-source projects can deliver world-class solutions, supporting the EU's vision of technological independence and digital sovereignty.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: I am of the opinion that, whatever problems the EU has, that business does not have sufficient access to European lawmakers is not one of them. As a simple citizen, I also resent business interests trying to weaken environmental and social regulation in a macroeconomic phase of record profit and high inequalities. I urge the European Commission to 1. NOT WEAKEN the checks and balances in the European lawmaking process in the name of "simplification". 2. REINFORCE protection of the European democratic forces from capture by vested interests. 3. DOUBLE DOWN on environmental, social and human rights impact assessment of regulation. 4. NOT EXEMPT deregulation from such impact assessment.
