feedback_text: As an EU resident (though not an EU citizen), it's important to me that the EU be working to promote independence from US-based corporations -- specifically technology corporations -- in its procurement processes. Recent global events have shown that it's not unthinkable that the US would use its power to curtail the availability of critical digital services like email and cloud storage. Just having critical digital services hosted in data centres located in the EU isn't enough. If the data centres are run by US corporations, they can still act as a choke point. These centralized services may be financially cheaper than diverse local options, but only if we ignore the externalities. The geopolitical risk of yielding control of EU data to US tech giants is enormous, and more plainly visible than ever before. However, just favouring EU-based corporations for procurement decisions isn't enough. No matter where they're based, for-profit corporations are vulnerable to economic pressures that often run contrary to the public interest. The EU should be stimulating public services and non-profit entities that develop and offer open-source software, with data portability, interoperability, and accessibility as key requirements. In financial terms, these services might seem more expensive than cloud-based hyperscale solutions -- but they might not! Even so, it would be wise to invest in a robust and resilient ecosystem of digital services instead of a uniform -- but vulnerable -- monoculture. Critical digital services, and the personal data of EU citizens must not be able to be held hostage by geopolitical or economic actors.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: — We have spent on a food of 100 % AB in laying hens, which poses real problems, is it possible to reverse this constraint? In addition, the same trend is planned for the supply of pullets at the end of 2026. This constraint will have an even greater impact on the feeding of young animals and will result in the risk of digestive disorder and thus deterioration of animal welfare. — The need to maintain the 350 m derogation between the traps and the bottom of the route without the addition of additional shelters – We are experiencing problems in laying the hens as a result of natural light on chicken rearing, and therefore an inability to stimulate animals for batches starting in the summer or early autumn. These changes in pullet regulation have the effect of delaying the laying start by sometimes several weeks and thus penalising the farmer’s income and increasing the cost price of BIO egg. — Review the withdrawal period of 48 hours following the administration of a chemically synthesised allopathic veterinary medicinal product despite a zero withdrawal period on those products.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Qu'attends l'Europe pour privilégier l'open source !??! Principales motivations stratégiques en faveur de l'utilisation des logiciels libres en Europe : -Réduire la dépendance aux plateformes non-EU (Microsoft, GAFA, OpenAI,...) et privilégier le contrôle national des technologies critiques telles que les systèmes dexploitation. Cet objectif est non seulement ECONOMIQUE, mais aussi essentiel à notre SECURITE nationale, au CONTROLE de nos infrastructures critiques et à notre SOUVERAINETE technologique. -Gagner en FLEXIBILITE, REDUIRE LES COÛTS et limiter l'exposition aux contraintes externes. -Créer un cercle vertueux en stimulant l'INNOVATION rapide et de développement des compétences. S'appuyer sur de vastes communautés de développeurs en Europe (notamment dans les secteurs technologiques porteurs), renforcer leur communication et encourager une collaboration transparente, permettrait à l'Europe de se positionner pour accélérer les progrès dans des domaines de pointe comme l'IA, le blockchain, l'informatique quantique et la conception de semi-conducteurs (domaines essentiels à la compétitivité et à la résilience à long terme). Qu'attend l'Europe pour agir ?? (D'autres grandes puissances comme la Chine ont déjà compris depuis longtemps les avantages de l'open source !) PS : je ne comprends pas pourquoi je suis obligée d'acheter une licence Windows quand j'achète un nouveau PC (sous peine de me voir refuser l'achat). Je pensais que les ventes liées et forcées étaient interdites en Europe.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Borders, responsibility and innovation in open digital ecosystems of the European Union.............. The European Union’s Open Digital Ecosystems Strategy aims to sustainably strengthen technological sovereignty, security, innovation capacity and competitiveness. The numerous responses to the current consultation show a high degree of practicality and innovation potential. At the same time, it is clear that many challenges are not primarily technical but are based on structural tensions. These tensions can be described as a conflict between two fundamental rationales, exemplified by the philosophical positions of Albert Camus and Jean-Paul Sartre. The Camus perspective represents real boundaries, prudence, protection, stability and responsibility through limitation. It is consistent with the logic of regulation, standards, safety, compliance and liability. The Sartre perspective stands for freedom, room for manoeuvre, project logic, innovation, experimentation and responsibility by decision. It follows the logic of open source, agile development, prototyping and community-driven innovation. Both perspectives are present in European digital policy at the same time, but are rarely explicitly distinguished. This leads to systemic conflicts: Security versus innovation, standardisation versus flexibility, liability versus openness and process stability versus project logic. These conflicts are not accidental, but reflect different worldviews in dealing with risk, responsibility and technical design. The risk for the EU is either to resolve these tensions unilaterally or implicitly. Over-emphasis on regulatory control can hamper innovation, while over-emphasis on openness can create security risks and loss of trust. Both would undermine the EU’s strategic objectives. Against this background, this position paper recommends a structural two-level architecture for open digital ecosystems. At the first level, a non-negotiable core should be established, including safety critical functions, data protection, liability, compliance with standards and stable core infrastructures. This level follows the Camus logic and protects people, systems and public goods. At the second level, an explicit innovation space should be created, enabling open source modules, plugins, community projects, simulations, testbeds, and research and prototyping. This level follows the Sartre logic and specifically creates space for technological design. The central architecture principle is: a stable, regulated core with a free, experimental edge. It does not play security and innovation against each other, but structurally decouples and strategically links them. Operationally, in the future, the EU should screen all open-source initiatives through two systematic filters: a Camus check (safety, risks, sustainability, liability) and a Sartre check (innovation potential, flexibility, technological sovereignty). This should be complemented by pilot projects, clear governance structures and transparent responsibilities. The key strategic finding is: Open Source is not just a technological issue, but a cultural and epistemic one. The key governance issue is not which software is used, but what kind of responsibility is organised. Innovation needs freedom. Security needs borders. Sustainability needs both at the same time. Camus protects the system. Sartre develops the system. Europe needs both structurally separated, strategically united. .................. Created using ChatGPT

feedback_text: FOSS is important for the HOW. But Open Standards are more important for the WHAT. Many standards today are quasi-standards based on US business software such as MS Office, Adobe PDF etc. There is a huge need and opportunity to define new EU open standards for Information Objects. They can be exchanged between citizens, governments and businesses. Freeing us from US dependence. And putting Digital information in the hands of people. Where its all compatible and secure, not locked in US silos or file formats. Contact me for my research work.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: How can the Commission better reconcile evidence-based policymaking with the need for urgent action? The Commission should rigorously implement the European Ombudsmans Recommendation of 25 November 2025 by ensuring predictable, consistent, and non-arbitrary use of Better Regulation rules. This requires clearly defining urgency, publicly documenting and justifying any exceptions to standard procedures, and explaining these decisions transparently in legislative proposals. When derogations are granted, safeguards must ensure that fast-track processes remain transparent, evidence-based, and inclusive, in line with EU law. Impact assessments should systematically consider the do nothing option, recognizing the often substantial social, environmental, and economic costs of inaction. They should also explicitly protect civic space, ensuring that EU policies strengthen rather than weaken civil society. In a geopolitical context marked by disinformation and foreign interference, policymaking must rely on independent scientific expertise and genuine democratic participation, in line with the European Democracy Shield. Adequate resources are needed to assess impacts on climate, biodiversity, health, and well-being. Transparency and access to information The Commission should publish impact assessments and opinions of the Regulatory Scrutiny Board as soon as they are available. Early transparency enables informed public debate and meaningful participation, as confirmed by the Court of Justice of the EU. Climate consistency Under the European Climate Law, all measures must be assessed for consistency with the 2030 and 2050 targets and made public. The Commission does not always provide clear evidence of compliance. Clear guidance on Article 6.4 is urgently needed to ensure that all proposals include accessible climate consistency assessments. How can holistic, effective, and inclusive consultations be ensured? Any revision of the Guidelines must strengthen, not weaken, public participation, in line with EU values, treaties, and international law. Participation is a democratic and legal obligation, not a formality. Claims of stakeholder fatigue are not evidence-based and must not justify limiting participation. Selective consultations should not replace open processes such as Have Your Say. In environmental and climate policies, young people and future generations must be recognized as key stakeholders. Restricted consultations risk corporate capture and exclude vulnerable groups, trade unions, and civil society. Communities most affected by climate change and environmental degradation require enhanced representation. This requires prioritizing social organisations, promoting accessible local-language engagement, giving greater weight to social and territorial impacts, and transparently disclosing participants and selection criteria. Sustainability, equity, and transparency in law-making Policymaking should systematically integrate the precautionary principle, the Sustainable Development Goals, and the do no harm principle. Impact assessments still prioritize short-term business costs over long-term social and environmental benefits, and this bias must be corrected. Economic reassurance cannot override health, environmental protection, and fundamental rights. Current frameworks insufficiently assess cumulative impacts on health, ecosystems, and natural resources. Intergenerational equity must be mainstreamed to ensure responsible and sustainable legislation. Finally, stronger transparency and accountability are essential for public trust. Citizens have the right to understand how decisions are made and how representatives act. Persistent opacity, notably in the authorization of hazardous chemicals, undermines democratic oversight. Broad, accessible access to environmental data, governance processes, and information on private interests influence is therefore necessary.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Open-Source is a great benefit for the world and I applaud the EU looking at the Open-Source community as a solution for digital sovereignty. However, I invite the commissioners to visit FOSDEM in Brussels to see and understand what the open-source community looks like. Open-source tools have low adoption because while often made by brilliant technical people, they rarely pay attention to the end-user experience. Creating useable and intuitive products is very hard and requires constant feedback and iterations on shallow interfaces. Something most highly technical people hate. As somebody who has done tech startups and managed engineers I can tell you that the biggest hurdle open-source has is that open-source is created mostly for other highly technical people. As such, open-source is deeply successful as a half-fabricate. A solution used by other technical people to create an end-solution. It is successful as a platform, saving engineering time but rarely as a standalone solution. Tech startups are much more successful in creating digital sovereignty, it suffices to solve the capital market (more equity investments) and to ensure high demand (protectionism/european preference). I would not look at the open-source community as a solution to digital sovereignty. Mandating public institutions to have european preference would enable a suite of startups delivering this for them. Once that is done it can be extended to sensitive companies and ultimately expanded to the entire economy. Protectionism has downsides but actually those can be circumvented by funding 100s of competitors which ensures high internal competition but also very cutthroat competition that would enable those companies to survive once protectionism is lifted. This answers 1-3 of your questions. There are areas where Open-Source could yield tremendous value if you consider that it is very successful in platforms (linux, android) The US and Chinese car companies have managed to transition to software-based cars while European ones are still stuck integrating different vendor components which are often written in different languages. An Open Car initiative could create the Android of cars, a global platform to speed up innovation. Similar for boats, planes, trucks and trains. Converting the software-stack of those large systems to open-source ecosystems could lead to wild, unexpected innovation. (E.g. applying the technology of self-driving cars to trains is perfectly possible) EU action on this level can unlock innovation by allowing very small teams to create improvements independent of the major car manufacturers.

feedback_text: This Act needs to be entirely revised or outright abandoned. The EU has very strong digital privacy rules, which is exactly how it should be, and it seems to be what is being undermined here. The proposition of what is essentially mass surveillance within this Act is contrary to the values many EU citizens concerned with their digital privacy hold, and we are very grateful for the protections ensured by the EU in that regard.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: medienhaus/ is a digital infrastructure collective at the Berlin University of the Arts (UdK Berlin) that develops and operates selfhosted, free and opensource services for teaching, collaboration and public engagement. Our project tried to understand prototypical systems as institutional digital commons rather than outsourced products, aligning with EU goals on technological sovereignty, cybersecurity and open digital ecosystems. 1. **Strengths, weaknesses, barriers** The EU opensource sector benefits from highly skilled communities, strong alignment with European rights and values, and decades of public investment in education and research that already rely on open technologies. Yet several structural weaknesses and barriers limit impact: - Underfunding of longterm maintenance and operations compared to shortterm innovation projects, which makes it difficult to sustainably run opensource infrastructures in public institutions. - Legal and administrative uncertainty in procurement, liability and compliance, which discourages universities and public bodies from adopting and contributing to open source at scale. - Strong lockin effects from large proprietary cloud and platform providers, including free offers to the education sector, which make open alternatives appear risky despite their strategic advantages. From our institutional practice, a key barrier is the lack of recognized hybrid roles (developer-researcher-administrator) and governance frameworks that embed open-source stewardship into the core mission of universities and public administrations. 2. **Added value and examples** Open source brings decisive value for both public and private sectors by increasing control over infrastructure, reducing dependency on nonEU vendors and enabling experimentation with alternative sociotechnical arrangements. At UdK Berlin, medienhaus/ operates opensourcebased services such as collaboration spaces and digital exhibition infrastructures that support everyday teaching and public events, while keeping data, identities and workflows under institutional control. The most important factors are: - Reduced longterm lockin and better risk management, as institutions can migrate, adapt and audit systems. - Improved security and transparency through open code, open standards and local expertise. - Greater flexibility and innovation capacity, since infrastructures can be tailored to specific pedagogical, artistic and organisational needs. - Alignment with the idea of digital commons, where infrastructures are cocreated and governed in the public interest by institutions, communities and civil society. 3. **Measures and actions at EU level** To fully realise this potential, the EU should: - Support longterm operation and maintenance of opensource infrastructures in public institutions (universities, schools, cultural institutions, administrations) through dedicated, multiyear funding and recognition of infrastructure work as a public task, not only research. - Adapt procurement and compliance frameworks so that public bodies can adopt opensource solutions and contribute back (e.g. model clauses, guidance, opensource first where secure solutions exist). - Develop shared European hosting, security and governance support for critical opensource projects, building on digital commons initiatives and enabling universities and research institutions to act as operational and governance nodes. - Fund pilots in which opensource, selfhosted alternatives replace or complement dominant proprietary platforms in education and culture, including evaluation and documentation so that patterns can be reused in other sectors. - Strengthen intermediary structures (foundations, cooperatives) that ensure sustainable governance and business models for opensource components crucial to EU sovereignty (identity, cloud, AI, middleware). More can be found at https://medienhaus.dev/ and the websites linked there.
text_raw: medienhaus/ is a digital infrastructure collective at the Berlin University of the Arts (UdK Berlin) that develops and operates selfhosted, free and opensource services for teaching, collaboration and public engagement. Our project tried to understand prototypical systems as institutional digital commons rather than outsourced products, aligning with EU goals on technological sovereignty, cybersecurity and open digital ecosystems. 1. **Strengths, weaknesses, barriers** The EU opensource sector benefits from highly skilled communities, strong alignment with European rights and values, and decades of public investment in education and research that already rely on open technologies. Yet several structural weaknesses and barriers limit impact: - Underfunding of longterm maintenance and operations compared to shortterm innovation projects, which makes it difficult to sustainably run opensource infrastructures in public institutions. - Legal and administrative uncertainty in procurement, liability and compliance, which discourages universities and public bodies from adopting and contributing to open source at scale. - Strong lockin effects from large proprietary cloud and platform providers, including free offers to the education sector, which make open alternatives appear risky despite their strategic advantages. From our institutional practice, a key barrier is the lack of recognized hybrid roles (developer-researcher-administrator) and governance frameworks that embed open-source stewardship into the core mission of universities and public administrations. 2. **Added value and examples** Open source brings decisive value for both public and private sectors by increasing control over infrastructure, reducing dependency on nonEU vendors and enabling experimentation with alternative sociotechnical arrangements. At UdK Berlin, medienhaus/ operates opensourcebased services such as collaboration spaces and digital exhibition infrastructures that support everyday teaching and public events, while keeping data, identities and workflows under institutional control. The most important factors are: - Reduced longterm lockin and better risk management, as institutions can migrate, adapt and audit systems. - Improved security and transparency through open code, open standards and local expertise. - Greater flexibility and innovation capacity, since infrastructures can be tailored to specific pedagogical, artistic and organisational needs. - Alignment with the idea of digital commons, where infrastructures are cocreated and governed in the public interest by institutions, communities and civil society. 3. **Measures and actions at EU level** To fully realise this potential, the EU should: - Support longterm operation and maintenance of opensource infrastructures in public institutions (universities, schools, cultural institutions, administrations) through dedicated, multiyear funding and recognition of infrastructure work as a public task, not only research. - Adapt procurement and compliance frameworks so that public bodies can adopt opensource solutions and contribute back (e.g. model clauses, guidance, opensource first where secure solutions exist). - Develop shared European hosting, security and governance support for critical opensource projects, building on digital commons initiatives and enabling universities and research institutions to act as operational and governance nodes. - Fund pilots in which opensource, selfhosted alternatives replace or complement dominant proprietary platforms in education and culture, including evaluation and documentation so that patterns can be reused in other sectors. - Strengthen intermediary structures (foundations, cooperatives) that ensure sustainable governance and business models for opensource components crucial to EU sovereignty (identity, cloud, AI, middleware). More can be found at https://medienhaus.dev/ and the websites linked there.

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: In this contribution, the University Alliance Stockholm Trio comprising Karolinska Institutet, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, and Stockholm University share reflections and recommendations to support the continued development of the European Research Area (ERA). We fully support the ambition to strengthen the ERA and realise the Fifth Freedom: enabling the free circulation of researchers, knowledge, and innovation across borders. While legislation can have a role, we believe that prescriptive legal measures are not the most effective or appropriate path forward for developing the ERA. We stress the importance of maintaining a careful balance between EU-level coordination and national autonomy, and of safeguarding academic freedom, research quality, and long-term perspectives. Lasting progress in the ERA will not come through prescriptive regulation, but through co-creation, mutual trust, and a commitment to enabling diverse and excellent research systems to thrive across Europe. While coordinated action at the EU level has value, we are concerned that the proposed ERA Act remains vague and may open the door to broader regulatory ambitions. The EUs legal competence in research is limited to supporting, coordinating, and complementing national efforts not replacing or steering them. Any steps toward legislation must therefore be taken with great caution and full respect for the diversity of national systems. We underline that quality assurance and research direction must remain grounded in the academic community and scientific judgement not in administrative or market logics. There is a particular risk that political priorities, however legitimate, may crowd out bottom-up, curiosity-driven research. For this reason, EU action should be clearly complementary, reinforcing national strengths rather than imposing uniform models. We also propose establishing an independent European-level mechanism, such as an ombudsperson, to help safeguard academic freedom in practice. For the full context and detailed response, please see the attached document.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Universities can play a key role in promoting the adoption of F/OSS technologies at European level (and in particular in the public sector). By adopting F/OSS solutions in the course of study, they can help to generate ‘basic awareness’ on the part of students (long-term outcomes) and support the market by stimulating the development and support of such solutions (short-term outcomes). In departments and study paths that are more geared towards IT, ‘adoption’ walks could be launched for at least one/two high-profile open-source projects, contributing to the development of such projects by involving, in a coordinated manner, test-makers, doctoral students and specialists who would become an integral part of the development teams for the projects selected. This would be win-win, with the (European) academic institution being recognised as a true open-source contributor, and the open-source world benefiting from the new energies brought. At state level, moreover, universities could be encouraged to use F/OSS solutions (already provided for in various regulatory frameworks) by means of rewarding those projects (both research and ‘operational’ management) based on F/OSS technologies. The European institutions could support actions aimed at building alternative social-networks, starting with the academic world and its students. A case in point is SURF (https://www.surf.nl/en/mastodon-pilot-for-research-and-education), whose ‘Mastodon’ experiment was more successful than expected. This success could be trivially replicated elsewhere, with exponential repercussions on the number of users and the number of messages exchanged. A broad adoption of F/OSS technologies and platforms in academic contexts would generate a similar demand in lower school systems (upper secondary education and lower education). In this regard, the operational/technological dimension of a single university is such that it can ‘support’ a relatively high number of schools in its own area, to which the university could offer F/OSS solutions in SAAS mode, thereby continuing to expand the spread of such platforms. With a significant diffusion in the context of education (universities + schools), F/OSS solutions would finally enter the daily life of citizens who would start touching these solutions by hand. In parallel to these initiatives – necessarily spread over a particularly long period of time – specific projects could be funded to implement something new. Although seemingly banal, the development of a smartwatch- oriented hardware and software platform is a particularly important objective: the smartwatch is increasingly an object that acquires and processes personal data of particular importance. To date – with the sole exception of AsteroidOS, which is compatible with very few relatively outdated smartwatches – there does not seem to me to be any smartwatches unlocked from US/CINA cloud platforms. Once again: an OS and software ecosystem suitable for smartwatches could become a platform also suitable for the diverse world of smart-appliances and, in the long term, when the hardware problem is solved, including for smartphones. Huawei’s post-ban US experience is certainly a case to be inspired (Harmony OS). Across all actions, stimulating an IT sector focused on 100 % open-source infrastructure components (OS, hypervisor, microservices and their orchestrators, Software-Defined-Storage, Automation Platforms, Code Development, Communication, etc., etc.) would further foster the emergence and/or growth of companies and skills with a strong F/OSS vocation.

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: The Irish Deaf Society (IDS) welcomes the European Commissions initiative to enhance the Strategy for the Rights of Persons with Disabilities up to 2030 and strongly supports renewed, time-bound actions that fully implement the UNCRPD. As the national representative organisation of Deaf people in Ireland, IDS emphasises that Deaf people are rights-holders, members of a linguistic and cultural minority whose rights are also secured through disability legislation and instruments such as the UNCRPD. While progress has been made under the current Strategy, Deaf people across the EU continue to face systemic barriers to full participation, equality and autonomy. These barriers are particularly acute in relation to accessibility, access to information and communication, education, employment, healthcare, political participation and justice. The enhanced Strategy must therefore explicitly address the specific situation of Deaf people and other sign language users, in line with Articles 9, 21, 24, 27 and 29 of the UNCRPD. IDS echoes the long-standing calls of the European Union of the Deaf (EUD) and the World Federation of the Deaf (WFD) for the EU to adopt a human-rights-based and language-rights-based approach to disability policy. National sign languages must be fully recognised, promoted and protected across all EU policies and funding programmes. Accessibility obligations should go beyond minimum standards and ensure the systematic provision of professional sign language interpreting, captioning, that information is provided in sign language, including in digital services, public administration, education, healthcare, emergency communications and EU-funded programmes. The enhanced Strategy should place stronger emphasis on meaningful engagement and co-creation with Disabled Persons Organisations (DPOs) in the design, implementation and monitoring of policies. IDS also stresses the importance of intersectionality. Deaf women, Deaf BIPOC, Deaf migrants, Deaf Travellers and Roma, Deaf+ (Deaf people with additional disabilities) and older Deaf people and Deaf children can experience multiple and compounded forms of discrimination. The enhanced Strategy should connect with other equality strategies and show clear results for people who face more than one type of discrimination. In line with EUD and WFD positions, IDS calls for stronger EU action to support inclusive education with sign languages as languages of instruction and for targeted measures to close the employment gap for Deaf people. Artificial intelligence and digitalisation must be inclusive by design and must not reinforce existing communication barriers or discriminatory practices. Finally, IDS underlines the need for robust monitoring, enforcement and funding. EU legislation on accessibility and free movement must be effectively implemented and enforced, with clear accountability mechanisms. EU funds should be conditional on compliance with accessibility and UNCRPD standards, including sign language accessibility and independent living. The enhanced Strategy represents a crucial opportunity to close implementation gaps and to ensure that Deaf people across the EU can fully enjoy their rights, dignity, autonomy and equal participation in society by 2030.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: IFOAM Organics Europe is the European umbrella organisation for organic food and farming. With almost 200 members in 34 European countries, our work spans the entire organic food chain and beyond: from farmers and processors organisations, retailers, certifiers, consultants, traders, and researchers to environmental and consumer advocacy bodies. IFOAM Organics Europe supports a targeted revisionstrictly limited to essential corrections that strengthen the regulations coherence, avoid deregulation, and support a thriving, credible organic sector. IFOAM Organics Europe stresses that the ongoing review should not become a pretext for deregulation. Instead, the revision should achieve three main goals: 1. Defend Organic Integrity: Safeguard high standards that guarantee environmental, health, and social benefits. 2. Reduce Bureaucracy: Simplify implementation and compliance for farmers and operators, focusing on practical and proportionate rules. 3. Future-Proof the Regulation: Enable the organic framework to evolve with technological, climatic, and market realities. Organic farming is presented as a strategic response to Europes multiple crisesgeopolitical instability, climate change, and rural decline. It reduces input dependency, builds soil fertility, enhances biodiversity, and revitalizes rural economies through shorter supply chains and local value creation. IFOAMs strategic approach includes: Identifying and prioritizing areas requiring Basic Act adjustments. Advancing simplification through secondary legislation and guidance. Promoting a European Organic Action Plan to boost demand and consumption. Key Proposals for Amendment of the Basic Act 1. Resolution for labelling of equivalent imported products (Herbaria Ruling) 2. Postponement of trade agreement deadlines 3. Adjusting criteria for setting up the list of cleaning and disinfection substances and products for processing and storage 4. Eligibility criteria for groups of operators Please, read our detailed contribution on the listed topics and our view on possible burden-reduction through secondary legislation and other non-legislative tools in the attachment.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: The update of the values provided is acknowledged. Although the MRR Regulation requires that all industrial CO emissions from fixed EU ETS installations be accounted for in fulfilling their compliance obligations, it also provides, in accordance with the EU ETS Directive, for the assignment of a zero-emission factor to emissions resulting from the combustion of sustainable biomass. This regime is applicable to sustainable biomethane, insofar as it is produced through the upgrading of sustainable biogas. In this context, we fully acknowledge the vital importance of the Union Database (UDB) as the definitive framework for safeguarding the sectors integrity. In line with current Regulation and interpretative guidance, the UDB serves as the necessary system for verifying biomass sustainability and providing the robust oversight necessary to prevent double counting and ensure market transparency. Nevertheless, the attribution of a zero-emission factor is applied irrespective of the carbon intensity effectively determined for biomethane under the relevant annexes of the MRR Regulation. The current regulatory approach limits innovation in biomethane projects. By capping recognition at a zero-emission factor irrespective of the actual - including negative - carbon intensity of biomethane, the EU ETS framework for the industrial sector removes economic incentive to invest in advanced production pathways capable of delivering net-negative emissions. This lack of differentiation creates a disincentive for technological breakthroughs and tends to divert biomethane deployment towards non-EU ETS sectors, where its superior carbon performance can be more effectively recognised or monetised.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: ERGaR is providing input to this consultation in order to contribute practical, market-based expertise to the revision of Annexes V and VI and to support the development of a clear, workable and harmonised GHG accounting framework for biomethane across the EU. Drawing on the operational experience of its members, ERGaRs contribution aims to address remaining legal and methodological uncertainties that risk undermining investment and cross-border market development. In particular, ERGaR calls for the explicit preservation of the option to use duly substantiated actual values alongside defaults, and for clear, consistent recognition of liquefaction by equivalence and grid-based business models, in order to ensure regulatory coherence, technological neutrality and legal certainty.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The use of open-source software within a regulatory framework that prioritizes the individual over profit is not only commendable but absolutely worth promoting. Over the years, Europe has chosen to delegate hardware and software development to third parties, and today, in this field as in many others, we risk facing disastrous consequences. It is essential to become as independent as possible from the major players in the sector and to provide adequate alternatives for businesses and citizens. However, this must be accompanied by user training and awareness-raising activities; otherwise, we risk tilting at windmills: those who use Microsoft Office today not only are unaware of the alternatives but also have no desire to learn how to use them. The same applies to mainstream applications, both in domestic and corporate environments, on PCs and smartphones alike. In Europe, we already have the know-how to introduce something new. I simply think of Linux distributions like Mint or OpenSUSE, or smartphone operating systems like MurenaOS or SailfishOS. Without having to start from scratch, it would be necessary to bring together the main European open-source actors in a consortium to create an ecosystem no longer dominated by American and Chinese giants such as Microsoft, Google, Apple, ByteDance, and the like. At the same time, education, starting from primary schools, should include up-to-date computer science curricula taught by teachers whose expertise is not limited to the Windows ecosystem. There is so much more to sayfrom privacy issues to the superficial way both adults and teenagers use applications. No one reads the terms and conditions, few use strong passwords, and even fewer adjust the settings on accounts created across various platforms (and sometimes its pointless anyway, since the average citizen has no way of knowing whether opting out of a particular setting actually stops tracking or not). One thing is certain: regardless of everything else, these issues must be addressed in the most serious, yet also the swiftest, way possible. Technology now moves at a dizzying pace, and legislation struggles to keep up, failing to regulate its use effectively. In fact, sometimes regulation intervenes in such a heavy-handed way that it ends up stifling development. Yet, for all its limitations, we have the GDPRsomething the rest of the world should envy us for. Open source, education, investment in transparent and ethical technology, independencethese are all issues of fundamental importance that must be turned into reality as soon as possible. By the way, this message has been translated to english using Mistral AI. Not Chatgpt or Gemini. Every little action has its meaning.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: The rules for organic production are unfortunately often perceived as difficult to understand, costly to apply, and overly detailed. The proportion of organic laying hens in Sweden has decreased from around 18% to just over 10% in recent years. This proportion will likely further decline when the transition period for organic pullet production ends and the rules on outdoor access for pullets come into force. By simplification, this could however help reverse the trend. Simplifications are therefore needed at all levels : National applications Interpretative notes and other guidance issued by the Commission Implementing and delegated regulations adopted by the Commission The basic regulation (EU) 2018/848. The following concrete proposals for simplifications would lead to a more competitive production and a reduced regulatory burden for organic egg and poultry producers : 1. Allow 5% conventional protein feed for laying hens throughout the entire laying period 2. Allow purified amino acids for poultry 3. Approve protein from conventionally produced insects for organic feed 4. Apply zero-day withdrawal periods for veterinary medicines in organic production, instead of the current 48 hours 5. Remove the requirement for outdoor access during rearing 6. Allow alternating use of facilities for organic and conventional pullets 7. Introduce negative lists instead of positive lists for permitted disinfectants and cleaning agents Please see the attached file for detailed comments.

Allow 5% conventional protein feed for laying hens throughout the entire laying period 2. Allow purified amino acids for poultry 3. Approve protein from conventionally produced insects for organic feed 4. Apply zero-day withdrawal periods for veterinary medicines in organic production, instead of the current 48 hours 5. Remove the requirement for outdoor access during rearing 6. Allow alternating use of facilities for organic and conventional pullets 7. Introduce negative lists instead of positive lists for permitted disinfectants and cleaning agents Please see the attached file for detailed comments.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: [Answer 4] What technology areas should be prioritised and why? Priority technology areas should create a secure, scalable, and sovereign foundationthen enable higherlevel capabilities like AI and domain services. Cloudnative platform core (onprem + sovereign service model): Build a modular, standardsbased cloud stack that runs both onprem and via trusted European operators. A single, convergent approach (e.g. aligning DeutschlandStack, Sovereign Cloud Stack, GaiaXaligned efforts) avoids duplicated work and creates a common operational baseline for migration, federation and multivendor operation. This is the platform on which everything else depends. Confidential computing & key sovereignty: Confidential computing enables running workloads on thirdparty infrastructure while the customer retains key control. For public sector usecases this allows accredited European cloud offerings to be used without compromising data protection or operational sovereigntylowering cost and expanding hosting choices. Efficient, sustainable AI model families (beyond bruteforce transformers): Prioritise research and adoption of more resourceefficient ML architectures (sparse models, distillation, retrievalaugmented approaches) to reduce cost, energy use and enable realistic onprem or edge deployment at scale. Robust deployment & developer toolchain (secure containers, CI/CD, SBOMs): Simple, reproducible deployment patternssecure container baselines, curated CI/CD templates, automated SBOM generation and standardized test pipelinesare essential to move solutions from prototype to production across many administrations. AI as integrator & automation enabler: Use AI to automate code quality checks, dependency hygiene, test generation and deployment validationmaking secure, auditable delivery at scale feasible. Mature OSS frameworks & curated reference implementations: Consolidate and harden proven OSS building blocks rather than proliferating competing stacks. Fund stewardship and provide productiongrade reference architectures for databases, messaging, identity, observability and more. Unified, federated digital identity (open standards, multilevel authentication): Provide an interoperable, federationready digital identity layer based on open standards (e.g., OIDC/OAuth2, eIDAScompatible flows) that supports multiple assurance levels. A broadly accepted digital identity enables secure single signon, strong authentication for sensitive services, federated access across administrations and trustable user consent models it is the glue for interoperable, usercentric public services. Open Hardware & hardware sovereignty: Include open hardware (e.g., RISCV ecosystems, open designs for edge devices) as a firstclass priority. Hardware sovereignty reduces singlesupplier dependency, improves auditability down the stack, and is essential for resilient edge and highperformance deployments. Especially development of KI- and efficiency/energy-optimized hardware, software and models could create a huge value creation for Europe rather than becoming the next level of dependency. Skills, education & practical knowhow (OSS, licensing, operational practice): Invest in training and capacity building: practical curricula on OSS development, licensing (how to handle mixedlicense stacks, contribution agreements), secure build pipelines and operational best practices. Offer handson workshops, apprenticeships and project clinics that pair administrations with experienced maintainers to transfer knowhow and reduce legal/operational friction. Practical, small wins accelerate adoption: adopt secure container baselines (e.g., ZenDiS SGCI pilots), provide EUhosted CI runners and registries, deploy a federated identity pilot across a set of administrations, and fund a few crossborder reference implementations.

initiative: Renewable energy – revising biofuel, bioliquid and biomass fuel production pathway values and modifying methodology
feedback_text: We welcome the opportunity granted by the European Commission to comment the review and update of Annexes V and VI of the Renewable Energy Directive. The update includes a thorough and scientifically robust revision of emission factors and background data carried out by the JRC. Taken as a whole, and in alignment with expert peers we consider the revision of these data to be scientifically consistent and technically plausible. In particular, we welcome the explicit and transparent treatment of methane leakages from biogas and biomethane plants. This is especially relevant given that many biogas and biomethane operators in Germany calculate actual values and now have a transparent refer-ence framework for accounting for and reducing such emissions. However, we would like to express our concern that the JRC report containing methodological details for the calculation of the new standard values was made available only with significant delay (12 January 2026) and was not linked among the documents on the consulta-tion platform. The information contained in this report is essential for transparency and in-dispensable for understanding ad properly assessing the proposed changes. Following a detailed review of the changes in the Annexes and the corresponding explanations provided in the JRC report we would like to share our findings and recommendation in the document attached.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: We welcome the Commission's initiative to refine the Better Regulation Framework. However, "simplifying" regulation must not serve as a pretext for eroding democratic values, fundamental rights, or the rigorous application of science. For EU lawmaking to remain credible, it must be transparent, participatory, and strictly evidence-based. THE CRISIS OF PROCEDURAL SAFEGUARDS: THE CASE OF THE WOLF A critical example of the current frameworks failure is the recent decision to downgrade the protection status of the wolf (Canis lupus). This process bypassed the very safeguards the Better Regulation Guidelines are meant to uphold: 1) Erosion of Evidence-Based Policymaking: Despite the Guidelines' commitment to "the best available evidence," the proposal to move the wolf from Annex IV to Annex V of the Habitats Directive was pushed forward despite scientific data showing that wolf populations in six out of seven EU biogeographical regions remain in an "unfavourable" or "inadequate" conservation status. 2) Misuse of "Urgency" and Political Expediency: The use of fast-tracked procedures for a non-emergency ecological file sets a dangerous precedent. In this case, political narratives and "populistic scaremongering" were prioritized over the Commissions own in-depth analysis, which indicated that culling is often an ineffective solution for livestock depredation compared to coexistence measures. 3) Flawed Consultations: As noted by the EU Ombudsman in related inquiries, skipping or rushing impact assessments deprives legislators of essential information. The consultation process regarding large carnivores was criticized for being non-transparent and failing to give due weight to the technical input of the scientific community and over 300 civil society organizations. OUR RECOMMENDATIONS FOR THE REVISED GUIDELINES To prevent future "arbitrary" decisions that undermine the EUs environmental acquis and democratic legitimacy, we urge the following: - Science-Based Impact Assessments: All decisions with significant environmental or social implications must undergo a rigorous, independent Impact Assessment. These should be protected from political interference and must explicitly address the Precautionary Principle. - Strict Definition of "Urgency": The Guidelines must clearly define "emergency" cases to prevent the bypass of public consultations and climate checks. Political pressure or "socio-economic challenges" that can be addressed through existing implementation tools should not qualify as grounds for fast-tracking. - Anchoring Core Principles: The "Do No Significant Harm" (DNSH) principle and non-regression clauses must be legally anchored within the Guidelines to ensure that "simplification" does not lead to a lowering of environmental protection standards. - Meaningful Stakeholder Engagement: Consultation should not be a "box-ticking" exercise. The Commission must demonstrate how scientific evidence and public input were considered, providing a thorough "statement of reasons" when expert advice is disregarded. TO CONCLUDE Real simplification of EU law lies in harmonization and proper implementation, not in the weakening of protection for vulnerable species or the bypass of procedural checks and balances. We call on the Commission to reinforce the Better Regulation Guidelines as a shield for evidence-based governance rather than a tool for political deregulation.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: Call for data from the European Commission organic production: simplification and specific updates of comments from the Union of Farmers’ Unions Transparency Register 643875852019-50 Union of Unions appreciates the European Commission’s efforts to simplify organic production rules and reduce administrative burdens, pending knowledge of the legislative texts. In any event, it considers it essential that this simplification does not compromise the integrity, traceability or credibility of the European ecological model or create competitive disadvantages compared to imports from third countries. The EU’s organic framework has consolidated a demanding model and recognised for its sustainability and food security. Their defence requires that all operators, whether European or not, be subject to the same rules. The organisation therefore maintains that products imported as organic must comply with exactly the same production and control rules. Continuity of equivalence conventions is not necessary, as their completion in December 2026 has been known for years and the countries concerned have had time to adapt to the conformity regime. Therefore, a general extension is not appropriate. From 2027, all imports should comply with the principle of full compliance, certified by bodies approved under Regulation (EU) 2018/848. Union of Unions argues that the future revision should include mirror clauses in trade agreements, so that no imported product can compete as green under lower standards. The equivalence system has allowed for substantial differences in production methods and tolerances, creating distortions and weakening consumer confidence. Strict implementation of the conformity regime would strengthen the credibility of the organic label and ensure a level playing field for European producers. The organisation calls for rigorous checks on foreign certifiers, full traceability of imported batches through TRACES, and documentary, identity and physical checks at the border, especially in origins with a history of non-compliance. It also considers it necessary to revise the labelling rules to ensure that only products produced under European rules use the EU organic logo and that the origin and type of certification are clearly visible when there are regulatory differences. Union of Unions also supports technically sound regulatory simplification that facilitates compliance, especially for small farms and extensive models, without weakening ecological discipline. Adaptations should be based on scientific evidence and take into account specific productive realities, ensuring that environmental sustainability is not achieved at the expense of the economic sustainability of farms. Finally, it calls for institutional transparency to be strengthened by means of regular public reports comparing checks on European and imported products, audits of external certifiers and measures taken in response to non-compliances. The organisation reiterates its commitment to robust and credible organic farming and considers that moving towards a system based on full compliance, strict controls and mirror clauses is the best way to protect the European organic model and ensure consumer confidence.

initiative: Communication on better regulation
feedback_text: The French asset management association (AFG) welcomes the ECs call for evidence on better regulation and shares its objective to improve its better regulation policy to remain fit for purpose and future challenges, especially within the SIU agenda. The EU AM industry being a highly regulated sector, it is important to ensure that simplicity by design underpins existing and new pieces of regulation (proportionate, evidence-based and not inducing excessive regulatory burden for market participants). Please see below our proposals. 1.Better reconciliation of the need for evidence-based policies and urgent action in the conduct of ECs better regulation activities AFG believes that integration of competitiveness at different levels is a cornerstone in the success of the better regulation initiative. From a regulatory perspective in the EU financial sector, the EU institutions have not prioritized competitiveness challenges. The fast-paced nature of EU regulation and the legalistic approach of entities like ESMA are impeding the competitiveness of EU actors, creating legal uncertainty: EU actors often experienced lack of synchronization between different regulations (e.g. SFDR with L1 rules entering into force before L2 rules were adopted and before CSRD was tabled). Efficient competitiveness checks should be implemented. As all key jurisdictions (US, Japan, Singapore, UK), ESAs and NCAs mandate shall include competitiveness and medium to long-term growth, including of financial player. The prospective capacity of the EU policy makers should be reinforced to better determine regulatory priorities and strengthen the competitiveness of EU financial sector. 2.Ensuring a holistic approach to stakeholder consultation with a view to implementing a more efficient and effective manner to gather essential information ESAs role should be reinforced with increased transparency in decision-making processes, especially regarding consideration of stakeholders views in public consultations. For instance, targeted consultations should systematically integrate professional associations. Establishing systematic industry workshops and holistic impact assessments led by expert panels before new regulations could alleviate silos approaches and ensure both the essential link of the rule making to the market practices (that are lacking today) and a more predictable legal environment. Results should be publicly available in annual reports issued by such authorities. 3.Practical steps to be undertaken to make EU laws simpler and easier to implement in practice (e.g. as regards the legal instruments, the use of delegated and implementing acts or the application of digital tools) From a global perspective, it is essential to maintain hierarchy of norms, consistency and better sequencing between all levels of EU regulation and limit legislative inflation. The role of each regulatory level should be respected: core principles inserted in L1 legislation while detailed technical rules in L2 or 3. Improving impact assessments from inception could enhance formulation of problems at L0. Legal certainty should be ensured by implementing proper sequencing between regulatory levels, with a possibility to introduce dynamic clauses of entry into force. Realistic timelines for the implementation and entry into force of new pieces of regulation should be systematically introduced to guarantee sufficient consultation time and proper synchronization. We also advocate for a moderate use of review clauses, to avoid constant reopening of the regulation. Existing supervisory tools should be more efficiently used, instead of creating new tools. wider use of no-action letters. Easier implementation also goes with greater supervisory convergence, from a bottom-up perspective, to remove barriers for cross-border activities, avoid discrepancies, duplication or gold-plating, which also goes with more data-sharing and practice-sharing between national authorities and ESA

initiative: European Research Area Act
feedback_text: The University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG) welcomes the European Commissions initiative to prepare a legislative proposal for an ERA Act. With regard to the Call for Evidence: R&D investment target: UMCG supports achieving the 3% R&D investment target but emphasizes that this must not come at the expense of basic or clinical research, nor result in disproportionate emphasis on defense-related R&D. A balanced approach should secure investments in health and biomedical research, prevention-oriented studies, international cooperation, and Social Sciences & Humanities (SSH). Framework conditions and infrastructures: Improvements to framework conditions are welcomed if they add value beyond existing instruments (ERC, MSCA, Erasmus+) and align with initiatives such as the Union of Skills, lifelong learning, and European Universities alliances. For the health domain, the ERA Act should address harmonisation of data access and GDPR implementation, as well as cross-border access to infrastructures such as biobanks, registries, and clinical trial networks, to avoid fragmentation and accelerate innovation. Mobility and research careers: UMCG stresses the need to resolve barriers in tax and premium levy systems for mobile scientific personnel. A level playing field is essential so that researchers and teachers are not disadvantaged by differences between Member States. Particular attention is required for clinician-scientists, including recognition of clinical training periods across the EU, support for combined care-and-research career paths, and portability of pensions and social benefits. Creating sustainable and attractive research careers, especially for young medical researchers, is crucial. Interdisciplinarity and societal impact: The ERA Act should promote interdisciplinary collaboration between medical sciences, social sciences, and data/AI disciplines to address key societal challenges such as ageing, chronic disease, and health inequities. Embedding societal impact, patient involvement, and citizen engagement into ERA evaluation frameworks is essential for relevance and public trust. Regional innovation ecosystems: Finally, UMCG highlights the importance of strengthening regional innovation ecosystems in healthcare and life sciences, such as those in the Northern Netherlands, and ensuring their integration into EU-wide networks. This will help reduce disparities between regions and contribute to a more inclusive European Research Area.
topic: Employment and social affairs, Research and innovation, Single market
text_raw: The University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG) welcomes the European Commissions initiative to prepare a legislative proposal for an ERA Act. With regard to the Call for Evidence: R&D investment target: UMCG supports achieving the 3% R&D investment target but emphasizes that this must not come at the expense of basic or clinical research, nor result in disproportionate emphasis on defense-related R&D. A balanced approach should secure investments in health and biomedical research, prevention-oriented studies, international cooperation, and Social Sciences & Humanities (SSH). Framework conditions and infrastructures: Improvements to framework conditions are welcomed if they add value beyond existing instruments (ERC, MSCA, Erasmus+) and align with initiatives such as the Union of Skills, lifelong learning, and European Universities alliances. For the health domain, the ERA Act should address harmonisation of data access and GDPR implementation, as well as cross-border access to infrastructures such as biobanks, registries, and clinical trial networks, to avoid fragmentation and accelerate innovation. Mobility and research careers: UMCG stresses the need to resolve barriers in tax and premium levy systems for mobile scientific personnel. A level playing field is essential so that researchers and teachers are not disadvantaged by differences between Member States. Particular attention is required for clinician-scientists, including recognition of clinical training periods across the EU, support for combined care-and-research career paths, and portability of pensions and social benefits. Creating sustainable and attractive research careers, especially for young medical researchers, is crucial. Interdisciplinarity and societal impact: The ERA Act should promote interdisciplinary collaboration between medical sciences, social sciences, and data/AI disciplines to address key societal challenges such as ageing, chronic disease, and health inequities. Embedding societal impact, patient involvement, and citizen engagement into ERA evaluation frameworks is essential for relevance and public trust. Regional innovation ecosystems: Finally, UMCG highlights the importance of strengthening regional innovation ecosystems in healthcare and life sciences, such as those in the Northern Netherlands, and ensuring their integration into EU-wide networks. This will help reduce disparities between regions and contribute to a more inclusive European Research Area.

feedback_text: Dear Sir/Madam, Food at present 100 % bio laliment is too fatty and not rich enough in protein. Because of this, the hens make very soft feelings, which salitis the eggs a lot. They also have to eat more to compensate, which increases our costs and creates stress in the lot. Ban on vermifuge. Without effective fouling, hens are quickly invaded from worms. They shrink, become weak, trap each other, and there are more infections. Mortality is increasing. This is clearly against the animal welfare we are trying to guarantee. Cordially

initiative: Enhancing the strategy for the rights of persons with disabilities up to 2030
feedback_text: Invisible welcomes the Commission's efforts to enhance the strategy for the rights of people with disabilities and calls for: Greater EU recognition of ostomy as an invisible disability/condition to better tackle limited patient involvement in political, economic and medical decision-making. Directly addressing the economic and labour impact of invisible conditions, such as ostomies, to improve working conditions and remote work options for those with disabilities, thereby enhancing the EUs workforce and competitiveness. Guaranteeing equal access and security of necessary medical supplies of ostomy patients to ensure physical and mental wellbeing.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I strongly welcome the European Commissions clear recognition of the strategic importance of open-source software and open digital ecosystems. Open source is essential for transparency, security, privacy, user autonomy, and long-term resilience. The acknowledgement that excessive dependence on non-EU (in particular US-based) technology providers undermines control over critical digital infrastructure is both accurate and overdue. Open source, by its very nature, aligns well with European values such as privacy, interoperability, and democratic oversight. Supporting open-source development, maintenance, and adoptionespecially in foundational layers such as cloud, AI frameworks, cybersecurity, and hardwareis a positive and necessary objective. However, I have serious concerns about how this objective is pursued, particularly regarding the role of EU-level governance and coordination. While reducing dependence on non-EU actors is desirable, replacing it with increased dependence on EU-level institutions risks creating a different form of centralisation. Technological sovereignty should not mean shifting control away from private monopolies only to concentrate it within supranational political structures. For many citizens, national sovereignty and democratic accountability remain fundamental, and EU-led frameworks are often perceived as distant, opaque, and politically driven. To address this, I would urge the Commission to explicitly design this strategy around decentralisation and subsidiarity, rather than central coordination alone. Concrete suggestions: 1. Prioritise national and regional implementation - EU action should focus on enabling Member States, not directing them. - Funding, procurement guidance, and best practices should be modular and optional, allowing national governments to decide how open-source solutions are adopted, governed, and sustained. - National public administrations should be encouraged to develop their own open-source strategies, adapted to local needs, legal systems, and political cultures. 2. Avoid EU-controlled single platforms - The success of open source depends on diversity and federation. - The EU should avoid creating or implicitly favouring single EU-managed repositories, cloud platforms, or foundation structures that could become de facto monopolies. - Instead, support interoperability between existing national, regional, and community-run infrastructures. 3. Strengthen subsidiarity in procurement - Public procurement rules should explicitly allow and encourage national and local authorities to prefer open-source solutions without forcing uniform EU-wide standards or vendors. - Member States should retain control over procurement decisions, especially for sensitive or critical infrastructure. 4. Support independent governance models - Open-source projects and digital commons should be governed by independent foundations, cooperatives, or community-based modelsnot directly by EU institutions. - EU funding should come with strict safeguards to prevent political capture or agenda-setting through financial leverage. 5. Focus EU action on coordination, not control - The EUs most constructive role is facilitating knowledge sharing, cross-border interoperability, and research collaboration. - Strategic alignment should emerge organically from cooperation between Member States, not from top-down frameworks. In summary, I strongly support the goal of strengthening open source as a means to reduce technological dependence on external actors and to improve privacy, security, and resilience. However, this goal will only gain broad public legitimacy if it respects national sovereignty, avoids new forms of centralisation, and remains faithful to the decentralised ethos that made open source successful in the first place. Technological independence should empower citizens, communities, and Member Statesnot merely shift dependency from one centre of power to another.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: I am an EU resident working on the EC supported CRA standardization efforts who has come to believe that open source software and its communities are a necessary resource for supporting an effective European standardization ecosystem capable of addressing software and EU digital sovereignty goals. ESO standardization efforts struggle to involve open source technical experts, delegates, and other contributors due to antiquated, overly formal, and time consuming methods and structures. These include excessive meetings, poor dissemination, secrecy, barriers to entry, processes that allow delay by single delegates, opaque governance, and a preference for multi-year timelines. This environment makes input from the FOSS community unlikely and so deprives EU software standardization of many of the most important voices in the industry, as well as those whose values are most aligned with European values. For software standards intended to become Harmonized European Norms or those supported by governmental funds, a lighter weight process could be imposed. Such a process incorporating asynchronous comment review tools instead of twenty year old spreadsheets, less hierarchical committee structures where experts or EC guidance cannot be sidelined or delayed by process manipulation, improved technology platforms, and an overall process designed to implement complex regulation. Transparent and streamlined processes could be imposed by contract or as a regularized part of the process of Harmonization and is necessary to if EU software standards wish to keep up with the pace of the industry. FOSS becomes especially valuable in this context as it represents a pool of expertise that the existing processes have not been able to meaningfully access. Key changes might include: 1. Regularizing a method for open source projects to delegate technical experts on a guest basis without full ESO membership. 2. Changes to IP policies to clarify the non-commercial nature of supported standards, such as a royalty-free patent and liberal copyright policies. 3. Drafting processes that emphasize asynchronous work and review rather than meetings. 4. Contracts with outside experts or contributors funded and designed based on regular pay for time worked at a rate appropriate to the software industry. This encourage efficiency among by the ESOs and help avoid alienating contracted experts and their professional communities, including FOSS. 5. A single web platform for public comment and internal comment (as well as industry-standard mechanisms through contributions via pull requests). 6. Realistic timelines that contain a detailed and specific breakdowns of the expected processes and number of hours expected time to produce each draft or other product instead of general milestones. 7. Large numbers of related standards efforts should be organized and staggered so that they receive sufficient attention and staffing. 8. Mechanisms for Commission representatives to easily override ESO decisions and resolve conflicts within drafting processes. 9. A continuous comment and resolution process that also includes mechanisms to better combine related comments and dismiss inappropriate, misdirected, or reoccurring and resolved comments. 10. Tools to mechanically sort public comments and tag or remove LLM generated and other spam comments, resolving them outside any formal process.

initiative: EU emissions trading system for maritime, aviation and stationary installations, and market stability reserve – review
feedback_text: The European Commission is preparing proposals to amend the Emissions Trading Directive. It gives the opportunity to comment on the review of the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS, here ETS 1: Maritime, aviation and fixed installations). Some aspects of the EU ETS will be revised to ensure that the EU ETS continues to contribute to the overarching objective of economy-wide carbon neutrality by 2050 in the most cost-effective and economically efficient way possible. In this context, the Commission intends to explore the possibility of including municipal waste incineration installations in the EU ETS as of 2028. What is the social housing sector proposing? The incineration of municipal waste generates unavoidable waste heat, which in many cases is used for heating. This waste heat should never be included in the ETS. Incineration of residual municipal waste is not alternative because the landfill capacitors are limited and need to be saved. It also has nothing to do with the usual industrial processes and energy use covered by the ETS. The reduction of residual waste and the pathway to the circular economy cannot be accelerated by the carbon price, and they are subject to different legal requirements. For example, rules on liability and approval of reused products in the construction sector need to be established. For heat supply, unavoidable waste heat, such as renewable energy, is assessed as CO2-free (Heat Planning Act and Building Energy Act, here only 20 g/kWh is used for auxiliary energy and support firing). This is important and particularly important for district heating networks. If this waste heat were to be used, they would not otherwise be able to provide CO2-free heat. On the other hand, it would be schizophrenic to assess the heat from unavoidable waste heat as carbon-free, but to charge a carbon price. There are two possible consequences of inclusion in the ETS: The district heating supplier could, for reasons of cost, refrain from using the waste heat from the incineration of municipal waste. This does not make sense because this volume of wine would then enter the air and would have to be produced elsewhere. Building owners could refrain from using district heating to avoid the carbon price, e.g. by building a local heat pump. Nor does this make sense. Therefore, unavoidable waste heat from the incineration of municipal waste must not receive a carbon price. The reduction of residual waste must be carried out by other means.

initiative: Organic production – targeted updates and simplification
feedback_text: I work in a regional organisation that supports the development of biological and local lalimation in public institutions, in particular by training kitchen teams and raising awareness of sustainable quality. As a professional engaged, I would like to express my support for a limited revision of Regulation (EU) 2018/848, limited to the necessary legal adjustments. The stability of the regulatory framework is essential to ensure consumer confidence and the economic sustainability of organic producers. On the ground, the jobholder is already showing that many people are lost as a result of the multiplicity of labels and struggle to understand what the organic label actually guarantees. Changing it today would risk adding further confusion, undermining the credibility of the label and giving new arguments to those who have long sought to discredit it. The priority must be to support the structuring of organic sectors and public policies that promote their access, particularly in mass catering. I therefore ask the European Commission to limit the revision to strictly necessary adjustments, without calling into question the founding objectives and principles of the European Biological Regulation.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: The initiative is very much appreciated. I believe it is important for the strategy to connect aspects of civil participation, democratic values and technical concepts like open source. The work that has been done for Digital Public Goods could be useful (see e.g. the digital public goods alliance), because the analogy to physical public goods makes it easier to understand why open source and open technologies are important for the community. In addition it would help public organisations to have clear guidance and regulation that digital public goods are not only used but that they also have to contribute to the ecosystem actively. This creates pressure for organisations to support and to align to the goal of open and value-based digital ecosystems.

initiative: EU public procurement rules – revision
feedback_text: To ensure project quality, economic sustainability, works safety and the protection of the public interest, the Public Procurement Directive should: 1. Enhancing competition in the intellectual services market: — Prohibit framework agreements for intellectual services, in particular for SIAs. — Limit the use of integrated procurement to exceptional cases only. — Prohibit agreements between PAs when the market is contestable. 2. Ensuring independence, quality and technical role of design: — Preserve the dependence of the designer and the distinction of roles between design and execution. — Promote design quality as a central criterion in awards. 3. Ensure competitive parity and limit direct public awards – Enable awards to public/investee bodies only after a market comparison and verification of the same capacity and reliability requirements as those required of private operators. — Limit the design centres to management/supervision/coordination functions, without replacing the technical services offered by the market. 4. Making contracts fairer and more sustainable – Extending the price review clause to designs that, due to delays or deficiencies in the public administration, are of a continuous nature. — Introduce a compulsory 10-year postuma insurance cover for executors who may fail. 5. Improving governance, transparency and market access – Establishing a European performance and performance monitoring system. — Strengthen pro-SMEs (proportionality requirements, accessibility, zoning). — Strengthen prevailing quality criteria and simplification tools (DPS, soccorso istruttorio). Final objective Building a more open, competitive and sustainable procurement system, fully in line with the Small Business Act, EU directives and national codes, capable of: ensuring high-quality results, reducing time and risk, avoiding concentrations and distortions, fostering SME participation, preventing the creation of empty boxes and improper public monopolies.
