Improving governance, transparency and market access – Establishing a European performance and performance monitoring system. — Strengthen pro-SMEs (proportionality requirements, accessibility, zoning). — Strengthen prevailing quality criteria and simplification tools (DPS, soccorso istruttorio). Final objective Building a more open, competitive and sustainable procurement system, fully in line with the Small Business Act, EU directives and national codes, capable of: ensuring high-quality results, reducing time and risk, avoiding concentrations and distortions, fostering SME participation, preventing the creation of empty boxes and improper public monopolies.

initiative: European Open Digital Ecosystems
feedback_text: Government officials from many countries increasingly use Signal messaging app. No government or large centralized entity can be trusted to provide secure communications. Only an open-source app can be truly proven to be private. As recently expressed by experts, AI agents are being deployed in operating systems and this will compromise the security of those operating systems. This is a major security vulnerability that requires prompt action. Linux usage is growing and the EU should support that growth. Our operating systems and communication channels mentioned here are the modern equivalent to roads and ports. They need to be entirely supported by public funds to maintain objective delivery to all all citizens free from corruption by centralised controllers. Integrity of information and social dialogue are at risk. Centralized algorithms and user data are used to favour chosen narratives and nudge social preference. (Read Cambridge Analytica, Emerdata, Emic Consulting, Auspex International, Data Propria etc). Open-source solutions like the Fediverse and ActivityPub decentralize this power. It allows users to choose between apps to join the discussion. The EU can host it own fediverse applications where users can connect. By reducing dependency on non-EU countries in this way the EU will also overcome any dependency on any single state. Rather than changing from one single point of failure to another we can empower a larger community of EU stakeholders. The connections and strength of this network delivers a competitive edge to the EU. It will empower citizens to choose international connections and build on these relationships within the most robust secure framework. An establish well connected Fediverse provides the EU with tools and connections to quickly disseminate projects and initiatives. The new era of organization provides connections to the EU's various digital ecosystems embedded within social networks. Showing citizens embedded (from source) information will help combat misinformation currently overwhelming their social timelines. Using the Fediverse users and instances (their chosen connection point) can control their connections and data flow, maintaining privacy. It is these connections and data which deliver a competitive advantage to the EU while preserving privacy from non-EU countries. Open source and decentralization are crucial parts of maintaining data integrity. No government or large centralized entity can be trusted to maintain objectivity. Wikipedia and other decentralized attempts to provide the public with objective information should be supported by the EU. While pursuing these open-source solutions, the EU will empower smaller member states who are trying to regulate corporations larger than them. Open source delivers the fastest, most secure applications. The EU will benefit from the contributions of open-source developers from all nations while preserving our connections and data privacy from non-EU countries. It is the transparency that makes open source applications more secure. By accepting this new paradigm first and acting to reward all open-source developers, the EU further gains a first mover competitive edge. Priorities are; using available feasible open-source software within EU government(Linux, Libre office, etc), using EU in-house developers to work on them, paying external contributors for improvements on the software you use, funding initiatives for development of community-centred open-source software, funding initiatives to set up local instances of Fediverse apps, developing pathways for open-source developers to earn, funding education around algorithms and maintaining data security in schools to inspire the next generation of solutions.

The EU will benefit from the contributions of open-source developers from all nations while preserving our connections and data privacy from non-EU countries. It is the transparency that makes open source applications more secure. By accepting this new paradigm first and acting to reward all open-source developers, the EU further gains a first mover competitive edge. Priorities are; using available feasible open-source software within EU government(Linux, Libre office, etc), using EU in-house developers to work on them, paying external contributors for improvements on the software you use, funding initiatives for development of community-centred open-source software, funding initiatives to set up local instances of Fediverse apps, developing pathways for open-source developers to earn, funding education around algorithms and maintaining data security in schools to inspire the next generation of solutions.
